<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse the **OSI layer** of switches and bridges ŌĆö both operate at Layer 2, but switches are *multi-port* bridges with **microsegmentation**.
> - The number of **collision domains and broadcast domains** is the most-marks-losing part of network hardware questions. Memorise the analytical matrix in ┬¦3.4.
> - For **topology** questions, never confuse *physical* topology (cable layout) with *logical* topology (data flow path). Token Ring has a *physical star* but a *logical ring*.
> - In **attenuation problems**, forgetting to convert dB back to linear ratio costs full marks. Always: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-A/10}$.

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Hub, Bridge, and Switch with respect to their OSI layer of operation and collision domain behavior.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Device | OSI Layer | Collision Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Hub | Layer 1 (Physical) | All ports share **1 collision domain** |
| Bridge | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Creates **2 separate collision domains** (port-wise) |
| Switch | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Each port is a **separate collision domain** (microsegmentation) |

A **hub** broadcasts incoming signals to all ports, a **bridge** uses MAC address tables to filter frames between two segments, and a **switch** is essentially a multi-port bridge that creates a dedicated collision domain per port using full-duplex communication.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Explain Total Internal Reflection with a neat diagram. State the condition for TIR in optical fiber.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** is the optical phenomenon where a light ray striking the boundary between two media at an angle greater than the critical angle is **completely reflected back** into the denser medium, with no refraction.

**Conditions for TIR:**
1. Light must travel from a **denser medium to a rarer medium** ($n_1 > n_2$).
2. The angle of incidence must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$.

**Critical Angle Formula:**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

In optical fiber, the core ($n_1$) and cladding ($n_2$) are designed so that light entering within the **acceptance cone** undergoes TIR repeatedly, allowing the signal to propagate along the fiber with minimal loss.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the different types of transmission media with suitable diagrams. Compare guided and unguided media.**

**Model Solution:**

#### 1. Guided (Wired/Bounded) Media:
The signal energy is **confined within a solid conductor**. Three main types:

**(i) Twisted Pair Cable:**
- Two insulated copper wires twisted helically.
- Types: UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) and STP (Shielded Twisted Pair).
- Used in telephone lines and Ethernet LANs (Cat 5e, Cat 6).
- Maximum segment length: **100 m**.

**(ii) Coaxial Cable:**
- Has a central conductor, dielectric insulator, metallic shield, and outer PVC jacket.
- Higher bandwidth and better noise immunity than twisted pair.
- Used in cable TV distribution and legacy Ethernet (10BASE-2/5).

**(iii) Optical Fiber Cable:**
- Transmits data as **light pulses** through a glass core surrounded by cladding.
- **Principle:** Total Internal Reflection.
- Types: Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) and Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF).
- Advantages: Extremely high bandwidth (THz), immune to EMI, very low attenuation.
- Disadvantages: Expensive, requires precise connectors and skilled installation.

#### 2. Unguided (Wireless) Media:
Signals propagate through **free space** as electromagnetic waves.

**(i) Radio Waves** (3 kHz ŌĆō 300 MHz): Omnidirectional, used in AM/FM, WiFi (2.4 GHz ISM band).
**(ii) Microwaves** (300 MHz ŌĆō 300 GHz): Line-of-sight, used in satellite and cellular.
**(iii) Infrared** (300 GHz ŌĆō 400 THz): Short range, line-of-sight, used in TV remotes.
**(iv) Satellite Communication:** Uses microwave transponders, covers wide geographic areas.

#### 3. Comparison Table:

| Property | Guided Media | Unguided Media |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Bounded (cable) | Free space / air |
| Security | More secure | Less secure (tappable) |
| Bandwidth | High (fiber) | Limited by spectrum |
| Installation | Costly (cabling) | Easier (no cables) |
| EMI Susceptibility | Twisted pair/coax affected | Affected (except light freq) |

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Switch. How is it different from a Router?**

**Model Solution:**

A **Switch** is a Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) device that forwards frames based on **MAC addresses**. It is essentially a multi-port bridge that uses a **Content Addressable Memory (CAM) table** to learn and forward frames.

#### Working:
1. **Learning:** When a frame arrives on a port, the switch reads the source MAC address and records it in its CAM table along with the port number.
2. **Forwarding:** For the destination MAC, the switch looks up the CAM table. If found, it forwards the frame **only to that specific port** (selective forwarding). If not found, it **floods** the frame out all ports except the source (unknown unicast flooding).
3. **Filtering:** Frames are filtered if the source and destination are on the same port.

#### Diagram (Conceptual ŌĆö described in text):
```
   [PC-A]---(Port 1)                   (Port 4)---[Server-S]
              \                         /
               \                       /
            [24-PORT ETHERNET SWITCH]
               /                       \
              /                         \
   [PC-B]---(Port 2)                   (Port 3)---[PC-C]
```

#### Switch vs Router:

| Property | Switch (L2) | Router (L3) |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) |
| Addressing | MAC address (48-bit) | IP address (32-bit / 128-bit) |
| Forwarding Unit | Frame | Packet |
| Broadcast Domain | Single (unless VLAN used) | Each interface is a separate BD |
| Routing Table | No (CAM table only) | Yes (IP routing table) |
| Connects | Hosts in same network | Different networks |
| Protocols | IEEE 802.1D, STP | OSPF, BGP, RIP, EIGRP |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the different network topologies with diagrams. List their advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Solution:**

A **network topology** is the geometric arrangement of nodes and links in a network.

#### 1. Bus Topology
All nodes are connected to a **single shared backbone cable** with terminators at both ends.

**Diagram (described):**
```
  [Node1]----[Node2]----[Node3]----[Node4]----[Node5]
       \________  Single Backbone  ________/
```

**Advantages:** Low cable cost, simple for small networks.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, performance degrades under heavy traffic, difficult to troubleshoot.

#### 2. Star Topology
All nodes connect to a **central hub or switch**.

```
              [Node1]
                 |
[Node2] --- (Central Hub) --- [Node3]
                 |
              [Node4]
```

**Advantages:** Easy to install and expand, single node failure does not affect others.
**Disadvantages:** Central device failure brings down the whole network.

#### 3. Ring Topology
Each node connects to exactly two neighbors, forming a **closed loop**.

```
       [Node1] ---- [Node2]
          |            |
       [Node4] ---- [Node3]
```

**Advantages:** Equal access, predictable performance (Token Ring), no need for a central server.
**Disadvantages:** One node failure disrupts the ring (solved by dual-ring topology).

#### 4. Mesh Topology
Every node connects to **every other node** (full mesh) or to selected nodes (partial mesh).

```
   [Node1] -------- [Node2]
     |\__\__\___\___/|
     |       X       |
   [Node4] -------- [Node3]
```

**Advantages:** Highly fault-tolerant, redundant paths, excellent performance.
**Disadvantages:** Very expensive (full mesh requires $n(n-1)/2$ links), complex cabling.

#### 5. Tree (Hierarchical) Topology
Groups of star-configured networks are connected in a **hierarchical structure**.

```
                    [Root]
                   /      \
              [Hub1]      [Hub2]
             /     \      /     \
         [PCs]   [PCs] [PCs]   [PCs]
```

**Advantages:** Scalable, easy to manage sub-networks.
**Disadvantages:** Root failure affects entire dependent branch.

#### 6. Hybrid Topology
Combination of two or more topologies (e.g., Star-Bus, Star-Ring). The **Internet** is the most prominent example.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the function of the following devices with respect to the OSI model: Repeater, Hub, Bridge, Router, Gateway, Modem, and NIC.**

**Model Solution:**

| Device | OSI Layer | Primary Function | Domain Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeater** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Regenerates weakened signals to extend cable range. | Single CD, single BD |
| **Hub** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Multi-port repeater; broadcasts incoming bits to all other ports. | Single CD, single BD |
| **Bridge** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Connects two LAN segments and filters frames using MAC addresses. | 2 CD, single BD |
| **Router** | Layer 3 (Network) | Forwards packets between distinct IP networks using a routing table. | Per port CD and BD |
| **Gateway** | Layers 4ŌĆō7 (TransportŌĆōApplication) | Translates between **dissimilar protocols** (e.g., TCP/IP to IBM SNA). | Per interface |
| **Modem** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Converts digital computer signals to analog for telephone line transmission (and vice versa). | Single CD |
| **NIC** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Physical interface of a host to the network medium; contains the unique 48-bit MAC address. | One CD per NIC |

#### Key Conceptual Hierarchy:
- A **repeater** and **hub** are Layer 1 devices ŌĆö they do not interpret addresses; they only deal with raw electrical/optical signals.
- A **bridge** and **switch** are Layer 2 ŌĆö they interpret **MAC addresses** and forward frames intelligently.
- A **router** is Layer 3 ŌĆö it interprets **IP addresses** and makes path-selection decisions across multiple networks.
- A **gateway** is the most powerful ŌĆö it operates at the highest layers and performs full **protocol conversion** between fundamentally different network architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Examiners often ask *"At which OSI layer does a device operate?"* ŌĆö this is a **3-mark definition question** in Part A. Memorise the table above.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision ŌĆö Module 1: Network Hardware**

- **Network Hardware = Transmission Media + Topologies + Interconnecting Devices**.
- **Three categories of networks:** LAN, MAN, WAN (also PAN). Scope: PAN $<$ LAN $<$ MAN $<$ WAN.
- **Guided Media:** Twisted Pair (UTP/STP, max 100 m), Coaxial (50/75 $\Omega$), Optical Fiber (TIR-based).
- **Unguided Media:** Radio, Microwave, Infrared, Satellite ŌĆö all use electromagnetic spectrum in free space.
- **Optical Fiber Principle:** Total Internal Reflection. Critical angle: $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- **Topologies:** Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid. Full mesh links = $n(n-1)/2$.
- **Device Layer Summary:**
  * Layer 1: Repeater, Hub, Modem
  * Layer 2: Bridge, Switch, NIC, Access Point
  * Layer 3: Router
  * Layer 4ŌĆō7: Gateway
- **Domain Rule:** Higher layer device = more isolated collision and broadcast domains. Only the **router** separates broadcast domains without VLANs.
- **Switch = Multi-port Bridge** with MAC table (CAM table); provides **microsegmentation**.
- **NIC contains 48-bit MAC address**; used at Layer 2 for unique host identification.
- **Attenuation Formula:** $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$.
- **Delay Formula:** $t_{end} = t_{proc} + t_{queue} + (L/R) + (d/v)$.
- **Client-Server** = centralized; **Peer-to-Peer** = decentralized, all nodes equal.
- **The Internet is a hybrid mesh of networks** (inter-network of networks).
- **Logical vs Physical Topology:** Token Ring = physical star, logical ring. Always check both.
- **Hybrid Topology** = most common in real-world enterprise networks.
- **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of fiber: $NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ ŌĆö frequently asked.
- **Single-Mode Fiber** uses **laser**; **Multi-Mode Fiber** uses **LED**.
- **Bus Topology** uses **terminators** at both ends to prevent signal reflection.
- **Mesh Topology** is the most fault-tolerant; ring with dual-counter-rotation is also fault-tolerant.
- **Coaxial cable impedance:** 50 $\Omega$ (Ethernet), 75 $\Omega$ (Cable TV).
- **WiFi operates at 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz** (ISM bands) ŌĆö unguided media.
- **Satellite communication** uses **microwave frequencies** with **geostationary orbit at ~36,000 km**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Most-Asked Mistake in KTU Valuation:** Forgetting that a **Layer 2 switch does NOT separate broadcast domains by default** (only VLANs do). A **router** is the device that *always* separates broadcast domains. Do not lose marks by writing the opposite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Software - Protocol Hierarchies - Design Issues for the Layers - Connection Oriented versus Connectionless Service. Reference Models - The OSI Reference Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Network Software - Protocol Hierarchies - Design Issues for the Layers - Connection Oriented versus Connectionless Service. Reference Models - The OSI Reference Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Network Software & Protocol Hierarchies **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** **Network software** is the collection of algorithms, protocols, and architectural frameworks that govern the communication of data across interconnected computing devices. It is structured as a **Protocol Hierarchy** ŌĆö a layered stack of protocols where each layer provides a specific set of services to the layer above it, using the services of the layer below. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Terminology ŌĆö KTU Board Standard** > - **Protocol:** A formal set of rules governing data exchange between peer entities. > - **Service:** What a layer provides to the entity above it (horizontal interaction between adjacent layers). > - **Interface:** Defines the operations and services a lower layer offers to the upper layer. > - **Peer Entity:** Two entities at the same layer on different machines communicating logically. **Conceptual Analogy (The Airline Travel System):** Imagine you are traveling from Kerala to New York. The journey involves many independent layers: 1. **Application Layer:** You book a ticket (Kerala ŌåÆ New York). 2. **Transport Layer:** The ticket office gives you a boarding pass (Kerala ŌåÆ Mumbai). 3. **Network Layer:** The gate assigns your seat on a specific plane (Mumbai ŌåÆ London). 4. **Data Link / Physical Layer:** The actual plane carries you physically through the air. > [!NOTE] > Just as each layer in air travel adds its own header (boarding pass, gate seat) and passes the packet down, **every layer in a protocol hierarchy appends its own header (encapsulation)** to the data unit, creating a new Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at each level. --- ### 1.2 Design Issues for the Layers The KTU 2024 syllabus (and Tanenbaum/Kurose-Ross) identifies **seven canonical design issues** that every layer's protocol must address: | # | Design Issue | Layer Where Solved | |---|--------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Reliability / Error Detection & Correction** | Data Link, Transport | | 2 | **Addressing** | Network, Transport | | 3 | **Routing** | Network | | 4 | **Flow Control** | Data Link, Transport | | 5 | **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing** | Transport | | 6 | **Connection Establishment / Termination** | Transport, Session | | 7 | **Security & Privacy** | Application, Presentation | --- ### 1.3 Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Service > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition ŌĆö Service Primitives** > Services are categorized by the *quality* and *sequence* of communication they provide, not by the protocol implementation. | Property | **Connection-Oriented Service** | **Connectionless Service** | |----------|-------------------------------|---------------------------| | Analogy | Telephone call | Postal letter | | Path | Pre-established (handshake) | Independent per packet | | Order | Guaranteed sequence | Not guaranteed | | State | Maintained at endpoints | Stateless | | Example | TCP, ATM | UDP, IP, SMTP (mail) | | KTU Keyword | **Virtual Circuit** | **Datagram** | **Three Stages of Connection-Oriented Service:** 1. **Connection Establishment** ŌåÆ Setup handshake (3-way handshake in TCP). 2. **Data Transfer** ŌåÆ Packets follow same path. 3. **Connection Release** ŌåÆ Tear-down with FIN packets. **Six Types of Services (KTU High-Yield):** 1. Reliable Connection-Oriented 2. Reliable Connectionless 3. Unreliable Connection-Oriented 4. Unreliable Connectionless (Datagram) 5. Acknowledged Datagram 6. Request-Reply --- ### 1.4 Reference Models ŌĆö The OSI Reference Model **The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model** was developed by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** as a **7-layer abstract reference model** to unify heterogeneous network architectures. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Aid: "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"** > (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application ŌĆö Bottom to Top) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö How Protocol Hierarchies Work When a message M is sent from source to destination, it travels **down** the source's stack and **up** the destination's stack. Each layer: 1. **Receives a Service Data Unit (SDU)** from the upper layer. 2. **Attaches its own header (and sometimes trailer)** ŌåÆ forming the **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)**. 3. **Passes the PDU down** to the next layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **PDU Nomenclature (KTU Frequently Asked)** > - **Layer 7ŌĆō5 (Application/Session/Presentation):** Data / Message > - **Layer 4 (Transport):** Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) > - **Layer 3 (Network):** Packet / Datagram > - **Layer 2 (Data Link):** Frame > - **Layer 1 (Physical):** Bit / Symbol --- ### 2.2 Detailed Layer-by-Layer Service Analysis #### **Layer 1 ŌĆö Physical Layer** - **Function:** Transmits raw **bits** over a physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless). - **Design Issues Solved:** Mechanical, electrical, optical specifications; bit synchronization; topology (bus, star, ring, mesh). - **Standards:** RS-232, Ethernet (10BASE-T), 100BASE-TX, 802.11 PHY. #### **Layer 2 ŌĆö Data Link Layer** - **Function:** Provides **error-free node-to-node** frame delivery. - **Sub-layers:** **LLC (Logical Link Control)** + **MAC (Media Access Control)**. - **Design Issues Solved:** Framing, **Error Detection** (CRC, parity), **Error Correction** (Hamming), **Flow Control** (Stop-and-Wait, Sliding Window), **Medium Access Control** (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA). - **PDU:** **Frame**. #### **Layer 3 ŌĆö Network Layer** - **Function:** Routes packets from **source host to destination host** across multiple networks. - **Design Issues Solved:** **Routing** (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, Distance Vector, Link State), **Logical Addressing** (IPv4, IPv6), **Fragmentation & Reassembly**, **Congestion Control**. - **PDU:** **Packet / Datagram**. #### **Layer 4 ŌĆö Transport Layer** - **Function:** Ensures **end-to-end** (process-to-process) reliable or unreliable data delivery. - **Design Issues Solved:** **Port-based addressing** (16-bit ports), **End-to-end flow control**, **Connection management** (3-way/4-way handshake), **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing**, **Segmentation & Reassembly**. - **Protocols:** **TCP** (connection-oriented, reliable), **UDP** (connectionless, best-effort). #### **Layer 5 ŌĆö Session Layer** - **Function:** Manages **dialogs (sessions)** between communicating processes ŌĆö establishes, maintains, terminates sessions. - **Services:** Authentication, reconnection after failure, dialog control (half-duplex / full-duplex / simplex), checkpointing. #### **Layer 6 ŌĆö Presentation Layer** - **Function:** Handles **data translation, encryption, and compression**. - **Standards:** ASCII, EBCDIC, JPEG, MPEG, TLS/SSL (encryption), gzip (compression). #### **Layer 7 ŌĆö Application Layer** - **Function:** Closest to the end user; provides **network services to applications** (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, SSH). --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula & Concept Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Exam Table ŌĆö Memorize This** | Concept | Standard / Equation | Unit / Range | |---------|--------------------|--------------| | IPv4 Address | 32-bit, dotted-decimal | $\vert 0 \to 255 \vert$ per octet | | IPv6 Address | 128-bit, hexadecimal | $2^{128}$ addresses | | TCP Port Number | 16-bit unsigned integer | $\vert 0 \to 65535 \vert$ | | Maximum PDU Payload (Ethernet MTU) | Standard MTU | **1500 bytes** | | Maximum PDU Payload (Jumbo Frame) | Extended MTU | **9000 bytes** | | Hamming Distance Minimum | $d_{min} \geq 2t + 1$ | $t$ = correctable errors | | 3-Way Handshake | SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK | 3 packets | | OSI Layers | ISO/IEC 7498-1 | **7** layers | | TCP/IP Layers | DARPA model | **4** layers | | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | bits | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the OSI Model (Top-to-Bottom):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing > (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical) --- ### 2.4 Engineering Utility The **layered reference model** is the foundational engineering principle behind: - **Internet (TCP/IP stack):** Powers the entire World Wide Web. - **5G/6G Mobile Networks:** Protocol stacks map directly to OSI layers. - **Industrial IoT & SCADA Systems:** Layered protocols (Modbus, OPC-UA) provide interoperability. - **Satellite Communication (ISRO, NASA deep-space networks):** CCSDS protocol stack uses OSI-like layering. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Logical Derivation: PDU Encapsulation Process > **Setup:** Suppose Application layer at host A wants to send a message $M$ of length $L$ bytes to host B using TCP over IPv4 over Ethernet. **Step 1 ŌĆö Application Layer (Layer 7):** The application creates an **Application Layer PDU** (e.g., an HTTP GET request). $$\text{PDU}_7 = M$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Transport Layer (Layer 4) ŌĆö Adds TCP Header:** TCP adds a **20-byte** header (minimum) containing source port, destination port, sequence number, ACK, flags, checksum. $$\text{PDU}_4 = \text{TCP\_Header}(20\text{B}) + \text{PDU}_7$$ This PDU is now called a **TCP Segment**. **Step 3 ŌĆö Network Layer (Layer 3) ŌĆö Adds IP Header:** IPv4 adds a **20-byte** header containing source/destination IP, TTL, protocol, header checksum. $$\text{PDU}_3 = \text{IP\_Header}(20\text{B}) + \text{PDU}_4$$ This PDU is now called an **IP Packet (Datagram)**. **Step 4 ŌĆö Data Link Layer (Layer 2) ŌĆö Adds Ethernet Header + Trailer:** Ethernet adds a **14-byte** header (MAC addresses, EtherType) and a **4-byte** trailer (CRC-32). $$\text{PDU}_2 = \text{Eth\_Header}(14\text{B}) + \text{PDU}_3 + \text{Eth\_Trailer}(4\text{B})$$ This PDU is now called an **Ethernet Frame**. **Step 5 ŌĆö Physical Layer (Layer 1) ŌĆö Converts to Bits:** The frame is converted into a stream of **bits** (symbols) and transmitted over the physical medium. $$\text{PDU}_1 = \text{BitStream}(\text{PDU}_2)$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö At Receiver (Host B):** Each layer **strips its corresponding header** (decapsulation) and passes the payload upward ŌĆö a perfect mirror reversal. > [!NOTE] > **Total Overhead Added:** $20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = \mathbf{58 \text{ bytes}}$ per packet header overhead (excluding any optional fields, authentication, or encryption). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Service Comparison > **Logical Proof: When is Connectionless Preferred over Connection-Oriented?** **Step A ŌĆö Define Service Requirements:** Let: - $T_{\text{setup}}$ = Time for connection setup (handshake overhead). - $T_{\text{data}}$ = Time to transmit actual data. - $T_{\text{teardown}}$ = Time to release connection. **Step B ŌĆö Total Time for Connection-Oriented Service:** $$T_{\text{CO}} = T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{data}} + T_{\text{teardown}}$$ **Step C ŌĆö Total Time for Connectionless Service:** $$T_{\text{CL}} = T_{\text{data}}$$ **Step D ŌĆö Decision Criterion:** $$T_{\text{CO}} > T_{\text{CL}} \quad \text{always, since } T_{\text{setup}}, T_{\text{teardown}} \geq 0$$ **Step E ŌĆö Conclusion (Engineering Insight):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Result:** Connectionless service is preferred when: > - The message is **small** (single packet, e.g., DNS query). > - The **latency budget is tight** (real-time voice/video). > - The network is **inherently unreliable** and retransmission is wasteful (e.g., multicast, live streaming). > > Connection-oriented is preferred when: > - Messages are **large and bursty** (file transfer). > - **Reliability is critical** (banking, SSH). > - The application needs **ordered delivery**. --- ### 3.3 Logical Derivation: Why 7 Layers in OSI? **Step 1 ŌĆö Principle of Layer Independence:** Each layer should be designed to perform a **distinct, well-defined function** that minimizes the number of interactions with adjacent layers. **Step 2 ŌĆö Function Segregation:** ISO decomposed network communication into **7 naturally distinct functions:** | Layer | Distinct Function | Justification | |-------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | Physical bit transmission | Hardware distinct from logic | | 2 | Node-to-node framing & error control | Bridging two physical links | | 3 | End-to-end routing | Cross-network addressing | | 4 | End-to-end process delivery | Multiplexing applications | | 5 | Dialog management | Distinct from data flow | | 6 | Syntax/semantic translation | Independent of session | | 7 | User interface services | Application-specific | **Step 3 ŌĆö Optimisation Proof (Information-Theoretic):** Combining two layers reduces modularity and increases inter-layer coupling. Splitting into 7 layers maximises **encapsulation efficiency** (each layer has a single, focused PDU format) and **reusability** (e.g., TCP can run over Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or optical fiber without modification). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö OSI 7-Layer Model (Architectural Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION
HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP"] L6["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION
TLS, SSL, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII"] L5["LAYER 5 - SESSION
NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP"] L4["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT
TCP, UDP, SCTP, DCCP"] L3["LAYER 3 - NETWORK
IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP"] L2["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK
Ethernet, WiFi 802.11, PPP, HDLC"] L1["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL
RS-232, 100BASE-TX, Fiber Optic"] L7 --> L6 L6 --> L5 L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L1 -. BIT STREAM .-> L1R["LAYER 1 - DESTINATION"] L1R --> L2R["LAYER 2 - DESTINATION"] L2R --> L3R["LAYER 3 - DESTINATION"] L3R --> L4R["LAYER 4 - DESTINATION"] L4R --> L5R["LAYER 5 - DESTINATION"] L5R --> L6R["LAYER 6 - DESTINATION"] L6R --> L7R["LAYER 7 - DESTINATION APPLICATION"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Protocol Hierarchy (Source-Destination Data Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SOURCE_HOST["SOURCE HOST - LAYERED STACK"] A7["App Layer
Message M"] A4["Transport Layer
Segment = TCP_Hdr + M"] A3["Network Layer
Packet = IP_Hdr + Segment"] A2["Data Link Layer
Frame = Eth_Hdr + Packet + CRC"] A1["Physical Layer
Bit Stream"] end MEDIUM["PHYSICAL MEDIUM
Copper / Fiber / Wireless"] subgraph DEST_HOST["DESTINATION HOST - REVERSE STACK"] B1["Physical Layer
Receives Bits"] B2["Data Link Layer
Strips CRC, Eth_Hdr"] B3["Network Layer
Strips IP_Hdr, Routes"] B4["Transport Layer
Strips TCP_Hdr, Reassembles"] B7["Application Layer
Delivers Message M"] end A7 --> A4 --> A3 --> A2 --> A1 --> MEDIUM --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B7 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Service Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CONN_ORIENTED["CONNECTION-ORIENTED SERVICE - TCP MODEL"] C1["STEP 1 - CONNECTION ESTABLISHMENT
3-Way Handshake: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK"] C2["STEP 2 - DATA TRANSFER
Sequenced, Acknowledged Packets"] C3["STEP 3 - CONNECTION RELEASE
4-Way Teardown: FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK"] C1 --> C2 --> C3 end subgraph CONN_LESS["CONNECTIONLESS SERVICE - UDP / DATAGRAM MODEL"] D1["PACKET 1
Independent Datagram"] D2["PACKET 2
Independent Datagram"] D3["PACKET 3
Independent Datagram"] D1 --> R["DESTINATION - NO ORDER GUARANTEE"] D2 --> R D3 --> R end ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö PDU Naming Convention ```mermaid flowchart TD PDU["PDU NAMING AT EACH LAYER"] PDU --> P7["Layer 7-5: DATA / MESSAGE"] PDU --> P4["Layer 4: SEGMENT or DATAGRAM"] PDU --> P3["Layer 3: PACKET"] PDU --> P2["Layer 2: FRAME"] PDU --> P1["Layer 1: BITS or SYMBOLS"] ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Encapsulation Process (Step-by-Step) ```mermaid flowchart TD E1["APPLICATION DATA: M
Length: L bytes"] E2["LAYER 4 PDU: TCP_SEGMENT
20-byte TCP_Hdr + M"] E3["LAYER 3 PDU: IP_PACKET
20-byte IP_Hdr + TCP_SEGMENT"] E4["LAYER 2 PDU: ETHERNET_FRAME
14-byte Eth_Hdr + IP_PACKET + 4-byte CRC"] E5["LAYER 1 PDU: BIT_STREAM
Transmitted on physical medium"] E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A ŌĆö 2 Mark Questions (Short Answer)** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** > **Q1. Define a Protocol. How does it differ from a Service in a layered architecture?** > > **Model Answer:** > A **protocol** is a set of rules that governs the communication between two *peer entities* at the same layer on different machines. A **service** is what a lower layer offers to the layer directly above it on the *same* machine. Protocols are **horizontal** (peer-to-peer), while services are **vertical** (layer-to-layer). Example: TCP is a protocol; reliable byte-stream delivery is the service it provides to HTTP. > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper 2]** > **Q2. Differentiate between Connection-Oriented and Connectionless Services with one example each.** > > **Model Answer:** > | Feature | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless | > |---------|-------------------|----------------| > | Path Setup | Required (handshake) | Not required | > | State | Stateful | Stateless | > | Reliability | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed | > | Example | **TCP** | **UDP** | > > Connection-oriented resembles a **telephone call** (handshake ŌåÆ talk ŌåÆ hangup), while connectionless resembles **posting letters** (each message is independent). --- ### **PART B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Choice)** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Past Year Style]** > > --- > > **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Explain the **OSI Reference Model** in detail. List all seven layers with their functions and at least one protocol/standard example for each layer. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** What are the **design issues** that must be addressed when designing the layers of a network? Explain any five with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):** The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model** is a 7-layer abstract reference model defined by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in the standard **ISO/IEC 7498**. It provides a universal framework for network communication. | Layer # | Layer Name | Function | Example Protocol/Standard | |---------|------------|----------|---------------------------| | 7 | Application | Interface to user; network services | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP | | 6 | Presentation | Data translation, encryption, compression | TLS/SSL, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII | | 5 | Session | Dialog control, synchronization | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP, PPTP | | 4 | Transport | End-to-end delivery, multiplexing, flow control | TCP, UDP, SCTP | | 3 | Network | Routing, logical addressing | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP | | 2 | Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11, PPP | | 1 | Physical | Bit transmission on physical medium | RS-232, 100BASE-TX, DSL | **Data Flow:** Data moves **down** the source stack (encapsulation) and **up** the destination stack (decapsulation). Each layer adds its own header (or trailer) to form its own **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)** ŌĆö also known as **Data**, **Segment**, **Packet**, **Frame**, and **Bit Stream** at layers 7, 4, 3, 2, and 1 respectively. **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):** The **design issues** for protocol layers are: 1. **Addressing:** Every layer needs a unique identifier for its entities (MAC address at L2, IP address at L3, Port number at L4). 2. **Error Control:** Detecting and correcting corrupted bits using CRC, parity, or Hamming codes. 3. **Flow Control:** Preventing the sender from overwhelming the receiver (Stop-and-Wait, Sliding Window Protocol). 4. **Routing:** Determining the optimal path from source to destination (Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford algorithms at L3). 5. **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing:** Allowing multiple applications to share a single network connection via port numbers (L4). 6. **Connection Management:** Establishing, maintaining, and terminating communication sessions (3-way handshake in TCP). 7. **Security:** Encryption (L6), authentication (L5/L7), firewalls. > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Past Year Style]** > > --- > > **QUESTION B (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Compare and contrast **Connection-Oriented** and **Connectionless** services. Discuss with diagrams the three phases of a connection-oriented service. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Explain the concept of **Protocol Hierarchies** with a real-world analogy. Show the encapsulation and decapsulation process with a labeled diagram. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):** **Connection-Oriented Service** establishes a logical connection (virtual circuit) between sender and receiver before any data is exchanged. The three phases are: 1. **Connection Establishment:** A 3-way handshake (SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK in TCP) sets up state at both ends. 2. **Data Transfer:** Packets travel through the established path, in sequence, with acknowledgments. 3. **Connection Release:** A 4-way termination (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) cleanly tears down the session. **Connectionless Service** transmits each packet (datagram) independently with no prior setup. Packets may take different routes and arrive out of order. Used in UDP, IP, and SMTP. | Property | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless | |----------|-------------------|----------------| | Setup | Yes (handshake) | No | | Order | Preserved | Not preserved | | State | Stateful | Stateless | | Example | TCP | UDP, IP | **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):** **Protocol Hierarchy** organizes network functions into a layered stack. Each layer performs a specific role and uses the services of the layer below. **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö Postal System:** 1. **Application Layer:** You write a letter (the message). 2. **Presentation Layer:** You translate it into a language the recipient understands. 3. **Session Layer:** You establish a correspondence (mailing address exchange). 4. **Transport Layer:** Postal service ensures end-to-end delivery. 5. **Network Layer:** Sorting office routes the letter via optimal path. 6. **Data Link Layer:** Local delivery van carries it between post offices. 7. **Physical Layer:** The letter physically travels by road/air. **Encapsulation:** Each layer adds a header to the data from the layer above (e.g., Transport adds TCP header, Network adds IP header, Data Link adds Ethernet header + CRC trailer). At the destination, each layer strips its corresponding header in reverse (**decapsulation**) and passes the payload upward to the next layer. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing PDU names:** Many students write "packet" for every layer. **KTU Board requires exact PDU names:** Data (L7), Segment (L4), Packet (L3), Frame (L2), Bits (L1). > 2. **Confusing Service vs Protocol:** Services are *vertical* (between adjacent layers on the same machine); Protocols are *horizontal* (between peer layers on different machines). This is a guaranteed 2-mark question. > 3. **Skipping PDU headers in diagrams:** When drawing encapsulation, **explicitly label the headers** (TCP_Hdr, IP_Hdr, Eth_Hdr, CRC) and their sizes (20B, 20B, 14B+4B). > 4. **Mixing up the OSI and TCP/IP models:** OSI is **7 layers** (theoretical); TCP/IP is **4 layers** (practical). Do not interchange them. > 5. **Forgetting the 3-stage lifecycle** of connection-oriented services: **Establishment ŌåÆ Data Transfer ŌåÆ Release**. Each stage is worth 2ŌĆō3 marks. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Revision ŌĆö KTU 2024 Board Ready** - **Protocol Hierarchy = Layered architecture** with each layer providing services to the layer above. - **5 PDU Names (HIGH-YIELD):** Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bits. - **Service = Vertical (same machine, adjacent layers).** Protocol = Horizontal (different machines, same layer). - **OSI Model = 7 layers** (ISO/IEC 7498). Mnemonic: **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing. - **TCP/IP Model = 4 layers** (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access). Mnemonic: **A**pple **T**ree **I**s **N**atural. - **Connection-Oriented = TCP** (handshake-based, reliable, ordered, stateful). - **Connectionless = UDP** (no handshake, unreliable, unordered, stateless). - **Three Stages of Connection-Oriented:** Establishment ŌåÆ Data Transfer ŌåÆ Release. - **Three Stages of Encapsulation:** Encapsulate at source ŌåÆ Transmit ŌåÆ Decapsulate at destination. - **7 Design Issues:** Addressing, Error Control, Flow Control, Routing, Multiplexing, Connection Management, Security. - **MTU Standard for Ethernet:** **1500 bytes.** Jumbo frames: **9000 bytes.** - **TCP Header:** Minimum 20 bytes; **IP Header:** 20 bytes; **Ethernet Header + Trailer:** 14 + 4 = 18 bytes. - **Real-World Analogy:** Airline Travel / Postal System for layered architecture; Telephone Call vs Postal Letter for connection types. - **Key Bodies:** ISO (OSI Model), IETF (TCP/IP Model), IEEE (802.x Standards), ITU-T (Telecom standards). - **KSU 2024 Favorite Lines:** *"Explain with a neat diagram"* ŌĆö Always draw the **OSI stack**, the **encapsulation process**, and the **3-way handshake**.

topics.title: The TCP/IP Reference Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The TCP/IP Reference Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **TCP/IP Reference Model** (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol Reference Model) is a layered, abstract description for communications and computer network protocol design, developed by the **DARPA** (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency) in the 1970s. It provides a vendor-independent framework for internetworking that allows diverse networks to interoperate over the global Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The TCP/IP Reference Model forms the practical backbone of modern Internet communication. Unlike the theoretical 7-layer OSI model, TCP/IP is a **4-layer (or 5-layer) protocol suite** that was implemented first, before any formal standardization was created around it.

The TCP/IP model is often described as having **4 layers** (RFC-based) or **5 layers** (hybrid academic model used in KTU textbooks such as *Tanenbaum* and *Forouzan*). The KTU 2024 syllabus typically refers to the **5-layer hybrid model** for exam purposes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a **registered parcel internationally**:
- **Application Layer** ŌåÆ You, the sender, writing the letter (the actual message).
- **Transport Layer** ŌåÆ The post office clerk who packs the letter into an envelope, writes the sender/receiver address, and decides whether the letter needs **registered (TCP)** or **ordinary (UDP)** delivery.
- **Internet Layer** ŌåÆ The routing agency that decides the **path** ŌĆö air, sea, or road ŌĆö and labels the parcel with a global tracking number (**IP address**).
- **Network Access / Link Layer** ŌåÆ The actual **truck, ship, or airplane** that physically transports the parcel across borders, handling the mechanics of the road/sea route.
- **Physical Layer** ŌåÆ The **concrete road, sea, and air** itself ŌĆö the medium on which everything travels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as the post office hierarchy works together to deliver your letter, every layer in TCP/IP communicates with its **peer layer** on the receiving computer and provides services to the **layer above it**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in TCP/IP

The TCP/IP model governs several **quantitative standards**:
- **IPv4 Address:** **32 bits** (e.g., 192.168.1.1)
- **IPv6 Address:** **128 bits** (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334)
- **TCP Header:** **20 to 60 bytes**
- **IP Header:** **20 to 60 bytes** (IPv4), **40 bytes** (IPv6)
- **Port Numbers:** **16 bits** (range 0ŌĆō65535)
- **Maximum Segment Size (MSS):** typically **1460 bytes** (1500 MTU ŌłÆ 40 IP/TCP headers)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four / Five Layers ŌĆö Operational Breakdown

#### **Layer 1: Application Layer (Top Layer)**

This is the layer closest to the end-user. It provides **network services directly to user applications**, enabling activities like web browsing, email, and file transfer.

- **Core Functions:**
  - High-level APIs (e.g., sockets).
  - Resource sharing, remote file access, directory services.
  - Email, web, name resolution.
- **Major Protocols:**
  - **HTTP / HTTPS** (Web)
  - **FTP / SFTP** (File transfer)
  - **SMTP / POP3 / IMAP** (Email)
  - **DNS** (Domain Name Resolution)
  - **TELNET, SSH** (Remote login)
  - **SNMP** (Network management)
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** **Data / Message**

#### **Layer 2: Transport Layer (Host-to-Host Layer)**

Provides **end-to-end communication services** between applications running on different hosts. Responsible for reliability, flow control, and multiplexing.

- **Core Functions:**
  - Process-to-process delivery (using **port numbers**).
  - Connection-oriented (TCP) vs. Connectionless (UDP) service.
  - Error recovery, sequencing, flow control, congestion control.
- **Major Protocols:**
  - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** ŌĆö Reliable, connection-oriented.
  - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** ŌĆö Unreliable, connectionless, fast.
- **PDU:** **Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)**

#### **Layer 3: Internet Layer (Network Layer in TCP/IP)**

Handles **logical addressing, routing, and packet forwarding** across interconnected networks. It is the layer that makes the Internet possible.

- **Core Functions:**
  - Logical addressing (IP addresses).
  - Routing of packets from source to destination.
  - Fragmentation and reassembly.
  - Best-effort delivery (no guarantee).
- **Major Protocols:**
  - **IP (Internet Protocol)** ŌĆö IPv4, IPv6.
  - **ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol)** ŌĆö Error & diagnostic messages (e.g., ping).
  - **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)** ŌĆö Multicast group management.
  - **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** ŌĆö Maps IP ŌåÆ MAC.
  - **RARP, OSPF, BGP** ŌĆö Routing protocols.
- **PDU:** **Packet / Datagram**

#### **Layer 4: Network Access / Link Layer**

Defines the **hardware addressing, framing, and topology** used to connect devices on the same physical network segment.

- **Core Functions:**
  - MAC addressing (**48-bit hardware address**).
  - Framing, error detection (CRC), media access control.
  - Defines how bits are placed on the medium.
- **Major Protocols:**
  - **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**
  - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**
  - **PPP, Frame Relay, ATM**
- **PDU:** **Frame**

#### **Layer 5: Physical Layer**

The actual **electrical, optical, or radio transmission** of raw bits. Defines voltage levels, cable specs, connector pinouts, and bit synchronization.

- **Standards:** 10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-LX, etc.
- **PDU:** **Bits / Symbols**

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" ŌĆö Layer Interaction

> [!NOTE]
> **Encapsulation Principle:** As data moves **down** the sender's stack, each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer). This is called **encapsulation**. On the receiver, each layer **decapsulates** (removes) its corresponding header and passes the payload up.

- **Why layering?** It allows **modularity, abstraction, and independent evolution**. We can change HTTP/2 to QUIC without modifying IP.
- **How?** Each layer communicates with its **peer** on the remote system using **headers**. The intermediate devices (routers, switches) only operate up to certain layers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| IPv4 Address Size | $2^{32}$ | addresses | Total IPv4 address space |
| IPv6 Address Size | $2^{128}$ | addresses | Astronomically larger |
| Port Number Range | $0$ to $2^{16}-1$ | integer | Well-known: 0ŌĆō1023, Registered: 1024ŌĆō49151, Dynamic: 49152ŌĆō65535 |
| TCP Header Min Size | $20$ | bytes | Without options |
| TCP Header Max Size | $60$ | bytes | With 40 bytes of options |
| IP Header (IPv4) | $20$ŌĆō$60$ | bytes | Same min/max as TCP |
| MSS (Max Segment Size) | $MTU - 40$ | bytes | Typical 1460 for 1500 MTU |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = Bandwidth \times RTT$ | bits | Capacity of the network pipe |
| Round Trip Time (RTT) | $2 \times \text{One-way delay}$ | seconds (ms) | Time for packet to go and return |
| Throughput (Stop-and-Wait) | $W = \dfrac{\text{Frame Size}}{\text{Frame Size}/Bandwidth + RTT}$ | bps | Window of 1 |
| Throughput (Sliding Window) | $W = \dfrac{n \times \text{Frame Size}}{\text{Time}}$ | bps | $n$ = window size |
| CRC Polynomial Degree | $n$ | ŌĆö | Determines error detection strength |
| MAC Address | $48$ | bits | Burned-in hardware address |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Routing Engineers** use the Internet Layer logic to design scalable BGP/OSPF topologies.
- **Web Developers** leverage Application Layer protocols (HTTP/3 over QUIC over UDP).
- **Cybersecurity Analysts** inspect each layer's headers during packet forensics (Wireshark).
- **Cloud Architects** rely on transport-layer multiplexing (port-based) for microservice communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Layer-by-Layer Comparison with OSI

| OSI Layer (7) | TCP/IP Layer (4/5) | PDU | Address Used | Key Protocols | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7. Application | Application | Data / Message | ŌĆö | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP | Web page request |
| 6. Presentation | Application | Data | ŌĆö | TLS, SSL, JPEG, MIME | Encryption |
| 5. Session | Application | Data | ŌĆö | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP | Session management |
| 4. Transport | Transport | Segment / Datagram | **Port Number (16 bits)** | TCP, UDP, SCTP | Process delivery |
| 3. Network | Internet | Packet | **IP Address (32/128 bits)** | IP, ICMP, ARP, OSPF | Routing |
| 2. Data Link | Network Access | Frame | **MAC Address (48 bits)** | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP | Node-to-node |
| 1. Physical | Physical | Bits | ŌĆö | 10BASE-T, Fiber, Radio | Voltage/light |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Encapsulation Bit Calculation

When a message of size **$M$ bytes** travels from Application to Physical, the **total transmitted bits** can be derived step by step.

Let:
- $M$ = Original application message size (bytes)
- $H_{app}$ = Application layer header (bytes)
- $H_t$ = Transport layer header (bytes)
- $H_n$ = Network (IP) layer header (bytes)
- $H_l$ = Link layer header (bytes)
- $H_{lt}$ = Link layer trailer (bytes)
- $H_p$ = Preamble + SFD (Physical) (bytes, typically $8$)

**Step 1: After Application Layer adds header**

$$\text{Size}_1 = M + H_{app}$$

**Step 2: After Transport Layer encapsulation**

$$\text{Size}_2 = M + H_{app} + H_t$$

**Step 3: After Internet (IP) Layer encapsulation**

$$\text{Size}_3 = M + H_{app} + H_t + H_n$$

**Step 4: After Network Access (Data Link) Layer encapsulation**

$$\text{Size}_4 = M + H_{app} + H_t + H_n + H_l + H_{lt}$$

**Step 5: After Physical Layer adds preamble**

$$\text{Size}_{total} = M + H_{app} + H_t + H_n + H_l + H_{lt} + H_p$$

**Final transmitted bit count:**

$$B_{total} = 8 \times \text{Size}_{total}$$

The **overhead ratio** is therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{M}{M + H_{app} + H_t + H_n + H_l + H_{lt} + H_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of each step:** As data descends, *each layer is unaware of upper layer semantics* ŌĆö it simply treats the incoming PDU as **payload** and prepends its own header. The receiver reverses this in **decapsulation**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: HTTP-over-TCP-over-IP-over-Ethernet Frame

Suppose a user requests a small web page with the following message:
- Application message: $M = 100$ bytes
- HTTP header: $H_{app} = 0$ (absorbed inside $M$)
- TCP header: $H_t = 20$ bytes
- IP header: $H_n = 20$ bytes
- Ethernet header + trailer: $H_l = 14$ bytes, $H_{lt} = 4$ bytes
- Preamble: $H_p = 8$ bytes

**Step 1: After Application ŌåÆ Transport**

$$100 + 20 = 120 \text{ bytes (Segment)}$$

**Step 2: After Transport ŌåÆ Internet**

$$120 + 20 = 140 \text{ bytes (Packet / IP Datagram)}$$

**Step 3: After Internet ŌåÆ Data Link**

$$140 + 14 + 4 = 158 \text{ bytes (Ethernet Frame)}$$

**Step 4: After Data Link ŌåÆ Physical**

$$158 + 8 = 166 \text{ bytes on the wire}$$

**Step 5: Convert to bits**

$$166 \times 8 = 1328 \text{ bits transmitted}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{100}{166} \approx 0.6024 = 60.24\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For small messages, **header overhead dominates** (Ōēł40% wasted). For large bulk transfers, efficiency approaches 100% as $M \to \infty$. This is why **TCP segmentation** and **HTTP/2 header compression (HPACK)** exist.

### 3.4 Bandwidth-Delay Product Derivation

The **BDP** determines how much data can be "in flight" before the sender must stop and wait for an acknowledgment.

$$\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$$

If bandwidth $B = 100$ Mbps and RTT $= 40$ ms:

$$\text{BDP} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 40 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 500 \text{ KB}$$

**Conclusion:** The TCP receive window must be at least **500 KB** to fully utilize a 100 Mbps link with 40 ms RTT. This is why **TCP window scaling** (RFC 1323) is essential for high-bandwidth, long-distance networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TCP/IP Reference Model ŌĆö Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Layer 5 - Application"]
        A1["HTTP / HTTPS"]
        A2["FTP / SFTP"]
        A3["SMTP / DNS / SNMP"]
    end
    subgraph B["Layer 4 - Transport"]
        B1["TCP - Reliable Stream"]
        B2["UDP - Unreliable Datagram"]
    end
    subgraph C["Layer 3 - Internet"]
        C1["IPv4 / IPv6"]
        C2["ICMP / IGMP"]
        C3["ARP / OSPF / BGP"]
    end
    subgraph D["Layer 4 - Network Access"]
        D1["Ethernet IEEE 802.3"]
        D2["Wi-Fi IEEE 802.11"]
        D3["PPP / Frame Relay"]
    end
    subgraph E["Layer 1 - Physical"]
        E1["Copper / Fiber / Radio"]
        E2["Voltage Levels / Bit Timing"]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E -.bits on medium.-> E
```

### 4.2 Data Encapsulation Flow ŌĆö Sender Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app["Application Data"] -->|HTTP Header| h1["Data: HTTP + Message"]
    h1 -->|TCP Header 20B| h2["Segment: TCP Header + Data"]
    h2 -->|IP Header 20B| h3["Packet: IP Header + Segment"]
    h3 -->|Ethernet Header 14B + Trailer 4B| h4["Frame: Header + Packet + Trailer"]
    h4 -->|Preamble 8B| h5["Bits on Physical Medium"]
```

### 4.3 Data Decapsulation Flow ŌĆö Receiver Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bits["Bits Received"] -->|Strip Preamble| frame["Ethernet Frame"]
    frame -->|Strip Header/Trailer| packet["IP Packet"]
    packet -->|Strip IP Header| segment["TCP Segment"]
    segment -->|Strip TCP Header| appdata["Application Data - HTTP Message"]
    appdata -->|Hand to Browser| web["Web Page Rendered"]
```

### 4.4 TCP/IP vs OSI ŌĆö Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OSI["OSI Model - 7 Layers"]
        O7["Layer 7 - Application"]
        O6["Layer 6 - Presentation"]
        O5["Layer 5 - Session"]
        O4["Layer 4 - Transport"]
        O3["Layer 3 - Network"]
        O2["Layer 2 - Data Link"]
        O1["Layer 1 - Physical"]
    end
    subgraph TCPIP["TCP/IP Model - 5 Layers"]
        T5["Application"]
        T4["Transport"]
        T3["Internet"]
        T2["Network Access"]
        T1["Physical"]
    end
    O7 --> T5
    O6 --> T5
    O5 --> T5
    O4 --> T4
    O3 --> T3
    O2 --> T2
    O1 --> T1
```

### 4.5 Process-to-Process Delivery ŌĆö Socket Addressing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hostA["Host A - 192.168.1.10:54321"] -->|TCP Segment| net["Internet Routers"]
    net -->|IP Forwarding| hostB["Host B - 10.0.0.5:80"]
    hostB -->|HTTP Response| net
    net --> hostA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the five layers of the TCP/IP reference model and write one protocol used at each layer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five layers of the TCP/IP reference model are:

| Layer | Protocol |
|---|---|
| 1. Application Layer | **HTTP** (or FTP, DNS, SMTP, SNMP) |
| 2. Transport Layer | **TCP** (or UDP) |
| 3. Internet Layer | **IP** (or ICMP, ARP) |
| 4. Network Access Layer | **Ethernet** (or PPP, Wi-Fi) |
| 5. Physical Layer | **10BASE-T** (or 100BASE-TX, Fiber) |

*(1 mark for listing layers correctly, 2 marks for protocols)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare TCP and UDP with respect to reliability, connection type, and header size.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Reliability | **Reliable** (uses ACK, retransmission) | **Unreliable** (no ACK) |
| Connection Type | **Connection-oriented** (3-way handshake) | **Connectionless** |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (min) | **8 bytes** |
| Speed | Slower (overhead of reliability) | Faster (low overhead) |
| Use Case | Web, Email, File transfer | DNS, Video streaming, VoIP |

*(1 mark per correct row, 3 rows = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the TCP/IP reference model with a neat diagram. List the protocols at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain **data encapsulation** and **decapsulation** in TCP/IP. Compute the total on-wire bytes and the efficiency for an application message of **800 bytes** transmitted over Ethernet, considering HTTP, TCP, IPv4, and Ethernet headers/trailers. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The TCP/IP reference model consists of **5 layers** (in KTU 2024 hybrid representation):

**1. Application Layer:** Provides network services to end-user applications.
- Protocols: **HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, TELNET, SNMP, SSH**

**2. Transport Layer:** Provides host-to-host (process-to-process) communication.
- Protocols: **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) ŌĆö reliable, connection-oriented**
- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol) ŌĆö unreliable, connectionless**

**3. Internet Layer:** Handles logical addressing and routing.
- Protocols: **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, ARP, RARP**

**4. Network Access / Data Link Layer:** Defines framing and MAC addressing.
- Protocols: **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, Frame Relay**

**5. Physical Layer:** Defines electrical/optical signaling.
- Standards: **10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-LX, SONET/SDH**

*Diagrammatic representation:* (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 5 layers listed ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Protocols at each layer ŌĆö **3 marks**
- Diagram ŌĆö **2 marks**

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Encapsulation** is the process by which a protocol at each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the PDU received from the upper layer before passing it to the lower layer. **Decapsulation** is the reverse ŌĆö each layer on the receiver strips its corresponding header and passes the payload upward.

**Step-by-step calculation** with $M = 800$ bytes:

- HTTP header: 0 (assumed embedded in $M$)
- TCP header: $H_t = 20$ bytes
- IPv4 header: $H_n = 20$ bytes
- Ethernet header: $H_l = 14$ bytes
- Ethernet trailer (CRC): $H_{lt} = 4$ bytes
- Preamble + SFD: $H_p = 8$ bytes

**Step 1 ŌĆö After Transport:**

$$800 + 20 = 820 \text{ bytes (Segment)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö After Internet:**

$$820 + 20 = 840 \text{ bytes (IP Packet)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö After Data Link:**

$$840 + 14 + 4 = 858 \text{ bytes (Frame)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö After Physical:**

$$858 + 8 = 866 \text{ bytes on wire}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{800}{866} = 0.9238 = 92.38\%$$

**Conclusion:** The total on-wire transmission is **866 bytes = 6928 bits**, and the encapsulation efficiency is **92.38%**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Encapsulation & decapsulation explanation with diagram ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Correct sequential calculation ŌĆö **3 marks**
- Final efficiency computation ŌĆö **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Distinguish between the **OSI reference model** and the **TCP/IP reference model** in detail. Mention at least **five points of difference**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the functions of the **Transport Layer** and **Internet Layer** of the TCP/IP model. Discuss the **TCP 3-way handshake** with a neat sequence diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | **7 layers** | **4/5 layers** |
| Origin | **ISO** (International Standards Organization) | **DARPA / IETF** (ARPANET project) |
| Development | Designed **first**, then protocols built | Protocols **implemented first**, model built later |
| Usage | Theoretical reference model | Practical Internet model |
| Session & Presentation | **Separate layers** (5 and 6) | **Merged** into Application Layer |
| Transport | Reliable TCP-like | TCP and UDP both available |
| Dependency | Protocol-independent | Built around specific protocols (TCP, IP) |

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 5 valid points ŌĆö **5 marks** (1 mark each)
- Examples and clarity ŌĆö **2 marks**

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Transport Layer Functions:**
- **Process-to-process delivery** using port numbers (16-bit).
- **Connection management** (3-way handshake in TCP).
- **Reliability** through ACK, sequencing, and retransmission.
- **Flow control** using sliding window.
- **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing** of application data.

**Internet Layer Functions:**
- **Logical addressing** (IP addresses).
- **Routing** of packets across networks.
- **Fragmentation & reassembly** of large packets.
- **Best-effort delivery** (no reliability guarantee).
- **Error reporting** via ICMP.

**TCP 3-Way Handshake (Connection Establishment):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Server
    Client->>Server: SYN (Seq = x)
    Server->>Client: SYN + ACK (Seq = y, Ack = x+1)
    Client->>Server: ACK (Seq = x+1, Ack = y+1)
    Note over Client,Server: Connection Established - Data Transfer Phase
```

**Step 1:** Client sends **SYN** with initial sequence number $x$.
**Step 2:** Server replies with **SYN + ACK**, sending its own sequence $y$ and acknowledging $x+1$.
**Step 3:** Client sends **ACK** with $x+1$, acknowledging $y+1$. Connection is now **ESTABLISHED**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Transport Layer functions ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Internet Layer functions ŌĆö **2 marks**
- 3-way handshake diagram + explanation ŌĆö **3 marks**

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make ŌĆö Avoid These to Secure Full Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TCP/IP layer count:** KTU 2024 expects the **5-layer hybrid model**. Writing only 4 layers will lose **1 mark** in Part A and possibly **2 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Wrong PDU names:** Remember: Application = **Data/Message**, Transport = **Segment/Datagram**, Internet = **Packet**, Link = **Frame**, Physical = **Bits**. Examiners penalize misuse.
> 3. **Forgetting trailer (CRC) in encapsulation calculations:** Ethernet adds **4-byte trailer** for CRC. Many students omit this ŌĆö a recurring error worth **1 mark**.
> 4. **OSI vs TCP/IP confusion:** OSI is a **theoretical** model designed first; TCP/IP is a **practical** protocol suite implemented first. Reversing this loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the preamble** in Physical Layer overhead calculations.
> 6. **3-way handshake sequence:** Writing "SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK" without mentioning **sequence numbers** loses **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 7. **Forgetting to specify units** (bytes vs bits) in numerical problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ The TCP/IP model has **4 layers (RFC)** or **5 layers (KTU 2024 hybrid)**.
- Ō£ģ Layers (top to bottom): **Application ŌåÆ Transport ŌåÆ Internet ŌåÆ Network Access ŌåÆ Physical**.
- Ō£ģ **PDUs:** Data ŌåÆ Segment ŌåÆ Packet ŌåÆ Frame ŌåÆ Bits.
- Ō£ģ **TCP = Reliable, Connection-Oriented, 20-byte header; UDP = Unreliable, Connectionless, 8-byte header.**
- Ō£ģ **IPv4 = 32-bit addresses, IPv6 = 128-bit addresses.**
- Ō£ģ **Port numbers = 16 bits; range 0ŌĆō65535.**
- Ō£ģ **Encapsulation** = headers added going down; **Decapsulation** = headers stripped going up.
- Ō£ģ **TCP/IP was implemented first** (ARPANET, 1970s); **OSI was designed first** (ISO, 1984).
- Ō£ģ The **3-way handshake** uses **SYN ŌåÆ SYN+ACK ŌåÆ ACK** to establish a TCP connection.
- Ō£ģ **MAC address = 48 bits**; **IP address = 32 bits (IPv4) / 128 bits (IPv6)**.
- Ō£ģ Key Application protocols: **HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, TELNET, SSH**.
- Ō£ģ Key Internet protocols: **IP, ICMP, IGMP, ARP, OSPF, BGP**.
- Ō£ģ **BDP = Bandwidth ├Ś RTT** determines optimal TCP window size.
- Ō£ģ **MSS = MTU ŌłÆ 40** for typical Ethernet (1500 MTU ŌåÆ 1460 MSS).
- Ō£ģ For **KTU 2024 exams**: Always label **all** PDUs, **all** layer functions, and provide **diagrams** for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of OSI and TCP/IP Reference Models. Physical Layer - Transmission Media Overview - Twisted Pair and Fiber Optics. Performance Indicators - Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of OSI and TCP/IP Reference Models. Physical Layer - Transmission Media Overview - Twisted Pair and Fiber Optics. Performance Indicators - Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reference Models in Computer Networks

A **Reference Model** is a logical blueprint that standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstraction layers. Without a common reference model, hardware and software from different vendors cannot interoperate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**: A reference model defines how information travels from an application on one device (sender) to an application on another device (receiver) through a structured hierarchy of functional layers. Each layer serves the layer above it and is served by the layer below it.

There are two dominant reference models in networking history:

- **OSI Reference Model (Open Systems Interconnection)** ŌĆō Conceptual, theoretical, 7-layer model developed by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in 1984. It is rarely implemented in practice but is the universal teaching tool.
- **TCP/IP Reference Model (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** ŌĆō Practical, pragmatic, 4-layer (or 5-layer hybrid) model developed by **DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)** of the U.S. Department of Defense. It is the foundation of the modern Internet.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ŌĆö The Postal Service**

Imagine you want to send a 500-page legal document to a lawyer in another country.

1. You place the document in an **envelope (Application layer ŌåÆ data)**.
2. You write it in a language the lawyer understands ŌĆö possibly translating it (**Presentation layer**).
3. You open a session and notify the lawyer: "I am about to send a confidential file" (**Session layer**).
4. You choose **registered airmail** with tracking (**Transport layer**).
5. You write the destination address (**Network layer ŌåÆ IP address**).
6. You seal it, the postal truck carries it on roads (**Data Link layer**).
7. The airplane flies it across the ocean using radio waves or fiber signals (**Physical layer**).

If any layer fails, the letter never arrives. Each layer in a network is a "service" passed down or up.

### 1.2 Physical Layer Transmission Media ŌĆö Overview

The **Physical Layer** (Layer 1 of OSI / Layer 1 of TCP/IP) is concerned with the **transmission and reception of the raw bitstream over a physical medium**. It defines:
- **Mechanical**: Cable connectors, pin layouts
- **Electrical**: Voltage levels, impedance
- **Functional**: Bit synchronization (start/stop bits, clock)
- **Procedural**: How connections are established/maintained

**Transmission Media** are the physical pathways that carry signals between devices. They are classified into two broad families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guided (Wired/Bounded) Media** ŌĆö Signal travels inside a solid conductor.
> Includes **Twisted Pair**, **Coaxial Cable**, and **Fiber Optic Cable**.
>
> **Unguided (Wireless/Unbounded) Media** ŌĆö Signal propagates freely as electromagnetic waves through air/vacuum. Includes radio waves, microwaves, infrared. *(Out of scope for this topic.)*

### 1.3 Twisted Pair Cable

A **Twisted Pair Cable** consists of two insulated copper conductors twisted around each other. The **twisting cancels out Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** from external sources and **Crosstalk** from adjacent pairs.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Telephone Line**
The landline telephone that runs from the exchange to your home is a twisted pair. The twists prevent the buzzing noise you would hear from a parallel power line.

**Two Variants:**

- **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)** ŌĆö No additional metallic shield. Cheaper, flexible, dominant in LANs (Cat5e, Cat6).
- **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)** ŌĆö Wrapped in a foil/braid shield. Used in industrial/noisy environments.

### 1.4 Fiber Optic Cable

A **Fiber Optic Cable** transmits data as **pulses of light (photons)** through a glass or plastic core, exploiting **Total Internal Reflection**. It offers enormous bandwidth, near-zero attenuation, and immunity to EMI.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Light Pipe**
Think of a long glass tube filled with water. If you shine a laser into one end at the right angle, the light bounces along the inside and exits the other end, even if the tube is bent. That is essentially how optical fiber works.

**Two Variants:**
- **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** ŌĆö Very thin core (~9 ┬Ąm), uses a laser, supports one propagation path ŌåÆ long distance, very high bandwidth.
- **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** ŌĆö Thicker core (50 or 62.5 ┬Ąm), uses LED, multiple propagation paths ŌåÆ short distance (LAN/datacenter).

### 1.5 Bandwidth ŌĆö The Performance Indicator

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024)**: **Bandwidth** is the maximum rate at which data can be transmitted over a communication channel, measured in **bits per second (bps)** and its multiples (kbps, Mbps, Gbps, Tbps).

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Highway**
Bandwidth is the **number of lanes** on a highway. More lanes = more cars can pass per second = more bandwidth. **Latency** is the time a single car takes to travel the length of the highway. **Throughput** is the actual number of cars that reach their destination per second, which is almost always less than bandwidth due to traffic signals, accidents (errors), and toll booths (protocol overhead).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 OSI vs TCP/IP ŌĆö Structural Deep Dive

The OSI model decomposes networking into **7 abstraction layers**, each with a tightly defined service. The TCP/IP model collapses these into **4 layers** (or 5 in the hybrid version widely used in academia) because the original designers prioritized pragmatism over purity.

**Layer-by-Layer Functional Analysis:**

- **Physical Layer (L1)** ŌĆö Converts frames into raw electrical, optical, or radio signals. Defines voltages, bit durations, connector shapes, and modulation schemes.
- **Data Link Layer (L2)** ŌĆö Provides **node-to-node** transfer, framing, error detection (CRC), MAC addressing. Examples: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP.
- **Network Layer (L3)** ŌĆö Provides **end-to-end logical addressing and routing** across multiple links. IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP.
- **Transport Layer (L4)** ŌĆö Provides **process-to-process** delivery, segmentation, flow control, and reliable (TCP) or unreliable (UDP) service.
- **Session Layer (L5)** ŌĆö Manages dialogues (sessions) between applications: setup, maintenance, termination. NetBIOS, RPC, SIP.
- **Presentation Layer (L6)** ŌĆö Handles **data representation, encryption, and compression**. TLS/SSL, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII.
- **Application Layer (L7)** ŌĆö Closest to the user. HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does TCP/IP have fewer layers?**
> In the original ARPANET design, the Session and Presentation functions were **rolled into the Application layer** because most early apps handled them ad-hoc. The Network Access layer of TCP/IP **bundles OSI's Physical + Data Link**, because in practice the hardware driver and the wire behave as one.

### 2.2 Comparison Table ŌĆö OSI vs TCP/IP (KTU High-Yield)

| **Parameter** | **OSI Reference Model** | **TCP/IP Reference Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| Developed by | ISO (International) | DARPA (U.S. DoD) |
| Year of Origin | 1984 (ISO 7498) | 1974 (Vint Cerf & Bob Kahn) |
| Number of Layers | **7** (Physical ŌåÆ Application) | **4** (or 5 in hybrid teaching model) |
| Layer Count Logic | Strictly separated by function | Combined pragmatically |
| Approach | Conceptual / Theoretical | Pragmatic / Protocol-Centric |
| Adoption | Reference / Teaching only | De facto Internet standard |
| Dependency | Protocol-independent model | Model built around TCP, IP, UDP |
| Session & Presentation | Explicit dedicated layers | Folded into Application layer |
| Physical & Data Link | Separate layers | Bundled as "Network Access" / "Link" |
| Suitability for description | Excellent for teaching layered concepts | Excellent for describing live Internet stack |
| Rigidity | Highly rigid; strict layer boundaries | Loose boundaries (e.g., TCP may touch IP directly) |
| OSI Layer 5, 6, 7 collectively map to | TCP/IP Application Layer | (Reversed) |
| Reliability of design | Over-engineered for the era | Battle-tested in ARPANET/Internet |
| Replacement Status | Never fully implemented | Still in use (IPv4/IPv6) |

### 2.3 Twisted Pair ŌĆö Deep Specifications

**Twisting Mechanics:**
- Each twist length is precisely calibrated. A common pitch is **1 to 1.5 twists per inch** for Cat5e.
- Adjacent pairs have **different twist rates** so that crosstalk does not align.

**Shielding Variants (per ISO/IEC 11801):**
- **U/UTP** ŌĆö Unshielded pair, unshielded cable (common Cat5e).
- **F/UTP** ŌĆö Foil shield around the bundle (Cat6 often labeled "F/UTP").
- **S/FTP** ŌĆö Each pair shielded + overall braid (Cat7, Cat8 for data centers).
- **SF/UTP** ŌĆö Overall foil + braid, unshielded pairs.

**Twisted Pair Categories (Critical for KTU):**

| **Category** | **Max Frequency** | **Max Data Rate** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cat 3** | 16 MHz | 10 Mbps | Legacy voice, 10BASE-T |
| **Cat 5** | 100 MHz | 100 Mbps | 100BASE-TX |
| **Cat 5e** | 100 MHz | **1 Gbps** | 1000BASE-T (most home LANs) |
| **Cat 6** | 250 MHz | 1ŌĆō10 Gbps | 10GBASE-T up to 55 m |
| **Cat 6a** | 500 MHz | 10 Gbps | 10GBASE-T up to 100 m |
| **Cat 7** | 600 MHz | 10 Gbps | S/FTP shielding, 100 m |
| **Cat 8** | 2000 MHz (2 GHz) | 25/40 Gbps | Data center switch-to-server |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Distinguish **bandwidth (Hz)** from **data rate (bps)**. Category 6 cable has 250 MHz of analog bandwidth, which supports up to 10 Gbps using advanced encoding (PAM-16 with 64b/66b block coding, NRZ/PAM-2 on lower categories). A higher **signal-to-noise ratio** is what converts Hz into bps.

**Advantages of Twisted Pair:**
- Cheapest guided media
- Easy to install and terminate (RJ-45)
- Reasonable bandwidth for LAN

**Disadvantages of Twisted Pair:**
- **Attenuation**: Signal weakens over distance; max ~100 m for Ethernet
- **Crosstalk** (NEXT, FEXT) and **EMI** susceptibility
- Limited bandwidth compared to fiber

### 2.4 Fiber Optics ŌĆö Deep Specifications

**Anatomy of a Fiber Optic Cable:**

1. **Core** (glass, 8ŌĆō62.5 ┬Ąm) ŌĆö Light carrier
2. **Cladding** (different refractive index glass, ~125 ┬Ąm outer) ŌĆö Causes total internal reflection
3. **Buffer Coating** (acrylic) ŌĆö Mechanical protection
4. **Strength Members** (Kevlar) ŌĆö Tensile strength
5. **Jacket** (PE/PVC) ŌĆö Environmental shielding

**Total Internal Reflection Condition:**
A light ray inside the higher-index core hitting the boundary with the lower-index cladding is totally reflected back into the core if the angle of incidence exceeds the **critical angle ╬Ėc**.

**Numerical Aperture (NA):** A measure of the light-gathering ability of a fiber. Higher NA ŌćÆ more light coupled into the core ŌćÆ more dispersion in multi-mode fiber.

### 2.5 Bandwidth ŌĆö Theoretical & Practical Limits

> [!NOTE]
> Bandwidth has two distinct meanings in networks:
> 1. **Analog Bandwidth (Hz)** ŌĆö The range of frequencies a channel can carry, measured in Hertz. For a channel passing 0 to $B$ Hz, the bandwidth is $B$ Hz.
> 2. **Digital Bandwidth (bps)** ŌĆö The maximum bit rate of a digital channel, measured in bits per second. This is the meaning used throughout computer networks (and in the KTU syllabus).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Law** | **Equation** | **Variables / Units** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel)** | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = signal levels | Max data rate in a noiseless channel |
| 2 | **Shannon Capacity (Noisy Channel)** | $C = B \log_2 (1 + S/N)$ | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (unitless) | Max data rate in a noisy channel |
| 3 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB** | $(S/N)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} (S/N)$ | $S/N$ = linear power ratio | Converting SNR between linear and dB |
| 4 | **Attenuation in dB** | $\alpha_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} (P_{in}/P_{out})$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}$ in Watts | Signal loss over a cable |
| 5 | **Attenuation per km** | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10} (P_{in}/P_{out})$ | $L$ = length in km | Loss budget for fiber runs |
| 6 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = B \times d_{prop}$ | $B$ = bandwidth (bps), $d_{prop}$ = one-way delay (s) | Amount of data "in-flight" on a link |
| 7 | **Propagation Delay** | $d_{prop} = \dfrac{D}{v}$ | $D$ = distance (m), $v$ = propagation speed (m/s) | Time for one bit to traverse medium |
| 8 | **Transmission Delay** | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = rate (bps) | Time to push all bits onto the wire |
| 9 | **Total Latency** | $d_{total} = d_{prop} + d_{trans} + d_{queue} + d_{proc}$ | All in seconds | End-to-end delay budget |
| 10 | **Throughput Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Data Rate}}{\text{Channel Bandwidth}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ | Real-world useful capacity |
| 11 | **Refractive Index** | $n = c / v$ | $c$ = speed of light, $v$ = speed in medium | Computing propagation speed in fiber |
| 12 | **Critical Angle (TIR)** | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$ | $n_1$ = core index, $n_2$ = cladding index | TIR condition in fiber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall ŌĆö KTU Exam**:
> Do not confuse $C = 2B \log_2 M$ (Nyquist) with $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ (Shannon).
> - **Nyquist** assumes a **noiseless** channel; the limit comes from bandwidth and signal levels.
> - **Shannon** assumes a **noisy** channel; the limit comes from bandwidth and SNR.
> - The **true** channel capacity is $\min(\text{Nyquist}, \text{Shannon})$. The Shannon limit is the **absolute ceiling** that no real system can exceed, regardless of clever encoding.
