# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Data Link Layer. List any four design issues of the Data Link Layer.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> The Data Link Layer is **Layer 2 of the OSI Reference Model**. It transforms the raw, error-prone bit stream provided by the Physical Layer into a reliable stream of well-defined units called **frames**, and transfers them between two adjacent nodes. It also handles error detection/correction and flow regulation.

> **Four Design Issues (1.5 Marks ŌĆö 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Services to Network Layer** ŌĆö Unacknowledged connectionless, acknowledged connectionless, acknowledged connection-oriented.
> 2. **Framing** ŌĆö Partitioning the bit stream into identifiable units using counts, flags, bit stuffing, or coding violations.
> 3. **Error Control** ŌĆö Detection using parity, checksum, or CRC; correction using Hamming codes or ARQ retransmissions.
> 4. **Flow Control** ŌĆö Regulating the sender's frame rate to match the receiver's processing capacity (Stop-and-Wait or Sliding Window).

---

### Question 2: What is bit stuffing? Why is it needed in the Data Link Layer?
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> Bit stuffing (also called **transparency encoding**) is a framing technique in which the sender inserts an extra **0 bit** after every sequence of **five consecutive 1 bits** in the data payload. The corresponding receiver automatically removes any 0 that follows five consecutive 1s, thereby restoring the original bit stream.

> **Need (2 Marks):**
> 1. It **prevents the flag pattern** (typically `01111110`) from accidentally appearing inside the data payload, which would otherwise cause the receiver to **mistakenly interpret** the middle of the data as a frame boundary.
> 2. It ensures **frame synchronization** is preserved even if the data contains any arbitrary bit pattern, providing **full transparency** for the upper layers.
> 3. It is used in **HDLC, USB, SDLC, and CAN bus** protocols, making it the most widely adopted framing mechanism in industry.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A: Design Issues and Framing

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the three types of services that the Data Link Layer can provide to the Network Layer. Give one example protocol for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Unacknowledged Connectionless Service (2.5 Marks):**
- The sender transmits independent frames **without establishing any connection** and **without expecting acknowledgments**.
- If a frame is lost due to a channel error, **no recovery is attempted** at the DLL; the responsibility falls on the upper layers.
- It is suitable for **low-error, high-speed channels** where retransmission overhead outweighs the cost of occasional frame loss.
- **Example:** **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** ŌĆö used in LANs where the bit error rate is very low ($\sim 10^{-9}$).

**2. Acknowledged Connectionless Service (2.5 Marks):**
- Each frame transmitted is **individually acknowledged** by the receiver, but **no persistent connection state** is maintained between successive frames.
- Lost frames are **detected (via timeout or duplicate ACK)** and **retransmitted** using ARQ logic.
- It is suitable for **unreliable channels** like wireless, where the BER is non-negligible.
- **Example:** **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** ŌĆö uses Stop-and-Wait or Selective Repeat ARQ at the MAC sublayer.

**3. Acknowledged Connection-Oriented Service (2 Marks):**
- A **three-phase logical connection** is established before any data transfer, every frame is numbered and acknowledged, and the connection is **formally torn down** at the end.
- Guarantees **in-order, error-free, duplicate-free** delivery of all frames.
- **Example:** **HDLC** with its SABM/UA/SABME handshake.

---

**(b) Explain the bit stuffing framing technique with a suitable example. How does the receiver destuff the frame? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
Bit stuffing is a transparency mechanism where the sender inserts an extra `0` bit **after every sequence of five consecutive `1` bits** in the data payload, and the corresponding receiver automatically removes any such inserted `0` bit to recover the original data.

**Flag Pattern (1 Mark):**
The HDLC flag used at both ends of the frame is `01111110` (six consecutive 1s). Since the rule ensures there are **never six consecutive 1s** in the payload, the flag pattern cannot appear within the data.

**Sender-Side Bit Stuffing Example (2 Marks):**

Original payload (un-stuffed):
```
0 1 1 1 1 1 1 0 0 1 0
```

After stuffing (insert 0 after every five 1s):
```
0 1 1 1 1 1 0 1 0 0 1 0
       ^   ^
    Five 1s ŌåÆ insert 0
```

**Receiver-Side Bit Destuffing Algorithm (2 Marks):**
1. Scan the incoming bit stream from left to right.
2. Count the number of consecutive `1` bits seen so far.
3. **If the count reaches 5**, examine the next bit:
   - If it is `0` ŌåÆ **remove it** (it is a stuffed bit), reset the counter to 0.
   - If it is `1` ŌåÆ the flag pattern `01111110` has been encountered ŌåÆ **end of frame**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Structure:** Award **2 marks** for stating the rule, **2 marks** for the worked example, **2 marks** for the destuffing algorithm, and **1 mark** for identifying that flag pattern is six 1s.

---

### Question B: Error Control and Flow Control

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol. A satellite link has a one-way propagation delay of $t_{prop} = 270$ ms and a frame transmission time of $t_{frame} = 30$ ms. Compute the link utilization and the minimum sliding window size for full utilization. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stop-and-Wait ARQ Explanation (3 Marks):**
- The sender transmits **one frame** and then **stops**, waiting for an acknowledgment (ACK) from the receiver.
- The receiver checks the frame for errors using CRC. If correct, it sends an ACK; otherwise, it discards the frame (or sends a NACK).
- The sender starts a **timer** when transmitting. If the timer expires before an ACK arrives, the sender **retransmits the same frame**.
- **Limitation:** Highly inefficient on long-delay, high-bandwidth links (long-fat pipes).

**Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

Step 1: Compute the parameter $a$:
$$
a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{270 \text{ ms}}{30 \text{ ms}} = 9
$$

Step 2: Compute Stop-and-Wait utilization:
$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 18} = \frac{1}{19} \approx 0.0526
$$

Convert to percentage:
$$
U \approx 5.26\%
$$

Step 3: Compute minimum sliding window size:
$$
W \geq 2a + 1 = 2(9) + 1 = 19
$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum window of **$W = 19$ frames** is needed for full link utilization.

---

**(b) Compare Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ in terms of retransmission strategy, receiver complexity, and minimum window size. Under what condition is Selective Repeat preferred? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks ŌĆö 1.25 each row):**

| Feature | Go-Back-N ARQ | Selective Repeat ARQ |
|---|---|---|
| **Retransmission on Error** | Retransmits **all frames from the damaged frame** onwards (even those received correctly). | Retransmits **only the damaged frame**. Correctly received out-of-order frames are buffered. |
| **Receiver Complexity** | **Low** ŌĆö receiver discards all out-of-order frames; only needs to track the next expected sequence number. | **High** ŌĆö receiver must **buffer out-of-order frames** and maintain complex state per slot in the window. |
| **Minimum Window Size** | $W \geq 2a + 1$ to avoid sequence number ambiguity. | $W \geq 2a$ (approximately equal to $2^{k-1}$ where $k$ is the sequence number bit length). |
| **Link Utilization** | Lower on error-prone links (wastes bandwidth by resending good frames). | Higher on error-prone links (minimal retransmission overhead). |

**When Selective Repeat is Preferred (2 Marks):**
Selective Repeat is preferred when the **bit error rate (BER) of the channel is high** and the **propagation delay is large** (i.e., the product $a \cdot P_e$ is non-negligible). In such environments, the wasted bandwidth of Go-Back-N's bulk retransmission becomes a major performance bottleneck. Example: **geostationary satellite channels** with high error rates due to atmospheric noise, where Selective Repeat recovers efficiently by retransmitting only the few corrupted frames.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the three service types** with the three ARQ protocols. They are *not* the same. Services are about **what is offered to the Network Layer**, while ARQ is a **mechanism to implement** acknowledged services. Examiners deduct 2 marks if you mix them up.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the "2" in $U = \frac{1}{1+2a}$**: The factor of 2 accounts for **two propagation delays** (frame forward + ACK return). Using $1+a$ loses full marks.
> 
> 3. **Stating "ARQ provides error correction"** ŌĆö this is technically imprecise. ARQ provides **retransmission-based recovery**; true error correction is done by **FEC (Forward Error Correction)** like Hamming or Reed-Solomon codes. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 
> 4. **Drawing the bit stuffing example incorrectly** ŌĆö a frequent error is stuffing after *four* 1s instead of *five*. The rule is "**five 1s followed by a 0**". Get this wrong, and you lose the entire 2-mark example portion.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the degree of the CRC polynomial in error analysis** ŌĆö when explaining what CRC can detect, always mention "**all single-bit errors, all double-bit errors (if $G(x)$ has $\geq 3$ terms), and all burst errors of length $\leq r$**". Vague statements lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Data Link Layer Design Issues:**

- Ō£ģ The Data Link Layer is **Layer 2** of the **OSI model**; it sits between the **Physical Layer** (Layer 1) and the **Network Layer** (Layer 3).
- Ō£ģ The **four design issues** are: **Services to Network Layer, Framing, Error Control, and Flow Control** ŌĆö state this enumeration first in every answer.
- Ō£ģ The **three DLL services** are: **Unacknowledged Connectionless (Ethernet), Acknowledged Connectionless (Wi-Fi), and Acknowledged Connection-Oriented (HDLC)**.
- Ō£ģ The **four framing methods** are: **Character Count, Byte Stuffing, Bit Stuffing, and Physical Layer Coding Violations**. Bit stuffing is the most widely used.
- Ō£ģ The **bit stuffing rule**: Sender inserts `0` after **five consecutive 1s**; receiver removes any `0` that follows five `1s`.
- Ō£ģ The **flag pattern** for bit stuffing is `01111110` (six 1s).
- Ō£ģ The **three error detection methods** are: **Parity (1 bit), Checksum (16/32 bits), and CRC (polynomial-based, most powerful)**.
- Ō£ģ **Hamming code inequality**: $2^r \geq m + r + 1$. For $m = 7$, you need $r = 4$ redundant bits.
- Ō£ģ The **three ARQ protocols** are: **Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, and Selective Repeat**.
- Ō£ģ **Stop-and-Wait utilization**: $U = \frac{1}{1+2a}$ where $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}}$.
- Ō£ģ **Sliding window size for full utilization**: $W \geq 2a+1$ (for Go-Back-N) and $W \geq 2a$ (for Selective Repeat).
- Ō£ģ **Flow control** is **receiver-driven** (the receiver dictates the sender's rate), while **error control** is **channel-condition-driven** (errors trigger retransmission).
- Ō£ģ The DLL **does not** guarantee end-to-end reliability ŌĆö that is the job of the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**. DLL only provides **hop-by-hop** reliability between adjacent nodes.
- Ō£ģ **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit) is typically **1500 bytes** for Ethernet.
- Ō£ģ **CRC of degree $r$** detects **all burst errors up to length $r$** and approximately **$1 - 2^{-r}$** of longer bursts.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Four Design Issues:** **"S-F-E-F"** ŌåÆ **S**ervices, **F**raming, **E**rror Control, **F**low Control. Use this acronym in your answer introductions to ensure examiners immediately recognize you have covered the full syllabus scope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Detection and Correction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Detection and Correction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Error Detection and Correction** refers to the set of mathematical and algorithmic techniques used in digital communication systems to identify and, where possible, repair bit-level corruptions that occur during data transmission over physical media. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET528 - Computer Network Systems), error control mechanisms form the **Data Link Layer's** primary responsibility, ensuring the integrity of frames exchanged between adjacent nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Error control is the *third* sub-layer responsibility of the Data Link Layer. It works *in conjunction* with flow control and is implemented using redundancy bits appended to the original data unit.

An **error** in a transmitted bit stream is defined as a **bit flip** ŌĆö a logical `0` received where a logical `1` was sent (or vice versa), caused by **thermal noise, impulse noise, crosstalk, attenuation, or electromagnetic interference (EMI)**.

### 1.2 Types of Errors

| Error Type | Definition | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Bit Error** | Only **1 bit** in a given data unit is corrupted | Very low SNR, isolated noise spike |
| **Burst Error** | **2 or more consecutive bits** are corrupted | Impulse noise, lightning, surge |
| **Multiple-Bit Error** | Multiple non-consecutive bits flipped | Severe interference (rare in practice) |

> [!NOTE]
> A burst error of length *B* does **not** necessarily mean *B* bits are wrong. It means the error span (from first to last corrupted bit) is *B* bits long. A burst of length 8 could have only 3 bits actually flipped.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Courier Tracking Code" ­¤Åż

Imagine you are sending a **cheque by courier** from Kottayam to Thiruvananthapuram.

- **The Cheque** ŌåÆ Your original data (e.g., a frame of bits)
- **The Courier Network** ŌåÆ The noisy physical channel (cables, wireless spectrum)
- **Damage in Transit** ŌåÆ Bit errors (water, tearing, smudging)
- **A Handwritten Checksum on the Envelope** ŌåÆ Redundancy bits added by the sender
- **The Receiver Verifying the Checksum** ŌåÆ Error detection at the receiver

If the receiver finds the envelope tampered with, they have two choices:
1. **Detect and Request Retransmission (ARQ)** ŌĆö Call the sender: *"Please send a new cheque!"*
2. **Detect and Correct on the Spot (FEC)** ŌĆö Use mathematical magic to *guess* and fix the damaged digits.

This is the fundamental trade-off in error control: **reliability vs. efficiency**.

### 1.4 Two Broad Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**

**A) Error Detection + Retransmission (ARQ ŌĆö Automatic Repeat reQuest)**
- Receiver detects an error and **requests the sender to retransmit** the corrupted frame.
- Used in: Stop-and-Wait ARQ, Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ.
- Higher efficiency when error rate is **low**.

**B) Forward Error Correction (FEC)**
- Receiver **detects AND corrects** the error without any retransmission.
- Used in: Real-time systems (voice, video streaming, deep-space communication).
- More efficient when error rate is **high** or **round-trip delay is large**.

### 1.5 Redundancy ŌĆö The Heart of Error Control

Every error detection/correction scheme is built on one principle:

$$\text{Transmitted Block} = \text{Original Data} + \text{Redundancy Bits}$$

The redundancy bits carry no new information ŌĆö they exist **only** to give the receiver a way to verify (or recover) the original data. The *amount* and *structure* of redundancy is what distinguishes the various schemes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Error Control Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **four principal schemes** (in increasing order of robustness):

1. **Parity Check** (Simplest)
2. **Checksum**
3. **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)** ŌĆö Most widely used in practice (Ethernet, Wi-Fi)
4. **Hamming Code** ŌĆö Only one in this list that supports **correction** (not just detection)

### 2.2 Parity Check ŌĆö Single-Bit Redundancy

**Concept:** Append a single bit called the **parity bit** to make the total number of 1s either **even** (even parity) or **odd** (odd parity).

- Sender computes parity bit $p$ such that:
$$p = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \oplus \dots \oplus d_n$$

  where $d_i$ are the data bits. This is the **XOR** of all data bits.
- Receiver recomputes parity on the received codeword. If it doesn't match the expected parity, an error is flagged.

**Limitations:**
- Detects **all single-bit errors** and any **odd number of bit errors**.
- **Fails** to detect any **even number of bit errors** (the parity remains unchanged).
- **Cannot correct** errors ŌĆö only detects.
- Probability of undetected error in random noise: $\approx 1/2$.

**Variants:** *2-Dimensional Parity* arranges bits in a matrix and adds both a row-parity and a column-parity bit, providing **single-bit error correction** capability.

### 2.3 Checksum ŌĆö Block Parity Summation

**Concept:** Break the data unit (say $k$ bits) into $n$-bit segments and sum them using **1's complement arithmetic**. The negated sum (the *checksum*) is appended to the data.

**Algorithm (Sender):**
1. Divide data into $k$-bit blocks (typically $k = 16$ for TCP/IP).
2. Initialize checksum = 0.
3. Add each block using 1's complement addition (wrap-around carry).
4. Take 1's complement of the final sum ŌåÆ this is the checksum.
5. Append checksum to the data and transmit.

**Algorithm (Receiver):**
1. Receive data + checksum.
2. Sum all blocks (including the checksum) using 1's complement addition.
3. If result = `1111...1` (all 1s), **no error**.
4. Otherwise, **error detected**.

**Advantages:** Simple, low hardware cost, used in TCP, UDP, IPv4, IPv6 headers.
**Disadvantage:** Vulnerable to certain burst errors where bits cancel out symmetrically.

### 2.4 Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) ŌĆö Polynomial Division

**Concept:** Treats bit patterns as **polynomials with coefficients in GF(2)** (the binary Galois Field). A generator polynomial $G(x)$ of degree $r$ is agreed upon by sender and receiver. The sender appends an $r$-bit **CRC remainder** to the message such that the entire transmitted codeword is **exactly divisible** by $G(x)$.

**The Math Behind CRC:**

Let:
- $M(x)$ = Message polynomial of degree $m$
- $G(x)$ = Generator polynomial of degree $r$ (predefined, e.g., CRC-32, CRC-CCITT)
- $T(x)$ = Transmitted codeword polynomial
- $R(x)$ = CRC remainder polynomial of degree $< r$

The construction rule is:

$$T(x) = x^r \cdot M(x) + R(x)$$

where $R(x)$ is the remainder when $x^r \cdot M(x)$ is divided by $G(x)$ in **GF(2)** arithmetic:

$$x^r \cdot M(x) = Q(x) \cdot G(x) + R(x)$$

The receiver computes $T(x) \mod G(x)$. If the result is 0, no error; otherwise, error detected.

**Key Properties of CRC:**
- Detection of all **single-bit errors** Ō£ō
- Detection of all **double-bit errors** Ō£ō (if $G(x)$ has at least 3 terms)
- Detection of all **burst errors of length $\leq r$** Ō£ō
- Detection of burst errors of length $> r$ with probability $1 - 2^{-r}$

**Standard Generator Polynomials:**

| Standard | Generator Polynomial $G(x)$ | Hex Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| CRC-8 | $x^8 + x^2 + x + 1$ | 0x07 | ATM HEC |
| CRC-10 | $x^{10} + x^9 + x^5 + x^4 + x^2 + 1$ | 0x233 | ATM AAL |
| CRC-16-CCITT | $x^{16} + x^{12} + x^5 + 1$ | 0x1021 | HDLC, Bluetooth |
| CRC-32 | $x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + x^{22} + x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{11} + x^{10} + x^8 + x^7 + x^5 + x^4 + x^2 + x + 1$ | 0x04C11DB7 | Ethernet, ZIP, PNG |

### 2.5 Hamming Code ŌĆö Error Detection *and* Correction

**Concept:** A family of **linear error-correcting codes** invented by Richard Hamming (1950). The Hamming(7,4) code encodes **4 data bits** into a **7-bit codeword** by inserting 3 parity bits in positions that are **powers of 2** (1, 2, 4).

**Generalized Hamming Rule:**

For a codeword of $n$ bits carrying $m$ data bits, the number of redundancy bits $r$ must satisfy:

$$2^r \geq m + r + 1$$

This guarantees that every single-bit error (and zero errors) maps to a **unique non-zero syndrome**.

| $m$ (data bits) | Min $r$ (redundancy) | $n = m+r$ (codeword) | Code Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 3 | 7 | Hamming(7,4) |
| 8 | 4 | 12 | Hamming(12,8) |
| 11 | 4 | 15 | Hamming(15,11) |
| 16 | 5 | 21 | Hamming(21,16) |
| 26 | 5 | 31 | Hamming(31,26) |
| 57 | 6 | 63 | Hamming(63,57) |

**Encoding Rule (Positions 1 to $n$):**
- Position 1: Parity for positions with LSB = 1 (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, ...)
- Position 2: Parity for positions with 2nd bit = 1 (2, 3, 6, 7, 10, 11, ...)
- Position 4: Parity for positions with 3rd bit = 1 (4, 5, 6, 7, 12, 13, 14, 15, ...)
- Position $2^k$: Parity for all positions whose binary representation has a 1 in bit $k$.

**Decoding Rule:**
- Receiver computes each parity check.
- A non-zero parity check contributes a `1` to a **syndrome** word.
- The syndrome is the **binary position number** of the erroneous bit (or 0 if no error).
- Flip the bit at the syndrome position ŌåÆ corrected codeword.

**Distance Properties:**
- Hamming distance between any two valid codewords is $\geq 3$.
- Can detect up to **2-bit errors** and correct up to **1-bit error**.

### 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet** ŌĆö Memorize these for Part A and Part B.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum redundancy bits $r$ | $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ | For single-bit error correction |
| Hamming distance | $d_{min} = \min$ pairwise XOR weight | All Hamming codes: $d_{min} = 3$ |
| Detection capability | Detect up to $d_{min} - 1$ errors | $d_{min} = 3 \Rightarrow$ detect 2 errors |
| Correction capability | Correct up to $\lfloor (d_{min}-1)/2 \rfloor$ errors | $d_{min} = 3 \Rightarrow$ correct 1 error |
| Single parity odd case | $p = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus \dots \oplus d_n$ | Odd parity: $p$ makes total 1s odd |
| CRC transmission | $T(x) = x^r M(x) + (x^r M(x) \mod G(x))$ | Sender appends remainder |
| CRC reception | $T'(x) \mod G(x) = 0 \Rightarrow$ no error | Any non-zero remainder ŌćÆ error |
| Burst error detection | All bursts of length $\leq r$ detected | CRC with $r$-bit generator |
| Code rate (efficiency) | $R = m / n$ | Higher $R$ ŌćÆ less redundancy |
| Parity bit position | $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \dots, 2^{r-1}$ | Powers of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, ...) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CRC-32** is the error detection backbone of **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **ZIP archives**, **PNG images**, and **SATA disks**.
- **Hamming(72,64)** ŌĆö known as **SECDED** (Single Error Correct, Double Error Detect) ŌĆö is used in **ECC RAM** in servers.
- **Reed-Solomon codes** (a generalization of Hamming) protect **CDs, DVDs, Blu-ray discs, QR codes, and DVB-T** digital TV.
- **Turbo codes and LDPC codes** (covered in advanced 5G modules) push FEC efficiency close to the **Shannon limit**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Single Parity Check (Even Parity)

**Problem:** Original data = `1001011`. Add an even parity bit.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Count the number of 1s in the data.**

The data is: `1 0 0 1 0 1 1`. Counting: there are **four** 1s.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decide the parity bit.**

For **even parity**, total number of 1s (data + parity) must be even. Since data already has 4 (even) ones, set $p = 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the transmitted codeword.**

$$\text{Codeword} = 1\,0\,0\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0$$

**Receiver-side check:** Recompute parity. If even parity holds, accept; else flag error.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Internet Checksum (16-bit)

**Problem:** Sender has three 16-bit words: `0110011001100000`, `0101010101010101`, `1000111100001100`. Compute the Internet checksum.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sum the first two words using 1's complement addition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
  &\ 0110011001100000 \\
+ &\ 0101010101010101 \\
\hline
  &\ 1011101110110101
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: `1011101110110101` (no carry-out, so no wrap).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Add the third word.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
  &\ 1011101110110101 \\
+ &\ 1000111100001100 \\
\hline
  &\ 1\,0100101011000001
\end{aligned}
$$

A **carry-out** of `1` is generated from bit 16. Apply **end-around carry** (add it back to the LSB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
  &\ 0100101011000001 \\
+ &\ 0000000000000001 \\
\hline
  &\ 0100101011000010
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take 1's complement to get the checksum.**

$$\text{Checksum} = \sim0100101011000010 = 1011010100111101$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The sender transmits** the three data words + this checksum.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Receiver adds all four words (three data + checksum) using 1's complement:**

The four-word sum should be `1111111111111111` (= 0 in 1's complement). If not, an error occurred.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö CRC Computation (Modulo-2 Division)

**Problem:** Given $M(x) = x^7 + x^5 + x^3 + x + 1$ and $G(x) = x^4 + x^3 + 1$. Compute the CRC for this message.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert polynomials to bit strings.**

- $M(x) = x^7 + x^5 + x^3 + x + 1 \rightarrow M = 10101011$ (degree 7)
- $G(x) = x^4 + x^3 + 1 \rightarrow G = 11001$ (degree 4, so $r = 4$)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Append 4 zeros to $M$ (multiply by $x^4$).**

$$M' = 10101011\,0000$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Perform modulo-2 division of $M'$ by $G$ (XOR-based long division).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\ \ \ 11111010 \\
G=11001\,) \overline{101010110000} \\
&\ \ \ \ \ 11001 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ 11000\,110000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ 11001 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ 00001\,10000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 00000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ 11000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ 11001 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 00010 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 00000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 10000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 11001 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 1001 \quad (\text{final remainder})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The remainder is `1001` (4 bits long, matching the degree of $G$).**

**Step 5 ŌĆö Construct transmitted codeword $T$:**

$$T = M + R = 10101011\,1001$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verification at receiver:** Receiver divides `101010111001` by `11001`. The remainder is **0** ŌćÆ no error detected.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Error simulation:** Suppose bit position 5 gets flipped during transmission, so receiver gets `101000111001`. Dividing by `11001` gives a non-zero remainder ŌćÆ error detected.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 ŌĆö Hamming(7,4) Encoding

**Problem:** Encode the 4-bit data `D = 1\,0\,1\,1$ using even-parity Hamming(7,4). The 4 data bits occupy positions 3, 5, 6, 7 (non-power-of-2 positions).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Allocate positions.**

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Role | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $D_1$ | $P_3$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
| Value | ? | ? | 1 | ? | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute parity bit $P_1$ (covers positions with LSB = 1: 1, 3, 5, 7).**

$$P_1 = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_4 = 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute parity bit $P_2$ (covers positions with 2nd bit = 1: 2, 3, 6, 7).**

$$P_2 = D_1 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 = 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute parity bit $P_3$ (covers positions with 3rd bit = 1: 4, 5, 6, 7).**

$$P_3 = D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final codeword.**

$$\text{Codeword} = P_1 P_2 D_1 P_3 D_2 D_3 D_4 = 0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,1$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 ŌĆö Hamming(7,4) Decoding with Error Correction

**Problem:** Suppose bit position 6 of the codeword above gets flipped in transit. The receiver gets: `0 1 1 0 0 0 1`.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Receiver recomputes all three parity checks.**

- $C_1$ (positions 1,3,5,7): $0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0$ ŌåÆ **Expected = 0, Got = 0 ŌćÆ OK**
- $C_2$ (positions 2,3,6,7): $1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ ŌåÆ **Expected = 0, Got = 1 ŌćÆ FAIL**
- $C_3$ (positions 4,5,6,7): $0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ ŌåÆ **Expected = 0, Got = 1 ŌćÆ FAIL**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the syndrome word $(C_3 C_2 C_1)_2$.**

$$\text{Syndrome} = (1\,1\,0)_2 = 6_{10}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The syndrome value is exactly the position of the corrupted bit (6). Flip position 6.**

$$\text{Corrected} = 0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,\mathbf{1}\,1$$

This matches the originally transmitted codeword. The error is **corrected**.

### 3.6 Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right Scheme

| Criterion | Parity | Checksum | CRC | Hamming |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redundancy per block | 1 bit | 16 or 32 bits | $r$ bits (16/32) | $r$ bits (logarithmic in $m$) |
| Detects single-bit errors | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |
| Detects burst errors | ŌØī | Partial | Ō£ģ (up to $r$ bits) | Partial |
| Corrects errors | ŌØī | ŌØī | ŌØī | Ō£ģ (1-bit) |
| Computational cost | Trivial | Low | Medium (XOR shifts) | Medium |
| Hardware implementation | 1 XOR gate | Adder | Linear Feedback Shift Register | XOR + syndrome logic |
| Typical KTU application | Serial comms | TCP/UDP/IP | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | ECC RAM |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Error Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Sender Application] --> dataA[Data Block M]
    dataA --> encA[Encoder: adds redundancy R]
    encA --> codeA[Codeword T = M + R]
    codeA --> chanA[Noisy Channel]
    chanA -. Bit errors .-> recvA[Received Codeword T']
    recvA --> decA[Decoder: checks redundancy]
    decA --> chkA{Syndrome zero?}
    chkA -- Yes --> okA[Accept M]
    chkA -- No --> errA[Error Detected]
    errA --> modeA{Strategy}
    modeA -- ARQ --> arqA[Request Retransmission]
    modeA -- FEC --> fecA[Apply Correction Algorithm]
    fecA --> okA
    arqA -.-> srcA
```

### 4.2 CRC Encoding Pipeline (LFSR-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stA[Start: Shift in M appending r zeros] --> stB[Bit enters LFSR]
    stB --> stC{XOR with generator taps?}
    stC -- Yes --> stD[Flip feedback bits]
    stC -- No --> stE[Shift right]
    stD --> stE
    stE --> stF{All r+k bits processed?}
    stF -- No --> stB
    stF -- Yes --> stG[LFSR holds remainder R]
    stG --> stH[Append R to M = T]
    stH --> stI[Transmit T]
```

### 4.3 Hamming Code Encoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hA[Input: m data bits] --> hB[Place data in non-power-of-2 positions]
    hB --> hC[Identify parity bit positions: 1, 2, 4, 8 ...]
    hC --> hD[For each parity position, list covered data positions]
    hD --> hE[Compute parity = XOR of covered data bits]
    hE --> hF[Insert parity bits into power-of-2 positions]
    hF --> hG[Output: 7-bit Hamming codeword]
    hG --> hH[Transmit over channel]
    hH --> hI[Receiver recomputes syndrome S]
    hI --> hJ{S equals 0?}
    hJ -- Yes --> hK[No error: accept codeword]
    hJ -- No --> hL[S points to corrupted bit position]
    hL --> hM[Flip bit at position S]
    hM --> hN[Corrected codeword accepted]
```

### 4.4 ARQ vs FEC Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dA{Application type?} -- Real-time voice/video --> dB[Use FEC]
    dA -- File transfer, web --> dC[Use ARQ]
    dA -- Deep space / Satellite --> dD[Use FEC long block codes]
    dB --> dE[Hamming / Reed-Solomon / LDPC]
    dC --> dF[Stop-and-Wait / Go-Back-N / Selective Repeat]
    dF --> dG{Channel error rate?}
    dG -- Low --> dH[Stop-and-Wait ARQ]
    dG -- Medium --> dI[Go-Back-N ARQ]
    dG -- High --> dJ[Selective Repeat ARQ]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between single-bit error and burst error. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **single-bit error** is a data transmission error in which only one bit of a given data unit (such as a byte, character, or packet) is changed from 1 to 0 or from 0 to 1. It is the simplest form of error and typically occurs in channels with low noise levels.
*Example:* A bit `1` transmitted as `0` in the character `10010110` ŌåÆ received as `00010110`.

A **burst error** is a data transmission error in which two or more consecutive bits in a data unit are corrupted. The length of the burst is measured from the first corrupted bit to the last corrupted bit. It typically occurs due to impulse noise such as lightning or electrical surges.
*Example:* A burst of length 8 in `10010110` could result in `10111100` (multiple consecutive bits flipped).

| Parameter | Single-Bit Error | Burst Error |
|---|---|---|
| Number of bits affected | Exactly 1 | 2 or more |
| Consecutiveness | N/A | Must be consecutive |
| Typical cause | Low SNR, random noise | Impulse noise, lightning |
| Detectability | Easy (parity suffices) | Requires CRC |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition ├Ś 2 (1.5 marks) + Example ├Ś 2 (1 mark) + Comparison note (0.5 marks).

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is CRC preferred over simple parity for error detection in modern networks?**

**Model Answer:**

CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) is preferred over simple parity in modern networks for the following reasons:

1. **Better Burst Error Detection:** Parity fails to detect any even number of bit errors. CRC detects all burst errors up to length $r$ (where $r$ is the degree of the generator polynomial) and detects longer bursts with probability $1 - 2^{-r}$.
2. **Stronger Mathematical Foundation:** CRC is based on polynomial division in GF(2), giving it a much higher probability of detection (e.g., CRC-32 has an undetected error probability of $\approx 2^{-32}$).
3. **Scalability:** The same algorithm handles arbitrary message lengths. Parity requires recomputation across the whole block.
4. **Hardware Efficiency:** CRC can be computed using a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) running at wire speed ŌĆö essential for Gigabit Ethernet.
5. **Standardization:** Industry-standard polynomials (CRC-32 for Ethernet, CRC-CCITT for HDLC) are universally adopted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *detection* capability with the *correction* capability. CRC **cannot** correct errors ŌĆö it can only *detect* them. Correction requires retransmission (ARQ) or a forward-correcting code like Hamming.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]`
**(a)** Explain the CRC error detection algorithm with a suitable example. Compute the CRC codeword for the message `1101011011` using the generator polynomial $G(x) = x^4 + x + 1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hamming(7,4) code is used for error correction with even parity. Encode the 4-bit data `1010`. If the 6th bit of the transmitted codeword is corrupted, show how the receiver detects and corrects the error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify message and generator polynomials.**

- Message $M = 1101011011$ (length 10, degree 9)
- Generator $G(x) = x^4 + x + 1 \rightarrow G = 10011$ (degree $r = 4$)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Append $r = 4$ zeros to $M$.**

$$M' = 1101011011\,0000$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Perform modulo-2 division of $M'$ by $G = 10011$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\ \ \ 1100001010 \\
G=10011\,) \overline{11010110110000} \\
&\ \ \ \ 10011 \\
&\ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ 01001\,110110000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ 00000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ 10011\,10110000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ 10011 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ 00001\,010000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ 00000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 10011\,00000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 10011 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 00001\,0000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 00000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 10000 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 10011 \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \downarrow \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 0011 \quad (\text{final remainder})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Remainder = `0011`. Append to original $M$ to form codeword.**

$$T = 1101011011\,0011$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Receiver verification:** Dividing $T$ by $G$ yields remainder = 0 ŌćÆ no error.

**Key Points to Mention (for full marks):**
- Modulo-2 arithmetic uses XOR (no carries/borrows).
- The generator $G$ must be agreed upon a priori.
- CRC detects all single-bit and most multi-bit errors.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify data and place it in non-power-of-2 positions.**

Data bits: $D_1 D_2 D_3 D_4 = 1\,0\,1\,0$

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Role | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $D_1$ | $P_3$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
| Value | ? | ? | 1 | ? | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P_1$ (covers 1, 3, 5, 7).**

$$P_1 = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_4 = 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $P_2$ (covers 2, 3, 6, 7).**

$$P_2 = D_1 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $P_3$ (covers 4, 5, 6, 7).**

$$P_3 = D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Transmitted codeword:**

$$T = P_1 P_2 D_1 P_3 D_2 D_3 D_4 = 1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,1\,0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Error injection:** Bit 6 flipped ŌćÆ received codeword = `1 0 1 1 0 0 0`.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Receiver parity recomputation:**

- $C_1$ (1,3,5,7): $1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 0$ ŌåÆ Expected 0, Got 0 ŌćÆ **OK**
- $C_2$ (2,3,6,7): $0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1$ ŌåÆ Expected 0, Got 1 ŌćÆ **FAIL**
- $C_3$ (4,5,6,7): $1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1$ ŌåÆ Expected 0, Got 1 ŌćÆ **FAIL**

**Step 8 ŌĆö Syndrome = $(C_3 C_2 C_1)_2 = (1 1 0)_2 = 6$**.

**Step 9 ŌĆö Flip position 6** ŌćÆ corrected = `1 0 1 1 0 1 0` Ō£ö

**Step 10 ŌĆö Extract data bits** from positions 3, 5, 6, 7 ŌćÆ $1\,0\,1\,0$ (original data recovered).

---

#### **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the two main strategies of error control: ARQ and FEC. Compare them in terms of bandwidth, delay, and complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Hamming code with $m = 4$ data bits, determine the number of redundancy bits required. Encode the data `0110` and demonstrate single-bit error correction if bit position 3 is flipped. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) ŌĆö Block Diagram Concept:**

Sender ŌåÆ Frame with CRC ŌåÆ Channel ŌåÆ Receiver ŌåÆ CRC check ŌåÆ If error: NACK sent back ŌåÆ Sender retransmits.

**FEC (Forward Error Correction) ŌĆö Block Diagram Concept:**

Sender ŌåÆ Data + Redundancy (Hamming/RS code) ŌåÆ Channel ŌåÆ Receiver ŌåÆ Decoder computes syndrome ŌåÆ Corrects directly.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | ARQ | FEC |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth utilization | Variable; can waste BW on retransmits | Constant, predictable |
| Delay | Variable; high for distant links | Fixed decoding delay |
| Round-trip requirement | Yes | No |
| Complexity | Simpler sender; receiver just detects | Complex encoder + decoder |
| Best suited for | Low BER, short links (LAN) | High BER, long-delay links (satellite) |
| Storage at sender | May need to buffer sent frames | Not required |

**Three ARQ Variants (Mention for full marks):**
1. **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** ŌĆö Send 1 frame, wait for ACK. Simple but inefficient.
2. **Go-Back-N ARQ** ŌĆö Sliding window; on error, retransmit N frames back. Medium complexity.
3. **Selective Repeat ARQ** ŌĆö Only the erroneous frame is retransmitted. Highest efficiency, highest complexity.

**Three FEC Schemes (Mention for full marks):**
1. **Hamming codes** ŌĆö single-bit correction.
2. **Reed-Solomon codes** ŌĆö burst error correction (CDs, DVDs).
3. **Convolutional / Turbo / LDPC codes** ŌĆö modern cellular (4G/5G).

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find minimum redundancy bits $r$ using $2^r \geq m + r + 1$.**

With $m = 4$: test $r = 3$: $2^3 = 8 \geq 4 + 3 + 1 = 8$ Ō£ö (just satisfies). So $r = 3$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Codeword length $n = m + r = 4 + 3 = 7$. This is Hamming(7,4).**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Place data $D_1 D_2 D_3 D_4 = 0\,1\,1\,0$.**

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Role | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $D_1$ | $P_3$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
| Value | ? | ? | 0 | ? | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute parity bits (even parity).**

$$P_1 = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_4 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$

$$P_2 = D_1 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$

$$P_3 = D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Transmitted codeword = `1 1 0 0 1 1 0`.**

**Step 6 ŌĆö Error injection:** Bit 3 flipped ŌćÆ received = `1 1 1 0 1 1 0`.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Receiver syndrome computation:**

- $C_1$ (1,3,5,7): $1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$ ŌåÆ Expected 0, Got 1 ŌćÆ **FAIL**
- $C_2$ (2,3,6,7): $1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$ ŌåÆ Expected 0, Got 1 ŌćÆ **FAIL**
- $C_3$ (4,5,6,7): $0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 0$ ŌåÆ Expected 0, Got 0 ŌćÆ **OK**

**Step 8 ŌĆö Syndrome = $(C_3 C_2 C_1)_2 = (0 1 1)_2 = 3$.**

**Step 9 ŌĆö Flip position 3** ŌćÆ corrected = `1 1 0 0 1 1 0` Ō£ö

**Step 10 ŌĆö Recovered data bits (positions 3,5,6,7): `0 1 1 0`** ŌĆö matches original. Ō£ö

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing syndrome order:** In Hamming code, the syndrome bit from $P_1$ (covering LSB) is the **LSB** of the syndrome word. Reversing the order yields a wrong bit position.
> 2. **Modulo-2 division in CRC:** Many students mistakenly use *normal* subtraction. Always use **XOR**. Drawing arrows in long division helps avoid this.
> 3. **Appending zeros count:** The number of zeros appended to $M$ must **equal the degree of $G(x)$**, not the length of $G$.
> 4. **Hamming formula:** Do NOT use $2^r \geq m + r$ ŌĆö the correct inequality is $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ (the `+1` accounts for the all-zero syndrome representing "no error").
> 5. **CRC ŌēĀ Correction:** CRC can only *detect* errors, not correct them. Stating "CRC corrects the error" will cost 1ŌĆō2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before the exam.**

Ō£ģ **Error types:** Single-bit (1 bit), Burst (Ōēź 2 consecutive bits), Multiple (non-consecutive).

Ō£ģ **Error control strategies:** ARQ (detect + retransmit) vs FEC (detect + correct, no retransmit).

Ō£ģ **Parity Check:** 1 bit appended. Detects odd number of bit errors. Cannot correct. Used in UARTs, serial comms.

Ō£ģ **2-D Parity:** Matrix layout. Can correct single-bit error. Used in early disk storage.

Ō£ģ **Checksum:** Sum of $k$-bit segments using 1's complement. Used in TCP, UDP, IPv4, IPv6. 16-bit (IPv4) or 16-bit one's-complement sum.

Ō£ģ **CRC:** Polynomial-based modulo-2 division. Generator degree $r$. Detects all bursts up to $r$ bits. Standard polynomials: CRC-8, CRC-16, CRC-32.

Ō£ģ **CRC transmission rule:** $T(x) = x^r M(x) + R(x)$ where $R(x) = x^r M(x) \mod G(x)$.

Ō£ģ **CRC reception rule:** Remainder = 0 ŌćÆ no error. Non-zero ŌćÆ error detected.

Ō£ģ **Hamming Code:** Provides both detection (2-bit) and correction (1-bit).

Ō£ģ **Hamming formula:** $2^r \geq m + r + 1$.

Ō£ģ **Parity bit positions:** Always at positions 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... (powers of 2).

Ō£ģ **Syndrome = bit position of error:** Computed from parity check failures.

Ō£ģ **Codeword length $n = m + r$.** For Hamming(7,4): $m=4$, $r=3$, $n=7$.

Ō£ģ **Code rate (efficiency):** $R = m/n$. Higher $R$ means less redundancy.

Ō£ģ **Hamming distance** $d_{min} = 3$ for all single-error-correcting Hamming codes.

Ō£ģ **Detection capability** of a code: $d_{min} - 1$ errors.
Ō£ģ **Correction capability** of a code: $\lfloor (d_{min} - 1)/2 \rfloor$ errors.

Ō£ģ **CRC-32** is the workhorse of Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi, ZIP, and PNG.

Ō£ģ **Hamming(72,64) SECDED** is the standard for ECC memory in servers and aerospace.

Ō£ģ **Real-world FEC beyond KTU syllabus:** Reed-Solomon (CDs/DVDs), Turbo & LDPC codes (4G/5G).

Ō£ģ **Golden rule:** "Parity detects, CRC detects strongly, Hamming detects AND corrects."

---

*End of Module 2 ŌĆö Error Detection and Correction Notes*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sliding Window Protocols. Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sliding Window Protocols. Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś MODULE 2: SLIDING WINDOW PROTOCOLS & MAC SUBLAYER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sliding Window Protocols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Sliding Window Protocol):**
> A **Sliding Window Protocol** is a Data Link Layer flow-control mechanism in which the sender and receiver maintain a "window" of permissible sequence numbers. The sender may transmit multiple frames before waiting for an acknowledgment, thereby utilizing the full bandwidth-delay product of the channel while ensuring reliable, in-order delivery through sequence numbering and acknowledgment strategies.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Train Carriage System:**
Imagine a long railway track with 8 carriages. The station master (sender) can only load parcels into carriages whose numbers fall inside a fixed-size "window" (say carriages 4 to 7). Once carriage 4's parcels are acknowledged as delivered, the window slides forward, freeing carriage 4 for new parcels. The receiver's window ensures it only accepts parcels in the expected sequence. This is exactly how Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat operate ŌĆö frames (parcels) are sent in batches, acknowledged in bulk, and the window "slides" to release new sequence numbers.

### 1.2 Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (MAC Sublayer):**
> The **Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer** is the lower sublayer of the OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2) responsible for governing how multiple nodes gain access to a shared transmission medium, addressing physical stations, and detecting/avoiding collisions in broadcast networks.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Round-Table Meeting:**
Picture a conference room with 20 professionals where only one person may speak at a time without chaos. A MAC protocol is the "chairperson" ŌĆö it sets the rules (raise hand, wait your turn, speak when quiet, etc.) so that everyone gets a fair chance to talk. **ALOHA**, **CSMA**, and **CSMA/CD** are different "chairing styles" with varying politeness and efficiency.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Summary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Frame** | Data Link Layer protocol data unit |
| **Sequence Number** | Unique identifier (mod 2ßĄÅ) for each frame |
| **ACK** | Positive acknowledgment from receiver |
| **NAK** | Negative acknowledgment (frame error) |
| **Timeout** | Time after which sender retransmits unacknowledged frame |
| **Channel Utilization (U)** | Fraction of channel capacity used productively |
| **Vulnerable Time** | Interval during which a collision can occur |
| **Bandwidth-Delay Product (B ├Ś Žä)** | Maximum number of bits "in flight" on the link |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module is a **high-weightage** topic, typically carrying **14ŌĆō18 marks** in university examinations. Focus areas: ARQ protocol comparisons, ALOHA throughput derivations, and CSMA/CD efficiency numericals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sliding Window Protocols ŌĆö Detailed Mechanics

### A. The Three Operational Variables

1. **Send Window Size (Ws):** Maximum number of unacknowledged frames the sender can have in transit.
2. **Receive Window Size (Wr):** Maximum number of out-of-order frames the receiver can buffer.
3. **Sequence Number Space (2ßĄÅ):** Modulo arithmetic on a k-bit sequence number.

### B. The Three Classical ARQ Strategies

| Protocol | Sender Behavior on Error | Receiver Behavior | Window Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stop-and-Wait** | Waits for ACK of every frame | Accepts only the next expected frame | Ws = 1, Wr = 1 |
| **Go-Back-N** | Resends the *errored frame and all subsequent* frames | Discards all out-of-order frames | Ws Ōēż 2ßĄÅ ŌłÆ 1, Wr = 1 |
| **Selective Repeat** | Resends *only the errored frame* | Buffers out-of-order frames | Ws Ōēż 2ßĄÅŌü╗┬╣, Wr = 2ßĄÅŌü╗┬╣ |

### C. Why Different Window Constraints?

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** In Go-Back-N, if the sender used Ws = 2ßĄÅ, an ACK for the *old* window could be mistaken for the *new* window ŌĆö a classic ambiguity. Hence Ws Ōēż 2ßĄÅ ŌłÆ 1. In Selective Repeat, the receiver can hold multiple frames, so the same ambiguity halves the allowable window to 2ßĄÅŌü╗┬╣.

### D. Operational Steps ŌĆö Generic Sliding Window

1. **Sender:** Transmit all frames within the send window; start a timer per frame.
2. **Receiver:** On arrival, if sequence number is within receive window, accept; else discard.
3. **Acknowledgment:** Send cumulative ACK (Go-Back-N) or individual ACK (Selective Repeat).
4. **Window Slide:** On valid ACK, slide the window forward; release sequence numbers.
5. **Retransmission:** On timeout/NAK, trigger appropriate retransmission policy.

---

## 2.2 MAC Sublayer ŌĆö Channel Allocation Strategies

### A. Static vs Dynamic Channel Allocation

- **Static (FDM/TDMA):** Fixed slots; simple but wastes bandwidth when traffic is bursty.
- **Dynamic (ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD):** Adapts to traffic; higher utilization at the cost of complexity.

### B. Multiple Access Protocols ŌĆö Classification

1. **Random Access (Contention-Based):** ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA
2. **Controlled Access:** Reservation, Polling, Token Passing
3. **Channelization:** FDMA, TDMA, CDMA

### C. The "Listening Before Talking" Philosophy

- **ALOHA:** Doesn't listen ŌĆö pure "transmit and pray."
- **CSMA:** Carrier Sense Multiple Access ŌĆö senses the carrier first.
- **CSMA/CD:** Adds **C**ollision **D**etection ŌĆö listens *while* transmitting (wired Ethernet).
- **CSMA/CA:** Adds **C**ollision **A**voidance ŌĆö uses ACK + backoff (Wi-Fi 802.11).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE & Module Tests.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stop-and-Wait Utilization | $U = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}$ | $T_f$ = frame transmission time, $T_p$ = propagation time |
| 2 | Sliding Window Utilization | $U = \dfrac{W \cdot T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}$ (if $W \le 2a+1$) | $W$ = window size, $a = T_p/T_f$ |
| 3 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = B \times T_p$ | $B$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| 4 | Pure ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $G$ = avg frames per frame-time (load) |
| 5 | Pure ALOHA Max Throughput | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | At $G = 0.5$ |
| 6 | Slotted ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | Time is slotted |
| 7 | Slotted ALOHA Max Throughput | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | At $G = 1$ |
| 8 | CSMA/CD Efficiency (1-persistent) | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ | $a = T_p/T_f$ (Ethernet) |
| 9 | CSMA Throughput (non-persistent) | $U = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | Approximate form |
| 10 | Max Cable Length (Ethernet) | $L = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{slot}}{2}$ | $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable |
| 11 | Go-Back-N Max Window | $W_s \le 2^k - 1$ | $k$ = sequence number bits |
| 12 | Selective Repeat Max Window | $W_s \le 2^{k-1}$ | $k$ = sequence number bits |
| 13 | Token Holding Time | $THT = \dfrac{\text{Ring Latency}}{\text{Number of Stations}}$ | Token Ring IEEE 802.5 |
| 14 | Vulnerable Time (Pure ALOHA) | $V_t = 2 \cdot T_f$ | Frame time itself |
| 15 | Vulnerable Time (Slotted ALOHA) | $V_t = T_f$ | One slot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive real protocol design ŌĆö e.g., IEEE 802.3 Ethernet uses $a = 0.01$ for **10BASE5**, optimized to keep CSMA/CD efficiency above 90%. Wi-Fi 802.11 uses CSMA/CA because wireless stations cannot detect collisions during transmission.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pure ALOHA Throughput

**Assumptions:**
- Infinite population of users generating new frames according to Poisson distribution with mean $G$ frames per frame-time.
- Vulnerable time for a frame = **2 ├Ś T_f** (one frame-time before and one after, since collision can occur with any frame starting in that window).

**Step 1: Probability of successful transmission**
The probability of zero arrivals in the vulnerable period of length $2T_f$ is given by the Poisson formula:

$$
P[\text{success}] = e^{-2G}
$$

**Step 2: Throughput S**
Throughput is the average number of successful frames per frame-time:

$$
S = G \cdot P[\text{success}]
$$

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

**Step 3: Maximize S with respect to G**
Take the derivative and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0
$$

Since $e^{-2G} \neq 0$, we get $1 - 2G = 0$, so:

$$
G = 0.5
$$

**Step 4: Maximum throughput**
Substitute $G = 0.5$ back:

$$
S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-2(0.5)} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e}
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx 0.184 \quad (\text{or } 18.4\%)
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Slotted ALOHA Throughput

**Assumption:** Time is divided into discrete slots of length $T_f$. A station may transmit *only* at the beginning of a slot.

**Step 1: Vulnerable time reduced**
Now the vulnerable period is only $T_f$ (the current slot), since frames cannot begin in the middle of a slot:

$$
P[\text{success}] = e^{-G}
$$

**Step 2: Throughput**

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

**Step 3: Maximize**

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1
$$

**Step 4: Maximum throughput**

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e}
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx 0.368 \quad (\text{or } 36.8\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Slotted ALOHA **doubles** the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA (0.368 vs 0.184) at the cost of requiring synchronized slots ŌĆö a classical KTU exam comparison.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of CSMA/CD Efficiency

**Setup:** A station that detects a collision aborts transmission. The wasted time during collision is at most $T_f$, and the total "race condition" between two stations at opposite ends is at most $2T_p$.

**Step 1: Define parameter a**

$$
a = \frac{\text{Propagation Time } (T_p)}{\text{Transmission Time } (T_f)}
$$

**Step 2: Average contention time**
For a 1-persistent CSMA/CD system (classic Ethernet), the average time the channel is wasted per attempt is approximately $2T_p$, plus an additional $n \cdot T_f / 2$ for collision detection (where n is the number of collisions).

**Step 3: Channel utilization**

$$
U = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p \cdot (\text{average collisions})}
$$

**Step 4: For heavy load (Ethernet approximation)**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44a}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical example ŌĆö 10 Mbps Ethernet, 2500 m cable, 512-bit frame**
- $T_f = 512/10^7 = 51.2 \ \mu s$
- $T_p = 2500 / (2 \times 10^8) = 12.5 \ \mu s$
- $a = 12.5 / 51.2 \approx 0.244$

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.244} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.572} = \frac{1}{2.572} \approx 0.389
$$

So only **~38.9%** of the channel is used efficiently under these conditions ŌĆö a common KTU numerical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | CSMA | 1-persistent CSMA/CD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Throughput | 0.184 | 0.368 | 0.529 (ideal) | ~0.50 typical |
| Vulnerable Time | $2T_f$ | $T_f$ | $T_p$ | $T_p$ |
| Synchronization | Not required | Required | Not required | Not required |
| Collision Detection | No | No | No | Yes |
| Best For | Satellite uplink | Slotted systems | Wired LANs | Classic Ethernet |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical ŌĆö Sliding Window Efficiency

**Problem:** A channel has $B = 1$ Mbps bandwidth, $T_p = 20$ ms one-way propagation. Frame size = 1000 bits. Find:
(a) Stop-and-Wait utilization
(b) Utilization with sliding window W = 7
(c) Minimum window size for 100% utilization

**Step 1: Compute frame transmission time**

$$
T_f = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute parameter a**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 20
$$

**Step 3: Stop-and-Wait**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 40} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 = 2.44\%
$$

**Step 4: Sliding Window with W = 7**

Since $W = 7 < 2a + 1 = 41$, the formula is:

$$
U = \frac{W}{1 + 2a} = \frac{7}{41} \approx 0.171 = 17.1\%
$$

**Step 5: Minimum W for 100% utilization**

$$
W_{min} = 2a + 1 = 2(20) + 1 = 41
$$

So **at least 41 frames** must be sent per window to fully utilize this channel.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sliding Window Protocol ŌĆö Sender & Receiver State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open Send Window] --> B[Transmit Frame in Window]
    B --> C[Start Timer for Frame]
    C --> D{ACK Received?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Slide Window Forward]
    D -- Timeout/NAK --> F[Retransmit Frame]
    E --> G{More Frames in Window?}
    F --> C
    G -- Yes --> B
    G -- No --> H[Idle: Wait for New Data]
    H --> A
```

## 4.2 Receiver Side ŌĆö Selective Repeat Buffering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Layer: Bit Stream] --> P2[Frame Boundary Detection]
    P2 --> P3{Check CRC}
    P3 -- Error --> P4[Discard Frame]
    P3 -- OK --> P5{Seq in Receive Window?}
    P5 -- No --> P4
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Buffer in Receive Window]
    P6 --> P7{In-Order Frame?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Deliver to Network Layer]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Wait for Missing Frame]
    P8 --> P10[Slide Receiver Window]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Send Cumulative ACK]
```

## 4.3 MAC Sublayer Position in the OSI/TCP Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7[Application Layer] --> L6[Transport Layer]
    L6 --> L5[Network Layer]
    L5 --> L4[Data Link Layer]
    L4 --> L3[Logical Link Control LLC]
    L3 --> M1[MAC Sublayer]
    M1 --> P1[Physical Layer]
    M1 --> M2[CSMA-CD or ALOHA or Token Passing]
    M1 --> M3[Addressing: MAC Address 48 bit]
    M1 --> M4[Frame Boundary Detection]
```

## 4.4 CSMA/CD Collision Detection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Station Has Frame] --> Q2{Channel Idle?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Transmit Frame]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Wait Random Backoff]
    Q4 --> Q2
    Q3 --> Q5{Collision Detected?}
    Q5 -- No --> Q6[Success: Send ACK]
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q7[Abort Transmission]
    Q7 --> Q8[Send Jam Signal]
    Q8 --> Q9[Compute Backoff Time]
    Q9 --> Q2
```

## 4.5 Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat ŌĆö Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GoBackN[Go-Back-N ARQ]
        GB1[Sender: Frames 0 to N-1 in Window]
        GB2[Receiver: Accepts only In-Order]
        GB3[On Error: Discard All Subsequent]
        GB4[Retransmit All from Errored Frame]
        GB1 --> GB2 --> GB3 --> GB4
    end

    subgraph SelRep[Selective Repeat ARQ]
        SR1[Sender: Frames 0 to N-1 in Window]
        SR2[Receiver: Buffers Out-of-Order]
        SR3[On Error: NAK for Specific Frame]
        SR4[Retransmit Only Errored Frame]
        SR1 --> SR2 --> SR3 --> SR4
    end
```

## 4.6 Channel Allocation Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TB
    CA[Channel Allocation in MAC] --> SA[Static Allocation]
    CA --> DA[Dynamic Allocation]
    SA --> SA1[FDM: Frequency Division]
    SA --> SA2[TDM: Time Division]
    DA --> RA[Random Access]
    DA --> CO[Controlled Access]
    DA --> CH[Channelization]
    RA --> RA1[Pure ALOHA]
    RA --> RA2[Slotted ALOHA]
    RA --> RA3[CSMA]
    RA --> RA4[CSMA-CD]
    RA --> RA5[CSMA-CA]
    CO --> CO1[Reservation]
    CO --> CO2[Polling]
    CO --> CO3[Token Passing]
    CH --> CH1[FDMA]
    CH --> CH2[TDMA]
    CH --> CH3[CDMA]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Sliding Window Protocol. What are its advantages over Stop-and-Wait?
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Sliding Window Protocol is a Data Link Layer protocol where both sender and receiver maintain a "window" of acceptable sequence numbers, allowing multiple frames to be in transit before requiring an acknowledgment.

**Advantages over Stop-and-Wait:**
1. **Higher throughput:** Multiple frames sent in one round-trip time eliminates idle channel periods.
2. **Better utilization of bandwidth-delay product:** Useful on long-delay, high-bandwidth links (e.g., satellite).
3. **Pipeline effect:** Overlaps transmission and acknowledgment, reducing total transmission time.

---

### Question 2: What is the vulnerable time in Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA?
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pure ALOHA vulnerable time = $2 \times T_f$** (one frame-time before and one after the current frame, because any frame starting in this window can collide).
- **Slotted ALOHA vulnerable time = $T_f$** (only the current slot, since transmission is restricted to slot boundaries, halving the collision window).

This is why Slotted ALOHA achieves double the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA.

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### ­¤ģ░’ĖÅ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice Option A
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Go-Back-N ARQ protocol with a suitable diagram. Derive the maximum window size constraint.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Protocol Operation (4 marks):**
- Sender maintains a window of $W_s$ frames; receiver accepts only the next in-order frame.
- On receiving an out-of-order frame, the receiver discards it and resends ACK for the last in-order frame (cumulative ACK).
- On timeout or receipt of NAK, the sender retransmits the *errored frame* and *all subsequently sent frames*.

**2. Window Size Constraint (3 marks):**
Using k-bit sequence numbers, the sender's window must satisfy:

$$
W_s \le 2^k - 1
$$

**Derivation:** If $W_s = 2^k$, an old ACK from the previous cycle (e.g., ACK-0) becomes indistinguishable from a new ACK-0 in the current cycle, creating ambiguity. Reducing by 1 eliminates this overlap.

**3. Diagram (Mermaid representation):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    S->>R: Frame 0
    S->>R: Frame 1
    S->>R: Frame 2
    R-->>S: ACK 0
    R-->>S: ACK 1
    S->>R: Frame 3 (error)
    Note over R: Discards Frame 3 and 4
    R-->>S: NAK 2
    S->>R: Frame 2 (retransmit)
    S->>R: Frame 3 (retransmit)
    S->>R: Frame 4 (retransmit)
    R-->>S: ACK 4
```

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat ARQ. Under what conditions is Selective Repeat preferred?

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|---|---|---|
| Receiver Window $W_r$ | 1 | $2^{k-1}$ |
| Retransmission | All frames from error onwards | Only errored frame |
| Network traffic on error | High | Low |
| Receiver complexity | Low (no buffering) | High (buffering needed) |
| Sequence number space | $2^k$ | $2^k$ (but window halved) |
| Efficiency on noisy links | Poor | Better |

**When Selective Repeat is preferred (2 marks):**
- **Long-fat networks** (high BDP, e.g., satellite links) where retransmitting many frames wastes bandwidth.
- **High error-rate channels** where frequent errors would force Go-Back-N to retransmit most of the window.
- **Receiver has sufficient memory** to buffer out-of-order frames.

---

### ­¤ģ▒’ĖÅ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice Option B
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the CSMA/CD protocol. How does collision detection work, and what is the role of the jam signal?

**Model Solution:**

**1. CSMA/CD Operation (3 marks):**
- **Carrier Sense:** Station listens to the medium before transmitting. If idle, transmits.
- **Collision Detection:** While transmitting, station monitors the signal level; if it detects energy above a threshold (= collision), it aborts immediately.
- **Binary Exponential Backoff:** After the $i$-th collision, the station waits a random number of slot times in $\{0, 1, ..., 2^i - 1\}$, up to a cap of 1023 (Ethernet).

**2. Role of Jam Signal (2 marks):**
A 32-bit jam pattern is transmitted after collision detection to:
- Ensure all other stations also detect the collision (since some may be far away and need to hear the collision).
- Reinforce the collision so that the receivers can properly identify the frame as corrupted and discard it.

**3. Minimum Frame Size & Slot Time (2 marks):**
For a station to detect a collision, transmission time must be at least $2 \times$ maximum propagation delay:

$$
T_{f,min} = 2 T_p \quad \implies \quad L_{min} = 2 \times v \times T_p
$$

For 10 Mbps Ethernet with 2500 m max cable: $L_{min} = 512$ bits = 64 bytes.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A network uses Pure ALOHA. If the average number of frames generated per frame-time is G = 0.4, find: (i) Probability of successful transmission, (ii) Throughput, (iii) Compare with Slotted ALOHA at the same load.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $G = 0.4$ frames/frame-time

**(i) Probability of success for Pure ALOHA:**

$$
P_{success} = e^{-2G} = e^{-2(0.4)} = e^{-0.8}
$$

$$
P_{success} = 0.4493 \approx 0.449
$$

**(ii) Throughput of Pure ALOHA:**

$$
S_{pure} = G \cdot e^{-2G} = 0.4 \times 0.4493 = 0.1797
$$

$$
S_{pure} \approx 0.180 \text{ (or 18%)}
$$

**(iii) Slotted ALOHA at the same load:**

$$
P_{success} = e^{-G} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703
$$

$$
S_{slotted} = G \cdot e^{-G} = 0.4 \times 0.6703 = 0.2681
$$

$$
S_{slotted} \approx 0.268 \text{ (or 26.8%)}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Probability of success | 0.449 | 0.670 |
| Throughput S | 0.180 | 0.268 |
| % improvement | ŌĆö | ~49% better |

**Conclusion:** Slotted ALOHA significantly outperforms Pure ALOHA at the same load because its vulnerable period is halved.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mistakes students make in this module that cost 2ŌĆō3 marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Pure and Slotted ALOHA formulas** ŌĆö Always check whether the problem mentions "slot" or not. Pure uses $e^{-2G}$, Slotted uses $e^{-G}$.
> 2. **Wrong window size formula** ŌĆö Go-Back-N is $2^k - 1$, Selective Repeat is $2^{k-1}$. Many students swap these.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by G** in throughput ŌĆö $P_{success}$ is not the throughput; throughput $= G \times P_{success}$.
> 4. **Using $|a|$ notation in formula tables** ŌĆö Use $\vert a \vert$ or $\text{abs}(a)$ in markdown tables; unescaped `|` breaks table rendering.
> 5. **In CSMA/CD numericals, forgetting the 6.44 constant** ŌĆö This is specific to Ethernet; theoretical CSMA uses 2 instead of 6.44.
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram** in 7-mark ARQ sub-parts ŌĆö Diagrams carry 1ŌĆō2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## Ō£ģ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Sliding Window** improves on Stop-and-Wait by pipelining frames within a fixed window.
- [x] **Go-Back-N** window constraint: $W_s \le 2^k - 1$; **Selective Repeat:** $W_s \le 2^{k-1}$.
- [x] **Pure ALOHA** vulnerable time = $2T_f$; max throughput = $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$.
- [x] **Slotted ALOHA** vulnerable time = $T_f$; max throughput = $1/e \approx 0.368$.
- [x] **CSMA/CD** is the foundation of IEEE 802.3 Ethernet; **CSMA/CA** is used in Wi-Fi 802.11.
- [x] **Jam signal** (32-bit) is sent after collision to alert all stations.
- [x] **Minimum Ethernet frame** = 64 bytes to ensure collision detection over max cable length.
- [x] **CSMA/CD efficiency** $U = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$, where $a = T_p/T_f$.
- [x] **Bandwidth-Delay Product** determines the minimum window size for full utilization.
- [x] **Receiver window** in Selective Repeat must equal sender window for in-order delivery.
- [x] **Cumulative ACK** used in Go-Back-N; **Selective ACK** in Selective Repeat.
- [x] **Pure ALOHA does NOT require synchronization**; Slotted ALOHA DOES.
- [x] **Token Ring (802.5)** has $W_s = 1$ but uses controlled access instead of contention.
- [x] **Examiners expect:** at least one diagram per 7-mark ARQ question and a clear comparison table.
- [x] **Memory tip:** "SLOP" = Slotted Loses One, Pure loses two ŌĆö vulnerable time mnemonic.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes ŌĆö Sliding Window Protocols & MAC Sublayer**
