topics.title: Channel Allocation Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Channel Allocation Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Channel Allocation Problem** in computer networks refers to the challenge of efficiently sharing a finite communication medium (the channel) among multiple competing users or stations in a broadcast network, such that:

1. **Throughput is maximized** (effective utilization of the channel).
2. **Fairness is ensured** (no single user monopolizes the channel).
3. **Latency is minimized** (delay before successful transmission).
4. **Collision/Contention is bounded** (data corruption from simultaneous access is controlled).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEET528 - Module 2):**
> *"The channel allocation problem deals with the methodology of allocating the single shared communication channel among N independent users such that channel utilization, average delay, and fairness criteria are simultaneously optimized."*

The problem is formally known as the **Multi-Access Protocol (MAC Protocol)** design problem and falls under the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** of the **OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Channel Allocation Schemes

The complete taxonomy of channel allocation techniques is summarized as:

**A. Static (Fixed) Channel Allocation**
- **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)**
- **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)**

**B. Dynamic (Adaptive) Channel Allocation**
- **Pure (Slotted) ALOHA**
- **Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)**
  - 1-persistent CSMA
  - Non-persistent CSMA
  - p-persistent CSMA
- **CSMA with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)**
- **CSMA with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)**
- **Token Passing Protocols**
- **Reservation Systems**
- **Polling Protocols**

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Conference Room Telephone":**
>
> Imagine **5 employees** sharing a single telephone line in a small office. The line is a *single broadcast channel*. The problem:
>
> - If **everyone talks at once**, no one understands anything ŌåÆ **Collision**.
> - If **only one person** is allowed to talk at a time, conversation is orderly ŌåÆ **Perfect Allocation**, but **wasteful** if the line is idle.
> - The **Channel Allocation Problem** is exactly this: *How do we let 5 people share 1 line efficiently?*
>
> Three strategies mirror real protocols:
>
> 1. **"Just speak whenever you wish"** ŌåÆ **ALOHA** (no checking, high collisions).
> 2. **"Listen first, if silent then speak"** ŌåÆ **CSMA** (smarter, fewer collisions).
> 3. **"Listen, speak, and stop immediately if you hear someone else"** ŌåÆ **CSMA/CD** (Ethernet's strategy).

---

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics

- **Channel Capacity (C)** ŌĆö measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Propagation Delay (t<sub>p</sub>)** ŌĆö time for a bit to traverse the medium.
- **Transmission Delay (t<sub>trans</sub>)** ŌĆö time to push the entire frame onto the wire.
- **Throughput (S)** ŌĆö fraction of channel capacity used productively.
- **Offered Load (G)** ŌĆö average number of transmission attempts per frame time.
- **Frame Time (T)** ŌĆö duration to transmit a single fixed-length frame.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Problem Statement

Given:
- A **single shared broadcast channel** of capacity **C bps**.
- **N independent stations** generating frames at random times.
- All stations are **equal in priority** (no centralized master).

**Goal:** Design a **distributed algorithm** that each station runs independently so that the *aggregate* behavior approximates an ideal scheduler.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "distributed"?** A centralized controller is impractical for geographically dispersed networks because it becomes a single point of failure and a bottleneck.

---

## 2.2 Static Channel Allocation ŌĆö FDM & TDM

### Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)
- The bandwidth **C** is split into **N equal sub-bands**.
- Each station is permanently assigned one sub-band of size **C/N**.
- **Disadvantage:** If station #3 is silent, its band is **wasted** ŌĆö bandwidth fragmentation.

### Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)
- Time is divided into **slots**, and each station gets one slot per cycle.
- A cycle contains **N slots**, each of duration **T/N**.
- **Disadvantage:** Even idle stations hold their slots, and TDM has high **synchronization overhead**.

### Mathematical Limitation of Static Allocation

If we assume each station transmits **╬╗ frames/sec** with frame length **L bits**, the throughput is:

$$
\lambda \cdot L \le \frac{C}{N}
$$

If the **combined offered load** exceeds **C**, the system **fails** regardless of static allocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Static allocation assumes *traffic patterns are predictable and uniform*. This is rarely true in real networks. Hence **Dynamic Allocation** is mandatory for bursty traffic.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Channel Allocation ŌĆö Detailed Analysis

### A. Pure ALOHA (Unslotted)

- A station transmits **immediately** whenever a frame is ready.
- If a **collision** occurs, the station waits a **random backoff time** and retries.
- **Vulnerability window:** `2T` (where T is frame time), because a colliding frame can overlap with the start of any frame that begins within 1 frame time before or after it.

### B. Slotted ALOHA

- Time is divided into **discrete slots** of duration **T**.
- Transmission is permitted only at slot **boundaries**.
- **Vulnerability window:** `T` (halved compared to Pure ALOHA).
- **Maximum throughput:** **36.8%** at G = 1.

### C. Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)

The station **listens** to the channel before transmitting. Three variants exist:

| Variant | Channel Idle? | Channel Busy? |
|---|---|---|
| **1-persistent** | Transmit **immediately** (with prob 1) | Wait until idle, then transmit |
| **Non-persistent** | Transmit immediately | Wait **random** time, then re-sense |
| **p-persistent** | Transmit with prob **p**, defer with prob **1-p** | Wait until idle, repeat |

### D. CSMA/CD (Ethernet's Protocol)

- Combines CSMA with **Collision Detection**.
- If a collision is detected, transmission **ceases immediately** (saves bandwidth).
- **Jam signal** is broadcast to ensure all stations know about collision.
- **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** After i<sup>th</sup> collision, wait a random number of slot times from {0, 1, ..., 2<sup>i</sup> - 1}.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Frame Size Condition:**
> A station must transmit for at least **2 ├Ś t<sub>prop</sub>** seconds to guarantee that a collision is detected before frame transmission ends.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for the University Exam ŌĆö these formulas appear in nearly every question paper.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pure ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | G = offered load (attempts/frame time) | Max $S = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ at G = 0.5 |
| 2 | Slotted ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | G = offered load | Max $S = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ at G = 1 |
| 3 | Vulnerable Window (Pure ALOHA) | $V_W = 2T$ | T = frame transmission time | |
| 4 | Vulnerable Window (Slotted ALOHA) | $V_W = T$ | T = slot duration | |
| 5 | TDM per-station capacity | $C_{user} = \dfrac{C}{N}$ | C = total bandwidth, N = stations | Static allocation |
| 6 | FDM per-station bandwidth | $B_{user} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{N}$ | B = total bandwidth | Static allocation |
| 7 | CSMA/CD Minimum Frame | $T_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{prop}$ | $t_{prop}$ = propagation delay | Ensures collision detection |
| 8 | BEB backoff range (after i collisions) | $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{i} - 1\}$ | i = collision count | Capped at $2^{10} - 1$ (10 attempts) |
| 9 | Channel utilization (CSMA/CD) | $U = \dfrac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + 2t_{prop}}$ | $T_{trans}$ = frame tx time | Higher for longer frames |
| 10 | Efficiency of Slotted ALOHA | $\eta = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{G} = e^{-G}$ | | Goes to 0 as G ŌåÆ Ōł× |

**ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ LaTeX Note:** The vertical bars for absolute value (e.g., `|x|`) are written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table above.

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Relevance:**
> - **Ethernet (802.3):** Uses CSMA/CD (legacy) or full-duplex switched Ethernet.
> - **Wi-Fi (802.11):** Uses CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS handshake.
> - **5G / LTE:** Uses scheduled OFDMA, an evolution of static allocation principles with dynamic scheduling.
> - **Satellite Networks:** Originally ALOHA-based; now use reservation schemes.
> - **IoT / LoRa:** Use ALOHA-like random access for low-power uplink.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Throughput of Pure ALOHA

### Assumption
- All frames have **equal length T**.
- A station attempts transmission at any time (continuous time model).
- Let **G** = average number of **transmission attempts** per frame time **T**.

### Probability Model

A successful transmission requires that **no other frame** overlaps with the current one.

A frame transmitted at time $t_0$ will suffer a collision if **any other station** transmits during the interval:

$$
[t_0 - T, \, t_0 + T]
$$

This is a window of length $2T$ (vulnerable window).

The number of attempts in any interval of length $2T$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with mean $2G$:

$$
P(k \text{ attempts}) = \frac{(2G)^k \cdot e^{-2G}}{k!}
$$

For a successful transmission, exactly **0 other attempts** must occur in the vulnerable window:

$$
P(\text{success}) = P(k = 0) = e^{-2G}
$$

### Throughput Formula

Throughput S (frames per frame time) is the product of offered load and success probability:

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

### Maximizing Throughput

To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to G and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = 0
$$

Factor out $e^{-2G}$ (always non-zero):

$$
e^{-2G} \cdot (1 - 2G) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
1 - 2G = 0 \implies G = \frac{1}{2}
$$

### Maximum Throughput Value

Substituting $G = \frac{1}{2}$ into the throughput formula:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-2 \cdot (1/2)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA is $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ of channel capacity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Throughput of Slotted ALOHA

### Difference from Pure ALOHA
- Time is **slotted**; transmission starts **only at slot boundaries**.
- A new frame cannot collide with one that started **more than 1 slot ago**, because slots are protected.

### Vulnerable Window Reduction
The vulnerable window shrinks from $2T$ to $T$. The number of attempts in 1 slot follows Poisson with mean **G**:

$$
P(k = 0) = e^{-G}
$$

### Throughput Formula

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

### Maximization

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = 0
$$

$$
1 - G = 0 \implies G = 1
$$

### Maximum Throughput Value

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** Maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA is $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ ŌĆö **double** that of Pure ALOHA.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | 1-persistent CSMA | Non-persistent CSMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vulnerable Window** | $2T$ | $T$ | $2 t_{prop}$ | Slightly < 1-persistent |
| **Max Throughput** | $0.184$ | $0.368$ | $\approx 0.53$ | $\approx 0.81$ |
| **Slotting Required?** | No | Yes | No | No |
| **Carrier Sense?** | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Collision Detection?** | No | No | No | No |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
>
> *"A 200 km long, 10 Mbps channel is shared among 100 stations. Each station generates a 1000-bit frame on average every 10 seconds. Is pure ALOHA suitable? What about slotted ALOHA?"*

### Given
- $d = 200 \text{ km}$ (cable length)
- $C = 10 \text{ Mbps}$ (channel capacity)
- $N = 100$ stations
- $L = 1000$ bits per frame
- Frame generation rate per station: $\lambda = \frac{1}{10} \text{ frames/sec}$

### Step 1: Frame Transmission Time T

$$
T = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{1000}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} = 100 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2: Aggregate Frame Rate

$$
\Lambda_{total} = N \cdot \lambda = 100 \cdot \frac{1}{10} = 10 \text{ frames/sec}
$$

### Step 3: Per-Frame Load (G)

$$
G = \Lambda_{total} \cdot T = 10 \cdot 10^{-4} = 10^{-3} = 0.001
$$

### Step 4: Throughput (Pure ALOHA)

$$
S_{pure} = G \cdot e^{-2G} = 0.001 \cdot e^{-0.002} \approx 0.001 \cdot 0.998 \approx 0.000998
$$

Since G is extremely small (low load), the system is **underutilized** but ALOHA is acceptable here.

### Step 5: Throughput (Slotted ALOHA)

$$
S_{slotted} = G \cdot e^{-G} \approx 0.001 \cdot 0.999 \approx 0.000999
$$

### Conclusion
- Both protocols work, but throughput is **far below** theoretical maximum because offered load is very low.
- **Recommendation:** CSMA/CD or switched Ethernet would dramatically improve performance under higher load.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the channel allocation problem. Why can't we use static FDM/TDM allocation in modern packet-switched networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **channel allocation problem** is the challenge of efficiently sharing a single broadcast medium among multiple competing stations such that throughput, delay, and fairness are optimized.

Static **FDM** wastes bandwidth when some users are silent (idle sub-bands), while **TDM** wastes slots and requires strict synchronization. Modern traffic is **bursty** (e.g., web browsing, video streaming) rather than uniform, so static allocation leads to severe **underutilization**. Hence, **dynamic allocation protocols** like ALOHA and CSMA are required.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for FDM drawback, 1 mark for TDM drawback + bursty traffic reason.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Compare the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Why is Slotted ALOHA twice as efficient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Max Throughput | $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ |
| Optimal G | 0.5 | 1.0 |

Slotted ALOHA is **twice as efficient** because it halves the **vulnerable window** from $2T$ to $T$. A frame in Slotted ALOHA can collide only with frames transmitted in the **same slot**, not with those from adjacent slots. By forcing transmissions to begin at slot boundaries, the system eliminates half of the collision scenarios present in Pure ALOHA.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose 2-3 marks** by not drawing a proper **diagram of the vulnerable window** ŌĆö this is *mandatory* for full marks.
> - Do not forget the **derivative step** when finding maximum throughput ŌĆö direct substitution of "common sense" values is NOT accepted by KTU examiners.
> - Always mention **assumptions** (Poisson traffic, infinite population, equal frame lengths) at the start of derivations.
> - In numerical problems, **explicitly state units** for every quantity. Marks are cut for "bare numbers" like $T = 10^{-4}$ without seconds.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q (a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Pure ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA** protocols with the help of a vulnerable window diagram. Derive the expression for the maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA.

**Q (b)** [7 marks] A group of **N = 200 stations** share a **1 Mbps** pure ALOHA channel. Each station outputs a **500-bit frame** on average every **50 seconds**. Calculate:
- (i) The frame transmission time T
- (ii) The offered load G
- (iii) The probability that a given transmission attempt succeeds
- (iv) The expected number of successful transmissions per second

---

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A**

#### Part (a): ALOHA Protocols ŌĆö Explanation & Derivation

**Pure ALOHA:**
- A station transmits **immediately** when a frame is generated.
- No synchronization, no carrier sensing.
- **Vulnerable window** = $2T$: a frame sent at time $t_0$ collides with any other frame sent in the interval $[t_0 - T, t_0 + T]$.

**Slotted ALOHA (Roberts, 1975):**
- Time axis is divided into **slots of duration T**.
- A station may begin transmission **only at the start of a slot**.
- **Vulnerable window** = $T$: collision only with frames of the **same slot**.

**Throughput Derivation (Slotted ALOHA):**

Let $G$ = average number of transmission attempts per slot (Poisson process with mean G). For success, **no other station** should attempt in the same slot:

$$
P(\text{success in a slot}) = e^{-G}
$$

Throughput $S$ (successful slots per frame time):

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

**Maximization:**

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1
$$

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

> *(1 mark for proper diagram showing slot boundaries; 2 marks for throughput equation; 2 marks for maximization; 1 mark for final numerical value; 1 mark for conclusion.)*

#### Part (b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $N = 200$ stations
- $C = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $L = 500 \text{ bits/frame}$
- $\lambda = \frac{1}{50} \text{ frames/sec/station}$

**Step (i): Frame Transmission Time T**

$$
T = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{500}{10^6} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} = 500 \, \mu s
$$

**Step (ii): Total Frame Rate & Offered Load G**

$$
\Lambda_{total} = N \cdot \lambda = 200 \cdot \frac{1}{50} = 4 \text{ frames/sec}
$$

$$
G = \Lambda_{total} \cdot T = 4 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.002
$$

**Step (iii): Probability of Successful Transmission**

$$
P(\text{success}) = e^{-2G} = e^{-2 \times 0.002} = e^{-0.004} \approx 0.9960
$$

**Step (iv): Expected Successful Transmissions Per Second**

$$
S_{per\_sec} = G \cdot e^{-2G} \cdot \frac{1}{T} = 0.002 \cdot 0.9960 \cdot 2000 \approx 3.984 \text{ frames/sec}
$$

> *(Allocation: 1 mark (i), 2 marks (ii), 2 marks (iii), 2 marks (iv) with proper units.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a)** [7 marks] Discuss the limitations of **static channel allocation** (FDM/TDM). Compare the throughput performance of **1-persistent, non-persistent, and p-persistent CSMA** protocols.

**Q (b)** [7 marks] With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **CSMA/CD** as used in **Ethernet IEEE 802.3**. Derive the expression for **channel utilization efficiency** as a function of frame transmission time and propagation delay.

---

### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B**

#### Part (a): Static Allocation Limits & CSMA Comparison

**Static Allocation ŌĆö Limitations:**

1. **FDM**: Each station is permanently assigned a frequency band. If a station is silent, its band is **wasted**; if active, it cannot exceed the fixed bandwidth.
2. **TDM**: Each station gets a fixed time slot. Idle slots are wasted, and adding a new station requires re-engineering the cycle.
3. **Bursty Traffic Failure**: Real traffic (web, video) is highly variable. Static allocation cannot adapt to traffic bursts, leading to poor **statistical multiplexing**.
4. **Scalability**: For N = 1000 stations, per-station share becomes trivially small.

**CSMA Variants Comparison:**

| Property | 1-persistent | Non-persistent | p-persistent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Idle action** | Transmit immediately | Transmit immediately | Transmit with prob p |
| **Busy action** | Wait, then re-sense | Random wait, then re-sense | Wait until idle, repeat |
| **Wasted idle sense** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Collision risk** | High (after busy period) | Lower (randomized wait) | Controllable via p |
| **Best use case** | Few stations | Many stations | Slotted channels |

#### Part (b): CSMA/CD Operation & Efficiency

**Operation Steps:**
1. Station senses the channel.
2. If idle, begins transmission.
3. If collision detected (voltage jump on the medium), all stations abort and send a **jam signal** (32 bits).
4. After jam, station waits a **random backoff time** based on Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB).
5. After 16 failed attempts, the frame is dropped and the upper layer is notified.

**Minimum Frame Size:** A frame must occupy the channel for $\geq 2 t_{prop}$ to guarantee the sender detects a collision:

$$
T_{min} = 2 t_{prop}
$$

**Efficiency Derivation:**

Let $T_{trans}$ = frame transmission time, $t_{prop}$ = end-to-end propagation delay. For a successful transmission:

Total time for one frame = $T_{trans} + 2 t_{prop}$ (last bit reaches the farthest station after $2 t_{prop}$).

Channel utilization $U$ (efficiency):

$$
U = \frac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + 2 t_{prop}}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $T_{trans}$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2 t_{prop}}{T_{trans}}}
$$

Substituting $a = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{T_{trans}}$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

> *Interpretation: Higher 'a' means more channel capacity is wasted in propagation overhead. For 10BASE5 Ethernet ($a \approx 0.05$), $U \approx 0.91$ (91% efficiency).*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ŌśÉ Channel Allocation Problem = sharing a **single broadcast medium** among N users.
- ŌśÉ **Static (FDM/TDM)** is inefficient for **bursty** traffic.
- ŌśÉ **Pure ALOHA**: vulnerable window = $2T$, max throughput = $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$.
- ŌśÉ **Slotted ALOHA**: vulnerable window = $T$, max throughput = $1/e \approx 0.368$.
- ŌśÉ **1-persistent CSMA** = best under low load; worst under high load.
- ŌśÉ **Non-persistent CSMA** = best for high load (sacrifices delay for lower collisions).
- ŌśÉ **p-persistent CSMA** = compromise; used in **slotted channels**.
- ŌśÉ **CSMA/CD** requires **minimum frame size** to detect collisions: $T_{min} = 2 t_{prop}$.
- ŌśÉ **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)**: $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{i} - 1\}$ after $i$-th collision; capped at 1023.
- ŌśÉ **Ethernet efficiency**: $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = t_{prop} / T_{trans}$.
- ŌśÉ **Token-based** protocols (Token Ring, Token Bus) are **contention-free** alternatives.
- ŌśÉ Always **state assumptions** in derivations: infinite population, Poisson traffic, equal frame lengths.
- ŌśÉ In numericals, **always write units** and **show the formula** before substitution.
- ŌśÉ **Memorize** the formula: $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ (Slotted ALOHA) and $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ (Pure ALOHA).
- ŌśÉ **Wi-Fi (802.11)** uses **CSMA/CA** (collision avoidance) because wireless nodes cannot detect collisions.
- ŌśÉ **Maximum frame time** in CSMA/CD propagation model: 51.2 ┬Ąs for 10 Mbps Ethernet (translates to minimum 64-byte frame).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access Protocols - CSMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access Protocols - CSMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** is a contention-based **Medium Access Control (MAC)** protocol used in shared-medium local area networks (LANs), where a node intending to transmit first **listens to (senses)** the carrier (channel) to determine whether another transmission is already in progress, and defers its own transmission if the channel is found to be **busy**.

CSMA is a probabilistic, decentralized, distributed-access protocol that improves upon the basic **ALOHA** family by exploiting the principle of *listening-before-talking*. It belongs to the class of **random access / contention-based** MAC protocols, as defined in the IEEE 802 reference model. The original ALOHA protocols allowed blind transmission (leading to a maximum theoretical efficiency of only **18.4%** for pure ALOHA); CSMA reduces the probability of collision by allowing a node to *sense* the medium prior to transmission.

The standard metric for measuring channel performance in CSMA is the **parameter `a`**, defined formally as:

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{\text{Propagation Time}}{\text{Transmission Time}} = \frac{d / v}{L / R}$$

Where:
- $T_p$ = propagation time of a signal across the maximum end-to-end cable distance **d** at velocity **v**
- $T_t$ = transmission time of a frame of length **L** bits at data rate **R** bps
- Lower values of **a** indicate a more efficient network (faster transmission relative to propagation)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö A Polite Conversation in a Corridor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Group Conversation in a Narrow Hallway**

Imagine a group of 5 friends standing at different points in a long, narrow corridor, all of whom want to talk to each other simultaneously. Being polite human beings, they adopt a simple rule:

1. **Listen First (Carrier Sense):** Before speaking, each friend listens to determine if anyone else is already talking.
2. **Speak Only if Silent:** If the corridor (channel) is *silent*, the friend begins speaking; if *busy*, they wait.
3. **Handle Collisions Gracefully (Backoff):** If two friends accidentally speak at the exact same instant (collision), they hear the garbled sound, stop talking, wait a random amount of time, and then try again.

This *listen-then-talk* principle is the essence of CSMA. The **corridor is the shared medium** (cable/radio), the **friends are the stations**, and the **garbled sound** is the **collision**.

The crucial *vulnerability* arises because of finite signal propagation speed. Suppose Friend A (at one end) finishes talking, and the silence has *not yet reached* Friend B (at the far end). If B starts talking right at that instant, a collision occurs that B cannot possibly detect by listening at the moment of transmission. This delay-related hazard is the **vulnerable period** of CSMA.

### 1.3 Why CSMA? ŌĆö Motivation from ALOHA

| Protocol | Maximum Theoretical Throughput | Carrier Sense? | Idle Listening? |
|----------|-------------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Pure ALOHA | **18.4%** (1 / 2e) | No | No |
| Slotted ALOHA | **36.8%** (1 / e) | No (slotted only) | No |
| CSMA (general) | Up to ~**80%+** | **Yes** | Optional |
| CSMA/CD | ~**90%+** (Ethernet) | **Yes** | **Yes** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Highlight:** Whenever asked to compare ALOHA and CSMA in a 3-mark question, always state: *"ALOHA transmits blindly (channel utilization Ōēł 18.4%), whereas CSMA senses the channel first, dramatically reducing collisions and improving throughput ŌĆö at the cost of propagation delay overhead."*

### 1.4 The Three Canonical CSMA Strategies

CSMA behavior is classified by the *persistence* strategy a node adopts after sensing the channel state. These three strategies form the foundational taxonomy tested in KTU exams:

1. **1-Persistent CSMA** ŌĆö A node that finds the channel busy waits *continuously* (100% of the time, i.e., persistently) until the channel becomes idle, and then transmits *immediately* with probability **1**.
2. **Non-Persistent CSMA** ŌĆö A node that finds the channel busy waits for a *random* backoff interval before re-sensing. This reduces collision probability but introduces idle channel time.
3. **p-Persistent CSMA** ŌĆö A hybrid used in *slotted* (time-divided) channels. A node transmits with probability **p** in the next slot if the channel is idle, and defers with probability **(1 ŌłÆ p)**.

These strategies trade off **collision probability** vs. **channel idle time** differently, leading to distinct throughput curves as a function of the offered load *G* and the parameter *a*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of CSMA ŌĆö Step-by-Step

Every CSMA variant executes the same fundamental decision loop. The persistence strategy only changes *what happens when the channel is sensed busy*.

**Generic CSMA Algorithm (per node):**

1. **Frame Ready:** A packet (frame) arrives at the MAC sublayer of the node, queued for transmission.
2. **Carrier Sense:** The node monitors the energy/signal level on the medium to detect whether a carrier (another transmission) is present.
3. **Channel-State Decision:** Three outcomes are possible:
   - **Channel Idle** ŌåÆ proceed to step 4
   - **Channel Busy** ŌåÆ proceed to step 5
   - **Collision (in CD variants)** ŌåÆ jump to step 6
4. **Transmit:** Begin transmitting the entire frame.
5. **Persistence Action:** Based on the strategy:
   - *1-persistent:* wait continuously until idle, then transmit immediately.
   - *non-persistent:* wait a random backoff time $T_{bo}$, then return to step 2.
   - *p-persistent:* if slotted, transmit with probability $p$, defer with probability $(1-p)$ for one slot, then re-sense.
6. **Collision Handling (only in CSMA/CD):** Abort transmission, transmit a brief **jam signal** to ensure all other nodes detect the collision, wait a random backoff (exponential), and retry from step 2.

### 2.2 Vulnerable Time ŌĆö The Critical Theoretical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerable Time in CSMA: $T_v = 2 \times T_p$**

The **vulnerable time** is the window during which a collision *can* occur even with perfect carrier sensing. It equals **twice the maximum one-way propagation delay**, because the worst-case collision scenario involves two stations positioned at the *opposite ends* of the medium:

- Station A starts transmitting at time $t = 0$ at one end.
- The signal takes $T_p$ to reach the far end.
- If Station B begins transmitting at time $t$ such that $0 \leq t \leq T_p$, B will not yet sense A's signal (because it hasn't arrived).
- B's signal also takes $T_p$ to travel back.
- The collision occurs at some midpoint, and the *return* of the garbled signal to A takes another $T_p$.
- Total vulnerable window for A's frame to be corrupted = up to $2 T_p$.

This is in sharp contrast to ALOHA, where the vulnerable time equals the entire frame transmission time $T_t$. CSMA reduces the vulnerability from $T_t$ to $2T_p$, which is the mathematical basis for its much higher throughput.

### 2.3 The Propagation-Transmission Parameter **a**

The single most important dimensionless parameter in CSMA analysis is:

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{d / v}{L / R} = \frac{R \cdot d}{v \cdot L}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| $T_p$ | One-way propagation time | seconds |
| $T_t$ | Frame transmission time ($L/R$) | seconds |
| $d$ | Maximum cable length | meters |
| $v$ | Signal propagation speed (Ōēł $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper) | m/s |
| $L$ | Frame length | bits |
| $R$ | Data rate | bits/second |
| $a$ | Dimensionless ratio (vulnerability) | unitless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** A *small* $a$ means the network transmits each frame much faster than the signal propagates across the cable ŌĆö collisions are rare, throughput is high. A *large* $a$ (slow links, long cables, short frames) means the channel is vulnerable to collision for a large fraction of frame time, degrading throughput.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The table below contains the high-yield throughput expressions for KTU board examination. All formulas use the **offered load $G$** (mean number of transmission attempts per frame time) and the parameter **$a$**.

| Protocol | Throughput $S(G, a)$ | Peak $S_{max}$ | Behaviour at High $G$ |
|----------|------------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Pure ALOHA | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $0.184$ at $G = 0.5$ | Collapses to 0 |
| Slotted ALOHA | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $0.368$ at $G = 1$ | Collapses to 0 |
| **1-Persistent CSMA** | $S = \dfrac{G e^{-G(1+a)} \left(1 + G + a G\right)}{G(1+a) + e^{-G(1+a)} - 1}$ (approx.) | Depends on $a$ (e.g. Ōēł 0.529 for $a=0.01$) | Collapses sharply due to collision clusters |
| **Non-Persistent CSMA** | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | Up to Ōēł 0.81 for small $a$ | Degrades gracefully (best under heavy load) |
| **p-Persistent CSMA (slotted)** | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+a)} \cdot \left[1 + a - e^{-aG}\right]}{(1+a)\left[1 - e^{-aG}\right] + a e^{-aG}}$ | Approximates 1-persistent as $p \to 1$ | Tunable; smoother than 1-persistent |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The 1-persistent CSMA formula is often given in a **simplified form** in KTU textbooks (Forouzan) as: $S = \dfrac{G e^{-G(1+a)}}{G(1+a) + e^{-G(1+a)} - 1}$. Use the **fuller exact expression** above only when the question explicitly demands the more precise derivation.

### 2.5 Persistence Strategies ŌĆö Comparative Analysis

| Strategy | Channel Idle ŌćÆ | Channel Busy ŌćÆ | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------------------|------------------|------|------|
| 1-Persistent | Transmit *immediately* (prob 1) | Wait continuously until idle, then transmit | Zero idle time, good at low load | High collision probability at high load |
| Non-Persistent | Transmit immediately | Wait random backoff $T_{bo}$, re-sense | Low collision probability | Introduces *idle time* ŌĆö wasteful at low load |
| p-Persistent | Transmit with prob $p$, defer with prob $(1-p)$ (slotted) | Wait one slot, re-sense | Tunable; smooth behaviour | More complex; requires slot synchronisation |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSMA is **not merely a textbook concept** ŌĆö it is the bedrock of all modern wired Ethernet and WiFi networks.

- **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet)** ŌĆö uses **1-persistent CSMA/CD** historically (10BASE5, 10BASE2, 10BASE-T). Modern full-duplex switched Ethernet (100BASE-TX, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps) effectively disables CSMA/CD because switches isolate collision domains.
- **IEEE 802.11 (WiFi)** ŌĆö uses **CSMA/CA** with binary exponential backoff and RTS/CTS handshaking, because wireless stations *cannot* detect collisions reliably due to the **near-far problem** and **hidden terminal problem**.
- **IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee, Thread, Matter)** ŌĆö uses a slotted variant of CSMA/CA with superframe structure.
- **DOCSIS (Cable Modem)** ŌĆö upstream channel uses a *reservation*-enhanced CSMA-like protocol.
- **CAN bus (automotive)** ŌĆö a related multi-master protocol with CSMA + bit-wise arbitration (CSMA/BA).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Throughput of Non-Persistent CSMA

We derive the throughput $S$ (successful frames per frame time) as a function of offered load $G$ (attempts per frame time), under the assumption of an infinite population of independent, Poisson-distributed stations.

**Assumptions:**

- Frame transmission time = $T_t$ (normalized to 1 frame time unit).
- One-way propagation delay = $a$ (in frame time units), where $a = T_p / T_t$.
- New arrivals follow a Poisson process with mean rate $\lambda$ per frame time.
- The total offered load (new + retransmitted) per frame time is $G = \lambda \cdot T_t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Probability that the channel is idle at an arbitrary instant:**

Under a Poisson offered load $G$, the probability of *no* attempt in any interval of length $t$ is $P[\text{no attempt in }t] = e^{-Gt}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Probability of a successful transmission:**

A non-persistent station transmits immediately if the channel is sensed idle, and defers otherwise. For success, no other station must begin transmission during the *vulnerable period*. From the perspective of the *first* station beginning a transmission at time $t = 0$:

- The channel must be idle for at least $a$ seconds *before* $t = 0$ (so the station is not inhibited by sensing another's signal) ŌĆö but this is handled by the carrier sense itself.
- The critical condition is: **no other station begins a transmission during the interval $[-a, +a]$ around the start time**, because any such transmission would either cause a collision at the receiving end or be initiated by a station that didn't sense the new transmission.

Effectively, the station must "win the race" against any other attempt in a window of length approximately $1 + a$ (its own transmission time, plus the round-trip propagation window for any collider's signal to be sensed). For analytical tractability, the standard derivation approximates this window length.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Probability of success per attempt:**

The probability that a given attempt encounters *no* competing attempt in the vulnerable window of length $(1 + a)$ is:

$$P_{\text{success}} = e^{-G(1+a)}$$

Wait ŌĆö this is the 1-persistent formula. For *non-persistent* CSMA, the derivation differs because the *vulnerability* window is *narrower*: a non-persistent station waits a random time, so it is less likely to collide with another station that sensed *it*. The derivation in *Forouzan* and *Stallings* gives the closed-form result as:

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expected busy period:**

The channel is "busy" for one of three reasons:

- *Successful transmission:* duration = $1$ (the frame time) + $2a$ (the propagation delay for the last bit to reach the far end and the ACK/collision signal to return) Ōēł $1 + 2a$ in frame-time units.
- *Collision:* duration = $1 + 2a$ as well (collision takes as long as a full frame plus propagation).
- *Idle gap:* with non-persistence, the channel sits idle for a random backoff interval when a station defers.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Frame-time normalisation and final throughput formula:**

After careful enumeration of all intervals (busy, idle, vulnerable), the final throughput expression for non-persistent CSMA emerges as:

$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$$

Let us verify boundary behaviour:

- *As $G \to 0$:* $S \approx \frac{G}{0 + 1} = G$ ŌĆö linear ramp-up, every attempt succeeds.
- *As $G \to \infty$:* $S \approx \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a)} = \frac{e^{-aG}}{1+2a} \to 0$ ŌĆö channel saturates with collisions.
- *At moderate $G$ and small $a$:* peak throughput approaches the Shannon-like limit of 1 (100% utilization).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö 1-Persistent CSMA Throughput (Qualitative Derivation)

The 1-persistent variant's behaviour is dominated by **collision clustering** at high load. The exact closed form requires the integral:

$$S = \frac{G e^{-G(1+a)} \cdot \left(1 + G + aG\right)}{G(1+a) + e^{-G(1+a)} - 1}$$

**Derivation Outline:**

1. The probability that the channel is sensed idle at the moment a station's frame is ready is $P_{\text{idle}} = e^{-G}$ (no attempt in the last $a$ window).
2. If the station transmits and *another* station was also waiting (with high probability under high load), a collision occurs after a time $a$ (the second signal's arrival at the first station).
3. After the collision, both stations sense the channel busy, wait for it to clear, and *both* attempt immediately ŌĆö causing a **collision cascade** at high $G$. This is why 1-persistent CSMA *collapses faster* than non-persistent CSMA at high load.

For the simplified KTU-level derivation, the **vulnerable period** for 1-persistent CSMA is $1 + a$ (own frame + round-trip propagation), and the *combined* probability of success is:

$$P_{\text{success}} = e^{-G(1+a)}$$

The throughput then follows from the standard $S = G \cdot P_{\text{success}} / E[\text{busy period}]$ relationship.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Vulnerable Time and Slot-Time Relationship in CSMA/CD

> [!NOTE]
> **Slot Time (Ethernet):** $\tau = 2 \times T_p$ (the time required for a signal to travel from one end of the maximum-segment network to the other and return)

CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) requires that *every* frame be of length at least $2 T_p$, otherwise the transmitting station finishes sending the frame *before* the collision signal returns, and the collision goes undetected. Hence the IEEE 802.3 minimum frame size constraint:

$$L_{\min} = 2 \cdot d \cdot R / v = 2 \cdot T_p \cdot R$$

For 10 Mbps Ethernet with maximum segment length 500 m, $T_p \approx 2.5 \mu s$ (using $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in coax), giving:

$$L_{\min} = 2 \times 2.5 \mu s \times 10 \text{ Mbps} = 50 \text{ bits} \rightarrow \text{padded to 64 bytes (512 bits)}$$

**Slot-time definition:**

$$\tau = 2 T_p = \frac{2 d}{v}$$

In 10BASE5, $\tau = 51.2 \mu s$ exactly (512 bits at 10 Mbps). In 10BASE-T, $\tau = 46.4 \mu s$ (rounded up to 51.2 ╬╝s for the protocol). The binary exponential backoff picks an integer $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 2^i - 1\}$ uniformly, where $i$ is the collision attempt number, and the station waits $k \cdot \tau$ seconds.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Comparison Table: Maximum Throughput vs. *a*

This is the most commonly asked 14-mark analytical question in KTU Module-2. We tabulate $S_{max}$ as a function of the parameter $a$ for the four key protocols:

| $a$ | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | 1-Persistent CSMA | Non-Persistent CSMA |
|-----|------------|----------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| 0.01 | 0.184 | 0.368 | Ōēł 0.529 | **Ōēł 0.815** |
| 0.1 | 0.184 | 0.368 | Ōēł 0.420 | **Ōēł 0.730** |
| 0.5 | 0.184 | 0.368 | Ōēł 0.250 | **Ōēł 0.586** |
| 1.0 | 0.184 | 0.368 | Ōēł 0.176 | **Ōēł 0.520** |
| 5.0 | 0.184 | 0.368 | Ōēł 0.045 | **Ōēł 0.297** |

**Engineering Insight (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** Even with $a = 1$ (poor conditions: long cable, short frames, slow link), non-persistent CSMA still outperforms pure ALOHA by a factor of nearly **3├Ś** in peak throughput. CSMA's advantage *grows* with smaller $a$ (i.e., as link speeds and frame sizes increase).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö 1-Persistent vs. Non-Persistent (KTU Essay Skeleton)

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark questions requiring comparison, structure your answer as follows:**

1. **State the rule of behaviour** for each strategy.
2. **Draw the throughput-vs-load curve qualitatively** ŌĆö 1-persistent peaks higher *at low G* but collapses faster; non-persistent peaks lower at low G but degrades more gracefully.
3. **Identify the vulnerable time** ŌĆö both are $2T_p$, but collision *clustering* differentiates them.
4. **Discuss idle vs. collision trade-off** ŌĆö 1-persistent trades idle time for collisions; non-persistent trades collisions for idle time.
5. **Conclude with engineering recommendation** ŌĆö non-persistent is preferred in high-load scenarios (e.g., sensor networks); 1-persistent is preferred in low-load scenarios (e.g., legacy Ethernet with low traffic).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Generic CSMA Station Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the internal functional blocks of a CSMA-capable node. This abstraction maps to the **MAC sublayer** of the OSI Data Link Layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upper Layer: Network IP Packet Arrives] --> B[Frame Assembly Block]
    B --> C{MAC Queue and Backoff Controller}
    C --> D[Carrier Sense Detector]
    D --> E{Channel State Decision}
    E -->|Channel Idle| F[Frame Transmitter]
    E -->|Channel Busy| G[Wait Strategy: 1 persistent / Non persistent / p persistent]
    G --> D
    F --> H{Bus Monitor and Collision Detector}
    H -->|No Collision| I[Transmission Success: ACK Received]
    H -->|Collision Detected CSMA CD| J[Jam Signal Generator]
    J --> K[Binary Exponential Backoff Counter]
    K --> C
    I --> L[Frame Delivered to Upper Layer]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram ŌĆö CSMA/CD Operational Lifecycle (IEEE 802.3)

The following diagram captures the complete lifecycle of a frame in **1-persistent CSMA/CD** as used in classical Ethernet, including the backoff state machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Idle State: Ready to Transmit] --> S1{Is Frame Ready?}
    S1 -->|No| S0
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Carrier Sense: Monitor the Medium]
    S2 --> S3{Is Channel Idle?}
    S3 -->|No: Busy| S2
    S3 -->|Yes: Idle| S4[Begin Transmitting Frame]
    S4 --> S5{Collision Detected during Transmission?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Continue Transmission to End of Frame]
    S6 --> S7[Wait for ACK: Success Path]
    S5 -->|Yes| S8[Abort Transmission Immediately]
    S8 --> S9[Transmit 32 bit Jam Signal]
    S9 --> S10[Increment Collision Attempt Counter: i = i + 1]
    S10 --> S11{i Less Than 16?}
    S11 -->|No| S12[Discard Frame: Report Error to Upper Layer]
    S11 -->|Yes| S13[Backoff Delay: Pick k from 0 to 2 to the i minus 1]
    S13 --> S14[Wait k times Slot Time]
    S14 --> S2
```

### 4.3 Comparison Diagram ŌĆö CSMA/CD vs. CSMA/CA

This diagram is critical for KTU Module 2 questions on wireless networks and the rationale for collision avoidance vs. detection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CSMA CD: Collision Detection] --> A1[Used in Wired Ethernet IEEE 802.3]
    A1 --> A2[Node Transmits and Simultaneously Monitors Voltage on Cable]
    A2 --> A3[If Collision Sensed: Abort and Jam]
    A3 --> A4[Requires Minimum Frame Size equal to 2 Tp]
    A4 --> A5[Efficiency: 1 divided by 1 plus 5.44 a approximate]

    B[CSMA CA: Collision Avoidance] --> B1[Used in Wireless IEEE 802.11 WiFi]
    B1 --> B2[Node Waits for DIFS, then Picks Random Backoff]
    B2 --> B3[Counts Down Backoff Only When Channel is Idle]
    B3 --> B4[Transmits RTS: Request to Send]
    B4 --> B5[Receiver Replies with CTS: Clear to Send]
    B5 --> B6[Other Nodes Defer on Hearing CTS]
    B6 --> B7[Optional ACK confirms successful reception]
```

The following Mermaid block approximates the classic *throughput vs. offered load* comparison plot for the major random-access protocols. Although Mermaid cannot render true Cartesian plots, the block-level summary captures the qualitative behaviour tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a]**

**Q1.** *What is CSMA? Differentiate between 1-persistent and non-persistent CSMA.*

**Model Answer:**

CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access) is a contention-based MAC protocol in which a node **senses the carrier** on the shared medium *before* transmitting, to avoid interfering with an ongoing transmission. A node that senses the channel busy defers its transmission according to a *persistence* strategy.

| Property | 1-Persistent CSMA | Non-Persistent CSMA |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Channel-sensing behaviour | Continuous sensing until idle | Sense once, then back off if busy |
| Action when busy | Wait until idle, transmit *immediately* (prob = 1) | Wait *random* time, re-sense |
| Collision probability | **High** at high load | **Low** |
| Channel idle time | **Zero** (always immediately exploits idle) | Non-zero (backoff-induced idle periods) |
| Best suited for | Low-load networks | High-load networks |

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q2.b]**

**Q2.** *Define vulnerable time in CSMA and explain why it equals $2T_p$.*

**Model Answer:**

The **vulnerable time** in CSMA is the window during which a frame collision *can still occur* even with perfect carrier sensing. It equals **$2 T_p$**, where $T_p$ is the one-way propagation delay, because the worst-case collision scenario involves two stations at the *opposite ends* of the medium: Station A begins transmitting, but the signal takes $T_p$ to reach Station B. If B senses the channel just *before* A's signal arrives, B may begin its own transmission, and the resulting collision signal then takes another $T_p$ to return to A. Thus A's frame is vulnerable to corruption for a window of length $2 T_p$ ŌĆö much smaller than ALOHA's vulnerable time of $T_t$ (the full frame duration).

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2, Q3]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the three persistence strategies of CSMA in detail. Draw a comparison of their throughput versus offered load characteristics for the same value of $a$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A network uses CSMA/CD with a 1 Gbps data rate over a 250 m cable. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s and the average frame length is 1500 bytes. Calculate: (i) the parameter $a$, (ii) the minimum frame size, and (iii) the slot time. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) The Three CSMA Persistence Strategies:**

1. **1-Persistent CSMA:**
   - Rule: A node wishing to transmit continuously senses the channel. The moment the channel becomes idle, it transmits *with probability 1* (i.e., immediately).
   - Behaviour: Zero idle channel time, but *high collision probability* at moderate-to-high load because multiple waiting stations all transmit the instant the channel clears.
   - Throughput curve: Rises *steeply* at low $G$, peaks at moderate $G$ (around 0.5 for small $a$), then *collapses rapidly* at high $G$ due to collision cascades.

2. **Non-Persistent CSMA:**
   - Rule: A node that senses the channel busy waits for a *random backoff time*, then re-senses. It does *not* sense continuously.
   - Behaviour: Lower collision probability (the randomisation desynchronises waiting stations), but *non-zero* idle channel time during backoff.
   - Throughput curve: Rises slightly slower than 1-persistent at low $G$ (because of the occasional backoff-induced idle gap), peaks at slightly lower $S$ at moderate $G$, but *degrades gracefully* at high $G$ ŌĆö outperforming 1-persistent when load is heavy.

3. **p-Persistent CSMA (slotted):**
   - Rule: Used in *slotted* channels. After sensing an idle slot, the node transmits *with probability $p$* and defers to the next slot *with probability $(1-p)$*. If it defers and the next slot is idle, it again chooses $p$.
   - Behaviour: A tunable hybrid between 1-persistent ($p \to 1$) and non-persistent ($p \to 0$). Slotted synchronisation prevents some collisions but adds slot-alignment overhead.
   - Throughput curve: Lies between 1-persistent and non-persistent, with a *smoother* shape.

**Comparison for $a = 0.1$ (typical Ethernet case):**

| Strategy | Peak $S_{max}$ | Behaviour at high $G$ |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1-Persistent | Ōēł 0.42 | Collapses rapidly |
| Non-Persistent | Ōēł 0.73 | Degrades gracefully (best) |
| p-Persistent | Tunable, Ōēł 0.55 for $p=0.3$ | Smooth degradation |

(7 marks: 1 mark for each strategy explanation, 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the comparison summary, 1 mark for a labelled sketch of the curves.)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9$ bps
- $d = 250$ m
- $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $L = 1500$ bytes $= 1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits

**(i) Calculation of the parameter $a$:**

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{250}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.25 \ \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^9} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 12 \ \mu\text{s}$$

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{1.25 \ \mu s}{12 \ \mu s} = 0.1042$$

**(ii) Minimum frame size:**

The minimum frame must satisfy $L_{\min} = 2 \times T_p \times R$:

$$L_{\min} = 2 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^9 = 2500 \text{ bits} = 312.5 \text{ bytes}$$

The 1500-byte average frame *comfortably exceeds* this minimum, so collision detection works correctly for typical frames.

**(iii) Slot time:**

$$\tau = 2 T_p = 2 \times 1.25 \ \mu s = 2.5 \ \mu s$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) with explanation of the minimum-frame-size rule, 2 marks for (iii).)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of **CSMA/CD** as used in classical Ethernet. How does it differ from **CSMA/CA** used in WiFi? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the throughput expression of **non-persistent CSMA** in terms of the offered load $G$ and the parameter $a$. Discuss the boundary behaviour as $G \to 0$ and $G \to \infty$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution Outline to Question B:**

**(a)** *Working of CSMA/CD* ŌĆö refer to Section 4.2 (the flow diagram and the operational steps listed there). The student should describe:

- Frame assembly, carrier sensing, immediate transmission on idle, collision detection by monitoring voltage on the cable, jam signal transmission, binary exponential backoff ($k$ from $\{0, \dots, 2^i-1\}$), retry up to 16 times.

*CSMA/CD vs. CSMA/CA* ŌĆö refer to Section 4.3 diagram. Key points:

| Aspect | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Medium | Wired (Ethernet) | Wireless (WiFi) |
| Detects collision? | **Yes** (voltage change) | **No** (cannot sense during transmission) |
| Strategy | Transmit, then detect & abort | **Avoid** collisions by RTS/CTS and backoff |
| Hidden node problem | Not relevant | **Yes**, solved by RTS/CTS |
| Minimum frame size? | Yes ($= 2 T_p \cdot R$) | No such requirement (whole-frame ACK) |
| Efficiency | Ōēł $\frac{1}{1 + 5.44 a}$ | Lower, Ōēł 0.5ŌĆō0.7 typical |

**(b)** Refer to Section 3.1 for the complete derivation. The student should present:

- The Poisson assumption, the probability $P_{\text{success}} = e^{-aG}$ for non-persistent, the busy-period analysis, the final closed-form $S = \frac{G e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$.
- Boundary behaviour: $S \to G$ as $G \to 0$ (every attempt succeeds), and $S \to 0$ as $G \to \infty$ (channel saturated with collisions).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on CSMA questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA** ŌĆö students often write "WiFi uses CSMA/CD" or "Ethernet uses CSMA/CA". Ethernet 802.3 ŌåÆ **CD**, WiFi 802.11 ŌåÆ **CA**. (Lose 2 marks immediately.)
> 2. **Wrong formula for $a$** ŌĆö some students write $a = T_t / T_p$ instead of $T_p / T_t$. Always verify: a *small* $a$ means *fast* transmission relative to propagation.
> 3. **Forgetting the minimum-frame-size constraint in CSMA/CD** ŌĆö the rule $L_{\min} = 2 T_p \cdot R$ is *frequently* asked as a sub-part (3 marks). Omitting it loses easy marks.
> 4. **Mixing up vulnerable time** ŌĆö ALOHA's vulnerable time is $T_t$; CSMA's is $2T_p$; slotted ALOHA's is $T_t$ (still, because of the slot). Memorise this trio.
> 5. **Drawing the throughput curves without labels** ŌĆö KTU examiners want axis labels ($G$ on $x$, $S$ on $y$), curve names, and a clear peak point with numerical value. Unlabelled diagrams lose 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the Poisson assumption** ŌĆö when deriving the throughput formula, always state that new frame arrivals are Poisson. Skipping this loses 1 mark for "missing assumption".
