### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for CSMA:**

- Ō£ģ CSMA = *Carrier Sense Multiple Access*: listen before you speak.
- Ō£ģ Vulnerable time in CSMA = $2 T_p$ (NOT $T_t$ as in ALOHA).
- Ō£ģ Key parameter: $a = T_p / T_t = (R \cdot d) / (v \cdot L)$.
- Ō£ģ **1-Persistent** ŌåÆ continuous sensing, transmit on idle with probability 1 ŌåÆ high collisions at high load.
- Ō£ģ **Non-Persistent** ŌåÆ random backoff after busy ŌåÆ low collisions, some idle time.
- Ō£ģ **p-Persistent** ŌåÆ slotted, transmits with probability $p$ ŌåÆ tunable hybrid.
- Ō£ģ **CSMA/CD** ŌåÆ *Collision Detection* (used in wired Ethernet IEEE 802.3) ŌåÆ minimum frame size = $2 T_p \cdot R$.
- Ō£ģ **CSMA/CA** ŌåÆ *Collision Avoidance* (used in WiFi IEEE 802.11) ŌåÆ uses RTS/CTS and binary exponential backoff.
- Ō£ģ Slot time in CSMA/CD: $\tau = 2 T_p$ (used as the unit of backoff).
- Ō£ģ Binary exponential backoff: pick $k$ from $\{0, 1, \dots, 2^i - 1\}$ after the $i$-th collision, wait $k \cdot \tau$.
- Ō£ģ Pure ALOHA peak throughput: **18.4%**; Slotted ALOHA: **36.8%**; Non-persistent CSMA: **up to ~80%** (small $a$).
- Ō£ģ WiFi *cannot* use CSMA/CD because of the *near-far problem* and *hidden terminal problem* ŌĆö hence CSMA/CA.
- Ō£ģ Jam signal in CSMA/CD: 32 bits, ensures all stations detect the collision.
- Ō£ģ Maximum retry limit: **16 attempts** in Ethernet; after which the frame is dropped and an error is reported to the upper layer.
- Ō£ģ Modern full-duplex switched Ethernet does *not* need CSMA/CD ŌĆö collision domains are isolated by switches.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Free Protocols. Ethernet - Switched Ethernet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collision Free Protocols. Ethernet - Switched Ethernet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Collision-Free Protocols ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Collision-Free Protocol** is a class of Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer protocols used in local and metropolitan area networks that **guarantee the absence of data collisions** on a shared broadcast channel during the contention resolution and data transmission phases. Unlike contention-based protocols (e.g., ALOHA, CSMA/CD) where multiple stations may transmit simultaneously causing collisions, collision-free protocols use either **reservation-based scheduling**, **binary contention arbitration**, or **physical/logical channel partitioning** to ensure that only one station transmits successfully at any given instant.

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies these protocols under the umbrella of **Channel Access Protocols (Data Link Layer ŌĆö MAC Sublayer)** and emphasizes the following canonical implementations:

1. **Bit-Map Protocol** (a.k.a. *Reservation Protocol*)
2. **Binary Countdown Protocol**
3. **Limited Contention Protocols** (e.g., *Adaptive Tree Walk Protocol*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to *compare and contrast* reservation-based protocols with contention-based protocols, derive the **maximum channel utilization** ($S_{\max}$), and analyze the **fairness index** for each scheme.

### 1.2 Ethernet and Switched Ethernet ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Ethernet** is a family of wired LAN technologies standardized under **IEEE 802.3** that defines the physical layer (PHY) and the Media Access Control (MAC) sublayer specifications. It uses **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** as its foundational channel access mechanism, supports variable-size frames, and has evolved across multiple speed grades (**10 Mbps ŌåÆ 10 Gbps ŌåÆ 100 Gbps**).

**Switched Ethernet** is the modern architectural evolution of Ethernet wherein a central device ŌĆö an **Ethernet Switch** (a multi-port bridge) ŌĆö replaces the shared bus or hub topology. Each host is connected to the switch via a **dedicated point-to-point full-duplex link**, eliminating the shared medium, eradicating collisions (in full-duplex mode), and providing **microsegmentation** with isolated collision domains per port.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Conference Room" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (for Collision-Free Protocols):** Imagine **8 professors** queuing to use **one projector**. In a *collision-prone* room, multiple professors grab the remote simultaneously (a collision), wasting time. In a **Bit-Map room**, a registrar walks down the line, and each professor shouts "YES (1)" or "NO (0)" in turn ŌĆö the ones who said YES then speak **in order, with no clashes**. In a **Binary Countdown room**, each professor declares their unique 3-bit ID code aloud at the same time; the loudest (highest-order bit) wins. The room stays perfectly orderly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (for Switched Ethernet):** Think of a **modern office building** with private telephone lines from each desk to a central **PBX switch**. In old "shared Ethernet" (hub), all desks were on a single loudspeaker (anyone hears everyone ŌĆö chaos and eavesdropping). In **Switched Ethernet**, the PBX connects **only the two parties** who need to talk, privately, simultaneously, with zero interruption. This is the *microsegmentation* idea.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Ethernet Minimum Frame Size | **64 bytes** |
| Ethernet Maximum Frame Size | **1518 bytes** |
| Original Ethernet Speed | **10 Mbps** |
| Maximum Cable Length (10BASE5) | **500 meters** |
| Slot Time (10 Mbps Ethernet) | **51.2 ┬Ąs** |
| Interframe Gap (IFG) | **96 bit-times** |
| Switch Forwarding Modes | **Store-and-Forward, Cut-Through, Fragment-Free** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bit-Map Protocol (Reservation Protocol)

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown):**

- The protocol operates in **discrete contention periods**, each subdivided into **N mini-slots** (where N = number of stations).
- If a station $i$ has a frame ready to transmit, it broadcasts a **"1" bit** in mini-slot $i$; otherwise, it broadcasts **"0"**.
- After all N mini-slots are exhausted, **every station knows exactly which stations wish to transmit** (the map is now globally known).
- Transmission then proceeds in **station-number order** (1, 2, 3, ..., N), giving strict priority to lower-numbered stations.
- The contention period overhead is **N bits per frame-transmission cycle**.

**Why it works:** Since the bit map is broadcast and globally observed, no two stations can both be granted the channel simultaneously ŌĆö collisions are mathematically impossible.

**How it works:** It uses a **two-phase access method**: (1) Reservation phase ŌåÆ (2) Data transmission phase.

**Performance Analysis:**

Assume each frame has a constant transmission time of **T** seconds, and the contention period occupies **d** seconds.

$$
\text{Channel Utilization } S = \frac{\text{Useful Transmission Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{T}{T + d}
$$

For N stations, where **d = N bit-times** in light load (only one station wants to send):

$$
S_{\text{low}} = \frac{T}{T + N \cdot t_b}
$$

where $t_b$ is the bit duration. In **high load** (all N stations always ready):

$$
S_{\text{high}} = \frac{N \cdot T}{N \cdot T + N \cdot t_b} = \frac{T}{T + t_b} \approx 1 \text{ (as } t_b \to 0\text{)}
$$

### 2.2 Binary Countdown Protocol

**Operational Logic:**

- Each station is assigned a **unique binary address** of length $\log_2 N$ bits.
- A station wishing to transmit **broadcasts its entire address** (bit-by-bit, MSB first) onto the shared channel.
- The channel performs a **logical OR** of all simultaneously broadcast bits ŌĆö if any station transmits a 1 in position $k$, all stations see a 1.
- **Rule:** If a station sees a 1 in any bit position higher than its own 1, **it must immediately stop transmitting** (it has lost the arbitration).
- The station whose address has the highest numerical value (or whose leftmost 1 is leftmost-positioned) wins and proceeds with full data transmission.

**Why it works:** It uses a **distributed priority arbitration** scheme ŌĆö addresses with higher values have inherent higher priority.

**How it works:** It exploits the **analog-OR property** of the physical medium: the bit-wise OR of multiple transmissions equals the logical OR, providing instantaneous global feedback.

**Advantage over Bit-Map:** Only $\log_2 N$ bits are needed per arbitration, regardless of N stations' contention status.

### 2.3 Limited Contention Protocols

A hybrid class designed to combine the **low delay of contention-based protocols at light load** with the **high efficiency of collision-free protocols at high load**. Canonical example: **Adaptive Tree Walk Protocol**.

- Stations are organized in a **binary tree** structure.
- Under light load, only leaf-level stations contend; under heavy load, the entire subtree is searched.
- The depth of search **adapts dynamically** to the estimated number of ready stations.

### 2.4 Ethernet ŌĆö IEEE 802.3 Architecture

**Layered Position:**

$$
\boxed{\text{OSI Layer 2 (Data Link)}} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{LLC Sublayer (802.2)} \\ \text{MAC Sublayer (802.3)} \end{cases} \rightarrow \text{PHY (802.3)}
$$

**CSMA/CD Protocol ŌĆö Operational Logic:**

1. **Carrier Sense:** A station listens to the medium; if idle, it may transmit.
2. **Collision Detection:** While transmitting, the station monitors for signal energy anomalies.
3. **Collision Detection Time Constraint:** The station must continue transmitting for at least **$2 \tau$** (round-trip propagation delay) to ensure any collision is detected.
4. **Jam Signal:** Upon collision, a 32-bit jam signal is broadcast.
5. **Binary Exponential Backoff:** The station waits a random number of slot times before retrying, where:
   - Slot time = **51.2 ┬Ąs** (for 10 Mbps Ethernet)
   - Random selection from $\{0, 1, ..., 2^k - 1\}$, where $k = \min(n, 10)$ and $n$ = number of collisions.
   - Maximum retries = **16**.

### 2.5 Switched Ethernet ŌĆö Architecture

**Core Components:**

- **Ethernet Switch:** A multi-port Layer 2 device with a high-speed backplane (e.g., **backplane capacity Ōēź sum of all port speeds**).
- **MAC Address Table (Forwarding Table):** Maps each MAC address to the corresponding switch port.
- **Three Forwarding Modes:**
  1. **Store-and-Forward:** Complete frame received, CRC checked, then forwarded.
  2. **Cut-Through:** Forwarding begins as soon as the destination MAC is read (~first 6 bytes).
  3. **Fragment-Free:** Special cut-through variant that waits for the first **64 bytes** (collision window) before forwarding.

**Microsegmentation:** Each port of the switch is its **own dedicated collision domain**. Since full-duplex operation is used, **collisions are physically impossible** in Switched Ethernet.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains KTU high-yield formulas. Commit to memory for ESE.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bit-Map Utilization (low load) | $S = \dfrac{T}{T + N \cdot t_b}$ | T = frame time, $t_b$ = bit time, N = stations | Worst case: 1 station waits full N-bit cycle |
| Bit-Map Utilization (high load) | $S = \dfrac{T}{T + t_b}$ | ŌĆö | Approaches unity as $t_b \to 0$ |
| Binary Countdown bits needed | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | N = number of stations | Always $\leq$ Bit-Map overhead |
| Minimum Frame Size | $F_{\min} = R \cdot 2 \tau_{\max}$ | R = bit rate, $\tau_{\max}$ = max one-way propagation | Ensures collision detection |
| Slot Time | $T_{\text{slot}} = 512 \text{ bit times}$ | ŌĆö | = 51.2 ┬Ąs @ 10 Mbps |
| Backoff Range | $r \in [0, 2^k - 1]$, $k = \min(n,10)$ | n = collision count | Binary exponential backoff |
| Max Backoff Delay | $T_{\max} = 1023 \cdot T_{\text{slot}}$ | ŌĆö | After 10 collisions |
| Switch Aggregate Bandwidth | $B_{\text{agg}} = N \cdot R$ | N = ports, R = port rate | Non-blocking switch requirement |
| Frame Transmission Time | $T_f = \dfrac{L \cdot 8}{R}$ | L = frame length (bytes), R = bit rate | seconds |
| Channel Efficiency (CSMA/CD) | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.4 \cdot d / L}$ | d = max propagation distance, L = frame bits | Simplified approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Collision-free protocols are foundational to **industrial control networks (PROFIBUS, Token Ring)** and **satellite MAC design**. Switched Ethernet is the literal **backbone of every cloud data center, enterprise LAN, and modern 5G fronthaul** ŌĆö your Wi-Fi router uses a switch chip internally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Channel Utilization for Bit-Map Protocol

**Given:**
- N = 8 stations
- Frame length L = 800 bits
- Bit duration $t_b = 1 \mu s$

**Step 1: Identify the cycle components**

A full cycle in Bit-Map consists of:
- **Contention period (map)**: N bit-slots = 8 ├Ś $t_b$ = 8 ┬Ąs
- **Data transmission phase**: T = L ├Ś $t_b$ = 800 ┬Ąs (assuming only 1 station transmits in low load)

**Step 2: Total cycle time**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot t_b + T
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = (8)(1 \mu s) + 800 \mu s = 808 \mu s
$$

**Step 4: Compute utilization**

$$
S = \frac{T}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{800 \mu s}{808 \mu s} = 0.9901
$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage**

$$
S = 99.01\%
$$

**Conclusion:** With one waiting station and a 100 ┬Ąs map overhead, utilization is ~99%. The 1% overhead is the reservation cost.

### 3.2 Derivation: Binary Countdown Arbitration ŌĆö Worked Example

**Given:** 4 stations with binary addresses:
- Station A: 0010
- Station B: 0100
- Station C: 1000
- Station D: 0110

All four wish to transmit simultaneously.

**Step 1: First bit (MSB) is broadcast**

All four stations transmit bit-3 (MSB):
- A: 0
- B: 0
- C: 1
- D: 0

OR result observed on channel = **1**

**Step 2: Each station compares**

Only Station C transmitted a 1 in the MSB; all others transmitted 0. Therefore, Stations A, B, and D see a "1" in a position where they sent "0" and **immediately withdraw**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Stations A, B, D cease transmission; Station C wins the arbitration.}}
$$

**Step 3: Station C transmits its frame**

No collision occurs. The arbitration cost was only $\log_2 4 = 2$ bit-times (resolution can stop once C's 1 is confirmed). Total arbitration time: 4 bit-times max, but typically $\log_2 N$ bit-times on average.

**Step 4: Compute utilization**

If each frame is 1000 bits, total cycle = 4 bits (arbitration) + 1000 bits (data) = 1004 bits.

$$
S = \frac{1000}{1004} = 0.996
$$

This is markedly better than Bit-Map under heavy load.

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size Constraint for Ethernet

**Given:**
- Max cable length = 500 m (10BASE5)
- Signal propagation speed Ōēł 2 ├Ś $10^8$ m/s (in coax)
- Data rate = 10 Mbps

**Step 1: Compute one-way propagation delay**

$$
\tau = \frac{500 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 2.5 \mu s
$$

**Step 2: Compute round-trip propagation delay**

$$
2 \tau = 5.0 \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Compute minimum frame size in bits**

For collision detection, the transmitting station must be on the air for at least $2\tau$ seconds.

$$
F_{\min} = R \times 2\tau = (10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}) \times (5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}) = 50 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Round up to nearest multiple of 8 (byte boundary)**

$$
F_{\min} = 64 \text{ bytes} = 512 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5: Reconcile with slot time**

The slot time is defined as $T_{\text{slot}} = 51.2 \mu s$, which is the time to transmit 512 bits at 10 Mbps.

$$
T_{\text{slot}} = \frac{512 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 51.2 \mu s
$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum frame size of **64 bytes (512 bits)** is the IEEE 802.3 standard, derived from the requirement that the first bit of the frame must propagate to the far end of the maximum network and a collision bit must return before the sender finishes transmission.

### 3.4 Derivation: Switched Ethernet Aggregate Throughput

**Given:**
- 24-port switch
- Each port: 1 Gbps full-duplex

**Step 1: Per-port full-duplex throughput**

Each port can simultaneously send AND receive at 1 Gbps.

$$
R_{\text{port}} = 1 \text{ Gbps (send)} + 1 \text{ Gbps (receive)} = 2 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 2: Worst-case aggregate bandwidth (all ports active)**

$$
B_{\text{agg}} = 24 \times 2 \text{ Gbps} = 48 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 3: Required switch backplane capacity (non-blocking)**

For a non-blocking switch:

$$
B_{\text{backplane}} \geq 48 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** A modern 24-port Gigabit switch requires a backplane of at least 48 Gbps. Most enterprise switches advertise "96 Gbps backplane" to provide headroom for full non-blocking operation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Collision-Free vs. Contention-Based Protocols

| Property | Bit-Map | Binary Countdown | CSMA/CD (Ethernet) | Switched Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Collision Possible? | **No** | **No** | **Yes** | **No (Full-Duplex)** |
| Arbitration Overhead | N bits | $\log_2 N$ bits | Variable (backoff) | None |
| Channel Utilization (Max) | ~100% (high load) | ~100% (high load) | ~80-90% | ~100% |
| Delay at Low Load | High (N bits wait) | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| Fairness | Unfair (priority by ID) | Unfair (priority by address) | Probabilistic Fair | Strict Per-Port |
| Implementation Complexity | Low | Medium | Low | High (switch fabric) |
| KTU Exam Weight | **High** | **High** | **High** | **Very High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bit-Map Protocol ŌĆö Frame Reservation Cycle

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the operational flow of the Bit-Map protocol across N = 8 stations, showing the contention period (8 mini-slots) followed by the sequential data transmission phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start of Cycle]) --> B[Contention Period: 8 Mini-Slots]
    B --> C{Station 1: 0 or 1?}
    C -->|0| D{Station 2: 0 or 1?}
    D -->|0| E{Station 3: 0 or 1?}
    E -->|0| F{Station 4: 0 or 1?}
    F -->|0| G{Station 5: 0 or 1?}
    G -->|0| H{Station 6: 0 or 1?}
    H -->|0| I{Station 7: 0 or 1?}
    I -->|0| J{Station 8: 0 or 1?}
    J -->|0| K[All stations idle - cycle restarts]
    J -->|1| L[Stations 1 to 8 Transmit in Order]
    L --> M[All frames sent - cycle restarts]
    K --> A
    M --> A
    C -->|1| L
    D -->|1| L
    E -->|1| L
    F -->|1| L
    G -->|1| L
    H -->|1| L
    I -->|1| L
```

### 4.2 Binary Countdown Protocol ŌĆö Arbitration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([All stations broadcast MSB of address]) --> B{Check Channel OR Result}
    B -->|Result is 0| C[All bits were 0 - check next bit]
    B -->|Result is 1| D{Is my bit in this position 1?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Keep transmitting - go to next lower bit]
    D -->|No| F[WITHDRAW from arbitration]
    E --> G{More bits to check?}
    G -->|Yes| A
    G -->|No| H[WINNER: Transmit data frame]
    F --> I([Wait for next cycle])
    C --> G
    H --> J([Data transmission completes])
    J --> K([Cycle restarts])
```

### 4.3 IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame Format ŌĆö Bit-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Preamble: 7 bytes 10101010...] --> S[SFD: 1 byte 10101011]
    S --> DA[Destination MAC: 6 bytes]
    DA --> SA[Source MAC: 6 bytes]
    SA --> L[Length/Type: 2 bytes]
    L --> DT[Data and Payload: 46 to 1500 bytes]
    DT --> PAD[Pad: 0 to 46 bytes if needed]
    PAD --> FCS[Frame Check Sequence CRC-32: 4 bytes]
    FCS --> END([Frame Ends - IFG 96 bit-times before next])
```

**Field-by-Field Architecture Mapping:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FRAME[IEEE 802.3 Standard Frame]
        direction LR
        A1[Preamble 7B] --> A2[SFD 1B] --> A3[DA 6B] --> A4[SA 6B] --> A5[Len 2B] --> A6[Payload 46-1500B] --> A7[Pad 0-46B] --> A8[FCS 4B]
    end
    A3 -.->|Hardware Address| B1[Identifies Receiver]
    A4 -.->|Hardware Address| B2[Identifies Sender]
    A5 -.->|802.3: Length| B3[Bytes in Payload]
    A5 -.->|Ethernet II: Type| B4[E.g. 0x0800 IPv4]
    A8 -.->|CRC-32 Polynomial| B5[Error Detection Code]
```

### 4.4 Switched Ethernet ŌĆö Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SW[24-Port Gigabit Ethernet Switch - Non-Blocking Backplane 48 Gbps]
        direction TB
        BC[Backplane Matrix 48 Gbps] --> FT[Forwarding Table: MAC to Port Mapping]
        FT --> FM{Forwarding Mode?}
        FM -->|Store and Forward| SF[Wait for full frame then CRC check]
        FM -->|Cut Through| CT[Forward after reading DA only - 6 bytes]
        FM -->|Fragment Free| FF[Forward after first 64 bytes]
        SF --> OUT1[Output to Destination Port]
        CT --> OUT1
        FF --> OUT1
    end
    H1[Host A Port 1] -->|1 Gbps Full Duplex| BC
    H2[Host B Port 2] -->|1 Gbps Full Duplex| BC
    H3[Host C Port 3] -->|1 Gbps Full Duplex| BC
    H4[Host N Port 24] -->|1 Gbps Full Duplex| BC
    BC --> H1
    BC --> H2
    BC --> H3
    BC --> H4
```

### 4.5 CSMA/CD ŌĆö Collision Detection and Backoff State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CarrierSense: Frame Ready to Send
    CarrierSense --> Transmitting: Medium Idle
    CarrierSense --> Defer: Medium Busy
    Defer --> CarrierSense: Wait for IFG
    Transmitting --> CollisionDetected: Signal Anomaly
    Transmitting --> FrameSent: Transmission Complete
    CollisionDetected --> JamSignal: Send 32 bit Jam
    JamSignal --> Backoff: Compute Random r in 0 to 2k minus 1
    Backoff --> CarrierSense: Wait r times Slot Time
    Backoff --> Backoff: Increment k and retry
    FrameSent --> [*]: Success
    Backoff --> Drop: 16 Collisions - Frame Discarded
    Drop --> [*]
```

### 4.6 Comparison: Shared Ethernet Hub vs. Switched Ethernet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HUB[Shared Ethernet Hub - One Collision Domain]
        H1A[Host A] --> HUB_NODE[Hub]
        H2A[Host B] --> HUB_NODE
        H3A[Host C] --> HUB_NODE
        H4A[Host D] --> HUB_NODE
        HUB_NODE -.->|Broadcast to all| H1A
        HUB_NODE -.->|Broadcast to all| H2A
        HUB_NODE -.->|Broadcast to all| H3A
        HUB_NODE -.->|Broadcast to all| H4A
    end
    subgraph SWITCH[Switched Ethernet - 4 Separate Collision Domains]
        S1A[Host A] --> SW1[Switch Port 1]
        S2A[Host B] --> SW2[Switch Port 2]
        S3A[Host C] --> SW3[Switch Port 3]
        S4A[Host D] --> SW4[Switch Port 4]
        SW1 <-->|Dedicated Full Duplex Link| SW2
        SW3 <-->|Dedicated Full Duplex Link| SW4
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*Compare and contrast the Bit-Map Protocol and the Binary Countdown Protocol in terms of channel access mechanism, overhead, and fairness.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Bit-Map Protocol | Binary Countdown |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Method** | Reservation via N-bit contention map | Bit-wise address arbitration |
| **Overhead** | N bits per cycle (worst case) | $\log_2 N$ bits per cycle |
| **Fairness** | Strictly fair to lower-numbered stations | Biased toward higher addresses |
| **Efficiency at High Load** | ~100% | ~100% |
| **Efficiency at Low Load** | Poor (N-bit wait even for one station) | Better (low fixed overhead) |

**Key distinction:** Bit-Map is **explicitly fair** in station ordering, while Binary Countdown provides **priority-based access** based on address values. Both guarantee no collisions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*With a neat diagram, explain the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format. List all fields and their standard sizes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The IEEE 802.3 standard frame consists of the following fields in sequence:

1. **Preamble** (7 bytes): Pattern `10101010...` used for receiver clock synchronization.
2. **Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)** (1 byte): Pattern `10101011` marks the start of the frame.
3. **Destination Address (DA)** (6 bytes): MAC address of the recipient.
4. **Source Address (SA)** (6 bytes): MAC address of the sender.
5. **Length/Type** (2 bytes): Specifies payload length (802.3) or protocol type (Ethernet II).
6. **Data and Payload** (46 ŌĆō 1500 bytes): Upper-layer data; minimum 46 bytes enforced.
7. **Pad** (0 ŌĆō 46 bytes): Used to meet the 64-byte minimum frame size.
8. **Frame Check Sequence (FCS)** (4 bytes): CRC-32 for error detection.

**Total frame size:** 64 bytes (minimum) to 1518 bytes (maximum, excluding preamble/SFD).

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of the **Binary Countdown Protocol**. Show the arbitration process when four stations with addresses `1000`, `0101`, `1100`, and `0011` contend simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages of **Switched Ethernet** over **Shared Ethernet** (Hub-based). Explain the concept of **microsegmentation** and how it eliminates collisions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Binary Countdown Protocol Operation:**

In Binary Countdown, each station that has a frame to send broadcasts its **unique binary address** starting from the most significant bit (MSB). The channel logically **OR-s** all transmitted bits. If a station sees a `1` on the channel in a position where it sent a `0`, it has lost the arbitration and **withdraws** immediately. The last remaining station wins and transmits its frame collision-free.

**Arbitration Trace for the four given stations:**

| Bit Position (MSB ŌåÆ LSB) | 1000 | 0101 | 1100 | 0011 | OR Result | Surviving Stations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bit 3 (8's place) | **1** | 0 | **1** | 0 | **1** | 1000, 1100 |
| Bit 2 (4's place) | 0 | **1** | **1** | 0 | **1** | 0101, 1100 |
| Bit 1 (2's place) | 0 | 0 | **1** | **1** | **1** | 1100, 0011 |
| Bit 0 (1's place) | 0 | **1** | 0 | **1** | **1** | ŌĆö |

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Bit 3 arbitration:** Stations 1000 and 1100 both send 1; stations 0101 and 0011 send 0. Stations 0101 and 0011 see a "1" in their 0 position ŌåÆ **WITHDRAW**.
2. **Bit 2 arbitration:** Surviving stations 1000 and 1100 continue. 1000 sends 0; 1100 sends 1. Station 1000 sees 1 in its 0 position ŌåÆ **WITHDRAW**.
3. **Bit 1 arbitration:** Station 1100 vs 0011. 1100 sends 1; 0011 sends 1. Both still alive.
4. **Bit 0 arbitration:** 1100 sends 0; 0011 sends 1. Station 1100 sees 1 in its 0 position ŌåÆ **WITHDRAW**.

**Result: Station `0011` (decimal 3) wins the arbitration and transmits its frame collision-free.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always show the *bit-by-bit* trace table ŌĆö this is the marking scheme's expected answer. Skipping the table loses up to 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Shared Ethernet (Hub-based) vs Switched Ethernet:**

| Feature | Shared Ethernet (Hub) | Switched Ethernet |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Single shared bus / hub | Star ŌĆö point-to-point links to switch |
| **Collision Domain** | One for all stations | One per port (microsegmentation) |
| **Bandwidth** | Shared (10 Mbps for all) | Dedicated per port (10/100/1000 Mbps) |
| **Mode** | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| **Collisions** | Possible (CSMA/CD) | **Impossible** (full-duplex) |
| **Aggregate Throughput** | Limited by medium | Sum of all port speeds |
| **Latency** | Higher (contention) | Lower (direct forwarding) |

**Microsegmentation ŌĆö Conceptual Explanation:**

*Microsegmentation* is the process of dividing a shared LAN into **individual point-to-point segments**, each connecting a single host directly to a switch port. Each segment becomes its own **isolated collision domain**, meaning that transmissions and collisions on one segment do not affect any other segment.

**How it eliminates collisions:**

1. In a **shared hub**, all stations are in **one collision domain**; if two transmit at once, signals collide on the shared medium.
2. In a **switched Ethernet**, each port has a **dedicated full-duplex link**. The switch can simultaneously transmit and receive on each port.
3. **CSMA/CD is no longer required** because there is no contention ŌĆö the sender is the sole user of its link.
4. The switch uses a **MAC address table** to forward frames only to the intended destination port (except for broadcasts/unknown unicasts, which are flooded).

**Engineering Benefit:** A 24-port Gigabit switch can theoretically deliver **24 Gbps of simultaneous throughput**, versus only 1 Gbps aggregate on a shared hub.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse *collision domain* with *broadcast domain*. **Each switch port = ONE collision domain**, but the **entire VLAN = ONE broadcast domain** (unless routers or VLANs are used). Mark deduction is common for this mistake.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bit-Map (Reservation) Protocol** with a neat timing diagram. Derive the expression for **channel utilization** and compute the maximum efficiency when N = 16 stations are all ready to transmit frames of 1000 bits each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the architecture of an **Ethernet Switch**. Explain the three forwarding modes: **Store-and-Forward, Cut-Through, and Fragment-Free**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Bit-Map Protocol ŌĆö Operation:**

The Bit-Map Protocol is a **collision-free, reservation-based MAC protocol** operating in two phases:

**Phase 1 ŌĆö Contention (Reservation) Phase:**
- Time is divided into **N mini-slots** (one per station).
- Station $i$ transmits a `1` in mini-slot $i$ if it has a frame to send; otherwise, it transmits `0`.
- All N stations observe the bit map after N bit-times.

**Phase 2 ŌĆö Data Transmission Phase:**
- Stations that broadcast `1` transmit their frames **in numerical order** (1, 2, 3, ..., N).
- No collisions occur because the schedule is deterministic.

**Timing Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
|Slot1|Slot2|Slot3|Slot4|...|SlotN| F1 | F2 | F3 |...| FN |
  1     0    1     0         1   <--- data transmitted
```

**Derivation of Channel Utilization:**

Let:
- $T$ = frame transmission time
- $N \cdot t_b$ = contention period duration (N bits ├Ś bit duration $t_b$)

**Case 1: Light Load (only 1 of N stations has a frame)**

$$
S = \frac{T}{T + N \cdot t_b}
$$

**Case 2: Heavy Load (all N stations have frames)**

$$
S = \frac{N \cdot T}{N \cdot T + N \cdot t_b} = \frac{T}{T + t_b}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: N = 16, L = 1000 bits, $t_b$ = 1 ┬Ąs (assume 1 Mbps)

- Frame time: $T = 1000 \times 1 \mu s = 1000 \mu s$
- Map time: $N \cdot t_b = 16 \times 1 \mu s = 16 \mu s$
- Total cycle (heavy load): $16 \times 1000 + 16 = 16016 \mu s$

$$
S_{\text{high}} = \frac{16000}{16016} = 0.9990 = 99.90\%
$$

For light load (1 station ready):

$$
S_{\text{low}} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 16} = \frac{1000}{1016} = 0.9843 = 98.43\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Channel efficiency approaches 100% as load increases, but suffers a slight penalty at light load due to fixed reservation overhead.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Ethernet Switch Architecture (Block Diagram Description):**

An Ethernet switch is a **Layer 2 multi-port bridge** with the following key building blocks:

1. **Port Interface Cards:** Each port connects to one host via a dedicated full-duplex link.
2. **Backplane / Switching Fabric:** A high-speed internal bus or crossbar that connects all ports. Non-blocking switches require backplane bandwidth Ōēź sum of all port speeds.
3. **MAC Address Table (Forwarding Table):** A CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table mapping each known MAC address to the corresponding port.
4. **Frame Buffers:** Memory queues for store-and-forward and congestion handling.
5. **Forwarding Logic:** Decides how to handle each incoming frame (forward, filter, flood, or discard).

**The Three Forwarding Modes:**

| Mode | Operation | Latency | Error Check? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Store-and-Forward** | Receives **entire frame** before forwarding | Highest (full frame delay) | Yes (CRC verified) | Production networks (default) |
| **Cut-Through** | Forwards as soon as **DA is read** (6 bytes) | Lowest | No (errors propagated) | High-speed backbones (legacy) |
| **Fragment-Free** | Forwards after first **64 bytes** received | Medium | Partial (collision window covered) | Real-time / low-error environments |

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Store-and-Forward:** The switch buffers the complete incoming frame, performs **CRC-32 verification**, and only forwards error-free frames. Discarded frames are dropped silently. This is the **most common and safest** mode.

2. **Cut-Through:** The switch reads only the **first 6 bytes (destination MAC address)** and immediately begins forwarding the frame out the destination port. This achieves the **lowest possible latency** but propagates corrupted frames and is unsuitable for high-error links.

3. **Fragment-Free:** A hybrid mode where the switch waits for the first **64 bytes** of the frame. Since the minimum legal Ethernet frame is 64 bytes, this guarantees that the switch will detect any **late collisions** (which occur in the first 64 bytes) before forwarding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common student mistake is to claim that "Cut-Through has zero latency." This is **incorrect** ŌĆö latency is still present for the first 6 bytes of frame reception. The correct phrasing is "Cut-Through has the **minimum possible** switching latency for that architecture." Forgetting to mention the **CRC checking** in Store-and-Forward loses 1 mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 2: Collision-Free Protocols & Ethernet**

- [x] **Bit-Map Protocol** uses an **N-bit reservation map**; collisions are impossible; low-numbered stations have priority.
- [x] **Binary Countdown** uses **$\log_2 N$ bit address arbitration**; higher addresses win; exploits the **analog-OR property** of the medium.
- [x] **Limited Contention Protocols** (e.g., Adaptive Tree Walk) adaptively balance delay at low load with efficiency at high load.
- [x] **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** uses **CSMA/CD** with **binary exponential backoff** and a **minimum 64-byte frame** size.
- [x] **Slot time = 51.2 ┬Ąs** (at 10 Mbps); 1 slot time = 512 bit-times.
- [x] **Maximum retries = 16**; beyond this, the frame is dropped.
- [x] **Switched Ethernet** replaces hubs with switches, providing **one collision domain per port**.
- [x] **Microsegmentation** = dedicated point-to-point full-duplex link per host.
- [x] **Full-duplex** operation makes **collisions physically impossible** ŌĆö CSMA/CD is disabled.
- [x] **Switch forwarding modes:** Store-and-Forward (safe), Cut-Through (fast), Fragment-Free (balanced).
- [x] **Switch backplane** must be Ōēź **2 ├Ś N ├Ś R** (for full-duplex non-blocking), where N = ports, R = per-port rate.
- [x] **Frame format order:** Preamble ŌåÆ SFD ŌåÆ DA ŌåÆ SA ŌåÆ Length/Type ŌåÆ Data ŌåÆ Pad ŌåÆ FCS.
- [x] **FCS uses CRC-32** for error detection; corrupted frames are silently dropped (no retransmission at MAC layer).
- [x] **Minimum frame = 64 bytes** ensures the sender is still transmitting when a collision bit returns from the far end.
- [x] **Interframe Gap (IFG) = 96 bit-times** ŌĆö mandatory silence between consecutive frames.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fast Ethernet and Gigabit Ethernet. Wireless LANs - 802.11 - Architecture and Protocol Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fast Ethernet and Gigabit Ethernet. Wireless LANs - 802.11 - Architecture and Protocol Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps IEEE 802.3u)

### Formal Definition
**Fast Ethernet** is a high-speed LAN technology standardized as **IEEE 802.3u** in **1995**, delivering a data rate of **100 Mbps** over various physical media. It preserves the **MAC sublayer protocol** (CSMA/CD), frame format (minimum 64 bytes, maximum 1518 bytes), and maximum segment length rules of classic 10BASE-T Ethernet, while operating **10 times faster** on the wire.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of classic 10 Mbps Ethernet as a **single-lane road** where cars (data frames) move at a fixed pace. Fast Ethernet is like turning that same road into a **10-lane expressway with the same speed limit** ŌĆö more cars can travel per unit time without changing the rules of the road. The traffic police (CSMA/CD) still work the same way, they just have to handle ten times more vehicles per second.

### Physical Layer Variants
> [!IMPORTANT] Fast Ethernet Physical Layer Specifications
> - **100BASE-TX** ŌĆö Two pairs of **Category 5 UTP** or **STP**, supports **Full-Duplex** and **Half-Duplex**, max segment **100 m**, uses **4B/5B** line coding with **MLT-3** signaling.
> - **100BASE-FX** ŌĆö Two strands of **multimode (or single-mode) optical fiber**, max segment **412 m (half-duplex)** or **2 km (full-duplex)**, uses **4B/5B** with **NRZ-I** signaling.
> - **100BASE-T4** ŌĆö Four pairs of **Category 3 UTP** (legacy), max segment **100 m**, uses **8B/6T** line coding. (Obsolete)
> - **100BASE-T2** ŌĆö Two pairs of **Category 3 UTP**, max segment **100 m**, uses **PAM-5 ├Ś 2** (5-level pulse amplitude modulation). (Rarely used)

### Key Advantages over Classic Ethernet
- **10├Ś bandwidth** with **same MAC layer** ŌåÆ trivial migration path.
- **Auto-Negotiation** (IEEE 802.3u clause 28) allows a port to negotiate the highest mutually supported speed (10/100) and duplex mode.
- **Full-Duplex mode eliminates CSMA/CD**, allowing collision-free operation and removing the 5-4-3 repeater rule (since repeaters aren't used in full-duplex).

> [!NOTE] **The 5-4-3 Repeater Rule (Classic/100 Mbps Half-Duplex)**
> Between any two stations, the maximum network can have **5 segments, 4 repeaters, and 3 populated segments**. This was carried over from 10BASE-5.

---

## 1.2 Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps & 10 Gbps)

### Formal Definition
**Gigabit Ethernet** is the next-generation Ethernet standard ratified as **IEEE 802.3z (1998)** for fiber/short copper and **IEEE 802.3ab (1999)** for twisted-pair. It delivers a data rate of **1 Gbps (1000 Mbps)** and, in its later iteration (**IEEE 802.3ae, 2002**), extends to **10 Gbps (10 Gigabit Ethernet)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
If Fast Ethernet is a 10-lane road, **Gigabit Ethernet is a 100-lane highway**, and **10 Gigabit Ethernet is a 1000-lane superhighway**. The rules of the road (frame format) remain identical, but at gigabit speeds, engineers had to redesign intersections (repeaters) to be more like overpasses (full-duplex switched networks) because the **slot time** would have become impractically small (4096 bit-times in classic Ethernet became unworkable at 1 Gbps).

### Physical Layer Variants

> [!IMPORTANT] Gigabit Ethernet Physical Layer Specifications
> | Standard | Medium | Coding | Max Distance |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **1000BASE-SX** | Multimode fiber (62.5/50 ╬╝m) | 8B/10B NRZ | 220ŌĆō550 m |
> | **1000BASE-LX** | Single-mode or multimode fiber | 8B/10B NRZ | 5 km (SM) / 550 m (MM) |
> | **1000BASE-CX** | Twinaxial copper (short) | 8B/10B NRZ | 25 m |
> | **1000BASE-T** | **4 pairs Cat-5e/Cat-6 UTP** | **PAM-5 (5-level)**, 4D-PAM5 | **100 m** |
> | **10GBASE-SR/LR/ER** | Multimode/Single-mode fiber | 64B/66B | 26 m ŌĆō 40 km |
> | **10GBASE-T** | Cat-6a/Cat-7 UTP | PAM-16, LDPC | 100 m |

### Critical Problem & Solution: The Slot Time Crisis
At 1 Gbps, a 64-byte frame takes only **512 ns** to transmit. A collision must be detected before the sender finishes, but the original 512-bit slot time (51.2 ╬╝s at 10 Mbps) would shrink to **5.12 ╬╝s**, allowing a network of only **40 m** of cable ŌĆö impractical.

**Solutions used:**
1. **Carrier Extension (IEEE 802.3z):** Frames shorter than 512 bytes are padded up to 512 bytes ("jabber"). Only used in **half-duplex** mode.
2. **Frame Bursting:** Multiple short frames can be sent consecutively without relinquishing the medium, up to a burst length limit.
3. **Switched Full-Duplex topology** ŌĆö the **dominant** approach today, eliminating CSMA/CD entirely.

> [!NOTE] **10 Gigabit Ethernet (10GbE) ŌĆö IEEE 802.3ae**
> Supports **only full-duplex**, **no CSMA/CD**, primarily **fiber-optic** media, designed for **MAN/WAN/backbone** use, frame format unchanged.

---

## 1.3 Wireless LANs ŌĆö IEEE 802.11 Architecture

### Formal Definition
**IEEE 802.11** (Wi-Fi) is the family of standards for **Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs)** operating in unlicensed radio bands (typically **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz**). The architecture defines a **station (STA)**, an **access point (AP)**, an **independent basic service set (IBSS)**, an **infrastructure basic service set (BSS)**, an **extended service set (ESS)**, and a **distribution system (DS)** that interconnects BSSs.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **coffee shop with Wi-Fi**. Your laptop is a **station (STA)**. The Wi-Fi router on the counter is the **access point (AP)**. The wireless coverage area around the AP is the **Basic Service Set (BSS)** ŌĆö it's the "cell" you live in. Now imagine the same coffee shop chain in 10 different cities. Connecting all those stores' routers with a wired network is the **Distribution System (DS)**, and the entire global network of stores is the **Extended Service Set (ESS)** ŌĆö same SSID, multiple physical cells.

> [!NOTE] **WLAN Topologies in 802.11**
> - **Independent BSS (IBSS) / Ad-hoc Network:** Direct STA-to-STA communication, **no AP**. Peer-to-peer Wi-Fi Direct.
> - **Infrastructure BSS:** All STAs communicate **only through the AP**. Most common Wi-Fi mode.
> - **Extended Service Set (ESS):** Multiple BSSs linked by a **Distribution System (DS)** to form a single logical network.
> - **Portal:** Component where the DS interfaces with a **non-802.11 network** (e.g., Ethernet).

---

## 1.4 IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack

### Formal Definition
The 802.11 standard specifies the **Physical Layer (PHY)** and the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** of the **OSI Data Link Layer**. The upper portion of the Data Link Layer ŌĆö the **Logical Link Control (LLC)** ŌĆö is shared with other 802 networks via **IEEE 802.2**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of Wi-Fi communication as a **postal system**. The **LLC sublayer** is the **post office counter** that standardizes addressing across all 802 networks. The **MAC sublayer** is the **mail truck driver** who decides when to drive, when to wait at a red light (CSMA/CA), and how to handle traffic jams (backoff). The **PHY layer** is the **actual road and radio waves** the truck drives on.

> [!IMPORTANT] **802.11 Protocol Stack Layers**
> - **Upper Layers:** Network (IP) and above
> - **LLC Sublayer:** IEEE 802.2 (shared)
> - **MAC Sublayer:** CSMA/CA, RTS/CTS, fragmentation, encryption (WEP/WPA/WPA2/WPA3), frame format
> - **PHY Layer:** DSSS, OFDM, MIMO, DSS, FHSS (legacy)

### The Three Generations of 802.11 PHY
- **802.11 (legacy, 1997):** 1ŌĆō2 Mbps, DSSS/FHSS in 2.4 GHz ISM band
- **802.11a/g/n/ac/ax/be:** OFDM-based (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) ŌåÆ 6 Mbps to **46 Gbps** (Wi-Fi 7/be theoretical max)
- **802.11ad/ay (WiGig):** 60 GHz, multi-Gbps for short-range in-room

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fast Ethernet ŌĆö Deep Dive

### Why "Fast" Without Changing the MAC?
The genius of Fast Ethernet was **reusing the CSMA/CD MAC** unchanged. This means:
- Same **maximum 1518-byte** frame
- Same **minimum 64-byte** frame (still 512 bits at the new speed ŌåÆ 5.12 ╬╝s minimum frame)
- Same **repeater rules** (5-4-3 in half-duplex)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Bit-Time and Slot-Time Scaling**
> - A **bit-time** is the time to transmit 1 bit.
> - At **10 Mbps**: 1 bit-time = 100 ns
> - At **100 Mbps**: 1 bit-time = **10 ns**
> - Slot time = **512 bit-times** = 51.2 ╬╝s (10 Mbps) and **5.12 ╬╝s** (100 Mbps) ŌĆö still long enough for a useful 250 m collision domain in half-duplex.

### Auto-Negotiation (Clause 28)
A Fast Ethernet port sends **FLP (Fast Link Pulse)** bursts ŌĆö a 16-clock NLP-like sequence encoding the advertised capabilities. The other end responds, and both sides select the **highest common denominator**:
1. **100BASE-TX Full-Duplex**
2. **100BASE-TX Half-Duplex**
3. **10BASE-T Full-Duplex**
4. **10BASE-T Half-Duplex**

> [!WARNING] **Duplex Mismatch Pitfall**
> If one end auto-negotiates to 100 Full-Duplex and the other is manually set to 100 Half-Duplex, you'll see massive late-collision errors, CRC errors, and poor performance. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

---

## 2.2 Gigabit Ethernet ŌĆö Deep Dive

### MAC Options for 1 Gbps
1. **Full-Duplex over Switches (default today):** No CSMA/CD needed. Each port is a private collision-free domain.
2. **Half-Duplex (rare, legacy repeaters):** Uses **Carrier Extension + Frame Bursting**.

### Carrier Extension Mechanism
For **half-duplex** gigabit:
- If a frame is **shorter than 512 bytes**, the sender appends **non-data symbols** until 512 bytes of wire-time have elapsed.
- The receiver strips these extension symbols before forwarding the frame up.
- Wastes bandwidth on short frames, but preserves collision-detection window.

### Frame Bursting
Allows a station to send **multiple frames back-to-back** in a single "burst" without releasing the channel, up to a burst limit (typically **8192 byte-times** of wire time). The channel is "held" via a special "extender" inter-frame spacing.

### 1000BASE-T Genius
- Uses **all 4 pairs** simultaneously
- **PAM-5 signaling** (5 voltage levels, only 4 data symbols + 1 error-detection)
- **125 MBaud symbol rate per pair** ├Ś 2 bits/symbol ├Ś 4 pairs = **1000 Mbps**
- Forward Error Correction (FEC) built-in to recover from near-end crosstalk (NEXT)

### 10 Gigabit Ethernet (10GbE)
- **802.3ae (2002)** introduced 10 Gbps over fiber
- **802.3an (2006)** introduced **10GBASE-T** over Cat-6a/Cat-7
- Uses **64B/66B block coding** (2% overhead) instead of 8B/10B (20% overhead)
- **No half-duplex mode exists** ŌĆö purely full-duplex, fiber-dominated
- Frame format: **completely identical** to 10/100/1000

---

## 2.3 802.11 Architecture ŌĆö Deep Dive

### Components in Detail

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **STA (Station)** | Any device with 802.11 wireless interface (laptop, phone, IoT) |
| **AP (Access Point)** | Device that bridges the wireless BSS to a Distribution System |
| **BSS (Basic Service Set)** | A single AP plus all STAs associated with it; coverage = **BSA (Basic Service Area)** |
| **IBSS (Independent BSS)** | Ad-hoc network, no AP, limited to a few stations |
| **DS (Distribution System)** | The wired (typically Ethernet) backbone connecting multiple APs |
| **ESS (Extended Service Set)** | The union of multiple BSSs interconnected by a DS, sharing the same **SSID** |
| **Portal** | Logical interface between the DS and a non-802 network |
| **SSID** | 32-character identifier (network name) for an ESS |

### Three-Tier Station-Service Model
802.11 defines **9 services**, split into two groups:

**Station Services (within a BSS):**
1. **Authentication** (Open, Shared Key, WPA2-PSK, WPA3-SAE)
2. **Deauthentication**
3. **Privacy** (WEP/TKIP/AES-CCMP encryption)
4. **MAC Service Data Unit (MSDU) Delivery**

**Distribution System Services (across ESS):**
5. **Association** ŌĆö STA joins a specific AP
6. **Disassociation**
7. **Reassociation** ŌĆö Roaming between APs
8. **Distribution** ŌĆö routing frames to correct AP
9. **Integration** ŌĆö translation to/from non-802 networks

### 802.11 Frame Format (Unified Across All 802.11 PHY)
The **802.11 MAC frame** has up to 9 fields:

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame Control** | 2 B | Type, subtype, To/From DS, retry, power mgmt, more fragments, WEP |
| **Duration/ID** | 2 B | Duration for NAV (Network Allocation Vector) |
| **Address 1** | 6 B | Receiver Address (RA) |
| **Address 2** | 6 B | Transmitter Address (TA) |
| **Address 3** | 6 B | Source or Destination depending on To/From DS bits |
| **Sequence Control** | 2 B | Fragment + sequence number |
| **Address 4** | 6 B | Used only in WDS (4-address mode) |
| **Frame Body** | 0ŌĆō2312 B | Payload |
| **FCS** | 4 B | CRC-32 integrity check |

> [!IMPORTANT] **4 Address Modes in 802.11**
> The **ToDS** and **FromDS** flags in Frame Control determine which addresses are used:
> - **00:** IBSS (Ad-hoc) ŌĆö uses Addr 1, 2, 3
> - **10:** From AP to STA ŌĆö uses Addr 1 (DA), 2 (BSSID), 3 (SA)
> - **01:** From STA to AP ŌĆö uses Addr 1 (BSSID), 2 (SA), 3 (DA)
> - **11:** WDS Mesh ŌĆö uses all 4 addresses

---

## 2.4 802.11 MAC Sublayer ŌĆö Deep Dive

### CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance)
Unlike wired Ethernet's CSMA/CD, wireless stations **cannot detect collisions** (the receiver is too weak, plus hidden node issue). So 802.11 **avoids** collisions via:
1. **Physical Carrier Sensing** ŌĆö listen to channel energy
2. **Virtual Carrier Sensing (NAV)** ŌĆö every frame carries a "Duration" field, stations set a timer (NAV) and defer transmission until it expires
3. **IFS-based priority** ŌĆö different **Inter-Frame Spaces** create priority

### Inter-Frame Space Hierarchy
- **SIFS (Short IFS) ŌĆö 10 ╬╝s (802.11b)** ŌĆö Highest priority (ACKs, CTS, frame fragments)
- **PIFS (PCF IFS) = SIFS + 1 slot** ŌĆö For PCF contention-free period
- **DIFS (DCF IFS) = SIFS + 2 slots** ŌĆö For DCF contention-based period (normal data)
- **EIFS (Extended IFS)** ŌĆö Used after a CRC error, much longer

### DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) ŌĆö Random Backoff
When the channel is busy, after DIFS:
1. Choose a **random backoff counter** in [0, CW], where **CW (Contention Window)** starts at **CWmin = 31** (802.11b/g) or **15** (802.11a/n/ac)
2. Decrement counter for every **slot time** the channel is idle
3. When counter = 0, transmit
4. On collision (no ACK), double CW up to **CWmax = 1023** (802.11b) or **15** (legacy, varies)

### RTS/CTS (Request to Send / Clear to Send)
Optional mechanism to solve the **Hidden Node Problem**:
1. Sender transmits a short **RTS** (20 bytes)
2. Receiver responds with **CTS** (14 bytes)
3. All stations hearing either RTS or CTS update their **NAV** and remain silent
4. Sender transmits data frame
5. Receiver replies with **ACK** after SIFS

### Hidden Node vs. Exposed Node

> [!NOTE] **Hidden Node Problem**
> Stations A and C are both within range of AP B, but **A and C cannot hear each other**. They both sense an idle channel and transmit simultaneously ŌåÆ collision at B.

> [!NOTE] **Exposed Node Problem**
> Stations B and C are within range of each other, but B's transmission to A prevents C from transmitting to D, even though C's transmission would not collide at A.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bit-time @ 10 Mbps = 100 ns** | 1 bit / 10 ├Ś 10ŌüČ bps |
| 2 | **Bit-time @ 100 Mbps = 10 ns** | 1 bit / 100 ├Ś 10ŌüČ bps |
| 3 | **Bit-time @ 1 Gbps = 1 ns** | 1 bit / 1 ├Ś 10Ōü╣ bps |
| 4 | **Min frame = 512 bits = 64 bytes** | 4 ├Ś 512-bit "slot time" path-length rule |
| 5 | **Slot time (10/100 Mbps) = 51.2 ╬╝s** | 512 bit-times |
| 6 | **Max end-to-end length = 2500 m** (10BASE-5) | 51.2 ╬╝s ├Ś 0.77 c in coax |
| 7 | **Carrier Ext. overhead = (512 - L) bytes per frame** (L < 64) | Half-duplex 1 Gbps |
| 8 | **802.11 CWmin = 31, CWmax = 1023** (802.11b/g) | DCF backoff bounds |
| 9 | **Backoff = Random ├Ś SlotTime** | Random Ōłł [0, CW] |
| 10 | **DIFS = SIFS + 2 ├Ś SlotTime** | DCF inter-frame space |
| 11 | **PIFS = SIFS + 1 ├Ś SlotTime** | PCF inter-frame space |
| 12 | **802.11 SIFS = 10 ╬╝s** (802.11b DSSS) | Shortest IFS |
| 13 | **802.11 SlotTime = 20 ╬╝s** (802.11b) | 1 slot for backoff |
| 14 | **802.11 Data rate (1 Gbps) overhead** = SIFS + DIFS + 2├ŚSlot + RTS + CTS + 2├ŚACK + backoff | Real-world efficiency |
| 15 | **Efficiency ╬Ę = T_data / T_total** | T_total = DIFS + Backoff + T_data + SIFS + T_ACK |
| 16 | **Max 802.11 MSDU = 2304 bytes** | 802.11 maximum frame body |
| 17 | **Max 802.11 frame body = 2312 bytes** | MSDU + overhead |
| 18 | **NAV update = Duration field of overheard frame** | Virtual carrier sense |
| 19 | **CW_new = (CW_old + 1) ├Ś 2 - 1** | Binary exponential backoff |
| 20 | **Theoretical max Wi-Fi throughput** = 1 / (1 + overhead ratio) | For 802.11n/ac/ax analyses |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1: Bit-Time & Slot-Time Conversion

**Question:** Convert the Ethernet slot time of 51.2 ╬╝s into bit-times at 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, and 1 Gbps. Then compute the maximum half-duplex cable length in each case.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Understand the fundamental relationship

$$\text{Slot Time (seconds)} = \text{N bits} \times \text{Bit-Time (seconds/bit)}$$

Where:
$$\text{Bit-Time} = \frac{1}{\text{Data Rate (bps)}}$$

**Step 2:** At 10 Mbps (Classic Ethernet)

$$\text{Bit-Time}_{10} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 100 \text{ ns/bit}$$

$$\text{Slot Time in bits} = \frac{51.2 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-9}} = 512 \text{ bits}$$

This matches: 64 bytes ├Ś 8 = 512 bits. Ō£ō

**Step 3:** At 100 Mbps (Fast Ethernet)

$$\text{Bit-Time}_{100} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns/bit}$$

$$\text{Slot Time in bits} = \frac{51.2 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = 5120 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4:** At 1 Gbps (Gigabit Ethernet)

$$\text{Bit-Time}_{1000} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^9} = 1 \text{ ns/bit}$$

$$\text{Slot Time in bits} = \frac{51.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 51200 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum half-duplex network reach (round-trip time must fit within slot time)

Propagation speed in coax Ōēł 0.77c Ōēł 0.77 ├Ś 3 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s

$$\text{Max Reach} = \frac{c_{\text{prop}} \times \text{Slot Time}}{2} = \frac{0.77 \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$\text{Max Reach} = \frac{0.77 \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-5}}{2} \approx 5913 \text{ m}$$

Practical max for 10BASE-5 = 2500 m (with safety margin + repeater delays).

**Step 6:** At 1 Gbps half-duplex, slot time kept at 512 bits would be:

$$T_{\text{slot @ 1G}} = 512 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 512 \text{ ns}$$

$$\text{Max Reach} = \frac{0.77 \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 512 \times 10^{-9}}{2} \approx 59 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE] This is why Gigabit Ethernet needs **Carrier Extension** and **Frame Bursting** in half-duplex, or ŌĆö more practically ŌĆö **full-duplex switched topology**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2: 802.11 DCF Throughput Calculation

**Question:** A station sends a 1500-byte MSDU using 802.11g (54 Mbps OFDM). Compute the **DCF throughput efficiency** if the backoff counter averages 15 slots, and the data is sent without RTS/CTS.

**Given:**
- Data rate = **54 Mbps**
- SlotTime = 9 ╬╝s (802.11g)
- SIFS = 10 ╬╝s
- DIFS = SIFS + 2 ├Ś SlotTime = 10 + 18 = 28 ╬╝s
- Average backoff = 15 slots ├Ś 9 ╬╝s = **135 ╬╝s**
- ACK frame = 14 bytes
- ACK rate = **24 Mbps** (typical control rate)
- Frame body = 1500 bytes
- PLCP preamble + header = 20 ╬╝s

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the time to transmit the data frame at 54 Mbps

$$T_{\text{data}} = \frac{(1500 \text{ B} + 28 \text{ B MAC overhead})}{54 \times 10^6} + T_{\text{preamble}}$$

$$T_{\text{data}} = \frac{1528 \times 8}{54 \times 10^6} + 20 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_{\text{data}} = 226.37 \text{ ╬╝s} + 20 \text{ ╬╝s} = 246.37 \text{ ╬╝s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the time to transmit ACK at 24 Mbps

$$T_{\text{ACK}} = \frac{14 \times 8}{24 \times 10^6} + 20 \times 10^{-6} = 4.67 \text{ ╬╝s} + 20 \text{ ╬╝s} = 24.67 \text{ ╬╝s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total channel time occupied

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{DIFS}} + T_{\text{backoff}} + T_{\text{data}} + T_{\text{SIFS}} + T_{\text{ACK}}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 28 + 135 + 246.37 + 10 + 24.67 = 444.04 \text{ ╬╝s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute useful data time (the payload bits only)

$$T_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{54 \times 10^6} = 222.22 \text{ ╬╝s}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the DCF efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{222.22}{444.04} \approx 0.5004 \text{ or } \mathbf{50.04\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Analytical Insight**
> The **50% efficiency** is a typical 802.11g result in low-contention scenarios. Real-world throughput drops to 30ŌĆō40% under heavy contention, and modern 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) with **OFDMA + BSS coloring + MU-MIMO** dramatically improves this through scheduled uplink access.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3: Backoff Counter Evolution

**Question:** A station's first transmission collides. Show the **CW progression** and the **average backoff** after 3 successive collisions.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Define initial contention window parameters (802.11b/g)

$$CW_{\min} = 31, \quad CW_{\max} = 1023$$

**Step 2:** Binary Exponential Backoff formula

$$CW_{i+1} = \min\left(2 \times (CW_i + 1) - 1,\ CW_{\max}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Iteration table

| Retry # | Contention Window | Backoff Range | Average Backoff (slots) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (1st try) | 31 | [0, 31] | 15.5 |
| 1 (after 1st collision) | 63 | [0, 63] | 31.5 |
| 2 (after 2nd collision) | 127 | [0, 127] | 63.5 |
| 3 (after 3rd collision) | 255 | [0, 255] | 127.5 |

**Step 4:** Average total backoff over 3 retries

$$\overline{B} = \frac{15.5 + 31.5 + 63.5 + 127.5}{4} = \frac{238}{4} = 59.5 \text{ slots}$$

**Step 5:** At SlotTime = 20 ╬╝s (802.11b DSSS):

$$T_{\text{backoff}} = 59.5 \times 20 \text{ ╬╝s} = 1190 \text{ ╬╝s} = 1.19 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE] **Why exponential backoff?**
> It adapts the contention window to the load. Under light load, small CW minimizes delay. Under heavy load, large CW minimizes collisions. This is a textbook example of **adaptive contention management**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem 4: 802.11 Frame Format Analysis

**Question:** A wireless data frame is transmitted from a STA to an AP in an infrastructure BSS, carrying 1000 bytes of application payload (IP packet). Calculate the **total bytes on the air** and **MAC-layer efficiency**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Determine the addressing mode. STA ŌåÆ AP, so **ToDS=1, FromDS=0**.

| Field | Size |
|---|---|
| Frame Control | 2 B |
| Duration/ID | 2 B |
| Address 1 (BSSID/RA) | 6 B |
| Address 2 (TA/SA) | 6 B |
| Address 3 (DA) | 6 B |
| Sequence Control | 2 B |
| Frame Body (MSDU = IP) | 1000 B |
| FCS (CRC-32) | 4 B |
| **Total MAC frame** | **1028 B** |

**Step 2:** Prepend PLCP preamble + header (20 ╬╝s) ŌĆö handled at PHY.

**Step 3:** Efficiency at MAC layer (excluding PHY preamble)

$$\eta_{\text{MAC}} = \frac{\text{Payload}}{\text{Total MAC Frame}} = \frac{1000}{1028} = 0.9727 = \mathbf{97.27\%}$$

> [!NOTE] **Why is MAC efficiency so high?**
> The MAC overhead (28 bytes) is fixed per frame. For large frames (like 1500 B), the overhead is only ~1.8%. This is why **fragmentation** of large frames hurts efficiency, but helps in noisy environments.

---

## 3.5 Worked Problem 5: Gigabit Ethernet Carrier Extension

**Question:** A station on 1000BASE-T (half-duplex) sends a 64-byte frame. How many **extension symbols** are added? What is the **total wire time**?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Minimum frame = 64 bytes = 512 bits.

**Step 2:** 1 Gbps bit-time = 1 ns.

**Step 3:** Minimum transmission time for half-duplex = 512 bit-times

$$T_{\text{min,1G}} = 512 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 512 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4:** Time for actual 64-byte frame

$$T_{\text{frame}} = 64 \times 8 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 512 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5:** Since 64 bytes = 512 bits, **no extension needed** for minimum frame. For 32-byte frame:

$$T_{\text{frame}} = 32 \times 8 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 256 \text{ ns}$$

Extension symbols needed:

$$N_{\text{ext}} = \frac{512 \text{ bits} - 256 \text{ bits}}{1 \text{ bit/symbol}} = 256 \text{ symbols}$$

> [!WARNING] **Efficiency Penalty**
> A 32-byte frame at 1 Gbps half-duplex has only **50% wire efficiency** due to extension. This is why **full-duplex switched gigabit is the only practical deployment** today.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model QP] 
**Differentiate between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA. Why does 802.11 use CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | CSMA/CD (802.3 Ethernet) | CSMA/CA (802.11 Wi-Fi) |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection vs Avoidance** | Detects collisions after they occur | **Avoids** collisions proactively |
| **Collision Sensing** | Station can listen while transmitting | Station cannot detect collisions (transmitter sees only its own strong signal) |
| **Hidden Node** | Not a problem in wired media | **Critical problem** ŌĆö CSMA/CA addresses via RTS/CTS and NAV |
| **Used in** | Wired Ethernet (10/100/1000 Mbps) | Wireless 802.11 |
| **Recovery mechanism** | Jam signal + binary exponential backoff | ACK timeout + binary exponential backoff |
| **Vulnerable to** | Propagation delay (causes collision window) | Hidden node + exposed node problems |

**Why CSMA/CA for wireless (key reason for 3 marks):**
1. The transmitting station's own signal **drowns out** the weaker remote signal, making collision detection impossible while transmitting.
2. **Hidden node problem** in wireless ŌĆö two stations can transmit simultaneously without sensing each other.
3. Wireless channel is **half-duplex by nature**, so we cannot transmit and listen at the same frequency simultaneously (practical radio design).
4. Solution: Use **ACK** + **RTS/CTS** + **NAV** to ensure reliability without on-the-fly collision detection.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model QP] 
**List and briefly explain the components of an IEEE 802.11 Extended Service Set (ESS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Station (STA):** Any wireless device (laptop, mobile, IoT) equipped with 802.11 NIC.
2. **Basic Service Set (BSS):** A single AP and the stations associated with it. The radio coverage area is the **Basic Service Area (BSA)**.
3. **Access Point (AP):** Bridge between the wireless BSS and the wired Distribution System. Operates at the Data Link Layer.
4. **Distribution System (DS):** The wired backbone (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple APs to form a larger network.
5. **Extended Service Set (ESS):** The union of two or more BSSs connected by a DS, presenting a single logical network (single SSID) to the user.
6. **Portal:** Logical interface where the DS connects to a **non-802.11 network** (e.g., the Internet via Ethernet/router).

> [!NOTE] **Drawing tip for exam:** Always draw a labelled diagram showing BSS1 + BSS2 + DS + Portal ŌåÆ 1 extra mark for clarity.

---

## ­¤ōŚ PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Ō£” OPTION A ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Dec 2024 Model]** 
**With neat diagrams, explain the architecture of IEEE 802.11 WLAN including IBSS, BSS, ESS, Distribution System, and Portal. Also explain the functions of the MAC sublayer.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the IEEE 802.11 architecture in detail with a diagram.

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 802.11 architecture defines the smallest building block of a WLAN, then composes them into larger networks.

**1. Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS) ŌĆö Ad-Hoc Mode (2 marks)**

- A **standalone network** with no infrastructure.
- Two or more stations communicate **directly peer-to-peer**.
- No Access Point required.
- Limited to a small geographical area (typically one room).
- Also called **ad-hoc network** or **Wi-Fi Direct** in modern usage.
- **Use case:** Quick file transfer between two laptops, gaming, IoT mesh.

**2. Basic Service Set (BSS) ŌĆö Infrastructure Mode (2 marks)**

- A BSS is the fundamental building block of an 802.11 network.
- Contains **one Access Point (AP)** and a set of **associated stations (STAs)**.
- The AP acts as a **master** ŌĆö all communication passes through it (even between two STAs in the same BSS).
- Coverage area = **Basic Service Area (BSA)**.
- Each BSS is identified by a **BSSID** (typically the AP's MAC address).
- **Use case:** Home Wi-Fi, office Wi-Fi.

**3. Distribution System (DS) and Extended Service Set (ESS) (2 marks)**

- A **DS** is a wired network (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple APs.
- The union of two or more BSSs interconnected by a DS forms an **Extended Service Set (ESS)**.
- All BSSs in an ESS share the same **SSID (Service Set Identifier)** ŌĆö the user sees one logical network.
- The **Portal** is the gateway between the DS and a non-802.11 network (e.g., Internet).
- **Use case:** Campus-wide Wi-Fi, corporate networks, hotspots.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Draw a labelled ESS diagram with 2 BSSs, a DS, APs, stations, and a portal. (Refer to Section 4.1.)

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the functions of the 802.11 MAC sublayer.

**Model Solution:**

The 802.11 MAC sublayer provides **reliable, contention-based, and contention-free** access to the wireless medium. Its key functions are:

**1. Channel Access ŌĆö Distributed Coordination Function (DCF) (2 marks)**

- Primary MAC method. Based on **CSMA/CA**.
- Stations must sense the channel idle for **DIFS**, then enter a random backoff.
- Random counter chosen in **[0, CW]**; CW starts at **CWmin = 31** and **doubles up to CWmax = 1023** on each collision (Binary Exponential Backoff).
- Counter decrements per slot while channel is idle; transmission starts when counter = 0.
- Receiver replies with **ACK** after SIFS ŌĆö absence of ACK = collision.

**2. Channel Access ŌĆö Point Coordination Function (PCF) (1 mark)**

- Optional, contention-free method.
- AP acts as a **Point Coordinator** during contention-free periods.
- Stations polled one by one; PCF provides near-isochronous service (mostly obsolete in modern Wi-Fi).

**3. Virtual Carrier Sensing ŌĆö NAV (1 mark)**

- Every frame carries a **Duration field** indicating how long the channel will be busy.
- All overhearing stations set their **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** to that duration and remain silent.
- Prevents collisions with hidden nodes (combined with RTS/CTS).

**4. RTS/CTS Handshake (1 mark)**

- Optional mechanism to solve the **Hidden Node Problem**.
- Sender ŌåÆ RTS (20 B) ŌåÆ Receiver ŌåÆ CTS (14 B) ŌåÆ all stations hearing either update their NAV.
- Particularly useful for large frames in noisy environments.

**5. Fragmentation and Reassembly (1 mark)**

- Large MSDU can be split into smaller **MPDUs** for transmission.
- Each fragment gets its own ACK. Reduces the cost of errors on noisy channels.
- Reassembled at the receiving MAC.

**6. Encryption and Authentication (1 mark)**

- MAC layer provides **WEP, WPA, WPA2 (CCMP/AES), WPA3 (SAE)**.
- Authentication: Open System, Shared Key, WPA2-PSK, WPA3-SAE.
- Privacy: Frame body encrypted; FCS remains unencrypted.

---

### Ō£” OPTION B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / May 2024 Model]** 
**Explain Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet, and 10 Gigabit Ethernet standards in detail. List their physical layer variants and highlight the slot time problem in gigabit Ethernet.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain Fast Ethernet and 10 Gigabit Ethernet.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Fast Ethernet ŌĆö IEEE 802.3u (1995) (3 marks)**

- Operates at **100 Mbps**, **10├Ś faster** than classic Ethernet.
- **Same MAC** as 10BASE-T (CSMA/CD, same 64-byte min, 1518-byte max frame) ŌåÆ trivial migration.
- Bit-time = **10 ns** (vs 100 ns for 10 Mbps), slot time = **51.2 ╬╝s** (512 bit-times, unchanged).
- **100BASE-TX:** 2 pairs Cat-5 UTP, 4B/5B MLT-3, 100 m, full/half-duplex.
- **100BASE-FX:** 2 strands multimode fiber, 4B/5B NRZ-I, 412 m (HD) / 2 km (FD).
- **100BASE-T4:** 4 pairs Cat-3 UTP, 8B/6T, 100 m ŌĆö obsolete.
- **Auto-Negotiation (802.3u, Clause 28):** FLP bursts, negotiates best common speed/duplex.

**2. 10 Gigabit Ethernet ŌĆö IEEE 802.3ae (2002) (4 marks)**

- Operates at **10 Gbps**, fiber-dominated initially.
- **Full-duplex only** ŌĆö CSMA/CD removed entirely.
- Uses **64B/66B block coding** (only 3.125% overhead vs 25% in 8B/10B).
- **10GBASE-SR/LR/ER/ZR:** OM3/OM4 multimode and single-mode fiber, 26 m to 80 km.
- **10GBASE-T (802.3an, 2006):** Cat-6a / Cat-7 UTP, **PAM-16** with LDPC, 100 m, 55 W power (later reduced to <4 W with LLDP).
- **Backbone + Metro + Data Center** use cases.
- Frame format **unchanged** ŌĆö interoperable with 10/100/1000 Ethernet.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain Gigabit Ethernet and the slot time problem.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Gigabit Ethernet ŌĆö IEEE 802.3z / 802.3ab (2 marks)**

- 1 Gbps data rate, available in fiber and copper variants.
- **1000BASE-T:** 4 pairs Cat-5e/Cat-6, **PAM-5**, 125 Mbaud ├Ś 2 bits ├Ś 4 pairs = 1000 Mbps, 100 m.
- **1000BASE-SX/LX/CX:** Fiber & short copper variants, 8B/10B coding, distances from 25 m to 5 km.
- Same 1518-byte max frame; auto-negotiation with 10/100.

**2. The Slot Time Problem (3 marks)**

- Classic Ethernet requires a 64-byte minimum frame so that a sender still transmitting can detect a collision.
- The original **slot time = 51.2 ╬╝s = 512 bit-times** is sized so a signal can travel 2500 m round-trip and back to detect collision.
- At 1 Gbps, 1 bit-time = 1 ns, so **512 bit-times = 512 ns**.
- Maximum cable length = 0.77c ├Ś 512 ns / 2 Ōēł **60 m** ŌĆö impractical for LANs.

**3. Solutions (2 marks)**

- **Carrier Extension (802.3z):** Frames shorter than 512 bytes are padded with non-data symbols. The receiver strips them. The slot time remains 512 bytes.
- **Frame Bursting:** A station may transmit multiple frames back-to-back in a "burst" up to 8192 byte-times, with extender IFG between them. This amortizes the slot time.
- **Full-Duplex Switched Topology:** The **dominant modern solution** ŌĆö switches provide dedicated collision-free domains. CSMA/CD is disabled. The 5-4-3 repeater rule disappears.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - **Don't** say "gigabit Ethernet uses CSMA/CD" without qualifying it as **half-duplex with carrier extension**.
> - **Don't** confuse 10GBASE-T's **PAM-16** with 1000BASE-T's **PAM-5**. KTU loves this trap.
> - **Always** mention **Auto-Negotiation** for Fast/Gigabit Ethernet; 3 marks are easily lost for omitting it.
> - **For 802.11 architecture questions**, draw the diagram first, then explain ŌĆö examiners award 2 marks for a clean diagram alone.

---

## ­¤öæ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fast Ethernet (802.3u, 100 Mbps)** retains the same 64-byte min frame, 1518-byte max frame, and 51.2 ╬╝s slot time as 10BASE-T ŌĆö only bit-time shrinks to 10 ns.
- [x] **100BASE-TX** uses 2 pairs Cat-5 UTP, 4B/5B MLT-3 line coding, 100 m, full/half-duplex.
- [x] **100BASE-FX** uses 2 fiber strands, 4B/5B NRZ-I, up to 2 km full-duplex, ideal for backbone.
- [x] **Gigabit Ethernet (802.3z / 802.3ab, 1 Gbps)** comes in fiber (SX/LX/CX) and copper (T) variants; 1000BASE-T uses **PAM-5 over 4 pairs** Cat-5e.
- [x] **The slot time crisis at 1 Gbps** (only 60 m max reach) is solved by **Carrier Extension + Frame Bursting** in half-duplex, and **Full-Duplex Switched topology** in practice.
- [x] **10 Gigabit Ethernet (802.3ae, 10 Gbps)** is full-duplex-only, fiber-dominated, uses **64B/66B** coding, designed for **MAN/WAN/Data Center** use.
- [x] **IEEE 802.11 architecture** has 3 building blocks: **STA, AP, BSS**; combined into **IBSS** (ad-hoc), **BSS** (infra), **ESS** (multi-BSS via DS).
- [x] **DS (Distribution System)** is the wired backbone connecting APs; **Portal** connects DS to non-802.11 networks.
- [x] **802.11 protocol stack** = **LLC (802.2) + MAC (802.11) + PHY**. LLC is shared with Ethernet for upper-layer IP continuity.
- [x] **CSMA/CA (not CD)** is used in 802.11 because collision detection is impossible while transmitting and **hidden nodes** exist in wireless.
- [x] **DCF uses Binary Exponential Backoff** with **CWmin=31, CWmax=1023**; IFS hierarchy = **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**.
- [x] **RTS/CTS handshake** solves the **Hidden Node Problem** by setting **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** at all overhearing stations.
- [x] **802.11 MAC frame** supports 4 address fields for **ToDS/FromDS** combinations (00, 01, 10, 11); max **MSDU = 2304 bytes**, total max frame body = 2312 bytes.
- [x] **802.11 services:** Station Services (auth, deauth, privacy, MSDU delivery) + DS Services (assoc, disassoc, reassoc, distribution, integration) = **9 services total**.
- [x] **802.11 PHY evolution:** DSSS (legacy) ŌåÆ OFDM (11a/g) ŌåÆ MIMO (11n) ŌåÆ MU-MIMO (11ac/ax) ŌåÆ OFDMA + BSS Coloring (11ax) ŌåÆ 320 MHz (11be Wi-Fi 7).
- [x] **802.11 security generations:** WEP (broken) ŌåÆ WPA (TKIP, transitional) ŌåÆ **WPA2 (AES-CCMP, mandatory for 11n+)** ŌåÆ **WPA3 (SAE, mandatory for Wi-Fi 6E/7)**.
- [x] **Modern 802.11** uses **MU-MIMO, OFDMA, beamforming, BSS coloring** to push real-world throughput from ~50% efficiency to >70% in dense deployments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Bridges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Bridges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Data Link Layer & LAN Interconnection
## Topic: Use of Bridges

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Bridge** is a **Layer-2 (Data Link Layer) networking device** defined by the IEEE 802 standards family that interconnects two or more LAN segments (collision domains), operating primarily using **48-bit MAC (Media Access Control) addresses** for intelligent frame forwarding. Unlike a hub (Layer-1 repeater), a bridge selectively filters, floods, or forwards frames based on a dynamically built **MAC address table (forwarding table)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Expected):**
> A bridge is a store-and-forward device that examines the destination MAC address of an incoming frame, consults its internal bridging table, and either *forwards*, *filters*, or *floods* the frame to the appropriate LAN segment, thereby reducing unnecessary traffic and extending the maximum geographic reach of a LAN beyond the limits imposed by signal attenuation and collision domain constraints.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Traffic Police at a Junction"

Imagine a **two-lane road converging into a smaller one-lane bridge** over a river. Every vehicle (frame) arriving at the bridge has a **license plate number (MAC address)**. A traffic police officer (the bridge) stands at the entry point, checks a **notebook (the MAC address table)** containing the destination and lane of each regular commuter, and decides:

- If the destination is on the **same side** as the source ŌåÆ *stop the vehicle* (FILTER).
- If the destination is on the **other side** ŌåÆ *let it cross* (FORWARD).
- If the destination is **not in the notebook** ŌåÆ *let it cross both ways to find it* (FLOOD).

This intelligent filtering is what makes a bridge far more efficient than a hub, which simply shouts every packet out of every port like a public announcement system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Hub** = Physical Layer (Layer 1) ŌåÆ Repeats bits blindly.
> - **Bridge** = Data Link Layer (Layer 2) ŌåÆ Uses MAC addresses to filter/forward.
> - **Switch** = Multi-port Bridge (essentially a bridge with many ports, often cut-through or store-and-forward).
> - **Router** = Network Layer (Layer 3) ŌåÆ Uses IP addresses for routing across networks.

### 1.3 Why Bridges Were Invented ŌĆö The Engineering Motivation

1. **Collision Domain Segmentation**: A single Ethernet LAN is limited by the 5-4-3 rule (max 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments) and by signal degradation. Bridges break the LAN into separate collision domains, dramatically increasing **available bandwidth per segment**.
2. **Traffic Isolation**: Local traffic stays local; only cross-segment traffic is forwarded, reducing overall congestion.
3. **Extended Geographic Coverage**: Bridges regenerate signals, allowing LAN segments to be placed farther apart than permitted by a single cable run.
4. **Heterogeneous LAN Interconnection**: A **translational bridge** can interconnect different 802 LANs (e.g., Ethernet 802.3 Ōåö Token Ring 802.5).
5. **Improved Security & Reliability**: A bridge isolates faults and broadcast storms to a degree.

### 1.4 Key Physical / Protocol Constants (Memorize for KTU)

| Constant / Standard | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| MAC Address Length | **48 bits (6 octets)** | Globally unique hardware address |
| OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) | First 24 bits | Assigned by IEEE to manufacturer |
| Maximum Ethernet Frame | **1518 bytes** | With 802.1Q tag: 1522 bytes |
| Spanning Tree Max Diameter | 7 hops (diameter) | IEEE 802.1D |
| Bridge Forwarding Delay | 2 ├Ś (Forward Delay Timer) | Default: 30 seconds |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Operational Functions of a Bridge

A bridge performs its job by continuously executing three sub-functions. Every KTU question on bridges is essentially a question on one or more of these.

#### A. Learning (Building the Forwarding Table)
The bridge maintains a **Bridging / Forwarding Table** mapping `<MAC Address, Port, Age Timer>`. For every incoming frame:
1. Read the **Source MAC Address (SA)** from the frame header.
2. Record `(SA, Port-on-which-frame-arrived, current_time)` in the table.
3. If the entry already exists, refresh the age timer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bridge **learns from the SOURCE**, not the destination. This is a frequently tested KTU trick question.

#### B. Filtering (Intelligent Local Containment)
If the frame's **Destination MAC (DA)** is in the same segment (port) as the source, the bridge **discards the frame** (does not forward it). This is the chief mechanism for traffic reduction.

#### C. Forwarding / Flooding
- **Forwarding**: If DA is in the table on a *different* port, transmit the frame *only* on that port.
- **Flooding**: If DA is **NOT** in the table (unknown unicast), transmit the frame out **all ports except the arrival port** (a process called *selective flooding*).
- **Broadcast / Multicast**: By default, these are always flooded out all other ports (LAN bridging rule).

### 2.2 The Decision Algorithm (Pseudo-Flow)

```
RECEIVE frame on Port_in
   Read SA and DA
   IF (SA, Port_in) NOT in table:
       ADD (SA, Port_in)  to table     // LEARN
   IF DA is broadcast/multicast:
       FORWARD out all ports except Port_in
   ELSE IF (DA, Port_out) IN table:
       IF Port_out == Port_in:
           FILTER (drop frame)         // LOCAL TRAFFIC
       ELSE:
           FORWARD out Port_out only
   ELSE:
       FLOOD out all ports except Port_in  // UNKNOWN UNICAST
```

### 2.3 The Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D) ŌĆö Critical for KTU
