When bridges are connected in loops (redundant topology for fault tolerance), frames can circulate forever, causing a **broadcast storm**. **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)** solves this by:

1. Electing a single **Root Bridge** (lowest Bridge ID = priority + MAC).
2. Selecting for each non-root bridge a **Root Port** (lowest cost path to root).
3. Selecting one **Designated Port** per segment (the bridge offering lowest cost to root).
4. **Blocking** all other ports ŌåÆ a loop-free active topology.

**BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit)** frames are exchanged every **2 seconds** by default (Hello Time) to maintain the tree.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students confuse the Root Bridge with Designated Bridge. Root Bridge = unique elected root. Designated Bridge = per-segment bridge that forwards frames toward root.

### 2.4 Types of Bridges (Highly Testable)

| Type | Operation | Where Used | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transparent Bridge** | Operates transparently; hosts unaware. Learns, filters, forwards using MAC table. | Ethernet LANs | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| **Source Routing Bridge (SRB)** | Frame carries full route in header (Token Ring). | IBM Token Ring 802.5 | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| **Source Routing Transparent (SRT)** | Hybrid of both. | Mixed Token Ring + Ethernet | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| **Translational Bridge** | Translates between different 802.x frame formats and bit orderings. | Ethernet Ōåö Token Ring | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Units / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bridge Filtering Rate = $\dfrac{1}{T_p}$** | Maximum frames/sec a bridge can process | frames per second |
| 2 | **Throughput Gain = $\dfrac{C}{N_{local}}$** | Bandwidth per segment after bridge splits N users | C = link capacity (bps) |
| 3 | **Path Cost (802.1D) = $\dfrac{\text{Reference}}{Bandwidth}$** | Lower cost = preferred path | Inverse to speed |
| 4 | **BPDU Hello Timer** | $2$ s | seconds |
| 5 | **Max Age Timer** | $20$ s | seconds |
| 6 | **Forward Delay** | $15$ s | seconds |
| 7 | **Convergence Time (approx)** | $\text{Forward Delay} \times 2$ | seconds |
| 8 | **MAC Address Format** | $OUI(24) \mid NIC(24)$ | bits |
| 9 | **Broadcast Domain** | NOT divided by bridges | Layer 2 boundary rule |
| 10 | **Collision Domain** | DIVIDED by every bridge port | Layer 1 boundary |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, the conceptual rule of thumb: **Bridges reduce collision domains but DO NOT reduce broadcast domains.** This single line often earns full marks on theory questions.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Campus Networks**: Bridges (and their modern descendants, Layer-2 switches) form the access and distribution layer of the Cisco three-tier model.
- **Industrial Control Networks**: Bridges segment noisy factory floors, preserving deterministic performance for SCADA systems.
- **Data Center Bridging (DCB) / Lossless Ethernet**: IEEE 802.1Qbb enables storage and data traffic to coexist on the same bridged fabric.
- **Wireless Bridging**: Point-to-point wireless bridges connect buildings kilometers apart without trenching fibre (e.g., Cisco Aironet, Ubiquiti AirMax).
- **Smart Grid & Substation Automation**: IEC 61850 networks rely on bridges for protection and metering segregation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forwarding-Table Convergence Analysis

This is the **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**: given a sequence of frames, build the bridge table step-by-step.

> **KTU 14-Mark Style Problem Statement:**
> Three LAN segments (LAN1, LAN2, LAN3) are interconnected by Bridge B1 (between LAN1 and LAN2) and Bridge B2 (between LAN2 and LAN3). The following frames are transmitted in the order given (format: `Source ŌåÆ Destination, LAN#`). Show the contents of both bridge tables after every transmission. Assume all tables are empty initially.

**Transmissions:**
1. `A ŌåÆ B, LAN1`
2. `D ŌåÆ C, LAN3`
3. `B ŌåÆ C, LAN1`
4. `C ŌåÆ D, LAN3`
5. `A ŌåÆ C, LAN1`
6. `E ŌåÆ A, LAN2`

**Step-by-Step Construction (Exhaustive):**

**Initial State:** Both tables empty.

---

**Step 1: `A ŌåÆ B` on LAN1**

For **B1** (port LAN1 receives the frame):
- Source `A` is *not* in B1's table ŌåÆ **LEARN**: Add `(A, LAN1-port, t1)`.
- Destination `B` *not* in table ŌåÆ **FLOOD** to LAN2.
For **B2** (port LAN2 receives the flooded frame):
- Source `A` *not* in B2's table ŌåÆ **LEARN**: Add `(A, LAN2-port, t1)`.
- Destination `B` *not* in table ŌåÆ **FLOOD** to LAN3.

> Result: `B1: {AŌåÆLAN1}`, `B2: {AŌåÆLAN2}`

---

**Step 2: `D ŌåÆ C` on LAN3**

For **B2** (LAN3-port receives):
- LEARN `(D, LAN3-port, t2)`.
- DA `C` not in table ŌåÆ FLOOD to LAN2.
For **B1** (LAN2-port receives):
- LEARN `(D, LAN2-port, t2)`.
- DA `C` not in table ŌåÆ FLOOD to LAN1.

> Result: `B1: {AŌåÆLAN1; DŌåÆLAN2}`, `B2: {AŌåÆLAN2; DŌåÆLAN3}`

---

**Step 3: `B ŌåÆ C` on LAN1**

For **B1** (LAN1-port receives):
- LEARN `(B, LAN1-port, t3)`.
- DA `C` not in table ŌåÆ FLOOD to LAN2.
For **B2** (LAN2-port receives):
- LEARN `(B, LAN2-port, t3)`.
- DA `C` not in table ŌåÆ FLOOD to LAN3.

> Result: `B1: {AŌåÆLAN1; DŌåÆLAN2; BŌåÆLAN1}`, `B2: {AŌåÆLAN2; DŌåÆLAN3; BŌåÆLAN2}`

---

**Step 4: `C ŌåÆ D` on LAN3**

For **B2** (LAN3-port receives):
- LEARN `(C, LAN3-port, t4)`.
- DA `D` IN table on LAN3-port. Since arrival port = DA port ŌåÆ **FILTER** (do not forward to LAN2).
For **B1** (no frame received; nothing changes).

> Result: `B1: {AŌåÆLAN1; DŌåÆLAN2; BŌåÆLAN1}`, `B2: {AŌåÆLAN2; DŌåÆLAN3; BŌåÆLAN2; CŌåÆLAN3}`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Insight:** Step 4 is the **first instance of filtering**ŌĆönotice how traffic containment immediately starts. Highlight this in your answer to earn a 2-mark bonus for analysis.

---

**Step 5: `A ŌåÆ C` on LAN1**

For **B1** (LAN1-port receives):
- SA `A` already on LAN1-port ŌåÆ refresh timer only.
- DA `C` not in B1's table (B1 never saw `C`'s source) ŌåÆ FLOOD to LAN2.
For **B2** (LAN2-port receives):
- LEARN `(A, LAN2-port, t5)`.
- DA `C` IN B2's table on LAN3-port. Arrival port (LAN2) ŌēĀ DA port (LAN3) ŌåÆ **FORWARD** to LAN3 only (no flooding).

> Result: `B1: {AŌåÆLAN1; DŌåÆLAN2; BŌåÆLAN1}`, `B2: {AŌåÆLAN2; DŌåÆLAN3; BŌåÆLAN2; CŌåÆLAN3}`

---

**Step 6: `E ŌåÆ A` on LAN2**

For **B1** (LAN2-port receives):
- LEARN `(E, LAN2-port, t6)`.
- DA `A` IN B1's table on LAN1-port. Arrival ŌēĀ DA port ŌåÆ **FORWARD** to LAN1 only.
For **B2** (LAN2-port receives same frame via shared LAN2, depending on physical layer ŌĆö assuming one bridge receives the frame first or they both do):
- LEARN `(E, LAN2-port, t6)`.
- DA `A` IN B2's table on LAN2-port. Arrival port = DA port ŌåÆ **FILTER**.

> Result: `B1: {AŌåÆLAN1; DŌåÆLAN2; BŌåÆLAN1; EŌåÆLAN2}`, `B2: {AŌåÆLAN2; DŌåÆLAN3; BŌåÆLAN2; CŌåÆLAN3; EŌåÆLAN2}`

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Final Bridge Tables

| Bridge | MAC Address | Port (LAN#) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B1** | A | LAN1 | Learnt (Step 1) |
| **B1** | B | LAN1 | Learnt (Step 3) |
| **B1** | D | LAN2 | Learnt (Step 2) |
| **B1** | E | LAN2 | Learnt (Step 6) |
| **B2** | A | LAN2 | Learnt (Step 5) |
| **B2** | B | LAN2 | Learnt (Step 3) |
| **B2** | C | LAN3 | Learnt (Step 4) |
| **B2** | D | LAN3 | Learnt (Step 2) |
| **B2** | E | LAN2 | Learnt (Step 6) |

### 3.3 Derivation: Spanning Tree Path Cost (802.1D)

Given a redundant bridged network with three candidate paths from Bridge X to the Root Bridge, with link speeds **10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, and 1 Gbps**, the IEEE 802.1D-1998 cost table assigns:

$$\text{Cost}_{802.1D} = \frac{1000}{\text{Link Speed in Mbps}}$$

Calculation per link:

$$\text{Cost}_{10\text{Mbps}} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100$$

$$\text{Cost}_{100\text{Mbps}} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10$$

$$\text{Cost}_{1\text{Gbps}} = \frac{1000}{1000} = 1$$

**Total Path Cost** is the **sum of link costs** along the path. The bridge elects the path with the **lowest total cost** to the root as its **Root Port**. The 1 Gbps path with cost 1 would be preferred over a 10 Mbps path with cost 100, demonstrating STP's preference for high-speed forwarding.

> [!NOTE]
> IEEE 802.1t (2001 revision) revised the cost values to start from 20,000,000 / Link Speed (Mbps) to support 10 Gbps and beyond. KTU typically uses the simpler 1000/x formula in problems.

### 3.4 Derivation: Total Convergence Time in STP

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{converge}} &= \text{Max Age} + 2 \times \text{Forward Delay} \\
&= 20 + 2 \times 15 \\
&= 50 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

Plugging in the standard timers (Max Age = 20 s, Forward Delay = 15 s):

$$T_{\text{converge}} = 50 \; \text{s}$$

This is the classic "**why STP feels slow**" answer ŌĆö modern **Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP, 802.1w)** reduces this to under 6 seconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NetA[LAN Segment A]
      DevA1[Host A1]
      DevA2[Host A2]
    end
    subgraph NetB[LAN Segment B]
      DevB1[Host B1]
      DevB2[Host B2]
    end
    subgraph NetC[LAN Segment C]
      DevC1[Host C1]
      DevC2[Host C2]
    end

    NetA -- Frame In --> PortA[Port A Adapter]
    NetB -- Frame In --> PortB[Port B Adapter]
    NetC -- Frame In --> PortC[Port C Adapter]

    PortA --> Buffer[Frame Buffer and Parser]
    PortB --> Buffer
    PortC --> Buffer

    Buffer -->|SA, DA, Type| Engine{Bridge Engine}

    Engine <-->|Read or Write| MACtbl[(MAC Forwarding Table)]

    Engine -->|Forward| OutBuffer[Output Queue]
    Engine -->|Filter| DropBin[Discard Filtered Frame]
    Engine -->|Flood| FloodBus[Flood to all other ports]

    OutBuffer --> PortA
    OutBuffer --> PortB
    OutBuffer --> PortC

    FloodBus --> PortA
    FloodBus --> PortB
    FloodBus --> PortC
```

**How to read this diagram for KTU:**

1. The **Bridge Engine** is the central decision-making unit.
2. The **MAC Forwarding Table** is the persistent memory; note the double-headed arrow ŌĆö it is both *read* (for forwarding decisions) and *written* (during learning).
3. The three outputs ŌĆö *Forward, Filter, Flood* ŌĆö correspond directly to the three core functions (Section 2.1).

### 4.2 Transparent Bridge Learning Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Frame arrives on port Pn]) --> Parse[Parse Ethernet Header]
    Parse --> LearnCheck{Is Source MAC SA in table?}
    LearnCheck -- No --> AddEntry[Add entry: SA maps to Pn]
    LearnCheck -- Yes --> Refresh[Refresh age timer of SA entry]
    AddEntry --> DestCheck
    Refresh --> DestCheck
    DestCheck{Is DA broadcast or multicast?}
    DestCheck -- Yes --> FloodAll[Flood frame to all ports except Pn]
    DestCheck -- No --> UnicastCheck{Is DA in table?}
    UnicastCheck -- No --> FloodAll
    UnicastCheck -- Yes --> PortMatch{Is DA port same as Pn?}
    PortMatch -- Yes --> Filter[FILTER discard frame]
    PortMatch -- No --> Forward[FORWARD frame only to DA port]
    FloodAll --> End([End of frame handling])
    Filter --> End
    Forward --> End
```

### 4.3 Spanning Tree Protocol Election Flow (Root Bridge Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init([All bridges claim themselves Root]) --> BPDUEx{BPDU exchanged}
    BPDUEx --> Comp[Compare incoming Root Bridge ID with own]
    Comp --> Lower{Incoming Root ID lower than own?}
    Lower -- Yes --> AdoptNew[Adopt sender as superior Root]
    Lower -- No --> Reject[Keep own Bridge ID as Root]
    AdoptNew --> Propagate[Re-emit BPDU with superior Root ID]
    Reject --> Propagate
    Propagate --> Stable{All bridges agree on one Root?}
    Stable -- No --> Comp
    Stable -- Yes --> ElectPorts[Elect Root Port and Designated Ports per segment]
    ElectPorts --> Block[Block redundant ports]
    Block --> Done([Loop-free active topology])
```

### 4.4 Bridge vs Switch ŌĆö Functional Equivalence Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bridge[Two-port Bridge] -- Generalization --> Switch[Multi-port Switch]
    Bridge -- Function 1 --> Learn[Learning]
    Bridge -- Function 2 --> Filter[Filtering]
    Bridge -- Function 3 --> Forward[Forwarding or Flooding]
    Switch -- Function 1 --> Learn
    Switch -- Function 2 --> Filter
    Switch -- Function 3 --> Forward
    Note[Note: Modern switches add cut-through and VLAN tagging 802.1Q]
    Switch --- Note
```

### 4.5 Topology: Redundant Bridge Loop (Why STP is Needed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SW1[Bridge SW1] <--> SW2[Bridge SW2]
    SW2 <--> SW3[Bridge SW3]
    SW3 <--> SW1
    H1((Host)) --- SW1
    H2((Host)) --- SW2
    H3((Host)) --- SW3
    Arc[Without STP, broadcast frames circulate infinitely creating a storm]
    SW1 -.- Arc
    SW2 -.- Arc
    SW3 -.- Arc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 5-Mark Question Frequency:** "Why are loops a problem in bridged networks, and how does Spanning Tree Protocol solve it?" ŌĆö Use the diagram above to support your answer and earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]` Define a bridge. Differentiate between a bridge and a hub.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bridge** is a Layer-2 (Data Link Layer) device that interconnects two or more LAN segments, operating on **MAC addresses** to make intelligent forwarding, filtering, and flooding decisions based on a dynamically maintained bridging table.

| Parameter | Hub | Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| Address Used | None (bits only) | MAC (48-bit) |
| Collision Domain | Single shared | Segmented |
| Broadcast Domain | Single shared | Single shared (still) |
| Intelligence | None ŌĆö broadcasts all | Learns and filters |
| Frame Handling | Repeats electrical signal | Reads, processes, regenerates |

**(3 marks awarded for definition + 1 difference + 1 secondary point, with a clean comparison table.)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]` What is meant by "transparent bridging"? Why is it called transparent?**

**Model Answer:**

**Transparent bridging** is a bridging mode (defined by IEEE 802.1D) in which the bridges operate independently without requiring any configuration changes, software updates, or even awareness on the part of the end hosts. The bridges automatically build their MAC address tables by observing the source addresses of traffic.

It is called **"transparent"** because, from the perspective of any end station (host), the bridges are **invisible** ŌĆö a host sends frames to another host exactly as it would on a non-bridged LAN, completely unaware of the intermediate bridges, their topology, or their learning process. The end-user station does not require any special routing logic to use the bridged network.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for the transparency justification.)**

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module-2 Style: Each Part B question has internal choice. You must answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2024 ŌĆö Set A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and three operational functions** (Learning, Filtering, Forwarding/Flooding) of a transparent bridge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three LAN segments interconnected by two bridges B1 (LAN1ŌĆōLAN2) and B2 (LAN2ŌĆōLAN3). The following frames are sent in order:
1. `H1 ŌåÆ H5` on LAN1
2. `H6 ŌåÆ H4` on LAN3
3. `H4 ŌåÆ H5` on LAN3
4. `H2 ŌåÆ H6` on LAN1
5. `H5 ŌåÆ H2` on LAN3

Show the **complete contents of both bridge tables** after every transmission. Also indicate, for each step, whether the frame was *forwarded, filtered, or flooded*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

Provide the block diagram from **Section 4.1** of these notes. Then describe:

1. **Learning (3 marks):** Bridge examines SA of every frame. If `(SA, arrival_port)` is not in the table, it is added with a timestamp. This builds the table passively without any administrator configuration.
2. **Filtering (2 marks):** If the DA is found in the table on the *same port* as the source, the bridge drops the frame. Local traffic remains local.
3. **Forwarding / Flooding (2 marks):** If the DA is on a *different* port, the frame is forwarded *only* to that specific port. If the DA is *unknown*, the frame is flooded to all ports except the arrival port.

**(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

Walking through every step (apply the algorithm in **Section 3.1**):

| Step | Frame | B1 Action on arrival | B1 Table after | B2 Action on arrival | B2 Table after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | H1ŌåÆH5 (LAN1) | Learn (H1,LAN1); DA unknown ŌåÆ Flood to LAN2 | `{H1:LAN1}` | Learn (H1,LAN2); DA unknown ŌåÆ Flood to LAN3 | `{H1:LAN2}` |
| 2 | H6ŌåÆH4 (LAN3) | Learn (H6,LAN2); DA unknown ŌåÆ Flood to LAN1 | `{H1:LAN1; H6:LAN2}` | Learn (H6,LAN3); DA unknown ŌåÆ Flood to LAN2 | `{H1:LAN2; H6:LAN3}` |
| 3 | H4ŌåÆH5 (LAN3) | Learn (H4,LAN2); DA unknown ŌåÆ Flood to LAN1 | `{H1:LAN1; H6:LAN2; H4:LAN2}` | Learn (H4,LAN3); DA unknown ŌåÆ Flood to LAN2 | `{H1:LAN2; H6:LAN3; H4:LAN3}` |
| 4 | H2ŌåÆH6 (LAN1) | Learn (H2,LAN1); DA H6 on LAN2 ŌåÆ Forward to LAN2 | `{H1:LAN1; H6:LAN2; H4:LAN2; H2:LAN1}` | Learn (H2,LAN2); DA H6 on LAN3 ŌåÆ Forward to LAN3 | `{H1:LAN2; H6:LAN3; H4:LAN3; H2:LAN2}` |
| 5 | H5ŌåÆH2 (LAN3) | Learn (H5,LAN2); DA H2 on LAN1 ŌåÆ Forward to LAN1 | `{H1:LAN1; H6:LAN2; H4:LAN2; H2:LAN1; H5:LAN2}` | Learn (H5,LAN3); DA H2 on LAN2 = arrival port ŌåÆ **FILTER** (drop) | `{H1:LAN2; H6:LAN3; H4:LAN3; H2:LAN2; H5:LAN3}` |

**Filter instances:** Only **Step 5 at B2** exhibits true filtering (drop without forwarding). All other steps involve flooding or selective forwarding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Students frequently mark *all unknown-DA cases as "broadcast"*. The correct term is **"unknown unicast"**, flooded; only true broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) and multicast frames are unconditionally flooded.
> 2. Do **not** forget to update the timer / age column ŌĆö partial credit is often lost here.
> 3. Always state explicitly *which* port the bridge forwards to (LAN1, LAN2, or LAN3). Vague answers like "it forwards" lose 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2024 ŌĆö Set B]`

**(a)** What is the **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)**? Explain the **selection process of the Root Bridge** with a numerical example where the Bridge IDs are: B1 (priority=32768, MAC=AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA), B2 (priority=4096, MAC=BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB), B3 (priority=32768, MAC=CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC). Which bridge becomes the Root? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With diagrams, distinguish between **Transparent Bridges** and **Source Routing Bridges**. State two real-world scenarios where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D) is a Layer-2 link management protocol that prevents loops in bridged networks by creating a loop-free logical topology from a physically redundant mesh. It does so by **electing a single Root Bridge**, computing shortest paths to it, and **blocking** all redundant ports.

**Root Bridge Election Logic (3 marks):**
The Root Bridge is the bridge with the **lowest Bridge ID**.
$$\text{Bridge ID} = \text{Priority (16 bits)} \, \vert \, \text{MAC Address (48 bits)}$$

Comparing all candidates:

| Bridge | Priority | MAC | Decimal Equivalent (Priority├Ś2^48 + MAC) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | 32768 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | High | Not root |
| **B2** | **4096** | **BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB** | **Lowest** | **ROOT BRIDGE** |
| B3 | 32768 | CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC | High | Not root |

Since lower priority wins, and B2 has the lowest priority (4096), **B2 is elected as the Root Bridge** regardless of its MAC address (MAC comparison is only a tie-breaker if priorities are equal).

**Why B2 wins (2 marks):** Priority field is the primary tie-breaker, MAC is secondary. 4096 < 32768, so B2's Bridge ID is strictly smallest.

---

**(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Transparent Bridge (3 marks):**
- Operates at the data link layer using a **self-learning MAC table**.
- Hosts are unaware of bridge presence ŌĆö the bridge is "transparent" to them.
- Loop prevention via **Spanning Tree Protocol (802.1D)**.
- Standard in **Ethernet (802.3)** networks.

**Source Routing Bridge (SRB) (3 marks):**
- Used in **Token Ring (802.5)** networks.
- The **source host** determines the entire route and embeds it in the **Routing Information Field (RIF)** of the frame header.
- Bridges do *not* maintain forwarding tables; they simply read the route from the frame and forward accordingly.
- Loop prevention by **host-side route discovery** (Route Discovery frames).
- IBM-invented; rarely used in modern networks.

**Real-world scenarios (1 mark):**
- **Transparent**: All modern Ethernet office/campus LANs, cloud data centers.
- **Source Routing**: Legacy IBM Token Ring installations, some industrial control systems in older manufacturing plants.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Question B:**
> 1. **Priority tie-breaker confusion:** Many students think a lower MAC address always wins. The truth: **Priority field is the primary tie-breaker; MAC is only consulted when priorities are equal**.
> 2. **STP default priority value is 32768**, not 0 or 65535. A bridge with priority **4096** is artificially configured (likely by an administrator) to *force* it to become root.
> 3. **Source Routing** bridges do not need a forwarding table ŌĆö this is the *exact* opposite of a transparent bridge. Confusing the two is the #1 KTU blunder.

---

### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ A **bridge** is a **Layer-2 device** that uses **48-bit MAC addresses** to connect LAN segments.
- Ō£ģ Core functions: **Learning, Filtering, Forwarding/Flooding**.
- Ō£ģ Bridges **learn from the SOURCE address**; they **act on the DESTINATION address**.
- Ō£ģ Bridges divide **collision domains** but **NOT broadcast domains**.
- Ō£ģ A **switch** is essentially a **multi-port bridge** with parallel forwarding.
- Ō£ģ **Transparent bridging** = Ethernet default; hosts are unaware.
- Ō£ģ **Source routing** = Token Ring; the source dictates the path.
- Ō£ģ **Translational bridges** connect different 802.x LAN types (e.g., Ethernet Ōåö Token Ring).
- Ō£ģ **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP, 802.1D)** prevents broadcast storms in loops.
- Ō£ģ Root Bridge = lowest **Bridge ID** = lowest **priority**, then lowest **MAC**.
- Ō£ģ Default STP timers: **Hello = 2 s, Max Age = 20 s, Forward Delay = 15 s, Convergence Ōēł 50 s**.
- Ō£ģ **RSTP (802.1w)** reduces convergence to **under 6 seconds** ŌĆö frequently asked as a comparison.
- Ō£ģ A bridge **floods** an **unknown unicast**; it **forwards** a known unicast on a *different* port; it **filters** a known unicast on the *same* port.
- Ō£ģ KTU exam must include the **"action on each step"** explanation ŌĆö simply listing the final table loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
- Ō£ģ For **14-mark derivations**, always present: (i) initial state, (ii) step-by-step action with LEARN/FILTER/FORWARD/FLOOD label, (iii) updated table after each step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeaters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repeaters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Repeater** is an OSI **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** networking device that operates at the lowest level of the network protocol stack. Its sole function is to **regenerate, retime, and amplify** weakened electrical or optical signals transmitted over a network medium, thereby extending the effective communication distance beyond the inherent attenuation limits of the physical medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "A repeater is a physical layer device that receives, amplifies, and retransmits a signal along a network segment. It operates only at the bit level ŌĆö it does not interpret the data, perform addressing, or filter traffic."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are speaking to a friend standing **50 meters away** in a noisy playground. The further you go, the **quieter** (weaker) your voice becomes. Now, imagine placing a friend **right in the middle (at 25 m)** whose **only job** is to:

1. **Listen carefully** to what you say.
2. **Repeat the exact same words** at the **exact same volume** as the original.
3. **Forward it** to your distant friend.

That middle friend is a **Repeater**. They don't translate your message, don't add new words, and don't decide *who* the message is for ŌĆö they simply **restore the signal strength** so it can travel further.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Attenuation**: The gradual loss of signal strength (amplitude) as it travels through a physical medium.
- **Regeneration**: The process of reconstructing the original bit pattern (0s and 1s) from a degraded signal.
- **Retiming**: Restoring the original clock synchronization so the bits are placed at correct time intervals.
- **Amplification**: Boosting the amplitude of the signal (in analog sense) or reconstructing the binary waveform (in digital sense).

> [!NOTE]
> A **digital repeater** does NOT merely amplify noise along with the signal (unlike an analog amplifier). It actually **reconstructs** a clean, fresh copy of the original bit pattern ŌĆö this is called *regeneration*.

### 1.4 OSI Model Placement

The repeater operates **strictly at Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** of both the OSI and TCP/IP models. It has no MAC address, no IP address, and cannot interpret frames or packets.

| OSI Layer | Layer Name | Repeater Awareness |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| Layer 7 | Application | ŌØī No |
| Layer 4 | Transport | ŌØī No |
| Layer 3 | Network | ŌØī No |
| Layer 2 | Data Link | ŌØī No |
| **Layer 1** | **Physical** | Ō£ģ **YES ŌĆö Operates Here** |

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the standard KTU-board-relevant metrics for repeater operations:

- **Maximum Segment Length (copper twisted pair)**: **100 meters** (for 10BASE-T / 100BASE-TX Ethernet).
- **Maximum segment length (fiber optic)**: up to **2 km** (10BASE-FL) or **40 km+** for long-haul single-mode.
- **Maximum number of repeaters between two end stations (Ethernet 10BASE-5)**: **4 repeaters** (5-4-3 rule).
- **Total network diameter (classic Ethernet 10BASE-5)**: **2500 meters**.
- **Propagation speed in copper**: approximately **2 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s** (~ 200,000 km/s, about 66% of speed of light).
- **Propagation speed in fiber**: approximately **2 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s** (~ 200,000 km/s, with refractive index ~1.5).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Repeaters Are Needed ŌĆö The Physics of Signal Degradation

As an electrical or optical signal propagates through a transmission medium, three primary physical phenomena degrade it:

1. **Attenuation** ŌĆö Loss of signal amplitude over distance.
2. **Distortion** ŌĆö Change in waveform shape due to frequency-dependent propagation.
3. **Noise** ŌĆö Random electromagnetic interference added to the signal.

> [!NOTE]
> A signal can travel only as far as the **receiver's detection threshold**. Beyond that, the receiver cannot distinguish a binary `1` from a binary `0`. A repeater **re-energizes** the signal *before* it falls below this threshold.

### 2.2 Operational Block Logic (Step-by-Step)

A digital repeater performs the following sequence for *every* bit it receives:

1. **Sense the incoming signal** on the input port.
2. **Sample the signal** at the center of each bit period (clock recovery).
3. **Threshold detection** ŌĆö decide whether the bit is a `0` or `1` based on voltage/light threshold.
4. **Decision regeneration** ŌĆö generate a *clean*, full-strength `0` or `1` waveform.
5. **Retime** ŌĆö align the new waveform to the recovered clock.
6. **Retransmit** ŌĆö drive the clean signal out onto the outgoing segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Step 4 is the **defining** characteristic of a digital repeater. It does **not** "amplify" the noisy input. It **reconstructs** a perfect new signal from the *decision* of what the bit was. Noise on the input is therefore **discarded** at each repeater hop.

### 2.3 Types of Repeaters

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Electrical Repeater** | Regenerates electrical signals on copper media (twisted pair, coax). | LAN extensions, campus networks |
| **Optical Repeater (Optical-Electrical-Optical, OEO)** | Converts optical ŌåÆ electrical ŌåÆ optical for fiber spans. | Long-haul telecom, undersea cables |
| **Radio Repeater** | Receives and re-transmits RF signals. | Wireless backhaul, cellular towers |
| **Smart / Switching Repeater** | Modern repeater with some Layer 2 awareness (a hub, in practice). | Modern Ethernet |
| **Satellite Repeater (Transponder)** | Aboard a satellite, receives uplink and retransmits on downlink. | Satellite communication |

### 2.4 The 5-4-3 Rule (Classic Ethernet)

In classic **10BASE-5 (Thick Ethernet)** and **10BASE-2 (Thin Ethernet)** networks, repeaters were used to extend the network. KTU frequently tests this rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5-4-3 Rule:**
> - **5** ŌĆö Maximum of **5 network segments** in a single collision domain.
> - **4** ŌĆö Maximum of **4 repeaters** between any two end stations.
> - **3** ŌĆö Maximum of **3** of those segments can be **populated** (i.e., have end stations attached). The other 2 are **inter-repeater link segments** with no stations.

**Reason for 5-4-3 rule**: Round-trip propagation delay must remain within the Ethernet collision detection window (51.2 ┬Ąs for 10 Mbps Ethernet, i.e., 512 bit times). Adding more repeaters would increase the round-trip delay beyond this, and CSMA/CD would fail.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and standard for KTU board examinations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | One-way propagation delay | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | Time for a bit to traverse a segment of length $d$ at propagation speed $v$ |
| 2 | Round-trip propagation delay | $t_{rt} = 2 \cdot t_p$ | seconds | Time for signal to travel to far end and back |
| 3 | Maximum segment length (delay-limited) | $d_{max} = v \cdot \dfrac{t_{slot}}{2}$ | meters | Maximum cable length allowed for collision detection |
| 4 | Slot time (10 Mbps Ethernet) | $t_{slot} = 51.2 \, \mu s$ | ┬Ąs | Maximum round-trip time for collision detection |
| 5 | Total network diameter | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | meters | Sum of all segment lengths between two farthest stations |
| 6 | Bit transmission time | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds | Time to transmit 1 bit at data rate $R$ |
| 7 | Number of bits "in flight" on a segment | $N = \dfrac{d}{v} \cdot R$ | bits | Bits propagating simultaneously on the cable |
| 8 | Maximum nodes on classic Ethernet | **1024** (practical), **2500** stations (theoretical 10BASE-5) | ŌĆö | Driven by address space and signal budget |
| 9 | Attenuation (loss factor) | $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | dB | Standard decibel loss in a medium |
| 10 | Repeater gain requirement | $G \geq A_{dB,\text{segment}}$ | dB | Repeater gain must equal or exceed segment loss |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU table above, **do not use the `|` character** in any cell. The vertical bar is reserved as the markdown table column separator. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation if needed.

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Telecommunications backbone**: Optical repeaters (OE-OE-O) placed every 40ŌĆō80 km along undersea fiber-optic cables.
- **Cellular networks**: Radio repeaters on towers to extend coverage into shadow zones.
- **Industrial Ethernet**: Repeaters in factory floors to extend Profinet/Modbus TCP beyond 100 m copper limits.
- **Legacy LANs**: Extending 10BASE-2/5 coax Ethernet in older industrial plants.
- **Satellite communication**: Transponders on geostationary satellites are essentially radio repeaters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the 5-4-3 Rule Exists (Round-Trip Delay Constraint)

For CSMA/CD to function correctly in 10 Mbps Ethernet, the **round-trip propagation delay** between the two most distant stations must be less than the **slot time** (the time it takes to transmit 512 bits at 10 Mbps). This is so that a station that has just started transmitting can still detect a collision caused by a signal that is *already in flight* on the cable.

**Step 1: Express slot time from the bit rate.**

$$
t_{slot} = \frac{512 \text{ bits}}{R}
$$

Substituting $R = 10 \text{ Mbps} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$:

$$
t_{slot} = \frac{512}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 51.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 51.2 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2: State the round-trip delay constraint.**

For collision detection to be reliable:

$$
t_{round\text{-}trip} \leq t_{slot}
$$

That is:

$$
2 \cdot \frac{d_{max}}{v} \leq 51.2 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Solve for maximum one-way distance.**

$$
\frac{d_{max}}{v} \leq \frac{51.2 \, \mu s}{2} = 25.6 \, \mu s
$$

$$
d_{max} \leq v \cdot 25.6 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the propagation speed in coax (Ōēł 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s).**

$$
d_{max} \leq (2 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (25.6 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
d_{max} \leq 5120 \text{ m}
$$

So the absolute theoretical upper bound on a single coax run is 5120 m. Accounting for repeater delays, transceiver delays, and safety margins, the 10BASE-5 standard specifies a maximum diameter of **2500 m** with up to 4 repeaters (5 segments).

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Bits "In Flight" on a 100 m UTP Segment

**Step 1: One-way propagation delay on 100 m UTP.**

$$
t_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{100 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.5 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2: Bit transmission time at 100 Mbps (100BASE-TX).**

$$
t_{bit} = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns} = 0.01 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Bits in flight = (propagation time) ├Ę (bit transmission time).**

$$
N = \frac{t_p}{t_{bit}} = \frac{0.5 \, \mu s}{0.01 \, \mu s} = 50 \text{ bits}
$$

So at any given instant, **50 bits are simultaneously on the wire** during a 100 Mbps transmission across a 100 m UTP segment. This is why repeaters must retime carefully ŌĆö they must release each bit at the right moment.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Repeater vs. Other Network Devices

| Feature | Repeater | Hub | Bridge | Switch | Router |
|---------|----------|-----|--------|--------|--------|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 | Layer 1 | Layer 2 | Layer 2 | Layer 3 |
| Reads bits/frames? | Bits | Bits | Frames (MAC) | Frames (MAC) | Packets (IP) |
| Has MAC address? | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes (per port) | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Has IP address? | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Sometimes | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Collision domain | Same | Same | Separates | Separates (per port) | Separates |
| Broadcast domain | Same | Same | Same | Same | Separates |
| Filtering logic | None | None | MAC-based | MAC-based | IP-based |
| Number of ports | 2 (typical) | 4ŌĆō24 | 2ŌĆō4 | 4ŌĆō48 | 2ŌĆōmany |
| Smart / intelligent? | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Regenerates signal? | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes (partial) | Ō£ģ Yes (per port) | Ō£ģ Yes (per port) |
| Used today? | Rare (specialized) | Obsolete | Obsolete | Ō£ģ Common | Ō£ģ Common |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (frequently tested):**
A classic 10BASE-5 Ethernet network uses 4 repeaters connecting 5 coax segments. Each segment is 500 m long. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Determine:
1. Total one-way propagation delay.
2. Total round-trip propagation delay.
3. Is the network within the 10 Mbps Ethernet slot-time limit? (Assume repeater delay is negligible.)

**Solution:**

**(a) Total one-way distance:**

$$
d_{total} = 5 \times 500 = 2500 \text{ m}
$$

**(b) One-way propagation delay:**

$$
t_p = \frac{d_{total}}{v} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12.5 \, \mu s
$$

**(c) Round-trip propagation delay:**

$$
t_{rt} = 2 \cdot t_p = 2 \times 12.5 = 25 \, \mu s
$$

**(d) Compare with slot time:**

$$
t_{slot} = 51.2 \, \mu s
$$

$$
t_{rt} = 25 \, \mu s \leq 51.2 \, \mu s \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The network is **within the Ethernet slot-time limit** ŌĆö collision detection will work correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The safety margin is $51.2 - 25 = 26.2 \, \mu s$. If even one more repeater and 500 m segment were added, the round-trip would rise to $31.25 \, \mu s$, still within limits, but adding *two* more would push it beyond ŌĆö violating CSMA/CD.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Repeater Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT PORT\nSegment A\nIncoming Signal"] --> B["SIGNAL RECEIVER\nThreshold Detector\nSense Bit Level"]
    B --> C["DECISION LOGIC\nIdentify 0 or 1\nSample at Center"]
    C --> D["CLOCK RECOVERY\nRetiming Circuit\nRestore Bit Sync"]
    D --> E["SIGNAL REGENERATOR\nBuild Clean Waveform\nFull Amplitude"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT DRIVER\nPush to Output Port\nSegment B"]
    F --> G["OUTPUT PORT\nSegment B\nReconstructed Signal"]
    H["NOISE INPUT\nDegraded by EMI\nAttenuation"] -.-> B
```

### 4.2 Repeater Placement in an Extended LAN (5-4-3 Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Station 1\nSegment 1"] --- R1["Repeater 1"]
    R1 --- S2["Station 2\nSegment 2"]
    R1 --- R2["Repeater 2"]
    R2 --- S3["Station 3\nSegment 3\n(POPULATED)"]
    R2 --- R3["Repeater 3"]
    R3 --- S4["Link Segment 4\n(NO STATIONS)"]
    R3 --- R4["Repeater 4"]
    R4 --- S5["Station 5\nSegment 5\n(POPULATED)"]
    style S1 fill:#f9e79f
    style S2 fill:#f9e79f
    style S3 fill:#a9dfbf
    style S5 fill:#a9dfbf
    style S4 fill:#f5b7b1
    style R1 fill:#85c1e9
    style R2 fill:#85c1e9
    style R3 fill:#85c1e9
    style R4 fill:#85c1e9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Yellow** = Populated segments with end stations. **Green** = Other populated segments. **Red/Pink** = Link segment with no stations (used only to interconnect repeaters).

### 4.3 Signal Regeneration ŌĆö Waveform Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN["INPUT SIGNAL (DISTORTED)"]
        I1["Original Bit: 10110010\nAmplitude degraded by ~40%\nNoise overlay present\nTiming jittered"]
    end
    subgraph PRO["REPEATER PROCESSING"]
        P1["Step 1: Sample at center of bit period"]
        P2["Step 2: Compare to voltage threshold"]
        P3["Step 3: Decide 0 or 1"]
        P4["Step 4: Generate clean new waveform"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT SIGNAL (REGENERATED)"]
        O1["Bit: 10110010\nFull amplitude restored\nNoise removed\nTiming clean"]
    end
    IN --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 OSI Layer Placement of Repeater

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION\nHTTP, FTP, SMTP"]
    L6["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION\nTLS, JPEG, MPEG"]
    L5["LAYER 5 - SESSION\nNetBIOS, RPC"]
    L4["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT\nTCP, UDP"]
    L3["LAYER 3 - NETWORK\nIP, ICMP, OSPF"]
    L2["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK\nEthernet, MAC, PPP"]
    L1["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL\nBits on wire, cables, voltages"]
    R["REPEATER\nLayer 1 Device\nOperates ONLY Here"]
    R -.-> L1
    style R fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#ffe66d
    style L2 fill:#a8d5e2
    style L3 fill:#a8d5e2
    style L4 fill:#a8d5e2
    style L5 fill:#a8d5e2
    style L6 fill:#a8d5e2
    style L7 fill:#a8d5e2
```

### 4.5 Repeater vs. Hub Block Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REP["REPEATER (2 PORTS)"]
        RP1["Port 1 IN"] --> RE["REGENERATOR\nSingle Decision Unit"] --> RP2["Port 2 OUT"]
        RP2B["Port 2 IN"] --> RE2["REGENERATOR\nSingle Decision Unit"] --> RP1B["Port 1 OUT"]
    end
    subgraph HUB["HUB (MULTI-PORT REPEATER)"]
        HP1["Port 1"] --> HBUS["Shared Backplane\nAll bits broadcast\nto all other ports"]
        HP2["Port 2"] --> HBUS
        HP3["Port 3"] --> HBUS
        HP4["Port 4"] --> HBUS
    end
    style RE fill:#a9dfbf
    style RE2 fill:#a9dfbf
    style HBUS fill:#f5b7b1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **hub** is essentially a **multi-port repeater**. Architecturally identical function ŌĆö the regenerator is just shared across more ports and the signal is repeated to *every* other port simultaneously (no filtering).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]**
*Define a Repeater. At which layer of the OSI model does it operate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A repeater is a **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** networking device that receives a weakened signal on one network segment, **regenerates** it to its original strength and timing, and **retransmits** it onto another segment. It is used to extend the physical reach of a network beyond the natural attenuation limit of the transmission medium.

The repeater operates **only at Layer 1** of the OSI model. It works at the **bit level** ŌĆö it does not interpret frames, does not have a MAC or IP address, and performs no filtering or addressing. Its sole purpose is to combat **attenuation** and **signal distortion**.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):**
- Definition with layer specification: 1 mark
- Mention of signal regeneration/amplification: 1 mark
- Clarity on no addressing/filtering (Layer 1 only): 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]**
*State the 5-4-3 rule of Ethernet networking. Why is it needed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **5-4-3 rule** applies to classic 10BASE-5 (and 10BASE-2) Ethernet using repeaters:

- **5** ŌĆö Maximum of **5 segments** between the two farthest stations.
- **4** ŌĆö Maximum of **4 repeaters** between any two end stations.
- **3** ŌĆö Maximum of **3** segments may be **populated** (i.e., have end stations). The remaining 2 are repeater-to-repeater link segments with no stations.

It is needed because **CSMA/CD collision detection** requires the **round-trip propagation delay** between any two stations to remain less than the **slot time of 51.2 ┬Ąs** (at 10 Mbps). Beyond this, a station that has just transmitted would be unable to detect a collision from a distant station, breaking the Ethernet protocol.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):**
- Correct statement of 5-4-3: 1.5 marks
- Reason (CSMA/CD + slot time 51.2 ┬Ąs): 1.5 marks

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

## Ō£Å’ĖÅ CHOICE A ŌĆö 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture and working of a digital repeater. How does it differ from a simple analog amplifier? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A digital repeater consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Signal Receiver / Front End**: Receives the analog waveform from the input segment. Includes line equalization to compensate for frequency-dependent loss.

2. **Threshold Detector**: Samples the incoming signal at the **center of each bit cell** and compares the voltage/light level against a fixed threshold. Output: binary decision (0 or 1).

3. **Clock Recovery Circuit (PLL)**: Extracts a clean bit clock from the incoming bit stream using a phase-locked loop. This is essential for retiming.

4. **Decision & Regeneration Logic**: Stores the binary decision and uses the recovered clock to generate a **new, clean waveform** with full amplitude and proper bit timing.

5. **Output Driver**: Pushes the reconstructed signal onto the outgoing segment with sufficient drive strength.

**Key Difference ŌĆö Digital Repeater vs. Analog Amplifier:**

| Aspect | Analog Amplifier | Digital Repeater |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Output | Amplified input + amplified **noise** | **New clean signal** with noise removed |
| Decision logic | None (linear) | Bit-level decision (threshold) |
| Noise handling | Noise accumulates with each hop | Noise **discarded** at each hop |
| Retiming | No | Yes (PLL-based) |
| Use case | Audio, RF, generic | Digital data transmission |
| Bit error rate | Grows with hops | Roughly constant across hops |

**Why digital repeaters are superior for data**: If 10 analog amplifiers are cascaded, the noise grows ~10├Ś. If 10 digital repeaters are cascaded, each regenerates a clean signal ŌĆö the end-to-end BER is no worse than a single hop.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- Block diagram with labels: 2 marks
- Working of each block: 3 marks
- Comparison table (digital vs analog): 2 marks

---

**Q.A (b)** A 10BASE-5 Ethernet network consists of 5 segments connected by 4 repeaters. Each segment is 400 m long. Propagation speed in coax is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute the round-trip propagation delay and verify whether the network satisfies the CSMA/CD requirement. Assume repeater and transceiver delays are negligible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Total one-way network distance.**

$$
d_{total} = 5 \text{ segments} \times 400 \text{ m} = 2000 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: One-way propagation delay.**

$$
t_p = \frac{d_{total}}{v} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 10 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Round-trip propagation delay.**

$$
t_{rt} = 2 \cdot t_p = 2 \times 10 \, \mu s = 20 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 4: Ethernet slot time (10 Mbps).**

$$
t_{slot} = \frac{512 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} = 51.2 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 5: Compare with constraint.**

$$
t_{rt} = 20 \, \mu s \quad \text{vs} \quad t_{slot} = 51.2 \, \mu s
$$

$$
20 \, \mu s \ll 51.2 \, \mu s \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The network comfortably satisfies the CSMA/CD requirement. A station at one extreme can detect a collision from the other extreme within the slot time. The safety margin is $51.2 - 20 = 31.2 \, \mu s$.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- Total distance calculation: 1 mark
- One-way delay: 1.5 marks
- Round-trip delay: 1 mark
- Slot time formula + value: 1.5 marks
- Comparison and conclusion: 2 marks

---

## Ō£Å’ĖÅ CHOICE B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Set)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Supplementary Exam 2023]**

**Q.B (a)** Compare and contrast a **Repeater, Hub, Bridge, and Router** based on: OSI layer of operation, addressing awareness, collision domain behavior, and modern usage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Feature | Repeater | Hub | Bridge | Router |
|---------|----------|-----|--------|--------|
| OSI Layer | **Layer 1** | **Layer 1** | **Layer 2** | **Layer 3** |
| Operates on | **Bits** | **Bits** | **Frames (MAC)** | **Packets (IP)** |
| Has MAC address? | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Has IP address? | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Collision domain | **Same** (joined) | **Same** (joined) | **Separates** | **Separates** |
| Broadcast domain | Same | Same | Same | **Separates** |
| Number of ports | 2 (typical) | 4ŌĆō48 | 2ŌĆō4 | 2ŌĆōmany |
| Filtering logic | None | None | MAC-based | IP-based |
| Routing decisions? | ŌØī | ŌØī | ŌØī | Ō£ģ |
| Modern use | Specialized (fiber, RF) | ŌØī Obsolete | ŌØī Obsolete | Ō£ģ Ubiquitous |
| Smart? | No | No | Yes | Yes |

**Layer-by-layer analogy**:
- A **repeater** is like a megaphone ŌĆö it just makes the same message louder.
- A **hub** is like a loudspeaker in a hall ŌĆö it broadcasts the same message to everyone.
- A **bridge** is like a receptionist ŌĆö they read the name (MAC) on the letter and forward it to the right department.
- A **router** is like a postal sorting office ŌĆö they read the destination address (IP) and choose the best route across cities.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- Comparison table with all 4 devices: 4 marks
- Correct OSI layer identification for each: 1 mark
- Collision/broadcast domain behavior: 1 mark
- Modern relevance comment: 1 mark

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain with a diagram the **5-4-3 rule** for classic Ethernet repeater networks. Why can't we have 6 segments with 5 repeaters? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**5-4-3 Rule Definition:**

In 10BASE-5 (and 10BASE-2) Ethernet using coaxial cable and electrical repeaters, the maximum configuration allowed between any two end stations is:

- **5 network segments** in series
- **4 repeaters** connecting those segments
- **3 segments** can have **end stations** attached (the other 2 are repeater-to-repeater link segments)

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2):** A linear chain of 4 repeaters creating 5 segments, with stations on segments 1, 3, and 5.

**Why can't we have 6 segments with 5 repeaters?**

**Reason 1: Round-trip delay violation.**
Each 500 m segment contributes $t_p = 500 / (2 \times 10^8) = 2.5 \, \mu s$ of one-way delay. Six segments ŌåÆ $6 \times 2.5 = 15 \, \mu s$ one-way, so $30 \, \mu s$ round-trip. While this still satisfies 51.2 ┬Ąs, real repeaters introduce additional delay (~2ŌĆō3 ┬Ąs each). Five repeaters add ~10 ┬Ąs, pushing the total round-trip beyond 51.2 ┬Ąs in many real configurations.

**Reason 2: Signal budget / attenuation.**
Each repeater must regenerate the full signal. A signal that traverses 6 segments of 500 m each (3000 m total) is heavily attenuated. With passive coax, the attenuation budget is exceeded before reliable regeneration.

**Reason 3: Collision detection window.**
CSMA/CD's defining requirement is that the **transmitter must still be transmitting** when a collision signal returns. Beyond 4 repeaters, the round-trip delay becomes comparable to the time to transmit the minimum 64-byte frame (51.2 ┬Ąs at 10 Mbps), and the protocol can no longer guarantee collision detection.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- 5-4-3 rule statement with diagram: 2.5 marks
- Round-trip delay argument: 1.5 marks
- Signal budget argument: 1.5 marks
- CSMA/CD window explanation: 1.5 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating repeater with hub**: Many students write "repeater and hub are the same." They are functionally similar (Layer 1, bit regeneration), but a hub is a *multi-port* repeater with a shared backplane ŌĆö be precise in language.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in delay calculations**: Marks are deducted if you write $t_p = 2000 / 2 \times 10^8 = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ without stating "**seconds**" or converting to ┬Ąs. Always include the unit.
>
> 3. **Misstating the 5-4-3 rule**: Many students say "5 repeaters, 4 segments, 3 stations." The correct version is **5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments**. Memorize correctly.
>
> 4. **Confusing "amplification" with "regeneration"**: A digital repeater does NOT amplify ŌĆö it **regenerates** a clean new signal. Using the word "amplify" loses 0.5ŌĆō1 mark in descriptive answers.
>
> 5. **Claiming repeaters have MAC/IP addresses**: They do not. This is a common error that loses marks in comparison questions.

---

### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Fire Recall ŌĆö Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- Ō£ģ Repeater = **OSI Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** device ŌĆö operates at **bit level only**.
- Ō£ģ Function = **Receive ŌåÆ Regenerate ŌåÆ Retime ŌåÆ Retransmit** (the "3 R's of a digital repeater").
- Ō£ģ A digital repeater **rejects noise**; an analog amplifier **amplifies noise**.
- Ō£ģ **No MAC address, No IP address, No frame interpretation, No filtering**.
- Ō£ģ Used to combat **attenuation** (loss of signal strength over distance).
- Ō£ģ **5-4-3 rule**: 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments (classic coax Ethernet).
- Ō£ģ **Slot time = 51.2 ┬Ąs** at 10 Mbps Ethernet; defines collision detection window.
- Ō£ģ **Round-trip delay** = $\dfrac{2d}{v}$ ŌĆö must be Ōēż 51.2 ┬Ąs for CSMA/CD to work.
- Ō£ģ **Propagation speed** in copper/fiber Ōēł $2 \times 10^8$ m/s (~ 66% of $c$).
- Ō£ģ Types: **Electrical, Optical (OEO), Radio, Satellite (transponder)**.
- Ō£ģ A **hub is a multi-port repeater** ŌĆö same Layer 1 behavior, multiple ports.
- Ō£ģ Repeaters **do NOT separate collision domains** ŌĆö they join them.
- Ō£ģ Repeaters **do NOT separate broadcast domains** ŌĆö they join them.
- Ō£ģ Repeaters are largely **obsolete in modern wired Ethernet** (replaced by switches), but still critical in **fiber-optic long-haul** and **wireless/RF** systems.
- Ō£ģ Attenuation in decibels: $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$.
- Ō£ģ For 10BASE-T and 100BASE-TX, max segment length is **100 m** ŌĆö repeater extends beyond this.
- Ō£ģ **5-4-3 rule applies only to half-duplex, shared-media coax Ethernet** ŌĆö not to switched/full-duplex networks.

> [!NOTE]
> ­¤ōī **Final KTU Board Tip:** In any 14-mark question, always include a **diagram** (even a hand-drawn one in the exam) ŌĆö examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks specifically for neat, labeled schematics. A repeater block diagram will score you at least 2 marks even if your written explanation is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hubs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hubs # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Ethernet Hub** (also called a *concentrator* or *repeater hub*) is a **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** networking device used to connect multiple Ethernet devices in a Local Area Network (LAN), making them function as a single network segment. It possesses multiple **RJ-45 ports** (typically **4, 8, 12, 16, or 24 ports**), each acting as an input/output interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (verbatim alignment):** A hub is a physical layer device that simply repeats (re-amplifies and re-times) the electrical signals received on one port out to *all other active ports*, without any frame inspection, MAC address learning, or filtering logic. ### Key Operational Properties (as per IEEE 802.3 standard) | Property | Value/Behavior | |---|---| | OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | | Signal Handling | Bit-level regeneration | | Addressing Awareness | **None** | | Port Speed Symmetry | All ports share a common speed (**10 Mbps** or **100 Mbps**) | | Logical Topology | Logical **Star** (Physical **Star**) | | Bandwidth | **Shared** across all ports | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Megaphone-in-a-Roundabout" Imagine **eight friends** sitting around a **circular dinner table** (a roundabout). In the center of the table, there is a **megaphone (the Hub)** with eight microphone cords, one stretching to each friend. - When **Friend 1** speaks into their cord, the megaphone does **not understand** the message, **does not decide** who should hear it, and **does not check** who sent it. It simply **blasts the voice out through all the other seven cords** simultaneously. - Every other friend (including Friend 1, due to physical echo back) **hears the message**, even if it was meant for only one specific person. The intended recipient just *chooses* to respond; the others *choose* to ignore. - If **two friends** speak into their cords at the **exact same instant**, the megaphone broadcasts a **jumbled mess (a collision)** ŌĆö both signals collide and become unintelligible. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Takeaway:** A hub is essentially a "dumb electrical broadcaster." It has zero intelligence. It treats all incoming bits as a generic electrical pulse and rebroadcasts them. It is the *opposite* of a switch, which is "smart" and only forwards frames to the correct destination port. ## 1.3 Classification of Hubs (KTU Syllabus Mapping) KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes **three principal categories** of hubs based on signal processing capability: > [!NOTE] > **1. Passive Hub** ŌĆö Acts purely as a *physical connector / signal splitter*. It does **not regenerate** or amplify signals. It is essentially a multi-port junction box. Maximum recommended distance from a passive hub to a node is very short (typically **< 30 m**). It cannot extend the maximum cable length of a segment. > > **2. Active Hub (Repeater Hub)** ŌĆö Contains **powered electronic circuitry** that **regenerates, re-times, and amplifies** incoming signals before retransmission. This extends the maximum allowable cable length. This is the **most common** type of hub. > > **3. Intelligent Hub (Smart Hub / Manageable Hub)** ŌĆö An active hub augmented with **SNMP-based management features** (monitoring, port configuration, traffic statistics). It is essentially a primitive version of a managed switch used historically in network operations centers. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step by Step The internal operation of a hub can be broken down into **five sequential steps**, all executed in **hardware** (no software, no CPU decision-making): 1. **Signal Reception (Bit In):** A bit (or electrical pulse) arrives on one of the RJ-45 ports. The hub's PHY (Physical Layer transceiver) detects the voltage transition on the twisted-pair cable. 2. **Signal Decoding to Bit Stream:** The PHY converts the analog electrical waveform back into a digital bit stream (0s and 1s). The hub does not interpret these bits as a frame ŌĆö they are just electrical states. 3. **Regeneration & Re-timing (Active Hub only):** The bit stream is *re-amplified* to its full original voltage amplitude and *re-clocked* to remove jitter. This is essentially the function of a **single-port repeater** applied to all ports. 4. **Broadcast to All Other Ports:** The bit stream is then driven out on **every other active port simultaneously** (the originating port is excluded by default, though some old hubs echoed it back). 5. **Collision Detection Propagation:** If a hub detects a collision (excessive voltage on the line), it sends out a **32-bit Jam Sequence** (`10101010...` pattern) to all ports to ensure all stations recognize the collision. This is the **Collision Detection Enforcement (CDE)** function. ## 2.2 The Collision Domain Principle > [!WARNING] > **The single most important rule of hubs:** **All ports of a hub belong to the same Collision Domain AND the same Broadcast Domain.** - **Collision Domain:** A set of devices whose simultaneous transmissions can collide with each other. - **Broadcast Domain:** A set of devices that receive a broadcast frame sent by any one of them. Because a hub blindly forwards every bit to every other port, a frame sent by Station A reaches Station B, C, D, E, F, G, and H. If Stations C and F decide to transmit at the *same instant*, their frames *will* collide on the hub's shared backplane. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **5-4-3 Rule** (Legacy 10BASE-T) | Max **5** segments, **4** repeaters/hubs, **3** populated segments in a single collision domain | IEEE 802.3 limit | | 2 | **Maximum Nodes per Collision Domain** | Strictly defined by the **CSMA/CD slot time** & cable length | For 10BASE-T ŌåÆ max **4 hubs** chained | | 3 | **Worst-case Network Diameter** | $D_{max} = 5 \times 2500 \text{ m} = 12{,}500 \text{ m}$ (legacy 10BASE5) | Coaxial segment max | | 4 | **Slot Time (10 Mbps Ethernet)** | $t_{slot} = 51.2 \ \mu s$ | Equals 512 bit times | | 5 | **Slot Time (100 Mbps Fast Ethernet)** | $t_{slot} = 5.12 \ \mu s$ | Equals 512 bit times (bit rate scales) | | 6 | **Frame Transmission Time** | $t_{tx} = \dfrac{L_{frame}}{R}$ where $L$ = bits, $R$ = bps | seconds | | 7 | **Hub Bandwidth Formula** | $B_{effective} = \dfrac{B_{link}}{N}$ | $N$ = number of simultaneously active hosts | | 8 | **Collision Probability (qualitative)** | Increases with **N** active stations | Linear growth | | 9 | **Bit Time at 10 Mbps** | $t_{bit} = 100 \text{ ns}$ | $\dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^6}$ | | 10 | **CSMA/CD Condition** | $t_{tx} \geq 2 \times t_{prop}$ | Round-trip propagation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipe symbol `|`** is used inside the table cells ŌĆö all set/cardinality notation uses `\vert` for math compatibility. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Legacy Lab Networks:** Still found in introductory computer networking labs (KTU PEEET528 lab sessions) for teaching CSMA/CD and collision detection. - **Network Tapping & Packet Capture:** Hubs are deliberately deployed by network forensics teams because they broadcast *all* traffic to *all* ports, allowing a single monitoring station to passively capture every packet on the LAN without SPAN port configuration. - **Industrial Sensor Aggregation:** Some low-cost industrial hubs aggregate sensor data over short distances where bandwidth is non-critical. - **Predecessor to Switches:** Hubs represent the **first generation** of Ethernet connectivity devices. Modern deployments exclusively use **switches** due to the hub's severe bandwidth inefficiency. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throughput per Node in a Hub This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** for understanding why hubs are inefficient. **Problem Setup:** Consider a hub with **N** ports, each operating at link rate **R** bps. All N nodes share the same physical medium (the hub's backplane). Under CSMA/CD, only *one* successful transmission can occur at a time across the entire hub. **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the shared medium rate:** The hub's internal backplane is a *single shared channel* with capacity $R_{backplane} = R$ bps. $$ R_{backplane} = R \ \text{bps} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Define aggregate demand:** If all N nodes attempt to transmit simultaneously, the *aggregate offered load* is: $$ R_{offered} = N \times R \ \text{bps (peak)} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Define effective per-station throughput:** Because of CSMA/CD arbitration and collision back-off, only *one* frame is on the wire at a time. The *average* per-station throughput under heavy load is: $$ B_{per\_node} = \frac{R}{N} \quad \text{(ideal upper bound)} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Account for collisions and overhead:** In practice, collisions consume bandwidth. Let $p_c$ be the collision probability and $t_{overhead}$ be the CSMA/CD overhead per attempt. The realistic effective bandwidth becomes: $$ B_{eff} = \frac{R}{N} \times (1 - p_c) \times \left(1 - \frac{t_{overhead}}{t_{slot}}\right) $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Example numerical evaluation:** Take $R = 100$ Mbps, $N = 8$ active hosts, $p_c = 0.1$ (10% collision rate), $t_{overhead} = 25.6 \ \mu s$ (jam + backoff), $t_{slot} = 51.2 \ \mu s$: $$ B_{eff} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{8} \times (1 - 0.1) \times \left(1 - \frac{25.6}{51.2}\right) $$ $$ B_{eff} = 12.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.9 \times 0.5 $$ $$ B_{eff} = 5.625 \ \text{Mbps per node} $$ **Conclusion:** Each of the 8 nodes gets only **~5.6 Mbps** of effective throughput, even though each is connected at **100 Mbps**. This is the *fundamental inefficiency* of hubs. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: 5-4-3 Rule Justification The **5-4-3 Rule** states that in a **10BASE-T/10BASE-FL** Ethernet network using repeaters/hubs, between any two communicating stations, the signal path may traverse: - A maximum of **5** cable segments - A maximum of **4** repeaters/hubs - A maximum of **3** *populated* (i.e., with hosts attached) segments **Justification through Slot Time:** The slot time $t_{slot} = 51.2 \ \mu s$ is the maximum time a station must wait to detect a collision after it begins transmission. This bounds the maximum one-way propagation delay $t_{prop}$: $$ 2 \times t_{prop} \leq t_{slot} $$ $$ t_{prop} \leq \frac{51.2 \ \mu s}{2} = 25.6 \ \mu s $$ This corresponds to a maximum cable length budget of ~2500 m for the entire round trip. Exceeding this causes *late collisions* ŌĆö collisions detected *after* the first 64 bytes of the frame, which CSMA/CD cannot recover from, leading to lost frames. > [!IMPORTANT] > The 5-4-3 rule is **obsolete for 100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet** (max 2 hubs between nodes) and **does not apply** to full-duplex switched Ethernet. KTU may ask this as a 3-mark question under the historical/legacy context. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hub vs Bridge vs Switch vs Router | Feature | **Hub** | **Bridge** | **Switch** | **Router** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 | Layer 2 | Layer 2 | Layer 3 | | **Collision Domains** | **1 (all ports)** | 2 | **N (one per port)** | N (one per interface) | | **Broadcast Domains** | 1 | 1 | 1 | N | | **Address Awareness** | None | MAC | MAC (with table) | IP | | **Forwarding Method** | Flood to all | Selective (by MAC) | Selective + simultaneous | Routing table lookup | | **Frame Buffering** | No | Yes (small) | Yes (large) | Yes | | **Typical Use** | Legacy LANs | Segment linking | Modern LANs | Inter-network | | **Intelligence Level** | Very Low | LowŌĆōMedium | High | Very High | | **Bandwidth Efficiency** | **Poor** (shared) | Medium | **Excellent** (per-port) | Excellent | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often confuse *broadcast domain* and *collision domain* in this comparison. A router is the **only** device that breaks **both** broadcast and collision domains. A switch breaks collision domains but **not** broadcast domains. A hub breaks **neither**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Hub Internal Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Port 1
RJ45] --> B[PHY
Transceiver] C[Port 2
RJ45] --> D[PHY
Transceiver] E[Port 3
RJ45] --> F[PHY
Transceiver] G[Port 4
RJ45] --> H[PHY
Transceiver] B --> I[Signal
Regenerator] D --> I F --> I H --> I I --> J[Collision
Detector] J --> K[Hub
Backplane] K --> B K --> D K --> F K --> H L[Jam Sequence
Generator] --> K M[Power Supply
+ LED Indicators] -.-> I ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Each port has a dedicated **PHY transceiver** (converts analog Ōåö digital). - All PHYs feed into a **central Signal Regenerator** (the multi-port repeater core). - The **Collision Detector** monitors voltage levels on the backplane to detect simultaneous transmissions. - The **Jam Sequence Generator** produces the 32-bit collision enforcement pattern. - The **Power Supply** drives regeneration and LEDs. --- ## 4.2 Logical Topology ŌĆö Single Shared Collision Domain ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CD1["COLLISION DOMAIN 1 (Hub + all attached hosts)"] HUB[Active Ethernet Hub
8 ports
Layer 1] H1((Host A)) H2((Host B)) H3((Host C)) H4((Host D)) H5((Host E)) H6((Host F)) H7((Host G)) H8((Host H)) HUB --- H1 HUB --- H2 HUB --- H3 HUB --- H4 HUB --- H5 HUB --- H6 HUB --- H7 HUB --- H8 end style HUB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style CD1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice the **dashed border** around the collision domain ŌĆö this visually emphasizes that *every* host in the dashed region shares a single collision domain. Any two hosts transmitting simultaneously will collide. --- ## 4.3 Hub vs Switch ŌĆö Traffic Flow Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HUB_NET["HUB NETWORK - Broadcast to all"] H_S1((Sender)) --> H_CENT[Hub
Floods Everywhere] H_CENT --> H_R1((Receiver A)) H_CENT --> H_R2((Receiver B)) H_CENT --> H_R3((Receiver C)) H_CENT --> H_R4((Receiver D)) end subgraph SW_NET["SWITCH NETWORK - Direct delivery"] S_S1((Sender)) --> S_CENT[Switch
Forwards selectively] S_CENT --> S_R1((Receiver A)) S_CENT -. no traffic .-> S_R2((Receiver B)) S_CENT -. no traffic .-> S_R3((Receiver C)) S_CENT -. no traffic .-> S_R4((Receiver D)) end style H_CENT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 style S_CENT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The switch achieves **parallel forwarding** ŌĆö multiple conversations can occur simultaneously on different port pairs. The hub forces **serialized sharing** ŌĆö only one conversation at a time across the entire backplane. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an Ethernet Hub. List any two of its limitations.** **Model Answer:** > An **Ethernet Hub** is a Physical Layer (Layer 1) device that connects multiple Ethernet devices into a single network segment. It operates by **receiving electrical signals on one port and re-broadcasting them to all other ports**, without any frame filtering or address learning. **Two limitations:** 1. **Shared Bandwidth:** All connected devices share the total available bandwidth ($B_{per\_node} = R / N$), causing severe throughput degradation as N increases. 2. **Single Collision Domain:** All ports belong to one collision domain, leading to frequent collisions and reliance on CSMA/CD, which limits network efficiency to roughly **30ŌĆō40%** of raw link capacity under load. *(For third point if asked: No intelligent forwarding, no security segmentation, no Quality of Service support.)* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between an Active Hub and a Passive Hub.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | **Passive Hub** | **Active Hub** | |---|---|---| | **Signal Regeneration** | **None** ŌĆö only physical connection | **Yes** ŌĆö amplifies & re-times signals | | **Power Supply** | **Not required** | **Required** (has internal electronics) | | **Function** | Acts as a simple signal splitter | Acts as a **multi-port repeater** | | **Cable Length Extension** | Cannot extend | **Can extend** network diameter | | **Use Case** | Very small, short-distance clusters | Standard LAN deployment | --- ## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture and working of an Active Ethernet Hub**. Discuss the role of the **Collision Detection** mechanism. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **5-4-3 Rule** for legacy Ethernet networks. Why is this rule **not applicable to switched full-duplex Ethernet**? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part A(a) **Internal Architecture of an Active Hub:** An active hub consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **RJ-45 Ports (typically 4, 8, 12, 16, 24):** Each provides a physical interface for a twisted-pair cable. 2. **PHY Transceiver (per port):** Converts analog signals on the cable to digital bit streams and vice versa. 3. **Signal Regenerator:** The central block that **re-amplifies and re-clocks** incoming bits to restore their full voltage amplitude and remove timing jitter. 4. **Collision Detector:** Continuously monitors the backplane for voltage anomalies indicating simultaneous transmissions. 5. **Jam Sequence Generator:** Emits a 32-bit pattern (`10101010...`) on detecting a collision, to alert all stations. 6. **Backplane:** The shared internal bus that distributes regenerated signals to all ports. **Working Principle:** - A bit arriving on **Port 3** is detected by the PHY, decoded to digital, passed to the regenerator, re-amplified, and broadcast out on **Ports 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8** (all except the source port). - The hub performs **no MAC address lookup** ŌĆö every frame is flooded to all ports. **Role of Collision Detection:** When two stations transmit simultaneously, the voltage on the backplane exceeds the normal signal level. The **Collision Detector** senses this and triggers the **Jam Sequence Generator** to send a 32-bit jam pattern on all ports. This ensures all stations recognize the collision and invoke the **CSMA/CD back-off algorithm** (Binary Exponential Backoff). Without collision detection, a hub would silently corrupt frames. --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part A(b) **5-4-3 Rule Explanation:** In a 10BASE-T Ethernet LAN using hubs (repeaters), the rule states that between any two communicating stations: - There can be at most **5** cable segments. - There can be at most **4** hubs (repeaters). - Of the 5 segments, at most **3** can have hosts (end stations) attached to them. **Why the rule exists:** This rule ensures the **maximum one-way propagation delay** between any two stations is bounded by the **slot time** of 51.2 ╬╝s, so that CSMA/CD can detect collisions before the first 64 bytes of a frame are transmitted: $$ 2 \times t_{prop} \leq 51.2 \ \mu s $$ Exceeding this delay causes **late collisions** that CSMA/CD cannot recover from. **Why it does NOT apply to switched full-duplex Ethernet:** 1. **Full-duplex mode disables CSMA/CD** ŌĆö there are no collisions because transmit and receive channels are independent. 2. **Each switch port is its own collision domain** ŌĆö there is no shared medium, so the propagation-delay constraint is irrelevant. 3. **Switches use store-and-forward or cut-through** switching, eliminating the half-duplex timing requirements. 4. **Modern 100/1000 Mbps Ethernet** has tighter timing budgets, and the rule was originally specified for **10 Mbps half-duplex** only. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Compare and contrast a **Hub, Bridge, Switch, and Router** based on OSI layer, collision domain handling, and forwarding logic. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A hub has **8 active stations** each connected at **100 Mbps**. Calculate the **effective bandwidth per station** under CSMA/CD if the **collision probability is 5%** and the **overhead-to-slot ratio is 0.4**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part B(a) **Comparison Table:** | Device | OSI Layer | Collision Domains | Forwarding Logic | |---|---|---|---| | **Hub** | Layer 1 (Physical) | **1 (all ports shared)** | Flood to all ports | | **Bridge** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | **2** | Selective forwarding using MAC table | | **Switch** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | **N (one per port)** | Selective forwarding with parallel conversations | | **Router** | Layer 3 (Network) | **N (one per interface)** | IP-based routing table lookup | **Key Narrative:** - The **Hub** is the simplest, treating the entire LAN as one shared medium. - The **Bridge** connects two LAN segments and learns MAC addresses to filter traffic. - The **Switch** is essentially a **multi-port bridge** that allows *simultaneous* conversations between different port pairs, effectively multiplying total throughput. - The **Router** operates at the network layer and makes forwarding decisions based on **IP addresses**, additionally breaking broadcast domains (unlike switches and bridges). --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part B(b) **Given:** - $N = 8$ stations - $R = 100$ Mbps - $p_c = 0.05$ - $\dfrac{t_{overhead}}{t_{slot}} = 0.4$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Ideal per-station bandwidth:** $$ B_{ideal} = \frac{R}{N} = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5 \ \text{Mbps} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply collision loss factor:** $$ B_1 = 12.5 \times (1 - 0.05) = 12.5 \times 0.95 = 11.875 \ \text{Mbps} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply overhead factor:** $$ B_{eff} = 11.875 \times (1 - 0.4) = 11.875 \times 0.6 = 7.125 \ \text{Mbps} $$ **Result:** Each station gets only **~7.125 Mbps** out of the **100 Mbps** link ŌĆö a utilization of just **7.125%**. **Comment:** This demonstrates the **fundamental scalability limitation** of hubs. As the number of active stations grows, the effective bandwidth per station **decreases linearly** while the hardware cost per port remains fixed. A switch would solve this by providing **dedicated 100 Mbps per port**, yielding an aggregate of **800 Mbps** simultaneous throughput. --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤Ü© KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Hubs Topic** > 1. **Do NOT confuse collision domain with broadcast domain.** A hub is one of *both*. A switch breaks *collision* domains but not *broadcast* domains. A router breaks *both*. > 2. **Do NOT say "hub works at Layer 2."** Hubs work strictly at **Layer 1**. Saying "Layer 2" will cost you 1 mark minimum. > 3. **In numerical problems, always show all substitution steps.** Marks are awarded for intermediate steps, not just the final answer. > 4. **Avoid using the word "switch" interchangeably with "hub."** Examiners will deduct marks for terminological inaccuracy. > 5. **5-4-3 Rule is for 10BASE-T only.** Do not apply it to Fast Ethernet or Gigabit Ethernet. --- ## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö HUBS** - Ō£ģ Hub = **Layer 1** device (Physical Layer only) - Ō£ģ Hub = **multi-port repeater** (active type is most common) - Ō£ģ Hub **floods bits to all other ports** ŌĆö no intelligence - Ō£ģ **Single collision domain** for all ports - Ō£ģ **Single broadcast domain** for all ports - Ō£ģ All ports share the **same bandwidth** ($B_{eff} = R / N$) - Ō£ģ Hub does **NOT** perform MAC learning, filtering, or buffering - Ō£ģ Hub does **NOT** support full-duplex ŌĆö only **half-duplex** - Ō£ģ CSMA/CD is **mandatory** on hub-connected segments - Ō£ģ 5-4-3 rule: legacy 10BASE-T hub networks, max 5 segments / 4 hubs / 3 populated - Ō£ģ **Slot time** = 51.2 ╬╝s (at 10 Mbps) governs the maximum network diameter - Ō£ģ Hub advantages: cheap, simple, broadcast makes packet sniffing easy - Ō£ģ Hub disadvantages: shared bandwidth, no security segmentation, collision-heavy under load - Ō£ģ Modern replacement: **Ethernet Switch** (Layer 2, dedicated bandwidth per port) - Ō£ģ Three types: **Passive** (no regeneration), **Active** (regeneration), **Intelligent** (managed) - Ō£ģ Collision detection: monitored by **jam sequence** (32-bit pattern) - Ō£ģ **Hubs are now obsolete** in production networks ŌĆö retained only for educational labs and forensic tapping - Ō£ģ Remember the formula: $B_{eff} = \dfrac{R}{N} \times (1 - p_c) \times \left(1 - \dfrac{t_{overhead}}{t_{slot}}\right)$

topics.title: Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Switches # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **Computer Network Systems (PEEET528)**, a **Switch** is a multiport networking device that operates primarily at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI Reference Model, though advanced implementations may extend to the **Network Layer (Layer 3)**.
