A switch establishes temporary, dedicated pathways between communicating nodes and uses **packet-switching** or **circuit-switching** techniques to forward frames (Layer 2) or packets (Layer 3) from a source port to one or more destination ports based on **MAC address tables** or **routing tables**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > The module classifies switching into three fundamental paradigms: > 1. **Circuit Switching** ŌĆö Reservation-based (traditional telephone networks). > 2. **Datagram Packet Switching** ŌĆö Connectionless forwarding (e.g., the modern Internet). > 3. **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** ŌĆö Connection-oriented packet forwarding (e.g., Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS). ### The Three Generations of Packet Switches | Generation | Architecture | Key Trait | |------------|--------------|-----------| | 1st Gen | Single shared bus with centralized CPU | Software-based forwarding, limited throughput | | 2nd Gen | Self-routing fabric (e.g., Banyan, Batcher-Banyan) | Hardware-accelerated, parallel switching | | 3rd Gen | Knockout/Input-Buffered/Crossbar | Low latency, high scalability, distributed control | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Postal Sorting Office" Analogy:** > Imagine a massive post office receiving thousands of letters every second. Each letter has a destination address printed on it. The post office (the **switch**) has many incoming trucks (**input ports**) and many outgoing trucks (**output ports**). A postal clerk (**forwarding engine**) reads the address (**header/label**) of every letter and places it on the correct outgoing truck. > > - If the outgoing truck is full, the letter is temporarily stored in a warehouse (**buffer**). > - If the destination is reserved in advance (like a guaranteed seat on a flight), it is **Circuit Switching**. > - If letters are simply dropped onto the right truck without reservation, it is **Datagram Switching**. > - If a "ticket number" (virtual circuit ID) is stamped on each letter guaranteeing delivery order and path, it is **Virtual Circuit Switching**. This analogy makes the abstract concepts of **forwarding**, **buffering**, **contention**, and **connection setup** immediately intuitive. ## 1.3 Core Terminology - **Port:** A physical interface on the switch (typically **1 Gbps**, **10 Gbps**, **40 Gbps**, or **100 Gbps** line rate). - **Line Rate (R):** The transmission speed of a single port, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. - **Switch Fabric (or Switching Fabric):** The internal "highway" connecting input ports to output ports. - **Aggregate Bandwidth:** Total data the switch can handle per second = **N ├Ś R**, where N is the number of ports. - **Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking:** A phenomenon where a packet at the head of an input queue is blocked, preventing subsequent packets from being forwarded even if their outputs are free. - **Cut-Through Switching:** A forwarding mode where the switch begins transmitting a frame before it has been fully received, after only reading the destination address. - **Store-and-Forward Switching:** The switch receives the **entire frame**, performs error checking (CRC), and then forwards it. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Values Used in KTU Numerical Problems:** > - Line rate **R** is typically in **Gbps** or **Mbps**. > - One-way propagation delay in fiber: **5 ┬Ąs/km**. > - Speed of light in fiber: **2 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s**. > - RTT (Round Trip Time) for cross-continental links Ōēł **50 ms**. > - Standard Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**. > - Standard cell size in ATM = **53 bytes** (48 payload + 5 header). > - Forwarding table lookup time in modern ASICs Ōēł **5 to 10 ns**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Switching Paradigms ŌĆö Detailed Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Circuit Switching A dedicated **physical path** (or set of channels) is reserved end-to-end **before** data transmission begins. The path remains allocated for the **entire duration** of the session, even during silence periods. **Operational Steps:** 1. **Connection Establishment:** Source sends a setup request through the network; switches along the path reserve bandwidth. 2. **Data Transfer:** Data flows through the reserved path at guaranteed bandwidth. 3. **Connection Teardown:** After transmission, the path is released. **Advantages:** - Guaranteed bandwidth (constant bit rate). - No contention or variable delay ŌĆö ideal for voice traffic. **Disadvantages:** - **Inefficient utilization** during idle/silent periods. - **Connection setup latency** before data flow begins. - **Inflexible** ŌĆö bandwidth cannot adapt dynamically to bursty traffic. --- ### 2.1.2 Datagram Packet Switching Each packet (called a **datagram**) is treated **independently**. No prior connection setup is needed. Every packet carries the **full destination address**, and the switch makes a forwarding decision on a **per-packet basis** using its routing table. **Operational Steps:** 1. Each incoming packet is read entirely (store-and-forward). 2. The destination address is looked up in the forwarding table. 3. The packet is forwarded to the appropriate output port. 4. Packets of the same flow may take **different routes** and arrive **out of order**. **Advantages:** - No connection setup delay. - Robust to link failures (packets can be rerouted dynamically). - Naturally supports **bursty traffic**. **Disadvantages:** - Higher per-packet header overhead (full destination address in every packet). - Packets may arrive **out of order** at the receiver. - No Quality-of-Service (QoS) guarantee. --- ### 2.1.3 Virtual Circuit Packet Switching A **hybrid** approach combining features of both circuit and datagram switching. A **logical path** (virtual circuit) is established through the network, but the physical resources (links, buffers) are **shared** with other users via statistical multiplexing. **Two Sub-Types:** - **Permanent Virtual Circuit (PVC):** Pre-configured by the network operator (like a leased line). - **Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC):** Established dynamically per session, similar to a phone call. **Operational Steps:** 1. **VC Setup:** A virtual circuit identifier (VCI) is assigned along the path. 2. **Data Transfer:** Each packet carries only the **short VCI**, not the full destination address. Switches forward based on VCI. 3. **VC Teardown:** The virtual circuit is released. **Advantages:** - Short header (VCI instead of full address) ŌåÆ **lower overhead**. - Packets arrive **in order**. - Supports **QoS** through resource reservation. **Disadvantages:** - Setup delay (though shorter than circuit switching). - Connection state must be maintained in every switch ŌĆö **memory overhead**. --- ## 2.2 Switch Fabric Architectures ### 2.2.1 Shared Memory Switch All input and output ports share a **common memory buffer**. Packets are written into memory by input ports and read out by output ports under the control of a **central arbiter**. **Mathematical Behavior:** - Memory bandwidth must support **2N** simultaneous read/write operations per slot (one write per input, one read per output). - Maximum aggregate throughput $\leq \frac{M}{2T}$, where $M$ is memory size and $T$ is the access time. - Bottleneck: **Memory access bandwidth**. ### 2.2.2 Shared Bus Switch (1st Generation) A single high-speed **bus** connects all line cards (input/output modules). Packets traverse the bus from input to output. **Mathematical Behavior:** - Bus bandwidth must equal **aggregate throughput**. - Maximum throughput $\leq$ **Bus Speed**. - Bottleneck: **Bus contention**. ### 2.2.3 Crossbar Switch (3rd Generation) A matrix of **N ├Ś N crosspoints** connects every input port to every output port. Each crosspoint acts as a switch that can be independently opened or closed. **Mathematical Behavior:** - Supports **N simultaneous transfers** (in the best case). - Maximum throughput = **N ├Ś R**. - No internal blocking in a true crossbar (non-blocking property). ### 2.2.4 Knockout Switch Uses an **N-to-k concentrator** at each output port. When multiple packets contend for the same output, only **k** "winners" proceed; the rest are dropped or retransmitted. **Knockout Principle:** - For **N inputs** and an output port with a concentrator of size **k**: - The probability of more than **k** packets arriving simultaneously decreases exponentially as **k** increases. - Practical choice: **k = 8** gives a packet loss probability below **10Ōü╗ŌüČ** for any N. --- ## 2.3 Buffering Strategies | Strategy | Buffer Location | Key Property | |----------|-----------------|--------------| | **Input Buffering** | At each input port | Suffers from HOL blocking; max throughput Ōēł **58.6%** of capacity | | **Output Buffering** | At each output port | Eliminates HOL blocking; requires memory bandwidth of **2N** | | **Combined (CIOQ)** | At both input and output | Best performance; uses speedup factor **S** | | **Shared Memory** | Common pool | Memory dynamically shared; efficient utilization | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 BOARD EXAMS** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Aggregate Throughput | $T_{agg} = N \times R$ | N ports at line rate R | | 2 | Nagle's Rule (Buffer Size) | $B = R \times RTT$ | Bandwidth-delay product | | 3 | HOL Blocking Throughput | $T_{HOL} = \dfrac{2}{2 - \sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{R}{N}$ | Upper bound Ōēł 58.6% ├Ś $T_{agg}$ | | 4 | Knockout Loss Probability | $P_{loss} = \dfrac{\rho^k}{(1+\rho)^N \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \binom{N}{i} \rho^i}$ | Bernoulli arrival model | | 5 | Speedup Factor | $S = \dfrac{\text{Internal Clock Rate}}{\text{Line Rate}}$ | For CIOQ switches | | 6 | Erlang B Loss Formula | $P_{block} = \dfrac{\rho^N / N!}{\sum_{i=0}^{N} \rho^i / i!}$ | Circuit switching blocking | | 7 | Maximum Crossbar Crosspoints | $C = N^2$ | For an N ├Ś N switch | | 8 | Cell/Packet Service Time | $t_{svc} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | L = packet length in bits | | 9 | Header Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{header}}$ | Efficiency of encapsulation | | 10 | Link Setup Delay (Circuit) | $D_{setup} = \sum_{i=1}^{H} t_{proc,i}$ | Sum over H hops | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Nagle's Rule** is used in production router design to size DRAM buffers (e.g., Cisco, Juniper). > - **Erlang B** is used by telecom engineers (BSNL, Airtel) to dimension trunk lines in PSTN. > - **HOL blocking analysis** is critical in designing data center switches (Arista, Cisco Nexus). > - **Crossbar switches** form the backbone of **MPLS label-switched routers** and **ATM switches**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Throughput under Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking This is a **classic KTU derivation** frequently tested in board exams. We will derive the asymptotic maximum throughput of an N├ŚN switch using **input buffering** under uniform i.i.d. Bernoulli traffic. ### Assumptions 1. Packets (cells) arrive at each input as **independent Bernoulli** processes with probability $p$ per time slot. 2. All packets have **fixed length** = 1 cell. 3. Each packet is destined for a **uniformly random** output port. 4. The switch operates **synchronously** ŌĆö one decision per slot. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Let $X_n$ denote the number of packets in the input queue at the beginning of slot $n$. Each non-empty input queue has its HOL packet contend for one of $N$ output ports. **Step 2:** By symmetry, the probability that a packet arriving at input port $i$ is destined for a specific output port $j$ is: $$ P[\text{dest} = j] = \frac{1}{N} $$ **Step 3:** The probability that **at least one** of the $(N-1)$ other HOL packets is also destined for the same output $j$ as our HOL packet is computed. For large $N$, this probability approaches a **non-zero limit** because the system is in statistical equilibrium. **Step 4:** Let $\rho$ be the offered load per input port. Define $q$ as the probability that a non-empty input queue's HOL packet is **blocked** in a given slot. In steady state: $$ q = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{(N-1)\rho} $$ **Step 5:** For large $N$, use the approximation $(1 - x)^{y} \approx e^{-xy}$: $$ q \approx 1 - e^{-(N-1)\rho \cdot (1/N)} = 1 - e^{-\rho \cdot (1 - 1/N)} $$ For $N \to \infty$: $$ q \approx 1 - e^{-\rho} $$ **Step 6:** The throughput per input is: $$ T_{per\,input} = \rho \cdot (1 - q) = \rho \cdot e^{-\rho} $$ **Step 7:** Maximize $T_{per\,input}$ with respect to $\rho$: $$ \frac{d}{d\rho} \left[ \rho e^{-\rho} \right] = e^{-\rho} - \rho e^{-\rho} = e^{-\rho}(1 - \rho) = 0 $$ Solving: $$ \rho^* = 1 $$ **Step 8:** Substitute $\rho^* = 1$ back: $$ T_{max,\,per\,input} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679 $$ **Step 9:** Total normalized throughput (across all N inputs): $$ T_{max,\,total} = N \cdot \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679 \cdot N $$ **Step 10:** As a fraction of peak capacity $N$: $$ \boxed{T_{HOL,\,max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 36.79\%} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Corrected KTU Result:** Some textbooks use a refined Markov-chain analysis yielding **58.6%** (the **$2 - \sqrt{2}$ bound**). The rigorous Karol-Hluchyj-Hogstrom (1987) result for an N├ŚN input-buffered switch under uniform i.i.d. Bernoulli arrivals is: $$ \boxed{T_{HOL,\,max} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.5858 = 58.58\%} $$ This is the value KTU examiners expect. The $1/e$ result is a **simpler asymptotic approximation** used for pedagogical clarity. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nagle's Rule for Buffer Sizing Nagle's rule (RFC 970) determines the minimum buffer size $B$ required at a TCP sender to fully utilize a link without under-utilizing it. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** The sender transmits a packet of size $L$ bits. The first bit arrives at the receiver after one-way delay $\tau$. **Step 2:** The receiver acknowledges (ACK) after a processing time $\delta$. The ACK arrives back at the sender after another $\tau$. The total **Round Trip Time** is: $$ RTT = 2\tau + \delta $$ For long-haul links, $\delta \ll 2\tau$, so: $$ RTT \approx 2\tau $$ **Step 3:** During the RTT, the sender is idle, waiting for the ACK. To keep the link fully utilized, the sender should have enough data in its buffer to continuously transmit for the entire RTT. **Step 4:** At line rate $R$ bits/sec, the amount of data that "fits in the pipe" is: $$ B = R \times RTT $$ This is the **bandwidth-delay product**. **Step 5:** Substituting $RTT = 2\tau$: $$ \boxed{B = 2 \tau R} $$ ### Worked Example A transcontinental link has $R = 1$ Gbps and one-way propagation delay $\tau = 25$ ms. $$ B = 2 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^9 = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ bits} \approx 5.96 \text{ MB} $$ This means the sender's buffer must hold **at least 6 MB** of unacknowledged data to fully utilize the link ŌĆö a key insight for **router buffer sizing** in long-fat networks (LFNs). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Erlang B Loss Probability for Circuit Switching The **Erlang B formula** gives the probability that a new call is **blocked** (rejected) in a circuit-switched network with $N$ trunks and offered traffic $A$ Erlangs. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Model the system as an **M/M/N/N** queue (birth-death process with $N$ servers and **no waiting room**). **Step 2:** Let $P_n$ be the steady-state probability of having $n$ calls in progress. By the **birth-death balance equations**: $$ (n+1) P_{n+1} \cdot (n+1) \mu = \lambda P_n $$ where $\lambda$ is the call arrival rate and $\mu$ is the call departure rate. **Step 3:** Define traffic intensity $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ (in Erlangs). Iterating the balance equations: $$ P_n = P_0 \cdot \frac{\rho^n}{n!} $$ **Step 4:** Normalization: $\sum_{n=0}^{N} P_n = 1$, so: $$ P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N} \frac{\rho^n}{n!} \right]^{-1} $$ **Step 5:** A new call is blocked when all $N$ trunks are busy, i.e., state $n = N$: $$ \boxed{P_{block} = \frac{\rho^N / N!}{\sum_{n=0}^{N} \rho^n / n!}} $$ ### Worked Example A telephone exchange has $N = 20$ trunks. The offered traffic is $A = 15$ Erlangs. Find the blocking probability. $$ P_{block} = \frac{15^{20} / 20!}{\sum_{n=0}^{20} 15^n / n!} $$ Numerical evaluation yields: $$ P_{block} \approx 0.0351 \approx 3.51\% $$ This is the **Grade of Service (GoS)** ŌĆö a standard metric used by telecom planners. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Switching Paradigms Comparison | Parameter | Circuit Switching | Datagram Switching | Virtual Circuit Switching | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------------------| | Connection Setup | **Required** | Not required | Required | | Path | **Fixed** (dedicated) | **Independent per packet** | **Fixed** (logical) | | Header Size | Small (channel ID) | **Large** (full destination address) | Small (VCI) | | Packet Ordering | **In order** | May be **out of order** | **In order** | | Resource Reservation | **Dedicated** | **Statistical** (best-effort) | **Statistical with reservation** | | Failure Recovery | Difficult (reroute path) | **Easy** (dynamic routing) | Difficult (reroute path) | | Delay Variability | **Constant** | **Variable** | Variable but bounded | | Typical Application | PSTN, ISDN | Internet (IP) | ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Switch Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN["INPUT SECTION"] P1[Input Port 1] P2[Input Port 2] PN[Input Port N] end subgraph BUF["BUFFER LAYER"] IB[Input Buffers] OB[Output Buffers] SM[Shared Memory Pool] end subgraph FAB["SWITCH FABRIC"] BUS[Shared Bus Gen 1] XB[Crossbar Matrix Gen 3] KO[Knockout Switch] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROL UNIT"] ARB[Central Arbiter] FT[Forwarding Table] SCH[Scheduler QoS] end subgraph OUT["OUTPUT SECTION"] Q1[Output Port 1] Q2[Output Port 2] QN[Output Port N] end P1 --> IB P2 --> IB PN --> IB IB --> FAB FAB --> OB OB --> Q1 OB --> Q2 OB --> QN ARB --> FAB FT --> ARB SCH --> ARB SM -.-> FAB ``` ## 4.2 Evolution of Packet Switch Generations ```mermaid flowchart LR G1["GEN 1: SHARED BUS
Central CPU Control
Software Forwarding
Limited Throughput"] G2["GEN 2: SELF-ROUTING
Banyan / Batcher Banyan
Hardware Path Setup
Parallel Switching"] G3["GEN 3: CROSSBAR
N x N Crosspoints
Non-Blocking
Distributed Control"] G1 -->|Evolution| G2 G2 -->|Evolution| G3 style G1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style G2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style G3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 N x N Crossbar Switch Fabric Internals ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN["INPUT PORTS"] I1["I1"] I2["I2"] I3["I3"] end subgraph XB["CROSSPOINT MATRIX"] X11["X11"] X12["X12"] X13["X13"] X21["X21"] X22["X22"] X23["X23"] X31["X31"] X32["X32"] X33["X33"] end subgraph OUT["OUTPUT PORTS"] O1["O1"] O2["O2"] O3["O3"] end I1 --- X11 I1 --- X12 I1 --- X13 I2 --- X21 I2 --- X22 I2 --- X23 I3 --- X31 I3 --- X32 I3 --- X33 X11 --- O1 X21 --- O1 X31 --- O1 X12 --- O2 X22 --- O2 X32 --- O2 X13 --- O3 X23 --- O3 X33 --- O3 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree: Cut-Through vs Store-and-Forward ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Frame Arrives at Input Port"] --> B{"Read Destination
Address Only?"} B -->|Yes| C["CUT-THROUGH SWITCHING
Begins transmission after
reading header
Latency: ~6 bytes"] B -->|No| D{"Read Entire
Frame?"} D -->|Yes| E{"Perform CRC
Check?"} E -->|Yes| F{"CRC Valid?"} F -->|Yes| G["STORE AND FORWARD
Forward the frame
Latency: full frame + CRC"] F -->|No| H["DROP the frame
CRC error detected"] E -->|No| I["REDUCED LATENCY
STORE AND FORWARD
Forward without check"] style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000 style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Switching Paradigm Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S{"Application
Type?"} S -->|Voice / Real-Time| CS["CIRCUIT SWITCHING
Guaranteed bandwidth
PSTN, ISDN"] S -->|Bursty Data| DS["DATAGRAM SWITCHING
Connectionless
Internet IP"] S -->|QoS Data| VC["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT
ATM, MPLS, Frame Relay
Resource reserved"] CS --> P1["Pro: Constant Delay"] DS --> P2["Pro: Robust, Flexible"] VC --> P3["Pro: Ordered, Efficient"] P1 --> CON1["Con: Inefficient
for bursty"] P2 --> CON2["Con: Variable delay
Header overhead"] P3 --> CON3["Con: Setup delay
State maintenance"] style CS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 style DS fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style VC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 ``` ## 4.6 Head-of-Line Blocking Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Q["INPUT QUEUE"] H["HOL Packet
Destined for BUSY Output"] P2["Packet 2
Destined for FREE Output"] P3["Packet 3
Destined for FREE Output"] end H -->|BLOCKED| X["Cannot Forward"] P2 -->|WAITING| X P3 -->|WAITING| X X -.->|Wastes| CAP["58.6% Capacity Lost"] style H fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style P2 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000 style P3 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000 style CAP fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **Differentiate between circuit switching and packet switching. Mention two advantages and one disadvantage of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching | |---------|------------------|------------------| | Connection Setup | **Required** | Not required (datagram) / Required (VC) | | Resource Allocation | **Dedicated** for entire session | **Shared** (statistical multiplexing) | | Delay | **Constant** after setup | **Variable** (queueing) | | Efficiency | Low for bursty traffic | High for bursty traffic | | Typical Use | **PSTN, ISDN** | **Internet, ATM** | **Two advantages of circuit switching:** 1. Guaranteed bandwidth and constant delay ŌĆö ideal for real-time voice. 2. No queuing delay once circuit is established. **One disadvantage of circuit switching:** 1. Wastes bandwidth during idle periods (e.g., silent gaps in speech). **Two advantages of packet switching:** 1. Efficient utilization through statistical multiplexing. 2. Robust and flexible ŌĆö supports dynamic rerouting. **One disadvantage of packet switching:** 1. Variable delay (jitter) makes it unsuitable for some real-time applications without QoS. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024] **Define the term "Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking" in the context of input-buffered switches. State its impact on maximum achievable throughput.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Head-of-Line (HOL) Blocking is a phenomenon in **input-buffered switches** where a packet at the **head of an input queue** is waiting for its destination output port to become free. Because FIFO discipline is maintained, **all packets behind it** ŌĆö even those destined for currently free outputs ŌĆö are also blocked from being forwarded. **Impact on Throughput:** Under uniform i.i.d. Bernoulli traffic, the maximum achievable throughput of an N├ŚN input-buffered switch is limited to: $$ T_{HOL,\,max} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.5858 = 58.58\% $$ This means approximately **41.4%** of the switch's nominal capacity is wasted due to HOL blocking. The result was proven by Karol, Hluchyj, and Hogstrom in 1987. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three generations of packet switches in detail. Compare their architectures with respect to throughput, latency, and scalability. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the maximum throughput of an N├ŚN input-buffered switch under uniform i.i.d. Bernoulli traffic using the HOL blocking analysis. State the limiting value of throughput. --- ### Question A ŌĆö Model Solution #### Part (a) ŌĆö Three Generations of Packet Switches [7 Marks] **First Generation: Shared Bus with Centralized CPU** - **Architecture:** A single high-speed bus connects all line interface cards. A central CPU reads packets from input cards via the bus, consults the routing table, and writes packets to output cards. - **Throughput:** Limited by bus bandwidth ŌĆö typically only a few hundred thousand packets per second. - **Latency:** High, because every packet traverses the bus **twice** (once in, once out) and must wait for CPU processing. - **Scalability:** Poor ŌĆö increasing the number of ports or line rate does not scale gracefully. **Second Generation: Self-Routing Fabrics** - **Architecture:** Uses multi-stage interconnection networks like **Banyan** or **Batcher-Banyan** networks. The packet header contains a "routing tag" that drives hardware decisions at each stage. - **Throughput:** Significantly higher than first generation; supports parallel paths. - **Latency:** Lower ŌĆö packets self-route in hardware without CPU intervention. - **Scalability:** Moderate ŌĆö Banyan networks are internally blocking but can be combined with **Batcher sorting networks** to achieve non-blocking behavior. **Third Generation: Space-Division Switches (Crossbar / Knockout)** - **Architecture:** N├ŚN matrix of crosspoints, each independently controlled. Every input can connect to any output simultaneously. - **Throughput:** Optimal ŌĆö theoretically can achieve **100%** of nominal capacity in non-blocking configurations. - **Latency:** Very low ŌĆö packets traverse the fabric in one switching cycle. - **Scalability:** Excellent ŌĆö distributed arbitration enables scaling to hundreds of ports. **Comparison Summary:** | Property | Gen 1 (Bus) | Gen 2 (Banyan) | Gen 3 (Crossbar) | |----------|-------------|----------------|--------------------| | Throughput | Low | Moderate | High | | Latency | High | Moderate | Low | | Scalability | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | | Control | Centralized | Distributed | Distributed | | Complexity | Low | Moderate | High | #### Part (b) ŌĆö HOL Blocking Throughput Derivation [7 Marks] (See Section 3.1 for full derivation.) The key steps: 1. Model packet arrivals as i.i.d. Bernoulli with parameter $p$. 2. Probability an HOL packet destined for output $j$ is blocked: $$ q = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{(N-1)p} $$ 3. Throughput per input: $$ T = p \cdot (1 - q) = p \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{(N-1)p} $$ 4. Taking the limit as $N \to \infty$: $$ T \to p \cdot e^{-p} $$ 5. Maximize: $\frac{dT}{dp} = e^{-p}(1-p) = 0 \Rightarrow p^* = 1$ 6. Maximum throughput: $$ T_{max} = e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679 $$ The **rigorous Karol-Hluchyj bound** (expected in KTU) is: $$ \boxed{T_{max} = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.5858 = 58.58\%} $$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Shared Memory Switch** and a **Knockout Switch**. Discuss how contention is handled in each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A router has 8 input ports and 8 output ports, each operating at $R = 1$ Gbps. Compute: (i) the aggregate throughput in the absence of HOL blocking, (ii) the maximum throughput under HOL blocking, (iii) the minimum buffer size required per output port if the RTT is 200 ms. --- ### Question B ŌĆö Model Solution #### Part (a) ŌĆö Shared Memory and Knockout Switches [7 Marks] **Shared Memory Switch:** The shared memory switch uses a **single common memory buffer** accessed by all input and output ports. Operation: 1. Each input port writes its incoming packet to the shared memory via a high-speed bus. 2. A **central arbiter** examines the packet headers and decides which output port each packet should go to. 3. Each output port reads its assigned packets from memory. **Contention Handling:** - If two input ports want to write simultaneously, the memory bus **serializes** them. - If multiple packets are destined for the same output, the arbiter **schedules** them using a queue in memory. - **Limitation:** Memory bandwidth must be at least **2N ├Ś R** (one write per input, one read per output per slot). **Knockout Switch:** The knockout switch solves the N-to-1 contention problem using an **N-to-k concentrator** at each output port: 1. Each input port is connected to **all N output concentrators** via a bus or fabric. 2. When multiple packets arrive at the same output in one slot, only the **first k** packets "win"; the rest are dropped or buffered for retransmission. 3. The knockout principle states that for **k = 8**, the probability of more than 8 simultaneous arrivals (loss probability) is less than **10Ōü╗ŌüČ** even for large N. **Contention Handling:** - The concentrator uses a **token-ring** arbiter: the first k packets to arrive acquire tokens and proceed. - Losers are buffered and retried in subsequent slots. - This design is **stateless** and easily scalable. **Comparison:** | Property | Shared Memory | Knockout | |----------|---------------|----------| | Memory Bandwidth Required | $2N \cdot R$ | Lower | | Packet Loss | None (until full) | Possible (above k winners) | | Scalability | Limited by memory | Excellent | | Complexity | High (central arbiter) | Moderate | #### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Problem [7 Marks] **Given:** - $N = 8$ input ports and $N = 8$ output ports. - Line rate $R = 1$ Gbps $= 10^9$ bits/sec. - $RTT = 200$ ms $= 0.2$ sec. **(i) Aggregate Throughput (No HOL Blocking):** $$ T_{agg} = N \times R = 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ Gbps} $$ **(ii) Maximum Throughput under HOL Blocking:** $$ T_{HOL} = (2 - \sqrt{2}) \times T_{agg} $$ $$ T_{HOL} = 0.5858 \times 8 \text{ Gbps} = 4.686 \text{ Gbps} $$ **(iii) Minimum Buffer Size (Nagle's Rule):** $$ B = R \times RTT = 10^9 \times 0.2 = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ bits} $$ Convert to bytes: $$ B = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{8} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes} = 25 \text{ MB} $$ **Final Answers:** 1. Aggregate throughput = **8 Gbps** 2. Max throughput with HOL = **Ōēł 4.69 Gbps** 3. Minimum buffer size = **25 MB** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing the $1/e$ result with the $2 - \sqrt{2}$ bound:** The simple Poisson limit gives $1/e \approx 36.8\%$, but the **rigorous KTU-expected answer** for finite N with FIFO input buffers is **$2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 58.6\%$**. Examiners deduct marks if you cite $1/e$ without justification. > > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by N:** When asked for total switch throughput, students often compute only **per-port** throughput. The **total** throughput is $N \times T_{per\,port}$. > > 3. **Units in Nagle's Rule:** Always express $R$ in **bits/sec** and $RTT$ in **seconds** to get buffer in **bits**, then convert to bytes (divide by 8). > > 4. **Erlang B vs Erlang C:** Erlang B is for **lost-call-cleared** (no queue). Erlang C is for **delayed-call**. KTU questions on circuit switching always expect **Erlang B**. > > 5. **Datagram vs Virtual Circuit confusion:** Remember ŌĆö **datagram is connectionless**, **virtual circuit is connection-oriented** but uses **packet switching**, not circuit switching. > > 6. **Cut-through switching does NOT check CRC:** Examiners penalize students who claim cut-through performs error checking. It only reads the destination address (~6 bytes for Ethernet). > > 7. **Knockout concentrator size:** Memorize **k = 8** as the standard value achieving $<10^{-6}$ loss probability. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam** - **Three Switching Paradigms:** Circuit (reserved), Datagram (independent packets), Virtual Circuit (logical path with short labels). - **Three Generations of Packet Switches:** Shared Bus (Gen 1), Self-Routing Banyan (Gen 2), Crossbar/Knockout (Gen 3). - **HOL Blocking:** Limits input-buffered switch throughput to **58.6%** of capacity ($2 - \sqrt{2}$). - **Nagle's Rule:** Buffer size $B = R \times RTT$ ŌĆö essential for long-fat network (LFN) design. - **Erlang B Formula:** $P_{block} = \frac{\rho^N / N!}{\sum_{n=0}^{N} \rho^n / n!}$ ŌĆö used for circuit-switched PSTN dimensioning. - **Crossbar Switch:** $N^2$ crosspoints, non-blocking, supports N simultaneous transfers. - **Knockout Principle:** N-to-k concentrator with **k = 8** achieves loss probability < **10Ōü╗ŌüČ**. - **Cut-Through Switching:** Latency Ōēł **6 bytes** (header only), no CRC check. - **Store-and-Forward Switching:** Latency = full frame + CRC processing. - **ATM Cell:** **53 bytes** = 5-byte header + 48-byte payload. - **Ethernet MTU:** **1500 bytes** standard. - **Aggregate Throughput:** $T_{agg} = N \times R$ where R is per-port line rate. - **Datagram is connectionless; Virtual Circuit is connection-oriented.** - **PSTN, ISDN use Circuit Switching; IP uses Datagram; ATM, MPLS use Virtual Circuit.**

topics.title: Routers and Gateways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Routers and Gateways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Router?

A **Router** is a Layer-3 (Network Layer) internetworking device that operates on **packets** and forwards them between two or more logically distinct **IP networks / subnets** based on a **routing table**. It is the primary device that performs **path determination** and **packet switching** across heterogeneous networks in the Internet.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, a router is defined as a **gateway device** that examines the destination IP address of every incoming packet, consults its routing table, identifies the best outgoing interface, and forwards the packet toward the next hop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim-style):** A router is an internetworking device that connects two or more packet-switched networks and forwards packets between them based on Layer-3 addressing (IP) and routing table lookups.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Post Office

Think of a **router as a digital post office**:
- A letter (packet) arrives with a destination address (destination IP).
- The postmaster (routing engine) looks at the address and decides which truck (outgoing interface) should carry it.
- If the postmaster does not know the exact city, the letter is sent to a regional hub (default route) or returned (ICMP destination unreachable).

Just as post offices route millions of letters daily, routers handle billions of packets per second using fast lookup tables and ASIC hardware.

### 1.3 What is a Gateway?

A **Gateway** is an internetworking device that operates at the **highest layer of the OSI model relevant to the networks being connected**. While routers typically work at Layer 3, a gateway can translate between **dissimilar network architectures, protocols, or data formats** (e.g., TCP/IP Ōåö SNA, IPv4 Ōåö IPv6, LAN Ōåö Mainframe).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In KTU Module-2, the term *Gateway* is used in two contexts:
> 1. **Default Gateway** ŌĆö the router on a local subnet to which a host sends all non-local traffic.
> 2. **Protocol/Application Gateway** ŌĆö a device that translates between two entirely different network protocol stacks.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Language Translator at an Airport

A **gateway is like a multilingual translator** at an international airport. When a Japanese passenger (TCP/IP network) wants to talk to a French passenger (SNA network), the translator converts the message so both sides understand. A **router**, in contrast, only directs people to the right terminal ŌĆö it does not translate languages.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Default Gateway IP address** ŌĆö usually the first or last usable IP in a subnet, e.g., `192.168.1.1` for `/24` networks.
- **Hop Count** ŌĆö number of routers a packet traverses. Maximum in IPv4 = **255** (TTL field).
- **Administrative Distance (AD)** ŌĆö trustworthiness of a routing source (e.g., OSPF = **110**, RIP = **120**, Static = **1**, Connected = **0**).
- **Cost / Metric** ŌĆö value used to choose best path; lower is preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** IPv4 TTL default value = **64** (Linux) or **255** (Cisco/Windows). Each router hop decrements TTL by 1. When TTL = 0, the packet is dropped and an **ICMP Time Exceeded** message is returned.

### 1.6 Router vs Gateway ŌĆö A Bird's-Eye Comparison

| Aspect | Router | Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Primarily Layer 3 | Any layer (typically Application/Layer-7) |
| Decision based on | IP address | Protocol, application, address, format |
| Translation | Same protocol family | Different protocol families |
| Speed | Very fast (hardware-based) | Slower (software translation) |
| Example | Home Wi-Fi router, Cisco ISR | Email gateway, VoIP gateway, IP/SNA gateway |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** In modern usage, the term "gateway" on a home network refers to the **default gateway** (which is technically a router). In the KTU theory context, always distinguish between the **Default Gateway (a router)** and the **Protocol Gateway (a translator)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Router

A modern router consists of four functional blocks, irrespective of vendor (Cisco, Juniper, Huawei):

1. **Input Ports** ŌĆö receive packets, perform physical-layer termination, data-link decapsulation, and lookup of destination IP in the **routing table** using high-speed TCAMs / ASICs.
2. **Switching Fabric** ŌĆö the internal backbone that moves the packet from input port to the correct output port. Three main types:
   - **Memory-based** (busy when packets contend; used in old PCs-as-routers).
   - **Bus-based** (single shared bus; limited throughput).
   - **Crossbar** (parallel paths; non-blocking; used in core/edge routers).
3. **Output Ports** ŌĆö queue packets (using QoS scheduling algorithms like WFQ, PQ, CBWFQ), encapsulate them, and transmit on the physical link.
4. **Routing Processor / Control Plane** ŌĆö runs the **routing protocols** (OSPF, BGP, RIP, IS-IS), builds the routing table, and manages the device.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A **routing table** has at minimum the following columns: *Destination Network, Subnet Mask, Next Hop, Outgoing Interface, Metric, Administrative Distance*.

## 2.2 Types of Routers (KTU Classification)

- **Interior Gateway Routers** ŌĆö operate within a single Autonomous System (AS). Run protocols like **RIP, OSPF, EIGRP**.
- **Exterior Gateway Routers** ŌĆö operate between Autonomous Systems. Run **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)**.
- **Core Routers** ŌĆö form the Internet backbone (high-speed, terabit capacity).
- **Edge / Access Routers** ŌĆö sit at the customer/ISP boundary.
- **Wireless Routers** ŌĆö combine Wi-Fi AP + router + 4-port switch (typical home router).
- **Brouter (Bridging Router)** ŌĆö hybrid device that bridges non-routable protocols and routes routable ones.

## 2.3 Routing Algorithm Categories

### (A) Distance Vector Routing (Bellman-Ford)
- Each router maintains a **distance table** to every destination.
- Routers share their **entire routing table** with **directly connected neighbours** periodically (e.g., every 30 s in RIP).
- Suffers from **count-to-infinity** problem.
- Examples: **RIP v1, RIP v2, IGRP**.

### (B) Link State Routing (Dijkstra)
- Every router builds a complete **Link State Database (LSDB)** of the entire topology.
- Uses **Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm (SPF)** to compute best path.
- Fast convergence, no count-to-infinity, but heavier CPU/memory.
- Examples: **OSPF, IS-IS**.

### (C) Path Vector Routing
- Used in inter-AS routing. Carries the **full AS-path**.
- Example: **BGP-4**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Hop count limit (IPv4) | TTL initialized, decremented per router | $\text{TTL}_{\max} = 255$ |
| Subnetting | $2^{n}$ subnets, $2^{h} - 2$ hosts ($h$ = host bits) | $n, h \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| Bellman-Ford (Distance Vector) | $D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \bigr\}$ | $D$ = distance, $c$ = link cost |
| Dijkstra (Link State, SPF cost) | $d(v) = \min \bigl\{ d(v),\ d(u) + w(u,v) \bigr\}$ | $w$ = link weight |
| OSPF Cost | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$ | Default ref = $10^{8}$ bps (100 Mbps) |
| RIP Metric | Hop count (max = 15, 16 = infinity) | $0 \leq \text{hops} \leq 15$ |
| Administrative Distance (preferred = lower) | Connected $= 0$, Static $= 1$, OSPF $= 110$, RIP $= 120$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| Longest Prefix Match (LPM) | Choose the route with **longest subnet mask** | CIDR ŌĆö bits count matters |
| End-to-end delay (queuing + propagation) | $d_{\text{end}} = d_{\text{proc}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{prop}}$ | All in **seconds** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note for Tables:** The vertical bar $\vert$ is used in the Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra rows above. In KTU board-exam answer sheets, use the standard absolute-value notation $\vert x \vert$ with care; this notes sheet uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ deliberately to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISP Backbone Engineering:** Routers run BGP-4 across AS boundaries. The Internet is essentially a federation of ASes interconnected by BGP-speaking routers.
- **Enterprise Campus Networks:** Multi-layer hierarchical design ŌĆö core, distribution, access routers/switches.
- **SD-WAN & NFV:** Modern software-defined routers run as virtual functions (vRouters) on commodity x86 servers.
- **Industrial IoT:** Ruggedized routers connect Modbus/Profinet field devices to cloud SCADA systems.
- **Automotive Networks:** In-vehicle routers manage CAN, LIN, and Ethernet domains inside modern cars.

## 2.6 Gateway Deep Dive

A **Protocol Gateway** performs **seven-layer translation**. The classic KTU example is connecting an **IBM SNA mainframe** to a **TCP/IP Ethernet LAN**:

1. Receive SNA frame from the mainframe port.
2. Strip SNA headers.
3. Translate the payload into TCP/IP.
4. Encapsulate into Ethernet frame.
5. Send out the LAN interface.

Other common gateways:
- **Email Gateway** ŌĆö SMTP Ōåö X.400.
- **VoIP Gateway** ŌĆö PSTN Ōåö SIP/RTP.
- **IoT Gateway** ŌĆö Zigbee/BLE Ōåö Wi-Fi/Ethernet.
- **Cloud Storage Gateway** ŌĆö S3 Ōåö NFS/SMB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Gateway Operation:** When a host wants to send a packet to a non-local IP, it performs an **AND** operation: `Destination IP AND Subnet Mask` and compares with its own network. If they differ, the packet is encapsulated in a frame addressed to the **Default Gateway's MAC address** (resolved via ARP), and sent on the local subnet. The router then takes full responsibility for onward delivery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Bellman-Ford Equation (Distance Vector)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a node $x$ that wishes to compute the minimum distance to destination $y$ in a graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge costs $c(u, v)$ for every edge $(u,v) \in E$.

Each node $x$ maintains a distance vector:

$$
D_x = \bigl[ D_x(y_1),\ D_x(y_2),\ \dots,\ D_x(y_n) \bigr]
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Single-hop cost.** If $x$ is directly connected to $y$ via one link of cost $c(x, y)$, then trivially:

$$
D_x(y) = c(x, y)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multi-hop generalisation.** Suppose the optimal path from $x$ to $y$ passes through an intermediate neighbour $v$ of $x$. Then the optimal cost decomposes as the cost from $x$ to $v$ plus the optimal cost from $v$ to $y$:

$$
D_x(y) = c(x, v) + D_v(y)
$$

This is the **principle of optimality** (Bellman's optimality principle).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Minimisation over all neighbours.** Since $x$ does not know a priori which neighbour lies on the optimal path, it minimises over the entire neighbour set $N(x)$:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl[ c(x, v) + D_v(y) \bigr]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Vector form.** Stacking this equation for all destinations $y \in V$:

$$
D_x = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl[ c(x, v) \cdot \mathbf{1} + D_v \bigr]
$$

where $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones vector and the $\min$ is applied component-wise.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Iterative update (Bellman-Ford relaxation).** At iteration $t$:

$$
D_x^{(t)}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl[ c(x, v) + D_v^{(t-1)}(y) \bigr]
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convergence.** The algorithm converges in at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations for a graph with no negative cycles, giving the optimal distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations?** The longest possible simple path in a graph with $\vert V \vert$ vertices has $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges. Each iteration propagates shortest paths one more hop along the path. Hence, after $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations, all optimal distances are known.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Consider the topology:
- AŌĆōB: cost 1, AŌĆōC: cost 4, BŌĆōC: cost 2, BŌĆōD: cost 5, CŌĆōD: cost 1.

Compute $D_A(D)$ using Bellman-Ford.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A^{(1)}(D) &= \min \bigl[ c(A,B) + D_B^{(0)}(D),\ c(A,C) + D_C^{(0)}(D) \bigr] \\
&= \min \bigl[ 1 + \infty,\ 4 + \infty \bigr] = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

After neighbours update and propagate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A^{(2)}(D) &= \min \bigl[ 1 + 5,\ 4 + 1 \bigr] = \min(6, 5) = 5 \\
\text{Optimal path:} \quad & A \to C \to D,\quad \text{total cost} = 4 + 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Routing Protocol Comparison

| Parameter | RIP (v1/v2) | OSPF | BGP-4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Distance Vector | Link State | Path Vector |
| Algorithm | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra (SPF) | Best Path + Policy |
| Metric | Hop count (max 15) | Cost (bandwidth-based) | AS-path length + attributes |
| Update | Periodic, full table (30 s) | Triggered, LSDB | Incremental, keepalive |
| Convergence | Slow | Fast | Very slow (policy-driven) |
| Hierarchy | Flat | 2-level area (backbone + areas) | AS-level (eBGP/iBGP) |
| Administrative Distance | 120 | 110 | 20 (eBGP) / 200 (iBGP) |
| Transport | UDP port 520 | IP protocol 89 | TCP port 179 |
| Scalability | Small networks | Large enterprise | Entire Internet |
| KTU Weight | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ (basics) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ (most asked) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ (mod-2 important) |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step ŌĆö Default Gateway Resolution

**Scenario:** Host H (`192.168.1.50/24`, default gateway `192.168.1.1`) sends a packet to `8.8.8.8`.

**Step 1:** Host H computes its own network via AND operation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
192.168.1.50\ \text{AND}\ 255.255.255.0 &= 192.168.1.0 \\
8.8.8.8\ \text{AND}\ 255.255.255.0 &= 8.8.8.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Since `192.168.1.0 \neq 8.8.8.0`, the destination is **non-local**.

**Step 3:** H checks its **ARP cache** for the default gateway's MAC address. If absent, it broadcasts an ARP request: *"Who has 192.168.1.1?"*

**Step 4:** Router R1 (the default gateway) replies with its MAC address, say `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`.

**Step 5:** H encapsulates the IP packet inside an **Ethernet frame** with:
- Source MAC = H's MAC
- Destination MAC = `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`
- Source IP = `192.168.1.50`
- Destination IP = `8.8.8.8` (unchanged end-to-end)

**Step 6:** R1 receives the frame, strips the Ethernet header, looks up `8.8.8.8` in its routing table, decrements TTL by 1, and forwards toward the next hop. The **MAC addresses change hop-by-hop, but the IP addresses remain unchanged** (except for NAT scenarios).

## 3.4 Why "Longest Prefix Match" is Critical

When a router has overlapping routes (e.g., `10.0.0.0/8` via ISP-A and `10.1.0.0/16` via ISP-B), it must pick the **most specific** (longest prefix) match.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Route A:} & \quad 10.0.0.0/8 \quad \text{(mask = 8 bits)} \\
\text{Route B:} & \quad 10.1.0.0/16 \quad \text{(mask = 16 bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For destination `10.1.5.7`, both routes match, but Route B has the **longer prefix** (16 > 8) and wins. This is implemented in hardware using **TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory)** that performs the lookup in a single clock cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Router Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Port 1] --> F[Switching Fabric]
    B[Input Port 2] --> F
    C[Input Port N] --> F
    F --> D[Output Port 1]
    F --> E[Output Port 2]
    F --> G[Output Port N]
    H[Routing Processor / Control Plane] -. updates .-> A
    H -. updates .-> B
    H -. updates .-> C
    H -. updates .-> D
    H -. updates .-> E
    H -. updates .-> G
    R[Routing Protocols OSPF BGP RIP] --> H
    T[Routing Table FIB RIB] --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the **data plane** (actual packet movement). Dotted arrows show the **control plane** (routing table updates).

## 4.2 End-to-End Packet Flow Through Multiple Routers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Host A 192.168.1.10] -->|Frame dest MAC = R1 MAC| R1A[Router R1 Edge Router]
    R1A -->|IP dest unchanged MAC = R2| R1B[Router R2 ISP Core]
    R1B -->|IP dest unchanged MAC = R3| R1C[Router R3 Edge of Dest AS]
    R1C -->|Frame dest MAC = Host B| H2[Host B 203.0.113.5]
    style R1A fill:#f9e79f
    style R1B fill:#aed6f1
    style R1C fill:#f9e79f
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAC addresses change at every hop; IP addresses remain unchanged (source/destination).** This is a KTU favourite 7-mark question.

## 4.3 Gateway Translation Between Dissimilar Networks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TCP IP LAN with IPv4 hosts] -->|IPv4 packet in| GW[Application Gateway Protocol Translator]
    GW -->|SNA frame out| B[IBM SNA Mainframe Network]
    GW <-->|Translation table| TR[Protocol Mapping Table]
    style GW fill:#abebc6
    style TR fill:#f5cba7
```

## 4.4 Routing Decision Flowchart (Longest Prefix Match)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Packet arrives at router input] --> L[Extract destination IP]
    L --> M{Lookup in FIB TCAM}
    M -->|Longest prefix match found| N[Get next hop and outgoing interface]
    M -->|No match| DEF[Use default route 0.0.0.0/0]
    DEF --> N
    N --> Q[Enqueue in output port buffer]
    Q --> S[Schedule via WFQ PQ etc]
    S --> T[Decrement TTL and transmit on outgoing link]
    T --> X[TTL is zero]
    X --> DROP[Drop packet and send ICMP Time Exceeded]
    T -->|TTL greater than zero| Y[Forwarded to next router]
```

## 4.5 Hierarchical View ŌĆö Router Placement in Internet

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST["HOST NETWORK"]
        H1L[Host 1] --> ACL[Access Layer Switch]
        H2L[Host 2] --> ACL
        ACL --> EDR[Edge Router]
    end
    EDR -->|BGP or static| ISPR[ISP Border Router]
    ISPR -->|iBGP within AS| ISPC[ISP Core Router]
    ISPC -->|eBGP to next AS| NAP[Internet Exchange Point]
    NAP -->|eBGP| ISPR2[Destination ISP Border Router]
    ISPR2 --> EDR2[Destination Edge Router]
    EDR2 --> DEST[Destination Host]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a router and a gateway. List any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Router | Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 (Network) | Any layer, often Layer 7 |
| Function | Forwards packets based on IP address | Translates between dissimilar protocols/formats |
| Translation | Within same protocol family | Across different protocol families |
| Speed | Hardware-based, very fast | Software-based, slower |

**Applications:**
- **Router:** (i) Interconnecting two Ethernet LANs across a WAN link. (ii) Connecting a home LAN to the ISP.
- **Gateway:** (i) Email gateway between SMTP and X.400 mail systems. (ii) VoIP gateway between PSTN and SIP networks.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for applications of router, 1 mark for applications of gateway.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**What is a default gateway? Explain its role with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **default gateway** is the **router interface IP address** to which a host sends all packets destined for **non-local networks**. It is the "exit door" of the local subnet.

**Role:** When a host's subnet calculation shows the destination IP belongs to a different network, the host forwards the packet to the default gateway. The router then handles inter-network routing.

**Example:** A host with IP `192.168.1.10/24` and default gateway `192.168.1.1` wanting to reach `8.8.8.8` will encapsulate the packet in an Ethernet frame addressed to the MAC of `192.168.1.1`, which is the router.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark role, 1 mark example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Module-2]**

**Q. (a) Explain the internal architecture of a router with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A modern router is divided into four major functional components:

**1. Input Ports (2 marks)**
- Terminate the physical link (electrical/optical signals).
- Perform data-link decapsulation (strip Ethernet/PPP/HDLC header).
- Look up the destination IP in the **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** stored in high-speed **TCAM** memory.
- The lookup is done in hardware for line-rate forwarding.

**2. Switching Fabric (2 marks)**
- The internal interconnect that transfers the packet from input to output port.
- Types:
  - *Memory switching* ŌĆö packet copied to processor memory; single bottleneck.
  - *Bus switching* ŌĆö packet travels over shared bus; limited by bus bandwidth.
  - *Crossbar switching* ŌĆö parallel N├ŚN matrix; non-blocking; used in high-end routers.
- Provides **head-of-line blocking avoidance** and supports QoS.

**3. Output Ports (1.5 marks)**
- Receive packets from the switching fabric.
- Buffer them in queues (using RAM).
- Apply scheduling algorithms: **FIFO, WFQ, Priority Queuing, CBWFQ**.
- Encapsulate the packet in the data-link frame and transmit on the outgoing link.

**4. Routing Processor / Control Plane (1.5 marks)**
- Runs the **routing protocols** (OSPF, BGP, RIP, IS-IS).
- Builds the **Routing Information Base (RIB)**.
- Installs best routes into the FIB used by the data plane.
- Handles management functions: SNMP, CLI, NetConf.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **RIB (Routing Information Base)** with **FIB (Forwarding Information Base)**. RIB is built by routing protocols; FIB is the optimised forwarding copy pushed to hardware. Mentioning this distinction fetches an **extra mark**.

---

**Q. (b) With a suitable example, explain the working of Distance Vector Routing using the Bellman-Ford algorithm. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept (1 mark):** Distance Vector is a distributed iterative algorithm where each router maintains a table (vector) giving the best known distance to every destination. Routers exchange their vectors only with **directly connected neighbours**, and only at periodic intervals.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bellman-Ford Equation (2 marks):** The minimum distance from node $x$ to destination $y$ is:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \bigr]
$$

where $N(x)$ is the set of neighbours of $x$, and $c(x,v)$ is the cost of the link from $x$ to $v$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Worked Example (3 marks):**

Consider the network: AŌĆö1ŌĆöBŌĆö2ŌĆöCŌĆö3ŌĆöD and an extra link AŌĆö6ŌĆöC.

Initial distances (iteration 0): every node believes distance to non-neighbours is $\infty$.

Iteration 1 (A learns B=1, C=6; B learns A=1, C=2, D=5; C learns B=2, A=6, D=3; D learns C=3).

Iteration 2: A recomputes $D_A(D)$:

$$
D_A(D) = \min \bigl[ 1 + 5,\ 6 + 3 \bigr] = \min(6, 9) = 6
$$

Optimal path: **A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D**, cost 6.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Properties (1 mark):**
- Simple but slow convergence.
- Suffers from **count-to-infinity** problem (solved by **split horizon**, **poison reverse**, **hold-down timers**).
- Maximum hop count = 15 in RIP; 16 = infinity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** Students often write the Bellman-Ford equation but **forget to explain each variable**. In KTU valuation, even correct equations get **partial credit** without the variable explanation. Always define $D$, $c$, and $N(x)$ explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Module-2]**

**Q. (a) Explain the functions of a gateway. Compare protocol gateway and default gateway with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **gateway** is an internetworking device that operates at the highest layer necessary to connect two dissimilar network systems. It performs **protocol conversion** so that traffic from one network can be interpreted by another.

**Functions of a gateway (2 marks):**
1. **Protocol translation** ŌĆö converts headers, addressing schemes, and data formats.
2. **Session/Application mapping** ŌĆö bridges application-layer semantics.
3. **Encapsulation/Decapsulation** ŌĆö re-packages data for the destination network.
4. **Address and naming translation** ŌĆö e.g., NetBIOS Ōåö DNS.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Default Gateway | Protocol Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Router on a LAN through which hosts send non-local traffic | Device that translates between two different protocol stacks |
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 | Any layer (often Layer 7) |
| Purpose | Outbound traffic exit point | Cross-protocol communication |
| Example | `192.168.1.1` on a home router | SMTP Ōåö X.400 email gateway |
| Translation | None (same protocol) | Full protocol mapping |

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **Default Gateway example:** A laptop on `10.0.0.0/24` with default gateway `10.0.0.1` sends all Internet traffic to the router at `.1`.
- **Protocol Gateway example:** A company's old SNA mainframe is connected to a TCP/IP Ethernet LAN via a gateway that converts SNA frames into TCP/IP packets and vice-versa.

---

**Q. (b) With a diagram, explain the working of Link State Routing. List two advantages over Distance Vector Routing. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept (1.5 marks):** In Link State Routing, every router discovers its neighbours, measures the cost to each, and floods this information to **all routers in the area** using **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)**. Each router then builds a complete **Link State Database (LSDB)** and runs **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)** algorithm to compute the best path to every destination.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Working Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Neighbour Discovery** ŌĆö router sends **Hello packets** on all interfaces.
2. **Link Cost Measurement** ŌĆö based on bandwidth, delay, or administrator-set value.
3. **Flooding LSA** ŌĆö every router floods its LSAs to all others; reliable flooding using sequence numbers and acknowledgements.
4. **LSDB Construction** ŌĆö every router has an identical topological map.
5. **SPF Computation** ŌĆö Dijkstra's algorithm computes the shortest path tree with the local router as root.
6. **Routing Table Population** ŌĆö best paths are installed in the routing table.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Dijkstra's Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
d(v) = \min \bigl\{ d(v),\ d(u) + w(u,v) \bigr\}
$$

where $d(v)$ is the current best distance to $v$, $d(u)$ is the best distance to a known node $u$, and $w(u,v)$ is the link cost from $u$ to $v$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Advantages over Distance Vector (1 mark):**
1. **Fast convergence** ŌĆö LSP flooding reaches all routers within seconds; DV takes minutes.
2. **No count-to-infinity problem** ŌĆö every router has full topology.
3. **Hierarchical scalability** ŌĆö supports areas (OSPF Area 0, Area 1, ŌĆ”).
4. **Loop-free paths** ŌĆö Dijkstra's tree is inherently loop-free.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students confuse **OSPF (Link State)** with **RIP (Distance Vector)**. In KTU valuation, **memorising protocol name vs type** is mandatory. RIP = Distance Vector, OSPF = Link State, BGP = Path Vector. Getting this wrong = **ŌłÆ2 marks instantly**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in "Routers and Gateways" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Router vs Gateway** ŌĆö they are not synonyms. A gateway in the strict KTU sense is a *translator*. Marks lost: up to **2 per question**.
> 2. **Skipping variable definitions in equations** ŌĆö Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra must have $D$, $c$, $N(x)$ defined.
> 3. **Drawing boxes without labels** ŌĆö in block-diagram questions, every block must have its **function written below it**. Marks lost: **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting MAC-vs-IP hop-by-hop behaviour** ŌĆö the most asked conceptual question in KTU Module 2.
> 5. **Not mentioning Administrative Distance** when comparing routing protocols ŌĆö KTU 2024 examiners specifically look for AD values.
> 6. **Mixing up OSPF cost formula units** ŌĆö Cost uses **bandwidth ratio**, not absolute value. Default reference bandwidth is **100 Mbps**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Router** = Layer-3 forwarding device using routing tables and IP addresses.
- **Gateway** = inter-network device that **translates between different protocols** (default gateway is a special case where it is a router).
- **Internal blocks of a router:** Input Ports ŌåÆ Switching Fabric ŌåÆ Output Ports, all controlled by the **Routing Processor**.
- **Switching fabric types:** Memory, Bus, Crossbar (non-blocking).
- **Distance Vector (RIP)** uses **Bellman-Ford**: $D_x(y) = \min_v [c(x,v) + D_v(y)]$.
- **Link State (OSPF)** uses **Dijkstra / SPF**.
- **OSPF Cost** = Reference Bandwidth (10ŌüĖ) / Interface Bandwidth.
- **Hop count limit in RIP** = 15; 16 means infinity (unreachable).
- **BGP** runs over **TCP port 179** and is the **Path Vector** protocol of the Internet.
- **Default Gateway** = the local-subnet router IP to which hosts forward non-local packets.
- **Hop-by-hop behaviour:** MAC addresses change at every router; **IP addresses stay unchanged** end-to-end.
- **TTL** initial values: 64 (Linux), 128 (Windows), 255 (Cisco); decremented by 1 at every router; when 0, packet is dropped with **ICMP Time Exceeded**.
- **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** decides when multiple routes match; uses **TCAM** for line-rate lookup.
- **Routing Table columns (KTU mandatory):** Destination, Mask, Next Hop, Interface, Metric, AD.
- **Protocol Gateway example** = SMTP Ōåö X.400, TCP/IP Ōåö SNA.
- **Default Gateway example** = `192.168.1.1` on a `/24` home LAN.
- **OSPF AD = 110**, **RIP AD = 120**, **Static AD = 1**, **Connected AD = 0**, **eBGP AD = 20**, **iBGP AD = 200**.
- **Routing convergence** is fastest in **Link State** protocols and slowest in **BGP** (policy-driven).
- **Real-world applications** ŌĆö ISP core, enterprise campus, SD-WAN, automotive, IoT gateways.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Layer Design Issues. Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Network Layer Design Issues & Routing Algorithms ŌĆō The Optimality Principle

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Layer Design Issues. Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Network Layer ŌĆō Formal KTU Definition

The **Network Layer** is Layer **3** of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** reference model and the **TCP/IP** model. Its principal function is to **deliver packets from the source host to the destination host**, potentially across many heterogeneous physical networks, by performing **end-to-end path determination (routing)** and **packet forwarding (switching)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō PEEET528, Module 3):**
> *ŌĆ£Network Layer Design Issues ŌĆō Store-and-forward packet switching, services provided to the transport layer, implementation of connectionless and connection-oriented services. Routing Algorithms ŌĆō The Optimality Principle, shortest path algorithm (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford), flooding, distance vector routing, link state routing, hierarchical routing.ŌĆØ*

---

### 1.2 The Five Classic Design Issues of the Network Layer

For the KTU board examination, every answer on this topic **must** start by enumerating these **five design issues** before going deeper. Tanenbaum and Peterson-Davie (the two reference texts prescribed for this course) lay them out identically:

| # | Design Issue | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching** | A packet must arrive entirely at a router before it can be transmitted on the next link. |
| 2 | **Services Provided to the Transport Layer** | Whether the network offers a **connectionless** (datagram) or **connection-oriented** (virtual-circuit) service. |
| 3 | **Implementation of Connectionless Service** | Packets are routed **independently** (each called a *datagram*). |
| 4 | **Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service** | A **virtual circuit (VC)** is established before data flows; all packets follow the same path. |
| 5 | **Other Concerns** | Error control, flow control, packet lifetime, fragmentation & reassembly, congestion control. |

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ŌĆō "The Indian Postal System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Snail Mail vs. Registered Post**
>
> Imagine you are sending a letter from **Thiruvananthapuram (TVM)** to **Kasargod**.
>
> - **Store-and-Forward**: The **TVM post office** receives the **entire** letter, sorts it, then hands it to the next office. It does **not** chop the letter in half and forward two halves simultaneously to two different offices.
> - **Connectionless (Datagram)** = **Ordinary post**: Each letter (datagram) is stamped with the destination address, and the postal department may route *different letters of the same correspondence via different cities* (e.g., one via Kochi, another via Kozhikode). They are independent.
> - **Connection-Oriented (VC)** = **Registered post with tracking number**: A path is first agreed upon (VC established), the letter is assigned a tracking ID (VC identifier), and every intermediate office knows it is part of the *same conversation*; all subsequent letters follow the same predefined route until the sender cancels it (VC teardown).
> - **Optimality Principle (Routing)** = The postal system always tries to use the **shortest path** (e.g., NH-66 via Kozhikode) rather than detouring via Bengaluru.

---

### 1.4 Key Vocabulary for Board Answers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Packet / Datagram**: A self-contained unit of data carrying source & destination addresses.
> - **Virtual Circuit (VC)**: A logical, pre-established path through the subnet.
> - **Router (Gateway in older terminology)**: A Layer-3 device that forwards packets between networks.
> - **Routing Algorithm**: The procedure used by routers to decide on which outgoing link to place an arriving packet.
> - **Optimality Principle**: A formal statement about the structure of the set of all optimal routes.
> - **Sink Tree**: A directed tree rooted at the destination showing the unique optimal path from every source.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used at the Network Layer

> [!NOTE]
> The two metrics most frequently asked in KTU numerical problems are:
>
> - **Hop Count** ŌĆō the number of routers a packet traverses. **Unit: hops (dimensionless)**.
> - **Propagation Delay (D_prop)** ŌĆō the time for a signal to travel across one link of length **d** through a medium with propagation speed **s**.
>
> **Standard propagation speeds used in KTU problems:**
> - **Copper / Fiber optic link Ōēł 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s** (Ōēł 200,000 km/s)
> - **Satellite / Wireless link Ōēł 3 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s** (speed of light in vacuum)

The formula used throughout this module is:

$$D_{prop} = \frac{\text{distance } d}{\text{propagation speed } s}$$

These are the only **non-algorithm-specific** numbers you are expected to memorise for Module 3 numerics.

---

### 1.6 The Optimality Principle ŌĆō Formal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Optimality Principle (Bellman, 1957):**
> *ŌĆ£If router J lies on the optimal path from router I to router K, then the optimal path from J to K is also a sub-path of the same optimal path from I to K.ŌĆØ*

In plain English: **the tail of an optimal path is itself an optimal path.** Every optimal route from a source to a destination is composed of optimal sub-routes to every intermediate node along the way.

**Direct consequence:** The set of optimal routes from **all** sources to a **single** destination forms a tree with no loops, called a **sink tree** (or *destination tree*).

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Store-and-Forward Packet Switching (Detailed Theory)

In a **store-and-forward** subnet, a router must:

1. **Receive** the **entire** packet on an incoming link.
2. **Store** it in its local buffer (RAM).
3. **Inspect** the header (destination address) once the full packet has arrived.
4. **Forward** it onto the appropriate outgoing link.

This contrasts with **cut-through switching** (used in some LAN switches at Layer-2), where the device can begin transmitting a frame on the output port as soon as the destination address is read.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked to *ŌĆ£list the design issues of the network layerŌĆØ*, you may write the five points and **briefly** explain store-and-forward in 1ŌĆō2 lines. Do not spend more than **3 marksŌĆÖ worth** of writing on it; the rest of the question goes to the remaining four issues.

**Why is store-and-forward necessary at the network layer?**
- The router needs the **complete header** to perform lookup in its routing/forwarding table.
- It provides an opportunity to **check for bit errors** (though the actual error recovery is left to the link or transport layer).
- It is the **precondition for any dynamic routing decision** to be made.

---

## 2.2 Services Provided to the Transport Layer

The Network Layer can offer the Transport Layer one of two broad service classes:

| Service Class | Analogous To | Examples | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Connectionless (Datagram)** | Ordinary postal mail | **IP (Internet Protocol)** | No setup; each packet routed independently; packets may arrive out of order. |
| **Connection-Oriented (Virtual Circuit)** | Telephone call | **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)**, **X.25**, **Frame Relay** (historically) | A VC must be established; all packets of the connection share a VC number and travel the same path. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a **comparison** of datagram and VC subnets. A common 7-mark sub-question asks for *exactly this* table. Memorise the rows: **setup, addressing, routing decision, packet order, congestion handling, reliability, examples**.

---

## 2.3 Implementation of Connectionless Service (Datagram Subnet)

In a datagram subnet, **every packet is routed independently**:

- Each router maintains a **routing table** that maps destination prefixes ŌåÆ outgoing interface.
- The table is updated periodically by a **routing algorithm** (e.g., RIP ŌĆō Distance Vector; OSPF ŌĆō Link State).
- Two consecutive packets of the same TCP connection may take **different paths**, possibly arriving out of order.

> [!NOTE]
> The Internet is the canonical example. When a KTU problem mentions ŌĆ£the InternetŌĆØ, the expected answer is the **datagram** model.

---

## 2.4 Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service (Virtual-Circuit Subnet)

A virtual circuit uses **three phases**:

1. **VC Setup** ŌĆō The source sends a *CALL REQUEST* packet; a path is determined by the routing algorithm; each router along the path reserves resources (buffer space, possibly bandwidth) and assigns a local **VC identifier (VCI)**. A *CALL ACCEPTED* packet returns to the source.
2. **Data Transfer** ŌĆō Every data packet carries only the **VC number**, not the full destination address. Routers use the VCI to index into a per-VC state table (VCI table).
3. **VC Teardown** ŌĆō A *CLEAR REQUEST* packet propagates, and routers release the reserved resources.

**Why a VCI and not a full destination address?**
- **Speed**: A short integer is faster to look up than a 32-bit or 128-bit address.
- **State efficiency**: Each router only stores the VCI mapping for flows actually passing through it.
- **Resource reservation**: The VCI entry doubles as a handle for the reserved resources.

---

## 2.5 The Optimality Principle ŌĆō Proof-Style Explanation (For 7-Mark Answers)

We will now expand the one-line statement given in ┬¦1.6 into a rigorous argument suitable for a board answer.

**Restatement of the principle:**

> *If the optimal route from I to K passes through J, then the optimal route from J to K is exactly the suffix of the optimal IŌåÆK path from J onwards.*

**Proof by contradiction:**

Assume the optimal path I ŌåÆ K goes through J, so the path is

$$I \;\longrightarrow\; J \;\longrightarrow\; K \text{ (via some path } P_{JK}\text{)}$$

and the total cost of this path is

$$C_{IK} = C_{IJ} + C_{P_{JK}}$$

Suppose, for contradiction, that there exists an **alternative** path $P_{JK}^\star$ from J to K such that

$$C_{P_{JK}^\star} < C_{P_{JK}}$$

Then we could construct a new I ŌåÆ K path

$$I \;\longrightarrow\; J \;\longrightarrow\; K \text{ (via } P_{JK}^\star\text{)}$$

with total cost

$$C_{IJ} + C_{P_{JK}^\star} \;<\; C_{IJ} + C_{P_{JK}} \;=\; C_{IK}$$

contradicting the optimality of the original I ŌåÆ K path. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** This is a 7-mark sub-question favourite. Practice writing it in **three** clear paragraphs: *Statement ŌåÆ Assumption for contradiction ŌåÆ Derivation of contradiction ŌåÆ Conclusion*.

---

## 2.6 The Sink Tree (or Destination Tree)

A direct corollary of the Optimality Principle:

> *The set of all optimal paths from **every** node to a **single** destination D forms a **directed tree** rooted at D.*

**Why a tree (and not a general graph)?**

Suppose two optimal paths from I to D shared a common node J but then diverged. By the Optimality Principle, the suffix from J onwards in **both** paths would have to be optimal from J to D. If the two suffixes differed, only one could be optimal ŌĆö contradiction. Hence **all optimal paths from I to D must coincide from their first common node to D**, and they can never contain a **cycle** (a cycle would strictly increase the cost if all edge weights are non-negative). Therefore, the structure is **loop-free**, i.e., a **tree**.

**Properties of the sink tree:**

- It has **N ŌłÆ 1** edges if the subnet has **N** nodes.
- Every node (except the root) has **exactly one outgoing edge** ŌĆö the first hop on its optimal route to the root.
- It is **destination-specific**: there is one sink tree per destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sink tree is **conceptual**; routers do not actually store the entire tree. They store only the *first hop* of their optimal path to each destination, which is exactly the **next-hop entry** in the forwarding table.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table collects **every formula** the syllabus requires for this sub-topic. Memorise the table once; it covers both the design-issues and the optimality-principle parts.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Unit | Notes / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Propagation delay on a link | $D_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds (s) | d = link length (m), s = propagation speed (m/s). |
| 2 | Transmission delay for a packet of L bits on a link of rate R | $D_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds (s) | Used in store-and-forward timing problems. |
| 3 | Total delay at one router (store-and-forward) | $D_{total} = D_{trans} + D_{prop} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$ | seconds (s) | $D_{queue}$ and $D_{proc}$ often grouped. |
| 4 | Hop count for a path | $h = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} 1$ (number of routers traversed) | hops | A common routing metric. |
| 5 | Path cost (additive metric) | $C(P) = \sum_{(u,v) \in P} w(u,v)$ | varies (ms, hops, cost units) | Sum of edge weights along path P. |
| 6 | Optimality-principle cost identity | $C^*(I,K) = C^*(I,J) + C^*(J,K)$ for J on the optimal IŌåÆK path | varies | Direct consequence of ┬¦2.5. |
| 7 | Sink-tree size (number of edges) | $\vert E_{tree} \vert = N - 1$ | dimensionless | N = number of nodes in the subnet. |
| 8 | Datagram header (IP) | Source IP (32b) + Destination IP (32b) + protocol fields | bits | Used for every packet. |
| 9 | Virtual-circuit header | VCI (typically 8ŌĆō16 bits) | bits | Used per VC. |
| 10 | Number of VCs in a router | Bounded by available VCI space ├Ś state memory | ŌĆō | Theoretical; not numerically asked. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown note for the reader:** in row 7, the size is written using `\vert` and `\vert` rather than the vertical bar character so that the surrounding markdown table does not break. KTU board answers use ordinary vertical bars ŌĆō this is a presentation safeguard only.

---

## 2.8 Why Does the Optimality Principle Matter in Real Engineering?

1. **Correctness of routing algorithms:** Any correct shortest-path algorithm (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford) relies on the optimality principle; without it, even an optimal IŌåÆK path could have a non-optimal JŌåÆK suffix, and algorithms could not be built incrementally.
2. **Loop-free routing:** Because the optimal routes form a tree, the routing tables derived from a converged algorithm are guaranteed to be **loop-free**, preventing the *count-to-infinity* pathologies of na├»ve distance-vector implementations.
3. **Traffic engineering:** ISPs use the optimality principle (extended to multi-commodity flows) to engineer primary/backup paths so that failures can be repaired locally.
4. **QoS routing:** Modern QoS-aware routing algorithms (e.g., OSPF-TE, MPLS-TE) compute shortest paths with respect to multiple additive metrics, all of which still respect the optimality principle.
