# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆō Computing the Sink Tree Manually (14-Mark Favourite)

> **Problem:**
> Consider the subnet below with the indicated link costs (non-negative integers).
> Find the **sink tree rooted at node D** using the Optimality Principle.
>
> | Edge | (A,B) | (A,C) | (B,C) | (B,D) | (C,D) | (C,E) | (D,E) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Cost | 2 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
>
> (The graph is undirected; the cost is the same in both directions.)

**Solution ŌĆō Step 1: Enumerate all paths from every node to D.**

We list every simple path from a given node to D, ignoring the obvious detour ones for now:

- **A ŌåÆ D:**
  - Path P1: A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D, cost = 2 + 4 = **6**
  - Path P2: A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D, cost = 2 + 1 + 3 = **6**
  - Path P3: A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D, cost = 5 + 3 = **8**
  - Path P4: A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D, cost = 5 + 1 + 4 = **10**
  - Path P5: A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ D, cost = 5 + 2 + 1 = **8**

- **B ŌåÆ D:**
  - Path Q1: B ŌåÆ D, cost = **4**
  - Path Q2: B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D, cost = 1 + 3 = **4**
  - Path Q3: B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ D, cost = 1 + 2 + 1 = **4**

- **C ŌåÆ D:**
  - Path R1: C ŌåÆ D, cost = **3**
  - Path R2: C ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ D, cost = 2 + 1 = **3**
  - Path R3: C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D, cost = 1 + 4 = **5**

- **E ŌåÆ D:**
  - Path S1: E ŌåÆ D, cost = **1**
  - Path S2: E ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D, cost = 2 + 3 = **5**

**Solution ŌĆō Step 2: Identify the minimum cost for each source.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^*(A,D) &= 6 \quad &\text{(P1 or P2 tie)} \\
d^*(B,D) &= 4 \quad &\text{(Q1, Q2 or Q3 tie)} \\
d^*(C,D) &= 3 \quad &\text{(R1 or R2 tie)} \\
d^*(E,D) &= 1 \quad &\text{(S1)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution ŌĆō Step 3: Choose one optimal first hop for each node.**

For each non-root node, we must select the *first hop* that lies on at least one optimal path:

- **A ŌåÆ B** (first hop of P1 = AŌåÆB) ŌĆö cost to D via B = 2 + 4 = 6 Ō£ō
- **B ŌåÆ D** (first hop of Q1 = BŌåÆD) ŌĆö direct cost 4 Ō£ō
- **C ŌåÆ D** (first hop of R1 = CŌåÆD) ŌĆö direct cost 3 Ō£ō
- **E ŌåÆ D** (first hop of S1 = EŌåÆD) ŌĆö direct cost 1 Ō£ō

> [!NOTE]
> Even though there is a **tie** (e.g., for B, both BŌåÆD and BŌåÆC are optimal), the resulting *next hop* in the forwarding table is allowed to be any one of the optimal first hops. The routerŌĆÖs algorithm will pick one deterministically (usually the lowest interface ID, or the first one learned).

**Solution ŌĆō Step 4: Build the sink tree.**

The sink tree is therefore:

```
            D
            |
   +--------+--------+
   |                 |
   B                 C ---- E
                    (B also allowed: A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D)
```

More precisely, the directed edges (pointing towards the root D) are:

$$
\text{Edges of sink tree} = \{A\!\to\!B,\; B\!\to\!D,\; C\!\to\!D,\; E\!\to\!D\}
$$

This tree has $N=5$ nodes and $N-1=4$ edges, **exactly as predicted by the formula** $\vert E_{tree}\vert = N - 1$ from ┬¦2.7, row 7.

**Verification of the Optimality Principle (KTU Examiner loves this check):**

Take the optimal A ŌåÆ D path: A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D, cost 6. By the optimality principle, the suffix from B to D, namely B ŌåÆ D, cost 4, **must itself be optimal** from B to D. We have $d^*(B,D)=4$, so the principle holds. Similarly the suffix from A to B (A ŌåÆ B, cost 2) must be optimal from A to B. Indeed the only path A ŌåÆ B is unique, so its cost 2 is trivially optimal. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆō Deriving Path Cost Using the Optimality Identity (6-Mark Type)

> **Problem:**
> In a subnet, the optimal path from A to E has cost 8 and passes through B, then C. The optimal path from A to B is known to have cost 2. The optimal path from C to E is known to have cost 5. Verify the consistency using the optimality identity, and find the cost of the optimal B ŌåÆ C path.

**Solution ŌĆō Step 1: Write the optimality identity.**

The optimal A ŌåÆ E path is A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ E, so

$$C^*(A,E) = C^*(A,B) + C^*(B,C) + C^*(C,E)$$

**Solution ŌĆō Step 2: Substitute the known values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 &= 2 + C^*(B,C) + 5 \\
C^*(B,C) &= 8 - 2 - 5 \\
C^*(B,C) &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution ŌĆō Step 3: Interpretation.**

The cost of the optimal B ŌåÆ C path is **1**. The identity $C^*(I,K) = C^*(I,J) + C^*(J,K)$ for every J on the optimal IŌåÆK path is therefore the **computational backbone** of all shortest-path algorithms in Module 3. (Both Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford, the next sub-topics in the syllabus, instantiate this identity repeatedly.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆō Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit

The following table is the **standard KTU model answer** for the 7-mark *ŌĆ£Compare datagram and virtual-circuit subnetsŌĆØ* sub-question. Practise reproducing it from memory.

| Parameter | Datagram Subnet | Virtual-Circuit Subnet |
|---|---|---|
| **VC Setup** | Not required. | Required. |
| **Addressing** | Each packet carries full source & destination address. | Each packet carries a short VCI. |
| **Routing decision** | Made **per-packet**. | Made **per-VC** (at setup). |
| **Path taken by packets of a flow** | May vary (different routes). | Same path for the entire VC lifetime. |
| **Effect of router failure** | Few packets lost; later packets routed around the failure. | All VCs through the failed router are terminated. |
| **Congestion control** | Harder; no per-flow state. | Easier; resources reserved per VC. |
| **QoS support** | Weak (best effort). | Strong (IntServ, DiffServ-like reservations possible). |
| **State at routers** | Routing table only. | Routing table + per-VC state. |
| **Packet ordering** | Not guaranteed; receiver must reorder. | Guaranteed (in-order delivery). |
| **Implementation complexity** | Low. | High. |
| **Examples** | IP, UDP-style usage. | ATM, X.25, Frame Relay, MPLS LSP. |
| **Used in the Internet today?** | **Yes (dominant).** | Only as a **tunnel overlay** (e.g., MPLS VPN, L2TP, GRE). |

---

## 3.4 Algebraic Derivation ŌĆō Why the Sink Tree is Loop-Free (For Curious Students)

Assume, for contradiction, that the optimal-path structure from all sources to destination D contains a **cycle** $X_1 \to X_2 \to \dots \to X_m \to X_1$ with $m \geq 2$. Let the cost of this cycle be

$$C_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w(X_i, X_{i+1}) \quad \text{with } X_{m+1} \equiv X_1$$

Since the problem only makes sense for non-negative link costs,

$$C_{cycle} \geq 0$$

and the cycle is *useful* only if $C_{cycle} > 0$ (if $C_{cycle} = 0$, the link weights are degenerate and we can pick any one edge; we ignore this pathological case).

**Claim:** The presence of this positive-cost cycle allows a *strictly shorter* path from some $X_i$ to D by **removing the cycle** and using the direct edge from $X_i$ to its successor outside the cycle. This contradicts the optimality of the original path that included the cycle.

Formalisation: let $P^*_i$ be the optimal path from $X_i$ to D. If $P^*_i$ traverses the entire cycle once, then

$$C(P^*_i) = C_{cycle} + C(P^*_{X_{i+1}} \text{ after the cycle})$$

But the path $X_i \to X_{i+1} \to \dots \to X_1 \to \dots \to D$ that *omits* the cycle has cost

$$C_{cycle\text{-}free} = C_{cycle} - C_{cycle} + C(P^*_{X_{i+1}} \text{ after the cycle}) = C(P^*_{X_{i+1}} \text{ after the cycle})$$

which is strictly smaller if $C_{cycle} > 0$. Therefore, the original path $P^*_i$ was not optimal, contradicting the assumption. Hence the optimal structure contains **no cycles** and must be a **tree**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Common Pitfall ŌĆō Tie Breaking in Sink Trees

When two optimal paths from a source to a destination have **the same cost**, a sink tree can be drawn in **multiple** valid ways. The KTU examiner will accept any one correct sink tree, but you **must** show the cost calculation for both alternatives so the examiner can see you understood the tie. A 14-mark question that just draws one tree without showing the tie will usually lose **2 marks** under the ŌĆ£workingŌĆØ component.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do **not** mix two different optimal next-hops for the *same* source in the same sink tree. A sink tree is a **deterministic** structure per source: every source picks **exactly one** outgoing edge in the tree, even if the underlying routing table allows several.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆō Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit

The following Mermaid diagram shows how a packet flows through a router in **datagram** and **virtual-circuit** modes, side by side. The functional blocks are the actual logic gates inside a router (routing table, VCI table, lookup, output queue).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DATAGRAM["DATAGRAM MODE  --  Per-packet decision"]
        d1["Incoming packet<br/>src = 10.0.0.1<br/>dst = 10.0.0.5"] --> d2["Parse header<br/>extract dst IP"]
        d2 --> d3["Lookup in<br/>ROUTING TABLE"]
        d3 --> d4["Forward via<br/>selected output port"]
    end

    subgraph VC["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT MODE  --  Per-VC decision"]
        v1["Incoming packet<br/>VCI = 47"] --> v2["Parse header<br/>extract VCI"]
        v2 --> v3["Lookup in<br/>VCI TABLE"]
        v3 --> v4["Forward via<br/>stored output port"]
    end

    classDef routerBlock fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#264653,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    class d1,v1 data
    class d2,d3,d4,v2,v3,v4 routerBlock
```

**How to read this diagram for a KTU answer:**

- The **left** branch is the **datagram** path. Every packet causes a fresh lookup in the routing table.
- The **right** branch is the **virtual-circuit** path. The lookup is in the **VCI table**, which is populated only at VC setup; data packets are switched purely on the VCI.
- The two branches are **functionally identical** in the sense of moving a packet out of the router; the difference is **where the lookup information comes from**.

**Notes for the examiner:**

- The **directed** edges (arrows) all point **towards the root D**; this is the canonical ŌĆ£drainŌĆØ or ŌĆ£sinkŌĆØ representation.
- The **red** root emphasises that this is a sink tree, not a generic spanning tree.
- The tree has exactly **N ŌłÆ 1 = 4** edges, as required.

---

## 4.4 Illustration of the Optimality Principle on a 3-Node Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I["I<br/>source"] -->|"optimal sub-path<br/>cost C*(I,J)"| J["J<br/>intermediate"]
    J -->|"optimal sub-path<br/>cost C*(J,K)"| K["K<br/>destination"]

    classDef node fill:#003049,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    class I,J,K node
```

**Reading guide:** The arc from I to J has cost $C^*(I,J)$ and is optimal by definition of being a suffix of the optimal IŌåÆK path. The arc from J to K has cost $C^*(J,K)$ and is **also optimal**, by the Optimality Principle. This picture is the *visual definition* of the principle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Two Questions)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Define the Optimality Principle. State the corollary that the set of optimal routes to a single destination forms a tree.**

**Model Answer (Ōēł 80ŌĆō100 words):**

The **Optimality Principle** states that *if router J lies on the optimal path from router I to router K, then the optimal path from J to K is also a sub-path of that same optimal path from I to K*. In other words, the suffix of an optimal path is itself optimal.

A direct corollary is that the collection of all optimal paths from **every** source to a **single** destination forms a **directed tree** rooted at the destination, called a **sink tree**. The tree has $N-1$ edges for an $N$-node subnet, contains no cycles (since cycles would strictly increase the cost), and uniquely defines the next-hop entry of every routerŌĆÖs forwarding table.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024]
**List any five design issues of the network layer as specified in the KTU syllabus.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching** ŌĆō a packet must be received in full at a router before being forwarded.
2. **Services Provided to the Transport Layer** ŌĆō choice between connectionless and connection-oriented service.
3. **Implementation of Connectionless Service** ŌĆō datagram subnet, each packet routed independently.
4. **Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service** ŌĆō virtual-circuit subnet, all packets of a connection follow the same path.
5. **Other concerns** ŌĆō error control, flow control, packet lifetime, fragmentation & reassembly, congestion control, and routing.

*(Any five of the above are acceptable; these are the canonical five as per TanenbaumŌĆÖs text, which the KTU syllabus directly references.)*

---

## Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

> **(a)** Explain **store-and-forward packet switching** at the network layer. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **implementation of a connectionless service** and the **implementation of a connection-oriented service** at the network layer. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** What is the **Optimality Principle**? Prove that the set of optimal routes from all sources to a single destination forms a **tree**. Draw the **sink tree** for the given graph with destination **D**. (7 marks)
>
> | Edge | (A,B) | (A,C) | (B,C) | (B,D) | (C,D) | (C,E) | (D,E) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Cost | 2 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |

**Model Solution ŌĆō Part (a) (7 marks):**

1. **Store-and-Forward Switching (2 marks):**
   - Definition: A router must **receive the entire packet**, **store it in a buffer**, **inspect the header** and only then **forward** it on the appropriate outgoing link.
   - **Why?** The router needs the complete header for routing-table lookup; partial packets cannot be routed.
   - **Delay** incurred at one router = $D_{trans} + D_{prop} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$, where $D_{trans} = L/R$ and $D_{prop} = d/s$.

2. **Connectionless Service (2.5 marks):**
   - No VC is set up.
   - Each packet (datagram) carries the **full destination address**.
   - Routers consult their **routing tables** on a **per-packet basis**.
   - Two packets of the same flow may take different paths and arrive out of order.
   - **Example:** the Internet using IP.

3. **Connection-Oriented Service (2.5 marks):**
   - A **virtual circuit (VC)** is established before data flows; the path is fixed for the entire VC.
   - Packets carry a short **VC identifier (VCI)** instead of the full destination address.
   - Routers maintain **per-VC state** (VCI tables) created at setup.
   - Three phases: **VC setup, data transfer, VC teardown**.
   - **Example:** ATM, X.25, MPLS LSP.

**Model Solution ŌĆō Part (b) (7 marks):**

1. **Optimality Principle (2 marks):** State the principle verbatim (see ┬¦1.6) and give the formal cost identity

$$C^*(I,K) = C^*(I,J) + C^*(J,K) \quad \text{for any J on the optimal I}\to\text{K path.}$$

2. **Proof that the optimal structure is a tree (2 marks):**
   - **Tree property 1 ŌĆō Connected:** every source has at least one optimal path to D by definition.
   - **Tree property 2 ŌĆō Acyclic:** assume a cycle exists; by the cycle-removal argument in ┬¦3.4, a strictly shorter path could be constructed, contradicting optimality. Hence the structure has no cycles.
   - **Tree property 3 ŌĆō Unique path:** a connected, acyclic graph on N nodes has exactly $N-1$ edges and a unique path between every pair of nodes.
   - Therefore, the optimal structure is a **tree** (the **sink tree**).

3. **Sink-tree computation for the given graph (3 marks):**
   - Compute the shortest distance from every node to D (Dijkstra or manual enumeration, see ┬¦3.1).
   - The minimum costs are $d^*(A,D) = 6$, $d^*(B,D) = 4$, $d^*(C,D) = 3$, $d^*(E,D) = 1$.
   - Choose the optimal first hop for each node: $A \to B$, $B \to D$, $C \to D$, $E \to D$.
   - Draw the sink tree (see ┬¦4.3) with D at the root.
   - Verify that the tree has $N-1 = 4$ edges, confirming the formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Compare **datagram subnets** and **virtual-circuit subnets** under the following heads: setup, addressing, routing decision, effect of router failure, congestion handling, packet ordering, and examples. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With the help of a suitable example, illustrate the **optimality principle** for a 5-node network. Show that the suffix of an optimal path is itself optimal. (7 marks)

**Model Solution ŌĆō Part (a) (7 marks):**

Reproduce the comparison table from ┬¦3.3. Award 1 mark per row ├Ś 7 rows = 7 marks. Highlight the **key insight**: the Internet uses **datagram**, whereas ATM/MPLS uses **VC**. VC gives easier QoS and congestion control at the cost of state; datagram gives resilience and simplicity at the cost of QoS guarantees.

**Model Solution ŌĆō Part (b) (7 marks):**

1. **State the principle (2 marks):** See ┬¦1.6 / ┬¦2.5.
2. **Choose a 5-node example (2 marks):** Nodes {A, B, C, D, E}; provide a graph with edge weights. For instance, the same graph as in ┬¦3.1.
3. **Show the optimal A ŌåÆ E path** (1 mark): For the example, A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ E with cost $2+1+2=5$ (or whichever path is shortest in your chosen example).
4. **Demonstrate the suffix property (2 marks):** The suffix from B to E is B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ E with cost $1+2=3$. Verify that $d^*(B,E) = 3$ (no shorter path exists from B to E in the graph). Similarly the suffix from A to C (A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C, cost $2+1=3$) is optimal from A to C. This confirms the principle: the suffix of an optimal path is itself optimal.

---

## KTU ExaminerŌĆÖs Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic**
>
> 1. **Skipping the proof.** A 7-mark sub-question asking ŌĆ£prove that the optimal-path structure is a treeŌĆØ requires a *formal argument* (e.g., the cycle-removal argument of ┬¦3.4). Stating the theorem without proof will fetch **at most 2 out of 7** marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the forwarding-table consequence.** Many students describe the sink tree but fail to mention that each router only stores the **first hop** in its forwarding table. Examiners explicitly test this with the prompt ŌĆ£what does a router actually store?ŌĆØ. You will lose **1ŌĆō2 marks** if you omit it.
> 3. **Drawing the sink tree with undirected edges.** A sink tree is **directed** with arrows pointing **towards the destination**. An undirected diagram may be accepted with a small deduction.
> 4. **Confusing ŌĆ£connectionlessŌĆØ with ŌĆ£unreliableŌĆØ.** The datagram model is *unreliable* by default, but reliability is a *property of the service*, not its *type*. A datagram service can be made reliable by the transport layer (TCP); this is a different question.
> 5. **Listing the design issues without explaining any.** A common 3-mark trap. Listing five points and giving no explanation earns at most 1 mark. You need a one-line explanation for **each** point to get the full 3.
> 6. **Mismatching the cost-identity formula.** Writing $C^*(I,K) = C^*(I,J) + C^*(J,K)$ for an *arbitrary* J (not necessarily on the optimal IŌåÆK path) is a *fatal* error. The principle is valid **only** for J that lies on the optimal IŌåÆK path.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **network layer** is Layer 3; its job is **end-to-end delivery of packets**.
- [x] The **five design issues** are: store-and-forward switching, services to transport, connectionless implementation, connection-oriented implementation, other concerns.
- [x] **Store-and-forward** = receive the *whole* packet, then forward.
- [x] **Datagram** = no setup, full address in every packet, per-packet routing; example **IP**.
- [x] **Virtual circuit** = setup phase, VCI in every packet, per-VC routing; example **ATM / MPLS**.
- [x] **Optimality Principle** = the suffix of an optimal path is itself optimal.
- [x] Formal cost identity: $C^*(I,K) = C^*(I,J) + C^*(J,K)$ for J on the optimal IŌåÆK path.
- [x] **Sink tree** = directed tree of all optimal paths to a single destination, $N-1$ edges, no cycles.
- [x] A router stores only the **first hop** (next-hop) of the optimal path to each destination.
- [x] Propagation speed on **copper/fiber Ōēł 2 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s**, on **wireless/satellite Ōēł 3 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s**.
- [x] Total delay at a store-and-forward router Ōēł $D_{trans} + D_{prop} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$.
- [x] The optimality principle is the *theoretical foundation* of **DijkstraŌĆÖs** and **Bellman-Ford** shortest-path algorithms (the next sub-topics in Module 3).
- [x] When two optimal paths tie, **any one** may be chosen for the sink tree; show both in your working.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision mantra for the KTU board exam:**
> *ŌĆ£Store-and-forward, two services, one routing algorithm, one optimality principle, one sink tree.ŌĆØ* If you can chant this and draw the diagram, you have this sub-topic mastered.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Path Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shortest Path Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shortest Path Routing** is a class of routing algorithms used in packet-switched computer networks in which the primary objective is to compute, for every sourceŌĆōdestination pair, a path through the network topology that minimizes a chosen cost metric (e.g., hop count, delay, or administrative weight). The result of the algorithm is a routing table that, for each node, lists the *next-hop* neighbour on the minimum-cost path to every destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, Shortest Path Routing is studied as the *theoretical foundation* of adaptive/non-adaptive routing. You must be able to (i) manually execute Dijkstra's link-state algorithm and the BellmanŌĆōFord distance-vector algorithm, and (ii) state the convergence, complexity, and loop-formation properties of each.

The two canonical algorithms that operationalize this paradigm are:

1. **Dijkstra's Algorithm (1959)** ŌĆö A *link-state* algorithm that computes shortest paths from a single source node to all other nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights. Complexity: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O(E + V \log V)$ with a priority queue.
2. **BellmanŌĆōFord Algorithm (1958)** ŌĆö A *distance-vector* algorithm that computes single-source shortest paths and can handle negative edge weights. Complexity: $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Google Maps View

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi** to **Bengaluru**. You do not simply follow one fixed road; instead, Google Maps evaluates every possible combination of highways (Kochi ŌåÆ Salem ŌåÆ Bengaluru, Kochi ŌåÆ Coimbatore ŌåÆ Bengaluru, Kochi ŌåÆ Mysuru ŌåÆ Bengaluru, etc.) and selects the route that minimizes **time** (or distance, or toll). Crucially:

- The **map of roads** is the *network graph* $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$.
- The **travel time on each road segment** is the *link cost* (weight) $c(u, v)$.
- The **chosen sequence of cities from start to destination** is the *shortest path* $P_{s \to d}$.
- The **re-evaluation triggered by a road closure** is the *reconvergence* of the routing protocol.

Shortest Path Routing in a computer network is exactly this: every router constantly maintains its own "map" of the network and re-evaluates routes whenever a link's cost changes (failure, congestion, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Shortest"?** It is a *misnomer*. The path is not necessarily shortest in physical length ŌĆö it is the path that yields the **minimum total cost** as defined by the chosen metric. The term is historical (graph-theory convention) and is retained in textbooks for compatibility.

### 1.3 Common Cost Metrics (KTU-Favoured)

| Metric | Symbol | Practical Use | Default In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hop Count | $H$ | Minimizing number of intermediate routers | RIP (legacy) |
| Delay (ms) | $D$ | Minimizing end-to-end latency | ARPANET (historical) |
| Bandwidth (Mbps) | $B$ | Maximizing throughput | Some QoS-aware schemes |
| Administrative Weight | $w$ | Manual operator-tuned cost | OSPF (Cisco default) |
| Composite (e.g., $D + \lambda \cdot (1-B)$) | $C$ | Multi-objective optimization | Research / SDN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In exam answers, when asked "what is the cost metric", default to **hop count** unless the question explicitly says otherwise. RIP uses hop count; OSPF uses a cost related to bandwidth; BellmanŌĆōFord in textbooks is almost always illustrated with hop count.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Routing Problem ŌĆö Formally Stated

Given a network modelled as a **weighted undirected (or directed) graph** $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$, where:

- $V$ = set of routers (nodes), with $\vert V \vert = n$
- $E$ = set of links (edges), with $\vert E \vert = m$
- $w(u, v) > 0$ = non-negative cost of the link between nodes $u$ and $v$ (for Dijkstra)
- $w(u, v) \in \mathbb{R}$ = real-valued cost (for BellmanŌĆōFord, allowing negatives)

**Objective:** For every node $s \in V$, find the minimum-cost path to every node $v \in V$:

$$
D(s, v) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s \to v}} \sum_{(u_i, u_{i+1}) \in P} w(u_i, u_{i+1})
$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{s \to v}$ is the set of all possible paths from $s$ to $v$.

### 2.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm ŌĆö The "Greedy" Approach

Dijkstra's algorithm builds the shortest-path tree (SPT) outward from the source by **greedily** selecting, at each iteration, the unvisited node with the currently smallest tentative distance. Once a node is *finalized*, its distance cannot be improved further.

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Assign to every node $v$ a tentative distance $d(v) = \infty$ and a predecessor $\pi(v) = \text{NIL}$. Set $d(s) = 0$ for the source.
2. **Iteration:** From the set of *unvisited* nodes $Q$, pick the node $u$ with the minimum $d(u)$. Mark $u$ as visited (finalize it).
3. **Relaxation:** For every neighbour $v$ of $u$ (i.e., $(u, v) \in E$), update:

$$
d(v) \leftarrow \min\bigl(d(v),\; d(u) + w(u, v)\bigr)
$$

4. **Repeat** Step 2 and Step 3 until $Q = \emptyset$ (all nodes finalized) or until the destination is finalized.
5. **Path Reconstruction:** Trace $\pi(v) \leftarrow \pi(\pi(v)) \leftarrow \dots$ backward from $v$ to $s$.

**Why does the greedy step work?**
At the moment node $u$ is selected from $Q$, any path from $s$ to $u$ via an unvisited node $w$ would have length at least $d(w) \geq d(u)$ (since $u$ had the minimum). Therefore, no alternative path through the unvisited set can be shorter, and $d(u)$ is final.

**Limitation:** Fails for **negative** edge weights because a later, unvisited node could retroactively provide a cheaper path through an already-finalized node.

### 2.3 BellmanŌĆōFord Algorithm ŌĆö The "Distributed" Approach

BellmanŌĆōFord is the workhorse of **distance-vector** protocols (RIP, IGRP, BGP path attributes). It iteratively relaxes all edges; after exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ passes, all shortest paths are guaranteed (assuming no negative cycles).

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \in V$; $d(s) = 0$.
2. **Repeat $\vert V \vert - 1$ times:** For every edge $(u, v) \in E$:

$$
d(v) \leftarrow \min\bigl(d(v),\; d(u) + w(u, v)\bigr)
$$

3. **Negative-Cycle Check (optional):** Run one more pass. If any distance decreases, a negative-weight cycle exists reachable from $s$.

**Distributed Variant ŌĆö Distance-Vector (DV) Protocol:**
In a real network, no node knows the whole graph. Each router $u$ maintains a table $D_u[x]$ = its current best estimate of distance to every destination $x$, and sends this to immediate neighbours. Whenever a neighbour $v$ advertises a new distance to $x$, router $u$ updates:

$$
D_u[x] \leftarrow \min\bigl(D_u[x],\; w(u, v) + D_v[x]\bigr) \quad \forall v \in \text{neighbours}(u)
$$

This is exactly BellmanŌĆōFord's relaxation rule, executed **asynchronously and distributedly** at every node ŌĆö a classic example of *embarrassingly parallel* computation.

**Limitation:** Suffers from the **count-to-infinity** problem ŌĆö slow convergence when a link fails, because bad news propagates one hop per iteration.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examinable** under KTU 2024 Scheme for this topic. Memorize the symbols, the conditions, and the units.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Applicable Algorithm | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d(v) \leftarrow \min(d(v), d(u) + w(u,v))$ | Edge relaxation | Both Dijkstra & BellmanŌĆōFord | Cost is unitless or per chosen metric |
| 2 | $d(s) = 0,\ d(v) = \infty\ \forall v \neq s$ | Initialization | Both | Standard start state |
| 3 | $T_{Dijkstra} = O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Time complexity | Dijkstra | Latter uses a min-heap |
| 4 | $T_{BellmanFord} = O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Time complexity | BellmanŌĆōFord | $\vert V \vert - 1$ passes, $\vert E \vert$ edges per pass |
| 5 | $N_{iter} = \vert V \vert - 1$ | Required passes for convergence (no neg. cycle) | BellmanŌĆōFord | Proven by path-length argument |
| 6 | $L_{max} = \vert V \vert - 1$ | Max number of edges in any simple shortest path | Both | An acyclic path can use at most $n-1$ edges |
| 7 | $D_{count-to-\infty} \leq 2^h - 1$ | Worst-case count-to-infinity distance | Distance-Vector | $h$ = hop-distance to failure site |
| 8 | $c_{OSPF} = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{c_{link}}$ | OSPF cost (Cisco default) | OSPF | Reference BW usually $10^8$ bps |
| 9 | $c_{OSPF,\text{normalized}} = \dfrac{10^8}{\text{BW}_{link}\ \text{in bps}}$ | OSPF cost normalized | OSPF | Result is integer (rounded down) |
| 10 | $D_{max} = (n-1) \cdot w_{max}$ | Upper bound on shortest path cost | Both | $w_{max}$ = max edge weight |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Internet Backbone (IS-IS, OSPF):** Both use Dijkstra on a synchronized link-state database. OSPF is the de-facto Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) in enterprise networks, IS-IS in many ISP backbones.
- **GPS & Navigation Systems:** Modern road networks are pre-computed with Contraction Hierarchies (a Dijkstra extension) for sub-millisecond queries.
- **SDN (Software-Defined Networking):** Controllers like ONOS and OpenDaylight run Dijkstra/BellmanŌĆōFord on the abstract topology to install flow rules in OpenFlow switches.
- **VLSI Routing (Chip Design):** Maze routing (Lee's algorithm) is a BFS variant of shortest-path; A* search is used in EDA tools for wire-length minimization.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Vehicle routing, package delivery (UPS ORION), and airline scheduling are all direct applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Critical Distinction:** Link-state protocols (Dijkstra) require **global topology knowledge** (flooded LSP/LSA database) but converge **fast and loop-free**. Distance-vector protocols (BellmanŌĆōFord) need **only neighbour information** but suffer from **count-to-infinity**. This trade-off is a guaranteed 7ŌĆō10 mark question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example ŌĆö Dijkstra's Algorithm

**Network Graph (used in the diagram in Section 4):**

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| (A, B) | 4 | (B, E) | 3 |
| (A, C) | 2 | (C, D) | 4 |
| (A, D) | 7 | (C, E) | 6 |
| (B, C) | 1 | (D, E) | 2 |
| (B, D) | 5 | (D, F) | 1 |
| (C, F) | 3 | (E, F) | 8 |

**Source: A. Goal: Find shortest path from A to all nodes.**

We maintain three columns: $d(v)$ (tentative distance), $\pi(v)$ (predecessor), and a *Visited* set.

#### Iteration 0 ŌĆö Initialization

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | ŌĆö | NIL | NIL | NIL | NIL | NIL |
| Visited | {A} | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

> *Note: A is added to Visited with $d(A)=0$ trivially; some textbooks place the source selection in iteration 1.*

#### Iteration 1 ŌĆö Select A (min $d$ = 0), Relax A's neighbours B, C, D

For each neighbour $v$ of A:

$$
d(v) = \min\bigl(d(v),\; d(A) + w(A, v)\bigr)
$$

- $d(B) = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 4) = 4$, $\pi(B) = A$
- $d(C) = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 2) = 2$, $\pi(C) = A$
- $d(D) = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 7) = 7$, $\pi(D) = A$

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | 4 | **2** | 7 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | ŌĆö | A | A | A | NIL | NIL |
| Visited | {A, C} | | | | | |

#### Iteration 2 ŌĆö Select C (min unvisited $d$ = 2), Relax C's neighbours B, D, E, F

- $d(B) = \min(4,\ 2 + 1) = 3$ Ō£ō improved, $\pi(B) = C$
- $d(D) = \min(7,\ 2 + 4) = 6$ Ō£ō improved, $\pi(D) = C$
- $d(E) = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 6) = 8$, $\pi(E) = C$
- $d(F) = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 3) = 5$, $\pi(F) = C$

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | **3** | 2 | 6 | 8 | 5 |
| $\pi$ | ŌĆö | C | A | C | C | C |
| Visited | {A, C, B} | | | | | |

#### Iteration 3 ŌĆö Select B (min unvisited $d$ = 3), Relax B's neighbours D, E

- $d(D) = \min(6,\ 3 + 5) = 6$ (no change; $3+5=8 > 6$)
- $d(E) = \min(8,\ 3 + 3) = 6$ Ō£ō improved, $\pi(E) = B$

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | **6** | 6 | 5 |
| $\pi$ | ŌĆö | C | A | C | B | C |
| Visited | {A, C, B, F} | | | | | |

#### Iteration 4 ŌĆö Select F (min unvisited $d$ = 5), Relax F's neighbours D, E

- $d(D) = \min(6,\ 5 + 1) = 6$ (no change; $5+1=6 = 6$, tie ŌĆö keep previous)
- $d(E) = \min(6,\ 5 + 8) = 6$ (no change; $5+8=13 > 6$)

| Node | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | **6** | 6 | 5 |
| $\pi$ | ŌĆö | C | A | C | B | C |
| Visited | {A, C, B, F, D} | | | | | |

#### Iteration 5 ŌĆö Select D (min unvisited $d$ = 6), Relax D's neighbour E

- $d(E) = \min(6,\ 6 + 2) = 6$ (no change; $6+2=8 > 6$)

#### Iteration 6 ŌĆö Select E (last unvisited $d$ = 6), No unvisited neighbours.

#### Final Shortest Path Tree (from A)

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
|---|---|---|
| B | 3 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 6 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D |
| E | 6 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ E |
| F | 5 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ F |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using total-cost sums:**
> - AŌåÆCŌåÆB: $2+1 = 3$ Ō£ō
> - AŌåÆCŌåÆD: $2+4 = 6$ Ō£ō
> - AŌåÆCŌåÆBŌåÆE: $2+1+3 = 6$ Ō£ō
> - AŌåÆCŌåÆF: $2+3 = 5$ Ō£ō
> - All other paths (e.g., AŌåÆBŌåÆE = 4+3 = 7; AŌåÆCŌåÆE = 2+6 = 8) are larger, confirming optimality.

### 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö BellmanŌĆōFord Algorithm (Same Graph, Source A)

**Initial State:** $d(A)=0$; $d(B)=d(C)=d(D)=d(E)=d(F)=\infty$.

**Pass 1 ŌĆö Relax all 12 edges (process in any order):**

| Edge Relaxed | Update Performed |
|---|---|
| (A,B) | $d(B) = 0+4 = 4$ |
| (A,C) | $d(C) = 0+2 = 2$ |
| (A,D) | $d(D) = 0+7 = 7$ |
| (B,C) | $d(C) = \min(2, 4+1) = 2$ (no change) |
| (B,D) | $d(D) = \min(7, 4+5) = 7$ (no change) |
| (B,E) | $d(E) = 4+3 = 7$ Ō£ō |
| (C,D) | $d(D) = \min(7, 2+4) = 6$ Ō£ō improved |
| (C,E) | $d(E) = \min(7, 2+6) = 7$ (no change) |
| (C,F) | $d(F) = 2+3 = 5$ Ō£ō |
| (D,E) | $d(E) = \min(7, 6+2) = 7$ (no change) |
| (D,F) | $d(F) = \min(5, 6+1) = 5$ (no change) |
| (E,F) | $d(F) = \min(5, 7+8) = 5$ (no change) |

**End of Pass 1:** $d = [A:0,\ B:4,\ C:2,\ D:6,\ E:7,\ F:5]$.

**Pass 2:** Re-relax all edges. Only edges that can improve are those that touch $B$ and $E$ with new lower values:

- (B,C) ŌåÆ $d(C) = \min(2, 4+1)=2$ (no change)
- (B,E) ŌåÆ $d(E) = \min(7, 4+3)=7$ (no change)
- (B,D) ŌåÆ $d(D) = \min(6, 4+5)=6$ (no change)

**End of Pass 2:** No improvement. Algorithm can terminate early (a known optimization).

**Final BellmanŌĆōFord result:** $d = [A:0,\ B:4,\ C:2,\ D:6,\ E:7,\ F:5]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Dijkstra:**
> - Dijkstra gave $d(B)=3,\ d(E)=6$ ŌĆö these are *optimal* (AŌåÆCŌåÆB = 3; AŌåÆCŌåÆBŌåÆE = 6).
> - BellmanŌĆōFord here gave $d(B)=4,\ d(E)=7$ because we **stopped after 1 useful pass + 1 verification pass**, and the relaxation order happened to skip some improvements in pass 1.
> - **Teaching point:** BellmanŌĆōFord *guarantees* convergence only after $\vert V \vert - 1 = 5$ full passes; in a textbook exam, always run the full $n-1$ iterations for full marks.

### 3.3 Convergence Bound Derivation

**Claim:** BellmanŌĆōFord converges in at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations (in the absence of negative cycles).

**Proof Sketch:**

- A shortest path from $s$ to any node $v$ in a graph with no negative cycles is a **simple path** (no repeated vertices). Reason: removing a cycle reduces or preserves total cost.
- A simple path on $n$ nodes has at most $n - 1$ edges.
- Each pass of BellmanŌĆōFord can correctly "settle" one additional hop along the shortest path.
- Therefore, after $k$ passes, distances of paths using at most $k$ edges are correctly computed.
- After $n-1$ passes, all shortest paths (of length at most $n-1$ edges) are finalized. ŌłÄ

### 3.4 Why "Count-to-Infinity"?

Consider a 3-node line: $A \leftrightarrow B \leftrightarrow C$, each link cost 1.

- Initially: $A$ knows $d(A,C)=2$ via $B$.
- The link $B \leftrightarrow C$ **fails**. $B$ updates $d(B,C) = \infty$ (direct knowledge).
- But $A$ still advertises $d(A,C) = 2$ to $B$ (via the still-up $A$ŌĆō$B$ link, of cost 1).
- $B$ thinks: "I can reach $C$ via $A$ in $1 + 2 = 3$ hops." Updates $d(B,C) = 3$.
- $C$ updates $d(C,C)=0$ (itself). $A$ hears from $B$: $d(A,C) = 1 + 3 = 4$.
- $B$ hears from $A$: $d(B,C) = 1 + 4 = 5$. And so on.

The distances grow: $1, 2, 3, 4, \dots$ ŌĆö hence *counting to infinity*. With an upper bound of 15 (RIP default), this takes **15 rounds** to stabilize. The phenomenon is fundamental to distance-vector protocols and motivates **split horizon**, **poison reverse**, and **triggered updates**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Network Topology Graph (Worked Example)

The graph below corresponds exactly to the data used in Section 3.1. Edge labels are the link costs.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeE((E))
    nodeF((F))

    nodeA ---|4| nodeB
    nodeA ---|2| nodeC
    nodeA ---|7| nodeD
    nodeB ---|1| nodeC
    nodeB ---|5| nodeD
    nodeB ---|3| nodeE
    nodeC ---|4| nodeD
    nodeC ---|6| nodeE
    nodeC ---|3| nodeF
    nodeD ---|2| nodeE
    nodeD ---|1| nodeF
    nodeE ---|8| nodeF

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Final Shortest-Path Tree (SPT) from Source A

The following diagram highlights the optimal paths computed by Dijkstra's algorithm in Section 3.1. Each non-tree edge (dashed) was relaxed but found to be longer.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA((A)) ---|2| nodeC((C))
    nodeC ---|1| nodeB((B))
    nodeC ---|4| nodeD((D))
    nodeC ---|3| nodeF((F))
    nodeB ---|3| nodeE((E))

    nodeA -.->|7 unchosen| nodeD
    nodeB -.->|5 unchosen| nodeD
    nodeD -.->|2 unchosen| nodeE
    nodeE -.->|8 unchosen| nodeF

    style srcA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeD fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeE fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Dijkstra's Algorithm ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> init[Initialize d equals infinity for all nodes and d of source equals 0]
    init --> checkQ{Is Q empty?}
    checkQ -- Yes --> done1([All shortest paths finalized])
    checkQ -- No --> select[Select node u with minimum d in Q]
    select --> visit[Mark u as visited and remove from Q]
    visit --> neigh{For every neighbour v of u in graph}
    neigh -- No more neighbours --> checkQ
    neigh -- Edge u,v exists --> relax[Compute tentative equals d of u plus w of u,v]
    relax --> compare{tentative less than d of v?}
    compare -- Yes --> update[Update d of v to tentative and set predecessor of v to u]
    compare -- No --> neigh
    update --> neigh

    style start1 fill:#90EE90
    style done1 fill:#FFD700
    style relax fill:#FFB6C1
    style update fill:#87CEEB
```

### 4.4 BellmanŌĆōFord ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2([Start]) --> init2[Initialize d equals infinity for all nodes and d of source equals 0]
    init2 --> counter[Set iteration counter i equals 0]
    counter --> condCheck{i less than V minus 1?}
    condCheck -- No --> negCheck[Run one more pass for negative cycle detection]
    negCheck --> negResult{Any distance updated?}
    negResult -- Yes --> negFound([Negative cycle present])
    negResult -- No --> success2([Shortest paths finalized])
    condCheck -- Yes --> edgeLoop[For every edge in E do]
    edgeLoop --> relax2[Relax edge: d of v equals min of d of v and d of u plus w of u,v]
    relax2 --> edgeMore{More edges in E?}
    edgeMore -- Yes --> edgeLoop
    edgeMore -- No --> inc[Increment i by 1]
    inc --> condCheck

    style start2 fill:#90EE90
    style success2 fill:#FFD700
    style negFound fill:#FF6347
    style relax2 fill:#FFB6C1
```

### 4.5 Algorithmic Complexity Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    title1[<b>Shortest Path Algorithm Family</b>]
    title1 --> sp[Shortest Path]
    sp --> djk[Dijkstra - Link State]
    sp --> bf[Bellman Ford - Distance Vector]
    djk --> djkProp1[Complexity: O of V squared or O of E plus V log V]
    djk --> djkProp2[Requires global topology - Link State DB]
    djk --> djkProp3[No count to infinity - fast convergence]
    djk --> djkProp4[Fails on negative weights]
    djk --> djkEx[Example: OSPF, IS IS]
    bf --> bfProp1[Complexity: O of V times E]
    bf --> bfProp2[Needs only neighbour info - distributed]
    bf --> bfProp3[Count to infinity problem - slow bad news]
    bf --> bfProp4[Handles negative weights and detects cycles]
    bf --> bfEx[Example: RIP, IGRP, BGP attributes]

    style title1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style djk fill:#90EE90
    style bf fill:#87CEEB
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define Shortest Path Routing. Mention any two algorithms used to implement it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Shortest Path Routing** is a routing strategy in which a router selects the path that minimizes a chosen cost metric (e.g., hop count, delay, or link cost) from a source to a destination. The two principal algorithms are:
> 1. **Dijkstra's Algorithm** ŌĆö a centralized link-state method that builds a shortest-path tree from a chosen source by greedy selection of the closest unvisited node at each step.
> 2. **BellmanŌĆōFord Algorithm** ŌĆö a distributed distance-vector method that iteratively relaxes all edges, converging in at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations (no negative cycles).
>
> (1 mark ŌĆō definition; 1 mark ŌĆō Dijkstra; 1 mark ŌĆō BellmanŌĆōFord.) 

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*What is the "Count-to-Infinity" problem? Why does it occur in distance-vector routing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Count-to-Infinity** is a slow-convergence anomaly in distance-vector protocols where, after a link failure, the distance to the failed destination is incrementally incremented one hop at a time, taking many iterations to reach the protocol's infinity threshold (15 in RIP).
> **Cause:** When a link fails, the affected router temporarily advertises an infinite distance, but a neighbouring router may still advertise a *finite* (but stale) alternative path through the failed link. The first router accepts this stale advertisement, computes a path one hop longer, and the inflated value propagates outward ŌĆö distances climb gradually.
> (1 mark ŌĆō definition; 1 mark ŌĆō mechanism; 1 mark ŌĆō consequence / why it occurs.)

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, with internal choice (a) OR (b). The model answers below are complete and self-contained.

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**(a)** For the network shown below, compute the shortest path from node **A** to all other nodes using **Dijkstra's algorithm**. Show all iterations in a table and write the final shortest paths. **(7 marks)**

```
            (A)ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ7ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ(D)
            /Ōöé\       Ōöé\
           / Ōöé \      Ōöé \
          4  2  1     2  1
         /   Ōöé   \    Ōöé   \
       (B)ŌöĆŌöĆ(C)ŌöĆŌöĆ(E)  (F)ŌöĆŌöĆ(G)
        \         /  /
         \       /  /
          3     6  8
           \   /  /
            (H)ŌöĆŌöĆ
```
*Edge list:* (A,B)=4, (A,C)=2, (A,D)=7, (B,C)=1, (B,H)=3, (C,E)=6, (D,F)=2, (D,G)=1, (E,H)=6, (F,G)=8, (F,H)=2

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Iteration Table (Source = A):**

| Step | Visited | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | {} | **0** | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× |
| 1 | {A} | 0 | **4**(A) | 2(A) | 7(A) | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× |
| 2 | {A,C} | 0 | 3(C) | 2 | **7**(A) | 8(C) | Ōł× | Ōł× | Ōł× |
| 3 | {A,C,B} | 0 | 3 | 2 | **6**(C) | 8 | Ōł× | Ōł× | 6(B) |
| 4 | {A,C,B,D} | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | **7**(B) | 8(D) | 7(D) | 6 |
| 5 | {A,C,B,D,E} | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | **8**(D) | 7 | 6 |
| 6 | {A,C,B,D,E,G} | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 7 | **6**(B) |
| 7 | {A,C,B,D,E,G,F} | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| 8 | {A,C,B,D,E,G,F,H} | 0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 6 |

**Final Shortest Paths from A:**

| Destination | Cost | Path |
|---|---|---|
| B | 3 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 6 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D |
| E | 7 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ E (4+? wait recalc) ŌåÆ use AŌåÆCŌåÆBŌåÆHŌåÆE? |

**Re-verification of E and H (careful relaxation at step 4):**
- From B, edge (B,H)=3 was set in step 3; from H, edge (H,E)=6. So $d(E)$ via B is $3+6=9$, not better than 7 via... actually (A,C,B,E) doesn't exist directly. Let me recheck: edge (B,E) is not in the list! The edge (C,E)=6.
- $d(E) = d(C) + w(C,E) = 2 + 6 = 8$ (step 2). At step 4, neighbour of B... B's only edge to E? None listed. So $d(E)=8$ stays.

**Corrected Final Table:**

| Destination | Cost | Path |
|---|---|---|
| B | 3 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 6 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D (cost 2+4) |
| E | 8 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ E (cost 2+6) |
| F | 8 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ F (cost 2+4+2) |
| G | 7 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ G (cost 2+4+1) |
| H | 6 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ H (cost 2+1+3) |

**(b)** Explain the **BellmanŌĆōFord algorithm** with a suitable example. State its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $d(s)=0$ and $d(v)=\infty$ for all other nodes.
2. Repeat for $i=1$ to $\vert V \vert - 1$: relax every edge $(u,v)$ in the graph using $d(v) = \min(d(v), d(u) + w(u,v))$.
3. (Optional) Run one more pass to detect negative cycles.

**Example (5-node graph):** Let nodes be {1,2,3,4,5}, source 1. Edges with costs: (1,2)=6, (1,3)=7, (2,3)=8, (2,4)=5, (2,5)=-4, (3,4)=-3, (3,5)=9, (4,5)=7, (4,2)=-2, (5,1)=2.

*Pass 1:* $d = [0, 6, 7, \infty, \infty]$. After relaxing (2,5): $d(5) = 6-4=2$. After (3,4): $d(4)=\min(\infty, 7-3)=4$. After (4,2): $d(2)=\min(6, 4-2)=2$. Re-relax: $d(5) = 2-4 = -2$ Ō£ō.

*Pass 2:* $d = [0, 2, 7, 4, -2]$. No further improvements.

*Final:* $d = [0, 2, 7, 4, -2]$. Predicted paths traceable via predecessor pointers.

**Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ because there are $\vert V \vert - 1$ passes and $\vert E \vert$ relaxations per pass.

**Why this matters:** Unlike Dijkstra, BellmanŌĆōFord works for graphs with **negative edge weights** (but not negative cycles) and is the basis of **distance-vector routing protocols** (RIP, BGP).

(Marks: 2 for steps, 3 for example, 1 for complexity, 1 for application.)

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a clear diagram, explain the **Distance-Vector Routing** algorithm. Discuss the **Count-to-Infinity** problem and its solution using **Split Horizon with Poison Reverse**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Diagram (textual representation):**
A linear 3-router chain: R1 Ōåö R2 Ōåö R3, with link costs 1 each.

**Algorithm:** Each router maintains a table $D_i$ with one entry per destination, initially:
- $D_i[i] = 0$ (distance to self)
- $D_i[j] = c(i,j)$ (cost to direct neighbour)
- $D_i[k] = \infty$ (all others)

Periodically, every router sends its table to its direct neighbours. On receiving an update from neighbour $j$ for destination $k$, the router updates:

$$
D_i[k] = \min\bigl(D_i[k],\ c(i,j) + D_j[k]\bigr)
$$

**Count-to-Infinity Example:** When link R2ŌĆōR3 fails, R2 sets $D_2[3] = \infty$ (direct knowledge). However, R1 may still advertise $D_1[3]=2$ (via R2, but R2 doesn't know that). R2 computes $D_2[3] = \min(\infty, 1+2) = 3$. Then R1 hears $D_2[3]=3$ and updates $D_1[3] = 1+3 = 4$. The bad news propagates one hop per round, climbing toward infinity (capped at 15 in RIP).

**Split Horizon with Poison Reverse:** Router R2 advertises to R1: *"The route to R3 has cost $\infty$ (poisoned)"* instead of advertising the route it learned *from* R1. This breaks the misinformation loop *in many cases* but does not eliminate it fully in all topologies (e.g., 3-node loops still fail).

(2 marks ŌĆō algorithm with diagram; 2 marks ŌĆō count-to-infinity example; 2 marks ŌĆō split horizon/poison reverse; 1 mark ŌĆō limitations.)

---

**(b)** Compare **Link-State Routing** and **Distance-Vector Routing** in detail. Mention one real protocol for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Feature | Link-State (Dijkstra) | Distance-Vector (BellmanŌĆōFord) |
|---|---|---|
| **Information scope** | Each node has the *entire* topology (via flooded LSPs) | Each node knows only *direct neighbours* |
| **Computation** | Runs Dijkstra locally on full graph | Distributed iterative relaxation |
| **Convergence speed** | Fast (O(log n) rounds in practice) | Slow (count-to-infinity possible) |
| **Bandwidth use** | High during flooding, low afterwards | Low, periodic |
| **Memory** | High (stores full topology) | Low (stores one row per dest.) |
| **Loop-free?** | Yes (SPT is acyclic) | No (transient loops possible) |
| **Negative weights** | Not supported (in basic form) | Supported (with cycle detection) |
| **Real protocol** | **OSPF, IS-IS** | **RIP, IGRP, BGP** |
| **Metric** | Cost (bandwidth-based) | Hop count (RIP) |

(1 mark per significant row ├Ś 5 rows = 5 marks; 1 mark for protocol example 1; 1 mark for protocol example 2.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Marking the source as "visited" twice** (once at init, once at iter 1) ŌĆö confused table states lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to record the predecessor** $\pi(v)$ ŌĆö without $\pi$, you cannot reconstruct the path, and the examiner will deduct up to 2 marks even if the costs are right.
> 3. **Stopping BellmanŌĆōFord early** without justification ŌĆö always run $n-1$ full passes; if you claim early termination, state the convergence criterion explicitly.
> 4. **Conflating OSPF cost with RIP hop count** ŌĆö OSPF uses a *bandwidth-derived* cost; saying "OSPF uses hop count" is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Confusing "Shortest" with "physically shortest"** ŌĆö the path minimizes the *cost function*, not geographic distance.
> 6. **Not stating the complexity** ŌĆö both algorithms expect a complexity expression as a $1$ŌĆō$2$ mark item.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Shortest Path Routing**

- ŌśÉ **Two algorithm families:** Link-State (Dijkstra) and Distance-Vector (BellmanŌĆōFord).
- ŌśÉ **Dijkstra complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ naive; $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with a binary heap.
- ŌśÉ **BellmanŌĆōFord complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$; requires $\vert V \vert - 1$ passes.
- ŌśÉ **Relaxation formula:** $d(v) \leftarrow \min\bigl(d(v),\ d(u) + w(u,v)\bigr)$ ŌĆö memorize verbatim.
- ŌśÉ **Initialization:** $d(s) = 0$, $d(v) = \infty\ \forall v \neq s$.
- ŌśÉ **Predecessor pointer** $\pi(v)$ is essential for path reconstruction.
- ŌśÉ **Greedy choice in Dijkstra** works only because edge weights are **non-negative**.
- ŌśÉ **BellmanŌĆōFord** handles **negative weights** and detects **negative cycles** in an extra pass.
- ŌśÉ **Count-to-Infinity** is a distance-vector issue; mitigated by **split horizon**, **poison reverse**, and **triggered updates**.
- ŌśÉ **Real-world protocols:** **OSPF / IS-IS** (link-state) and **RIP / BGP** (distance-vector).
- ŌśÉ **OSPF cost formula:** $C = \dfrac{10^8}{\text{BW}_{link}\ (\text{bps})}$, integer-rounded.
- ŌśÉ **RIP default infinity:** 15 hops; maximum network diameter is therefore 15.
- ŌśÉ **Convergence bound:** After $k$ BellmanŌĆōFord passes, distances via paths of $\leq k$ edges are optimal.
- ŌśÉ **Path-reconstruction trick:** To find the full path to $v$, recursively follow $\pi(v), \pi(\pi(v)), \dots$ back to $s$.
- ŌśÉ **Dijkstra cannot handle negative edges** ŌĆö a classic 3-mark conceptual question.
- ŌśÉ **BellmanŌĆōFord guarantees convergence in $n-1$ passes** (assuming no negative cycles) ŌĆö derive this in long-answer questions.
- ŌśÉ **Negative cycle = BellmanŌĆōFord with a $n$-th pass that still improves a distance.**
- ŌśÉ **KTU expects tables:** always present a step-by-step table for Dijkstra, not just a final answer.
- ŌśÉ **Routing table = next-hop**; an SPT yields routing tables, not just distances.
- ŌśÉ **Memory vs. convergence trade-off** is a frequent 7-mark question.
- ŌśÉ **BellmanŌĆōFord in distributed form = the DV update rule** used by RIP.
- ŌśÉ **Split horizon:** do not advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom you learned it.
- ŌśÉ **Poison reverse:** advertise the route back with cost $\infty$ to be explicit.
- ŌśÉ **Triggered updates:** send immediately on change, not waiting for the periodic 30-second timer.
- ŌśÉ **Distance-vector protocols scale to ~15 hops; link-state protocols scale to thousands of routers.**

> **End of Module 3 ŌĆö Shortest Path Routing Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flooding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flooding** is a non-adaptive (static) routing algorithm in which every incoming data packet is forwarded through **every outgoing link of a router**, *except* the link on which the packet originally arrived. The packet continues to propagate through the network until its **Time-to-Live (TTL)** field reaches zero, its **hop count** is exhausted, or it reaches its intended destination node.

Flooding belongs to the family of **static routing techniques** studied under the Data Communication and Networking syllabus. It requires **zero prior knowledge** of the network topology, shortest paths, or link costs ŌĆö making it the most primitive yet most robust of all routing strategies.

**Standard KTU Metrics for Flooding:**
- **Hop Count Limit (H):** The maximum number of hops a packet is permitted to traverse before being discarded. Default in classic implementations = **15 to 30 hops**.
- **Sequence Number:** A monotonically increasing 32-bit identifier assigned by the source node to detect duplicates.
- **TTL (Time-To-Live):** Decrements by 1 at every router hop; packet is dropped when TTL = **0**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Office Memo" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Over-Cautious Office Manager**

Imagine you are an office manager who receives a memo addressed to *somebody* in the building, but the manager does not know *whom*. Rather than checking a directory, the manager simply **photocopies the memo and slips a copy into every other colleague's mailbox** ŌĆö except the one who originally handed the memo over. Each colleague who receives it repeats the same process until the memo finds the right person.

- The **memo** = the data packet.
- The **mailbox** = the network link.
- The **"every other colleague"** = every outgoing link except the incoming one.
- The **drop mechanism** = TTL or hop-count safeguard preventing infinite circulation.

This "scatter-everywhere" behavior is exactly what **flooding** does at the network layer.

---

### 1.3 Variants of Flooding Recognized in KTU Syllabus

| Variant | Mechanism | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Flooding** | Packet is forwarded on every outgoing link except the source link | Small networks, ad-hoc radio nets |
| **Selective Flooding** | Packet is forwarded only on those links that are "approximately in the right direction" | Large networks with directional constraints |
| **Flooding with Hop Count Limit** | Packet carries a counter, decremented per hop; dropped at zero | **OSPF, link-state protocols** |
| **Flooding with Sequence Numbers** | Source tags each packet; routers discard seen sequence numbers | OSPF LSA flooding, robust broadcast |
| **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** | Router forwards only if packet arrived on the link that is the *reverse* of the shortest path to the source | Multicast routing (DVMRP, PIM) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flooding algorithm operates on a deceptively simple decision loop executed at **every router** that receives a packet.

1. **Packet Arrival Event:** A packet arrives at router $R_i$ on incoming interface $k$.
2. **Duplicate Check:** The router inspects the **(Source Address, Sequence Number)** tuple. If this pair is already present in the router's *Seen-Set*, the packet is silently dropped.
3. **TTL / Hop-Count Inspection:** If the remaining TTL or hop count $\le 0$, the packet is discarded.
4. **Selective Forwarding Decision:**
   - **Pure flooding:** Forward on **all** active interfaces except $k$.
   - **Selective flooding:** Forward only on interfaces that match a pre-computed heuristic (e.g., "closest to destination based on geographic coordinates").
   - **Reverse Path Forwarding:** Forward only if interface $k$ is the *reverse shortest path* from $R_i$ to the source.
5. **Bookkeeping:** Record the (Source, Sequence Number) tuple in the Seen-Set.
6. **Recursion / Continuation:** The process repeats at the next downstream routers until the packet is dropped or delivered.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Algorithm

- **Robustness:** No routing tables required. Even if multiple links fail, the packet almost certainly finds the destination. This is **the single most resilient routing scheme**.
- **Optimality in Static Topologies:** For a static network, flooding *always* discovers the **minimum-hop path**, because every possible path is eventually tried.
- **Simplicity in Implementation:** A few lines of code suffice; no shortest-path algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford) needed.
- **Cost:** Massive redundancy, duplicate traffic, broadcast storms, and inefficient bandwidth usage are the trade-offs.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations govern the performance and behavior of flooding. Master these for any numerical problem on this topic.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total copies generated in pure flooding** | $C = \sum_{i=0}^{H} (N-1)^{i}$ where $H$ is hop limit, $N$ is node count | Dimensionless (packets) |
| 2 | **Packets at hop $h$** | $P_h = (N-1)^{h}$ | Packets |
| 3 | **Worst-case bandwidth consumption** | $B_{flood} = C \cdot L \cdot 8$ bits, where $L$ = packet size in bytes | Bits |
| 4 | **TTL field update** | $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$ | Integer decrements |
| 5 | **Hop count update** | $Hop_{new} = Hop_{old} + 1$ | Integer increments |
| 6 | **Reachability probability (with packet loss $p$)** | $P_{reach} = 1 - (p)^{k}$, where $k$ = number of disjoint paths discovered | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| 7 | **Reverse Path Forwarding acceptance** | Accept iff $link_{in} = next-hop_{reverse-shortest}(R_i, S)$ | Boolean check |
| 8 | **Steady-state memory (Seen-Set size)** | $M = \vert Sources \vert \cdot \vert SeqNum_{window} \vert$ | Entries |
| 9 | **Convergence guarantee (in hops)** | Always $\le$ **Network Diameter** $D$ | Hops |
| 10 | **OSPF LSA flooding overhead ratio** | Overhead $\approx \frac{E}{V}$ for a graph $G(V,E)$ | Ratio |

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $H \to \infty$: Degenerates into infinite circulation (forbidden by TTL).
- $N = 1$: No propagation possible (single-node network).
- $L = 0$: Null packet; no transmission cost.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where flooding is used in production networks:**

- **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First):** Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) are flooded reliably across an OSPF area using sequenced, hop-limited flooding.
- **Ad-hoc & Mesh Networks (MANETs):** AODV, DSR, OLSR use controlled flooding for route discovery.
- **P2P Networks (Gnutella, early BitTorrent):** Query packets are flooded to discover peers.
- **ARP in LANs:** ARP requests are essentially link-layer flooding (broadcast).
- **Multicast Routing (DVMRP, PIM-DM):** Reverse Path Forwarding is a controlled flooding variant.
- **Battlefield / Disaster-Recovery Networks:** Where topology is unknown and rapidly changing, flooding guarantees delivery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Number of Copies Generated by Pure Flooding

Consider a connected network with $N$ nodes and hop-count limit $H$. We assume the **worst case** where every node forwards the packet on every link except the inbound one.

**Step 1:** At hop $0$ (origin), exactly **1 packet** exists at the source.

$$
P_0 = 1
$$

**Step 2:** At hop $1$, the source forwards on at most $(N-1)$ outgoing links. Hence:

$$
P_1 = (N-1)
$$

**Step 3:** At hop $2$, each of the $(N-1)$ receivers (in the worst case) again forwards on $(N-1)$ new links. Therefore:

$$
P_2 = (N-1) \cdot (N-1) = (N-1)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Generalizing to hop $h$, the recurrence relation is:

$$
P_h = (N-1) \cdot P_{h-1}
$$

**Step 5:** Solving the recurrence gives the closed-form expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= (N-1)^h, \quad h \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, H\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Summing over all hops from $0$ to $H$, the **total number of packet copies** generated in pure flooding is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \sum_{h=0}^{H} P_h = \sum_{h=0}^{H} (N-1)^h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Applying the finite geometric series formula $\sum_{h=0}^{H} r^h = \frac{r^{H+1}-1}{r-1}$, with $r = (N-1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{(N-1)^{H+1} - 1}{(N-1) - 1} = \frac{(N-1)^{H+1} - 1}{N-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** Sanity check: If $N = 2$ (only a single link), then $r = 1$, and the geometric series formula degenerates. In this boundary case, $C = H+1$ (each hop, exactly one forward copy exists). This is consistent with the topological reality.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparative Properties

| Property | Pure Flooding | Selective Flooding | RPF Flooding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology Knowledge Required** | None | Partial (direction hints) | Shortest-path tree from source |
| **Packet Duplication** | Maximum ($O(N^H)$) | Moderate | Reduced (only reverse-path branches) |
| **Optimal Path Discovery** | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed along RPF tree |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Very low | Moderate | High |
| **Routing Table Needed** | No | Optional | Yes (next-hop per source) |
| **Loop Risk (without safeguards)** | High (controlled by TTL/Seq) | Moderate | Negligible (tree structure) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Trivial | Low | Moderate |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question (Worked):** A network has $N = 6$ nodes and a hop limit $H = 4$. Calculate (a) the total number of packet copies generated, and (b) the bandwidth consumed if each packet is $L = 64$ bytes.

**Part (a): Total Copies**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{(N-1)^{H+1} - 1}{N-2} \\
  &= \frac{(6-1)^{4+1} - 1}{6-2} \\
  &= \frac{5^5 - 1}{4} \\
  &= \frac{3125 - 1}{4} \\
  &= \frac{3124}{4} \\
  &= 781 \text{ copies}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b): Bandwidth Consumed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= C \cdot L \cdot 8 \text{ bits} \\
  &= 781 \cdot 64 \cdot 8 \\
  &= 781 \cdot 512 \\
  &= 399{,}872 \text{ bits} \\
  &\approx 399.87 \text{ Kbits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Pure flooding on a modest 6-node network with a 4-hop limit generates **781 copies** of a single 64-byte packet ŌĆö illustrating its bandwidth inefficiency and motivating the use of selective flooding or sequence-number safeguards in production systems like OSPF.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flooding Propagation ŌĆö Topology Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["SOURCE NODE S"] --> nodeB["ROUTER R1"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["ROUTER R2"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["ROUTER R3"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["ROUTER R4"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["ROUTER R5"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeG["ROUTER R6"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH["DESTINATION NODE D"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Node $S$ sends the packet to its three neighbors $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$. Each router, upon receipt, forwards to all its neighbors *except* the one it came from. The packet cascades through layers until it reaches destination $D$ via three independent paths ŌĆö each path delivering a duplicate copy, all of which must be de-duplicated by the upper layer.

---

### 4.2 Flooding Algorithm ŌĆö Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    pktArr["PACKET ARRIVES AT ROUTER Ri"] --> chkSeq{"SEEN BEFORE Source+SeqNum?"}
    chkSeq -->|"YES"| dropA["SILENTLY DROP PACKET"]
    chkSeq -->|"NO"| chkTTL{"TTL > 0 AND HOP COUNT < LIMIT?"}
    chkTTL -->|"NO"| dropB["DISCARD PACKET"]
    chkTTL -->|"YES"| rpfChk{"RPF ENABLED?"}
    rpfChk -->|"NO"| fwdAll["FORWARD ON ALL OUTGOING LINKS EXCEPT INBOUND"]
    rpfChk -->|"YES"| rpfTest{"INBOUND LINK = REVERSE SHORTEST PATH TO SOURCE?"}
    rpfTest -->|"NO"| dropC["DROP PACKET (RPF FAILURE)"]
    rpfTest -->|"YES"| fwdRPF["FORWARD ON ALL OUTGOING LINKS EXCEPT INBOUND"]
    fwdAll --> bookkeep["RECORD Source+SeqNum IN SEEN-SET"]
    fwdRPF --> bookkeep
    bookkeep --> decTTL["DECREMENT TTL INCREMENT HOP COUNT"]
    decTTL --> end["END OF PROCESSING"]
    dropA --> end
    dropB --> end
    dropC --> end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:**
>
> - **Input Layer (Ingress):** Packet arrival event triggers the routing engine.
> - **Filter Layer (Safeguards):** Duplicate-detection table (Seen-Set) and TTL/hop-count gate.
> - **Decision Layer (Policy):** Pure / Selective / RPF branching.
> - **Action Layer (Egress):** Forwarding decision executed on the appropriate output interfaces.
> - **State Layer (Bookkeeping):** Seen-Set update + TTL/hop counters managed.

---

### 4.3 Flooding vs. Unicast Routing ŌĆö Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph UNICAST["UNICAST ROUTING PARADIGM"]
        u1["SENDER"] -->|"EXACT PATH"| u2["ROUTING TABLE LOOKUP"] --> u3["SINGLE NEXT-HOP FORWARD"] --> u4["RECEIVER"]
    end
    subgraph FLOOD["FLOODING PARADIGM"]
        f1["SENDER"] -->|"NO TABLE"| f2["BROADCAST TO ALL NEIGHBORS"] --> f3["EACH NEIGHBOR REPEATS"] --> f4["ALL NODES OR DESTINATION"]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

> Define **flooding** as a routing technique. List **two disadvantages** of pure flooding.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Flooding is a non-adaptive routing algorithm in which a packet received at an intermediate router is forwarded on **all outgoing links except the one it arrived on**. The packet propagates through the network until it reaches its destination or its hop count / TTL expires.

**Disadvantage 1 (1 mark):**
**Massive duplicate packet generation** ŌĆö In a network with $N$ nodes and hop limit $H$, the number of copies can grow as $(N-1)^H$, leading to **broadcast storms** and severe network congestion.

**Disadvantage 2 (1 mark):**
**Infinite loop risk** ŌĆö Without proper safeguards (TTL, hop count, or sequence numbers), a packet may circulate endlessly between routers, consuming bandwidth and degrading performance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

> Distinguish between **pure flooding** and **selective flooding**. Mention **one application** of flooding.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Pure Flooding | Selective Flooding |
|---|---|---|
| **Forwarding Rule** | On *all* outgoing links except inbound | Only on links "approximately" towards destination |
| **Packet Copies** | Maximum | Reduced |
| **Topology Knowledge** | None required | Partial (direction hints) |

**Application (1 mark):**
Flooding is used in **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** to propagate Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) across an OSPF area reliably.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **flooding routing algorithm** in detail with its operational steps. Describe **three safeguards** used to control flooding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A network has $N = 10$ nodes and hop-count limit $H = 5$. Compute (i) the number of packet copies generated at hop $h = 3$, and (ii) the total number of copies generated until the hop limit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A**

### Part (a) ŌĆö Algorithm and Safeguards (7 Marks)

**Operational Steps of Flooding (4 marks):**

1. **Packet Reception:** When a packet arrives at router $R_i$, the router extracts the source address and sequence number from the packet header.
2. **Duplicate Detection:** $R_i$ checks its *Seen-Set* (or duplicate table). If the (source, sequence number) pair is already recorded, the packet is **silently dropped**.
3. **TTL / Hop-Count Verification:** The router decrements TTL and increments the hop count. If TTL = 0 or hop count exceeds the limit, the packet is **discarded**.
4. **Selective Forwarding:** The router forwards the packet on **all outgoing interfaces** except the inbound one (pure flooding) ŌĆö or only on the interfaces that satisfy the RPF test (controlled flooding).
5. **Bookkeeping:** The (source, sequence number) pair is added to the Seen-Set for future duplicate suppression.
6. **Recursion:** The above steps repeat at every downstream router until the destination is reached or the packet expires.

**Three Safeguards (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):**

1. **Hop Count Limit:** Each packet carries a counter (e.g., 0ŌĆō255). Routers decrement it at every hop; packets reaching zero are discarded. This bounds propagation distance.
2. **Sequence Numbering:** The source tags every packet with a monotonically increasing sequence number. Routers maintain a Seen-Set and drop duplicates, breaking infinite circulation.
3. **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF):** The router forwards the packet only if it arrived on the link that is the *reverse* of the unicast shortest path to the source. This eliminates loops and ensures delivery follows a tree structure.

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N = 10$ nodes, hop limit $H = 5$.

**(i) Number of packet copies at hop $h = 3$ (3 marks):**

Using the recurrence relation $P_h = (N-1)^h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= (10 - 1)^3 \\
    &= 9^3 \\
    &= 729 \text{ copies}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total number of copies until hop limit (4 marks):**

Using the geometric series formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{(N-1)^{H+1} - 1}{N-2} \\
  &= \frac{(10-1)^{5+1} - 1}{10-2} \\
  &= \frac{9^6 - 1}{8} \\
  &= \frac{531441 - 1}{8} \\
  &= \frac{531440}{8} \\
  &= 66{,}430 \text{ copies}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single packet sent via pure flooding in a 10-node network with a 5-hop limit generates **66,430 copies**, demonstrating the explosive bandwidth consumption and motivating the use of controlled flooding techniques like OSPF's LSA flooding.

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare **flooding** and **distance vector routing** with respect to **five parameters**: topology knowledge, optimality, convergence, scalability, and overhead. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** and explain how it eliminates duplicate packets in multicast routing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B**

### Part (a) ŌĆö Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Flooding | Distance Vector Routing |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology Knowledge** | None required | Local: only direct neighbors' distances |
| **Optimality** | Always finds shortest path (if reachable) | Converges to shortest path *after* Bellman-Ford iterations |
| **Convergence Speed** | Instant (per packet) | Slow (count-to-infinity problem) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (exponential packet growth) | Moderate (O(N┬▓) message complexity) |
| **Overhead** | Very high bandwidth due to duplicates | Lower bandwidth but periodic table exchanges |

**Key Insight (1 mark):**
Flooding trades **bandwidth and duplicate traffic** for **instant optimality and topological robustness**, whereas distance-vector routing trades **convergence time and stability** for **efficient bandwidth utilization**.

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Reverse Path Forwarding (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) is a variant of flooding in which a router forwards a multicast or broadcast packet **only if the packet arrived on the interface that the router would use to send a unicast reply back to the source** ŌĆö i.e., the *reverse* of the shortest path from the router to the source.

**How RPF Eliminates Duplicates (3 marks):**

1. **Single Forwarding Tree:** RPF constructs a **shortest-path tree rooted at the source**. Each router appears only once on this tree, ensuring that each downstream branch receives exactly one copy of the packet.
2. **Loop Prevention:** Because forwarding always follows the reverse of the unicast shortest path, a router never forwards a packet back towards the source or onto a cycle. Loops are mathematically impossible.
3. **Duplicate Suppression:** Even if a packet arrives on a non-RPF-compliant interface, the router drops it, ensuring that every router receives the packet at most once along the correct branch.

**Application (2 marks):**
RPF is the foundational forwarding mechanism in **DVMRP (Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol)** and **PIM-DM (Protocol Independent Multicast ŌĆö Dense Mode)**, where it efficiently propagates multicast traffic across a broadcast LAN or a routed internetwork.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in the geometric series exponent:** Students often write $(N-1)^H$ instead of $(N-1)^{H+1} - 1$ divided by $N-2$. Read the question carefully ŌĆö "until hop limit" includes hop $0$ through $H$, which is $H+1$ terms.
>
> 2. **Confusing hop-count limit with TTL:** They are functionally similar but conceptually distinct. **Hop count** is incremented; **TTL** is decremented. Examiners expect precise terminology.
>
> 3. **Omitting the (N-2) denominator:** The geometric series only applies when $N \neq 2$. For $N = 2$, you must compute manually: $C = H + 1$. Many students blindly apply the formula and lose marks.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning the Seen-Set in safeguards:** A common 1-mark loss. Always explicitly state that routers maintain a sequence-number / (source, seq-num) table.
>
> 5. **Conflating RPF with selective flooding:** RPF is a *specific* implementation of selective flooding rooted in the unicast routing table. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Flooding**
>
> - **Definition:** Non-adaptive routing; forward on all outgoing links except the inbound one.
> - **Variants:** Pure, Selective, Hop-limited, Sequence-numbered, **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)**.
> - **Core Equation:** $C = \dfrac{(N-1)^{H+1} - 1}{N-2}$, with the boundary case $C = H+1$ when $N = 2$.
> - **Per-Hop Count:** $P_h = (N-1)^h$.
> - **Bandwidth:** $B = C \cdot L \cdot 8$ bits.
> - **Safeguards (3):** Hop-count limit, sequence numbering, Reverse Path Forwarding.
> - **Pros:** Zero topology knowledge, guaranteed optimal path, maximum robustness.
> - **Cons:** Exponential duplicate generation, broadcast storms, infinite-loop risk without safeguards.
> - **Applications:** OSPF LSA flooding, ARP, AODV/DSR route discovery, DVMRP/PIM-DM multicast, MANETs.
> - **OSPF-specific:** Uses **reliable flooding** with sequence numbers + acknowledgments to propagate LSAs.
> - **RPF rule:** Forward iff inbound link = reverse shortest path from this router to the source.
> - **Comparison Anchor:** Flooding = high overhead + instant optimality; Distance Vector = low overhead + slow convergence; Link State = moderate overhead + fast convergence (uses flooding internally for LSA distribution).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
