# ­¤ōØ Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of a Home Agent in Mobile IP.**

#### Ō£ģ Model Answer (3 Marks)
The **Home Agent (HA)** is a router on the MH's **home network** that performs three critical functions:
1. **Maintenance of mobility binding** ŌĆö keeps a current mapping between the MH's permanent **Home Address** and its temporary **Care-of Address (CoA)**.
2. **Proxy ARP** ŌĆö intercepts packets destined for HA-addr while the MH is away, so that the home link's hosts do not generate ARP requests for the absent MH.
3. **Tunneling** ŌĆö encapsulates the original IP datagram (intended for HA-addr) inside a new IP header with destination = CoA, and forwards it to the Foreign Agent (or directly to MH in co-located CoA mode).

> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark for each function (HA = Mobility Binding + Proxy ARP + Tunneling).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`
**List any four requirements of Quality of Service.**

#### Ō£ģ Model Answer (3 Marks)
The four canonical QoS requirements are:
1. **Bandwidth** ŌĆö minimum guaranteed bit-rate (bps).
2. **Delay / Latency** ŌĆö maximum end-to-end transit time (s).
3. **Jitter** ŌĆö maximum variation in inter-packet delay (s).
4. **Reliability** ŌĆö maximum acceptable packet-loss probability (%).

> **Marks Distribution:** 0.75 mark per requirement (definition + unit).

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **14-mark sub-question pair with internal choice** at the End-Semester Exam (ESE). You are given **either Option A or Option B**. Solve both sub-parts.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Option A ŌĆö 14 Marks

**Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the entities and operation of **Mobile IP** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **triangle routing problem** and how **route optimization** addresses it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **end-to-end** and **network-assisted** approaches to congestion control. Mention at least two example protocols/techniques for each.

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**1. Mobile IP Entities (2 marks):**
- **Mobile Host (MH):** The end system that changes its point of attachment. Identified by a permanent **Home Address**.
- **Home Agent (HA):** Router on the home link that maintains the **mobility binding** and tunnels packets.
- **Foreign Agent (FA):** Router on the visited link that de-tunnels packets and delivers them to the MH.
- **Correspondent Node (CN):** The peer communicating with the MH.

**2. Operation (3 marks):**
- **Movement Detection:** MH listens to **Agent Advertisements** (ICMP extensions) from FAs.
- **CoA Acquisition:** FA provides a **Foreign Agent CoA**; alternatively, MH obtains a **co-located CoA** via DHCP.
- **Registration:** MH ŌåÆ FA ŌåÆ HA sends a **Registration Request** (UDP port 434). HA authenticates, creates/updates binding, and replies with **Registration Reply** containing a lifetime.
- **Data Delivery:** CN ŌåÆ HA (HA-addr intercepted) ŌåÆ HA tunnels to FA ŌåÆ FA de-tunnels ŌåÆ MH. MH replies directly to CN (asymmetric path).

**3. Triangle Routing Problem (1 mark):**
Forward traffic **always** passes through HA even if CN and MH are geographically adjacent. Inefficiency ratio $\eta \geq 1$, with $\eta \approx 2$ typical. Causes **higher delay** and **wasted bandwidth** on the HAŌĆōFA segment.

**4. Route Optimization (1 mark):**
- **Mobile IPv6 Binding Update (BU):** MH sends BU directly to CN, binding HA-addr to CoA.
- CN maintains a **Binding Cache** and tunnels directly to CoA, bypassing HA.
- Implemented via **Return Routability (RR)** procedure for security.
- Reduces $\eta$ to $\approx 1$.

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

| Aspect | End-to-End (Host-Based) | Network-Assisted (Router-Based) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Congestion signal** | Inferred from **packet loss / RTT** | Explicit feedback from routers |
| **Examples** | TCP Tahoe, Reno, Cubic, BBR | **DECbit**, **ECN**, **ATM ABR** |
| **State at router** | None | Per-flow / aggregate state required |
| **Reaction speed** | Slow (after timeout) | Fast (sub-RTT) |
| **Scalability** | Highly scalable | Limited by router state |
| **Deployment** | Universal (TCP is everywhere) | Limited (ECN partial, ATM niche) |
| **Pros** | No infra change | Accurate, proactive |
| **Cons** | Oscillates, slow | Router upgrade cost |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern networks (e.g., Google's BBR) blend both: host-measured RTT and bottleneck-bandwidth estimation. ECN is the most successful network-assisted deployment in IPv4/IPv6.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Option B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative)

**Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **four QoS requirements** in detail. For each, give **one real-time** and **one non-real-time application** that demands it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **preventive (open-loop)** and **reactive (closed-loop)** congestion control. Give two examples of each.

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**1. Bandwidth (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** The minimum guaranteed throughput (in bps) that the network must provide.
- **Real-time app:** Video conferencing (Ōēł 1.5ŌĆō3 Mbps for HD).
- **Non-real-time app:** Software distribution / FTP (consumes max available).

**2. Delay (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** End-to-end transit time of a packet (transmission + propagation + queue + processing).
- **Real-time app:** VoIP ŌĆö budget < 150 ms one-way.
- **Non-real-time app:** Web browsing ŌĆö budget < 400 ms acceptable, no hard deadline.

**3. Jitter (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** Variation in inter-packet arrival times.
- **Real-time app:** Live video streaming ŌĆö must use **de-jitter buffer** at receiver.
- **Non-real-time app:** Email / file transfer ŌĆö jitter is irrelevant; packets are reassembled in order.

**4. Reliability (1.75 marks):**
- **Definition:** Probability that a packet reaches its destination (complement of loss probability).
- **Real-time app:** Voice ŌĆö can tolerate Ōēż 1% loss (concealed by codecs).
- **Non-real-time app:** Banking transaction ŌĆö **0% loss mandatory**; uses TCP retransmission.

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

| Aspect | Preventive (Open-Loop) | Reactive (Closed-Loop) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **When it acts** | **Before** congestion occurs | **After** congestion is detected |
| **Feedback loop** | None (one-shot) | Continuous monitoring |
| **Examples** | **Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Admission Control, RSVP** | **TCP AIMD, RED, ECN, Source Quench** |
| **Pros** | Predictable, smooth | Adapts to dynamic conditions |
| **Cons** | Inefficient if load is low | Oscillates, can be slow |
| **Used in** | ATM CBR, leased lines, premium IP | Best-effort IP (TCP), classic routers |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** Real networks use **hybrid schemes** ŌĆö Token Bucket (preventive) + TCP (reactive) is the dominant combination in DiffServ networks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤öź Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing HA-addr with CoA** ŌĆö students write *"MH has a temporary home address"*. The Home Address is **permanent**; the CoA is **temporary**. (ŌłÆ2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the asymmetry** ŌĆö describing triangle routing as "bidirectional". The **return path is direct**, only forward is tunneled. (ŌłÆ1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing M/M/1 delay formula** ŌĆö using $D_q = 1/\mu$ instead of $D_q = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$. The former is **mean service time**, not queuing time. (ŌłÆ2 marks)
> 4. **Mis-mapping QoS to applications** ŌĆö saying *"VoIP needs high bandwidth"*. VoIP needs **low delay + low jitter**; a 64 kbps codec works fine. (ŌłÆ1.5 marks)
> 5. **Skipping the application** in QoS answers ŌĆö a list of 4 requirements without application-mapping loses 2ŌĆō3 of 7 marks.
> 6. **Writing "TCP Reno" in the Congestion Control answers** ŌĆö the syllabus says *"Details not required"*, so Reno's state machine is **out of scope**. Stick to the **classification**. (ŌłÆ1.5 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤ÜĆ Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

- [ ] **Mobile IP has 3 entities** ŌĆö MH, HA, FA. CN is the peer.
- [ ] **Home Address = permanent identity** (never changes).
- [ ] **CoA = temporary location pointer** (changes per visited network).
- [ ] **Registration uses UDP port 434**.
- [ ] **Triangle routing is asymmetric**: forward (CNŌåÆHAŌåÆFAŌåÆMH) vs return (MHŌåÆCN direct).
- [ ] **Route Optimization** (Mobile IPv6) eliminates triangle by sending Binding Update to CN.
- [ ] **Foreign-Agent CoA** = FA's address (tunnel ends at FA).
- [ ] **Co-located CoA** = MH's own temporary address (tunnel ends at MH, IPv6-friendly).
- [ ] **Handoff** = MH registers with new FA; old binding expires; transient loss possible.
- [ ] **Congestion Control** = 2 ├Ś 2 matrix: (End-to-End / Network-Assisted) ├Ś (Preventive / Reactive).
- [ ] **End-to-end** uses packet loss as signal (TCP).
- [ ] **Network-assisted** uses explicit feedback (ECN, DECbit).
- [ ] **Preventive** = Leaky/Token Bucket, Admission Control, RSVP.
- [ ] **Reactive** = TCP AIMD, RED, ECN.
- [ ] **KTU syllabus = classification only**; avoid deep state-machine details.
- [ ] **QoS = 4 pillars**: Bandwidth (bps), Delay (s), Jitter (s), Reliability (loss %).
- [ ] **M/M/1 delay formula**: $D_q = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$ (NOT $1/\mu$).
- [ ] **Little's Law**: $\bar{N} = \lambda \cdot \bar{D}_q$.
- [ ] **Triangle inequality** guarantees $\eta \geq 1$ for triangle routing.
- [ ] **VoIP** = low delay + low jitter > raw bandwidth.
- [ ] **Banking** = 0% loss, reliability dominates.
- [ ] **Video conf.** = bandwidth + delay + jitter all critical.
- [ ] **Utilization $\rho$ should stay < 0.8** to avoid runaway queueing delay.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Achieving Good QoS - Traffic Shaping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Achieving Good QoS - Traffic Shaping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Traffic Shaping ŌĆö The Formal KTU Definition

**Traffic Shaping** is a *Quality of Service (QoS) mechanism* employed in computer networks to regulate the **rate, timing, and burst characteristics** of data transmission entering a network. The primary goal is to transform an *irregular, bursty stream* of packets generated by applications into a *smooth, predictable, and compliant* stream that conforms to a predefined traffic contract or Service Level Agreement (SLA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Traffic shaping is a congestion management technique that **delays** (rather than drops) packets to make them conform to a desired traffic pattern. It is implemented at the network edge, typically at the ingress router or at the source itself.

### 1.2 Why Do We Need Traffic Shaping?

Modern networks carry heterogeneous traffic:

- **VBR (Variable Bit Rate)** video streams
- **Bursty** web traffic (TCP)
- **Real-time** voice (VoIP)
- **Bulk data** transfers (FTP)

Without shaping, sources can inject traffic at rates far exceeding the negotiated *Committed Information Rate (CIR)*, causing:

1. **Buffer overflow** in downstream routers
2. **Jitter** for real-time flows
3. **Unfair bandwidth consumption**
4. **Violation of SLAs**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction ŌĆö Shaping vs. Policing:**
> - **Shaping:** *Delays* excess packets in a buffer (lossless but adds latency).
> - **Policing:** *Drops* or *marks* excess packets (lossy but low latency).

### 1.3 The Two Foundational Traffic Shaping Algorithms

The two principal algorithms mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| # | Algorithm | Core Characteristic |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Leaky Bucket** | Forces **constant** output rate; converts bursty input ŌåÆ smooth output |
| 2 | **Token Bucket** | Allows **controlled bursts**; enforces **average** output rate |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Bucket of Water"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for Leaky Bucket:**
> Imagine a **bathroom bucket with a small hole at the bottom**. You can pour water in *any amount*, *any time* (this is your bursty input). The bucket will *leak out* water at a **fixed, constant rate** (this is your smooth output). If you pour faster than it leaks, the bucket *fills up* and **overflows** ŌĆö excess water is *lost* (analogous to dropped packets).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for Token Bucket:**
> Imagine a **theme park ride where tokens are printed every 5 seconds**. People (packets) can only enter the ride if they hold a token. If no one is in line, **tokens pile up** in a jar up to a maximum capacity. When a **crowd suddenly arrives** (burst), the accumulated tokens let many people ride back-to-back. The token jar refills at a constant rate, ensuring a **long-term average rate** of entry.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Traffic Shaping

- **Žü (rho)** ŌĆö Output rate in **bits/sec** or **packets/sec**
- **╬▓ (beta)** ŌĆö Bucket capacity in **bits** or **packets**
- **r** ŌĆö Token generation rate
- **c** ŌĆö Token bucket capacity
- **B** ŌĆö Burst size in **bytes**
- **T_max** ŌĆö Maximum transmission time in **seconds**
- **CIR** ŌĆö Committed Information Rate (in ATM/Frame Relay contexts)
- **EIR** ŌĆö Excess Information Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Leaky Bucket Algorithm ŌĆö Operational Logic

The Leaky Bucket is conceptually a **single-server queueing system with a constant service time**. Packets enter a finite-capacity buffer (the "bucket") and depart at a **fixed, deterministic rate Žü** regardless of the input pattern.

### 2.1.1 Operational Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Packet Arrival:** A packet arrives at the shaper with instantaneous rate $\lambda(t)$ (possibly bursty).
2. **Bucket Fill Check:** If the bucket has space, the packet is admitted into the buffer. The bucket level $L(t)$ increases by the packet size $P$.
3. **Output Drain:** Simultaneously, the bucket is drained at a **constant rate Žü** bits/sec.
4. **Overflow Condition:** If the bucket is **already full** (i.e., $L(t) = \beta$) and a new packet arrives, the packet is **dropped** (or marked).
5. **Steady State:** The output stream is now perfectly smooth at rate Žü.

### 2.1.2 Why and How It Works

- **The "Why":** Networks have **limited bandwidth** and **finite buffers**. Burst traffic that exceeds these limits causes congestion, packet loss, and jitter.
- **The "How":** By holding excess packets in a buffer and releasing them at a constant rate, the leaky bucket acts as a **rate pacer** ŌĆö smoothing the traffic before it enters the network core.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Model of the Leaky Bucket

Let:
- $L(t)$ = bucket level (bits) at time $t$
- $r(t)$ = input rate (bits/sec) at time $t$
- $\rho$ = constant output rate (bits/sec)
- $\beta$ = bucket capacity (bits)

The differential equation governing the bucket level is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dL(t)}{dt} &= r(t) - \rho \quad \text{(when } 0 \leq L(t) < \beta \text{)} \\
L(t) &= \beta \quad \text{and excess packets are dropped when } L(t) = \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stability Condition:** For no long-term packet loss:

$$
\bar{r} \leq \rho
$$

where $\bar{r}$ is the time-averaged input rate.

### 2.1.4 Maximum Waiting Time in Leaky Bucket

A packet of size $P$ bits that arrives when the bucket is full must wait until enough bits have drained out. The maximum waiting time is:

$$
T_{\text{wait, max}} = \frac{\beta}{\rho}
$$

This is the time required to **completely empty** the bucket.

---

## 2.2 The Token Bucket Algorithm ŌĆö Operational Logic

The Token Bucket is a more **flexible** shaping mechanism that allows for **controlled bursts** while still enforcing a long-term average rate. It uses a conceptual "bucket" that holds **tokens** (not data).

### 2.2.1 Operational Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Token Generation:** Tokens are generated and added to the bucket at a constant rate $r$ tokens/sec.
2. **Bucket Capacity:** The bucket can hold at most $c$ tokens. Excess tokens are discarded (the bucket never overflows).
3. **Packet Arrival:** A packet of size $P$ bits (or requiring $k$ tokens) arrives.
4. **Token Consumption:** The packet is transmitted **only if** sufficient tokens are present. Each transmitted packet removes tokens from the bucket.
5. **Burst Allowance:** If many tokens have accumulated, a **burst of packets** can be transmitted back-to-back up to the bucket capacity $c$.
6. **No Tokens? Delay:** If insufficient tokens are available, the packet is **delayed** (queued) until enough tokens accumulate.

### 2.2.2 Why and How It Works

- **The "Why":** Many applications (e.g., web browsing, compressed video) are *inherently bursty*. A pure leaky bucket would force such traffic to be transmitted at a flat rate, wasting bandwidth during idle periods and degrading application performance.
- **The "How":** The token bucket allows the source to "**save up**" permission to send during silence, and "**spend**" it during a burst. This preserves the **long-term average rate** while permitting **short-term bursts**.

### 2.2.3 Mathematical Model of the Token Bucket

The number of tokens $T(t)$ in the bucket evolves as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(t+1) &= \min\{c, \, T(t) + r \cdot \Delta t - n(t)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $T(t)$ = tokens at time $t$
- $r \cdot \Delta t$ = tokens added in time $\Delta t$
- $n(t)$ = tokens consumed by packets sent in interval $\Delta t$
- $c$ = bucket capacity

**Average Output Rate:** Over a long interval $\tau$:

$$
\text{Average Rate} = \lim_{\tau \to \infty} \frac{\text{Packets sent in } \tau}{\tau} \leq r
$$

**Maximum Burst Size:** Up to $c$ packets (or $c \times P$ bits) can be sent instantaneously.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas ŌĆö they appear every KTU exam cycle!**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $L(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (r(\tau) - \rho) \, d\tau$ | Leaky bucket level (continuous time) | bits |
| 2 | $T_{\text{wait,max}} = \beta / \rho$ | Max waiting time in leaky bucket | seconds |
| 3 | $\bar{r} \leq \rho$ | Stability condition (no loss) | bits/sec |
| 4 | $B_{\text{max}} = c$ | Token bucket max burst (in packets) | packets |
| 5 | $R_{\text{avg}} \leq r$ | Token bucket long-term average rate | packets/sec |
| 6 | $T_{\text{burst}} = (B - c) / r$ | Time to transmit a burst of $B$ packets | seconds |
| 7 | $L_{n+1} = \min\{ \beta, \, L_n + A_n - \rho \cdot \Delta t\}$ | Discrete leaky bucket update | bits |
| 8 | $T_{n+1} = \min\{ c, \, T_n + r \cdot \Delta t - D_n\}$ | Discrete token bucket update | tokens |
| 9 | $D = P / \rho$ | Drain time for a single packet of size $P$ | seconds |
| 10 | $\text{Effective Rate} = B / T_{\text{burst}}$ | Effective rate during burst | bits/sec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation Notation Convention:**
> In all formula derivations, $A_n$ = bits arriving in the $n$-th interval, $D_n$ = bits departing in the $n$-th interval, $\Delta t$ = sampling interval.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **ATM Networks** | GCRA (Generic Cell Rate Algorithm) is mathematically equivalent to a leaky bucket |
| **Traffic Policing (RFC 2697/2698)** | srTCM and trTCM use dual token buckets |
| **Linux Traffic Control** | `tc` command uses `tbf` (Token Bucket Filter) qdisc |
| **Frame Relay** | CIR/EIR enforcement via leaky bucket |
| **Video Streaming** | Token bucket smooths VBR streams to meet SLA |
| **5G QoS** | QFI flows shaped at gNB using similar bucket logic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Burst Time for Token Bucket

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard):**
A token bucket has a capacity of $c$ tokens. Tokens are generated at a rate of $r$ tokens per second. A burst of $B$ tokens (i.e., $B$ packets waiting to be sent) suddenly arrives. Assuming the link rate is **not** the bottleneck (i.e., limited by tokens), find the **total time** required to transmit the entire burst.

### Step 1: Identify the Initial Conditions

At the instant the burst arrives, the bucket may have any number of tokens between $0$ and $c$. The **worst-case** (maximum delay) scenario occurs when the bucket is **completely full** with $c$ tokens.

$$
T_{\text{initial}} = c
$$

### Step 2: Set Up the Conservation Equation

The total number of packets that can be transmitted in time $T$ consists of:
- **Initial tokens** available at the start: $c$
- **Tokens generated** during the transmission window: $r \cdot T$

$$
B = c + r \cdot T
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Total Time $T$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= c + r \cdot T \\
B - c &= r \cdot T \\
T &= \frac{B - c}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The maximum time to drain a burst of $B$ packets is:
> $$T_{\text{burst,max}} = \frac{B - c}{r}$$
> This formula is the **most frequently tested** token bucket equation in KTU exams.

### Step 4: Derive the Effective Transmission Rate

The effective rate during the burst is the total data sent divided by total time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{B}{T_{\text{burst}}} \\
&= \frac{B}{(B - c) / r} \\
&= \frac{B \cdot r}{B - c}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** As $B \to \infty$ (very large burst), $R_{\text{eff}} \to r$ (the token generation rate). This confirms that the token bucket enforces the **average rate** $r$ over long bursts.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem ŌĆö Leaky Bucket (KTU Model Question)

**Problem Statement:**
A leaky bucket is used for traffic shaping. The bucket has a capacity of $\beta = 8$ MB. The constant output rate is $\rho = 2$ MB/sec. The input traffic pattern is as follows:
- From $t = 0$ to $t = 3$ sec: input arrives at $r_1 = 4$ MB/sec
- From $t = 3$ to $t = 5$ sec: input arrives at $r_2 = 1$ MB/sec

Determine the bucket level $L(t)$ at $t = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ seconds. Does any packet get dropped?

### Step 1: Establish the Update Equation

For discrete time steps of $\Delta t = 1$ sec:

$$
L_{n+1} = L_n + (r_n - \rho) \cdot \Delta t
$$

with the constraint $0 \leq L_{n+1} \leq \beta$.

### Step 2: Compute the Net Change Per Interval

**Interval 1** ($t = 0 \to 1$): $r_1 = 4$, $\rho = 2$, $\Delta t = 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_1 &= (r_1 - \rho) \cdot \Delta t = (4 - 2) \cdot 1 = +2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interval 2** ($t = 1 \to 2$): Same input

$$
\Delta L_2 = (4 - 2) \cdot 1 = +2 \text{ MB}
$$

**Interval 3** ($t = 2 \to 3$): Same input

$$
\Delta L_3 = (4 - 2) \cdot 1 = +2 \text{ MB}
$$

**Interval 4** ($t = 3 \to 4$): $r_2 = 1$

$$
\Delta L_4 = (1 - 2) \cdot 1 = -1 \text{ MB}
$$

**Interval 5** ($t = 4 \to 5$): $r_2 = 1$

$$
\Delta L_5 = (1 - 2) \cdot 1 = -1 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 3: Update the Bucket Level

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 0 \text{ MB (assumed empty initially)} \\
L_1 &= L_0 + \Delta L_1 = 0 + 2 = 2 \text{ MB} \\
L_2 &= L_1 + \Delta L_2 = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ MB} \\
L_3 &= L_2 + \Delta L_3 = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ MB} \\
L_4 &= L_3 + \Delta L_4 = 6 + (-1) = 5 \text{ MB} \\
L_5 &= L_4 + \Delta L_5 = 5 + (-1) = 4 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Check for Overflow

Maximum bucket level reached = **6 MB** at $t = 3$ sec.
Bucket capacity $\beta = 8$ MB.
Since $6 \text{ MB} < 8 \text{ MB}$, **no packet is dropped** Ō£ō

### Step 5: Present the Result Tabularly

| Time $t$ (sec) | Input Rate (MB/s) | Output Rate (MB/s) | Net Change (MB) | Bucket Level $L(t)$ (MB) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | 0 |
| 1 | 4 | 2 | +2 | 2 |
| 2 | 4 | 2 | +2 | 4 |
| 3 | 4 | 2 | +2 | 6 |
| 4 | 1 | 2 | ŌłÆ1 | 5 |
| 5 | 1 | 2 | ŌłÆ1 | 4 |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show the *initial condition* (typically $L_0 = 0$) explicitly. Many students lose marks by skipping the boundary condition.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem ŌĆö Token Bucket (KTU Model Question)

**Problem Statement:**
A token bucket has:
- Capacity $c = 1$ MB (i.e., can hold tokens worth 1 MB of data)
- Token generation rate $r = 2$ MB/sec
- A burst of $B = 5$ MB arrives at the shaper
- The link rate is assumed to be much higher than $r$

Compute:
1. The total time $T$ to transmit the entire burst
2. The effective rate during the burst

### Step 1: Apply the Token Bucket Burst Time Formula

Using the derived formula from Section 3.1:

$$
T = \frac{B - c}{r}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{5 \text{ MB} - 1 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ MB/sec}} \\
T &= \frac{4 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ MB/sec}} \\
T &= 2 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Effective Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{B}{T} = \frac{5 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ sec}} = 2.5 \text{ MB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Verify with the Closed-Form Expression

Using the formula $R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{B \cdot r}{B - c}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{5 \times 2}{5 - 1} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5 \text{ MB/sec} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

- The first **1 MB** of the burst is sent **immediately** using the stored tokens.
- The remaining **4 MB** are transmitted over **2 seconds** at the token generation rate of 2 MB/sec.
- The **average rate** of 2.5 MB/sec is **higher** than the token rate (2 MB/sec) **only** because of the pre-stored burst credit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** If the link rate were **lower** than $r$, the link would become the bottleneck and the effective rate would be limited by the link, not the tokens.

---

## 3.4 Comparison: Leaky Bucket vs. Token Bucket

| Parameter | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Output pattern | Strictly constant rate | Variable (allows bursts) |
| Burst handling | All bursts smoothed away | Bursts up to capacity $c$ permitted |
| Long-term average rate | $\leq \rho$ | $\leq r$ |
| Behavior on overflow | Drops packets | Drops *tokens* (not packets) |
| Implementation | Counter + timer | Counter + token generator |
| Major drawback | Wastes bandwidth for idle sources | Can violate short-term rate |
| Best suited for | Constant-rate traffic (e.g., voice) | Bursty traffic (e.g., web, video) |
| Equivalent standard | GCRA in ATM | TBF in Linux `tc` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Leaky Bucket ŌĆö Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input Arrival Bursty Traffic] --> A2{Bucket Level L t less than beta}
    A2 -->|Yes| A3[Admit Packet to Bucket]
    A2 -->|No| A4[Drop Packet and Increment Drop Counter]
    A3 --> A5[Increment Bucket Level L t by P]
    A4 --> A6[Overflow Indicator to Network Manager]
    A5 --> A7[Constant Drain Process at rate rho]
    A7 --> A8[Decrement Bucket Level at rate rho]
    A8 --> A9[Send Packet to Output Link at Constant Rate]
    A9 --> A10[Smooth Output Stream to Network]
    A6 --> A10
```

## 4.2 Token Bucket ŌĆö Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Token Generator running at rate r] --> B2[Token Bucket Capacity c]
    B2 --> B3{Token Count T t less than c}
    B3 -->|Yes| B4[Add Newly Generated Token]
    B3 -->|No| B5[Discard Excess Token Silently]
    B4 --> B6[Wait for Next Token Tick]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B2
    C1[Incoming Packet from Source] --> C2{Token Count T t greater than or equal to k}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3[Consume k Tokens and Transmit Packet]
    C2 -->|No| C4[Queue Packet in Waiting Buffer]
    C4 --> C5[Wait for Tokens to Accumulate]
    C5 --> C2
    C3 --> C6[Output Stream Allows Bursts up to c]
```

## 4.3 Combined QoS Traffic Shaping Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Source Application] --> S2[Traffic Classifier]
    S2 --> S3[Token Bucket Shaper per Flow]
    S3 --> S4[Queue FIFO Priority WFQ]
    S4 --> S5[Output Interface at rate R]
    S5 --> S6[Network Core]
    M1[QoS Policy Manager] -.->|Sets r c and rho| S3
    M1 -.->|Sets Queue Weights| S4
    P1[Traffic Policer Optional] -.->|Monitors Compliance| S6
```

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram ŌĆö Token Bucket

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Filling
    Filling --> Full : Token count reaches c
    Full --> Filling : Token consumed by packet
    Full --> Full : Excess token discarded
    Filling --> Draining : Packet arrives consumes token
    Draining --> Filling : More tokens added than consumed
    Draining --> Empty : Tokens reach zero
    Empty --> Filling : New tokens generated
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Leaky Bucket Diagram:** Trace the path from a bursty input on the left to a smooth, constant output on the right. The overflow branch represents dropped packets.
> - **Token Bucket Diagram:** The top loop is the *token generator*; the bottom loop is the *packet transmitter*. Notice they are **decoupled** ŌĆö this is what allows bursts.
> - **Architecture Diagram:** Shows where traffic shaping sits in the broader QoS pipeline ŌĆö between classification and queuing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model 1]**

### Q1. Define traffic shaping. How does it differ from traffic policing?

**Model Answer:**

**Traffic Shaping** is a congestion management technique that regulates the average rate and burst characteristics of data transmission by **delaying** packets to make the traffic flow conform to a desired pattern (typically specified by an SLA).

**Difference between Shaping and Policing:**

| Aspect | Traffic Shaping | Traffic Policing |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Action on excess | **Delays** (buffers) packets | **Drops** or **marks** packets |
| Latency impact | Adds variable delay | Minimal added delay |
| Implementation | Requires buffering | Often stateless |
| Typical use | Source/edge router | Network ingress points |
| Effect on flow | Smooths traffic | Enforces hard limits |

[3 marks]

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model 2]**

### Q2. Mention any **three** differences between the leaky bucket and token bucket algorithms.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Output Pattern:** Leaky bucket produces a **strictly constant** output rate, whereas token bucket allows **variable output** with controlled bursts up to capacity $c$.

2. **Burst Handling:** Leaky bucket **discards** excess data when the bucket fills, while token bucket **discards excess tokens** (never packets) and only delays packets waiting for tokens.

3. **Implementation:** Leaky bucket can be implemented using a **single counter** that decreases at a fixed rate, while token bucket requires a **separate token generator** and a counter for tokens, plus packet-arrival handling.

[3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Slot]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the operation of the **leaky bucket algorithm** used for traffic shaping. Draw a neat functional diagram and state its advantages and limitations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A leaky bucket has a capacity of **10 MB** and an output rate of **2 MB/sec**. The input traffic arrives in the following pattern:
- $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ sec: input rate = **3 MB/sec**
- $t = 4$ to $t = 6$ sec: input rate = **0.5 MB/sec**

Assuming the bucket is initially empty, calculate the bucket level at $t = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ sec. State whether any packet is dropped. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The leaky bucket algorithm works as follows:

1. **Bucket Concept:** A finite-capacity buffer (the "bucket") of size $\beta$ holds incoming packets.
2. **Constant Drain:** Packets are drained from the bucket at a **fixed rate $\rho$** bits/sec, regardless of input rate.
3. **Overflow:** When the bucket is full and a new packet arrives, that packet is **dropped**.
4. **Result:** The output stream is perfectly **smooth** at rate $\rho$.

**Functional Diagram:**

```
   INPUT (Bursty)
        |
        v
   +---------+
   | BUCKET  |---(Drops if full)---+
   | (size ╬▓)|                     |
   +---------+                     v
        |                    [DROP COUNTER]
        |
   Constant drain at rate Žü
        |
        v
   OUTPUT (Smooth, rate Žü)
```

**Advantages:**
- Provides perfectly smooth output ŌĆö ideal for constant-bit-rate emulation
- Simple to implement (one counter + timer)
- Protects downstream routers from bursty congestion
- Enforces a hard rate limit

**Limitations:**
- Cannot exploit idle periods to send bursts
- Adds latency to all packets (even those that don't need shaping)
- Drops packets on overflow ŌĆö no warning or remarking
- Not efficient for naturally bursty traffic (e.g., compressed video)

[7 marks ŌĆö 3 for explanation, 2 for diagram, 2 for advantages/limitations]

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $\beta = 10$ MB
- $\rho = 2$ MB/sec
- $r_1 = 3$ MB/sec for $0 \leq t \leq 4$
- $r_2 = 0.5$ MB/sec for $4 < t \leq 6$
- Initial level $L_0 = 0$

**Update equation:** $L_{n+1} = L_n + (r_n - \rho) \cdot \Delta t$

**Interval-by-Interval Calculation:**

| Interval | $r_n$ (MB/s) | $\rho$ (MB/s) | $\Delta t$ (s) | $\Delta L_n$ (MB) | $L_n$ (MB) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $t=0 \to 1$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | $+1$ | 0 ŌåÆ 1 |
| $t=1 \to 2$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | $+1$ | 1 ŌåÆ 2 |
| $t=2 \to 3$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | $+1$ | 2 ŌåÆ 3 |
| $t=3 \to 4$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | $+1$ | 3 ŌåÆ 4 |
| $t=4 \to 5$ | 0.5 | 2 | 1 | $-1.5$ | 4 ŌåÆ 2.5 |
| $t=5 \to 6$ | 0.5 | 2 | 1 | $-1.5$ | 2.5 ŌåÆ 1 |

**Overflow Check:**
Maximum level reached = **4 MB** at $t = 4$ sec.
Since $4 \text{ MB} < \beta = 10 \text{ MB}$, **no packet is dropped**. Ō£ō

[7 marks ŌĆö 5 for the table, 1 for the conclusion, 1 for the overflow check]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **token bucket algorithm** with a neat diagram. Compare it with the leaky bucket algorithm on any **four** parameters. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A token bucket has a capacity of **$c = 1$ MB** and tokens are generated at the rate of **$r = 1.5$ MB/sec**. If a burst of **$B = 7$ MB** suddenly arrives and the link rate is sufficiently high, calculate:
   (i) The total time to transmit the entire burst
   (ii) The effective transmission rate during the burst
   (iii) The number of tokens accumulated in the bucket after the burst is fully drained **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The token bucket algorithm operates as follows:

1. **Token Generation:** Tokens are added to a logical bucket of capacity $c$ at a constant rate $r$ tokens/sec.
2. **Capacity Limit:** The bucket can hold at most $c$ tokens. Tokens that arrive when the bucket is full are **silently discarded**.
3. **Packet Transmission:** Each packet of size $k$ tokens (typically $k=1$ per packet, or $k = P$ bytes) is transmitted **only if** sufficient tokens are present. Upon transmission, $k$ tokens are removed.
4. **Burst Allowance:** If $c$ tokens are available, up to $c$ packets can be sent back-to-back.
5. **No Tokens:** If insufficient tokens are present, the packet is **queued** until tokens accumulate.

**Functional Diagram:**

```
   Token Generator ----> [Token Bucket (max c)]
   (Rate r tokens/s)            |
                                | (Tokens consumed)
                                v
   Input Packet ---> {Tokens >= k?} --Yes--> [Transmit Packet]
                          |
                          No
                          v
                     [Queue Packet]
```

**Comparison Table (4 parameters):**

| Parameter | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1. Output rate | Strictly **constant** at $\rho$ | **Variable** with burst allowance |
| 2. Burst handling | Bursts are **smoothed away** | Bursts up to $c$ are **permitted** |
| 3. On overflow | **Packets are dropped** | **Tokens are dropped** (not packets) |
| 4. Best use case | CBR voice, circuit emulation | Web, VBR video, bursty data |

[7 marks ŌĆö 3 for explanation, 2 for diagram, 2 for comparison table]

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $c = 1$ MB
- $r = 1.5$ MB/sec
- $B = 7$ MB
- Link rate is sufficiently high (not the bottleneck)

**Part (i): Total Burst Time**

Using the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{B - c}{r} \\
T &= \frac{7 - 1}{1.5} \\
T &= \frac{6}{1.5} \\
T &= 4 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii): Effective Transmission Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{B}{T} = \frac{B \cdot r}{B - c} \\
&= \frac{7 \times 1.5}{7 - 1} \\
&= \frac{10.5}{6} \\
&= 1.75 \text{ MB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii): Tokens After Burst Drained**

After the burst is transmitted, the bucket starts filling with tokens again at rate $r$. The number of tokens depends on the time elapsed since the burst ended. If the question asks the **steady-state** count assuming no further arrivals, the bucket will fill to capacity $c$ in time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{fill}} &= \frac{c}{r} = \frac{1}{1.5} \approx 0.67 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

So after approximately **0.67 seconds** post-burst, the bucket contains **$c = 1$ MB** worth of tokens again.

[7 marks ŌĆö 2 + 2 + 3 for the three sub-parts]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Traffic Shaping Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bucket capacity cap (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** Students often compute $L(t) = L_{n-1} + (r - \rho) \cdot \Delta t$ without checking $\min\{L(t), \beta\}$. If the bucket would exceed $\beta$, you MUST cap it and declare the excess as **dropped**.
>
> 2. **Mixing Leaky and Token Bucket formulas (ŌĆō2 marks):** A frequent blunder is using the leaky-bucket *constant rate* assumption on a token-bucket problem. Remember: in the token bucket, packets can be sent in **bursts** up to $c$, then sustained at $r$.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency (ŌĆō1 mark):** If the bucket is in **MB** and the rate in **Mbps**, you must convert. $1$ byte = $8$ bits.
>
> 4. **Skipping the initial condition (ŌĆō1 mark):** Always state $L_0 = 0$ (or whatever is given). Examiners explicitly look for this.
>
> 5. **No diagram = ŌĆō2 to ŌĆō3 marks:** In 7-mark sub-parts, a "neat diagram" is **mandatory**. Hand-drawn ASCII or descriptive block diagrams are acceptable.
>
> 6. **Confusing $\rho$ with $r$:** $\rho$ is leaky bucket's output rate; $r$ is token bucket's generation rate. Don't interchange them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Traffic Shaping**

- [x] **Traffic Shaping** regulates rate/burst by *delaying* packets; **Policing** regulates by *dropping* them.
- [x] **Leaky Bucket** = constant output rate Žü; smoothes bursts; drops on overflow.
- [x] **Token Bucket** = average output rate r; allows bursts up to capacity c; drops excess *tokens*.
- [x] Leaky bucket stability: $\bar{r} \leq \rho$ (long-term average input Ōēż output).
- [x] Token bucket max burst: $B_{\max} = c$ packets (or $c \times P$ bits).
- [x] Token bucket burst time: $T = (B - c) / r$.
- [x] Effective rate during burst: $R_{\text{eff}} = B \cdot r / (B - c)$.
- [x] GCRA (ATM) ŌēĪ Leaky Bucket mathematically.
- [x] Linux `tc tbf` ŌēĪ Token Bucket Filter.
- [x] Token bucket **never** drops packets due to bucket overflow ŌĆö only delays.
- [x] Leaky bucket **always** drops packets when bucket is full.
- [x] Key exam triggers: "calculate bucket level", "maximum waiting time", "burst time", "effective rate".
- [x] For numericals: ALWAYS tabulate time, input rate, output rate, net change, and level.
- [x] Final formula to remember: $\boxed{T = (B - c) / r}$ ŌĆö appears in ~80% of token-bucket numericals.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic:**
> *"Leaky bucket = leaky faucet (constant drip); Token bucket = toll booth (passes when you have a token)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packet Scheduling. IPv4 Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packet Scheduling. IPv4 Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Packet Scheduling ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Packet Scheduling** is the core *Quality of Service (QoS)* mechanism operating at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI/TCP-IP reference model. It is the process by which a router or switch decides the **order in which buffered packets are transmitted** onto an outgoing link, especially when multiple flows (voice, video, data, control) compete for the **same finite bandwidth** of an output interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Packet scheduling algorithms determine the sequence in which packets from different queues/flows are transmitted onto the outgoing link, providing mechanisms for bandwidth allocation, delay bound guarantees, and jitter control."

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine an airport security line where **5 different gates** feed passengers into **one X-ray scanner**:
- **FIFO** = One single line. Whoever came first goes first. No priority.
- **Priority Queuing** = Three separate lines: First-Class, Business, Economy. The scanner always picks from the First-Class line first.
- **Round Robin** = The scanner processes one passenger from Gate 1, then one from Gate 2, then one from Gate 3 ŌĆö strictly cycling.
- **Weighted Fair Queuing** = The scanner spends 50% of its time on Gate 1, 30% on Gate 2, 20% on Gate 3 ŌĆö based on a "weight" (priority share).

The "scanner" is the **outgoing link**; the "passengers" are **packets**; the "gates" are **input flows/queues**.

---

## 1.2 IPv4 Protocol ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Internet Protocol version 4 (IPv4)** is a **connectionless, unreliable, best-effort** network-layer protocol defined in **IETF RFC 791 (September 1981)**. It provides **logical host addressing** and **global packet routing** across heterogeneous networks. Each IPv4 datagram consists of a **20-byte (minimum) header** followed by a payload of **0 to 65,535 ŌłÆ 20 bytes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties of IPv4:**
> - **Addressing width:** 32 bits ŌåÆ theoretical maximum of **2┬│┬▓ Ōēł 4.29 billion** unique host addresses.
> - **Header size:** 20 bytes (fixed) + 0 to 40 bytes (options) = **20 to 60 bytes**.
> - **Service model:** Best-effort, no delivery guarantee, no sequencing, no flow control.
> - **Unit of transfer:** Datagram (self-contained packet).

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Postal Letter

Think of an **IPv4 datagram as a physical letter** sent through a global postal system:
- The **envelope** = the IP header (contains sender & receiver addresses, postal rules, type of letter).
- The **letter inside** = the payload (TCP segment, UDP datagram, ICMP message, etc.).
- The **postal service** = the routed Internet (each post office = a router).
- **The postmark and routing stamps** = TTL, header checksum, identification number.
- If the letter is too big for one mailbox slot, the post office **tears it into smaller pieces** (each piece numbered so the recipient can reassemble) ŌĆö this is **fragmentation**.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Topics are Co-Listed in Module 3

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 3 groups both topics because they represent the **Network Layer's dual responsibilities**:
1. **Forwarding decisions** (how packets are queued and transmitted ŌåÆ Scheduling).
2. **Packet structure and addressing** (how packets are formatted ŌåÆ IPv4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Together they form the bridge between Module 2 (Data Link Layer ŌĆö switching, error control) and Module 4 (Routing algorithms and transport-layer protocols).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Packet Scheduling Algorithms ŌĆö Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 First-In-First-Out (FIFO / FCFS)

- **Logic:** Packets are placed in a single queue and transmitted in the exact order of arrival.
- **Implementation:** Simplest ŌĆö a pointer to head and tail of a circular buffer.
- **Drawbacks:** No differentiation between flows; a single bursty TCP flow can starve VoIP packets (causing "bufferbloat" with delays of **several seconds**).
- **Complexity:** $O(1)$ enqueue and dequeue.

### 2.1.2 Priority Queuing (PQ)

- **Logic:** Packets are classified into **N priority classes** (e.g., High, Medium, Low). The scheduler always services the highest non-empty queue first.
- **Starvation risk:** Low-priority traffic may experience **unbounded delay** if high-priority traffic is continuous.
- **Use case:** Routing protocols (OSPF hellos get high priority), network management traffic.

### 2.1.3 Round Robin (RR)

- **Logic:** Each queue gets a **time slot** in cyclic order. If a queue is empty, the scheduler moves to the next.
- **Fairness:** Equal share of bandwidth to all active flows.
- **Limitation:** A packet of length **1500 bytes** and one of **64 bytes** both consume one slot ŌĆö unfair when packet sizes differ drastically.

### 2.1.4 Weighted Round Robin (WRR)

- **Logic:** Extension of RR where each queue $i$ is assigned a **weight** $w_i$. In one cycle, queue $i$ transmits $w_i$ packets.
- **Example:** $w_1 = 5$, $w_2 = 3$, $w_3 = 2$ ŌåÆ out of every 10 slots, queue 1 gets 5, queue 2 gets 3, queue 3 gets 2.
- **Bandwidth share:** $\frac{w_i}{\sum_{j} w_j}$.

### 2.1.5 Deficit Round Robin (DRR) ŌĆö *KTU High-Yield*

- **Logic:** Each queue $i$ is assigned a **quantum** $Q_i$ and maintains a **deficit counter** $D_i$.
- **Algorithm (per round):**
  1. Add $Q_i$ to $D_i$ for every queue.
  2. If the packet at head has size $\le D_i$, transmit it and subtract its size from $D_i$.
  3. Otherwise, move to the next queue.
- **Advantage:** Handles **variable packet sizes** fairly without knowing mean packet size in advance.

### 2.1.6 Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ)

- **Logic:** Approximates **Generalized Processor Sharing (GPS)**. Each flow $i$ is assigned a weight $\phi_i$. The scheduler computes a **virtual finish time** $F_i$ for each packet based on its arrival and weight.
- **GPS finish time formula:**
$$F_i = \max(V(t_{arrival}),\, F_{i,prev}) + \frac{L_i}{\phi_i}$$
  where $V(t)$ is the **virtual time function** and $L_i$ is the packet length.
- **Packet ordering:** Transmit the packet with the **smallest $F_i$** next.
- **Use case:** QoS in 5G core, MPLS, ATM networks.

### 2.1.7 Token Bucket vs Leaky Bucket (related shapers)

- **Token Bucket:** Tokens accumulate at rate $r$; a packet of size $L$ is transmitted only if $L$ tokens are available. Allows bursts up to bucket size $b$.
- **Leaky Bucket:** Output rate strictly constant; excess packets are dropped or queued.

---

## 2.2 IPv4 Header ŌĆö Field-by-Field Analysis

The IPv4 header consists of **12 fixed fields** plus an **Options** field. Total length = **20 to 60 bytes** (must be a multiple of 4 bytes).

| # | Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|-------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Version** | 4 | Protocol version; always `0100` (= 4) for IPv4 |
| 2 | **IHL** (Internet Header Length) | 4 | Header length in **32-bit words**; min = 5, max = 15 |
| 3 | **Type of Service (ToS) / DSCP+ECN** | 8 | QoS priority, congestion notification |
| 4 | **Total Length** | 16 | Entire datagram size (header + data); max = 65,535 bytes |
| 5 | **Identification** | 16 | Unique ID for fragments of one original datagram |
| 6 | **Flags** | 3 | bit 0=Reserved, bit 1=DF (Don't Fragment), bit 2=MF (More Fragments) |
| 7 | **Fragment Offset** | 13 | Position of fragment's data in original datagram, in **8-byte units** |
| 8 | **Time To Live (TTL)** | 8 | Hop counter; decremented at each router; packet dropped at 0 |
| 9 | **Protocol** | 8 | Next-layer protocol: 1=ICMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 89=OSPF |
| 10 | **Header Checksum** | 16 | 16-bit ones' complement checksum of the header only |
| 11 | **Source IP Address** | 32 | Sender's IPv4 address |
| 12 | **Destination IP Address** | 32 | Receiver's IPv4 address |
| 13 | **Options** (optional) | 0ŌĆō320 | Record route, timestamp, source route, etc. (rarely used today) |
| 14 | **Padding** | 0ŌĆō31 | Zero-pad to make header a multiple of 4 bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The **Fragment Offset** field is multiplied by **8** to get the actual byte offset. This is because the offset must fit in 13 bits but represent values up to 65,528 ŌĆö saving 3 bits.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Range |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| **Header length** | $H = \text{IHL} \times 4$ | bytes (20 to 60) |
| **Total datagram length** | $L_{total} = L_{header} + L_{data}$ | bytes (max 65,535) |
| **Max data per fragment** | $D_{max} = \text{MTU} - L_{header}$ | bytes |
| **Number of fragments** | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{data}}{D_{max}} \right\rceil$ | integer |
| **Fragment offset (bytes)** | $O_{byte} = \text{offset field value} \times 8$ | bytes |
| **Fragment offset (field value)** | $O_{field} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{data bytes already sent}}{8} \right\rfloor$ | integer (0 to 8191) |
| **Last fragment data length** | $D_{last} = L_{data} - D_{max} \times (N - 1)$ | bytes |
| **Header Checksum** | $\text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} W_i}$ (ones' complement) | 16 bits |
| **WFQ finish time** | $F_i = \max(V(t), F_{i,prev}) + \dfrac{L_i}{\phi_i}$ | virtual time units |
| **WRR share** | $BW_i = BW_{total} \times \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_j w_j}$ | bps |
| **Leaky bucket drain time** | $t = \dfrac{\text{Queue length (bytes)}}{\text{Output rate (bytes/s)}}$ | seconds |
| **Token bucket burst size** | $B_{max} = b + r \cdot \Delta t$ | bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fragmentation is critical in **GRE tunnels, IPSec VPNs, and PPPoE** where the effective MTU shrinks. **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD)** uses the DF flag to prevent fragmentation, and routers send **ICMP "Fragmentation Needed" (Type 3, Code 4)** back to the source.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application of Scheduling | Application of IPv4 |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **5G Core (3GPP)** | WFQ for QoS bearers | IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack in UPF |
| **Data Center Fabrics** | DCTCP + ECN with strict priority for storage | ECMP hashing of source+destination IPv4 |
| **Satellite Networks** | Priority for ACKs over data | IPv4 fragmentation across variable MTU links |
| **Industrial IoT** | Time-sensitive networking (TSN) with scheduled queues | IPv4 with static addressing for SCADA |
| **Cyber Security** | Rate-limiting DDoS via token bucket | IPv4 header inspection in IDS/IPS |

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Header Checksum Calculation (RFC 1071)

The IPv4 header checksum is computed at every router (because TTL changes). The exact algorithm:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup:** Treat the 20-byte header as a sequence of **five 16-bit words** $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4$. Initialize sum $S = 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Accumulate (one's complement addition):**
$$S = W_0 \oplus W_1 \oplus W_2 \oplus W_3 \oplus W_4$$
(where $\oplus$ denotes one's complement addition ŌĆö i.e., add in binary, then add any carry-out back to the low bit).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute checksum:**
$$\text{Checksum} = \overline{S} = \text{bitwise NOT}(S)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verification at receiver:** Same accumulation over the received header (with checksum field included). A result of all **1s** means no error.

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a 4-word header (for simplicity) has:
$$W_0 = 0x4500,\ W_1 = 0x003C,\ W_2 = 0x1C46,\ W_3 = 0x4000$$

**Step A ŌĆö Sum $W_0 + W_1$:**
$$0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C \quad \text{(no carry)}$$

**Step B ŌĆö Add $W_2$:**
$$0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182 \quad \text{(no carry)}$$

**Step C ŌĆö Add $W_3$:**
$$0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182 \quad \text{(no carry)}$$

So $S = 0xA182$.

**Step D ŌĆö One's complement:**
$$\text{Checksum} = \overline{0xA182} = 0x5E7D$$

**Step E ŌĆö Verification:** At receiver, include the checksum in the sum:
$$0xA182 + 0x5E7D = 0xFFFF \quad \text{(all ones Ō£ō, no error)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivative: Fragmentation ŌĆö Standard KTU Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the SINGLE most repeated KTU numerical question for IPv4. Master this pattern.**

### Problem Setup

- Original IPv4 datagram has **Total Length = 4020 bytes**, **IHL = 5** (i.e., 20-byte header).
- Original **Data payload = 4020 ŌłÆ 20 = 4000 bytes**.
- The datagram traverses a network link with **MTU = 1500 bytes** (standard Ethernet).
- DF flag = 0 (fragmentation is allowed).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate maximum data per fragment:**
$$D_{max} = \text{MTU} - L_{header} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate number of fragments:**
$$N = \left\lceil \frac{4000}{1480} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.7027 \right\rceil = 3 \text{ fragments}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute fragment offset field values:**

Fragment offsets are in **8-byte units**. Crucially, **1480 is divisible by 8** (1480 ├Ę 8 = 185), so the math is clean.

| Fragment | Data Carried (bytes) | Total Length (bytes) | Offset (bytes) | Offset Field Value | MF Flag |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------|---------|
| F1 | 1480 (bytes 0ŌĆō1479) | 1500 | 0 | $0 \div 8 = 0$ | 1 (more coming) |
| F2 | 1480 (bytes 1480ŌĆō2959) | 1500 | 1480 | $1480 \div 8 = 185$ | 1 (more coming) |
| F3 | $4000 - 2960 = 1040$ (bytes 2960ŌĆō3999) | $20 + 1040 = 1060$ | 2960 | $2960 \div 8 = 370$ | 0 (last fragment) |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Common student errors to watch:**

> [!WARNING]
> - **Error 1:** Writing offset as `1480` instead of `185` (forgetting the 8-byte unit division).
> - **Error 2:** Forgetting that the last fragment's **Total Length** is shorter (1060, not 1500).
> - **Error 3:** Setting MF = 0 on the first fragment or MF = 1 on the last fragment.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Reassembly check:**
The destination host buffers all 3 fragments (identified by matching **Identification** field), sorts by offset, and concatenates the data portions: $1480 + 1480 + 1040 = 4000$ bytes Ō£ō

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Scheduling Algorithms

| Algorithm | Fairness | Complexity | Variable Packet Size | QoS Support | Starvation Risk | KTU Importance |
|-----------|----------|-----------|---------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **FIFO** | None | $O(1)$ | N/A | No | None | ŌŁÉ Low |
| **Priority Queuing** | Per-class only | $O(\log N)$ | N/A | Yes (4ŌĆō8 classes) | High for low priority | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Medium |
| **Round Robin** | Equal (per-packet) | $O(1)$ | Poor (biased to small pkts) | Weak | None | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Medium |
| **WRR** | Weighted (per-packet) | $O(1)$ | Poor (same as RR) | Yes (coarse) | None | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ High |
| **DRR** | Weighted (per-bit) | $O(1)$ amortized | **Excellent** | Yes | None | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Very High |
| **WFQ** | Weighted (per-bit) | $O(\log N)$ | Excellent | Yes (per-flow) | None (bounded) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Highest |
| **Token Bucket** | N/A (shaper) | $O(1)$ | N/A | Yes (rate limit) | N/A | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ High |
| **Leaky Bucket** | N/A (shaper) | $O(1)$ | N/A | Yes (rate limit) | N/A | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Justification:** WFQ provides a **worst-case delay bound** for flow $i$ of $\frac{L_{max}}{C \cdot \phi_i / \sum \phi_j}$ where $C$ is link capacity and $L_{max}$ is the maximum packet size. This makes it provably QoS-capable, which is why it is favored in carrier-grade routers (Cisco CBWFQ, Juniper WFQ).

---

## 3.4 Derivative: WFQ Virtual Finish Time

To prove the correctness of WFQ ordering, we derive the finish time for a flow $i$ sending a packet of length $L_i$ arriving at virtual time $V(t)$:

$$F_i = \max\left(V(t_{arrival}),\, F_{i,prev}\right) + \frac{L_i}{\phi_i}$$

**Interpretation of each term:**

- $V(t_{arrival})$ = The virtual clock value at the moment the packet arrives. It advances only when the **total busy backlog** is non-zero.
- $F_{i,prev}$ = The finish time of the **previous** packet of the same flow. This enforces FIFO ordering *within* a flow.
- $\frac{L_i}{\phi_i}$ = The "service time" the packet would take under ideal fluid GPS, where the flow gets $\phi_i / \sum \phi_j$ fraction of the link.

**Why we take the max:** A packet cannot finish before the previous packet of its own flow finishes (in-order delivery), nor before the link is virtually "available" to it.

**Example:** Link rate 1 Mbps, $\phi_1 = \phi_2 = 1$ (equal weights). Flow 1 sends a 1500-byte packet, Flow 2 sends a 500-byte packet. Times in microseconds:
- $F_1 = 0 + \frac{1500 \times 8}{10^6 \times 0.5} = 24\, \mu s$
- $F_2 = 0 + \frac{500 \times 8}{10^6 \times 0.5} = 8\, \mu s$

So Flow 2's packet is transmitted first (smaller finish time), exactly the desired **bit-level fair share**.

## 4.1 Packet Scheduling ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards applied: alphanumeric node IDs, plain text labels, no markdown inside quotes -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Flow A Input] --> qA[Queue A]
    inB[Flow B Input] --> qB[Queue B]
    inC[Flow C Input] --> qC[Queue C]
    inD[Flow D Input] --> qD[Queue D]

    qA --> sch{Packet Scheduler}
    qB --> sch
    qC --> sch
    qD --> sch

    sch -->|Algorithm 1 FIFO| out1[Single Output Link]
    sch -->|Algorithm 2 Priority PQ| out1
    sch -->|Algorithm 3 Round Robin RR| out1
    sch -->|Algorithm 4 WRR| out1
    sch -->|Algorithm 5 DRR| out1
    sch -->|Algorithm 6 WFQ| out1

    out1 --> tx[Transmit to Next Hop Router]
    tx --> net[Wide Area Network]
```

**Interpretation:** Multiple input flows (A, B, C, D) feed their respective queues. The central **scheduler** module selects packets based on one of six algorithms and pushes them onto a single output link. This is the architecture of every Layer-3 router.

## 4.2 Round Robin Scheduling ŌĆö Service Cycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cycle1[Service Cycle Round 1]
        direction LR
        s1[Step 1 Serve Queue A] --> s2[Step 2 Serve Queue B] --> s3[Step 3 Serve Queue C] --> s4[Step 4 Serve Queue D] --> s5[Return to A]
    end
    Cycle1 --> Cycle2[Service Cycle Round 2]
    Cycle2 --> Cycle3[Service Cycle Round 3]

    note1[Each queue is served exactly one packet per cycle] -.-> Cycle1
```

## 4.3 Deficit Round Robin (DRR) State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Router Receives Packets]) --> init[Initialize deficit counter D for each queue]
    init --> loop{For each queue i in round}
    loop -->|Add quantum| addQ[D_i equals D_i plus Q_i]
    addQ --> check{Packet head size L less than or equal to D_i}
    check -->|Yes| tx[Transmit packet]
    tx --> subD[D_i equals D_i minus L]
    subD --> check
    check -->|No, move on| loop
    loop -->|All queues done| loop
```

**Interpretation:** This flow shows the iterative DRR algorithm. Note the **inner loop** (check ŌåÆ transmit ŌåÆ subtract ŌåÆ check again) that allows one queue to send *multiple* packets per round if its quantum is large enough ŌĆö solving the variable-packet-size problem of plain RR.

## 4.5 IPv4 Fragmentation ŌĆö End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Host<br>4000 bytes data] --> r1[Router 1<br>MTU 4000<br>No fragmentation needed]
    r1 --> r2[Router 2<br>MTU 1500<br>Must fragment]
    r2 -->|Fragment 1<br>Offset 0 MF 1| r3[Router 3]
    r2 -->|Fragment 2<br>Offset 185 MF 1| r3
    r2 -->|Fragment 3<br>Offset 370 MF 0| r3
    r3 --> r4[Router 4<br>MTU 1500<br>Forward as is]
    r4 --> dst[Destination Host<br>Reassemble]

    style r2 fill:#fc9,stroke:#900
    style dst fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
```

**Interpretation:** Fragmentation occurs at **Router 2** where the outgoing MTU is smaller than the incoming packet. All three fragments traverse the rest of the path independently. Reassembly happens only at the **final destination** (not at intermediate routers), preventing redundant work and out-of-order delivery issues.

## 4.6 WFQ ŌĆö Virtual Time & Finish Number Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    arr[Packet Arrives at Router] --> vt[Read current virtual time Vt]
    vt --> prev[Lookup previous finish number of same flow]
    prev --> mx[Take max of Vt and previous finish]
    mx --> div[Divide packet length L by weight phi]
    div --> add[Add result to max]
    add --> store[Store new finish number for this flow]
    store --> sort[Insert into priority queue keyed by finish number]
    sort --> sel[Select packet with smallest finish number]
    sel --> tx[Transmit selected packet]
```

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Packet Scheduling. Differentiate between FIFO and Priority Queuing. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Packet Scheduling** is the QoS mechanism in routers and switches that determines the order in which packets buffered in queues are forwarded onto the outgoing link. It enables bandwidth allocation, delay control, and jitter management across competing traffic flows.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Parameter | FIFO (First-In-First-Out) | Priority Queuing (PQ) |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Number of queues | Single queue | Multiple queues (typically 4) |
| Service order | Strictly by arrival time | Strictly by priority class |
| Complexity | $O(1)$, simplest | $O(\log N)$, needs classifier |
| QoS support | None | Yes (4 priority levels) |
| Starvation | Not possible | Low-priority traffic may starve |
| Use case | Best-effort Internet | Routing protocols, VoIP |
| Delay bound | Unbounded | Bounded for high priority, unbounded for low |

**Conclusion:** FIFO is the simplest and most common default in home routers, whereas PQ is essential in enterprise networks where critical traffic (e.g., OSPF hellos, voice signaling) must be expedited.

---

### Question 2: List all fields of the IPv4 header. Explain the purpose of TTL and Header Checksum. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**IPv4 Header Fields (in order):**

1. **Version** (4 bits) ŌĆö IP version number (= 4)
2. **IHL** (4 bits) ŌĆö Header length in 32-bit words (5 to 15)
3. **Type of Service / DSCP+ECN** (8 bits) ŌĆö QoS marking
4. **Total Length** (16 bits) ŌĆö Datagram size in bytes (max 65,535)
5. **Identification** (16 bits) ŌĆö Unique ID for fragmentation
6. **Flags** (3 bits) ŌĆö DF and MF bits
7. **Fragment Offset** (13 bits) ŌĆö Position of fragment, in 8-byte units
8. **TTL** (8 bits) ŌĆö Hop limit
9. **Protocol** (8 bits) ŌĆö Next-layer protocol number
10. **Header Checksum** (16 bits) ŌĆö Error detection on header only
11. **Source IP Address** (32 bits) ŌĆö Sender address
12. **Destination IP Address** (32 bits) ŌĆö Receiver address
13. **Options** (0ŌĆō320 bits) ŌĆö Optional features

**Purpose of TTL:** TTL (Time To Live) is an 8-bit field initialized by the source (typical value: 64 or 128). Every router that forwards the packet **decrements TTL by 1**. If TTL reaches **0**, the router **drops the packet** and sends an **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11)** message to the source. This prevents packets from looping forever in case of routing cycles.

**Purpose of Header Checksum:** It is a 16-bit ones' complement checksum computed over the **header fields only** (not the payload). It is recomputed at every router because TTL changes. If the recomputed checksum does not match the stored value, the header is corrupt and the packet is dropped. The payload has its own checksums (TCP/UDP) for end-to-end integrity.
