## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**Q1. List any three advantages of IPv6 over IPv4.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Larger Address Space:** IPv6 uses **128-bit addresses** ($2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$), providing an effectively unlimited address pool compared to IPv4's 32-bit ($2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^9$).
2. **Simplified Header:** IPv6 has a **fixed 40-byte header** with only 8 fields, eliminating header checksum and fragmentation processing at every router ŌĆö improving forwarding speed.
3. **Built-in Security:** IPv6 was designed with **IPSec (AH and ESP)** support as integral, providing authentication and confidentiality.
4. (Bonus) **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC):** Nodes can self-configure their IPv6 address from the link prefix and their MAC-derived interface ID, removing dependence on DHCP for basic connectivity.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Supplementary, Module 3]**

**Q2. What is the role of the Next Header field in IPv6?** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
The **Next Header (8 bits)** field in the IPv6 base header identifies the **type of the header that immediately follows** the base header. It serves two roles:
1. If the next entity is an **extension header**, the value indicates the extension type (e.g., `0` for Hop-by-Hop Options, `43` for Routing, `44` for Fragment, `50` for ESP, `51` for AH, `60` for Destination Options).
2. If there are no extension headers, it identifies the **upper-layer protocol** (e.g., `6` = TCP, `17` = UDP, `58` = ICMPv6).

The Next Header field therefore forms a **linked-list chain** allowing IPv6 to support optional information while keeping the base header minimal.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Choice Questions (14 marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation Pattern, Module 3]**

---

### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a) Explain the IPv6 header format in detail. Compare it with the IPv4 header.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö IPv6 Header Fields (Table form, 4 marks):**

The IPv6 base header is **fixed at 40 bytes (320 bits)** and contains exactly **8 fields**:

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 bits | Always `0110` = 6 |
| Traffic Class | 8 bits | QoS / DSCP / ECN |
| Flow Label | 20 bits | Identifies a packet flow for QoS |
| Payload Length | 16 bits | Length of payload (max 65,535 B) |
| Next Header | 8 bits | Pointer to next header |
| Hop Limit | 8 bits | Decremented per hop; packet dropped at 0 |
| Source Address | 128 bits | Originator |
| Destination Address | 128 bits | Final destination (or intermediate if routing header present) |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Comparison with IPv4 (3 marks):**

| Feature | IPv4 | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| Address size | 32 bits | 128 bits |
| Header size | 20ŌĆō60 bytes (variable) | **40 bytes (fixed)** |
| Header fields | 12 + options | 8 (no options in base) |
| Header checksum | Yes | **Removed** |
| Fragmentation at routers | Allowed | **Not allowed** (only at source) |
| Broadcast | Yes | **No** (replaced by multicast) |
| Security (IPSec) | Optional | **Mandatory design** |
| Flow control | No | **Flow Label** field added |
| Configuration | Manual / DHCP | **SLAAC** / DHCPv6 |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Fields Removed from IPv4 (remaining marks):**

IHL, Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset, Header Checksum, Options, Padding ŌĆö all removed, making IPv6 processing more efficient.

**(b) Discuss the different types of IPv6 addresses with suitable examples.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Three Primary Types (1 mark):**
Unicast, Multicast, Anycast.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Unicast Sub-Types (3 marks):**

- **Global Unicast Address (GUA)** ŌĆö `2000::/3`, e.g., `2001:DB8:ACAD::1`. Public, internet-routable.
- **Link-Local Unicast** ŌĆö `FE80::/10`, e.g., `FE80::203:BFFF:FE12:3456`. Auto-configured on every IPv6 interface; valid only on the local link; not routed.
- **Unique Local Address (ULA)** ŌĆö `FC00::/7`, e.g., `FD00:1234:5678::1`. Private, non-globally-routable (analogous to IPv4 `10.x.x.x`).
- **Loopback** ŌĆö `::1/128`. Localhost.
- **Unspecified** ŌĆö `::/128`. Used as source address by a host that has not yet obtained an address.
- **IPv4-Mapped** ŌĆö `::FFFF:192.168.1.1`. Represents an IPv4-only node as IPv6.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multicast (2 marks):**
- Prefix `FF00::/8`. Replaces IPv4 broadcast.
- Examples: `FF02::1` (All Nodes), `FF02::2` (All Routers), `FF02::1:FFXX:XXXX` (Solicited-Node Multicast).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Anycast (1 mark):**
- Identical in format to unicast; assigned to multiple interfaces. Routed to the *nearest* instance (e.g., DNS root servers `2001:503:BA3E::2:30`).

---

### **Question B (14 marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the three transition mechanisms for moving from IPv4 to IPv6 with suitable diagrams.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Dual Stack (2.5 marks):**
- The host/router runs **both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks concurrently**.
- The application requests both `A` and `AAAA` DNS records; DNS returns both. The transport layer uses whichever is appropriate.
- Advantage: native, no encapsulation overhead.
- Disadvantage: requires IPv4 address for every dual-stack device (defeats IPv4 exhaustion goal).
- **Example:** Modern smartphones, which have both 4G IPv4 and 5G native IPv6.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tunneling (2.5 marks):**
- IPv6 packets are **encapsulated inside IPv4 packets** (Protocol field = **41**) to traverse IPv4-only backbones.
- **Configured Tunnel:** Endpoints manually specified.
- **6to4 (RFC 3056):** Uses `2002::/16`; the next 32 bits encode the IPv4 of the tunnel endpoint. Any host with a global IPv4 can be a 6to4 router.
- **Teredo (RFC 4380):** Tunnels over **UDP port 3544** for traversal of IPv4 NATs.
- **ISATAP (RFC 5214):** For intra-site; treats IPv4 network as a virtual NBMA link.
- **6rd (RFC 5969):** ISP-grade 6to4.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Translation / NAT64 (2 marks):**
- A gateway translates headers between IPv6-only and IPv4-only networks.
- **NAT64 (RFC 6146)** + **DNS64** allows an IPv6-only client to reach an IPv4 server.
- Translation prefix: `64:FF9B::/96` (well-known prefix).

**(b) Explain ICMPv6 and the Neighbor Discovery Protocol. How does IPv6 replace ARP?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö ICMPv6 Overview (2 marks):**
- Defined in **RFC 4443**.
- Next Header value **58**.
- Functions: error reporting, diagnostics, and Neighbor Discovery.

**Step 2 ŌĆö ICMPv6 Error Messages (2 marks):**
- **Type 1** Destination Unreachable (with codes: no route, communication administratively prohibited, address unreachable, port unreachable).
- **Type 2** Packet Too Big ŌĆö used for **Path MTU Discovery**.
- **Type 3** Time Exceeded ŌĆö `traceroute6` source.
- **Type 4** Parameter Problem ŌĆö header error.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Informational Messages (1 mark):**
- **Type 128** Echo Request, **Type 129** Echo Reply (used by `ping6`).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) (2 marks):**
- **Router Solicitation (Type 133)** ŌĆö host asks for routers.
- **Router Advertisement (Type 134)** ŌĆö router announces prefix, MTU, default gateway, lifetime. Enables **SLAAC**.
- **Neighbor Solicitation (Type 135)** ŌĆö replaces ARP Request.
- **Neighbor Advertisement (Type 136)** ŌĆö replaces ARP Reply.
- **Redirect (Type 137)** ŌĆö better first-hop available.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Hop Limit and TTL.** Marks are deducted for stating IPv4 has TTL and IPv6 also has TTL. The correct terminology: IPv4 has TTL, **IPv6 has Hop Limit**.
> 2. **Forgetting "No Broadcast in IPv6."** Many students mention broadcast is replaced by multicast but fail to state that **broadcast does not exist in IPv6** at all.
> 3. **Wrong Next Header value for ICMPv6.** It is **58**, not 1. (ICMPv4 is Protocol 1; ICMPv6 is Next Header 58.)
> 4. **Confusing "Payload Length" with IPv4's "Total Length."** IPv6's Payload Length does **not** include the 40-byte base header, while IPv4's Total Length includes the IPv4 header.
> 5. **Not mentioning the EUI-64 derivation for link-local addresses** when discussing stateless autoconfiguration.
> 6. **Mentioning the deprecated NAT-PT** instead of modern NAT64/DNS64 in transition questions.
> 7. **Drawing ARP in IPv6 networks** ŌĆö completely wrong. The correct mechanism is **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] IPv6 address is **128 bits**; base header is **fixed 40 bytes** with **8 fields**.
- [ ] Five IPv4 fields removed: **IHL, Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset, Header Checksum, Options, Padding**.
- [ ] Two new IPv6 fields: **Flow Label (20 bits)** and **Traffic Class (8 bits)**.
- [ ] **No broadcast** in IPv6 ŌĆö replaced by **multicast**. All-nodes multicast is `FF02::1`.
- [ ] **Hop Limit** replaces IPv4's TTL. Decremented at every router; discarded at 0.
- [ ] **Next Header field** (8 bits) is a pointer chain: extension headers ŌåÆ upper-layer protocol.
- [ ] Extension headers should be processed in the **recommended order**: Hop-by-Hop ŌåÆ Destination ŌåÆ Routing ŌåÆ Fragment ŌåÆ Destination ŌåÆ AH ŌåÆ ESP ŌåÆ Upper-Layer.
- [ ] **Fragmentation in IPv6 is done only by the source**, never by routers. Uses Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD) + Type 2 "Packet Too Big" ICMPv6 message.
- [ ] **ICMPv6 Next Header = 58.** ICMPv4 Protocol = 1. Do NOT confuse.
- [ ] **Global Unicast prefix: `2000::/3`.** **Link-Local prefix: `FE80::/10`.** **ULA prefix: `FC00::/7`.** **Multicast prefix: `FF00::/8`.** **Loopback: `::1/128`.** **Unspecified: `::/128`.**
- [ ] **6to4 prefix: `2002::/16`.** Encodes IPv4 in the next 32 bits.
- [ ] **Teredo uses UDP port 3544.** **IPv6-in-IPv4 tunneling uses Protocol 41.**
- [ ] **Anycast** is syntactically identical to unicast; multiple interfaces share it; routed to the nearest.
- [ ] **Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP)** replaces ARP using **Type 135 (NS)** and **Type 136 (NA)**.
- [ ] **SLAAC** uses Type 133 (RS) and Type 134 (RA) ŌĆö no DHCP server required.
- [ ] **Transition mechanisms:** Dual Stack, Tunneling, Translation (NAT64/DNS64).
- [ ] Address compression rules: leading zeros omitted per group; `::` replaces **one** longest run of zero groups; `::` may appear **only once** per address.
- [ ] **EUI-64 Interface ID:** insert `FF:FE` in middle of 48-bit MAC, then flip bit 1 (U/L bit) of the first byte.
- [ ] **Solicited-Node Multicast = `FF02::1:FF` + last 24 bits** of the unicast Interface ID.
- [ ] IPv6 mandates **IPSec support** in design (AH, ESP).
- [ ] Maximum payload per standard IPv6 packet = **65,535 bytes** (Jumbogram extension for larger).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet Control Protocols - Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Internet Control Protocols - Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)** is a supporting protocol in the Internet Protocol suite as defined by **RFC 792**. It is used by network devices (routers, hosts, gateways) to send **error messages** indicating that a requested service is not available or that a host/router could not be reached. ICMP is considered an integral part of IP (Internet Protocol), but it is architecturally layered **directly above IP** in the TCP/IP model ŌĆö meaning ICMP messages are carried as the **payload of an IP datagram**, not as a separate independent protocol. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Verbatim):** ICMP is used by hosts and routers to communicate network-layer information back to a sender. It is mandated that students understand ICMP message types, message format, and the operational difference between *error reporting messages* and *query messages*. ICMP messages are typically generated in response to errors in IP datagrams (as specified in RFC 1122) or for diagnostic purposes (such as `ping` and `traceroute`). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sending a **registered postal parcel** from Kochi to Delhi. If the recipient's house is locked, the postman leaves a **"Return to Sender ŌĆö Address Incomplete"** slip inside your mailbox a few days later. That slip is **not** your original parcel ŌĆö it is a *meta-message* about your parcel. ICMP works exactly like this return slip: - Your **IP datagram** = the postal parcel - The **destination host or router** = the post office - The **ICMP message** = the return slip telling you *why* delivery failed > [!NOTE] > **Why ICMP exists:** IP itself is an **unreliable, connectionless** protocol ŌĆö it makes a "best-effort" delivery and forgets. ICMP fills the gap by providing limited **feedback** about failures and by supporting simple **diagnostic tools** (e.g., `ping`, `traceroute`). ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics & Standard Constants | Parameter | Value / Standard | |---|---| | IP Protocol Number for ICMP | **1** | | Defined in | **RFC 792** (updated by RFC 1122, RFC 1812) | | ICMP Header Length | **8 bytes** (Type + Code + Checksum + Rest of Header) | | Minimum ICMP Message | **8 bytes** (header) + payload | | ICMP Datagram Size | Should not exceed **576 bytes** total | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Memorize the **ICMP Protocol Number = 1**. It appears frequently in short-answer questions and is the most common "one-mark" trap in MCQs. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture ŌĆö Where ICMP Fits ICMP is **not** a transport-layer protocol (like TCP/UDP). It sits **at the network layer**, alongside IP. The encapsulation order from a sender's perspective is: $$ \text{Application Data} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ICMP Header} \;\rightarrow\; \text{IP Header} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Link-Layer Frame} $$ The reverse order occurs on the receiver side. Critically, **ICMP does not make IP reliable** ŌĆö it only reports problems. ### 2.2 Logical Breakdown ŌĆö Why and How ICMP Operates 1. **A datagram is sent** by a source host to a destination. 2. **A router or destination host encounters a problem** (e.g., TTL = 0, destination unreachable, port closed). 3. **The device discards the original datagram** and crafts an ICMP error message. 4. **The ICMP message is encapsulated inside a NEW IP datagram** addressed back to the original source. 5. **The source receives the ICMP message** and can react (e.g., `ping` reports failure; TCP can re-route). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of ICMP:** ICMP error messages **never** be sent in response to: > - An ICMP error message (to prevent infinite ping-pong). > - A datagram destined to a **broadcast** or **multicast** address. > - A datagram with a **broadcast source address** (RFC 1122). > - A fragment that is **not the first fragment** of a datagram. ### 2.3 ICMP Message Format ŌĆö Field-by-Field Every ICMP message begins with a standardized **8-byte header**: | Field | Size (bits) | Description | |---|---|---| | **Type** | 8 | Identifies the ICMP message category (e.g., 3 = Destination Unreachable) | | **Code** | 8 | Sub-category specifying the exact reason within the type | | **Checksum** | 16 | One's complement checksum over the entire ICMP message | | **Rest of Header** | 32 | Purpose varies by Type (e.g., identifier + sequence for Echo) | | **Data / Payload** | Variable | Often includes the IP header + first 8 bytes of the original datagram that caused the error | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ŌĆö ICMP Message Types > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners expect students to **memorize at least 6ŌĆō8 critical Type values** and their corresponding Codes. The following table is the **minimum required** for the 2024 Scheme. | Type | Name | Category | Common Codes (Code : Meaning) | |---|---|---|---| | **0** | Echo Reply | Query | 0 : Echo Reply (used by `ping`) | | **3** | Destination Unreachable | Error | 0 : Network unreachable, 1 : Host unreachable, 3 : Port unreachable | | **4** | Source Quench (deprecated) | Error | 0 : Congestion control (RFC 6633 deprecated) | | **5** | Redirect | Error | 0 : Redirect for Network, 1 : Redirect for Host | | **8** | Echo Request | Query | 0 : Echo Request (used by `ping`) | | **9** | Router Advertisement | Query | 0 : Router Advertisement | | **10** | Router Solicitation | Query | 0 : Router Solicitation | | **11** | Time Exceeded | Error | 0 : TTL exceeded in transit, 1 : Fragment reassembly time exceeded | | **12** | Parameter Problem | Error | 0 : Pointer indicates error, 1 : Missing required option, 2 : Bad length | | **13** | Timestamp Request | Query | 0 : Timestamp | | **14** | Timestamp Reply | Query | 0 : Timestamp Reply | | **15** | Information Request (deprecated) | Query | ŌĆö | | **16** | Information Reply (deprecated) | Query | ŌĆö | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications - **`ping` (ICMP Echo):** Measures **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** between two hosts. Formula: $\text{RTT} = T_{\text{reply}} - T_{\text{send}}$. - **`traceroute` (ICMP Time Exceeded):** Discovers the **path** and per-hop **latency** by sending datagrams with increasing TTLs. - **Path MTU Discovery (PMTU):** Uses ICMP Type 3 Code 4 ("Fragmentation Needed and DF set") to discover the largest packet size a path supports. - **Router Discovery:** Hosts use Type 9/10 to find default gateways dynamically. - **Network Diagnostics & Fault Localization:** ISP technicians and `netmiko`/`Wireshark` analysts rely on ICMP codes to pinpoint failure points. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough ŌĆö How an ICMP Error Message is Constructed **Scenario:** Host A in Trivandrum sends a UDP datagram to a closed port on Host B in Bangalore. Router R2 on the path detects the closed port and must inform Host A. #### Step 1: Trigger Event A UDP datagram arrives at Host B, but the destination port (e.g., 50000) has no listening socket. The IP layer in Host B marks the datagram as undeliverable. **Conversion logic:** Transport layer (UDP) cannot deliver ŌåÆ IP layer must notify the source via ICMP. #### Step 2: ICMP Message Construction Host B's IP layer crafts a new ICMP packet of **Type = 3, Code = 3** (Destination Unreachable / Port Unreachable). $$ \text{ICMP Message} = \underbrace{\text{Type(8) + Code(8) + Checksum(16)}}_{\text{4 bytes}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Unused(32)}}_{\text{4 bytes}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{IP Header (20B) + 8B of original UDP payload}}_{\text{triggering datagram excerpt}} $$ **Conversion logic:** The "Rest of Header" for Type 3 is **unused (set to 0)**. The "Data" section carries the **original IP header + first 8 bytes** so the source can match the error to the right socket. #### Step 3: Checksum Computation The 16-bit ICMP checksum is the **one's complement of the one's complement sum** of the ICMP message (treated as a sequence of 16-bit words). $$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \right)}_{\text{16-bit one's complement}} $$ Where $W_i$ are the 16-bit words of the ICMP message (header + data). **Step-by-step evaluation (illustrated with 4 words of header $W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4$):** $$ \begin{aligned} S_1 &= W_1 + W_2 \\ S_2 &= S_1 + W_3 \\ S_3 &= S_2 + W_4 \\ \text{Checksum} &= \overline{S_3} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > At the receiver, all 16-bit words (including the checksum field) are summed. A correct packet yields $\text{FFFF}_{16}$ ŌĆö the all-ones pattern. Any other result indicates corruption. #### Step 4: Encapsulation into a NEW IP Datagram Host B creates a fresh IP datagram: $$ \text{IP Header (src = Host B, dst = Host A, proto = 1)} \;+\; \text{ICMP message} $$ **Conversion logic:** Notice that the **protocol field = 1**, which is the IP protocol number for ICMP. #### Step 5: Delivery and Reaction Host A receives the ICMP error. Its `ping`/`traceroute` application decodes Type 3, Code 3 and prints: *"Destination port unreachable."* ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö ICMP Type Classification | Dimension | Error Reporting Messages | Query Messages | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Report problems with IP datagram delivery | Obtain information from hosts/routers | | **Types** | 3, 4 (deprecated), 5, 11, 12 | 0, 8, 9, 10, 13, 14 | | **Direction** | From the device that detects the problem back to the source | Typically requestŌĆōreply pair | | **Payload** | IP header + 8 bytes of original datagram | Variable (timestamp, identifier, etc.) | | **Example Use** | TTL exceeded (`traceroute`) | `ping` echo | | **Frequency** | Generated only on error conditions | Generated on demand | ### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö RTT Calculation from ICMP Echo Suppose Host A sends an **ICMP Echo Request (Type 8)** at timestamp $T_1 = 08:00:00.100$ seconds. Host B replies with an **ICMP Echo Reply (Type 0)** which arrives at Host A at $T_2 = 08:00:00.148$ seconds. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTT} &= T_2 - T_1 \\ &= 0.048 \text{ s} \\ &= 48 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ If the path is roughly symmetric, the **one-way delay** is: $$ D_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{\text{RTT}}{2} = 24 \text{ ms} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assumption Warning:** The $D_{\text{one-way}} = \text{RTT}/2$ formula assumes the forward and reverse paths have **identical propagation and queuing delays**, which is rarely true in practice. KTU questions, however, often use this simplification. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ICMP Position in the TCP/IP Architecture ```mermaid graph TD APP[Application Layer
HTTP, DNS, ping, traceroute] TRP[Transport Layer
TCP, UDP] NET[Network Layer
IP and ICMP] LLC[Link Layer
Ethernet, Wi-Fi] PHY[Physical Layer
Cables, Radio Waves] APP --> TRP TRP --> NET NET --> LLC LLC --> PHY ICMPNODE[ICMP sits at the Network Layer
encapsulated directly inside IP] NET -.- ICMPNODE ``` ### 4.2 ICMP Message Encapsulation Stack ```mermaid graph TB subgraph FRAME[Link-Layer Frame] LH[Link Header] subgraph IPDG[IP Datagram - Protocol Field = 1] IH[IP Header] subgraph ICMPMSG[ICMP Message] TH[Type 8 bits] CD[Code 8 bits] CK[Checksum 16 bits] RH[Rest of Header 32 bits] PL[Variable Payload] end end LT[Link Trailer] end LH --> IH --> TH --> CD --> CK --> RH --> PL --> LT ``` ### 4.3 ICMP Error-Reporting Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Host A sends IP datagram to Host B] --> B{Router R1
forwards} B --> C{Router R2
TTL = 0} C -- TTL=0 --> D[Router R2 discards datagram] D --> E[Router R2 builds ICMP Type 11 Code 0
Time Exceeded] E --> F[Encapsulates ICMP inside new IP datagram
dst = Host A] F --> G[Host A receives ICMP Time Exceeded] G --> H[traceroute prints hop address
and increments TTL] B --> I{Host B
port closed} I -- Port closed --> J[Host B builds ICMP Type 3 Code 3
Destination Unreachable Port] J --> K[Encapsulates inside new IP datagram
dst = Host A] K --> L[Host A receives Port Unreachable] L --> M[Application reports failure] ``` ### 4.4 ICMP Message Type Taxonomy ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[ICMP Messages] ROOT --> ERR[Error Reporting] ROOT --> QRY[Query Messages] ERR --> T3[Type 3
Destination Unreachable] ERR --> T4[Type 4
Source Quench Deprecated] ERR --> T5[Type 5
Redirect] ERR --> T11[Type 11
Time Exceeded] ERR --> T12[Type 12
Parameter Problem] QRY --> T0[Type 0
Echo Reply] QRY --> T8[Type 8
Echo Request ping] QRY --> T9[Type 9
Router Advertisement] QRY --> T10[Type 10
Router Solicitation] QRY --> T13[Type 13
Timestamp Request] QRY --> T14[Type 14
Timestamp Reply] ``` ### 4.5 ICMP Echo Exchange Block Diagram (ping operation) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HA as Host A participant HB as Host B HA->>HB: ICMP Type 8 Code 0
Echo Request
Identifier = ID, Seq = 1 HB-->>HA: ICMP Type 0 Code 0
Echo Reply
Same ID and Seq = 1 HA->>HA: Compute RTT
RTT equals reply time minus send time ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper]`** **Q1. What is ICMP? List any four ICMP message types with their purpose.** *(3 Marks ŌĆö Blooms Level: Remember / Understand)* **Model Answer:** ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is a network-layer protocol defined in RFC 792 used by hosts and routers to send error messages and operational information regarding the processing of IP datagrams. ICMP messages are encapsulated inside IP datagrams with the protocol field set to **1**. Four important ICMP message types: | Type | Name | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 3 | Destination Unreachable | Reports that a datagram cannot be delivered | | 5 | Redirect | Informs a host of a better route | | 8 | Echo Request | Used by `ping` to test reachability | | 11 | Time Exceeded | Reports that TTL has reached 0 | --- > **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`** **Q2. Differentiate between ICMP Error Reporting Messages and ICMP Query Messages with examples.** *(3 Marks ŌĆö Blooms Level: Understand)* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Error Reporting Messages | Query Messages | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Notify the source about delivery problems | Obtain diagnostic information | | **Trigger** | Generated automatically on error | Generated on user request | | **Examples** | Type 3 (Destination Unreachable), Type 11 (Time Exceeded) | Type 8/0 (Echo), Type 13/14 (Timestamp) | | **Payload** | IP header + 8 bytes of original datagram | Variable test data (identifier, sequence, timestamp) | | **Direction** | Always from problem-detector to source | Bidirectional requestŌĆōreply | --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the ICMP message format in detail with a neat diagram. Mention the role of each field. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Explain how `traceroute` uses the ICMP Time Exceeded message to discover the path to a destination. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) ICMP Message Format:** The ICMP message begins with an 8-byte header followed by a variable-length data section. | Field | Size | Function | |---|---|---| | Type | 8 bits | Identifies the ICMP message class (e.g., 0, 3, 5, 8, 11) | | Code | 8 bits | Specifies the sub-reason within the type | | Checksum | 16 bits | 16-bit one's complement checksum covering the entire ICMP message | | Rest of Header | 32 bits | Function depends on Type; unused for Type 3, identifier + sequence for Echo | | Data | Variable | For error messages: IP header + 8 bytes of the original datagram that triggered the error | **Diagram of the ICMP header layout:** $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Type (8 bits)} & \text{Code (8 bits)} & \text{Checksum (16 bits)} \\ \hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{\text{Rest of Header (32 bits)}} \\ \hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{\text{Data Section (Variable Length)}} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Role of each field:** - **Type** routes the message to the correct handler. - **Code** provides fine-grained diagnostic detail. - **Checksum** ensures the ICMP message itself is not corrupted. - **Rest of Header** carries type-specific information. - **Data** provides context (the offending IP header) so the source can match the error to the right datagram. **(b) Traceroute Mechanism Using ICMP Type 11:** `traceroute` discovers the route to a destination by exploiting the **TTL (Time To Live)** field in the IP header and the **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11, Code 0)** response. **Step-by-step procedure:** 1. The source sends a UDP datagram (or ICMP Echo) with **TTL = 1** to the destination. 2. The first router decrements TTL to 0, discards the datagram, and returns **ICMP Type 11 Code 0** to the source. 3. The source records the IP address of the replying router as **Hop 1** and the RTT. 4. The source sends a new datagram with **TTL = 2**. The second router replies with ICMP Time Exceeded. 5. This process continues with TTL = 3, 4, 5, ... until the destination itself is reached. 6. When the destination is reached, it returns an **ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3 ŌĆö Port Unreachable)** because the chosen UDP port is unused. This signals `traceroute` to **stop**. **Why this works:** Each router along the path that decrements TTL to 0 is forced by IP to send ICMP Type 11 back to the source, thereby revealing its identity. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Describe the operation of `ping` using ICMP. Explain how RTT is calculated and mention any two limitations of `ping`. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Explain ICMP Redirect (Type 5) and Destination Unreachable (Type 3) messages. List at least four common codes for Destination Unreachable. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Operation of `ping`:** `ping` is a diagnostic utility that uses **ICMP Echo Request (Type 8)** and **ICMP Echo Reply (Type 0)** messages to test the reachability of a host and measure the round-trip time (RTT). **Procedure:** 1. The source creates an **ICMP Echo Request** with a unique identifier and a sequence number starting at 1. 2. The ICMP message is encapsulated inside an IP datagram addressed to the target host. 3. The target host, upon receiving the Echo Request, swaps the source and destination addresses, changes Type from 8 to 0, recomputes the checksum, and sends it back as an **ICMP Echo Reply**. 4. The source records the time difference between sending the request and receiving the reply. **RTT Calculation:** $$ \text{RTT}_i = T_{\text{reply}, i} - T_{\text{send}, i} $$ The total elapsed time after $N$ echo exchanges: $$ \text{Total Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{RTT}_i $$ Average RTT: $$ \overline{\text{RTT}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{RTT}_i $$ **Two Limitations of `ping`:** 1. **Firewall/ACL Blocking:** Many networks block ICMP at the perimeter for security. A failed `ping` does not necessarily mean the host is offline ŌĆö it may simply be filtering ICMP. 2. **No Path Information:** `ping` only confirms end-to-end reachability; it cannot identify *which* hop failed. (Use `traceroute` for that.) 3. **No Reliability Guarantee:** A successful `ping` does not guarantee that higher-layer services (HTTP, SSH) are functional, since those may run on different ports or be independently blocked. 4. **Symmetric Path Assumption:** The $D_{\text{one-way}} = \text{RTT}/2$ simplification breaks down in asymmetric networks. **(b) ICMP Redirect (Type 5) and Destination Unreachable (Type 3):** **ICMP Redirect (Type 5):** When a router $R_1$ receives a datagram from a host on the same subnet, and $R_1$ knows that another router $R_2$ on the same subnet is a better next-hop for the destination, $R_1$ forwards the datagram to $R_2$ **and** simultaneously sends an **ICMP Redirect** message back to the original host. The host then updates its routing table to use $R_2$ directly for future traffic. - **Code 0:** Redirect for the Network - **Code 1:** Redirect for the Host - **Code 2:** Redirect for Type of Service and Network - **Code 3:** Redirect for Type of Service and Host **ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3):** Generated when a router or host cannot deliver a datagram. Common codes: | Code | Meaning | |---|---| | 0 | Network Unreachable | | 1 | Host Unreachable | | 2 | Protocol Unreachable | | 3 | Port Unreachable | | 4 | Fragmentation Needed and DF flag set (used in PMTUD) | | 5 | Source Route Failed | | 6 | Destination Network Unknown | | 7 | Destination Host Unknown | | 9 | Communication Administratively Prohibited | The ICMP error message includes the **original IP header + first 8 bytes of the original datagram** so the sender can identify which flow caused the error. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the protocol number (1):** A surprisingly common error. Always mention that ICMP sits in IP with `protocol = 1`. > 2. **Drawing ping as a TCP exchange:** `ping` uses **ICMP directly**, not TCP. The connection is NOT a three-way handshake. > 3. **Confusing Type 0 and Type 8:** Type 8 = Request (sent **to** the target). Type 0 = Reply (sent **back** from the target). Examiners specifically test this swap. > 4. **Skipping the payload in error messages:** When describing Type 3 or Type 11, always mention that the ICMP message carries the **IP header + 8 bytes of the original datagram**. Omitting this costs 2 marks. > 5. **Stating ICMP is at the Transport Layer:** Wrong. ICMP is at the **Network Layer**, even though it is a "message" protocol. > 6. **Confusing `traceroute`'s transport:** Most implementations use **UDP** with a high port, but on Windows it uses **ICMP Echo**. Mention both if asked. > 7. **Ignoring the "no ICMP for ICMP" rule:** Examiners love to ask when ICMP is NOT generated. Remember the four exceptions (ICMP error, broadcast, multicast, non-first fragment). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **ICMP = Network-layer protocol, RFC 792, IP protocol number = 1**, encapsulated as IP payload. - **Two main categories:** **Error Reporting** (Types 3, 4, 5, 11, 12) and **Query** (Types 0, 8, 9, 10, 13, 14). - **Header = 8 bytes:** Type (8) + Code (8) + Checksum (16) + Rest of Header (32). - **Error messages always include** the IP header + first 8 bytes of the original datagram that triggered the error. - **`ping` uses Type 8 (Request) and Type 0 (Reply)** ŌĆö and computes RTT as $T_{\text{reply}} - T_{\text{send}}$. - **`traceroute` exploits Type 11 Code 0 (Time Exceeded)** by sending datagrams with incrementing TTLs. - **Path MTU Discovery relies on Type 3 Code 4** (Fragmentation Needed, DF set). - **ICMP is NEVER generated** in response to: another ICMP error, a broadcast/multicast, a broadcast-source datagram, or a non-first fragment. - **The checksum is a 16-bit one's complement sum** of the ICMP message; a valid packet produces $\text{FFFF}_{16}$ at the receiver. - **Type 4 (Source Quench) is officially deprecated** by RFC 6633 ŌĆö congestion control is now handled by transport protocols (TCP). - **ICMP does not make IP reliable** ŌĆö it only provides feedback. Reliability is the job of TCP. - **Highest-weight questions** (14 marks) in KTU often ask for the **format + a real-world use case** (e.g., ping, traceroute, PMTUD). - **Memorize these Type values cold:** **0, 3, 5, 8, 11** ŌĆö they account for ~80% of all KTU questions on this topic.

topics.title: Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is a **Layer 2 / Layer 3 boundary protocol** defined in **RFC 826 (1982)** that dynamically maps a **32-bit logical IP address** to its corresponding **48-bit physical MAC (Media Access Control) address** within a local broadcast domain (LAN). ARP operates directly above the **Data Link Layer** and is encapsulated within **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) frames**, enabling seamless interoperability between the **Internet Protocol (Network Layer)** and the **underlying LAN technologies** such as Ethernet, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), and Token Ring.

In the **TCP/IP protocol suite architecture**, ARP is classified as a **link-layer assist protocol** that bridges the gap between the **network interface layer (IP)** and the **physical network interface (MAC)**. Whenever a host needs to construct a frame for delivery to a destination IP address on the **same subnet**, it consults the **ARP cache (ARP table)**. If no mapping exists, the host broadcasts an **ARP Request** packet across the local network. The target host (or its designated proxy) replies with an **ARP Reply** containing its hardware address, which is then cached locally for future use.

**Standard Metrics & Protocol Parameters (as per IEEE/RFC standards):**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| IPv4 Address Length | **32 bits** | RFC 791 |
| MAC Address Length | **48 bits** | IEEE 802.3 |
| ARP Operation Code (Request) | **1** | RFC 826 |
| ARP Operation Code (Reply) | **2** | RFC 826 |
| ARP Cache Entry Timeout (Linux) | **~60 seconds** (base) / up to **1200 seconds** | RFC 5227 |
| ARP Cache Entry Timeout (Windows) | **15ŌĆō45 seconds** (reachable) / **300ŌĆō900 seconds** (stale) | Microsoft Docs |
| Default ARP Cache Size | **Yes ŌåÆ typically 256ŌĆō4096 entries** | OS-dependent |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy ŌĆö The Office Building Phone Directory**

Imagine you are a new employee in a large corporate office. You know your colleague **Mr. Rahul** by his **employee ID (IP address = "EMP-4723")**, but you do not know his **desk phone extension (MAC address = "Ext-204")**. You have a **phone in your hand (Ethernet NIC)** ready to dial, but you need the actual extension to ring his phone.

Here is what happens:

1. You shout loudly across the open office floor: *"Hey everyone, I am looking for the person with Employee ID **EMP-4723**. Please tell me your desk extension."*  
   ŌåÆ This is the **ARP Request (broadcast to all hosts ŌĆö FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF)**.

2. Every employee hears your shout, but only **Mr. Rahul** (whose ID matches EMP-4723) responds directly to you: *"My extension is **Ext-204**."*  
   ŌåÆ This is the **ARP Reply (unicast back to the requester)**.

3. You immediately write down on your **personal notepad**: *"EMP-4723 ŌåÆ Ext-204"*, so you do not have to shout again.  
   ŌåÆ This is the **ARP Cache (dynamic ARP table update)**.

4. Now you can dial Mr. Rahul directly in future without any broadcast.

**In networking terms:**
- **Employee ID** = **IP address** (logical, hierarchical, network-wide unique)
- **Desk Extension** = **MAC address** (physical, flat, hardware-burned)
- **Office Floor** = **Local Subnet / Broadcast Domain**
- **Shouting** = **Broadcast**
- **Direct Reply** = **Unicast**
- **Your Notepad** = **ARP Cache / ARP Table**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think of ARP as **"Address Resolution Protocol"** ŌĆö it **resolves** (finds, translates) an **IP into a MAC**. The keyword is **"Resolution"** ŌĆö like resolving a query in a database.

---

## 1.3 Why ARP is Needed ŌĆö The Address Mismatch Problem

The **TCP/IP model** uses IP addresses for **end-to-end, global, logical addressing** across the entire Internet. However, every physical network (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, etc.) uses **MAC addresses** for **node-to-node, local, hardware addressing** within a single LAN. This creates a **fundamental translation gap**:

- **IP Address** ŌåÆ Logical, software-configurable, hierarchical (network + host), routed across the Internet
- **MAC Address** ŌåÆ Physical, hardware-burned into NIC, flat (no hierarchy), used only inside the LAN

**ARP fills this gap** by providing a dynamic translation mechanism ŌĆö without ARP, an IP packet destined for a host on the local Ethernet network would have no way to be encapsulated into a valid Ethernet frame.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ARP Packet Format (RFC 826 ŌĆö Complete Bit-Level Structure)

The ARP message is encapsulated inside an **Ethernet frame** with the Ethernet Type field set to **0x0806**. The packet is exactly **28 bytes** (for IPv4 over Ethernet) before any optional Padding or Trailer bytes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU exams:** Memorize the following **28-byte ARP packet structure** ŌĆö it is a frequent 7ŌĆō14 mark question topic. The fields highlighted in bold are the most frequently asked.

| # | Field Name | Size (Bytes) | Purpose / Description |
|---|------------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Hardware Type** | 2 | **1** = Ethernet (10 Mbps), **6** = IEEE 802 (Wi-Fi/Token Ring) |
| 2 | **Protocol Type** | 2 | **0x0800** = IPv4, **0x86DD** = IPv6 (but IPv6 uses NDP, not ARP) |
| 3 | Hardware Address Length | 1 | **6** for Ethernet MAC |
| 4 | Protocol Address Length | 1 | **4** for IPv4 |
| 5 | **Operation Code (Opcode)** | 2 | **1** = ARP Request, **2** = ARP Reply, **3** = RARP Request, **4** = RARP Reply |
| 6 | **Sender Hardware Address (SHA)** | 6 | MAC address of the *sender* |
| 7 | **Sender Protocol Address (SPA)** | 4 | IP address of the *sender* |
| 8 | **Target Hardware Address (THA)** | 6 | MAC address of the *target* (set to 00:00:00:00:00:00 in request, filled in reply) |
| 9 | **Target Protocol Address (TPA)** | 4 | IP address being queried |
| | **TOTAL** | **28 Bytes** | (plus 18-byte Ethernet header = 46 bytes minimum) |

### Visual Layout of the 28-Byte ARP Packet

$$
\underbrace{\text{HW Type}}_{2B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Proto Type}}_{2B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{HW Len}}_{1B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Proto Len}}_{1B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{2B}
$$
$$
\underbrace{\text{SHA}}_{6B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{SPA}}_{4B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{THA}}_{6B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{TPA}}_{4B}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethernet Minimum Frame Size Rule:** A standard Ethernet frame must be at least **64 bytes (minimum)** excluding the preamble. Since the Ethernet header is 14 bytes and ARP is 28 bytes (total 42 bytes), a **4-byte padding trailer** is added to reach the 46-byte minimum payload size, then the CRC (4 bytes) is appended ŌĆö totalling **64 bytes on the wire**. This is a **classic KTU question**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step ARP Workflow

The complete ARP resolution process can be broken into **5 structured phases**:

### **Phase 1 ŌĆö Cache Lookup (Local Decision)**
When Application Layer sends data ŌåÆ Transport (TCP/UDP) ŌåÆ Network Layer (IP) ŌåÆ Data Link Layer needs MAC.

- Host checks its **ARP Cache Table** (in RAM).
- If entry **IP ŌåÆ MAC** exists and is **fresh (not timed out)** ŌåÆ uses it directly.  
  ŌåÆ *No broadcast needed. No delay.*

### **Phase 2 ŌĆö ARP Request Construction (Broadcast)**
If no valid cache entry exists:

- Host constructs an **ARP Request packet** with:
  - **SHA** = Host's own MAC
  - **SPA** = Host's own IP
  - **THA** = 00:00:00:00:00:00 (unknown ŌĆö will be filled by the target)
  - **TPA** = Destination IP being queried
  - **Opcode** = 1
- This packet is encapsulated in an **Ethernet broadcast frame** with:
  - **Destination MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF** (all 48 bits = 1 ŌåÆ broadcast to every device on the LAN)
  - **Source MAC** = Sender's MAC
  - **EtherType** = 0x0806 (ARP)

### **Phase 3 ŌĆö Network Broadcast & Reception**
- The Ethernet switch forwards the frame out of **all ports** in the same VLAN (flooding behavior, unless switch has learned all MACs).
- Every host on the LAN receives the frame and passes it up to the ARP layer.
- Each host checks: *"Is **TPA == my IP**?"*  
  - If **No** ŌåÆ silently **discards** the packet.
  - If **Yes** ŌåÆ this is the intended target. Proceed to Phase 4.

### **Phase 4 ŌĆö ARP Reply (Unicast)**
- The target host constructs an **ARP Reply** with:
  - **SHA** = Target's own MAC (this is the answer!)
  - **SPA** = Target's own IP
  - **THA** = Original sender's MAC (now known, copied from request's SHA)
  - **TPA** = Original sender's IP (copied from request's SPA)
  - **Opcode** = 2
- The reply is sent as a **unicast** Ethernet frame directly back to the original requester (no broadcast needed ŌĆö requester's MAC is already known).

### **Phase 5 ŌĆö Cache Update & Forwarding**
- The original requester receives the ARP Reply.
- It **updates its ARP cache** with the new mapping: **Target IP ŌåÆ Target MAC**.
- The original IP packet is now encapsulated in an Ethernet frame with the correct MAC and **forwarded to the destination**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic ŌĆö "BUC, CUB"** for remembering broadcast/unicast:
> - ARP **R**equest = **B**roadcast (one to all)
> - ARP **R**eply = **U**nicast (one to one)
> - **R**ARP **R**equest = **B**roadcast
> - **R**ARP **R**eply = **U**nicast

---

## 2.3 Types of ARP

| Variant | Full Name | Purpose | RFC |
|---------|-----------|---------|-----|
| **ARP** | Address Resolution Protocol | IP ŌåÆ MAC resolution (most common) | RFC 826 |
| **RARP** | Reverse Address Resolution Protocol | MAC ŌåÆ IP resolution (used by diskless clients at boot) | RFC 903 |
| **Proxy ARP** | Proxy Address Resolution Protocol | Router responds to ARP requests on behalf of another host on a different subnet | RFC 1027 |
| **Gratuitous ARP** | Unsolicited ARP | Host broadcasts its own IP-to-MAC mapping to detect duplicates (used for IP conflict detection & to update switch MAC tables) | RFC 5227 |
| **Inverse ARP (InARP)** | Inverse ARP | Used in Frame Relay to discover DLCI Ōåö IP mappings | RFC 2390 |
| **ARP Spoofing / Poisoning** | ŌĆö | **Security attack** where attacker sends fake ARP replies to redirect traffic through itself (MITM attack) | ŌĆö |
| **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** | (IPv6) | Replaces ARP in IPv6 using ICMPv6 Router Solicitation/Advertisement | RFC 4861 |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table contains the most important numerical and protocol parameters ŌĆö memorize all of them.**

| Formula / Parameter | Expression / Value | Unit | Notes |
|--------------------|-------------------|------|-------|
| ARP Packet Size (IPv4) | $2+2+1+1+2+6+4+6+4 = 28$ | Bytes | Fixed for IPv4-over-Ethernet |
| Ethernet Header Size | $6+6+2 = 14$ | Bytes | DST MAC + SRC MAC + EtherType |
| Ethernet Minimum Frame Size | $\geq 64$ | Bytes | Excludes preamble (7B) + SFD (1B) |
| Ethernet Maximum Frame Size | $\leq 1518$ | Bytes | Excludes preamble + SFD |
| Required Padding for ARP | $64 - 14 - 28 = 22$ (sometimes 4) | Bytes | To meet minimum frame size ŌĆö depends on whether preamble is counted |
| ARP Cache Entry (Linux) | $t_{cache} = 60\text{s base} \rightarrow \leq 1200\text{s}$ | Seconds | RFC 5227 |
| ARP Cache Entry (Windows) | $t_{reachable} = 15\text{s} \text{ to } 45\text{s}$ | Seconds | RFC 5227 |
| Hardware Type (Ethernet) | $\text{HType} = 1$ | ŌĆö | RFC 1340 |
| Protocol Type (IPv4) | $\text{PType} = 0x0800$ | Hexadecimal | RFC 1340 |
| Opcode (Request) | $\text{Op} = 1$ | Decimal | ŌĆö |
| Opcode (Reply) | $\text{Op} = 2$ | Decimal | ŌĆö |
| Broadcast MAC Address | $\text{B-MAC} = \text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}$ | Hex | All 48 bits = 1 |
| Encapsulation EtherType | $\text{EthType} = 0x0806$ | Hex | Identifies ARP frame |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** When asked "What is the size of an ARP packet?", write **"28 bytes"** ŌĆö not "28 bits". Marks lost on units is a common examiner complaint.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **LAN Switch Operation** | ARP drives MAC learning tables (CAM tables) in switches. Each ARP frame updates the switch's MAC address table. |
| **Network Troubleshooting** | `arp -a` (Windows/Linux) and `ip neigh` (Linux) are first-line diagnostic commands. |
| **Cybersecurity** | ARP Spoofing is the basis of **MITM (Man-in-the-Middle) attacks** in public Wi-Fi networks ŌĆö countered by **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** on managed switches. |
| **Virtualization & Cloud** | Hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM) use **gratuitous ARP** to inform switches that a VM's MAC has migrated to a new physical host (VMotion/Live Migration). |
| **IoT & Embedded Systems** | Diskless network boot (PXE) uses **RARP** or **BOOTP/DHCP** for initial IP assignment ŌĆö modern systems prefer DHCP. |
| **IPv6 Transition** | **ARP is fully replaced by NDP** in IPv6. This eliminates ARP's broadcast overhead, security flaws, and lack of authentication. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö ARP Packet Total Size for IPv4 over Ethernet

We will derive the **on-the-wire byte count** of a complete ARP Request frame, step by step, as would be expected in a 14-mark KTU question.

### **Step 1: Identify all sub-fields of the ARP packet (RFC 826)**

The ARP message has 9 logical fields. For the **most common case ŌĆö IPv4 over IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** ŌĆö the field sizes are as follows:

- Hardware Type (HType) = **2 bytes** (always 1 for Ethernet)
- Protocol Type (PType) = **2 bytes** (always 0x0800 for IPv4)
- Hardware Address Length (HLen) = **1 byte** (always 6 for MAC-48)
- Protocol Address Length (PLen) = **1 byte** (always 4 for IPv4)
- Operation Code (Opcode) = **2 bytes** (1 = request, 2 = reply)
- Sender Hardware Address (SHA) = **6 bytes** (MAC address of sender)
- Sender Protocol Address (SPA) = **4 bytes** (IPv4 address of sender)
- Target Hardware Address (THA) = **6 bytes** (00:00:00:00:00:00 in request)
- Target Protocol Address (TPA) = **4 bytes** (IP address being resolved)

### **Step 2: Sum the field sizes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARP Packet Size} &= \underbrace{2}_{\text{HType}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{PType}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{HLen}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{PLen}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{Opcode}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{6}_{\text{SHA}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{SPA}} + \underbrace{6}_{\text{THA}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{TPA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARP Packet Size} &= 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 4 \\
&= 4 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 10 + 4 + 6 + 4 \\
&= 5 + 2 + 14 + 6 + 4 \\
&= 7 + 14 + 10 \\
&= \mathbf{28 \; Bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The first 5 fields (2+2+1+1+2) total **8 bytes** (the fixed ARP header). The address quadruplet (SHA + SPA + THA + TPA) totals **6+4+6+4 = 20 bytes** (the variable ARP payload). Total = 8 + 20 = **28 bytes** Ō£ō.

### **Step 3: Compute the total Ethernet frame size**

An Ethernet II frame has the following layout:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Preamble} &= 7 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)} &= 1 \text{ byte} \\
\text{Destination MAC} &= 6 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Source MAC} &= 6 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{EtherType} &= 2 \text{ bytes} \; (0x0806 \text{ for ARP}) \\
\text{Payload (ARP Packet)} &= 28 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Padding (if any)} &= P \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Frame Check Sequence (CRC-32)} &= 4 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per IEEE 802.3**, the **minimum frame size (excluding preamble + SFD) is 64 bytes**, and the **maximum is 1518 bytes**.

### **Step 4: Determine padding required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame size (no padding)} &= 6 + 6 + 2 + 28 + 4 = 46 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Minimum required} &= 64 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Padding } P &= 64 - 46 = 18 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **total on-the-wire size of an ARP Request frame** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 7_{\text{preamble}} + 1_{\text{SFD}} + 14_{\text{header}} + 28_{\text{ARP}} + 18_{\text{pad}} + 4_{\text{CRC}} \\
&= \mathbf{72 \; bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Without preamble and SFD, the frame is 64 bytes; with them, it is 72 bytes on the wire.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example ŌĆö Complete ARP Exchange Trace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Standard Problem:** "Host A (192.168.1.10, MAC AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA) wants to send an IP packet to Host B (192.168.1.20). Describe the complete ARP exchange, including all field values at each step, assuming Host A's cache is empty and both hosts are on the same Ethernet LAN."

### **Given Data**

| Host | IP Address | MAC Address | Cache State |
|------|------------|-------------|-------------|
| Host A (Sender) | 192.168.1.10 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | Empty (no entry for 192.168.1.20) |
| Host B (Target) | 192.168.1.20 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB | ŌĆö |

### **Step 1: Host A checks its ARP Cache**

Host A consults its ARP table for **192.168.1.20**. No entry exists.

ŌåÆ *Host A must perform ARP broadcast.*

### **Step 2: Host A constructs the ARP Request**

| Field | Value (Hex/Decimal) | Explanation |
|-------|---------------------|-------------|
| HType | 0x0001 | Ethernet |
| PType | 0x0800 | IPv4 |
| HLen | 6 | MAC is 6 bytes |
| PLen | 4 | IPv4 is 4 bytes |
| Opcode | 1 (0x0001) | Request |
| SHA | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | Host A's MAC |
| SPA | 192.168.1.10 | Host A's IP |
| THA | 00:00:00:00:00:00 | Unknown yet |
| TPA | 192.168.1.20 | Querying for Host B |

### **Step 3: Ethernet frame encapsulation for the Request**

| Ethernet Field | Value |
|----------------|-------|
| Destination MAC | **FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF** (broadcast) |
| Source MAC | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA |
| EtherType | 0x0806 (ARP) |

### **Step 4: Host B receives & processes**

- All hosts on the LAN receive the broadcast.
- Host B sees **TPA = 192.168.1.20 = its own IP** ŌåÆ accepts the packet.
- Hosts C, D, E... see **TPA ŌēĀ their IP** ŌåÆ silently discard.

### **Step 5: Host B constructs the ARP Reply**

| Field | Value | Explanation |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| HType | 0x0001 | Ethernet |
| PType | 0x0800 | IPv4 |
| HLen | 6 | ŌĆö |
| PLen | 4 | ŌĆö |
| Opcode | **2 (0x0002)** | Reply |
| SHA | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB | Host B's MAC (the **answer**) |
| SPA | 192.168.1.20 | Host B's IP |
| THA | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | Copied from request's SHA |
| TPA | 192.168.1.10 | Copied from request's SPA |

### **Step 6: Ethernet frame encapsulation for the Reply**

| Ethernet Field | Value |
|----------------|-------|
| Destination MAC | **AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA** (unicast to A) |
| Source MAC | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB |
| EtherType | 0x0806 |

### **Step 7: Host A receives the Reply & updates cache**

Host A's ARP Cache becomes:

| IP Address | MAC Address | Type | Age |
|------------|-------------|------|-----|
| 192.168.1.20 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB | Dynamic | 0 s (just learned) |

### **Step 8: Original IP packet is forwarded**

Host A now encapsulates the original IP packet in an Ethernet frame with **DST MAC = BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB** and transmits it. The switch uses its CAM table to forward the frame out the correct port.

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** During the ARP exchange, **Host B also learns Host A's MAC** (from the SHA field of the request), so it can send back unicast replies directly. **ARP is bidirectional in learning** ŌĆö both sides update their caches.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common ARP Field Value Misconceptions (Exam Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **This table is the single most-likely source of 1ŌĆō2 mark losses in KTU exams. Verify these.**

| Misconception | Correct Fact |
|---------------|--------------|
| "ARP Request has the target MAC filled in" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö THA is **00:00:00:00:00:00** in request |
| "ARP Reply is broadcast" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö Reply is **unicast** to the requester |
| "ARP is a Network Layer protocol" | ŌØī Ambiguous ŌĆö It operates at the **Layer 2/3 boundary**, encapsulated in Layer 2 frames |
| "ARP is used in IPv6" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö IPv6 uses **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** via ICMPv6 |
| "ARP table entries are permanent" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö Entries **expire** (typically 60ŌĆō1200 s) |
| "Gratuitous ARP is a request for someone's MAC" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö It is a **broadcast of one's OWN IP-to-MAC** mapping (used for IP conflict detection) |
| "Proxy ARP forwards IP packets" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö Proxy ARP only **answers ARP requests on behalf of another host**; the host still sends to the router's MAC |
| "RARP maps IP to MAC" | ŌØī WRONG ŌĆö RARP maps **MAC to IP** (the reverse) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARP Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

The following flowchart depicts the **complete ARP request-reply cycle** from the perspective of Host A trying to communicate with Host B.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Host A wants to send IP packet to 192.168.1.20] --> B{Check ARP Cache\nfor 192.168.1.20}
    B -- Entry Found\nValid and Fresh --> C[Use Cached MAC\nConstruct Ethernet Frame]
    C --> Z[Send Frame Directly\nUnicast to Target]
    B -- No Entry Found --> D[Construct ARP Request Packet\nSHA=A_MAC, SPA=192.168.1.10\nTHA=00:00:00:00:00:00, TPA=192.168.1.20\nOpcode=1]
    D --> E[Encapsulate in Ethernet Frame\nDST_MAC=FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF\nSRC_MAC=A_MAC\nEtherType=0x0806]
    E --> F[Broadcast to ALL Hosts\non Local Subnet]
    F --> G{Every Host Receives\nand Checks TPA}
    G -- TPA does not match\nMy IP --> H[Silently Discard\nARP Request]
    G -- TPA matches\nMy IP = 192.168.1.20 --> I[Host B Accepts\nand Constructs ARP Reply]
    H --> X[Stop - No Action]
    I --> J[Build ARP Reply\nSHA=B_MAC, SPA=192.168.1.20\nTHA=A_MAC, TPA=192.168.1.10\nOpcode=2]
    J --> K[Encapsulate in Ethernet Frame\nDST_MAC=A_MAC Unicast\nSRC_MAC=B_MAC]
    K --> L[Send Reply Directly to\nHost A as Unicast]
    L --> M[Host A Receives Reply\nExtract B_MAC from SHA]
    M --> N[Update ARP Cache\nIP=192.168.1.20 -> MAC=B_MAC]
    N --> O[Construct Original Ethernet Frame\nwith Correct DST_MAC=B_MAC]
    O --> P[Forward IP Packet to Host B\nvia Switch CAM Table Lookup]
    P --> Q[End: Successful Communication]
```

## 4.2 ARP Packet Structure Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This diagram presents the **28-byte ARP packet layout** as a visual block, highlighting each field's size and relative position.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARP_Packet["ARP Packet - Total 28 Bytes"]
        direction LR
        A1["HType\n2 Bytes\n0x0001 Ethernet"] --- A2["PType\n2 Bytes\n0x0800 IPv4"]
        A2 --- A3["HLen\n1 Byte\n6 for MAC"] --- A4["PLen\n1 Byte\n4 for IPv4"]
        A4 --- A5["Opcode\n2 Bytes\n1=Req, 2=Reply"]
        A5 --- A6["SHA\n6 Bytes\nSender MAC"]
        A6 --- A7["SPA\n4 Bytes\nSender IP"]
        A7 --- A8["THA\n6 Bytes\nTarget MAC\n00:00 in Req"]
        A8 --- A9["TPA\n4 Bytes\nTarget IP"]
    end
```

## 4.3 ARP Encapsulation in Ethernet Frame (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram illustrates the **complete encapsulation hierarchy** ŌĆö showing how an ARP packet is wrapped in an Ethernet frame on the wire.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Wire["On the Wire - Complete Frame 64-1518 Bytes"]
        direction TB
        B1["Preamble\n7 Bytes\n1010... Pattern"] --> B2["SFD\n1 Byte\n10101011"]
        B2 --> B3["Destination MAC\n6 Bytes\nFF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF for Req"]
        B3 --> B4["Source MAC\n6 Bytes\nSender MAC"]
        B4 --> B5["EtherType\n2 Bytes\n0x0806 = ARP"]
        B5 --> B6["ARP Packet Payload\n28 Bytes\nFull ARP Header + Addresses"]
        B6 --> B7["Padding\n0-18 Bytes\nTo Meet 64B Minimum"]
        B7 --> B8["FCS / CRC-32\n4 Bytes\nError Detection"]
    end
```

## 4.4 ARP Cache / ARP Table Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This diagram shows the **logical structure of an ARP cache entry** as stored in the host's RAM.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARPCache["ARP Cache Table in RAM"]
        direction TB
        E1["Entry 1:\nIP=192.168.1.1\nMAC=11:22:33:44:55:66\nType=Dynamic\nAge=15s"]
        E2["Entry 2:\nIP=192.168.1.20\nMAC=BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB\nType=Dynamic\nAge=0s Just Learned"]
        E3["Entry 3:\nIP=192.168.1.30\nMAC=Static_Entry_33\nType=Static Permanent\nAge=Infinite"]
        E4["Entry 4:\nIP=192.168.1.40\nMAC=99:88:77:66:55:44\nType=Dynamic\nAge=895s About to Expire"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Proxy ARP Operational Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates how a **router running Proxy ARP** answers ARP requests on behalf of a host on a different subnet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host A\n192.168.1.10\nWants to Reach 192.168.2.20] -- ARP Request\nBroadcast for 192.168.2.20 --> R[Router with Proxy ARP\nEnabled\nHas Route to 192.168.2.0/24]
    R -- Inspects Request --> R2{Is Target on\nLocal Subnet?}
    R2 -- No, on Remote --> R3[Router Replies with\nITS OWN MAC as\nTarget Hardware Address]
    R3 -- ARP Reply Unicast\nTHA=Router_MAC --> A2[Host A Updates Cache\n192.168.2.20 = Router_MAC]
    A2 -- Sends IP Packet\nto Router_MAC --> R4[Router Decapsulates\nLooks Up Routing Table\nForwards to Actual Host]
    R4 --> B[Host B\n192.168.2.20\nSees Packet via Router]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Proxy ARP effectively **tricks the sender** into thinking the remote host is on the same subnet. This is why **Proxy ARP can break subnet design principles** and is **disabled by default on most modern routers**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reference (as per PEEET528 syllabus):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** 2 questions ├Ś 3 marks = 6 marks ŌĆö *Direct conceptual/definition type*
> - **Part B (14 marks):** 2 questions (choice-based) ├Ś 14 marks = 28 marks ŌĆö *Deep analytical type with sub-parts*

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Define Address Resolution Protocol (ARP). What is the size of an ARP packet when used with IPv4 over Ethernet?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is a protocol used to **map a 32-bit logical IP address to its corresponding 48-bit physical MAC address** on a local area network (LAN). It operates at the boundary of the Data Link and Network layers and is defined in **RFC 826**.
>
> The size of an ARP packet for IPv4 over Ethernet is calculated as:
>
> ARP Packet Size = 2 (HType) + 2 (PType) + 1 (HLen) + 1 (PLen) + 2 (Opcode) + 6 (SHA) + 4 (SPA) + 6 (THA) + 4 (TPA) = **28 bytes**.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: Differentiate between ARP Request and ARP Reply in terms of addressing (broadcast/unicast) and Opcode values.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ARP Request | ARP Reply |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Opcode** | 1 | 2 |
| **Destination MAC (Ethernet)** | FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (**Broadcast**) | Unicast (sender's MAC from request) |
| **Target Hardware Address (THA)** | 00:00:00:00:00:00 (unknown) | Filled with original requester's MAC |
| **Direction** | One-to-all | One-to-one (direct) |

> The request uses **broadcast** because the sender does not know the target's MAC; the reply uses **unicast** because the requester's MAC is now known from the SHA field of the request.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP ŌĆō Choice A]`

**Q: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP). Discuss the ARP packet format in detail. Also explain the difference between ARP and RARP.**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks: ARP Operation & Diagram**

**Model Answer:**

ARP is used when a host needs to send an IP packet to another host on the **same physical network** (same subnet), but the host only knows the destination's **IP address** and not its **MAC address**.

**Step-by-step operation (with reference to the diagram in Section 4.1):**

1. **Host A** (IP = 192.168.1.10) wants to send a packet to **Host B** (IP = 192.168.1.20). Host A checks its ARP cache. No entry found.

2. Host A constructs an **ARP Request** with:
   - SHA = AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA, SPA = 192.168.1.10
   - THA = 00:00:00:00:00:00, TPA = 192.168.1.20, Opcode = 1.

3. The ARP Request is encapsulated in an **Ethernet broadcast frame** (DST MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) and sent out on the LAN.

4. All hosts on the LAN receive the broadcast. Each compares the TPA field with its own IP. Only **Host B** matches.

5. Host B constructs an **ARP Reply** containing its MAC address and sends it **unicast** back to Host A.

6. Host A receives the reply, updates its ARP cache with the new IP-to-MAC mapping, and sends the original IP packet encapsulated with the correct destination MAC.

> **Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** for the complete flowchart.

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks: ARP Packet Format & ARP vs RARP**

**ARP Packet Format (28 bytes ŌĆö refer to Section 4.2):**

The ARP packet consists of 9 fields as detailed in the table in Section 2.1 above. Key fields include the **Operation Code (1=Request, 2=Reply)**, **Sender Hardware/Protocol Address**, and **Target Hardware/Protocol Address**.

**ARP vs RARP ŌĆö Comparison Table:**

| Feature | ARP (RFC 826) | RARP (RFC 903) |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| **Mapping Direction** | IP ŌåÆ MAC | MAC ŌåÆ IP |
| **When Used** | Normal data communication | **Diskless clients at boot time** |
| **Server Required** | No (peer-to-peer) | Yes (RARP server needed) |
| **Modern Replacement** | Still in use for IPv4 | Replaced by **BOOTP / DHCP** |
| **Broadcast Required** | Request: Yes; Reply: No | Request: Yes; Reply: No |
| **Layer** | Layer 2/3 | Layer 2/3 |

> **RARP Limitation:** RARP only provides IP address ŌĆö it cannot provide subnet mask, default gateway, or DNS servers. **DHCP** overcomes all these limitations, making RARP obsolete in modern networks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model QP ŌĆō Choice B]`

**Q: Explain the various types of ARP in detail. Discuss ARP Spoofing attacks and the security measures to prevent them.**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks: Types of ARP**

**Model Answer:**

There are several variants of ARP, each serving a specific purpose:

1. **Standard ARP (RFC 826):** The most common form, used to map an IP address to a MAC address on a local network via broadcast requests and unicast replies.

2. **RARP (Reverse ARP, RFC 903):** Maps a MAC address to an IP address. Used by diskless workstations that know their MAC (burned into NIC) but need an IP. Requires a RARP server on the LAN. Now obsolete ŌĆö replaced by **BOOTP** and then **DHCP**.

3. **Proxy ARP (RFC 1027):** A router (running Proxy ARP) responds to ARP requests on behalf of hosts on **other subnets**. It replies with **its own MAC address** as the target hardware address, causing the original sender to send the packet to the router, which then forwards it to the actual destination. Useful when a host is misconfigured and lacks subnet information.

4. **Gratuitous ARP (RFC 5227):** A host **broadcasts its own IP-to-MAC mapping** without being asked. Purposes:
   - Detect **IP address conflicts** (two hosts with same IP).
   - Update **switch MAC/CAM tables** (especially after VM migration or NIC failover).
   - Refresh other hosts' ARP caches when the sender's MAC changes.

5. **Inverse ARP (InARP, RFC 2390):** Used in **Frame Relay** networks to dynamically discover the IP address of the device at the other end of a virtual circuit (identified by a DLCI).

6. **ARP Spoofing / Poisoning:** A **malicious technique** where an attacker sends forged ARP replies to a victim's machine, associating the **attacker's MAC** with the **gateway's IP** (or any other IP). This redirects the victim's traffic through the attacker's machine, enabling **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks**, session hijacking, and credential theft.

7. **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol, RFC 4861):** The **IPv6 equivalent** of ARP. Uses **ICMPv6** messages (Router Solicitation, Router Advertisement, Neighbor Solicitation, Neighbor Advertisement) instead of broadcast. Provides additional features like stateless address autoconfiguration (SLAAC) and is inherently more secure when used with **SEND (Secure NDP)**.

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks: ARP Spoofing & Prevention**

**How ARP Spoofing Works (refer to the diagram in Section 4.5 for related concept):**

1. Attacker (Host X) sends **gratuitous ARP replies** to the victim (Host A), claiming: *"I am 192.168.1.1 (the gateway), my MAC is XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX."*
2. Host A **updates its ARP cache** with this false mapping.
3. All traffic from Host A destined for the gateway is sent to the **attacker's MAC address**.
4. The attacker can now **eavesdrop, modify, or drop** the traffic before forwarding it to the real gateway (a true MITM attack).

**Security Measures to Prevent ARP Spoofing:**

| # | Measure | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Static ARP Entries** | Manually configure ARP entries on critical hosts (`arp -s`). Not scalable. |
| 2 | **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** | Switch feature that validates ARP packets against a trusted DHCP snooping database. Drops invalid ARP packets. |
| 3 | **DHCP Snooping** | Builds a trusted MAC-IP binding table that DAI uses for validation. |
| 4 | **802.1X Port Authentication** | Authenticates devices before allowing them on the LAN. |
| 5 | **Encryption (HTTPS, SSH, VPN, IPsec)** | Even if traffic is intercepted, encryption prevents content reading. |
| 6 | **IPv6 + SEND** | Move to IPv6 with Secure Neighbor Discovery (cryptographic authentication). |
| 7 | **ARP Spoofing Detection Tools** | Software like `arpwatch`, `XArp`, `Wireshark` monitoring for unusual ARP activity. |

> **Real-world example:** In 2017, a major cryptojacking attack used ARP poisoning to inject mining scripts into web traffic on public Wi-Fi networks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on ARP questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Opcode values** ŌĆö Always write `Opcode = 1` for Request and `Opcode = 2` for Reply. Examiners explicitly check this.
>
> 2. **Incorrectly stating THA field** ŌĆö In a *request*, THA = **00:00:00:00:00:00** (since the MAC is unknown). Many students wrongly fill in a random MAC. **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Confusing ARP's OSI layer** ŌĆö ARP is **not purely Layer 2 or Layer 3**; it operates at the **boundary** (encapsulated in Layer 2, assists Layer 3). Writing "Layer 3" alone is incomplete. **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Wrong packet size** ŌĆö Writing "28 bits" instead of "**28 bytes**" is a common unit error. **ŌłÆ0.5 to ŌłÆ1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Missing the padding calculation** ŌĆö When asked "minimum frame size," students forget to compute **18 bytes of padding** to meet the 64-byte Ethernet minimum. **ŌłÆ2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning EtherType 0x0806** ŌĆö When describing ARP encapsulation, the **EtherType field = 0x0806** must be stated. **ŌłÆ0.5 mark**.
>
> 7. **Confusing Gratuitous ARP with normal ARP** ŌĆö Gratuitous ARP is **self-broadcast** (sender IP = target IP), used for IP conflict detection, not for resolving a remote host's MAC. **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
>
> 8. **Proxy ARP misconception** ŌĆö Students think Proxy ARP **forwards packets**. It only **answers ARP requests**; the actual packet forwarding is done by normal IP routing. **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
>
> 9. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** ŌĆö A diagram is **mandatory**. KTU examiners deduct **2ŌĆō3 marks** for missing or poorly labeled diagrams.
>
> 10. **Spelling "Protocal" instead of "Protocol"** ŌĆö Examiners note spelling errors in technical terms. Always write **"Protocol"** correctly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Quick-Revision Bullet Points ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam:**

- Ō£ģ ARP stands for **Address Resolution Protocol**, defined in **RFC 826** (1982, David C. Plummer).
- Ō£ģ ARP maps a **32-bit IPv4 address ŌåÆ 48-bit MAC address** on a local network.
- Ō£ģ ARP is encapsulated in **Ethernet frames with EtherType = 0x0806**.
- Ō£ģ ARP packet size = **28 bytes** (for IPv4 over Ethernet).
- Ō£ģ ARP Request: **Opcode = 1**, **Broadcast** (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF), **THA = 00:00:00:00:00:00**.
- Ō£ģ ARP Reply: **Opcode = 2**, **Unicast**, THA filled with requester's MAC.
- Ō£ģ **RARP** maps MAC ŌåÆ IP (reverse direction, used by diskless clients, now replaced by DHCP).
- Ō£ģ **Proxy ARP**: Router answers ARP requests for hosts on **remote subnets** using **its own MAC**.
- Ō£ģ **Gratuitous ARP**: A host broadcasts its **own** IP-to-MAC mapping (sender IP = target IP); used for **IP conflict detection** and **VM migration updates**.
- Ō£ģ ARP is a **Layer 2/3 boundary protocol** ŌĆö it is **not a pure Network Layer** protocol.
- Ō£ģ ARP cache entries **expire** (typically **60ŌĆō1200 s**) ŌĆö they are not permanent.
- Ō£ģ **ARP Spoofing** is a security attack (MITM) where attacker sends fake ARP replies to redirect traffic.
- Ō£ģ **Defense against ARP Spoofing:** **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, **DHCP Snooping**, **Static ARP entries**, **Encryption (VPN/HTTPS)**.
- Ō£ģ ARP is **replaced by NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** in **IPv6** (uses ICMPv6).
- Ō£ģ Minimum Ethernet frame size = **64 bytes** (excluding preamble + SFD); padding is added if payload < 46 bytes.
- Ō£ģ Ethernet maximum frame size = **1518 bytes** (excluding preamble + SFD).
- Ō£ģ Hardware Type = **1** (Ethernet); Protocol Type = **0x0800** (IPv4).
- Ō£ģ ARP is **broadcast-based**, which causes scalability issues in large LANs ŌåÆ mitigated by **VLANs** and **IP subnetting**.
- Ō£ģ Commands to view ARP cache: **`arp -a`** (Windows/Linux), **`ip neigh`** (Linux), **`show ip arp`** (Cisco IOS).
- Ō£ģ **Inverse ARP (InARP)** is used in **Frame Relay** networks to map **DLCI ŌåÆ IP**.
- Ō£ģ **RARP has been replaced** by **BOOTP** (RFC 951) and then **DHCP** (RFC 2131/2132), which provide IP, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS in one step.

> **End of Module 3 ŌĆö Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) Notes** ­¤Ä»
