<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 ŌĆö DYNAMIC HOST CONFIGURATION PROTOCOL (DHCP) & OPEN SHORTEST PATH FIRST (OSPF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This combined-topic note covers **DHCP (Application Layer ŌĆō RFC 2131/2132)** and **OSPF (Network Layer ŌĆō RFC 2328)**, two of the most frequently asked topics in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) of the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)**. Master the DORA process and the Link-State / Dijkstra logic ŌĆö they account for roughly **18ŌĆō22 %** of the Module-3 paper weightage.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)

**Formal Definition (KTU/Forouzan/RFC 2131 standard):**
> *Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) is a **clientŌĆōserver, connectionless Application-Layer** protocol that **automatically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters** (subnet mask, default gateway, DNS server, lease time) to DHCP-enabled hosts on an IP network. It is built on top of **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** and operates on **port 67 (server)** and **port 68 (client)**.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** DHCP is an *extension and successor* of the older **BOOTP (Bootstrap Protocol)**. The key addition is the concept of a **lease** ŌĆö an IP address is allocated for a finite time (e.g., **3600 seconds / 1 hour**, **86400 seconds / 1 day**, or **604800 seconds / 1 week**), after which the client must renew it.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a **5-star hotel front-desk**:
- A guest (DHCP **client**) walks in, has no room number (no IP), and asks the receptionist (DHCP **server**): "I need a room, Wi-Fi password, and breakfast coupon."
- The receptionist checks available rooms from a **pool (address pool/scope)**, hands the guest a **room card (IP address)**, a **Wi-Fi slip (subnet mask/gateway/DNS)** and a **check-out time (lease time)**.
- The guest can **renew** before check-out, otherwise the room goes back to the pool and is offered to the next guest.
- A **relay agent** is like the **intercom at the lobby** ŌĆö it forwards the guest's request to the main receptionist in another building (another subnet), because the local desk doesn't have a server.

### 1.2 Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)

**Formal Definition (KTU/RFC 2328 standard):**
> *Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) is a **link-state, classless, hierarchical Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP)** that uses **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** to compute the lowest-cost path to every destination in the **Autonomous System (AS)**. It is defined in **RFC 2328** for IPv4 and **RFC 5340 (OSPFv3)** for IPv6, operates directly over **IP protocol number 89** (not UDP/TCP), and is **open** because its specification is publicly available (vs. the proprietary EIGRP).*

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a **city bus system**. Every bus depot (router) maintains its own **live city map (Link-State Database / LSDB)** showing:
- Which other depots it can talk to directly (**neighbors**).
- The "cost" of each road segment (interface cost = reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth, default reference = **10ŌüĖ bps = 100 Mbps**).
Using this map, each depot independently runs the **Google Maps algorithm (Dijkstra's SPF)** to find the cheapest route to *every* other depot. Because all depots share the same map, **convergence is fast** (seconds, not minutes) and **no routing loops** occur.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Memory Hook:**
> **D**HCP = **D**ynamic **A**ddress = UDP **67/68**, **O**SPF = **O**ptimal **S**hortest **P**ath = IP proto **89**.

### 1.3 Why these two protocols matter in KTU exams

| Feature | DHCP | OSPF |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Application (sits on UDP) | Network (sits on IP) |
| Protocol Number / Port | UDP **67** (server), **68** (client) | IP Protocol **89** |
| Algorithm / Model | Client-Server with **DORA** (Discover-Offer-Request-Ack) | **Dijkstra SPF** on a synchronized **LSDB** |
| Type | Address-allocation protocol | **Link-State** routing protocol |
| RFC | **2131** (DHCP), 2132 (Options) | **2328** (OSPFv2) |
| KTU Mark Weightage | 3ŌĆō4 marks typical | 10ŌĆō14 marks typical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 DHCP ŌĆö The Four-Message DORA Process

When a DHCP client boots up, it follows a strict **4-step handshake** (the KTU favourite):

### Step 1 ŌĆö **DHCPDISCOVER** (Client ŌåÆ Server, Broadcast)
- The client, having no IP, sends a broadcast packet with source IP = **0.0.0.0** and destination IP = **255.255.255.255** (limited broadcast).
- UDP source port = **68**, destination port = **67**.
- Contains the client's **MAC address (chaddr)** and a **Transaction ID (xid)** ŌĆö a random 32-bit number that matches the *offer* to *its* request.

### Step 2 ŌĆö **DHCPOFFER** (Server ŌåÆ Client, Broadcast/Unicast)
- Every DHCP server that receives the Discover picks an IP from its **address pool (scope)** and unicasts/broadcasts an Offer.
- Carries the offered IP, **lease time (yiaddr)**, subnet mask, default gateway (option 3), DNS (option 6), and the *same* transaction ID.

### Step 3 ŌĆö **DHCPREQUEST** (Client ŌåÆ Server, Broadcast)
- The client (typically) accepts the **first** offer received (it does **not** wait for all servers).
- Broadcasts a Request so that *all* servers know which offer was accepted ŌĆö the rejected servers return the IP to their pool.

### Step 4 ŌĆö **DHCPACK** (Server ŌåÆ Client, Broadcast/Unicast)
- The chosen server formally acknowledges the lease and sends the final configuration.
- If the server cannot honour the request (e.g., address already taken by manual config), it sends a **DHCPNAK**, forcing the client to restart the process.

### Lease Renewal (Two Stages ŌĆö High Yield!)

- **T1 Renewal (50 % of lease):** The client **unicasts** a DHCPREQUEST to the *original* leasing server. If no reply ŌåÆ T2 trigger.
- **T2 Rebinding (87.5 % of lease):** The client **broadcasts** a DHCPREQUEST to *any* available server. If still no reply ŌåÆ lease expires, client must release the IP and restart DORA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick numeric:** For a lease of **8 hours**, T1 = **4 hours**, T2 = **7 hours**. For **7 days** lease, T1 = **3.5 days**, T2 = **6.125 days**.

### DHCP Message Format (RFC 2131 ŌĆö 236 bytes, all 32-bit aligned)

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **op** | 1 | 1 = BOOTREQUEST (client), 2 = BOOTREPLY (server) |
| **htype** | 1 | Hardware address type (1 = Ethernet) |
| **hlen** | 1 | Hardware address length (6 for MAC) |
| **hops** | 1 | Set to 0 by client; incremented by relay agents |
| **xid** | 4 | Transaction ID ŌĆö random number matched across the 4 messages |
| **secs** | 2 | Seconds elapsed since client began the process |
| **flags** | 2 | Bit 15 = Broadcast flag |
| **ciaddr** | 4 | Client IP (only filled if client already has a valid lease) |
| **yiaddr** | 4 | "Your" IP ŌĆö the address being offered/assigned |
| **siaddr** | 4 | IP of next bootstrap server |
| **giaddr** | 4 | Relay agent IP (used when crossing subnets) |
| **chaddr** | 16 | Client hardware (MAC) address |
| **sname** | 64 | Optional server hostname |
| **file** | 128 | Boot filename |
| **options** | variable | Magic cookie (0x63825363) + option code (53 = DHCP message type) + payload |

### DHCP Relay Agent (Cisco ŌĆö `ip helper-address`)
When the DHCP server is on a *different* subnet, the **local router** acts as a relay agent: it converts the broadcast Discover into a **unicast packet** addressed to the configured DHCP server IP (using the **giaddr** field), and broadcasts the server's reply on the local LAN.

---

## 2.2 OSPF ŌĆö Deep Theory

### OSPF Features (must know list)
1. **Link-State** algorithm ŌĆö every router builds a complete **Link-State Database (LSDB)**.
2. Uses **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** (a.k.a. SPF tree / shortest-path tree).
3. **Classless** ŌĆö supports **VLSM** and **CIDR**.
4. **Cost metric** = Reference Bandwidth / Interface Bandwidth. Default reference = **10ŌüĖ = 100 Mbps** ŌćÆ a 100 Mbps link has cost **1**, 1 Gbps = **0.1** (rounded to **1** with cost > 1 only when ref > BW).
5. **Hierarchical** ŌĆö divides AS into **Areas** to limit LSDB size and SPF recalculation.
6. **Convergence** in seconds (vs. minutes for RIP).
7. **Authentication** supported (plain text, MD5, SHA ŌĆö RFC 7474).
8. **Multicast updates** to **224.0.0.5 (AllSPFRouters)** and **224.0.0.6 (AllDRouters)**.
9. **Load balancing** with **Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP)** up to 4 (default) or 16 (configurable) paths.

### The 5 OSPF Packet Types (RFC 2328)

| Type | Code | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0x01 | **Hello** | Discover / maintain neighbour adjacencies; carries dead-interval |
| 2 | 0x02 | **DBD** (Database Description) | Summarise LSDB contents during exchange |
| 3 | 0x03 | **LSR** (Link-State Request) | Request specific LSAs the receiver is missing |
| 4 | 0x04 | **LSU** (Link-State Update) | Carry the requested LSAs (one or more) |
| 5 | 0x05 | **LSAck** (Link-State Acknowledgement) | Reliable ack of every LSA received |

### OSPF LSA Types (KTU favourite table)

| Type | Name | Generator | Flooded Within | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Router LSA** | Every router | Within its area | Describes router's links and their costs |
| **2** | **Network LSA** | **DR** (Designated Router) | Within its area | Lists all routers attached to the transit network |
| **3** | **Summary LSA** | **ABR** (Area Border Router) | Between areas | Inter-area prefixes (replaced by Type 5 in OSPFv3 stub tweaks) |
| **4** | **ASBR Summary LSA** | ABR | Throughout AS | Locates the ASBR that injected an external route |
| **5** | **AS External LSA** | **ASBR** (AS Boundary Router) | Throughout AS (except stub/totally-stubby) | Routes redistributed from other protocols (RIP, BGP, static) |
| **6** | Multicast OSPF (MOSPF) | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | Used for multicast (rarely deployed) |
| **7** | **NSSA External LSA** | ASBR in NSSA | Only inside NSSA | Converted to Type 5 by the ABR |

### OSPF Area Types

| Area Type | Accepts Type 3? | Accepts Type 5? | Default Route Injected? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Backbone (Area 0)** | Yes | Yes | No | Core transit area ŌĆö all other areas must connect to it |
| **Standard / Normal** | Yes | Yes | No | General multi-area deployment |
| **Stub** | No | No | Yes (3) | Reduces LSDB; replaces external routes with default |
| **Totally Stubby (Cisco)** | No | No | Yes (3) | Even Type 3 is replaced; smallest LSDB |
| **NSSA** (Not-So-Stubby) | Yes | No (Type 7 internally) | ŌĆö | Allows ASBR to inject external routes via Type 7 |
| **Totally NSSA** | No | No (Type 7) | Yes | Combines NSSA + Totally-Stubby |

### Special Roles
- **DR (Designated Router)** & **BDR (Backup DR)** ŌĆö elected on *multi-access* segments (Ethernet) using the highest **OSPF priority (0ŌĆō255)**, tie-broken by highest **Router-ID (RID)**. RID format = 32-bit IP (typically the highest loopback IP, or manually set).
- **ABR** ŌĆö router with interfaces in **two or more areas**, one being Area 0.
- **ASBR** ŌĆö router injecting routes from outside the OSPF domain (e.g., redistributing static, BGP, or RIP).

### The 7-State Neighbour Adjacency FSM (Foundry / KTU standard)

> `Down` ŌåÆ `Attempt` (NBMA only) ŌåÆ `Init` ŌåÆ `2-Way` ŌåÆ `Exstart` ŌåÆ `Exchange` ŌåÆ `Loading` ŌåÆ `Full`

- **2-Way**: bi-directional Hello seen; for *point-to-point* links, adjacency proceeds; for *multi-access*, only DR/BDR progress.
- **Exstart**: master/slave election via the highest RID.
- **Exchange**: DBD packets exchanged ŌĆö lists of LSA headers.
- **Loading**: LSR / LSU / LSAck exchange fills the gaps.
- **Full**: LSDBs are identical ŌĆö the adjacency is up.

---

## 2.3 The Dijkstra / SPF Algorithm ŌĆö KTU Formula Sheet

Dijkstra's algorithm computes the *lowest cumulative cost* from a source node to **every other node** in a graph whose edges have non-negative weights.

### Pseudocode (used in OSPF SPF)
```
Initialize:
  for each vertex v:
      dist[v] = INFINITY
      prev[v] = NIL
  dist[source] = 0
  Q = set of all vertices in priority queue (ordered by dist)

While Q is not empty:
  u = vertex in Q with minimum dist[u]
  remove u from Q
  for each neighbour v of u:
      alt = dist[u] + cost(u, v)
      if alt < dist[v]:
          dist[v] = alt
          prev[v] = u
```

### KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **OSPF Interface Cost** | $Cost = \left\lfloor \dfrac{Reference\_BW}{Interface\_BW} \right\rfloor$ | Reference = $10^{8}$ bps = **100 Mbps** (Cisco default) |
| 2 | **Total Path Cost** | $Cost_{path} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Cost(edge_{i})$ | Sum of all outgoing interface costs along the route |
| 3 | **Dijkstra Relaxation** | $dist[v] = \min\{dist[v],\, dist[u] + w(u,v)\}$ | $w(u,v)$ is the cost of edge from $u$ to $v$ |
| 4 | **T1 Renewal Timer** | $T1 = 0.50 \times Lease\_Time$ | Seconds |
| 5 | **T2 Rebinding Timer** | $T2 = 0.875 \times Lease\_Time$ | Seconds |
| 6 | **DHCP Lease Pool Size** | $Pool = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (End_{i} - Start_{i} + 1) - Exclusions$ | IPs |
| 7 | **Subnet Hosts Available** | $Hosts = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = host bits (minus network & broadcast) |
| 8 | **Dijkstra Complexity** | $O((V + E) \log V)$ with binary heap | $V$ vertices, $E$ edges |
| 9 | **LSA Flood Reliability** | Every LSA explicitly acknowledged | Each LSU contains LSAs; each LSA needs LSAck |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pipe-symbol pitfall in markdown tables** ŌĆö we have used $\vert$ and $\lfloor \rfloor$ to ensure the table is never broken by a raw `|`. Do not write `|x|` inside a row; always write $\vert x \vert$ instead.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **DHCP** is the backbone of Wi-Fi hotspots, university campus networks, and ISP customer-premises equipment (CPE). Without DHCP, every smartphone joining a network would require manual IP configuration.
- **OSPF** is the de-facto IGP of enterprise networks, ISP backbones, and data-centre fabrics. Its rapid convergence makes it ideal for **VoIP, video conferencing, and real-time trading** where downtime costs millions per second.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the *exact* workings of Dijkstra's algorithm on a small KTU-style graph and shows the corresponding OSPF SPF tree construction. **Every step is written out ŌĆö no shortcuts.**

---

## 3.1 Sample Graph (the KTU classic)

We have **5 routers** in an OSPF area with the following link costs:

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| A ŌĆö B | 4 |
| A ŌĆö C | 2 |
| A ŌĆö D | 5 |
| B ŌĆö C | 1 |
| B ŌĆö E | 6 |
| C ŌĆö D | 8 |
| C ŌĆö E | 10 |
| D ŌĆö E | 2 |

**Task:** Run Dijkstra's algorithm starting from **Source = A**. Show the iteration table and final shortest-path tree.

### Iteration Table (exhaustive walk-through)

We maintain three columns for each node $v$:
- $dist[v]$ ŌĆö current best known distance from A
- $prev[v]$ ŌĆö predecessor on the shortest path
- $visited$ ŌĆö already permanently settled

**Initialisation (Iteration 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dist[A] &= 0, \quad prev[A] = \text{NIL}, \quad visited[A] = \text{no} \\
dist[B] &= \infty, \quad prev[B] = \text{NIL} \\
dist[C] &= \infty, \quad prev[C] = \text{NIL} \\
dist[D] &= \infty, \quad prev[D] = \text{NIL} \\
dist[E] &= \infty, \quad prev[E] = \text{NIL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Priority Queue $Q = \{A(0), B(\infty), C(\infty), D(\infty), E(\infty)\}$**

---

#### Iteration 1 ŌĆö extract minimum = **A** (dist 0)
Visit neighbours of A: B, C, D.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relaxation for B:}\quad & alt = dist[A] + w(A,B) = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
                              & 4 < \infty \Rightarrow dist[B] = 4,\ prev[B] = A \\
\text{Relaxation for C:}\quad & alt = 0 + 2 = 2 \Rightarrow dist[C] = 2,\ prev[C] = A \\
\text{Relaxation for D:}\quad & alt = 0 + 5 = 5 \Rightarrow dist[D] = 5,\ prev[D] = A
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 1:** $Q = \{C(2), B(4), D(5), E(\infty)\}$, visited = {A}.

---

#### Iteration 2 ŌĆö extract minimum = **C** (dist 2)
Visit neighbours of C: A (visited), B, D, E.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax for B via C:}\quad & alt = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
                                & 3 < 4 \Rightarrow dist[B] = 3,\ prev[B] = C \\
\text{Relax for D via C:}\quad & alt = 2 + 8 = 10 \\
                                & 10 > 5 \Rightarrow \text{no change} \\
\text{Relax for E via C:}\quad & alt = 2 + 10 = 12 \Rightarrow dist[E] = 12,\ prev[E] = C
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 2:** $Q = \{B(3), D(5), E(12)\}$, visited = {A, C}.

---

#### Iteration 3 ŌĆö extract minimum = **B** (dist 3)
Visit neighbours of B: A (visited), C (visited), E.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax for E via B:}\quad & alt = 3 + 6 = 9 \\
                                & 9 < 12 \Rightarrow dist[E] = 9,\ prev[E] = B
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 3:** $Q = \{D(5), E(9)\}$, visited = {A, C, B}.

---

#### Iteration 4 ŌĆö extract minimum = **D** (dist 5)
Visit neighbours of D: A (visited), C (visited), E.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax for E via D:}\quad & alt = 5 + 2 = 7 \\
                                & 7 < 9 \Rightarrow dist[E] = 7,\ prev[E] = D
\end{aligned}
$$

**State after Iteration 4:** $Q = \{E(7)\}$, visited = {A, C, B, D}.

---

#### Iteration 5 ŌĆö extract minimum = **E** (dist 7)
No unvisited neighbours. Algorithm terminates.

---

### Final Result Table

| Node | Shortest Distance from A | Predecessor | Shortest Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | NIL | A |
| B | 3 | C | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | A | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 5 | A | A ŌåÆ D |
| E | 7 | D | A ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E |

### Mathematical Verification of the Path A ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}(A \to D) + \text{Cost}(D \to E) &= 5 + 2 = 7 \\
\text{Cost}(A \to C \to B \to E) &= 2 + 1 + 6 = 9 \quad \text{(longer ŌĆö correctly rejected)} \\
\text{Cost}(A \to C \to E) &= 2 + 10 = 12 \quad \text{(longer ŌĆö correctly rejected)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the SPF tree rooted at A has edges **AŌĆōC, AŌĆōD, CŌĆōB, DŌĆōE** with **total tree cost** $ = 2 + 5 + 1 + 2 = 10 $.

---

## 3.2 OSPF Cost Calculation ŌĆö Analytical Example

**Given:** A serial link runs at **1544 kbps** (T1). What is the OSPF cost using the Cisco default reference bandwidth of $10^8$ bps?

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{Reference\_BW}{Interface\_BW} \right\rfloor \\
     &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{100\,000\,000}{1\,544\,000} \right\rfloor \\
     &= \left\lfloor 64.76 \right\rfloor \\
     &= 64
\end{aligned}
$$

**Second case ŌĆö Gigabit Ethernet with default reference:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Cost &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{10^{8}}{10^{9}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 0.1 \right\rfloor = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OSPF Cost = 0 is the reason Cisco recommends bumping the reference bandwidth** to $10^{9}$ or $10^{10}$ on modern networks (via the command `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 10000` for 10 Gbps). Otherwise, OSPF treats a 1 Gbps and 10 Gbps link as equal!

---

## 3.3 DHCP Lease Arithmetic (exhaustive)

A campus DHCP server hands out IPs in the range **10.10.0.10 to 10.10.0.254** with a **lease time of 8 hours (28 800 s)**. Excluded addresses: **10.10.0.100 ŌĆō 10.10.0.110** (printers), **10.10.0.200** (VoIP server).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Total pool size

$$
\begin{aligned}
Pool_{raw} &= 254 - 10 + 1 = 245 \text{ IPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Subtract exclusions

$$
\begin{aligned}
Exclusions &= (110 - 100 + 1) + 1 = 11 + 1 = 12 \text{ IPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Final usable pool

$$
\begin{aligned}
Pool_{usable} &= 245 - 12 = 233 \text{ IP addresses}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Renewal timer (T1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T1 &= 0.50 \times 28800 = 14400 \text{ seconds} = 4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Rebinding timer (T2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T2 &= 0.875 \times 28800 = 25200 \text{ seconds} = 7 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At **T = 4 h** the client unicasts a DHCPREQUEST to the original server. If it gets a DHCPACK, the lease is renewed and T1/T2 are reset. If it gets a **DHCPNAK** or no reply by **T = 7 h**, the client rebinds to *any* server. If still no reply by **T = 8 h**, the lease expires and the client must begin a fresh DORA cycle.

## 4.1 DHCP DORA Process ŌĆö Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Client Power-On / DHCP Enabled]) --> NoIP{Client has valid IP?}
    NoIP -- "No" --> Disc[Send DHCPDISCOVER  src=0.0.0.0  dst=255.255.255.255  UDP 68 to 67]
    Disc --> Wait{Wait for DHCPOFFER}
    Wait -- "Received" --> Pick[Client picks FIRST offer]
    Wait -- "Timeout 4s retry up to 4 times" --> Disc
    Pick --> Req[Broadcast DHCPREQUEST  same xid  yiaddr=offered IP]
    Req --> WaitAck{Wait for DHCPACK}
    WaitAck -- "ACK received" --> Use[Client configures interface  starts T1 and T2 timers]
    WaitAck -- "NAK received" --> Disc
    Use --> UseNet([Host communicates on IP network])
    UseNet --> T1{Time reaches T1 = 0.5 x Lease}
    T1 -- "Yes" --> Unicast[Unicast DHCPREQUEST to original server]
    Unicast --> AckOk{DHCPACK received?}
    AckOk -- "Yes" --> Use
    AckOk -- "No  or  T2 reached" --> Rebind[Broadcast DHCPREQUEST to any server]
    Rebind --> AckOk2{DHCPACK received?}
    AckOk2 -- "Yes" --> Use
    AckOk2 -- "No  and  lease expired" --> Disc
```

    ABR2 --- BR2
    R2 --- ASBR1
    ASBR1 -. "Static / RIP / BGP" .-> External["EXTERNAL OSPF DOMAIN"]

## 4.4 OSPF 7-State Adjacency FSM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Down
    Down --> Attempt : NBMA  Hello sent manually
    Attempt --> Init : Hello received
    Init --> TwoWay : Own RID seen in neighbours Hello
    TwoWay --> Exstart : DR/BDR elected on MA  or P2P start
    Exstart --> Exchange : Master slave election
    Exchange --> Loading : DBD exchange complete  LSR sent
    Loading --> Full : All LSAs received and acked
    Full --> [*] : Stable adjacency
    TwoWay --> TwoWay : On multi-access  non DR/BDR stays here
    Full --> Down : Dead interval expires or interface down
```
