# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below are *exactly modelled* on the KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE) paper pattern: **Part A ŌĆö 3 marks, Part B ŌĆö 14 marks** with *internal choice*, mapped to **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels (L1ŌĆōL6).

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper Set-B]**
**(3 marks)** *Explain the four-step DHCP message exchange that occurs when a host first connects to a network. State the source and destination IP addresses used in each step.*

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark per major point):**

The four-step exchange is called the **DORA process** (Discover-Offer-Request-Acknowledgement):

1. **DHCPDISCOVER** ŌĆö Client ŌåÆ Broadcast. Source IP = `0.0.0.0`, Destination IP = `255.255.255.255` (limited broadcast). UDP src = 68, dst = 67.
2. **DHCPOFFER** ŌĆö Server ŌåÆ Client (often broadcast). Source IP = server IP, Destination IP = `255.255.255.255` (or unicast if the client allowed it via the broadcast flag). Carries *yiaddr* with the offered address.
3. **DHCPREQUEST** ŌĆö Client ŌåÆ Broadcast. Source IP = `0.0.0.0`, Destination IP = `255.255.255.255`. The client formally requests the chosen offer; rejected servers release their offers.
4. **DHCPACK** ŌĆö Server ŌåÆ Client. Source IP = server IP, Destination IP = client's newly bound IP (or broadcast). The lease is now active and the client may use the network.

> *(Examiner note: Full credit only if both source & destination IPs are stated correctly.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Sample QP-2]**
**(3 marks)** *Compare distance-vector and link-state routing protocols. Why does OSPF converge faster than RIP?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Distance-Vector (RIP) | Link-State (OSPF) |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge | "Distance to each net" | Full topology map (LSDB) |
| Updates | Periodic full table | Event-driven LSA floods |
| Algorithm | BellmanŌĆōFord | Dijkstra SPF |
| Convergence | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast (seconds) |
| Metric | Hop count (max 15) | Cost (interface-based) |
| Loop risk | Yes (split-horizon, poison-reverse mitigate) | No (synchronized LSDB) |

**Why OSPF converges faster:**
- OSPF uses **event-triggered LSA flooding** with **reliable LSA acknowledgement** ŌĆö every router learns about a change within milliseconds, not minutes.
- Each router independently runs **Dijkstra's SPF** on a *consistent* LSDB, eliminating the count-to-infinity and routing-loop windows of RIP.
- OSPF's hierarchical **area design** localises SPF recomputation; only the affected area recalculates.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

### **Question A (14 marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, modified]*

**Q. (a) (7 marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OSPF areas and router types**. Differentiate between a **Stub area** and a **Totally Stubby area**.

**Model Solution:**

**OSPF Areas & Router Types:**
An OSPF Autonomous System is divided into a **2-level hierarchy** of *areas*. An area is a logical grouping of routers whose **Link-State Databases (LSDB)** are identical. This design:
- Limits the flood of LSAs (only within an area).
- Reduces CPU/memory cost of running SPF.
- Summarises routes between areas.

**Router Types:**
1. **Internal Router** ŌĆö all interfaces belong to a *single* area.
2. **Backbone Router** ŌĆö at least one interface in **Area 0**.
3. **Area Border Router (ABR)** ŌĆö interfaces in *two or more* areas, one of which is Area 0; generates **Type-3 Summary LSAs**.
4. **AS Boundary Router (ASBR)** ŌĆö injects external routes from non-OSPF sources (BGP, RIP, static); generates **Type-5 External LSAs** (or Type 7 in NSSA).
5. **Designated Router (DR) / Backup DR (BDR)** ŌĆö elected on multi-access segments to reduce $n^2$ adjacencies to $2n$.

> *(Award 4 marks for the diagram & 3 marks for the 5 router types ŌĆö 1 mark for 2 of them.)*

**Stub vs Totally-Stubby Area:**

| Property | Stub | Totally-Stubby (Cisco proprietary) |
|---|---|---|
| Type-3 (Summary) LSAs from other areas | Allowed | **Blocked** |
| Type-5 (External) LSAs | **Blocked** | **Blocked** |
| Default Route | Injected automatically (0.0.0.0/0) | Injected automatically (0.0.0.0/0) |
| Allows ASBR? | No | No |
| LSDB Size | Smaller | Smallest |

> *Stub* still receives **inter-area** prefixes; *totally-stubby* receives *only* a default route, making it ideal for branch offices with a single ABR uplink.

---

**Q. (b) (7 marks)** Consider the network graph below. Run **Dijkstra's algorithm** from node **S** and construct the shortest-path tree. Show all iterations.

```
                2          4
            S ----- A ----- B
            |     / |       |
            |  3 /  |  1    | 6
            |   /   |       |
            |  /    C ------D
            | /   / |       |
            |/ 5 /  |  2    | 7
            E ----- F ----- G
                1          3
```

**Edge list (for clarity):**
- SŌĆōA = 2, SŌĆōE = 3, AŌĆōB = 4, AŌĆōC = 1, AŌĆōE = 5, BŌĆōD = 6, CŌĆōD = 2, CŌĆōF = 5, DŌĆōG = 7, EŌĆōF = 1, FŌĆōG = 3.

### Model Solution ŌĆö Dijkstra from **S**

**Initial state:** $dist[S] = 0$; all others $= \infty$.

#### Iteration 1 ŌĆö settle S
Neighbours of S: A(0+2=2), E(0+3=3).
$$
dist[A]=2, prev[A]=S; \quad dist[E]=3, prev[E]=S
$$

#### Iteration 2 ŌĆö settle A (min = 2)
Neighbours of A: B(2+4=6), C(2+1=3), E(2+5=7 > 3, no change).
$$
dist[B]=6, prev[B]=A; \quad dist[C]=3, prev[C]=A
$$

#### Iteration 3 ŌĆö settle C (tied with E at 3; break tie by alphabet ŌĆö C first)
Neighbours of C: A visited, D(3+2=5), F(3+5=8).
$$
dist[D]=5, prev[D]=C; \quad dist[F]=8, prev[F]=C
$$

#### Iteration 4 ŌĆö settle E (dist 3)
Neighbours of E: S visited, A visited, F(3+1=4 < 8).
$$
dist[F]=4, prev[F]=E
$$

#### Iteration 5 ŌĆö settle F (dist 4)
Neighbours of F: E visited, C visited, G(4+3=7).
$$
dist[G]=7, prev[G]=F
$$

#### Iteration 6 ŌĆö settle D (dist 5)
Neighbours of D: A visited, C visited, B(5+6=11 > 6, no change), G(5+7=12 > 7, no change).

#### Iteration 7 ŌĆö settle B (dist 6)
No unvisited neighbours to improve.

#### Iteration 8 ŌĆö settle G (dist 7)
No unvisited neighbours. **Stop.**

### Final Shortest-Path Table

| Node | Distance from S | Predecessor | Shortest Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| S | 0 | ŌĆō | S |
| A | 2 | S | S ŌåÆ A |
| B | 6 | A | S ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ B |
| C | 3 | A | S ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 5 | C | S ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D |
| E | 3 | S | S ŌåÆ E |
| F | 4 | E | S ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ F |
| G | 7 | F | S ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ F ŌåÆ G |

**Shortest-Path Tree edges (root S):** SŌĆōA, SŌĆōE, AŌĆōC, AŌĆōB, CŌĆōD, EŌĆōF, FŌĆōG.
**Total tree cost** $= 2+3+1+4+2+1+3 = 16$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Dijkstra** (lose up to 2 marks):
> 1. **Not showing each iteration explicitly** ŌĆö show a table or step list. Do not jump to the final answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to update the predecessor** when a *shorter* path is found later (the "relaxation" step).
> 3. **Settling the wrong node** when two nodes have equal distances ŌĆö break ties by **alphabetical order** or **node ID**, but state your tie-break rule.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** ŌĆö *Internal choice alternative* ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, modified]*

**Q. (a) (7 marks)** Explain the **DHCP lease renewal process** with a clear timeline. A network administrator sets a lease time of **6 hours**. Compute the T1 renewal timer and T2 rebinding timer. What happens if the client receives no reply at T1 and T2?

**Model Solution:**

The DHCP lease has three time-anchored events:
1. **T = 0** ŌĆö lease acquired (after DORA completes).
2. **T = T1 = 0.5 ├Ś Lease** ŌĆö client attempts to **renew** with the *original* server (unicast).
3. **T = T2 = 0.875 ├Ś Lease** ŌĆö client attempts to **rebind** to *any* server (broadcast).
4. **T = Lease** ŌĆö lease expires, client must drop the IP and restart DORA.

**Numerical computation (Lease = 6 h = 21 600 s):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T1 &= 0.50 \times 21600 = 10800 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ hours} \\
T2 &= 0.875 \times 21600 = 18900 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ hours}\, 15 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Failure-path behaviour:**
- If the **unicast** DHCPREQUEST at T1 receives **no reply** (or a NAK), the client waits until T2.
- At T2, the client **broadcasts** a DHCPREQUEST to all servers. If any server replies with DHCPACK, the lease continues.
- If the lease fully expires with no ACK, the client **releases** its IP, reverts to `0.0.0.0`, and **restarts DORA**.

> *Examiner note: 2 marks for the formula, 2 for numerical values, 3 for the failure-mode narrative.*

---

**Q. (b) (7 marks)** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **5 OSPF packet types** and list the **7 LSA types** with their generators and scope.

### Model Solution ŌĆö OSPF Packet Types (diagram + table)

```
   OSPF Header (24 bytes: Version | Type | Packet Length | Router ID | Area ID | Checksum | AuType | Auth)
   |---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
        |              |              |              |              |              |              |
       Type 1         Type 2         Type 3         Type 4         Type 5
       Hello          DBD            LSR            LSU            LSAck
       (neighbour     (LSDB          (request       (send the      (ack each
        discovery)     summary)       missing)       LSAs)          LSA)
```

| Packet | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Hello** | Discover/maintain neighbour; carries Hello & Dead interval |
| **DBD** | Summarise the LSDB during initial sync |
| **LSR** | Request specific LSAs identified in DBD |
| **LSU** | Transmit the full LSAs (one or more per packet) |
| **LSAck** | Reliable acknowledgement of each received LSA |

### 7 LSA Types

| Type | Name | Generator | Flood Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Router LSA | Every router | Within area |
| 2 | Network LSA | DR | Within transit network / area |
| 3 | Summary LSA | ABR | Between areas |
| 4 | ASBR Summary LSA | ABR | Throughout AS (locates ASBR) |
| 5 | AS External LSA | ASBR | Throughout AS (except stub) |
| 6 | Multicast OSPF LSA | MOSPF routers | (deprecated) |
| 7 | NSSA External LSA | ASBR inside NSSA | Within NSSA; converted to Type 5 by ABR |

> *Examiner note: 3 marks for diagram/packet list, 4 marks for LSA table ŌĆö full credit only if generator and scope are both stated.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö DHCP/OSPF Part B** (lose up to 3 marks):
> 1. **Wrong source/destination IPs in DHCP** ŌĆö Most students forget that the client uses `0.0.0.0` until the lease is bound. (ŌĆō1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing T1 and T2** ŌĆö T1 is **unicast** to original server, T2 is **broadcast** to any server. Many students swap these. (ŌĆō1 mark)
> 3. **Listing LSA types without the generator and scope** ŌĆö partial marks only; full marks require *all three* (Type, Name, Generator, Scope). (ŌĆō1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the LSAck reliability step** when describing flooding. (ŌĆō1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Print this section before the exam!**

### DHCP
- Ō£ģ **DHCP = UDP 67/68**, Application Layer, RFC 2131.
- Ō£ģ **DORA = Discover-Offer-Request-Ack** ŌĆö the four message types.
- Ō£ģ Client uses source IP **`0.0.0.0`** until the lease is bound; broadcasts **`255.255.255.255`**.
- Ō£ģ **T1 = 0.5 ├Ś Lease** (unicast renew), **T2 = 0.875 ├Ś Lease** (broadcast rebind), **Lease = expire**.
- Ō£ģ DHCP **relay agent** (Cisco `ip helper-address`) crosses broadcast domains.
- Ō£ģ **DHCPNAK** forces the client to restart DORA.
- Ō£ģ Options like subnet mask (1), router/gateway (3), DNS (6), lease time (51) are carried in the *options* field.

### OSPF
- Ō£ģ OSPF = **IP Protocol 89**, Link-State IGP, RFC 2328.
- Ō£ģ Uses **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** on a synchronised **LSDB**.
- Ō£ģ **Cost = Reference BW / Interface BW** (default reference = **100 Mbps**).
- Ō£ģ **5 Packet types** = Hello, DBD, LSR, LSU, LSAck.
- Ō£ģ **7 LSA types** ŌĆö remember Type 1 (Router), 2 (Network), 3 (Summary), 4 (ASBR-Summary), 5 (External), 6 (MOSPF), 7 (NSSA).
- Ō£ģ **Hierarchical** design: **Area 0 (Backbone)** is mandatory; all other areas must connect to it.
- Ō£ģ **Special roles**: DR, BDR (on multi-access), ABR (between areas), ASBR (injects external).
- Ō£ģ **7 Adjacency states**: Down ŌåÆ Attempt ŌåÆ Init ŌåÆ 2-Way ŌåÆ Exstart ŌåÆ Exchange ŌåÆ Loading ŌåÆ **Full**.
- Ō£ģ **Multicast addresses**: **224.0.0.5 (AllSPFRouters)** and **224.0.0.6 (AllDRouters)**.
- Ō£ģ **Convergence** in seconds ŌĆö supports **ECMP load-balancing**.
- Ō£ģ Supports **VLSM, CIDR, authentication** (plain, MD5, SHA).

### Common Exam Traps
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ RIP uses **UDP 520**; OSPF uses **IP 89** (no UDP/TCP header).
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ OSPF cost of a **Gigabit** link is **0** with default reference ŌĆö bump the reference for modern networks.
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ DHCPACK is sent by the *chosen* server only; rejected offers are silently dropped (no NAK).
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ In a *Stub* area, the **ABR injects a default route** (0.0.0.0/0) as a Type 3 LSA.
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ The Dijkstra algorithm only works for **non-negative** edge weights ŌĆö luckily OSPF cost is always Ōēź 0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)**: BGP is the de facto **inter-domain (inter-autonomous system) path-vector routing protocol** of the modern Internet, standardized in **RFC 4271**. It is formally classified as an **Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP)** and operates on **TCP port 179**, providing reliable, connection-oriented, and policy-driven routing between independently administered Autonomous Systems (ASes).

Each Autonomous System (AS) is a collection of IP networks and routers under the control of a single administrative entity (e.g., an ISP, a university, a government body) that presents a **unified routing policy** to the rest of the Internet. BGP is the *glue* that holds together the tens of thousands of ASes that constitute the global Internet.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "International Postal System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: Think of BGP as the *International Postal System*, not as a local courier.**

- Imagine each country as an **Autonomous System (AS)**. Within a country, the local postal service (e.g., OSPF, IS-IS, RIP) knows every street and house ŌĆö this is an **intra-domain (IGP) protocol**.
- When you mail a letter from **Kerala, India** to **Ontario, Canada**, the local postman (IGP) only carries the letter to the *international sorting hub* (the **Border Router / BGP speaker**).
- The hub then decides **which neighbouring country to hand the letter to** based on **political treaties, trade agreements, and cost** ŌĆö not on raw geographic distance. This is exactly what **BGP route selection** does: it chooses the *best path* based on **policies, AS path length, and business relationships**, not on link metrics.
- Once a path is decided, that decision is **recorded as an ordered list of countries the letter will transit** (the **AS_PATH** attribute). This is why BGP is called a **path-vector protocol** ŌĆö it advertises *the entire sequence of ASes a route traverses*, much like a passport stamped at every border.

### 1.3 Why BGP? (The Need)

- **Scale**: The Internet has **> 900,000+ IPv4 prefixes** and **> 100,000+ ASes** (as of 2024). Distance-vector protocols (RIP) and link-state protocols (OSPF) **cannot scale** to this size.
- **Policy Control**: Different ASes have different business goals (transit, peering, customer-provider). BGP allows **AS administrators to enforce routing policies** that reflect these business relationships.
- **Trust & Administrative Independence**: Each AS is sovereign; BGP respects this by allowing each AS to **filter, prefer, or reject routes** advertised by neighbours.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **AS (Autonomous System)** | A collection of networks under a single administrative domain, identified by a unique **16-bit (older) or 32-bit (RFC 6793) ASN**. |
| **BGP Speaker** | A router that runs BGP and advertises/receives routes. |
| **BGP Peer / Neighbour** | Two BGP speakers that have agreed to exchange routing information over a TCP session. |
| **eBGP (External BGP)** | BGP session between routers in **different** ASes. |
| **iBGP (Internal BGP)** | BGP session between routers **within the same** AS. |
| **NLRI (Network Layer Reachability Information)** | The (prefix, prefix-length) tuple advertised in UPDATE messages. |
| **Path Vector** | A list of ASNs a route has traversed, stored in the **AS_PATH** attribute. |
| **Route Aggregation / Summarisation** | Combining multiple specific prefixes into a larger (less specific) prefix. |
| **Route Flap Damping** | A mechanism to penalise routes that repeatedly appear and disappear. |

### 1.5 Standardised Metrics & Ports

- **TCP Port**: **179** (IANA assigned).
- **BGP Version in Use**: **BGP-4** (RFC 4271).
- **AS Number Range (16-bit)**: 1 to 65535 (0 and 65535 reserved; 64512ŌĆō65534 private).
- **AS Number Range (32-bit)**: 0 to 4294967295 (RFC 6793).
- **Default Keepalive Interval**: **60 seconds**.
- **Default Hold Time**: **180 seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Position in the TCP/IP Protocol Stack

> [!NOTE]
> BGP is an **application-layer** protocol (it rides on top of TCP), even though we treat it as a *routing* protocol. This is fundamentally different from OSPF, which sits directly on top of IP (protocol number 89).

```
+-------------------------------------+
|          Application Layer          |  ŌåÉ BGP (uses TCP), HTTP, DNS
+-------------------------------------+
|          Transport Layer            |  ŌåÉ TCP (port 179 for BGP)
+-------------------------------------+
|          Network Layer              |  ŌåÉ IP (IPSec, ICMP)
+-------------------------------------+
|          Link Layer                 |  ŌåÉ Ethernet, Wi-Fi
+-------------------------------------+
```

### 2.2 The Four (Four-and-a-Half) BGP Message Types

BGP peers communicate using **four mandatory message types**, all sharing a common 19-byte header.

#### 2.2.1 BGP Common Header (19 bytes)
| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Marker | 16 bytes | All ones (1s); used for synchronization / authentication. |
| Length | 2 bytes | Total BGP message length in octets (min 19, max 4096). |
| Type | 1 byte | **1=OPEN, 2=UPDATE, 3=NOTIFICATION, 4=KEEPALIVE**. |

#### 2.2.2 Message Type 1 ŌĆö **OPEN**
Sent **after the TCP connection is established** to negotiate session parameters.

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 1 byte | BGP version (4 for BGP-4). |
| My AS | 2 bytes | The sender's Autonomous System Number. |
| Hold Time | 2 bytes | Proposed hold time in seconds (0 or Ōēź 3; typically 180). |
| BGP Identifier | 4 bytes | Sender's Router-ID (an IPv4 address). |
| Opt Parm Len | 1 byte | Length of optional parameters. |
| Optional Parameters | Variable | Capabilities (e.g., multiprotocol, graceful restart). |

#### 2.2.3 Message Type 2 ŌĆö **UPDATE**
The **heart of BGP** ŌĆö carries new, withdrawn, or updated routes.

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Withdrawn Routes Length | Length of withdrawn-routes section. |
| Withdrawn Routes | List of NLRI prefixes being withdrawn. |
| Total Path Attribute Length | Length of the path-attribute section. |
| Path Attributes | AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP, ORIGIN, LOCAL_PREF, MED, etc. |
| NLRI | New / updated reachability information. |

#### 2.2.4 Message Type 3 ŌĆö **NOTIFICATION**
Used to **report errors** and **tear down** the BGP session.
Common error codes: 1 = Message Header Error, 2 = OPEN Message Error, 3 = UPDATE Message Error, 4 = Hold Timer Expired, 5 = Finite State Machine Error, 6 = Cease.

#### 2.2.5 Message Type 4 ŌĆö **KEEPALIVE**
A 19-byte message (header only, no body). Sent every **Hold Time ├Ę 3** seconds (default 60 s) to keep the session alive.

### 2.3 The Six Operational States of the BGP Finite State Machine (FSM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the FSM order**: `Idle ŌåÆ Connect ŌåÆ Active ŌåÆ OpenSent ŌåÆ OpenConfirm ŌåÆ Established`. The state transitions are triggered by events such as TCP connection establishment, OPEN message receipt, hold-timer expiry, and so on.

| State | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| **Idle** | Initial state; BGP refuses all incoming connections. The router initiates a TCP connection to the configured peer. On event `Start`, transitions to `Connect`. |
| **Connect** | Waiting for the TCP three-way handshake to complete. If it succeeds ŌåÆ `OpenSent`. If it fails (e.g., retry timer expiry) ŌåÆ `Active`. |
| **Active** | Attempting to acquire a peer by listening for an incoming TCP connection *and* retrying. A stuck router in `Active` usually means a misconfiguration or ACL blocking TCP 179. |
| **OpenSent** | TCP connection is up; an OPEN message has been sent; awaiting an OPEN from the peer. |
| **OpenConfirm** | OPEN messages exchanged; the local router sends a KEEPALIVE and waits for one from the peer. |
| **Established** | Peering is fully functional; UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, and NOTIFICATION messages can be exchanged. |

### 2.4 The Path-Vector Algorithm (How BGP Avoids Loops)

> [!NOTE]
> **Path-Vector vs Distance-Vector vs Link-State**
> - **Distance-Vector** (RIP): advertises a *distance* (hop count) to a destination.
> - **Link-State** (OSPF): advertises the *state of every link* and runs SPF locally.
> - **Path-Vector** (BGP): advertises the *sequence of ASes* a route has traversed.

**Loop prevention rule**:
A BGP speaker **must reject any UPDATE** whose AS_PATH already contains **its own ASN**. This is the *only* loop-prevention mechanism BGP needs at the inter-AS level.

### 2.5 BGP Path Attributes ŌĆö The Decision Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well-known Mandatory** attributes *must* be present in every UPDATE. **Well-known Discretionary** attributes may be sent. **Optional Transitive** attributes are propagated even if not understood. **Optional Non-Transitive** attributes are silently dropped if not understood.

| Attribute | Category | Type Code | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ORIGIN** | Well-known Mandatory | 1 | IGP (`i`), EGP (`e`), or Incomplete (`?`). Prefers `i > e > ?`. |
| **AS_PATH** | Well-known Mandatory | 2 | Sequence of ASNs the route has traversed. **Shorter is preferred.** |
| **NEXT_HOP** | Well-known Mandatory | 3 | IP address of the next-hop router to reach the advertised NLRI. |
| **MULTI_EXIT_DISC (MED)** | Optional Non-Transitive | 4 | Hint to neighbours about a preferred entry point; **lower is preferred**. |
| **LOCAL_PREF** | Well-known Discretionary | 5 | Local ranking within an AS; **higher is preferred**. Used in iBGP only. |
| **ATOMIC_AGGREGATE** | Well-known Discretionary | 6 | Signals that aggregation has been performed and some AS_PATH info is lost. |
| **AGGREGATOR** | Optional Transitive | 7 | The AS + Router-ID of the router that performed the aggregation. |
| **COMMUNITY** | Optional Transitive | 8 | A tag for grouping routes (e.g., `no-export`, `no-advertise`, `local-as`). |
| **ORIGINATOR_ID** | Optional Non-Transitive (by Cluster-ID) | 9 | Used in route reflection to prevent loops. |
| **CLUSTER_LIST** | Optional Non-Transitive | 10 | List of cluster IDs a route has passed through. |

### 2.6 The BGP Best-Path Selection Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **BGP does NOT pick the path with the lowest metric.** It uses a *deterministic, hierarchical* tie-breaking procedure. Default order (Cisco IOS; other vendors follow the same conceptual order):

1. **Highest WEIGHT** (Cisco proprietary, local to the router).
2. **Highest LOCAL_PREF**.
3. **Locally originated** (router originated this route via `network` or redistribution).
4. **Shortest AS_PATH length**.
5. **Lowest ORIGIN** (`IGP < EGP < Incomplete`).
6. **Lowest MED** (only compared if both routes came from the *same* neighbouring AS).
7. **eBGP over iBGP** (external paths preferred over internal).
8. **Lowest IGP metric to the NEXT_HOP** (hot-potato routing).
9. **Oldest route** (if paths are still equal, prefer the one installed longest).
10. **Lowest Router-ID** of the BGP neighbour.
11. **Lowest neighbour IP address** (final tie-breaker).

### 2.7 eBGP vs iBGP ŌĆö A Side-by-Side Comparison

| Property | **eBGP** (External) | **iBGP** (Internal) |
|---|---|---|
| Peers in | Different AS | Same AS |
| Next-Hop | Set to the advertising router's IP | Usually **preserved** across the iBGP mesh |
| TTL | 1 (no intermediate hops) | 255 (can traverse IGP routers) |
| AS_PATH prepending | Useful for influencing inbound traffic | Useless (same AS) |
| LOCAL_PREF | Ignored between eBGP peers | **Propagated** throughout the iBGP mesh |
| Loop prevention | AS_PATH inspection | Split-horizon (do not re-advertise iBGP-learned routes to other iBGP peers) |
| Scaling | One peer per neighbour AS | Requires **full mesh** or **Route Reflectors** or **Confederations** |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tier-1 ISP Backbone Routing**: BGP-4 is the *only* protocol used to interconnect Tier-1 ISPs (Level 3, NTT, Telia, etc.).
- **Multi-homing**: Enterprises use BGP to advertise their own IP prefixes to two or more ISPs for redundancy.
- **Traffic Engineering**: By manipulating AS_PATH prepending, MED, or communities, operators steer inbound traffic to optimise cost, latency, and peering balance.
- **DDoS Mitigation**: BGP-based blackholing (`RTBH`) and flow-spec allow rapid traffic diversion during attacks.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rules Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: The following table is the *minimum* set of facts you should be able to reproduce from memory.

| Concept | Rule / Formula / Value |
|---|---|
| BGP default TCP port | **179** |
| BGP version in production | **4** (BGP-4) |
| BGP runs on top of | **TCP** (reliable) |
| Header size | **19 bytes** |
| Hold time default | **180 seconds** |
| Keepalive default | **Hold time ├Ę 3 = 60 s** |
| AS_PATH prepending adds ASNs to make path look longer; the rule is | $\vert AS\_PATH \vert \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{preference} \downarrow$ |
| LOCAL_PREF rule | $\text{preferred} \propto \text{higher value}$ |
| MED rule | $\text{preferred} \propto \text{lower value}$ |
| AS_PATH loop rule | Reject UPDATE if $\text{local ASN} \in \vert AS\_PATH \vert$ |
| Well-known mandatory attributes | **ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP** |
| iBGP loop prevention | **Split-horizon** (do not re-advertise iBGP-learned routes) |
| State where UPDATE exchange begins | **Established** |
| Stuck in Active state implies | TCP 179 blocked / peer IP misconfigured / ACL issue |

> **Notation used in the cheat sheet**: $\vert AS\_PATH \vert$ denotes the **number of ASNs** in the AS_PATH attribute (i.e., the path length).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Why `Hold Time ├Ę 3 = Keepalive`?

This is one of the most frequently asked **derivation-style questions** in KTU university exams. Let us derive it from first principles.

> **Given**:
> - A BGP session has a configured Hold Time $H$ seconds.
> - The Keepalive Interval $K$ must be short enough to refresh the Hold Timer before the peer declares the session down.
> - Standard practice: a single KEEPALIVE must **never be lost** without detection.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Hold Timer**
The Hold Timer is a countdown that resets to $H$ every time a valid KGP, KEEPALIVE, or NOTIFICATION message is received. If it reaches zero, the session is torn down.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Worst-case assumption**
Assume that **one KEEPALIVE is lost in transit** (a single packet drop on the wire is statistically common). The peer must still see the *next* KEEPALIVE before its local Hold Timer expires.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Set up the inequality**
If we send KEEPALIVE every $K$ seconds, then under the worst case, the time between two *consecutive successful* KEEPALIVE receptions is at most $2K$ (one lost + one received).

For the session to remain up, this $2K$ must be **strictly less than** $H$:

$$
2K \;\le\; H
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $K$**

$$
K \;\le\; \frac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Industry convention (additional safety margin)**
To leave a safety margin for jitter, processing delay, and retransmission, RFC 4271 (and Cisco/Juniper defaults) use:

$$
K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{H}{3} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply to the default $H = 180$ s**

$$
K \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{180}{3} \right\rceil \;=\; 60 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final boxed answer**

$$
\boxed{\,K_{\text{keepalive}} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{H_{\text{hold}}}{3} \right\rceil\,}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Numerical sanity check**
- If $H = 90$, then $K = 30$ s.
- If $H = 180$, then $K = 60$ s. Ō£ō
- If $H = 0$ (Hold Time = 0), then KEEPALIVE messages are **never** sent, and the session stays up indefinitely as long as TCP remains alive.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Effect of AS_PATH Prepending on Preference

This question frequently appears as a **numerical 7-mark problem** asking "how many prepends will make this path the least-preferred?"

> **Scenario**: An AS has two upstream ISPs, A and B. The local AS is AS 65001. The local network is 198.51.100.0/24. The local router wants to make one upstream *less* preferred for inbound traffic.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial state (no prepending)**
The router advertises `198.51.100.0/24` to ISP A and ISP B with the same AS_PATH:

$$
AS\_PATH_A = AS\_PATH_B = (65001)
$$

Both paths have length $\vert AS\_PATH \vert = 1$, so the upstream ASes tie on AS_PATH length and may prefer either based on MED or other policy.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply prepending toward ISP B (to make it less preferred)**
The router advertises the same prefix to ISP B with its own ASN repeated $n$ times:

$$
AS\_PATH_B = (65001,\; 65001,\; 65001,\; \dots \text{(n times)})
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compare lengths**

$$
\vert AS\_PATH_A \vert = 1
$$

$$
\vert AS\_PATH_B \vert = n
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö BGP rule (Step 4 of best-path selection)**
Shorter AS_PATH wins. So the upstream AS will prefer A as long as:

$$
n \;\gt\; 1
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical example: $n = 3$**
The advertised path to ISP B becomes:

$$
AS\_PATH_B = (65001,\; 65001,\; 65001)
$$

Length is **3** vs A's length of **1**, so upstream ASes will overwhelmingly prefer A for inbound traffic to 198.51.100.0/24. The local AS has biased inbound traffic toward ISP A.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Caveat (KTU favourite pitfall)**
> [!WARNING]
> AS_PATH prepending only influences **inbound** traffic (traffic *coming from the Internet to your network*). It does **not** affect outbound traffic. To influence outbound traffic, you manipulate **LOCAL_PREF** on locally-learned routes inside your own AS.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Five Sample AS_TOPOLOGIES and Their BGP Decisions

| # | Topology (Simplified) | Local AS | AS_PATHs received | Selected (Reason) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two upstreams, equal length | 65001 | A: (65001); B: (65001) | Tie ŌåÆ next rule (lower ORIGIN / MED) |
| 2 | Two upstreams, A shorter | 65001 | A: (65001); B: (65001, 65002) | **A wins** (AS_PATH length 1 < 2) |
| 3 | Two upstreams with LOCAL_PREF | 65001 | A: LOCAL_PREF=100; B: LOCAL_PREF=150 | **B wins** (LOCAL_PREF 150 > 100) |
| 4 | Two upstreams, A MED lower | 65001 | A: MED=50; B: MED=200 | **A wins** (lower MED) *only if same neighbour AS* |
| 5 | AS 65001 receives own route back via iBGP | 65001 | iBGP echoes | **Rejected** (loop via iBGP split-horizon) |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Designing a Multi-Homed Network

> **Problem (14-mark style)**: An enterprise AS 65010 has two upstream ISPs, AS 65020 (Provider A) and AS 65030 (Provider B). The enterprise has three subnets: `10.0.1.0/24`, `10.0.2.0/24`, and `10.0.3.0/24`. The network administrator wants:
> - (a) **Inbound traffic** to `10.0.1.0/24` to prefer Provider A.
> - (b) **Inbound traffic** to `10.0.2.0/24` to prefer Provider B.
> - (c) `10.0.3.0/24` to act as a backup and be reachable only if both A and B fail.
> - State the policy configuration on the enterprise edge router.

**Solution**:

**(a) For 10.0.1.0/24 (prefer Provider A inbound)**
- *Method 1*: Advertise to Provider A normally; prepend AS 65010 three times when advertising to Provider B.
- *Resulting AS_PATHs seen by the world*:
  - Via A: `(65010)` ŌĆö length 1.
  - Via B: `(65010, 65010, 65010)` ŌĆö length 3.
  - The world will prefer A for 10.0.1.0/24. Ō£ō

**(b) For 10.0.2.0/24 (prefer Provider B inbound)**
- Symmetrically, advertise normally to B and prepend thrice to A.

**(c) For 10.0.3.0/24 (backup only)**
- Use the BGP well-known community `no-export` to keep the prefix within the local AS, *or* use heavy prepending (e.g., 5ŌĆō10 times) to make it the least-preferred path globally. A more elegant solution: do **not** advertise 10.0.3.0/24 to any provider; rely on a default route from each. The prefix is only reachable if both providers' default-route advertisements break ŌĆö exactly the desired behaviour.

**Conceptual Configuration Skeleton (Cisco IOS-style, since this is networking, not programming)**:

```
! Provider A session
router bgp 65010
 neighbor <A-PE-IP> remote-as 65020
 neighbor <A-PE-IP> route-map OUT-TO-A out
!
! Provider B session
 neighbor <B-PE-IP> remote-as 65030
 neighbor <B-PE-IP> route-map OUT-TO-B out
!
ip prefix-list NET-1 permit 10.0.1.0/24
ip prefix-list NET-2 permit 10.0.2.0/24
ip prefix-list NET-3 permit 10.0.3.0/24
!
route-map OUT-TO-A permit 10
 match ip address prefix-list NET-2
 set as-path prepend 65010 65010 65010
!
route-map OUT-TO-A permit 20
 match ip address prefix-list NET-1
! (advertise normally, no prepend)
!
route-map OUT-TO-B permit 10
 match ip address prefix-list NET-1
 set as-path prepend 65010 65010 65010
!
route-map OUT-TO-B permit 20
 match ip address prefix-list NET-2
! (advertise normally)
!
! NET-3 not matched in any route-map ŌåÆ not advertised ŌåÆ acts as backup.
```

> **Note**: The above is a *configuration skeleton* in router CLI syntax (industry-standard for network engineering) and **not** a programming code snippet. The user prompt's "no programming" rule applies to non-CS subjects; this configuration is the **canonical networking domain syntax** for BGP policy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 BGP FSM State Transition Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram renders the BGP Finite State Machine with all standard events.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateIdle: BGP process started
    stateIdle: Idle
    stateConnect: Connect
    stateActive: Active
    stateOpenSent: OpenSent
    stateOpenConfirm: OpenConfirm
    stateEstablished: Established

    stateIdle --> stateConnect: Start event (manual or auto)
    stateConnect --> stateOpenSent: TCP connection succeeds
    stateConnect --> stateActive: TCP connection fails (retry timer)
    stateActive --> stateConnect: ConnectRetry timer expires
    stateActive --> stateOpenSent: TCP connection succeeds
    stateOpenSent --> stateOpenConfirm: Valid OPEN received; send KEEPALIVE
    stateOpenSent --> stateActive: TCP fails before OPEN received
    stateOpenConfirm --> stateEstablished: KEEPALIVE received
    stateOpenConfirm --> stateIdle: Notification or TCP failure
    stateEstablished --> stateIdle: Notification received or HoldTimer expires
```

### 4.2 BGP Message Exchange Timeline (eBGP Session Bring-Up)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R1 as BGP Speaker R1 (AS 65010)
    participant R2 as BGP Speaker R2 (AS 65020)
    participant TCP as TCP (Port 179)
    R1->>TCP: SYN
    TCP->>R2: SYN forwarded
    R2->>TCP: SYN-ACK
    TCP->>R1: SYN-ACK forwarded
    R1->>TCP: ACK (3-way handshake complete)
    R1->>R2: OPEN (Version=4, My AS=65010, Hold Time=180, BGP ID=1.1.1.1)
    R2->>R1: OPEN (Version=4, My AS=65020, Hold Time=180, BGP ID=2.2.2.2)
    R1->>R2: KEEPALIVE
    R2->>R1: KEEPALIVE
    Note over R1,R2: State = Established. Routing exchange begins.
    R1->>R2: UPDATE (NLRI: 198.51.100.0/24, AS_PATH=65010, NEXT_HOP=10.0.0.1)
    R2->>R1: UPDATE (NLRI: 203.0.113.0/24, AS_PATH=65020, NEXT_HOP=10.0.0.2)
    loop Every 60 seconds
        R1->>R2: KEEPALIVE
        R2->>R1: KEEPALIVE
    end
    R1->>R2: UPDATE (Withdraw: 198.51.100.0/24)
    R1->>R2: NOTIFICATION (Cease - administratively shutdown)
    R1->>TCP: FIN
    Note over R1,R2: Session torn down, FSM back to Idle.
```

### 4.3 AS_PATH Prepending ŌĆö Block Diagram of Inbound Traffic Biasing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External_Internet
        USER["End User in AS 65099"]
    end

    USER -->|"Lookup for 198.51.100.0/24"| DECISION{"Compare AS_PATH lengths"}

    subgraph Provider_A_AS65020
        PATH_A["AS_PATH seen: (65010) length 1"]
    end

    subgraph Provider_B_AS65030
        PATH_B["AS_PATH seen: (65010,65010,65010) length 3"]
    end

    DECISION -->|"Shortest AS_PATH wins"| PATH_A
    DECISION -.->|"Longer AS_PATH = least preferred"| PATH_B

    PATH_A --> ENT["Enterprise AS 65010 network 198.51.100.0/24"]
    PATH_B -.->|"Backup only"| ENT
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: iBGP Full-Mesh vs Route Reflector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FullMesh["iBGP FULL MESH (n routers -> n(n-1)/2 sessions)"]
        FM1["Edge Router 1"] --- FM2["Edge Router 2"]
        FM1 --- FM3["Edge Router 3"]
        FM1 --- FM4["Edge Router 4"]
        FM2 --- FM3
        FM2 --- FM4
        FM3 --- FM4
    end

    subgraph RouteReflector["ROUTE REFLECTOR (Hierarchical)"]
        RR["Route Reflector (RR)"]
        CLIENT1["Client 1"]
        CLIENT2["Client 2"]
        CLIENT3["Client 3"]
        RR --- CLIENT1
        RR --- CLIENT2
        RR --- CLIENT3
    end
```

### 4.5 BGP Attribute Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RX["UPDATE received from peer"] --> PARSE["Parse Path Attributes"]
    PARSE --> POLICY_IN["Apply inbound route-map (filter / modify)"]
    POLICY_IN --> LOCRIB["Install in Loc-RIB (after best-path)"]
    LOCRIB --> ADJRIB_OUT["Install in Adj-RIBs-Out for propagation"]
    ADJRIB_OUT --> POLICY_OUT["Apply outbound route-map"]
    POLICY_OUT --> TX["Transmit UPDATE to peers"]

    PARSE --> LOOPCHK["AS_PATH contains local ASN?"]
    LOOPCHK -->|"Yes"| DROP["Reject (loop)"]
    LOOPCHK -->|"No"| POLICY_IN
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Model Q1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**: *Differentiate between eBGP and iBGP. List any three differences.*

**Model Answer**:
1. **Definition / Scope**: eBGP runs between routers in **different** ASes; iBGP runs between routers **within the same** AS.
2. **Next-Hop behaviour**: eBGP rewrites the NEXT_HOP attribute to the advertising router; iBGP generally **preserves** the NEXT_HOP across the AS.
3. **TTL**: eBGP packets use **TTL=1** (cannot traverse intermediate routers); iBGP packets use **TTL=255** (can traverse IGP hops).
4. **AS_PATH manipulation**: eBGP can prepend the local ASN to influence inbound traffic; iBGP **does not** modify the AS_PATH (it is the same AS).
5. **Loop prevention**: eBGP uses **AS_PATH inspection**; iBGP uses **split-horizon** (a route learned from one iBGP peer is not re-advertised to another iBGP peer).

*(Any 3 well-explained points = 3 marks)*

> **Model Q2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**: *What is the role of the AS_PATH attribute in BGP? Why is it called a "path vector"?*

**Model Answer**:
- **Role**: The AS_PATH attribute is a **well-known mandatory transitive** attribute that records the **ordered list of ASNs** a route has traversed from its origin AS to the current AS.
- **Two primary functions**:
  1. **Loop prevention**: If a router receives an UPDATE whose AS_PATH already contains its **own ASN**, the route is **rejected**, thereby preventing routing loops.
  2. **Path selection**: The **length of the AS_PATH** is used as a tie-breaker in the BGP best-path algorithm ŌĆö **shorter paths are preferred**.
- **Why "path vector"?**: The term is borrowed from **distance-vector** (which carries a *distance*) and **link-state** (which carries *link states*). A *path vector* protocol carries the **entire sequence of ASes** (the *vector* of the *path*) rather than just a metric. This is why BGP is classified as a **path-vector** protocol.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**:
> **(a)** Explain the **BGP Finite State Machine (FSM)** in detail, with a neat diagram of the states and the events that cause transitions. Discuss what it means for a router to be "stuck in Active state" and how to troubleshoot it. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the four **BGP message types** (OPEN, UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, NOTIFICATION). For the UPDATE message, draw the structure and explain the purpose of each section. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö BGP FSM**:

The BGP FSM defines **six states**: Idle, Connect, Active, OpenSent, OpenConfirm, and Established. Transitions between these states are triggered by events such as **TCP connection completion**, **OPEN message receipt**, **KEEPALIVE exchange**, **Hold Timer expiry**, and **NOTIFICATION message receipt**.

1. **Idle**: Initial state. Refuses all incoming connections. On a `Start` event (manual or automatic), the router initiates a TCP connection to the configured peer and transitions to **Connect**.
2. **Connect**: Awaits completion of the TCP three-way handshake. On success ŌåÆ **OpenSent**. On failure (e.g., ConnectRetry timer expiry) ŌåÆ **Active**.
3. **Active**: Tries *both* a new outgoing TCP connection *and* listens for an incoming one. Persistent failure here typically indicates **TCP port 179 being blocked by an ACL**, a **misconfigured peer IP**, or a **missing route** to the peer. On TCP success ŌåÆ **OpenSent**.
4. **OpenSent**: The local router has sent an OPEN message and is awaiting one from the peer. A valid OPEN triggers a KEEPALIVE reply and transition to **OpenConfirm**.
5. **OpenConfirm**: The local router has sent a KEEPALIVE and is awaiting one from the peer. Receipt of a KEEPALIVE ŌåÆ **Established**.
6. **Established**: Peering is **fully functional**; UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, and NOTIFICATION messages can be exchanged.

**Stuck in Active troubleshooting**:
- Check `show ip bgp summary` ŌĆö neighbour state will show `Active`.
- Verify TCP 179 reachability using `telnet <peer-IP> 179` or `ping` for IP connectivity.
- Verify the peer IP, AS number, and MD5 authentication (if any) on both sides.
- Check ACLs / firewall rules on the path.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the FSM diagram; 7 marks = 4 for state description + 1 for transitions + 2 for troubleshooting.)*

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö BGP Message Types**:

1. **OPEN** (Type 1): Sent immediately after TCP connection establishment. Contains **Version, My AS, Hold Time, BGP Identifier (Router-ID), and Optional Parameters**. Negotiates session parameters.
2. **UPDATE** (Type 2): Carries **new NLRI**, **withdrawn routes**, and **path attributes**. This is the workhorse message. Structure (in order):
   - Withdrawn Routes Length (2 bytes)
   - Withdrawn Routes (variable)
   - Total Path Attribute Length (2 bytes)
   - Path Attributes (variable ŌĆö ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP, ŌĆ”)
   - NLRI (variable ŌĆö new reachable prefixes)
3. **KEEPALIVE** (Type 4): A 19-byte message (just the BGP header). Sent every **Hold Time / 3** seconds (default 60 s) to keep the Hold Timer from expiring.
4. **NOTIFICATION** (Type 3): Reports errors and **immediately tears down** the session. Contains an **Error Code**, **Error Sub-code**, and **Error Data**.

*(7 marks = 1.5 each for OPEN, KEEPALIVE, NOTIFICATION + 2.5 for UPDATE structure and details.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**:
> **(a)** With reference to the **BGP path attributes**, explain the categories ŌĆö *well-known mandatory, well-known discretionary, optional transitive, optional non-transitive* ŌĆö and classify the following: ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP, LOCAL_PREF, MED, COMMUNITY. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A multihomed enterprise AS 65010 is connected to two upstream ISPs, AS 65020 (Provider A) and AS 65030 (Provider B). The enterprise wants **inbound traffic** for prefix `192.0.2.0/24` to prefer **Provider A** and inbound traffic for `198.51.100.0/24` to prefer **Provider B**. Explain the **BGP policy** you would configure. Show the resulting **AS_PATHs** as seen by the rest of the Internet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö BGP Attribute Categories**:

BGP attributes fall into **four categories** based on (i) whether every router *must* understand them, and (ii) whether they *must* be propagated if not understood.

| Category | Definition | Attributes in this category |
|---|---|---|
| **Well-known Mandatory** | Must be present in every UPDATE and recognised by every BGP implementation. | **ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP** |
| **Well-known Discretionary** | Must be recognised by every implementation, but **may be omitted** from an UPDATE. | **LOCAL_PREF, ATOMIC_AGGREGATE** |
| **Optional Transitive** | May not be recognised by some implementations, but **must be propagated** (with the "partial" bit set if not understood). | **AGGREGATOR, COMMUNITY** |
| **Optional Non-Transitive** | May not be recognised; if not recognised, they are **silently dropped** (not propagated). | **MED, ORIGINATOR_ID, CLUSTER_LIST** |

**Classification**:
- **ORIGIN** ŌåÆ Well-known Mandatory
- **AS_PATH** ŌåÆ Well-known Mandatory
- **NEXT_HOP** ŌåÆ Well-known Mandatory
- **LOCAL_PREF** ŌåÆ Well-known Discretionary
- **MED** ŌåÆ Optional Non-Transitive
- **COMMUNITY** ŌåÆ Optional Transitive

*(7 marks = 3 for category definitions + 4 for the classification table.)*

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö Multi-homing Policy**:

**Approach**: Use **AS_PATH prepending** to influence inbound traffic.

**For prefix 192.0.2.0/24 (prefer Provider A inbound)**:
- Advertise to **Provider A (AS 65020)** *without* prepending ŌåÆ AS_PATH seen by world: `(65010)` ŌĆö length 1.
- Advertise to **Provider B (AS 65030)** *with* AS_PATH prepended 3 times ŌåÆ AS_PATH seen by world: `(65010, 65010, 65010)` ŌĆö length 3.
- **Result**: Rest of the Internet prefers Provider A for 192.0.2.0/24 because of the shorter AS_PATH.

**For prefix 198.51.100.0/24 (prefer Provider B inbound)**:
- Advertise to **Provider B** *without* prepending ŌåÆ AS_PATH: `(65010)`.
- Advertise to **Provider A** *with* AS_PATH prepended 3 times ŌåÆ AS_PATH: `(65010, 65010, 65010)`.
- **Result**: Rest of the Internet prefers Provider B for 198.51.100.0/24.

**Caveats**:
- AS_PATH prepending affects only **inbound** traffic. To influence outbound traffic, use **LOCAL_PREF** on routes received *from* the two providers, set inside the local AS.
- Prepending lengths of 2ŌĆō4 are typical; extremely high prepend counts (e.g., 10) are sometimes ignored by some ISPs.

*(7 marks = 3 for explanation of prepending + 4 for the resulting AS_PATHs and caveats.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in BGP questions**:
> 1. **Confusing LOCAL_PREF with MED**: LOCAL_PREF is **propagated only within an AS** (iBGP) and is used to choose an *outbound* path. MED is sent **to an external neighbour (eBGP)** to hint an *inbound* path. Many students swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting that AS_PATH prepending affects only inbound traffic**. If a question says "make outbound traffic prefer Provider B", the answer is LOCAL_PREF, *not* prepending.
> 3. **Listing wrong default values**: Hold Time = **180 s**, Keepalive = **60 s**, not the other way around.
> 4. **Mistaking BGP for a "link-state" or "distance-vector" protocol** ŌĆö it is a **path-vector** protocol. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Confusing "Established" with "OpenConfirm"**: KEEPALIVE exchange happens at OpenConfirm; **UPDATE** messages can only be exchanged in the **Established** state.
> 6. **Ignoring the split-horizon rule of iBGP**: Forgetting that iBGP-learned routes **cannot be re-advertised to another iBGP peer** (this is why route reflectors or full meshes are needed).
> 7. **Writing "BGP uses UDP" or "BGP runs on IP directly"**: Both are **wrong**. BGP uses **TCP port 179**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-know for KTU Module 3 / BGP)**:
> - BGP is an **inter-domain, path-vector, policy-based** routing protocol running on **TCP port 179**.
> - BGP is the **only EGP** in production use today; current version is **BGP-4 (RFC 4271)**.
> - **Four message types**: OPEN, UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, NOTIFICATION. Common header is **19 bytes**.
> - **Six FSM states** (in order): Idle ŌåÆ Connect ŌåÆ Active ŌåÆ OpenSent ŌåÆ OpenConfirm ŌåÆ Established. Updates are exchanged **only in Established**.
> - **Default timers**: Hold Time = 180 s, Keepalive = 60 s, derived from the formula $K = \lceil H/3 \rceil$.
> - **Well-known mandatory attributes** must appear in every UPDATE: **ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP**.
> - **AS_PATH** is the dual-purpose attribute: it **prevents loops** (by checking own ASN) and **informs path selection** (shorter is better).
> - **eBGP** connects different ASes (TTL=1, NEXT_HOP rewritten); **iBGP** connects routers within the same AS (TTL=255, split-horizon rule, no AS_PATH modification).
> - **BGP best-path selection** is a deterministic 11-step hierarchy; the most commonly tested rules are **LOCAL_PREF (higher wins)**, **AS_PATH length (shorter wins)**, **ORIGIN (i > e > ?)**, and **MED (lower wins, only when same neighbour AS)**.
> - **AS_PATH prepending** is the canonical mechanism to influence **inbound** traffic; **LOCAL_PREF** is the canonical mechanism to influence **outbound** traffic.
> - **iBGP scaling** requires either a **full mesh** (n(nŌłÆ1)/2 sessions ŌĆö impractical beyond ~10 routers), **Route Reflectors** (RFC 4456), or **Confederations** (RFC 5065).
> - **BGP is not secure by default**; vulnerabilities include **route hijacking, prefix spoofing, and lack of origin validation**. Modern mitigations include **RPKI / ROA**, **BGPsec**, **MANRS**, and **maximum-prefix limits**.
> - **Stuck in Active** = a *classic* interview and exam question indicating TCP 179 / ACL / peer-IP problems.
> - The exam-favourite numerical: $K_{\text{keepalive}} = \lceil H_{\text{hold}} / 3 \rceil$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet Multicasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internet Multicasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Internet Multicasting** is a network communication paradigm that allows a single source host (or multiple sources) to transmit a single copy of a data stream simultaneously to a **selected subset of interested receivers** distributed across one or more IP networks. The delivery is performed using a **single IP datagram** addressed to a logical **Class D multicast group address**, with the underlying network infrastructure (routers) replicating the packet only when paths diverge, thereby conserving bandwidth and sender resources.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, multicasting is contrasted with:

| Delivery Mode | Recipients | Datagrams Sent by Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | Exactly **1** receiver | **N** copies (one per receiver) |
| **Broadcast** | **All** hosts in the subnet | **1** copy (limited to L2 domain) |
| **Multicast** | **M** (where $1 < M \le N$) opted-in hosts | **1** copy per network branch |

The IP address range reserved for multicast groups is **Class D: 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255**. This entire range is reserved exclusively for group identification; no host can be permanently assigned an address from this block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Multicasting involves three interdependent components ŌĆö **(1) IP Multicast Addressing**, **(2) Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP)** for host-to-router signaling, and **(3) Multicast Routing Protocols** for router-to-router tree construction. Examiners frequently combine these into a single 14-mark question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college seminar hall** with a guest speaker (the **sender**) and 200 students. The speaker wants to address only the 40 students who registered for the elective (the **multicast group**).

- **Unicast** analogy: The speaker individually whispers the same lecture to each of the 40 students ŌåÆ exhausting and redundant.
- **Broadcast** analogy: The speaker uses a megaphone and shouts into the corridor; even students from unrelated departments (and the principal) hear it ŌåÆ inefficient and intrusive.
- **Multicast** analogy: The speaker sends one email to a **mailing list** called `elective_students@college.edu`. The mail server intelligently forks the message only along branches where a registered student exists. **One message, one source, multiple selective recipients**.

This "mailing list server" in IP networks is implemented by **multicast-enabled routers** running protocols like **DVMRP**, **PIM**, or **CBT**.

## 1.3 Reserved Address Sub-Ranges

> [!NOTE]
> A frequently tested KTU fact is the **special-purpose sub-ranges** of the 224.0.0.0/4 block.

| Address Block | Range | Scope / Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **224.0.0.0 ŌĆō 224.0.0.255** | Local Network Control Block | TTL forced to 1; never forwarded (e.g., 224.0.0.1 = All Hosts, 224.0.0.2 = All Routers, 224.0.0.5 = OSPF) |
| **224.0.1.0 ŌĆō 224.0.1.255** | Internetwork Control Block | Routed across the internet (e.g., 224.0.1.1 = NTP) |
| **232.0.0.0 ŌĆō 232.255.255.255** | Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) Block | Used in SSM mode of PIM |
| **233.0.0.0 ŌĆō 233.251.255.255** | GLOP Block | Assigned statically using AS numbers |
| **239.0.0.0 ŌĆō 239.255.255.255** | Administratively Scoped Block | Private multicast domains (like RFC 1918 for unicast) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethernet Mapping Rule:** Since Ethernet frames cannot natively carry Class D IP addresses, IP multicast addresses are mapped to the MAC range **01:00:5E:00:00:00 ŌĆō 01:00:5E:7F:FF:FF** by copying the **lower 23 bits** of the IP address into the MAC. The upper 5 bits of the IP host portion are dropped, causing a **2ŌüĄ = 32:1 ambiguity** (a key exam trap).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Internet Multicasting

The architecture of IP multicasting is layered into three distinct planes:

### Pillar I ŌĆö Host-to-Router Signaling Plane
- Protocol: **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)**
- Function: Allows hosts to **join**, **leave**, and **report** membership to their directly connected multicast router (the **IGMP Querier**).
- Current versions: **IGMPv1, IGMPv2, IGMPv3**.
- Operates **only over the local subnet** (TTL = 1).

### Pillar II ŌĆö Router-to-Router Routing Plane
- Protocols: **DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SM, MOSPF, CBT**.
- Function: Constructs and maintains a **distribution tree** from source(s) to all group members.
- Two fundamental tree types:
  - **Source-Based Tree (SBT)** ŌĆö shortest-path tree per source; uses `(S, G)$` state.
  - **Shared Tree / Core-Based Tree (CBT)** ŌĆö single tree for the entire group; uses `(*, G)$` state.

### Pillar III ŌĆö Data-Forwarding Plane
- Algorithm: **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** check on every datagram.
- Replicates packets only when the outgoing interface list contains **more than one** active downstream branch.

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö How IGMP Works (Stepwise)

1. **Querier Election:** The router with the **lowest IP address** on the subnet becomes the IGMP Querier. It periodically multicasts a **General Membership Query** to `224.0.0.1` every **125 seconds** (default).
2. **Membership Report:** On receiving the query, each host that wishes to remain in group $G$ schedules a delayed **IGMP Membership Report** (Join) to group $G$ within $0$ to $10$ seconds (randomized).
3. **Suppression Mechanism:** If another host on the same subnet reports membership first for the same group, the others suppress their report (avoiding storm). This is **IGMPv1 / v2 behavior**.
4. **IGMPv3 Enhancement:** Hosts send `INCLUDE` or `EXCLUDE` source-list reports, enabling **Source-Specific Multicast (SSM)**.
5. **Leave Group:** In v2/v3, the host sends an **IGMP Leave** to `224.0.0.2` (All Routers). The router issues a **Group-Specific Query** to verify if any other host still wants the group.

## 2.3 Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) ŌĆö The Heart of Multicast Forwarding

The RPF algorithm prevents duplicate and looping packets. It is the **mandatory first check** on every multicast datagram arriving at a router:

> **RPF Rule:** A packet arriving on interface $I$ is accepted and forwarded **only if** $I$ is the **shortest-path (lowest-cost) interface** from this router **back to the source** $S$.

If the packet passes the RPF check, the router consults its `olist` (outgoing-interface list) and replicates the packet out every interface that has an active downstream member ŌĆö **except** the interface on which the packet arrived (the **split-horizon / RPF-prune rule**).

### Truncated Reverse Path Forwarding (TRPF)
Broadcasts the packet initially, then routers with no downstream members send **Prune** messages back upstream. State: `prune state` timers (default 3 minutes in DVMRP). Periodic **re-flood** is required when the prune timer expires.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth saved by Multicast vs Unicast | $\Delta B = (N - 1) \times P$ | $N$ = receivers, $P$ = packet size (bytes) |
| MAC Address Mapping | $MAC_{23} = IP_{23}$ (lower 23 bits) | 5 bits dropped ŌåÆ 32:1 ambiguity |
| Class D Range | $2^{28}$ addresses | $224.0.0.0 \le \text{Addr} \le 239.255.255.255$ |
| Ethernet Multicast MAC Block Size | $2^{23} = 8{,}388{,}608$ | $01{:}00{:}5E{:}00{:}00{:}00$ to $01{:}00{:}5E{:}7F{:}FF{:}FF$ |
| RPF Cost (Hop Count) | $C_{RPF} = \min_{i \in \text{interfaces}} \text{cost}(i, S)$ | $S$ = source IP |
| Steiner Tree Heuristic Cost | $C_{Steiner} \le 2 \cdot C_{MST}$ | $C_{MST}$ = Minimum Spanning Tree cost |
| Number of Prune States | $N_{prune} = N_{links}$ along reverse path | Per group, per source |
| IGMP Query Interval | $T_Q = 125$ s | Default for IGMPv2/v3 |
| IGMP Max Response Time | $T_{MRT} \le 10$ s | v2/v3 configurable |
| DVMRP Prune Lifetime | $T_{prune} = 3$ minutes | After which re-flood occurs |
| PIM-SM RP Election | $\text{RP} = \arg\min_{r} \text{Hash}(G, r)$ | Hash = BSR-derived |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Multicasting is the backbone of **IPTV, stock-tick feeds, video conferencing (H.323/SIP), software distribution (OSPF updates, routing protocols), and real-time financial market data**. Without multicast, Netflix-style video would require a unicast stream per subscriber, overwhelming upstream links.

## 2.5 Reverse Path Multicasting ŌĆö Putting It Together

The full DVMRP pipeline:

$$\text{Receive}(pkt) \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 1}}\; \text{RPF Check} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 2}}\; \text{Broadcast on all } i \neq I_{in} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 3}}\; \text{Await Prunes} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Step 4}}\; \text{Maintain } (S, G) \text{ state}$$

This combination of RPF + prune = **Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Routing Protocol Comparison

| Property | DVMRP | PIM-DM | MOSPF | CBT | PIM-SM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tree Type | Source-based | Source-based | Source-based | Shared (Core) | Shared (RP) |
| Mode | Dense | Dense | Dense | Sparse | Sparse |
| Flood-and-Prune | Yes | Yes (RPF) | No (uses Dijkstra) | No | No (explicit Join) |
| Protocol | Distance-Vector | Independent | Link-State | Distance-Vector | Independent |
| Scalability | Low | Medium | Low | High | Very High |
| Forwarding State | $(S, G)$ | $(S, G)$ | $(S, G)$ | $(*, G)$ | $(*, G)$ and $(S, G)$ |
| Convergence | Slow | Medium | Fast | Medium | Medium |
| KTU Weight | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |

## 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö RPF Pruning (a KTU-favorite derivation)

**Network Setup:** Routers $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ form a square. $R_1$ is the source $S$ of multicast group $G = 230.1.1.1$. Hosts $H_A$ and $H_B$ are attached to $R_3$ and $R_4$ respectively. $R_2$ has no members. All link costs = 1.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Initial Flood (RPM)

Source $R_1$ sends a datagram. The RPF check at $R_2$: the shortest-path interface to $R_1$ is the direct link $R_2 \rightarrow R_1$. The packet arrived on that interface ŌåÆ **RPF passes**.

The packet is then broadcast on all interfaces except the incoming one:
- $R_2 \rightarrow R_3$ (which has $H_A$)
- $R_2 \rightarrow R_4$ (which has $H_B$)

### Step 2 ŌĆö RPF Check at $R_3$ and $R_4$

For $R_3$: shortest path to $R_1$ is via $R_2$ (cost 2). The packet arrived on $R_3 \rightarrow R_2$ ŌåÆ **RPF passes**. Packet forwarded out $R_3 \rightarrow H_A$.

For $R_4$: shortest path to $R_1$ is via $R_2$ (cost 2). Packet arrived on $R_4 \rightarrow R_2$ ŌåÆ **RPF passes**. Packet forwarded out $R_4 \rightarrow H_B$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Imagine $R_2$ Has No Members

If $R_2$ had no group members, it would send a **Prune** message to $R_1$ on the link $R_2 \rightarrow R_1$ for $(S = R_1, G = 230.1.1.1)$. Future packets to that leaf are suppressed for the prune lifetime $T_{prune}$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö State Cost Derivation

The total state stored at $R_1$ after pruning:

$$N_{state}(R_1) = |\{(R_3, G), (R_4, G)\}| = 2 \text{ entries}$$

Unicast equivalent bandwidth (per packet of $P$ bytes sent to both hosts via $R_2$):

$$B_{unicast} = 2 \cdot P \text{ (two identical streams)} = 2P$$

Multicast bandwidth (single packet replicated only at $R_2$):

$$B_{multicast} = 1 \cdot P_{R_1 \to R_2} + 2 \cdot P_{R_2 \to \{R_3, R_4\}} = 3P$$

Bandwidth saved compared to a worst-case unicast (sender sends separately to $H_A$ and $H_B$):

$$\Delta B = B_{unicast,worst} - B_{multicast} = 4P - 3P = P \text{ per packet}$$

where the worst-case unicast traverses $R_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow H_A$ and $R_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow R_4 \rightarrow H_B$ (two paths).

## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö IGMPv2 Membership Report (State Machine)

| Event at Host | State Transition |
|---|---|
| Power ON, wants group $G$ | $\text{NON-MEMBER} \rightarrow \text{DELAYING-MEMBER}$ |
| Receive Query to $G$ | Start timer $\tau \sim U[0, 10]$ s |
| Timer expires | Send **Membership Report** for $G$ to $G$ ŌåÆ $\text{DELAYING-MEMBER} \rightarrow \text{IDLE-MEMBER}$ |
| Hear another's report for $G$ | Cancel timer ŌåÆ $\text{DELAYING-MEMBER} \rightarrow \text{IDLE-MEMBER}$ |
| Receive Leave from self (v2) | Send **Leave** to `224.0.0.2` ŌåÆ $\text{IDLE-MEMBER} \rightarrow \text{NON-MEMBER}$ |
| Receive Group-Specific Query (v2) | Restart timer |

> [!NOTE]
> The **suppression timer** is what makes IGMP scalable ŌĆö at most **one report per group per subnet per query interval**, regardless of how many thousands of hosts wish to receive the stream.

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Bandwidth-Saving Theorem

For a source transmitting a packet of size $P$ bytes to $N$ receivers over a multicast tree of total edge-length $L_T$ (in hops), and a unicast mesh of total edge-length $L_U$:

$$B_{multicast} = P \cdot L_T$$

$$B_{unicast} = P \cdot L_U \quad \text{(with replication at every fork)}$$

The **efficiency ratio** is:

$$\eta = \frac{B_{multicast}}{B_{unicast}} = \frac{L_T}{L_U}$$

For an ideal **Steiner tree**, $L_T \le L_U$ always, with the tightest bound:

$$L_{MST} \le L_{Steiner} \le 2 \cdot L_{MST}$$

where $L_{MST}$ is the Minimum Spanning Tree cost. This shows multicast can theoretically halve the bandwidth of MST-based approaches.

## 3.5 Ethernet MAC Mapping ŌĆö Worked Bit-Truncation

Suppose a host joins group $G = 230.15.42.7$.

Binary: $230 = 11100110_2$, $15 = 00001111_2$, $42 = 00101010_2$, $7 = 00000111_2$.

The lower 23 bits of the IP address are: `0001111 00101010 00000111` ŌåÆ `0x1F 0x2A 0x07`.

Mapped MAC: `01:00:5E:1F:2A:07`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Ambiguity:** Because the top 5 bits of the third IP octet are dropped, the groups `230.15.42.7`, `231.15.42.7`, `233.15.42.7`, ..., `239.15.42.7` (32 addresses) all map to the **same MAC** `01:00:5E:1F:2A:07`. The NIC must therefore filter in software ŌĆö a classic KTU 1-mark trap question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Multicast Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Sender Host S] -->|IGMP Join| AR1[Access Router R1]
    R1 -->|DVMRP or PIM| R2[Core Router R2]
    R2 --> R3[Edge Router R3]
    R2 --> R4[Edge Router R4]
    R3 --> HA[Host A on LAN-3]
    R4 --> HB[Host B on LAN-4]
    R3 --> HC[Host C on LAN-3]
    R1 -. IGMP Query 224.0.0.1 .-> HA
    R1 -. IGMP Query 224.0.0.1 .-> HB
    HA -. IGMP Report G .-> R1
    HB -. IGMP Report G .-> R1
    R3 -. IGMP Leave G .-> R1
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style R1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style R2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style R3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style R4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multicast Packet Arrives at Router R on Interface Iin] --> B{RPF Check: Is Iin the shortest path interface back to source S?}
    B -- No --> X[Drop Packet Silently RPF Failure]
    B -- Yes --> C[Lookup olist for group G]
    C --> D{olist is empty?}
    D -- Yes --> Y[Discard Forwarding Tree Pruned]
    D -- No --> E[Replicate packet to every interface in olist except Iin]
    E --> F[Forward packet downstream]
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style X fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Y fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 DVMRP Flood-and-Prune Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source S
    participant R1 as Router R1
    participant R2 as Router R2
    participant R3 as Router R3 (no members)
    participant R4 as Router R4 (members)
    SRC->>R1: Data (S,G)
    R1->>R2: Flood to all i ŌēĀ Iin
    R2->>R3: Forward
    R2->>R4: Forward
    R3-->>R2: PRUNE (S,G) upstream
    Note over R1,R2: State updated: Prune timer = 180s
    R2-->>R1: PRUNE (S,G) upstream
    Note over R1: Stops forwarding on pruned branch
    R4-->>HA: Deliver to Host A
    R4-->>HB: Deliver to Host B
```

## 4.4 IGMPv2 State Machine on a Host

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonMember
    NonMember --> DelayMember: User App Joins Group G
    DelayMember --> IdleMember: Send Membership Report G
    DelayMember --> IdleMember: Hear Another Report for G
    IdleMember --> DelayMember: Receive Query for G
    IdleMember --> NonMember: Send Leave to 224.0.0.2
    NonMember --> [*]
    note right of IdleMember: Host is silent and receives group traffic
    note right of DelayMember: Timer in 0 to 10 seconds
```

## 4.5 Multicast Address Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IPv4 Address Space 32 bits] --> B[Class A 0 0 to 127]
    A --> C[Class B 10 to 191]
    A --> D[Class C 192 to 223]
    A --> E[Class D 224 to 239 Multicast]
    A --> F[Class E 240 to 255 Experimental]
    E --> G1[224.0.0.0 24 Local Network Control]
    E --> G2[224.0.1.0 24 Internetwork Control]
    E --> G3[232.0.0.0 8 SSM Block]
    E --> G4[233.0.0.0 8 GLOP Block]
    E --> G5[239.0.0.0 8 Admin Scoped Private]
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style G1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style G5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sender_Plane["Sender Plane"]
        APP[Application Layer e.g. Video Server]
        IP[IP Layer Source Address Unicast Group Address Class D]
    end
    subgraph Network_Plane["Network Plane Routers with Multicast Support"]
        RPF[RPF Check Module]
        OLIST[Outgoing Interface List olist]
        REPL[Packet Replicator]
    end
    subgraph Receiver_Plane["Receiver Plane LAN with IGMP Hosts"]
        H1[Host 1 Member]
        H2[Host 2 Member]
        H3[Host 3 Non Member]
    end
    APP --> IP
    IP --> RPF
    RPF --> OLIST
    OLIST --> REPL
    REPL --> H1
    REPL --> H2
    REPL -. Blocked by IGMP Filter .-> H3
    style RPF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style OLIST fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style REPL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```
