# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** Distinguish between unicast, multicast, and broadcast communication with an appropriate diagram.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Unicast | Multicast | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of receivers | One | Subset (1 to N) | All in the domain |
| IP address type | Class A/B/C | Class D (224.0.0.0/4) | Limited broadcast 255.255.255.255 or subnet-directed |
| Packets sent by source | N copies | 1 copy, replicated at forks | 1 copy |
| Routing | Standard unicast routing | Multicast routing protocols (PIM, DVMRP) | Not routed (TTL=1 typically) |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low for many receivers | High | Lowest (wastes LAN BW) |
| Use case | Web browsing, email | IPTV, stock feeds, OSPF updates | ARP, DHCP discover |

**Diagram:** Single source with one-to-one, one-to-many, one-to-all fan-out arrows. *(Marks: 3)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** Explain the role of IGMP in multicasting. Why is IGMP restricted to the local subnet?

**Model Answer:**

**Role of IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol):**
- IGMP operates between a **host** and its **directly connected multicast router**.
- Three message types: **Query** (router ŌåÆ all hosts, 224.0.0.1), **Membership Report / Join** (host ŌåÆ group), **Leave** (host ŌåÆ all routers, 224.0.0.2 in v2/v3).
- It allows the router to learn which groups have interested listeners on each of its attached subnets, enabling it to forward group traffic only on interfaces with members.

**Why local-subnet only?**
- IGMP packets are encapsulated in IP with **TTL = 1**, so they cannot cross any router.
- This restriction prevents IGMP state from leaking across domains and keeps the protocol lightweight.
- Inter-router multicast group management is handled by separate routing protocols (PIM, DVMRP, etc.), not IGMP. *(Marks: 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *DVMRP & RPF Focus*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]** **(a)** Explain **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** with a suitable network diagram. How does **Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM)** extend RPF? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the operation of the **Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol (DVMRP)** including flood-and-prune, graft messages, and the role of tunnels. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Reverse Path Forwarding ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Definition:** RPF is a loop-prevention check performed by every multicast router on every incoming multicast datagram. A packet is accepted and forwarded only if it arrives on the interface that the router would use to **send a unicast packet back to the source**.

**Algorithm (exhaustive steps):**

1. Router $R$ receives multicast packet $P$ on interface $I_{in}$, with source IP = $S$, group = $G$.
2. $R$ consults its **unicast routing table** (or MRIB in PIM) and identifies the **shortest-path (RPF) interface** $I_{RPF}$ toward $S$.
3. **Decision:** If $I_{in} = I_{RPF}$ ŌåÆ **RPF passes**; proceed to step 4. Else ŌåÆ **drop the packet silently** (RPF failure).
4. $R$ looks up the `olist` (outgoing-interface list) for entry $(S, G)$.
5. If `olist` is empty ŌåÆ **discard** (the group has been pruned on all branches).
6. Otherwise, **replicate** $P$ out every interface in `olist` **except $I_{in}$** (split-horizon rule).

**Diagram:** A 4-router square with arrows showing the RPF-interface election and the prune flow on a leaf branch.

**Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM) extension:**
RPM layers **prune semantics** on top of RPF broadcast:
- Initial delivery is **flood-style** to all routers on the RPF tree.
- Leaf routers with **no group members** send **Prune** messages upstream, propagating them toward the source.
- Prune state has a lifetime (e.g., 180 s in DVMRP); after expiry, the data is re-flooded to detect new members.
- RPM thus converts an inefficient broadcast into a **pruned shortest-path tree**. *(7 marks)*

**(b) DVMRP Operation ŌĆö 7 marks**

DVMRP is a **dense-mode, source-based, distance-vector** multicast routing protocol. It predates PIM and is the basis of the historic **mbone** (multicast backbone).

**Key Mechanisms:**

1. **Neighbor Discovery:** Uses **IGMP** to discover neighboring multicast routers on a shared subnet (probe / report exchange).
2. **Distance-Vector Exchange:** Periodically (every 60 s) advertises routing tables with **metrics = hop count** (max 32 hops). This builds the unicast RPF table.
3. **Flood-and-Prune:** First packet to a new $(S, G)$ pair is flooded via RPF. Prunes are sent by routers whose downstream `olist` is empty.
4. **Prune Lifetime:** Prunes are **soft-state** ŌĆö they expire after ~3 minutes, forcing periodic re-flood to re-discover members.
5. **Graft Messages:** When a previously pruned branch acquires a new member, it sends a **Graft** message to its upstream neighbor to immediately cancel the prune (avoids waiting for timer expiry).
6. **Tunnels:** DVMRP supports IP-in-IP encapsulation for **non-multicast-capable clouds** in the Internet, allowing mbone islands to interconnect.

**Limitations:** Scalability is poor because every active source keeps state at every router. This motivated the move to **PIM-SM** in modern networks. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *CBT & PIM-SM Focus (Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024]** **(a)** Compare **Source-Based Trees** and **Shared (Core-Based) Trees**. When is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Core-Based Trees (CBT)** and **PIM-Sparse Mode (PIM-SM)** with reference to the role of the **Rendezvous Point (RP)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Source-Based vs Shared Trees ŌĆö 7 marks**

| Property | Source-Based Tree (SBT) | Shared Tree / Core-Based Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Tree type | One tree per source $(S, G)$ | One tree per group $(*, G)$ |
| Root | Each source | A single shared root (Core / RP) |
| Path | Shortest path from $S$ to each receiver | May be **sub-optimal** (longer) |
| State at routers | $(S, G)$ entries ŌĆö scales with $S \cdot G$ | $(*, G)$ entries ŌĆö scales with $G$ only |
| Bandwidth use | Optimal per source | Possibly wasteful if receivers are clustered |
| Best suited for | Dense topologies, few sources | Sparse topologies, many sources |
| Examples | DVMRP, PIM-DM, MOSPF | CBT, PIM-SM |

**Selection criteria:**
- Dense topology with **few sources, many receivers**: SBT preferred (optimal paths).
- Sparse topology (e.g., wide-area ISP) with **many sources**: Shared tree preferred (less state). *(7 marks)*

**(b) CBT and PIM-SM ŌĆö 7 marks**

**CBT (Core-Based Tree):**
- A single router called the **Core** acts as the rendezvous point for the entire group.
- Receivers send an explicit **Join** message toward the Core using **unicast routing**; the path becomes a branch of the shared tree.
- Sources **unicast** their data to the Core, which then distributes it down the tree.
- CBT maintains only `(*, G)$` state ŌĆö very scalable.
- Drawbacks: Sub-optimal paths; single point of failure (Core).

**PIM-SM (Sparse Mode):**
- Designed for environments where receivers are **sparsely distributed** and bandwidth across most links must be conserved.
- Uses a **Rendezvous Point (RP)** ŌĆö analogous to CBT's Core but elected dynamically via the **Bootstrap Router (BSR)** mechanism using a hash function over the group address.
- **Operation:**
  1. Receivers send PIM `Join` toward RP ŌåÆ builds a shared `(*, G)$` tree.
  2. Sources register with RP via unicast **PIM Register** messages.
  3. RP may later initiate an **SRT (Shortest-Path Tree) switch** by sending a `Join` directly toward the source, after which the source's data flows over an optimal $(S, G)$ tree.
- Used today by virtually all large-scale IPTV deployments (e.g., telecom operators). *(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse IGMP with multicast routing protocols.** IGMP is **only host-to-router**, while DVMRP/PIM/CBT are **router-to-router**. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **TTL = 1 for IGMP** is a must-mention fact. Without it, you lose a key technical point.
> 3. **32:1 MAC ambiguity** in Ethernet mapping: Always quote `01:00:5E` prefix and the lower-23-bit rule.
> 4. For **DVMRP**, do not forget **Graft messages**; many students describe only Prune, losing 1 mark.
> 5. For **PIM-SM**, mention **RP election via BSR** and the **SRT switch** ŌĆö not just "trees are built."
> 6. **Numerical derivations:** When asked bandwidth savings, write the explicit unicast cost vs multicast cost; do not just state "multicast saves bandwidth."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Class D Range:** $\mathbf{224.0.0.0 \;\text{to}\; 239.255.255.255}$ ŌĆö these addresses identify **groups**, not hosts.
- **Ethernet MAC for Class D:** `01:00:5E` prefix + lower **23 bits** of IP ŌåÆ **32:1 ambiguity**.
- **Three Components:** **IGMP** (hostŌåörouter), **Multicast Routing** (routerŌåörouter), **RPF** (forwarding check).
- **IGMP TTL = 1**, scoped to local subnet only.
- **RPF Rule:** Accept packet only if it arrived on the shortest-path interface back to the source.
- **Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM):** RPF + Prune ŌåÆ pruned shortest-path tree.
- **DVMRP:** Dense-mode, source-tree, flood-and-prune with 3-min prune lifetime; uses **Graft** to undo prunes; supports **tunnels** for non-multicast clouds.
- **PIM-DM:** Same as DVMRP in spirit but **protocol-independent** (works over any unicast table).
- **CBT:** Sparse-mode, shared tree rooted at **Core**; uses `(*, G)$` state.
- **PIM-SM:** Sparse-mode, **RP** via **BSR** hash election; supports **SRT switch** to $(S, G)$ tree.
- **MOSPF:** Link-state, uses Dijkstra; embedded in OSPF; not widely deployed.
- **IGMP Suppression:** Only **one** Membership Report per group per query interval per subnet.
- **Local Network Control Block (224.0.0.0/24):** Never forwarded ŌĆö TTL forced to 1.
- **Steiner Tree Bound:** $L_{MST} \le L_{Steiner} \le 2 \cdot L_{MST}$.
- **Bandwidth Saving Theorem:** $\Delta B = B_{unicast} - B_{multicast} = P \cdot (L_U - L_T)$.
- **Practical Use:** IPTV, video conferencing, stock feeds, software distribution, OSPF link-state updates.
- **Key RFCs (do not memorize numbers for exam, but know concepts):** RFC 1112 (IGMPv1), RFC 2236 (IGMPv2), RFC 3376 (IGMPv3), RFC 1075 (DVMRP), RFC 2362 (PIM-SM), RFC 2189 / 2201 (CBT).
- **Always quote** for full marks: **TTL = 1 for IGMP**, **Class D = 224/4**, **MAC prefix = 01:00:5E**, **23-bit mapping**, **RPF-interface election**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model**, the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** acts as the critical liaison between the **lower, network-dependent layers** (Physical, Data Link, Network) and the **upper, application-oriented layers** (Session, Presentation, Application). The **Transport Service** is defined as the complete set of services that the Transport Layer offers to the Session Layer (or, in the **TCP/IP model**, directly to the Application Layer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Transport Service is the end-to-end, host-to-host, process-to-process communication service provided by the transport layer, which shields the upper layers from the inherent unreliability, heterogeneity, and topological complexities of the underlying network.*

Formally, the Transport Layer provides its services through two primary **Service Access Points (SAPs)** known as **Transport Service Primitives**:

- **LISTEN** ŌĆō Issued by the server-side transport entity to indicate willingness to accept incoming connections.
- **CONNECT** ŌĆō Issued by the client-side transport entity to establish a connection.
- **SEND** ŌĆō Used by either party to transmit a Transport Service Data Unit (TSDU).
- **RECEIVE** ŌĆō Used by either party to accept an incoming TSDU.
- **DISCONNECT** ŌĆō Used to terminate the connection.

These primitives are classified into four types per the **ISO 8072** standard:
1. **T-Request** ŌĆō Issued by the user (upper layer).
2. **T-Indication** ŌĆō Issued by the transport provider.
3. **T-Response** ŌĆō Issued by the user in response to an indication.
4. **T-Confirm** ŌĆō Issued by the provider to confirm the request.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The International Postal System ­¤ō¼**

Imagine the Transport Layer as the **national postal service** (e.g., India Post), and the upper layers as the **citizens** (users). The citizens do not need to know *which truck, route, or sorting hub* their letter passes through. They simply write the recipient's **house number + street + city + PIN code** (analogous to **IP address + port number**) and hand it to the post office. The post office guarantees:
- The letter reaches the *right person* (process-to-process delivery).
- The letter is *intact* (reliability, error control).
- The recipient is *ready to receive* (connection establishment).
- Letters are delivered in *order* (sequencing).
- If the recipient's mailbox is full, the postman *waits* (flow control).

Citizens (upper layers) only interact with the post office's **counter service** (transport service primitives) ŌĆö they are completely insulated from the logistics. This insulation is the essence of the **Transport Service**.

## 1.3 Why Is Transport Service Needed?

The lower three layers (1ŌĆō3) deal with **node-to-node** communication and depend on the specific network technology (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, optical fiber, satellite links). They cannot guarantee:
- **End-to-end reliability** (bit errors, packet loss).
- **Process-level addressing** (multiple applications running on the same host).
- **Ordered delivery** (packets may arrive out of order).
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** differentiation.

The Transport Layer abstracts all these concerns and offers a **clean, uniform, reliable interface** to the upper layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight ŌĆö The Transport Layer is the FIRST layer from the bottom that is concerned purely with END-TO-END (host-to-host) semantics, rather than hop-by-hop (node-to-node) semantics.**

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Transport Layer Number** | **Layer 4** of the OSI model |
| **Port Number Range (Well-Known)** | **0 ŌĆō 1023** |
| **Port Number Range (Registered)** | **1024 ŌĆō 49151** |
| **Port Number Range (Dynamic/Private)** | **49152 ŌĆō 65535** |
| **Max TPDU Header Size (TCP)** | **60 bytes** (20 fixed + 40 options) |
| **Max TPDU Header Size (UDP)** | **8 bytes** |
| **Max TCP Payload (MSS default)** | **536 bytes** (RFC 1122) / **1460 bytes** (Ethernet) |
| **Total IP Datagram Size (IPv4)** | **65,535 bytes** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Service Inventory Provided to Upper Layers

The Transport Layer offers the following **seven core services** to the upper layers. Each is a high-frequency KTU examination topic.

### 2.1.1 Process-to-Process (End-to-End) Addressing ŌĆö *The MOST Fundamental Service*

- The Network Layer identifies **hosts** using **IP addresses (32-bit in IPv4, 128-bit in IPv6)**.
- The Transport Layer identifies **specific processes/applications** on those hosts using **16-bit port numbers**.
- A complete transport endpoint is therefore the pair: **(IP Address, Port Number)** ŌĆö known as a **Socket**.
- **Mechanism:** A socket is created by the OS; the upper layer (application) uses socket APIs (`socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()`, `connect()`).

### 2.1.2 Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Services

| Service Type | Description | Example Protocol | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Connection-Oriented** | Three-phase: Establishment ŌåÆ Data Transfer ŌåÆ Termination. Reliable, ordered, stateful. | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | *Virtual Circuit* analogy |
| **Connectionless** | Best-effort, stateless, no handshake. | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** | *Datagram* analogy |

### 2.1.3 Reliable Data Transfer (Error Control)

- The Transport Layer provides **end-to-end error detection and recovery**.
- Uses **checksums**, **Acknowledgements (ACKs)**, **Negative Acknowledgements (NACKs)**, **timeouts**, and **retransmissions** (ARQ ŌĆö Automatic Repeat reQuest).
- **Important:** UDP also has a checksum (RFC 1122 mandates it for IPv4), but does **not** perform retransmission ŌåÆ hence it is "unreliable" only in the recovery sense.

### 2.1.4 Flow Control

- Prevents a **fast sender** from overwhelming a **slow receiver**.
- **Credit-based / Sliding Window** mechanism: receiver advertises a **Window Size** indicating how many bytes/segments it can buffer.
- Equation (KTU must-know): 

$$\text{Effective Window} = \min(\text{Receiver Window } rwnd, \text{ Congestion Window } cwnd)$$

### 2.1.5 Congestion Control

- Prevents the network from being flooded ŌåÆ avoids **congestion collapse**.
- The transport layer senses congestion via **packet loss** or **explicit ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** bits.
- Algorithms: **Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery** (TCP Reno, TCP Cubic, BBR).

### 2.1.6 Multiplexing and Demultiplexing

- **Multiplexing (Upward):** Multiple application-layer processes send data over a single network interface; transport layer adds port numbers to distinguish.
- **Demultiplexing (Downward):** Incoming segments are delivered to the correct socket (process) based on the destination port number.
- Without this, a single host could not run a browser, email client, and video call simultaneously.

### 2.1.7 Quality of Service (QoS) / Performance Guarantees

- Optional services (mainly in connection-oriented mode) include:
  - **Throughput guarantee** (minimum bandwidth).
  - **Latency guarantee** (maximum delay).
  - **Jitter control** (delay variation).
  - **Residual error rate specification**.

## 2.2 Service Primitives ŌĆö The Formal Interaction Model

Per **ISO 8073 (Transport Protocol Specification)** and **ISO 8072 (Transport Service Definition)**, services are invoked using four primitive types. The KTU board repeatedly tests the state-transition diagram of these primitives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection-Oriented Service Primitive Sequence (KTU Hot Topic):**
> 
> 1. `T-CONNECT.request` ŌåÆ `T-CONNECT.indication` (Client ŌåÆ Server)
> 2. `T-CONNECT.response` ŌåÉ `T-CONNECT.confirm` (Server ŌåÆ Client)
> 3. `T-DATA.request` ŌåÆ `T-DATA.indication` (Both directions, multiple times)
> 4. `T-DISCONNECT.request` ŌåÆ `T-DISCONNECT.indication` (Abrupt termination)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Window | $W_{eff} = \min(rwnd, cwnd)$ | bytes | Used in TCP flow + congestion control |
| 2 | RTT (Round Trip Time) | $RTT = 2 \times \text{Propagation Delay} + \text{Transmission Delay}$ | seconds | $T_t = L/R$ |
| 3 | Transmission Delay | $T_t = L / R$ | seconds | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| 4 | Throughput | $\rho = W / \text{RTT}$ | segments/sec | For a window of $W$ segments |
| 5 | Stop-and-Wait Utilization | $U = 1 / (1 + 2a)$ | dimensionless | where $a = T_p / T_t$ |
| 6 | Sliding Window Utilization | $U = W / (1 + 2a)$ (for $W < 1+2a$) | dimensionless | $W$ = window size |
| 7 | Checksum (16-bit) | $\Sigma_{i=1}^{N} x_i + \text{carry} \rightarrow \text{1's complement}$ | bits | One's complement sum |
| 8 | Socket Identifier | $(\text{IP Address}, \text{Port Number})$ | ŌĆö | 32+16 = 48 bits minimum |
| 9 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \times RTT$ | bits | Max unacknowledged data in flight |
| 10 | Max TCP Window | $65535$ bytes (16-bit field) | bytes | RFC 1323 ŌåÆ Window Scaling option |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Required Transport Service |
|---|---|
| **HTTP/HTTPS (Web)** | TCP ŌĆö connection-oriented, reliable |
| **DNS (Domain Lookup)** | UDP ŌĆö fast, simple, single request-response |
| **Video Streaming (Netflix, YouTube)** | UDP (RTP) ŌĆö tolerates loss, needs low latency |
| **VoIP (WhatsApp, Skype calls)** | UDP ŌĆö latency-sensitive, jitter-controlled |
| **Email (SMTP, IMAP, POP3)** | TCP ŌĆö reliable, ordered delivery of messages |
| **Online Gaming** | UDP with reliability patches (e.g., QUIC) |
| **File Transfer (FTP, SFTP)** | TCP ŌĆö must be 100% accurate |
| **IoT Telemetry** | UDP ŌĆö small, periodic, loss-tolerant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The choice of transport service (TCP vs UDP) is **not random** ŌĆö it is dictated by the application's tolerance to *delay* vs. *loss*. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Services vs. Quality Attributes

The following **2D matrix** (frequently tested in KTU Part B questions) maps each Transport Service against the quality attributes it provides to the upper layers.

| Transport Service | Reliability | Ordering | Timing/Throughput Guarantee | Process Addressing | Flow Control | Congestion Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Connection-Oriented (TCP-like)** | Ō£ģ Yes (ARQ + checksum) | Ō£ģ Yes (sequence numbers) | ŌØī No (best-effort) | Ō£ģ Yes (port numbers) | Ō£ģ Yes (rwnd) | Ō£ģ Yes (cwnd) |
| **Connectionless (UDP-like)** | ŌØī No (only checksum) | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes (port numbers) | ŌØī No | ŌØī No |
| **Real-Time (RTP-like)** | ŌØī Partial (FEC optional) | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Partial (jitter buffer) | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī Partial |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Why the Effective Window Equation Matters

**Problem Context (KTU-style 7-mark question):** A TCP sender has `cwnd = 10 KB` and the receiver has advertised `rwnd = 4 KB`. What is the maximum data the sender can push in the next RTT?

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the governing rule.**

The TCP sender MUST respect the smaller of the two constraints: the **receiver's buffer limit** and the **network's carrying limit**.

$$W_{eff} = \min(rwnd, cwnd)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the given values.**

Given: $cwnd = 10 \text{ KB} = 10240 \text{ bytes}$, $rwnd = 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$.

$$W_{eff} = \min(10240, 4096)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the minimum.**

Since $4096 < 10240$:

$$W_{eff} = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation (the "Why" step).**

The sender is allowed to push only **4 KB**, because the **receiver's buffer** is the bottleneck ŌĆö not the network. The congestion window of 10 KB suggests the network *could* carry more, but the receiver would be overwhelmed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Students often blindly use `cwnd` only. The correct approach is the **$\min$** of both. Marks are specifically deducted in the valuation key for ignoring `rwnd`.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Stop-and-Wait Protocol Efficiency

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the parameters.**

Let $T_t$ = transmission time, $T_p$ = propagation time (one-way), and $a = T_p / T_t$.

The total time to send one frame and receive its ACK is:

$$T_{total} = T_t + T_p + T_p + T_t = 2T_t + 2T_p$$

(Sender transmits ŌåÆ frame propagates ŌåÆ ACK propagates back ŌåÆ ACK received.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor out $T_t$.**

$$T_{total} = 2T_t (1 + a)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define utilization.**

Utilization $U$ is the fraction of time the channel is *actually* used to carry useful data:

$$U = \frac{T_t}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_t}{2T_t(1 + a)} = \frac{1}{2(1 + a)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final form.**

$$\boxed{U_{SAW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation (KTU-style).**

Suppose link bandwidth $R = 1 \text{ Mbps}$, frame length $L = 1000 \text{ bits}$, and one-way propagation delay $T_p = 10 \text{ ms}$.

$$T_t = L / R = 1000 / 10^6 = 1 \text{ ms}$$

$$a = T_p / T_t = 10 / 1 = 10$$

$$U = 1 / (1 + 2 \times 10) = 1/21 \approx 0.0476 = 4.76\%$$

**Interpretation:** The link is utilized only ~4.76% of the time ŌĆö Stop-and-Wait is **catastrophically inefficient** for high-bandwidth, high-latency links (e.g., satellite). This is *exactly why sliding window protocols were invented.*

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Sliding Window Protocol Utilization

**Step 1 ŌĆö Generalize Stop-and-Wait.**

With a window of size $W$ (number of unacknowledged frames), the sender can transmit up to $W$ frames before needing an ACK.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Time to push $W$ frames.**

$$T_{window} = W \cdot T_t$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Time until the first ACK arrives (one RTT after the first frame).**

$$T_{first\_ack} = 2T_p + T_t$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compare $T_{window}$ to $T_{first\_ack}$.**

- If $W \cdot T_t \geq 2T_p + T_t$ (i.e., $W \geq 1 + 2a$), the sender never idles: $U = 1$.
- If $W \cdot T_t < 2T_p + T_t$ (i.e., $W < 1 + 2a$), the sender idles:

$$U_{SW} = \frac{W \cdot T_t}{2T_p + T_t} = \frac{W}{1 + 2a}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final compact formula.**

$$\boxed{U_{SW} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{W}{1+2a} & \text{if } W < 1+2a \\ 1 & \text{if } W \geq 1+2a \end{cases}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** A 7-mark question often presents a satellite link (high $a$) and asks the student to **compute the minimum window size** required to achieve 100% utilization. Use $W_{min} = 1 + 2a$.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Analysis: Checksum Calculation (1's Complement Sum)

**Problem:** Calculate the 16-bit one's complement checksum for three 16-bit words: `0x4500`, `0x003C`, `0x1C46`.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sum the words (regular binary addition).**

```
   0x4500
 + 0x003C
 + 0x1C46
 --------
   0x6182
```

(Note: $0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C$; $0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182$.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Detect carry overflow.**

The result `0x6182` fits in 16 bits ŌåÆ no carry.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take one's complement (flip all bits).**

Checksum = $\overline{0x6182} = 0x9E7D$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Receiver verification.**

The receiver sums all 16-bit words **including the checksum**. A valid packet gives a result of `0xFFFF` (all 1s); if any bit is 0, an error occurred.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**List any four services provided by the Transport Layer to the upper layers.**

**Model Answer (For 3 marks):**

The four services provided by the Transport Layer to the upper layers are:

1. **Process-to-Process Delivery (End-to-End Addressing):** Identifies specific application processes on host machines using 16-bit port numbers combined with IP addresses (sockets), enabling multiple applications to communicate simultaneously.

2. **Reliable Data Transfer (Error Control):** Guarantees error-free delivery of data from source to destination using checksums, sequence numbers, acknowledgements, and retransmission mechanisms (ARQ), which hides network unreliability from the upper layers.

3. **Flow Control:** Regulates the rate of data transmission so that a fast sender does not overwhelm a slow receiver, typically using sliding window protocols with advertised receiver window size (`rwnd`).

4. **Connection Management:** Establishes, maintains, and terminates end-to-end connections (three-way handshake and four-way termination in TCP), providing a stateful communication channel for upper-layer applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Any four of the seven services discussed in Section 2.1 are acceptable. Using keywords like *"end-to-end," "reliable," "port numbers," "flow control"* guarantees full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between a Transport Service and a Transport Protocol? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (For 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transport Service | Transport Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The **set of operations** (primitives) the transport layer offers to the upper layers. | The **set of rules and formats** used to implement those operations within the transport layer. |
| **Audience** | Visible to upper layers (Session/Application). | Invisible to upper layers; internal to the transport layer. |
| **Nature** | *What* is provided. | *How* it is provided. |
| **Example** | Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream service. | **TCP** (the protocol implementing that service). |
| **Example 2** | Connectionless, best-effort, message-oriented service. | **UDP** (the protocol implementing that service). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The *service* is the abstraction; the *protocol* is the implementation. The upper layers do NOT see TCP/UDP ŌĆö they see the *service interface* (e.g., `socket()`, `send()`, `recv()`).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

### **Question:**
**(a)** Explain the various services provided by the Transport Layer to the upper layers. Discuss the role of **multiplexing and demultiplexing** in detail with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat state-transition diagram, explain the **Transport Service Primitives** for a connection-oriented service as defined by ISO. List the four types of primitives and describe any two phases in detail. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for (a):**

The Transport Layer (Layer 4) provides the following key services to the upper layers:

1. **Process-to-Process Addressing:** Using sockets (IP + Port).
2. **Connection-Oriented and Connectionless Services:** TCP-like and UDP-like.
3. **Reliable Data Transfer:** ARQ, checksums, sequence numbers.
4. **Flow Control:** Sliding window, `rwnd`.
5. **Congestion Control:** `cwnd`, slow start, AIMD.
6. **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing:** Detailed below.
7. **Quality of Service:** Throughput, latency, jitter guarantees.

**Multiplexing and Demultiplexing ŌĆö Detailed Explanation:**

- **Multiplexing (at the sender host):** Multiple application processes (e.g., browser, email, video call) want to send data through a single network interface. The transport layer adds a **unique source port number** to each application's data segment so that they can be distinguished on the receiver side. This is "upward multiplexing" ŌĆö many-to-one.

- **Demultiplexing (at the receiver host):** The receiver's transport layer examines the **destination port number** in the incoming segment header and delivers the payload to the correct socket, which in turn wakes up the correct application process. This is "downward demultiplexing" ŌĆö one-to-many.

- **Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
  Sender Host                               Receiver Host
 ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ                              ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
 Ōöé Browser  ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ                       ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║Ōöé Browser  Ōöé
 Ōöé  P1:443  Ōöé  Ōöé  Multiplexing         Ōöé   Ōöé S1:443   Ōöé
 ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś  Ōöé   (add src port)      Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
 ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ  Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ        Ōöé   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
 Ōöé Email    ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöż   Transport  Ōöé  IP    Ōöé   Ōöé Email    Ōöé
 Ōöé  P2:25   Ōöé  Ōöé     Layer    ŌöéŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║Ōöé   Ōöé S2:25    Ōöé
 ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś  Ōöé              Ōöé        Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
 ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ  Ōöé              Ōöé        Ōöé   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
 Ōöé Video    ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöś              Ōöé        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║Ōöé Video    Ōöé
 Ōöé P3:5004  Ōöé                 Ōöé            Ōöé S3:5004  Ōöé
 ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś                 Ōöé            ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```
