## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Email Architecture ŌĆö Layered Model

The email system operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the OSI model and is fully **text-based** (ASCII line-oriented commands), which makes it inspectable with tools like `tcpdump` or `Wireshark` for learning purposes.

**Transport layer dependency:**
- **SMTP** uses **TCP port 25** (MTA-to-MTA) and **TCP port 587** (MUA-to-MSA submission).
- **POP3** uses **TCP port 110** (plain) and **TCP port 995** (POP3S over TLS).
- **IMAP4** uses **TCP port 143** (plain) and **TCP port 993** (IMAPS over TLS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TCP and not UDP?**
> Email demands **reliable, in-order, connection-oriented** delivery. A single lost segment could corrupt an entire message body. Therefore, SMTP/POP3/IMAP all run on **TCP** with a three-way handshake (`SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK`) before any mail command is exchanged.

### 2.2 Detailed Component Flow

**Step-by-step operational sequence of a single email:**

1. The **MUA** composes a message conforming to **RFC 5322** (header) + **MIME** (body).
2. MUA ŌåÆ **MSA (Mail Submission Agent)** via **SMTP submission** (port 587, with `STARTTLS` and `AUTH`).
3. MSA forwards to the **MTA** which performs **DNS MX-record lookup** to find the destination mail server.
4. MTA establishes **SMTP session** (port 25) with the remote MTA. Envelope: `MAIL FROM:<...>` / `RCPT TO:<...>`.
5. Possibly routed through **multiple relay MTAs** (store-and-forward).
6. Final **MDA** stores the message in the recipient's mailbox file (e.g., `/var/mail/username` or `Maildir/`).
7. **MRA** (POP3/IMAP server) exposes the mailbox to the recipient's MUA on demand.

### 2.3 SMTP ŌĆö The Workhorse Protocol (RFC 5321)

**SMTP is a push protocol** ŌĆö it pushes mail from sender to receiver. It is **stateful, command-response**, and **8-bit clean** (with `8BITMIME` extension).

**SMTP Transaction Phases:**

| Phase | Purpose | Example Commands |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Connection Setup** | Open TCP, greet, identify | `220`, `EHLO client.example.com` |
| **Mail Transaction** | Envelope, sender, recipients | `MAIL FROM:`, `RCPT TO:` |
| **Data Transfer** | Headers + body (terminated by `<CR><LF>.<CR><LF>`) | `DATA` |
| **Connection Termination** | Close gracefully | `QUIT` |

**Common SMTP Commands (must memorize):**

- `HELO / EHLO` ŌĆö Client identifies itself (`EHLO` for Extended SMTP).
- `MAIL FROM:<address>` ŌĆö Initiates mail transaction, identifies sender (envelope).
- `RCPT TO:<address>` ŌĆö Identifies one recipient; can be repeated for multiple recipients.
- `DATA` ŌĆö Tells server the following lines (until a single dot on a line) are the message body.
- `RSET` ŌĆö Abort current transaction.
- `VRFY <user>` ŌĆö Verify a username (often disabled for security).
- `NOOP` ŌĆö No operation; keep-alive.
- `QUIT` ŌĆö Close the connection.

**SMTP Reply Codes ŌĆö The Three-Digit Semantics:**

| Code Prefix | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2yz** | Positive Completion | `250 OK` |
| **3yz** | Positive Intermediate | `354 Start mail input` |
| **4yz** | Transient Negative (retry) | `421 Service not available` |
| **5yz** | Permanent Negative (do not retry) | `550 Mailbox not found` |

### 2.4 POP3 ŌĆö The Post Office Protocol (RFC 1939)

**POP3 is a pull protocol** designed for *offline* mail processing. It **downloads** mail from the server to the local MUA, then (optionally) deletes it from the server.

**POP3 Session ŌĆö Three States:**

1. **AUTHORIZATION State** ŌĆö Client authenticates.
   - Commands: `USER <name>`, `PASS <password>`, `APOP` (authenticated POP), `QUIT`.
2. **TRANSACTION State** ŌĆö Client retrieves/manages messages.
   - Commands: `STAT` (mailbox stats), `LIST` (list sizes), `RETR n` (retrieve msg n), `DELE n` (mark for delete), `NOOP`, `RSET`, `TOP n <lines>`, `UIDL` (unique ID listing).
3. **UPDATE State** ŌĆö Server executes queued deletions and closes the socket.
   - Triggered by `QUIT`.

### 2.5 IMAP4 ŌĆö The Intelligent Mail Access (RFC 3501)

**IMAP4 is a pull protocol** for *online* / *disconnected* / *offline* mail processing. It **leaves mail on the server** and allows sophisticated folder management.

**Unique IMAP features (vs POP3):**

- **Server-side folders** (create, rename, delete).
- **Server-side search** (`SEARCH SUBJECT "exam"`).
- **Partial fetch** (`FETCH 1:5 (BODY[HEADER])`).
- **Multiple mailbox access** in one connection.
- **State flags** (`\Seen`, `\Answered`, `\Flagged`, `\Deleted`, `\Draft`).
- **Synchronization via `UID` validity** ŌĆö ideal for multiple devices.

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Parameter Table ŌĆö Memorize for the Exam**

| Parameter | Value / Format | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SMTP TCP Port (MTA-MTA) | **25** | Standard relay port |
| SMTP Submission Port (MUA-MSA) | **587** | Authenticated submission |
| POP3 TCP Port | **110** | Plaintext |
| POP3S TCP Port | **995** | TLS encrypted |
| IMAP TCP Port | **143** | Plaintext |
| IMAPS TCP Port | **993** | TLS encrypted |
| DATA terminator | **`<CR><LF>.<CR><LF>`** | End-of-message marker |
| Mail Header standard | **RFC 5322** | Internet Message Format |
| MIME standard | **RFC 2045ŌĆō2049** | Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions |
| Max SMTP line length | **998 octets** | Including CRLF |
| Recommended max line | **78 octets** | For readability |
| SMTP reply format | **XYZ text** | X=class, YZ=number |
| POP3 success response | `+OK` | Positive |
| POP3 failure response | `-ERR` | Negative |
| IMAP response tagging | `tagged OK` / `tagged NO` | Per-command tag |
| Default port for DNS MX query | **53/UDP** | Mail exchanger lookup |

### 2.7 MIME ŌĆö The Multimedia Enabler

Since SMTP only transfers **7-bit ASCII**, binary/multimedia content requires encoding. **MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)** adds structured headers:

- `MIME-Version: 1.0`
- `Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8` or `image/jpeg`, `application/pdf`, `multipart/mixed`, `multipart/alternative`.
- `Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64` / `quoted-printable` / `7bit` / `8bit` / `binary`.
- `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="x.pdf"`.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Industry**
> - **Cloud email platforms** (Gmail, Microsoft 365, Zoho Mail) run the same SMTP/POP/IMAP stack on backend servers like **Postfix + Dovecot** on Linux.
> - **DevOps engineers** use SMTP for **transactional alerts** (password resets, OTPs).
> - **Cybersecurity analysts** analyze SMTP traffic to detect **phishing**, **spoofing** (mitigated by **SPF / DKIM / DMARC**), and **open relay abuse**.
> - **IoT and embedded systems** often implement lightweight SMTP clients for alert emails.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Traces & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete SMTP Session ŌĆö Line-by-Line Trace

Below is an exhaustive, real-world SMTP conversation between a sending client (`client.example.com`) and a receiving server (`mail.kerala.edu`). Every line is mandated by the protocol specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace Notation:** Lines starting with `C:` are sent by the **Client**. Lines starting with `S:` are sent by the **Server**. Each command/response is followed by its semantic explanation.

**Step 1: TCP Connection Establishment**

The client opens a TCP connection to port 25 of the server. The three-way handshake `SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK` is transparent at the SMTP level.

**Step 2: Server Greeting (220)**

$$
\text{S:} \;\; \texttt{220 mail.kerala.edu ESMTP Postfix}
$$

*Explanation:* The server announces it is ready and supports **Extended SMTP (ESMTP)**. Class `2yz` = Positive Completion.

**Step 3: Client Identifies itself using EHLO**

$$
\text{C:} \;\; \texttt{EHLO client.example.com}
$$

*Explanation:* `EHLO` (Extended HELO) replaces `HELO` and requests the extended feature list. The domain argument is the **client's canonical hostname** (used in `HELO` checks).

**Step 4: Server lists supported extensions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250-mail.kerala.edu} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250-PIPELINING} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250-SIZE 20480000} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250-STARTTLS} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250-8BITMIME} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250-AUTH PLAIN LOGIN} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{250 ENHANCEDSTATUSCODES}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each `250-` (dash-suffixed) is a continuation line; the final `250 ` (space-suffixed) is the last line of the multi-line reply. Extensions include maximum message size and authentication mechanism.

**Step 5: Sender Declaration ŌĆö MAIL FROM**

$$
\text{C:} \;\; \texttt{MAIL FROM:<alice@client.example.com>}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **envelope sender** (often different from the `From:` header). It initiates the mail transaction and resets any previous `RCPT TO` list.

**Step 6: Server acknowledges the sender**

$$
\text{S:} \;\; \texttt{250 2.1.0 Ok}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Recipient Declaration ŌĆö RCPT TO**

$$
\text{C:} \;\; \texttt{RCPT TO:<bob@kerala.edu>}
$$

*Explanation:* `RCPT TO` may be repeated for multiple recipients (carbon copy at the envelope layer). It also serves as the **relay authorization** check (anti-spam).

**Step 8: Server acknowledges the recipient**

$$
\text{S:} \;\; \texttt{250 2.1.5 Ok}
$$

**Step 9: Data transfer initiation**

$$
\text{C:} \;\; \texttt{DATA}
$$

**Step 10: Server prompts client to send the message**

$$
\text{S:} \;\; \texttt{354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>}
$$

*Explanation:* Reply class `3yz` = Positive Intermediate. The client now streams the message headers and body.

**Step 11: Headers and body (RFC 5322 + MIME)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{From: Alice <alice@client.example.com>} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{To: Bob <bob@kerala.edu>} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Subject: KTU Exam Timetable} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Date: Mon, 18 Nov 2024 09:30:00 +0530} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{MIME-Version: 1.0} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Dear Bob,} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Please find the KTU exam schedule attached.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Regards,} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{Alice}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 12: End of message ŌĆö single dot on a line**

$$
\text{C:} \;\; \texttt{.}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the literal **`CRLF . CRLF`** terminator. Lines beginning with a dot in the body are escaped as `..` (dot-stuffing) to avoid early termination.

**Step 13: Server accepts the message**

$$
\text{S:} \;\; \texttt{250 2.0.0 Ok: queued as 4F7A1B22}
$$

**Step 14: Quit and connection close**

$$
\text{C:} \;\; \texttt{QUIT}
$$

$$
\text{S:} \;\; \texttt{221 2.0.0 Bye}
$$

*Explanation:* `221` is the standard "Service closing transmission channel" reply. TCP `FIN` is then exchanged.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö SMTP vs POP3 vs IMAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Analytical Matrix (High-Yield for KTU 14-mark questions)**

| Feature | **SMTP** | **POP3** | **IMAP4** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction** | Push (clientŌåÆserver) | Pull (clientŌåÉserver) | Pull (clientŌåÉserver) |
| **Default Port** | **25** (587 for submission) | **110** (995 for POP3S) | **143** (993 for IMAPS) |
| **Underlying TCP** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Connection Type** | Persistent during session | Short-lived | Persistent & stateful |
| **Mail Storage** | Transient queue | Downloads to client (server copy deleted) | Server-side permanent |
| **Folder Management** | None | None (one INBOX only) | Full server-side folders |
| **Search Capability** | None | None on server | Powerful `SEARCH` command |
| **Partial Fetch** | N/A | Limited (TOP n lines) | Yes (`FETCH BODY[2]<0.100>`) |
| **Offline Use** | N/A | Excellent (download once) | Limited (cached mode) |
| **Multi-device Sync** | N/A | Poor (downloads delete from server) | Excellent (UID validity) |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | N/A | High initial transfer | Low (only deltas) |
| **Authentication** | Yes (when using submission) | Yes (`USER`/`PASS` or `APOP`) | Yes (`LOGIN`/`AUTHENTICATE`) |
| **TLS Support** | `STARTTLS` | `STLS` (POP3S implicit) | `STARTTLS` (IMAPS implicit) |
| **RFC** | RFC 5321 | RFC 1939 | RFC 3501 |
| **Typical Use Case** | Mail relay between servers | Single-device offline access | Multi-device cloud mail |

### 3.3 POP3 Session ŌĆö Authoritative Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK Dovecot ready.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{CAPA} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK Capabilities follow} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{STLS} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{USER} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{UIDL} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{USER bob} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{PASS secret123} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK Logged in.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{STAT} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK 3 8456} \quad \text{(3 messages, 8456 octets total)} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{LIST} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK 3 messages} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{1 1200} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{2 2500} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{3 4756} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{RETR 1} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK 1200 octets} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{<headers + body of msg 1>} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{DELE 1} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK message 1 deleted} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{QUIT} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{+OK Logging out, messages deleted.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:*
- `STAT` returns `<count> <size>`.
- `RETR` downloads the entire message.
- `DELE` only **marks** for deletion; actual deletion occurs in the **UPDATE** state on `QUIT`.

---

### 3.4 IMAP Session ŌĆö Authoritative Trace (Abbreviated)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* OK [CAPABILITY IMAP4rev1 STARTTLS LOGIN] Dovecot ready.} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{a001 LOGIN bob secret123} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{a001 OK [CAPABILITY IMAP4rev1] Logged in} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{a002 SELECT INBOX} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* FLAGS (\\Answered \\Flagged \\Deleted \\Seen \\Draft)} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* 3 EXISTS} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* 0 RECENT} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{a002 OK [READ-WRITE] SELECT completed} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{a003 UID SEARCH SUBJECT "KTU"} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* SEARCH 1 3} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{a003 OK SEARCH completed} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{a004 UID FETCH 1 (FLAGS BODY[HEADER.FIELDS (SUBJECT)]) } \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* 1 FETCH (UID 1 FLAGS (\\Seen) BODY[HEADER.FIELDS (SUBJECT)] \{31\}} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{Subject: KTU Exam Timetable} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{)} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{a004 OK FETCH completed} \\
\text{C:} \;\; &\texttt{a005 LOGOUT} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{* BYE} \\
\text{S:} \;\; &\texttt{a005 OK LOGOUT completed}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:*
- Every client command carries a **unique alphanumeric tag** (here `a001` ŌĆ” `a005`) for asynchronous correlation.
- Server responses prefixed with `*` are **untagged**; responses with the client's tag are **tagged** and indicate command completion.
- The IMAP **UID** is the key synchronization primitive that survives sessions.

---

### 3.5 Email Header Structure (RFC 5322)

A standard mail header has the form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Header} &= \text{Field-Name} \; \text{":"} \; \text{[Field-Body]} \; \text{CRLF} \\
\text{Required Fields} &= \{\text{Date, From}\} \cup \{\text{one of: To, Cc, Bcc}\} \\
\text{Optional Fields} &= \{\text{Subject, Message-ID, In-Reply-To, References, MIME-*}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **`From:`** ŌĆö Author's display name and address. Used by readers.
- **`Reply-To:`** ŌĆö Address used when the user clicks "Reply".
- **`To:` / `Cc:` / `Bcc:`** ŌĆö Recipients (Bcc is stripped from delivered copies).
- **`Date:`** ŌĆö RFC 2822 timestamp.
- **`Message-ID:`** ŌĆö Globally unique identifier `<local-part@domain>`.
- **`Received:`** ŌĆö Tracing header prepended by each MTA in the path.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Email System ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end email architecture**, illustrating the path of a message from the sender's MUA through multiple MTAs to the recipient's MUA, including the separate retrieval protocols.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    senderUser[Sender User]
    senderMUA[Sender MUA<br/>Outlook Thunderbird]
    senderMSA[Sender MSA<br/>Submission Agent]
    senderMTA1[Sender MTA<br/>Postfix Sendmail]
    dnsServer[DNS Server<br/>MX Lookup]
    relayMTA[Relay MTA<br/>Internet Hop]
    recvMTA[Receiver MTA<br/>Dovecot MTA]
    recvMDA[Receiver MDA<br/>Local Delivery]
    mailbox[(Recipient Mailbox<br/>var mail Maildir)]
    mra[Mail Retrieval Agent<br/>POP3 IMAP Server]
    recvMUA[Recipient MUA<br/>Gmail App]

    senderUser --> senderMUA
    senderMUA -->|SMTP Port 587| senderMSA
    senderMSA --> senderMTA1
    senderMTA1 -->|DNS Query MX| dnsServer
    dnsServer -->|MX Record| senderMTA1
    senderMTA1 -->|SMTP Port 25| relayMTA
    relayMTA -->|SMTP Port 25| recvMTA
    recvMTA --> recvMDA
    recvMDA --> mailbox
    mailbox --> mra
    mra -->|POP3 Port 110 OR IMAP Port 143| recvMUA
    recvMUA --> recipientUser[Recipient User]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The composition happens inside the **Sender MUA** (left side).
2. The first hop is to the **MSA** using **SMTP submission** (port 587, authenticated).
3. The MSA delegates to the **Sender MTA**, which performs a **DNS MX lookup** to locate the destination mail exchanger.
4. The message may traverse **zero or more relay MTAs** (greylisting, anti-spam filtering).
5. The **Receiver MTA** accepts the message and forwards it to the local **MDA**.
6. The **MDA** stores the message in the recipient's **mailbox** (a file system structure).
7. The **MRA** (POP3/IMAP server) makes the mailbox accessible.
8. The **Recipient MUA** retrieves the message using either POP3 or IMAP.

---

### 4.2 SMTP Transaction State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Connect : TCP SYN
    Connect --> Greeting : Server sends 220
    Greeting --> EHLO : Client identifies
    EHLO --> MailTx : 250 reply
    MailTx --> Envelope : MAIL FROM
    Envelope --> Recipients : RCPT TO loop
    Recipients --> Data : 250 reply
    Data --> Streaming : 354 reply
    Streaming --> StreamBody : Client sends headers body
    StreamBody --> Terminated : CRLF dot CRLF
    Terminated --> MailTx : 250 Ok queued
    Terminated --> Quit : QUIT
    Quit --> Closed : 221 Bye
    Closed --> [*] : TCP FIN
```

---

### 4.3 POP3 vs IMAP ŌĆö Access Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    client[MUA Client]
    decision{Which Protocol}
    pop3[POP3 Port 110<br/>Download and Optionally Delete]
    imap[IMAP Port 143<br/>Read on Server Keep Sync]
    local[Local Mail Storage<br/>PST MBOX Files]
    server1[(Server Mailbox<br/>Cleared after retrieval)]
    server2[(Server Mailbox<br/>Retained Synced)]
    multidev[Multiple Devices Always in Sync]

    client --> decision
    decision -->|Single Device Offline| pop3
    decision -->|Multi Device Cloud| imap
    pop3 --> local
    pop3 -.->|Optional Delete| server1
    imap --> server2
    server2 --> multidev
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exam**
> Always annotate arrows with the **port number** and the **protocol name** (e.g., `SMTP/25`, `POP3/110`, `IMAP/143`). Examiners award marks for correctly labeled flow paths.

---

### 4.4 MIME Multipart Message Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootMsg[Email Message]
    headers[RFC 5322 Headers<br/>From To Subject Date]
    mimeHdr[MIME Headers<br/>MIME Version Content Type]
    boundary[multipart mixed boundary]
    part1[Part 1<br/>text plain]
    part2[Part 2<br/>image jpeg base64]
    part3[Part 3<br/>application pdf]

    rootMsg --> headers
    headers --> mimeHdr
    mimeHdr --> boundary
    boundary --> part1
    boundary --> part2
    boundary --> part3
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
> **List the three primary components of the email architecture and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **MUA (Mail User Agent):** End-user application used to compose, read, and manage emails (e.g., Outlook, Thunderbird). *(1 mark)*
2. **MTA (Mail Transfer Agent):** Server-side agent that transfers mail between hosts using the SMTP protocol. *(1 mark)*
3. **MDA (Mail Delivery Agent):** Local delivery agent that deposits the message into the recipient's mailbox file. *(1 mark)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
> **State the default TCP ports used by (a) SMTP, (b) POP3, and (c) IMAP4.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Protocol | Default TCP Port | Secure Variant Port |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (a) **SMTP** | **25** (MTA-MTA), **587** (submission) | **465** (SMTPS) |
| (b) **POP3** | **110** | **995** (POP3S) |
| (c) **IMAP4** | **143** | **993** (IMAPS) |

*(1 mark per row ŌĆö must include both plain and secure ports for full credit.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> **Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, a Module question is worth 14 marks with internal choice. Provide sub-parts of 7 marks each. The following two questions are *alternatives* ŌĆö only one should be answered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the electronic mail system**. List the roles of **MUA, MTA, MDA, and MRA** in the email delivery process. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **SMTP protocol in detail**, including the connection setup, mail transaction, and termination phases. Explain the meaning of the SMTP reply codes `220`, `250`, `354`, and `550`. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Email Architecture (7 marks):**

The email system is a **store-and-forward, asynchronous, application-layer service** built on top of TCP/IP. The functional blocks are:

- **MUA (Mail User Agent):** Runs on the user's device. Composes, reads, and manages messages. Examples: Microsoft Outlook, Mozilla Thunderbird, Gmail Web.
- **MSA (Mail Submission Agent):** Receives mail from the MUA using **SMTP submission (port 587)** with `AUTH` and `STARTTLS`.
- **MTA (Mail Transfer Agent):** Routes the message hop-by-hop across the internet using **SMTP (port 25)**. Performs **DNS MX-record** lookups to find the destination mail server.
- **MDA (Mail Delivery Agent):** Final local agent that places the message into the recipient's mailbox file (e.g., `/var/spool/mail/user` or `~/Maildir/`).
- **MRA (Mail Retrieval Agent):** Server exposing the mailbox to the recipient's MUA via **POP3 (port 110)** or **IMAP (port 143)**.
- **DNS:** Provides the **MX (Mail Exchanger) record** that maps `domain.com ŌåÆ mail.domain.com`.

*(Award 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for each of MUA/MTA/MDA, 1 mark for MRA, 1 mark for DNS, 1 mark for completeness of flow.)*

**(b) SMTP Protocol Details (7 marks):**

**SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol, RFC 5321)** is a **text-based, command-response, push protocol** that uses **TCP** for reliable delivery. A complete SMTP session has three phases:

1. **Connection Setup:** The client opens a TCP connection to port 25. The server replies with `220 <server> ready`. The client sends `EHLO <hostname>` to identify itself. The server returns a multi-line `250` reply listing supported ESMTP extensions (e.g., `STARTTLS`, `SIZE`, `8BITMIME`).

2. **Mail Transaction:** The client issues `MAIL FROM:<sender@domain>` to declare the envelope sender. The server replies `250 OK`. For each recipient, the client issues `RCPT TO:<recipient@domain>`; the server replies `250 OK` if the address is accepted (else `550`).

3. **Data Transfer:** The client sends `DATA`. The server replies `354 Start mail input`. The client then transmits the RFC 5322 headers and MIME body, terminating with the literal sequence **`<CR><LF>.<CR><LF>`**. The server replies `250 OK: queued as <ID>`.

4. **Termination:** The client sends `QUIT`. The server replies `221 Bye` and closes the TCP connection.

**Reply code meanings:**

- `220` ŌĆö *Service ready* (positive completion, connection setup).
- `250` ŌĆö *Requested mail action okay, completed* (positive completion, generic OK).
- `354` ŌĆö *Start mail input; end with <CRLF>.<CRLF>* (positive intermediate, awaiting data).
- `550` ŌĆö *Requested action not taken: mailbox unavailable* (permanent negative; e.g., unknown user).

*(1 mark per phase, 1 mark for TCP usage, 1 mark for ASCII command nature, 1 mark per reply code with meaning.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **POP3 and IMAP4** in terms of architecture, message handling, server interaction, and typical use cases. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **POP3 protocol states and commands**. List at least six POP3 commands with their purpose. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) POP3 vs IMAP4 Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | **POP3** | **IMAP4** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction** | Pull ŌĆö downloads mail to client | Pull ŌĆö reads mail directly on server |
| **Default Port** | 110 (995 for POP3S) | 143 (993 for IMAPS) |
| **Server Storage** | Mail normally deleted after download | Mail permanently stored on server |
| **Folder Support** | Single INBOX only | Server-side folders with full CRUD |
| **Search** | Client-side only | Server-side `SEARCH` command |
| **Partial Fetch** | `TOP n <lines>` only | Granular `FETCH BODY[part]<offset.length>` |
| **Multi-device** | Poor (no sync) | Excellent (UID-based sync) |
| **Offline Use** | Excellent | Limited (requires cached mode) |
| **Connection Lifetime** | Short ŌĆö connect, retrieve, disconnect | Long-lived, stateful session |
| **Best For** | Single-device offline access | Multi-device cloud email |

*(1 mark for each major contrasting row ├Ś 6 rows = full marks; partial credit for fewer rows.)*

**(b) POP3 Protocol States and Commands (7 marks):**

**POP3 (Post Office Protocol Version 3, RFC 1939)** operates as a finite state machine with **three states**:

1. **AUTHORIZATION State:** The client connects to port 110 and the server sends `+OK`. The client authenticates using `USER <name>` followed by `PASS <password>`. (Alternative: `APOP` for challenge-response.) If authentication fails, the server replies `-ERR` and the client retries or `QUIT`s.

2. **TRANSACTION State:** After successful authentication, the client can issue retrieval and management commands. The mailbox is locked, and the client may request statistics (`STAT`), list messages (`LIST`), retrieve (`RETR n`), mark for deletion (`DELE n`), or request headers only (`TOP n <lines>`). All commands receive `+OK` or `-ERR`.

3. **UPDATE State:** Triggered by the client's `QUIT` command. The server executes all previously marked deletions, releases the mailbox lock, and closes the TCP connection.

**Six essential POP3 commands (with purpose):**

- `USER <name>` ŌĆö Provide the mailbox username. *(1 mark)*
- `PASS <password>` ŌĆö Provide the mailbox password. *(1 mark)*
- `STAT` ŌĆö Return the count and total size of messages in mailbox. *(1 mark)*
- `LIST [n]` ŌĆö List message sizes (or size of message n if specified). *(1 mark)*
- `RETR n` ŌĆö Retrieve the full content of message n. *(1 mark)*
- `DELE n` ŌĆö Mark message n for deletion (executed on `QUIT`). *(1 mark)*
- `QUIT` ŌĆö Commit deletions and close the session. *(Bonus credit)*

*(1 mark for each state explanation with transition, 1 mark for command list quality ŌĆö total 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing SMTP submission port (587) with MTA-MTA port (25).** Examiners specifically test this distinction. Memorize both.
> 2. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table.** This breaks the table renderer. Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX contexts.
> 3. **Forgetting the DATA terminator** `<CR><LF>.<CR><LF>`. A vague "end with a dot" loses marks ŌĆö write the full terminator.
> 4. **Mixing up `250` and `550` reply semantics.** `250` = success, `550` = permanent failure (e.g., user unknown). Do not interchange.
> 5. **Claiming POP3 supports folders.** It does not ŌĆö only IMAP does. Examiners dock marks for this mistake.
> 6. **Saying SMTP uses UDP.** It does not ŌĆö SMTP uses **TCP** for reliability. UDP-based mail is not standard.
> 7. **Omitting the `EHLO` vs `HELO` distinction.** `EHLO` initiates **Extended SMTP (ESMTP)** which is the modern default.
> 8. **Forgetting MIME in multimedia handling.** SMTP itself is 7-bit ASCII; multimedia is only possible because of **MIME** encoding.
> 9. **Drawing diagrams without labels.** Always label protocol names and port numbers on the arrows.
> 10. **Skipping the UPDATE state in POP3.** It is the state where deletions actually occur ŌĆö a frequent trick question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**
>
> Ō£ģ **Email is an asynchronous application-layer service built on TCP/IP.**
>
> Ō£ģ **Three core protocols: SMTP (push), POP3 (pull-download), IMAP4 (pull-manage).**
>
> Ō£ģ **Default Ports ŌĆö MUST memorize:** SMTP=**25** (587 submission, 465 SMTPS), POP3=**110** (995 POP3S), IMAP=**143** (993 IMAPS).
>
> Ō£ģ **MUA ŌåÆ MSA ŌåÆ MTA ŌåÆ (relay MTAs) ŌåÆ MDA ŌåÆ Mailbox ŌåÆ MRA ŌåÆ MUA.**
>
> Ō£ģ **SMTP uses TCP, is line-oriented ASCII, command-response (e.g., `EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `QUIT`).**
>
> Ō£ģ **DATA terminator is the literal byte sequence `<CR><LF>.<CR><LF>`.**
>
> Ō£ģ **SMTP reply codes: 2yz = success, 3yz = intermediate, 4yz = transient failure, 5yz = permanent failure.**
>
> Ō£ģ **POP3 has three states: AUTHORIZATION ŌåÆ TRANSACTION ŌåÆ UPDATE (deletions happen here).**
>
> Ō£ģ **POP3 typical commands: USER, PASS, STAT, LIST, RETR, DELE, QUIT, TOP, UIDL, NOOP, RSET.**
>
> Ō£ģ **IMAP leaves mail on the server, supports folders, server-side search, partial fetch, UID-based sync.**
>
> Ō£ģ **IMAP commands are tagged (e.g., `a001 LOGIN`) and responses are tagged or untagged (`*`).**
>
> Ō£ģ **MIME (RFC 2045ŌĆō2049) is mandatory for non-ASCII content** ŌĆö uses `Content-Type`, `Content-Transfer-Encoding` (base64 / quoted-printable), and `multipart/*` boundaries.
>
> Ō£ģ **RFC standards: RFC 5321 (SMTP), RFC 5322 (Message Format), RFC 1939 (POP3), RFC 3501 (IMAP4), RFC 2045ŌĆō2049 (MIME).**
>
> Ō£ģ **DNS MX-record lookup is what MTAs use to route mail** to the correct destination server.
>
> Ō£ģ **Security extensions: STARTTLS (opportunistic TLS), SMTPS, POP3S, IMAPS, SPF, DKIM, DMARC** ŌĆö beyond scope but good to know.
>
> Ō£ģ **POP3 = best for single-device offline access. IMAP = best for multi-device cloud sync.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Wide Web (WWW) - Architectural Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** World Wide Web (WWW) - Architectural Overview # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is a distributed, hypertext-based, client-server application system that operates as a global information space over the Internet, allowing linked digital documents and resources (web pages, images, videos, APIs) to be retrieved, rendered, and interacted with by client software (web browsers) using standardized protocols ŌĆö most notably the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)**. Architecturally, the WWW is **not** the Internet itself; rather, it is a **distributed application layer service** that *uses* the Internet as its underlying transport infrastructure. The Internet provides the pipes; the WWW provides the content and the means to navigate it. The four foundational pillars of the WWW architecture are: 1. **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** ŌĆö the addressing/naming scheme. 2. **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** ŌĆö the request-response application protocol. 3. **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** ŌĆö the document/content representation format. 4. **Web Server (Daemon)** ŌĆö the software entity that hosts and serves resources. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:** The WWW is a **distributed hypermedia system** built on top of the Internet, enabling the retrieval and display of documents containing *hyperlinks* via the **client-server model** using **HTTP/HTML/URL**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The WWW as a Global Library" Imagine the WWW is a **giant, self-locating library without walls**: | WWW Component | Library Analogy | Function | |---------------|-----------------|----------| | Web Server | The librarian's desk | Stores and dispenses books | | URL | The call number of a book (e.g., `Section 4, Shelf 12, Book 78`) | A unique, structured address for every resource | | HTTP | The verbal request/response protocol between you and the librarian | A standardised language for asking and receiving | | HTML | The printed, formatted pages inside a book | The content presentation format | | Browser | You, the reader | Renders content and lets you click "see also" footnotes | | Hyperlink | A footnote that, when followed, takes you to another book or chapter | Non-linear navigation between resources | > [!NOTE] > Just as you don't need to know *how* the library acquires its books (the plumbing) ŌĆö you only need the *call number* (URL), the *language* (HTTP), and the *ability to read* (browser rendering) ŌĆö the WWW cleanly separates **transport** (Internet), **naming** (URL), **protocol** (HTTP), and **content** (HTML). ## 1.3 Why the WWW is Architecturally Significant - It is the **largest distributed application** ever deployed in human history. - It demonstrated the viability of **stateless request-response** communication at planetary scale. - It pioneered the **hyperlink** as a first-class primitive, enabling associative navigation. - It established the **client-server model** as the default architectural pattern for the modern Internet. > [!TIP] > **Boundary Fact for KTU Board Answers:** The WWW was proposed by **Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989**. The first browser, the *WorldWideWeb* (later renamed *Nexus*), and the first web server (*info.cern.ch*) were both created by him in **1990**. The first public web page went live in **August 1991**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The WWW Client-Server Model ŌĆö Operational Walk-Through The WWW is a **classic synchronous, pull-based, client-server architecture**. Below is the full logical flow of a single user interaction (e.g., typing `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus`): ### Step 1: User Intent Initiation The user enters a URL in the **address bar** of a browser (e.g., Chrome, Firefox). The browser is the **WWW client**. ### Step 2: URL Parsing The URL is parsed into its constituent parts: $$ \text{scheme} :// \text{host} [:\text{port}] / \text{path} [? \text{query}] [\# \text{fragment}] $$ For `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus/cs/2024`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{scheme} &= \text{https (HTTP over TLS/SSL)} \\ \text{host} &= \text{www.ktu.edu.in} \\ \text{port} &= \text{443 (default for HTTPS)} \\ \text{path} &= \text{/syllabus/cs/2024} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: DNS Resolution The client extracts the hostname `www.ktu.edu.in` and asks the **Domain Name System (DNS)** to resolve it to an **IP address** (e.g., `192.0.2.45`). This is the *name-to-address* mapping step, sitting one layer below the WWW proper. ### Step 4: TCP Connection Establishment Because HTTP is built on top of TCP, a **three-way handshake** is performed: - Client ŌåÆ Server: `SYN` - Server ŌåÆ Client: `SYN-ACK` - Client ŌåÆ Server: `ACK` The default TCP port for HTTP is **80**; for HTTPS, it is **443**. ### Step 5: HTTP Request Transmission The browser constructs and sends an **HTTP request message**: ```text GET /syllabus/cs/2024 HTTP/1.1 Host: www.ktu.edu.in User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (...) Accept: text/html Connection: keep-alive ``` The verb `GET` requests retrieval; alternatives include `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`. ### Step 6: Server-Side Processing The **web server software** (e.g., Apache, Nginx, Microsoft IIS) accepts the request, looks up the resource (either a static file on disk or a dynamic script), executes any required server-side logic (PHP, Python, Node.js, Java), and prepares an HTTP response. ### Step 7: HTTP Response Transmission The server returns an HTTP response: ```text HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 Content-Length: 5840 ... ``` The **status code** `200` indicates success. The well-known code classes are: | Class | Range | Meaning | |-------|-------|---------| | 1xx | 100ŌĆō199 | Informational | | 2xx | 200ŌĆō299 | Success (e.g., 200 OK, 201 Created) | | 3xx | 300ŌĆō399 | Redirection (e.g., 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified) | | 4xx | 400ŌĆō499 | Client Error (e.g., 404 Not Found, 403 Forbidden) | | 5xx | 500ŌĆō599 | Server Error (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) | ### Step 8: Browser Rendering Engine The browser's **rendering engine** (e.g., Blink, Gecko, WebKit) parses the HTML, builds a **DOM tree**, fetches sub-resources (CSS, JS, images), and paints the final visual representation on screen. > [!NOTE] > **Why "stateless"?** Each HTTP request is *independent*. The server retains no memory of past requests from the same client. State is re-created per request using mechanisms such as **cookies**, **URL parameters**, or **sessions**. --- ## 2.2 Core Architectural Components ŌĆö Theory Summary ### 2.2.1 Uniform Resource Locator (URL) A URL is a **string identifier** that uniquely names a resource on the WWW. Its grammar (RFC 3986) is: $$ URI = \text{scheme} : \text{hier-part} \; [? \text{query}] \; [\# \text{fragment}] $$ It serves three functions: **identification** (what the resource is), **location** (where to find it), and **access mechanism** (how to reach it). ### 2.2.2 HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP) HTTP is an **application-layer protocol** defined in the IETF standards track. It follows a strict **request-response** model where the client always initiates communication. Key properties: - **Stateless**: No built-in memory of prior requests. - **Connectionless at the request level**: Persistent connections (`keep-alive`) are an optimisation, not a requirement. - **Media-independent**: Carries any MIME-typed payload. ### 2.2.3 HyperText Markup Language (HTML) HTML is a **declarative markup language** based on **SGML** (later HTML5 on a custom XML-like grammar). It allows text to be annotated with tags that describe structure (`

`, `

`) and embed references to other resources (``, ``). ### 2.2.4 Web Server A **web server** is a long-running daemon process that listens on a TCP port (80/443) and serves HTTP requests. Modern web servers often act as **reverse proxies** in front of application servers for load balancing, TLS termination, and static-asset acceleration. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Fact Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below consolidates every **numerical, port-based, and code-based constant** that the KTU board has historically asked in ESE questions on this topic. **Memorise it.** | Parameter | Value / Format | Purpose / KTU Use | |-----------|----------------|-------------------| | Default HTTP port | **80 (TCP)** | Plaintext HTTP server port | | Default HTTPS port | **443 (TCP)** | TLS-encrypted HTTP server port | | Default URL scheme | `http://` or `https://` | Identifies protocol to client | | DNS resolution port | **53 (UDP/TCP)** | Used in step 3 of the request flow | | HTTP version (current) | HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, **HTTP/3** (over QUIC) | KTU may ask which is the latest | | Status code 200 | OK | Successful resource retrieval | | Status code 301 | Moved Permanently | Permanent URL redirection | | Status code 404 | Not Found | Resource absent on server | | Status code 500 | Internal Server Error | Server-side application crash | | TCP handshake | 3 packets (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | Cost of establishing HTTP session | | Average RTT for a web request | 50ŌĆō300 ms (regional), 300+ ms (trans-continental) | Drives user-perceived latency | | MIME type for HTML | `text/html` | Tells the browser how to render | | MIME type for JSON | `application/json` | API responses | | MIME type for PNG | `image/png` | Static images | | RFC for HTTP/1.1 | **RFC 7230 ŌĆō RFC 7235** | Examiner's favourite reference | | RFC for URI generic syntax | **RFC 3986** | URL grammar specification | | Statelessness of HTTP | **Yes, by design** | High-frequency board question | | HTTP request verbs | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, PATCH, OPTIONS | RESTful architecture | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, the WWW architecture is the basis of: - **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart) ŌĆö multi-tier client-server. - **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** (Google Workspace, Microsoft 365). - **Single Page Applications (SPAs)** ŌĆö modern web apps using AJAX/REST. - **Microservices & Web APIs** ŌĆö HTTP as the universal glue language. - **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)** ŌĆö distributed web caching at the edge. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** Almost every modern distributed system (gRPC, GraphQL, WebSockets) inherits its core design patterns ŌĆö statelessness, request-response, MIME typing ŌĆö directly from the original WWW architectural model proposed by Tim Berners-Lee. Understanding the WWW is therefore **not merely history**; it is the conceptual key to understanding **every modern API**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Time Required to Fetch a Web Object This is a classic **KTU board derivation** that combines transport-layer timing with application-layer semantics. We will derive the total time to fetch a single HTTP object from a remote server. ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup > A client requests a single HTTP object of size $S$ bytes from a web server. The one-way propagation delay between client and server is $d_{prop}$ seconds. The transmission rate of the bottleneck link is $R$ bits/second. The control plane (DNS + TCP handshake) takes $T_{setup}$ seconds. Neglect packet headers and assume HTTP/1.1 with a single persistent connection. **Given:** $S$ (object size), $R$ (link rate), $d_{prop}$ (one-way propagation), $T_{setup}$ (control-plane delay). **Find:** Total elapsed time $T_{total}$ to fetch the object. --- ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 ŌĆö Control Plane Delay (DNS + TCP Three-Way Handshake)** The client must first resolve the hostname (DNS) and establish a TCP connection. For a single new connection over a single RTT, the cost is: $$ T_{setup} = 1 \cdot RTT = 2 \cdot d_{prop} \quad \text{(TCP handshake only, ignoring DNS for simplicity)} $$ If we include DNS resolution as an additional full round trip: $$ T_{setup} = RTT_{DNS} + RTT_{TCP} = 2 \cdot d_{prop,DNS} + 2 \cdot d_{prop,TCP} $$ For the standard KTU textbook (Kurose & Ross), the control plane is abstracted as a single delay term $T_{setup}$. **Step 2 ŌĆö HTTP Request Transmission** The HTTP request itself is small (typically < 1 KB). Since transmission time is $S/R$ and $S$ is tiny, the request is delivered in negligible time. The request reaches the server after one-way propagation: $$ T_{req} = d_{prop} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Server Processing Time** The server must read the request, look up the file, and start transmitting. We model this as $T_{server}$ (often negligible compared to network delays, hence omitted in simplified derivations). **Step 4 ŌĆö HTTP Response Transmission (First Bit)** The first bit of the response must travel across the link. Its arrival time at the client is: $$ T_{first\_bit} = d_{prop} + \frac{S}{R} $$ This follows the standard **transmission + propagation** model where transmission time is file size divided by link bandwidth, and propagation time is the one-way delay. **Step 5 ŌĆö HTTP Request-Response Round Trip** The total round-trip time for the request-response exchange (ignoring server processing) is: $$ \begin{aligned} RTT_{HTTP} &= T_{req} + T_{resp} \\ &= d_{prop} + \left( d_{prop} + \frac{S}{R} \right) \\ &= 2 \cdot d_{prop} + \frac{S}{R} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Total Time (Persistent Connection, Single Object)** Summing the control plane and the request-response: $$ \boxed{T_{total} = T_{setup} + RTT_{HTTP} = 2 \cdot d_{prop} + \left( 2 \cdot d_{prop} + \frac{S}{R} \right) = 4 \cdot d_{prop} + \frac{S}{R}} $$ If we treat DNS+TCP as two separate RTTs (3-way handshake + DNS), the generalised form is: $$ T_{total} = 2 \cdot RTT_{DNS} + RTT_{TCP} + RTT_{HTTP} + \frac{S}{R} $$ **Step 7 ŌĆö For Multiple Objects (Non-Pipelined HTTP/1.0)** If the page contains $N$ objects, each requires its own RTT (assuming serial, non-pipelined fetching): $$ T_{total,N} = T_{setup} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( 2 \cdot d_{prop} + \frac{S_i}{R} \right) = T_{setup} + N \cdot 2 \cdot d_{prop} + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} S_i}{R} $$ **Step 8 ŌĆö For Multiple Objects (Pipelined HTTP/1.1)** With pipelining, all $N$ requests are sent back-to-back, so only one RTT penalty is paid: $$ T_{total,N}^{pipe} = T_{setup} + 2 \cdot d_{prop} + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} S_i}{R} $$ **Step 9 ŌĆö Numerical Example (KTU Favourite)** Suppose $S = 1$ MB $= 8 \times 10^6$ bits, $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps, $d_{prop} = 50$ ms $= 0.05$ s, and $T_{setup}$ accounts for DNS + TCP (2 RTTs). $$ \begin{aligned} T_{setup} &= 2 \cdot 2 \cdot d_{prop} = 4 \cdot 0.05 = 0.20 \text{ s} \\ T_{trans} &= \frac{S}{R} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 8 \text{ s} \\ T_{total} &= 0.20 + 0.05 + 8 = 8.25 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ The **transmission time dominates** for large objects, while the **propagation time** dominates for small, latency-sensitive requests (e.g., REST API calls). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Static vs. Dynamic Web Content | Dimension | Static Web Page | Dynamic Web Page | |-----------|-----------------|------------------| | Content source | Fixed file on disk (e.g., `.html`, `.css`, `.jpg`) | Generated on-the-fly by server-side code (PHP, Python, Java, Node.js) | | Server processing | Minimal ŌĆö direct file I/O | Heavy ŌĆö script execution, database queries, template rendering | | Response time | Predictable, fast | Variable, depends on DB load | | Personalisation | None | Per-user (e.g., username, recommendations) | | Caching | Highly cacheable | Generally non-cacheable (unless explicitly designed) | | HTTP status typical | 200 OK | 200 OK with dynamic `Content-Type` | | Example | Wikipedia revision page (after caching) | Your KTU portal dashboard after login | | MIME type | `text/html` (fixed) | `text/html` (generated) | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Caching, Cookies, and Proxies | Mechanism | Layer | Purpose | KTU Insight | |-----------|-------|---------|-------------| | **Web Cache (Proxy Cache)** | Application | Stores recent responses closer to client to reduce latency and server load | Conditional GETs use `If-Modified-Since` header | | **Cookie** | Application | Tiny stateful token stored by client; sent back to server with every request | Enables session tracking despite HTTP being stateless | | **Reverse Proxy** | Network/Application | Fronts a web server to provide load balancing, TLS termination, and caching | Often implemented using Nginx or HAProxy | | **CDN** | Application/Network | Geographically distributed caches that serve content from the nearest edge | Akamai, Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront | | **Load Balancer** | Network | Distributes incoming HTTP requests across multiple server replicas | Round-robin, least-connections, consistent hashing | --- ## 3.4 Conditional GET and Cache Validation (Important KTU Topic) When a cached copy exists but may be stale, the client issues a **conditional GET**: ```text GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 Host: www.ktu.edu.in If-Modified-Since: Sun, 10 Mar 2024 12:00:00 GMT ``` If the file has not changed, the server replies: ```text HTTP/1.1 304 Not Modified ``` with **no body**, saving bandwidth. This is the **fundamental web caching primitive** that the KTU board loves to test. $$ \text{Bandwidth Saved} = S_{object} \quad \text{(entire object size is saved on a 304)} $$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WWW Architectural Block Diagram The diagram below depicts the **end-to-end WWW request flow** from the user's keystroke to the rendered page, including all architectural components and supporting infrastructure. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Types URL in Browser] --> B[Browser URL Parser] B --> C{URL Valid?} C -- No --> Z1[Display Error: Malformed URL] C -- Yes --> D[DNS Resolver] D --> E[DNS Server Hierarchy] E --> F[Resolved IP Address Returned] F --> G[TCP Three-Way Handshake] G --> H[HTTP Request Message Constructed] H --> I[Internet / Transport Network] I --> J[Web Server Daemon on Port 80 or 443] J --> K{Static or Dynamic Resource} K -- Static --> L1[Read File from Disk] K -- Dynamic --> L2[Invoke CGI Script or App Server] L2 --> M[Database Query] M --> N[Generate HTML Response] L1 --> N N --> O[HTTP Response with Status Code] O --> P[Browser Receives Response] P --> Q[Rendering Engine Parses HTML] Q --> R[Build DOM Tree] R --> S[Fetch Sub-Resources CSS JS Images] S --> T[Paint Final Web Page on Screen] ``` --- ## 4.2 Client-Server Component Architecture This diagram shows the **layered architecture of the WWW** ŌĆö separating client-side, server-side, and protocol layers. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CLIENT_SIDE [Client Side Browser] C1[Address Bar and URL Input] C2[Rendering Engine] C3[JavaScript Engine] C4[Network Stack] C5[Cookie Storage] end subgraph NETWORK_LAYER [Network and Transport Layer] N1[DNS Resolver] N2[TCP IP Stack] N3[TLS SSL for HTTPS] end subgraph SERVER_SIDE [Server Side] S1[Web Server Daemon Nginx Apache] S2[Application Server] S3[Database Engine] S4[File System for Static Assets] end C1 --> C2 C2 --> C4 C4 --> N1 N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 N3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S2 --> S4 S3 --> S2 S2 --> S1 S1 --> N3 N3 --> C4 C4 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C2 --> C5 ``` --- ## 4.3 State Diagram of a Single HTTP Transaction ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> DNSResolving: URL entered DNSResolving --> TCPSetup: IP address obtained TCPSetup --> SendingRequest: SYN ACK complete SendingRequest --> WaitingResponse: HTTP request sent WaitingResponse --> ReceivingHeaders: Status line and headers received ReceivingHeaders --> ReceivingBody: Body transfer starts ReceivingBody --> Rendering: All bytes received Rendering --> Idle: Page displayed WaitingResponse --> Error: Timeout or 4xx 5xx ReceivingHeaders --> Error: 4xx 5xx status code Error --> Idle: Browser shows error page Rendering --> [*]: User navigates away ``` --- ## 4.4 WWW Tier Architecture (Multi-Tier Web Application) Modern web applications are rarely a single client-server pair. They follow a **multi-tier architecture**: ```mermaid flowchart TB U[End User] --> LB[Load Balancer] LB --> W1[Web Server 1] LB --> W2[Web Server 2] LB --> W3[Web Server 3] W1 --> APP1[App Server 1] W2 --> APP2[App Server 2] W3 --> APP3[App Server 3] APP1 --> DB1[(Primary Database)] APP2 --> DB1 APP3 --> DB1 APP1 --> DB2[(Read Replica)] APP2 --> DB2 APP3 --> DB2 APP1 --> CACHE[(Redis or Memcached)] APP2 --> CACHE APP3 --> CACHE ``` --- > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Answers:** When asked to "explain WWW architecture with a diagram" in the ESE, you are expected to draw **at minimum**: a *client block* (browser), *server block* (web server), the *protocol arrow* labelled HTTP, and *supporting services* (DNS, database, proxy). The diagrams above give you the **canonical KTU blueprint** ŌĆö replicate the structure, label every block, and you will secure full marks. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style** ### Question 1: Differentiate between the World Wide Web (WWW) and the Internet. Is one a subset of the other? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The **Internet** is the global, packet-switched network infrastructure that interconnects millions of devices using the **TCP/IP protocol suite**. It is a *network of networks*, providing generic connectivity services such as email, file transfer, and web access. The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is a specific *application* that runs on top of the Internet, using HTTP, HTML, and URLs to enable the retrieval and display of hyperlinked documents. **Key Differences:** | Aspect | Internet | WWW | |--------|----------|-----| | Nature | Infrastructure (network) | Application / service | | Protocol | TCP/IP (transport) | HTTP (application) | | Scope | Email, FTP, SSH, video, etc. | Web pages and web APIs | | Relationship | Substrate | Built on the substrate | **Conclusion:** The WWW is a **subsystem** of the Internet, not the other way around. The Internet can exist without the WWW, but the WWW cannot exist without the Internet. --- ### Question 2: Explain why HTTP is called a "stateless" protocol. How is state maintained in practice despite this design? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** HTTP is **stateless** because each request from a client to a server is **processed independently** ŌĆö the server retains **no memory** of any previous requests from the same client. Every request must contain all the information the server needs to fulfil it. **Advantages of statelessness:** Simpler server design, high scalability, easy load balancing, and fault tolerance. **How state is achieved in practice (despite statelessness):** 1. **Cookies** ŌĆö small key-value tokens stored by the client and sent back with every request. 2. **URL Parameters** ŌĆö `?sessionid=abc123` appended to URLs. 3. **Hidden Form Fields** ŌĆö ``. 4. **Server-side Sessions** ŌĆö server maintains a session table keyed by a session ID. 5. **HTTP Authentication Headers** ŌĆö `Authorization: Bearer ` carrying JWTs or OAuth tokens. --- ## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 / Model Paper Style** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): WWW Architectural Overview and Request-Response Flow** **Part (a)** ŌĆö [7 Marks] ŌĆö With a neat block diagram, describe the **client-server architecture of the World Wide Web**. Explain the role of each component: **browser, URL, HTTP, web server, and HTML**. **Part (b)** ŌĆö [7 Marks] ŌĆö A client fetches a web page consisting of **1 base HTML object of 50 KB** and **5 embedded image objects of 200 KB each** from a remote server. The one-way propagation delay is **80 ms**, the link bandwidth is **2 Mbps**, and the TCP connection setup (including DNS) takes **2 RTTs**. Compute the **total time** to retrieve the entire page assuming: (i) **non-persistent HTTP**, and (ii) **persistent HTTP with pipelining**. State one real-world advantage of pipelining. --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) ŌĆö Block Diagram and Component Roles** The WWW follows a **client-server architecture** as shown below (verbal description; see Section 4.1 for full diagram): 1. **Browser (Client)** ŌĆö the user-facing software (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) that issues HTTP requests, parses HTML responses, and renders pages. Internally contains a rendering engine, a JavaScript engine, and a cookie store. 2. **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** ŌĆö a structured string that uniquely identifies every resource on the web. It has the form: $$\text{scheme} :// \text{host} [:\text{port}] / \text{path} [? \text{query}] [\# \text{fragment}]$$ The browser parses the URL into its components to determine *which* server to contact, *which* port, and *which* resource to request. 3. **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** ŌĆö the **application-layer, request-response protocol** that defines how messages are formatted and exchanged. The browser sends an `HTTP GET` request, and the server replies with an `HTTP 200 OK` response carrying the HTML body. HTTP is **stateless**, **text-based**, and **connection-oriented (over TCP)**. 4. **Web Server (Daemon)** ŌĆö a long-running server-side process (Apache, Nginx, IIS) listening on port **80 (HTTP)** or **443 (HTTPS)**. It accepts incoming TCP connections, reads the HTTP request, locates or generates the resource, and returns the HTTP response. 5. **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** ŌĆö the **markup language** used to structure the content. The server returns HTML in the response body, the browser parses it into a **DOM tree**, fetches referenced sub-resources (CSS, JS, images), and renders the final visible page. **Component Interaction Flow:** 1. User enters URL ŌåÆ Browser parses URL. 2. Browser performs DNS lookup of the hostname. 3. Browser establishes TCP connection (3-way handshake). 4. Browser sends `GET HTTP/1.1` request. 5. Web server processes request, retrieves/generates resource. 6. Server returns `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` with HTML body. 7. Browser renders the HTML; sub-objects fetched in turn. **Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation** **Given Data:** - 1 HTML object: $S_0 = 50$ KB $= 50 \times 1024 \times 8 = 409,600$ bits - 5 image objects: $S_i = 200$ KB $= 200 \times 1024 \times 8 = 1,638,400$ bits each - Propagation delay: $d_{prop} = 80$ ms $= 0.08$ s - Bandwidth: $R = 2$ Mbps $= 2 \times 10^6$ bps - Setup: 2 RTTs for DNS + TCP **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Common Quantities** Total bytes: $S_{total} = 50 + 5 \times 200 = 1050$ KB $= 1050 \times 1024 \times 8 = 8,601,600$ bits Transmission time for the entire page: $$T_{trans,total} = \frac{S_{total}}{R} = \frac{8,601,600}{2 \times 10^6} = 4.3008 \text{ s}$$ Round-trip time: $$RTT = 2 \cdot d_{prop} = 2 \times 0.08 = 0.16 \text{ s}$$ Setup time: $$T_{setup} = 2 \cdot RTT = 2 \times 0.16 = 0.32 \text{ s}$$ **Case (i) ŌĆö Non-Persistent HTTP (Sequential, Each Object New Connection)** In non-persistent HTTP, **each of the 6 objects (1 HTML + 5 images) requires a separate TCP connection**. Each connection pays: - 1 RTT for TCP handshake - 1 RTT for HTTP request-response (ignoring tiny request transmission) So each object costs $2 \times RTT$ of overhead, plus its own transmission time. For each object $i$ with size $S_i$: $$T_i = 2 \cdot RTT + \frac{S_i}{R}$$ Total time: $$T_{non-persistent} = T_{setup} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \left( 2 \cdot RTT + \frac{S_i}{R} \right)$$ $$= 0.32 + 6 \cdot (2 \times 0.16) + \frac{8,601,600}{2 \times 10^6}$$ $$= 0.32 + 6 \cdot 0.32 + 4.3008$$ $$= 0.32 + 1.92 + 4.3008$$ $$\boxed{T_{non-persistent} \approx 6.54 \text{ seconds}}$$ **Case (ii) ŌĆö Persistent HTTP with Pipelining** In persistent HTTP with pipelining, **all requests are sent back-to-back over a single TCP connection**. Only **one RTT** is paid for the HTTP request-response exchange, after the initial setup. $$T_{persistent} = T_{setup} + 1 \cdot RTT + \frac{S_{total}}{R}$$ $$= 0.32 + 0.16 + 4.3008$$ $$\boxed{T_{persistent} \approx 4.78 \text{ seconds}}$$ **Real-World Advantage of Pipelining:** Reduces the **per-object latency overhead** by amortising the RTT penalty across all objects. In high-latency environments (e.g., satellite, trans-Pacific links), pipelining can yield **40ŌĆō60% improvement** in perceived page load time. Modern HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 protocols **multiplex** requests at the framing layer to overcome the **head-of-line blocking** limitation of HTTP/1.1 pipelining. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): HTTP Mechanics, Statelessness, and Caching** **Part (a)** ŌĆö [7 Marks] ŌĆö Describe the **format of an HTTP request and response message** with a neat diagram. Explain the significance of the following status codes: **200, 301, 304, 404, 500**. Why is HTTP/2 considered superior to HTTP/1.1? **Part (b)** ŌĆö [7 Marks] ŌĆö Explain the concept of **web caching with a proxy**. How does the **conditional GET** mechanism work, and how does it save bandwidth? A cache has a **hit rate of 0.4**. If the access link bandwidth is **1.5 Mbps** and the average object size is **200 KB**, compute the **percentage reduction in access link utilisation** when the cache is deployed. Assume requests arrive at a rate of **20 requests per second**. --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) ŌĆö HTTP Message Format and Status Codes** **HTTP Request Format:** ``` ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Request Line: METHOD SP URL SP HTTP-Version Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż Ōöé Header Line 1: Host: www.example.com Ōöé Ōöé Header Line 2: User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 Ōöé Ōöé Header Line 3: Accept: text/html Ōöé Ōöé ... more headers ... Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż Ōöé (Empty Line) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż Ōöé Body (optional, e.g., for POST requests) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ``` **HTTP Response Format:** ``` ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Status Line: HTTP-Version SP Code SP Phrase Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż Ōöé Header Line 1: Content-Type: text/html Ōöé Ōöé Header Line 2: Content-Length: 5840 Ōöé Ōöé ... more headers ... Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż Ōöé (Empty Line) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż Ōöé Body (the requested resource) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ``` **Status Code Significance:** | Code | Phrase | Significance | |------|--------|--------------| | 200 | OK | Request succeeded; resource returned in body | | 301 | Moved Permanently | Resource has a new permanent URL; client should update bookmarks | | 304 | Not Modified | Cached copy is still valid; do not re-download (saves bandwidth) | | 404 | Not Found | Server cannot locate the requested resource | | 500 | Internal Server Error | Server-side code crashed; not the client's fault | **HTTP/2 vs HTTP/1.1:** HTTP/2 (RFC 7540) introduced: - **Binary framing** instead of textual framing ŌĆö faster to parse. - **Multiplexed streams** ŌĆö many requests on one TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking. - **Header compression** using HPACK ŌĆö saves bandwidth on repeated headers. - **Server push** ŌĆö server can proactively send resources before the client requests them. - **Stream prioritisation** ŌĆö important resources load first. **Part (b) ŌĆö Web Caching and Conditional GET** A **web cache (proxy cache)** is an intermediate entity that stores copies of recently fetched HTTP objects closer to the client. When a client requests a URL: 1. The browser first contacts the **cache**. 2. If the cache has the object (a **cache hit**), it returns the object directly. 3. If not (a **cache miss**), the cache forwards the request to the origin server, stores the response, and returns it to the client. **Conditional GET Mechanism:** When the cache already has a copy, but it may be stale, it issues a conditional request: ``` GET /page.html HTTP/1.1 Host: example.com If-Modified-Since: Wed, 21 Oct 2023 07:28:00 GMT ``` If the object has not been modified, the server responds with: ``` HTTP/1.1 304 Not Modified (empty body) ``` This saves the **entire object body** from being retransmitted ŌĆö only the headers travel. Bandwidth saved = full object size per 304 response. **Numerical Computation ŌĆö Cache Bandwidth Reduction:** **Given:** - Hit rate: $h = 0.4$ (40% of requests served by cache) - Access link bandwidth: $R = 1.5$ Mbps - Average object size: $S = 200$ KB $= 200 \times 1024 \times 8 = 1,638,400$ bits - Request rate: $\lambda = 20$ requests/second **Step 1 ŌĆö Traffic Without Cache** All requests traverse the access link: $$T_{no\_cache} = \lambda \cdot S = 20 \times 1,638,400 = 32,768,000 \text{ bps} = 32.768 \text{ Mbps}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Traffic With Cache** Only cache misses traverse the access link: $$T_{with\_cache} = (1 - h) \cdot \lambda \cdot S = 0.6 \times 20 \times 1,638,400 = 19,660,800 \text{ bps} = 19.66 \text{ Mbps}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Percentage Reduction** $$\text{Reduction} = \frac{T_{no\_cache} - T_{with\_cache}}{T_{no\_cache}} \times 100$$ $$= \frac{32.768 - 19.66}{32.768} \times 100 = \frac{13.108}{32.768} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{Reduction} \approx 40\%}$$ **Note:** The percentage reduction in link utilisation equals the **hit rate** exactly (here 40%) because the cache eliminates a fraction $h$ of the traffic from the access link. This is a fundamental result: **cache hit rate = fractional bandwidth reduction on the access link**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing WWW with Internet:** Writing "WWW is the Internet" is the #1 mark-killer. Always clarify that the **Internet is the infrastructure** and the **WWW is an application built on it**. > 2. **Forgetting TCP setup in timing calculations:** Board answers often forget to add the **TCP handshake + DNS resolution delay** before the HTTP request-response. Always include $T_{setup}$ explicitly. > 3. **Treating HTTP as stateful:** HTTP is **stateless** by design. Mention cookies, sessions, or JWTs when explaining how state is achieved. > 4. **Confusing 301 and 304:** 301 is a **permanent** redirect (URL moved); 304 is a **cache validation** response (object unchanged). Examiners deduct 0.5ŌĆō1 mark for this mix-up. > 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A WWW question without a labelled block diagram typically loses **2ŌĆō3 marks** out of 14. Always draw: Browser ŌåÆ HTTP ŌåÆ Web Server ŌåÆ Database/Resources. > 6. **Mismatched units:** Convert KB to bits correctly using $\times 8 \times 1024$ (binary) or $\times 8 \times 1000$ (decimal). KTU expects the binary form unless otherwise stated. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorise Before Exam** - Ō£ģ The WWW is an **application layer service** running over the Internet using **HTTP**, **HTML**, and **URLs**. - Ō£ģ Created by **Tim Berners-Lee at CERN, 1989ŌĆō1990**; first website went live in **August 1991**. - Ō£ģ Architecture is **client-server**, **stateless**, and **pull-based** (client initiates). - Ō£ģ **Default ports:** HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443 (both TCP). - Ō£ģ **URL grammar:** `scheme://host:port/path?query#fragment` ŌĆö RFC 3986. - Ō£ģ **HTTP methods:** GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, PATCH. - Ō£ģ **Status codes to memorise:** 200 (OK), 301 (Moved Permanently), 304 (Not Modified), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Internal Server Error). - Ō£ģ **State management:** Cookies, URL parameters, hidden form fields, server-side sessions, JWTs. - Ō£ģ **Caching primitive:** Conditional GET using `If-Modified-Since` header ŌåÆ server replies `304 Not Modified` with empty body to save bandwidth. - Ō£ģ **Cache hit rate Ōēł fractional reduction in access link traffic** (linear relationship). - Ō£ģ **Non-persistent HTTP** pays 2 RTT overhead per object; **persistent HTTP with pipelining** pays 1 RTT per page. - Ō£ģ **HTTP/2 advantages:** binary framing, multiplexing, HPACK header compression, server push. - Ō£ģ **Static web pages** are cacheable; **dynamic web pages** are generally not (unless explicitly designed). - Ō£ģ **Total request time formula:** $T_{total} = T_{setup} + RTT_{HTTP} + \frac{S}{R}$. - Ō£ģ **Three architectural tiers in modern web:** Presentation (browser) ŌåÆ Logic (web/app server) ŌåÆ Data (database). - Ō£ģ **HTTP message structure:** Request-line / Status-line ŌåÆ Headers ŌåÆ Empty line ŌåÆ Body (optional). ---

topics.title: HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** is an **application-layer request-response protocol** standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** that forms the foundational communication language used for transmitting hypermedia documents (HTML, images, videos, etc.) between clients (typically web browsers) and servers on the **World Wide Web (WWW)**. It operates as a **stateless** protocol over **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** at the transport layer, conventionally using **port 80** (HTTP) and **port 443** (HTTPS).

The protocol is currently defined in the IETF Request For Comments (RFC) standards:
- **RFC 9110** ŌĆö HTTP Semantics
- **RFC 9111** ŌĆö HTTP Caching
- **RFC 9112** ŌĆö HTTP/1.1 Message Syntax

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of HTTP as the **language of a restaurant**.
> - The **customer (client/browser)** walks in, sits at a table, and tells the **waiter (HTTP)** exactly what dish they want from the **menu (URL/URI)**.
> - The waiter walks to the **kitchen (web server)**, conveys the order, and returns with the **dish (HTML page, image, JSON data)**.
> - The waiter does **not remember** what the customer ordered last time ŌĆö this is the **stateless** nature of HTTP.
> - If the customer wants a custom dish with extra specifications (e.g., "no onions"), they write it on a **chit (HTTP headers)**.
> - The bill at the end (the **status code**) tells the customer whether the dish was prepared successfully or not.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Default Port:** TCP **80** (HTTP), TCP **443** (HTTPS over TLS)
- **Underlying Transport:** **TCP** with a three-way handshake
- **Statefulness:** **Stateless** (each request is independent)
- **MIME-like Content Typing:** Uses `Content-Type` headers
- **Object Referencing:** Via **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)**

## 1.4 Position in the TCP/IP Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HTTP sits at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of both the **OSI model** and the **TCP/IP model**, relying on TCP for reliable, ordered byte-stream delivery. It is the de-facto standard for **distributed, collaborative, hypermedia information systems** as defined in RFC 9110.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Properties of HTTP

### 2.1.1 Statelessness
- Every HTTP request stands alone; the server retains **no memory** of prior requests from the same client.
- Session state is achieved externally using **Cookies**, **URL rewriting**, or **hidden form fields**.

### 2.1.2 Request-Response Paradigm
- A **client** initiates communication.
- A **server** listens and replies.
- Communication flows: **Client $\rightarrow$ Server (Request)**, then **Server $\rightarrow$ Client (Response)**.

### 2.1.3 Media Independence
- HTTP is **type-tagged** via `Content-Type` and `Accept` headers, allowing any data format (HTML, JSON, XML, JPEG, PDF).

### 2.1.4 Connection Management
- **Non-persistent (HTTP/1.0 default):** A new TCP connection per object.
- **Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default):** A single TCP connection carries multiple objects.

## 2.2 HTTP Request Methods (Verbs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The method token in the request line indicates the **action** to be performed on the resource identified by the **Request-URI**.

| Method | Semantic Meaning | Safe? | Idempotent? | Body? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GET** | Retrieve a representation of a resource | Yes | Yes | No |
| **POST** | Submit data to be processed (e.g., form submission) | No | No | Yes |
| **PUT** | Replace the target resource with the request payload | No | Yes | Yes |
| **DELETE** | Remove the target resource | No | Yes | Optional |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET but transfers only headers (no body) | Yes | Yes | No |
| **OPTIONS** | Describe the communication options for the target | Yes | Yes | No |
| **PATCH** | Apply partial modifications to a resource | No | No | Yes |
| **TRACE** | Echo the received request (for diagnostics) | Yes | Yes | No |
| **CONNECT** | Establish a tunnel through a proxy (used in HTTPS) | No | No | No |

> **Definitions:**
> - **Safe Method:** Should not alter server state.
> - **Idempotent Method:** Multiple identical requests yield the same result.

## 2.3 HTTP Status Codes

> [!NOTE]
> Status codes are **3-digit integers** indicating the outcome of the server's attempt to satisfy the request. The first digit defines the **class of response**.

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1xx Informational** | 100ŌĆō199 | Request received, continuing process | 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200ŌĆō299 | Request successfully received and accepted | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300ŌĆō399 | Further action needed to fulfill request | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400ŌĆō499 | Request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500ŌĆō599 | Server failed to fulfill a valid request | 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable |

## 2.4 HTTP Versions Evolution

| Version | Year | Key Features | Transport |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HTTP/0.9** | 1991 | GET only, plain HTML, no headers | TCP |
| **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | Headers, methods, status codes, non-persistent | TCP |
| **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 (rev. 2014, 2022) | Persistent connections, pipelining, chunked transfer, host header | TCP |
| **HTTP/2** | 2015 | Binary framing, **multiplexing**, HPACK header compression, server push | TCP + TLS |
| **HTTP/3** | 2022 | **QUIC** (UDP-based), 0-RTT handshake, stream multiplexing | QUIC (over UDP) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö HTTP Performance Timing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations, questions frequently ask for **end-to-end response time** calculations involving **Round Trip Time (RTT)** and **file/object transmission time**.

### 2.5.1 Non-Persistent HTTP (Sequential Object Loading)

For a base HTML file containing **N referenced objects** (e.g., images, CSS, JS) over **N+1 separate TCP connections**, where each TCP connection requires a **three-way handshake** (1 RTT) and the HTTP request/response exchanges 1 RTT:

$$
T_{total} = (N+1) \cdot \left( 2 \cdot RTT + T_{file} \right)
$$

> **Explanation:**
> - Each of the **(N+1) objects** opens a **new TCP connection**.
> - **1 RTT** is consumed by the **TCP three-way handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK).
> - **1 RTT** is consumed by the **HTTP request and HTTP response** exchange.
> - **$T_{file}$** is the actual file transmission time.

### 2.5.2 Persistent HTTP (Without Pipelining)

$$
T_{total} = \left( 2 \cdot RTT + T_{HTML} \right) + N \cdot \left( RTT + T_{object\_i} \right)
$$

> **Explanation:**
> - The first object uses **2 RTT** (handshake + first request-response).
> - Subsequent objects reuse the same TCP connection, costing only **1 RTT** each (request-response only).

### 2.5.3 Persistent HTTP (With Pipelining)

$$
T_{total} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{HTML} + \max_{i=1..N} \left( T_{object\_i} \right)
$$

> **Explanation:**
> - All objects are requested **back-to-back without waiting** for individual responses.
> - The limiting factor becomes the **largest object transmission time**.

### 2.5.4 Per-Object Time (Single Object)

$$
T_{object} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{file}
$$

> **Justification of "2 RTT":**
> - 1st RTT: TCP three-way handshake (`SYN`, `SYN-ACK`, `ACK`).
> - 2nd RTT: Client sends `HTTP request`, server returns `HTTP response`.

### 2.5.5 HTTP/3 0-RTT Handshake

$$
T_{HTTP3} = 1 \cdot RTT + T_{file} \quad \text{(no TCP handshake; QUIC combines transport + crypto)}
$$

> **Engineering Note:** In real-world CDNs, **persistent connections with pipelining** and **parallel connections** (typically **6** per browser) reduce the effective number of distinct TCP connections required.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Browsers & Crawlers:** Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox use HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 for blazing-fast page loads.
- **RESTful APIs:** Mobile apps and microservices use HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 with `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`.
- **CDNs (Content Delivery Networks):** Akamai, Cloudflare use HTTP caching (Conditional GET) to serve 70%+ of web traffic from edge servers.
- **IoT Devices:** Lightweight HTTP requests (e.g., CoAP over UDP) are used for sensor telemetry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Response Time of Non-Persistent vs. Persistent HTTP

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently repeated 14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE papers. We will exhaustively derive the formula for downloading a web page.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a base HTML file of size **$S_{HTML} = 100$ KB** that references **N = 10 inline images**, each of size **$S_{img} = 50$ KB**. The one-way propagation delay is **$d_{prop} = 50$ ms**, and the link transmission rate is **$R = 1$ Mbps**. Neglect the TCP handshake overhead for the very first connection (assume persistent after the first object). Find the total response time for:
- (a) Non-persistent HTTP (parallel connections allowed, up to 6 at a time)
- (b) Persistent HTTP with pipelining

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Compute the RTT

$$
RTT = 2 \cdot d_{prop} = 2 \cdot 50 \text{ ms} = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2: Compute File Transmission Times

For HTML file:

$$
T_{HTML} = \frac{S_{HTML}}{R} = \frac{100 \text{ KB}}{1 \text{ Mbps}} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^3 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 0.8 \text{ s}
$$

For each image:

$$
T_{img} = \frac{S_{img}}{R} = \frac{50 \text{ KB}}{1 \text{ Mbps}} = \frac{50 \times 8 \times 10^3}{10^6} = 0.4 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3(a): Non-Persistent HTTP with Parallel Connections

> [!NOTE]
> Browsers typically open **up to 6 parallel TCP connections** per origin. We can model the loading in **batches** of 6.

Total objects including HTML: **N + 1 = 11** objects.

Number of batches = $\lceil \frac{11}{6} \rceil = 2$ batches
- **Batch 1:** HTML + 5 images = 6 objects
- **Batch 2:** Remaining 5 images = 5 objects

For each object in non-persistent mode, time per object:

$$
T_{obj} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{file} = 2 \cdot 0.1 + T_{file} = 0.2 + T_{file} \text{ seconds}
$$

Assuming parallel execution within a batch, the time for Batch 1 is dominated by the largest object (the HTML):

$$
T_{batch1} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{HTML} = 0.2 + 0.8 = 1.0 \text{ s}
$$

For Batch 2 (all 5 images of 0.4 s each):

$$
T_{batch2} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{img} = 0.2 + 0.4 = 0.6 \text{ s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{non-persistent} = T_{batch1} + T_{batch2} = 1.0 + 0.6 = 1.6 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 3(b): Persistent HTTP with Pipelining
