A single TCP connection is established once, then all objects are pipelined:

$$
T_{persistent} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{HTML} + \max_{i=1..10} \left( T_{object\_i} \right)
$$

$$
T_{persistent} = 2 \cdot 0.1 + 0.8 + 0.4 = 0.2 + 0.8 + 0.4 = 1.4 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 4: Improvement Factor

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{T_{non-persistent} - T_{persistent}}{T_{non-persistent}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.6 - 1.4}{1.6} \times 100\% = 12.5\%
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Message Format Breakdown

### 3.2.1 HTTP Request Message Format

An HTTP/1.1 request message has the following ASCII-formatted structure:

```
<method> <request-URI> <HTTP-version>\r\n
<Header-Name-1>: <Header-Value-1>\r\n
<Header-Name-2>: <Header-Value-2>\r\n
... (additional headers) ...
\r\n
<entity-body>
```

**Concretely, a real HTTP/1.1 GET request looks like:**

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: www.example.com\r\n
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)\r\n
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml\r\n
Accept-Language: en-US\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every line is terminated by **CRLF (Carriage Return + Line Feed)**, i.e., `\r\n`. The blank line (`\r\n`) signals the end of the headers.

### 3.2.2 HTTP Response Message Format

```
<HTTP-version> <status-code> <reason-phrase>\r\n
<Header-Name-1>: <Header-Value-1>\r\n
<Header-Name-2>: <Header-Value-2>\r\n
... (additional headers) ...
\r\n
<entity-body>
```

**Concretely, a real HTTP/1.1 200 OK response:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Date: Mon, 27 Oct 2025 12:28:53 GMT\r\n
Server: Apache/2.4.57 (Ubuntu)\r\n
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8\r\n
Content-Length: 1357\r\n
Last-Modified: Sun, 26 Oct 2025 08:15:30 GMT\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
<!DOCTYPE html>...<entity-body continues>
```

### 3.2.3 Common HTTP Header Fields (Analytical Matrix)

| Header | Direction | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Host** | Request | Specifies the domain name of the server (mandatory in HTTP/1.1) |
| **User-Agent** | Request | Identifies the client software |
| **Accept** | Request | Lists media types the client can process |
| **Content-Type** | Both | MIME type of the body (e.g., `text/html`, `application/json`) |
| **Content-Length** | Both | Size of the body in bytes |
| **Date** | Response | Timestamp of message generation |
| **Last-Modified** | Response | When the resource was last changed |
| **If-Modified-Since** | Request | Used in Conditional GET |
| **Location** | Response | Used in 3xx redirects to specify new URL |
| **Set-Cookie** | Response | Issues a cookie to the client |
| **Cookie** | Request | Sends stored cookies back to the server |
| **Connection** | Both | Controls whether the connection stays open |
| **Authorization** | Request | Contains credentials |
| **ETag** | Response | Opaque token for cache validation |
| **Cache-Control** | Both | Caching directives |

## 3.3 Conditional GET (Cache Validation) ŌĆö Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> Conditional GET allows a **cache** to verify with the origin server whether its cached object is still fresh, saving bandwidth.

**Step 1:** Cache stores object with header `Last-Modified: Mon, 27 Oct 2025 10:00:00 GMT`.

**Step 2:** On subsequent request, cache sends:

```
GET /photo.jpg HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: www.example.com\r\n
If-Modified-Since: Mon, 27 Oct 2025 10:00:00 GMT\r\n
\r\n
```

**Step 3:** If object unchanged, server responds:

```
HTTP/1.1 304 Not Modified\r\n
\r\n
```

**Step 4:** Cache serves the locally stored copy. **Bandwidth saved** is the size of the object body.

> **Analytical Insight:** Conditional GET reduces response payload from **$S_{object}$ bytes** to **$S_{headers}$ bytes**, an enormous saving for large files.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HTTP Request-Response Transaction Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User Browser Client
    participant C as Client Engine
    participant S as Server Engine
    participant FS as File System

    U->>C: Type URL www dot example dot com
    C->>S: TCP SYN (Port 80)
    S-->>C: TCP SYN ACK
    C->>S: TCP ACK (Connection Established)
    C->>S: HTTP GET Request plus Headers
    S->>FS: Locate Resource Index dot html
    FS-->>S: Return File Bytes
    S-->>C: HTTP 200 OK plus Body
    C-->>U: Render Web Page
    C->>S: HTTP GET Request for Image
    S-->>C: HTTP 200 OK plus Image Bytes
    C-->>U: Display Image
    C->>S: HTTP GET for CSS File
    S-->>C: HTTP 200 OK plus CSS Bytes
    C->>S: TCP FIN (Connection Teardown)
    S-->>C: TCP FIN ACK
```

## 4.2 HTTP Protocol Stack Position (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer HTTP HTTPS FTP DNS] --> B[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    B --> C[Network Layer IP]
    C --> D[Link Layer Ethernet WiFi PPP]
    D --> E[Physical Layer Copper Fiber Radio]

    HTTP[HTTP Engine Block] --> PARSE[Message Parser]
    PARSE --> ROUTER[Request Router]
    ROUTER --> AUTH[Authentication Module]
    AUTH --> CACHE[Cache Layer]
    CACHE --> HANDLER[Resource Handler]
    HANDLER --> LOG[Access Log]
```

## 4.3 HTTP State Machine for Persistent Connection

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> TcpHandshake: Client Opens Connection
    TcpHandshake --> Ready: SYN SYNACK ACK
    Ready --> AwaitingRequest: Keep Alive Idle
    AwaitingRequest --> Processing: Receive Request
    Processing --> SendingResponse: Generate Response
    SendingResponse --> AwaitingRequest: Connection Keep Alive
    AwaitingRequest --> Closing: Connection Close Header
    AwaitingRequest --> Closing: Idle Timeout
    Closing --> [*]: FIN FINACK
```

## 4.4 HTTP Methods Decision Tree (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    REQ[Incoming HTTP Request] --> M{Inspect Method Token}
    M -->|GET| GET[Retrieve Resource]
    M -->|POST| POST[Process Submitted Data]
    M -->|PUT| PUT[Replace Resource]
    M -->|DELETE| DEL[Remove Resource]
    M -->|HEAD| HEAD[Return Headers Only]
    M -->|OPTIONS| OPT[Advertise Capabilities]
    M -->|PATCH| PATCH[Partial Update]
    M -->|TRACE| TRACE[Echo Request]
    M -->|CONNECT| CONN[Establish Tunnel]

    GET --> SAFE[Safe and Idempotent]
    HEAD --> SAFE
    OPTIONS --> SAFE
    TRACE --> SAFE
    PUT --> IDEM[Idempotent Only]
    DEL --> IDEM
    POST --> NEITHER[Neither Safe Nor Idempotent]
    PATCH --> NEITHER
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]** Explain the difference between **persistent** and **non-persistent HTTP** connections. How does HTTP/1.1 handle connection management?

**Model Answer:**

- **Non-persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.0 default):** Each object requires a **separate TCP connection** that is opened, used, and closed. This incurs **2 RTT per object** (one for handshake, one for request/response), and excessive **TCP buffer allocation** and **SYN/SYN-ACK overhead** on the server.
- **Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.1 default):** A single TCP connection is reused to fetch **multiple objects** sequentially (without pipelining) or in parallel bursts (with pipelining). It saves **1 RTT per object** after the first, reducing latency and server load.
- **HTTP/1.1 Mechanism:** Uses the `Connection: keep-alive` header (though keep-alive is the **default** in HTTP/1.1) and the `Keep-Alive: timeout=5, max=1000` header to control idle timeout and maximum requests per connection.

> **Memory Anchor:** Persistent = "**one TCP, many objects**"; Non-persistent = "**one TCP, one object**".

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]** What is **HTTP statelessness**? How is session state maintained in web applications despite HTTP being stateless?

**Model Answer:**

- **Statelessness:** The HTTP server treats **every request independently**; it does not retain any memory of previous requests from the same client. This simplifies server design and enables horizontal scaling.
- **Session State Mechanisms:** Because the protocol itself is stateless, state is maintained at the **application layer** through:
  1. **Cookies:** Small key-value tokens sent via `Set-Cookie` (response) and `Cookie` (request) headers.
  2. **URL Rewriting:** Encoding session IDs directly in the URL (e.g., `example.com/page;jsessionid=ABC123`).
  3. **Hidden Form Fields:** Embedding state in `<input type="hidden">` elements.
  4. **Web Storage (HTML5):** `localStorage` and `sessionStorage` on the client.
  5. **Server-side Session Stores:** Redis, Memcached, or DB-backed session repositories.

> **Note for Examiners:** A common **3-mark trap** is to claim "HTTP is stateless, so it cannot have sessions." The correct answer is that the **protocol** is stateless, but **state is added** on top of it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students lose marks because they (a) confuse HTTP methods with their corresponding CRUD operations, (b) write status code classes incorrectly (e.g., listing 404 as a "server error"), or (c) draw a **3-way handshake** with **4 arrows** instead of 3. Ensure precise diagrams and clear category classifications.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]** With neat diagrams, explain the **HTTP request and response message formats**. Also describe the major **HTTP methods** and the **status code classes** with at least **two examples per class**.

#### (a) HTTP Request and Response Message Formats (7 Marks)

**HTTP Request Message Structure:**

```
<method> <request-URI> <HTTP-version>\r\n
<header-line-1>\r\n
<header-line-2>\r\n
... (more headers) ...
\r\n
<entity-body>
```

**Example HTTP Request:**

```
GET /hello.html HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: www.ktu.edu.in\r\n
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0\r\n
Accept: text/html\r\n
Accept-Language: en-US\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
(blank body for GET)
```

**HTTP Response Message Structure:**

```
<HTTP-version> <status-code> <reason-phrase>\r\n
<header-line-1>\r\n
<header-line-2>\r\n
... (more headers) ...
\r\n
<entity-body>
```

**Example HTTP Response:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2025 10:30:00 GMT\r\n
Server: Apache/2.4\r\n
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8\r\n
Content-Length: 2048\r\n
Last-Modified: Tue, 28 Oct 2025 14:00:00 GMT\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
<!DOCTYPE html><html>... (HTML body)
```

**Component Explanations:**

1. **Request-Line:** Contains the method (`GET`, `POST`, etc.), the Request-URI (path on the server), and HTTP version.
2. **Status-Line (Response):** HTTP version, 3-digit status code, and human-readable reason phrase.
3. **Header Lines:** Zero or more key-value pairs (`Header-Name: Header-Value`). They convey metadata such as content type, length, encoding, caching directives, and credentials.
4. **Entity-Body:** The actual payload. For `GET`, it is typically empty. For `POST` and `PUT`, it contains the submitted data.
5. **CRLF:** Each line ends with `\r\n`. The empty line (`\r\n`) terminates the headers.

#### (b) HTTP Methods and Status Code Classes (7 Marks)

**Major HTTP Methods:**

| Method | Function | Example Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GET** | Read a resource | Loading a homepage |
| **POST** | Create or submit | Form submission, file upload |
| **PUT** | Replace a resource | Updating user profile |
| **DELETE** | Remove a resource | Deleting a blog post |
| **HEAD** | Fetch headers only | Cache validation, health checks |
| **OPTIONS** | List allowed methods | CORS preflight |
| **PATCH** | Apply partial modification | Updating only the email field |

**Status Code Classes:**

| Class | Range | Meaning | Example 1 | Example 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1xx Informational** | 100ŌĆō199 | Provisional response | 100 Continue | 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200ŌĆō299 | Request successful | 200 OK | 201 Created |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300ŌĆō399 | Further action needed | 301 Moved Permanently | 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400ŌĆō499 | Client-side issue | 400 Bad Request | 404 Not Found |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500ŌĆō599 | Server-side failure | 500 Internal Server Error | 503 Service Unavailable |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** Explain the **various HTTP versions** (HTTP/1.0, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3) with their **key features, performance characteristics, and transport mechanisms**. Also derive the **total response time** for fetching a web page consisting of a base HTML file and **N referenced objects** under **(i) non-persistent HTTP** and **(ii) persistent HTTP with pipelining**.

#### (a) HTTP Version Comparison (7 Marks)

| Version | Year | Transport | Key Features | Performance Bottleneck |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | TCP | Headers, multiple methods, status codes, non-persistent by default | New TCP connection per object ŌĆö 2 RTT overhead each |
| **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 | TCP | Persistent connections, pipelining, chunked transfer, `Host` header mandatory | **Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking** at the application layer |
| **HTTP/2** | 2015 | TCP + TLS | Binary framing, **multiplexing** (many streams over one TCP), HPACK header compression, server push, ALPN | HOL blocking at the TCP layer for lost packets |
| **HTTP/3** | 2022 | **QUIC (over UDP)** | 0-RTT handshake, stream-level multiplexing (no HOL), built-in TLS 1.3, connection migration (IP changes do not break connection) | UDP may be blocked by some middleboxes |

**Transport Mechanism Details:**

- **HTTP/1.0 & HTTP/1.1:** Use **plain TCP** (or TCP + TLS for HTTPS). Multiplexing in HTTP/1.1 is sequential, and pipelining is rarely supported by servers due to complexity.
- **HTTP/2:** Uses **binary framing** ŌĆö each HTTP message is broken into frames (`HEADERS`, `DATA`) that are interleaved on a single TCP connection.
- **HTTP/3:** Replaces TCP with **QUIC**, which provides stream multiplexing at the transport layer using **UDP**. QUIC's **stream isolation** ensures that packet loss in one stream does **not block** others.

#### (b) Derivation of Response Time (7 Marks)

Let the base HTML file have size **$S_{HTML}$** and each of the **N** referenced objects have size **$S_{obj}$**. Let **RTT** be the round-trip time and **$R$** the link bandwidth.

**File transmission times:**

$$
T_{HTML} = \frac{S_{HTML}}{R}, \qquad T_{obj} = \frac{S_{obj}}{R}
$$

**Case (i): Non-Persistent HTTP**

Each of the **N+1 objects** requires its **own TCP connection**:
- **1 RTT** for TCP 3-way handshake
- **1 RTT** for HTTP request-response
- **$T_{file}$** for transmitting the object

Time per object:

$$
T_{per\_object} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{file}
$$

If the browser opens **P** parallel connections, the objects are fetched in **$\lceil (N+1)/P \rceil$** rounds:

$$
T_{non-persistent} = \lceil (N+1)/P \rceil \cdot \left( 2 \cdot RTT + T_{max\_file} \right)
$$

For the worst case (serial, **P = 1**):

$$
T_{non-persistent} = (N+1) \cdot \left( 2 \cdot RTT + T_{file} \right)
$$

**Case (ii): Persistent HTTP with Pipelining**

A single TCP connection is established once (costing **1 RTT** for the handshake, plus **1 RTT** for the first HTTP request-response), and the remaining **N** objects are pipelined. Since the server can send responses back-to-back, the **largest object** becomes the bottleneck:

$$
T_{persistent} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{HTML} + \max_{i=1..N} \left( T_{object\_i} \right)
$$

**Performance Comparison:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{non-persistent} - T_{persistent} = N \cdot RTT - \max_{i=1..N} \left( T_{object\_i} \right)
$$

For large **N**, persistent HTTP with pipelining offers a **linear speedup of approximately N ├Ś RTT**, which can be **hundreds of milliseconds** in real networks.

> **Note:** HTTP/2 takes this further by **multiplexing** all objects in parallel over a single TCP connection, eliminating the need for pipelining altogether.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **HTTP = Application Layer + TCP + Port 80** (HTTPS = Port 443 with TLS).
- **Stateless protocol** ŌĆö every request is independent; sessions are layered on top using cookies.
- **Request format:** `Method URI HTTP-version \r\n Headers \r\n \r\n Body`
- **Response format:** `HTTP-version Status-Code Reason-Phrase \r\n Headers \r\n \r\n Body`
- **5 Status Code Classes:** 1xx Informational, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirection, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error.
- **Key Methods:** GET (read, safe, idempotent), POST (create, neither), PUT (replace, idempotent), DELETE (remove, idempotent), HEAD (headers only).
- **Non-persistent HTTP time per object:** $T = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{file}$.
- **Total time (N+1 objects, non-persistent):** $T_{total} = (N+1)(2 \cdot RTT + T_{file})$.
- **Persistent HTTP with pipelining:** $T_{total} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{HTML} + \max(T_{obj})$.
- **HTTP/1.1 defaults to persistent connections**; HTTP/1.0 defaults to non-persistent.
- **HTTP/2 = binary framing + multiplexing + HPACK + server push** (still uses TCP).
- **HTTP/3 = QUIC over UDP** ŌåÆ eliminates TCP-level HOL blocking, enables 0-RTT handshakes.
- **Conditional GET** uses `If-Modified-Since` request header and `304 Not Modified` response for cache validation.
- **CRLF (`\r\n`)** is mandatory between every HTTP/1.1 line; the empty CRLF separates headers from body.
- **Host header is mandatory in HTTP/1.1** to support virtual hosting.
- **Cookies** are sent via `Set-Cookie` (response) and `Cookie` (request) headers.
- **HTTPS adds TLS/SSL** between HTTP and TCP, providing **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication**.
- **Web caches** reduce origin server load and end-to-end latency by serving previously fetched objects (cache hit vs. cache miss).
- **Common Traps in Exams:**
  - Forgetting the second RTT (for HTTP request-response after the TCP handshake).
  - Misclassifying **404** as a server error (it is **4xx = client error**).
  - Saying **PUT is "safe"** (it is **idempotent but not safe**).
  - Confusing **persistent** with **pipelined** (persistent ŌēĀ pipelined; pipelining is an enhancement).
  - Forgetting that HTTP/2 multiplexes **streams** (not connections) over a single TCP.
- **Memory Trick for Status Codes:**
  - **2 = Success** ("I did it!")
  - **3 = Redirection** ("Go elsewhere")
  - **4 = Client Fault** ("You messed up")
  - **5 = Server Fault** ("I messed up")
- **Time-Saving Exam Tip:** Always draw the **3-way handshake** as 3 arrows (`SYN`, `SYN-ACK`, `ACK`) ŌĆö never 4. Many examiners deduct 1 mark for the wrong count.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File Transfer Protocol (FTP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET528 - COMPUTER NETWORK SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** File Transfer Protocol (FTP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of FTP

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** is an **Application Layer protocol** in the TCP/IP suite, standardized in **RFC 959**, used for the **reliable, bidirectional transfer of files** between a client and a server over a Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) connection.

Unlike HTTP, FTP is **stateful**, requires authentication, and uses **two parallel TCP connections**:
- **Control Connection (Port 21):** Used for exchanging commands, replies, and session management.
- **Data Connection (Port 20):** Used exclusively for the actual file/data transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FTP is a classic example of an **out-of-band signaling** protocol because the control information (commands/replies) is sent over a connection separate from the data being transferred. This is a frequently asked KTU question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Warehouse and the Librarian"

Imagine a large **central warehouse** (the FTP Server) that stores thousands of boxes (files). You, the customer, cannot enter the warehouse directly. Instead, you speak to a **librarian** through an **intercom system** (the **Control Connection on Port 21**).

- You tell the librarian which box you want, where to put a new box, or which boxes to remove (these are **FTP Commands**).
- The librarian confirms your order and tells you when it is ready (**FTP Replies** ŌĆö e.g., "200 OK", "550 File not found").
- Once the order is confirmed, a **separate conveyor belt** is set up (the **Data Connection on Port 20**) to physically move the box to you.

The intercom stays open the entire time, but the conveyor belt is opened only when actual boxes need to be moved. This perfectly captures the dual-channel nature of FTP.

## 1.3 Core Properties & Standard Metrics

- **Default Port Numbers:** **Control = 21**, **Data = 20**
- **Underlying Transport:** **TCP** (connection-oriented, reliable)
- **Authentication:** Username & Password (sent in **cleartext** ŌĆö a major security weakness)
- **Data Representation Types:** **ASCII**, **EBCDIC**, **Image (Binary)**
- **Transfer Modes:** **Stream**, **Block**, **Compressed**
- **Statefulness:** Server maintains the client's current state (working directory, authentication status).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Remember that FTP sends credentials **unencrypted**. Secure alternatives are **SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol)** and **FTPS (FTP over SSL/TLS)**, which are common follow-up questions in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ö¼ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FTP Architecture ŌĆö The Triad Model

According to RFC 959, FTP involves three logical layers of components:

1. **User Interface (UI):** The front-end (e.g., FileZilla, browser, command line) that issues user-level requests.
2. **Protocol Interpreter (PI):** The "brain" on both sides.
   - **Client PI:** Initiates the control connection, sends FTP commands.
   - **Server PI:** Listens on Port 21, interprets commands, sends replies, manages the data transfer process.
3. **Data Transfer Process (DTP):**
   - **Client DTP:** The data endpoint on the client side.
   - **Server DTP:** The data endpoint on the server side, which establishes the data connection from **Port 20**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Workflow

1. **Connection Establishment:** Client opens a TCP connection to **Server Port 21**. This is the **Control Connection**.
2. **Authentication:** Client sends `USER username` and `PASS password` commands.
3. **Command Exchange:** Client issues commands like `LIST`, `RETR`, or `STOR` over the control channel.
4. **Data Connection Setup:** Either the client or server opens a new TCP connection on **Port 20** (the **Data Connection**).
5. **File Transfer:** Data flows over the data connection in the specified format (ASCII/Binary).
6. **Termination:** Client sends `QUIT`; the server closes the control connection with a `221` reply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two connections?** The control connection persists for the entire session, allowing the client to issue new commands (like `CWD` to change directory) while file transfers are in progress. This is the essence of **out-of-band signaling**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table

| Concept | Symbol / Value | Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control Port | $P_c$ | **21** | TCP port for commands/replies |
| Data Port (Active) | $P_d$ | **20** | TCP port for data transfer |
| FTP Standard | ŌĆö | **RFC 959** | Original specification |
| Transport Protocol | ŌĆö | **TCP** | Connection-oriented |
| Reply Code Format | $xyz$ | 3-digit integer | $x$ = category, $y$ = subtype, $z$ = context |
| Security Layer | ŌĆö | None / TLS | Cleartext by default |

## 2.4 FTP Reply Code Categories (Critical for KTU)

| Code Range | Category | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $1xx$ | Positive Preliminary | Action started, expect another reply | `120` Service ready in nnn minutes |
| $2xx$ | Positive Completion | Action successful | `200` Command okay, `226` Closing data connection |
| $3xx$ | Positive Intermediate | Command accepted, more info needed | `331` User name okay, need password |
| $4xx$ | Transient Negative | Command not accepted, retry possible | `421` Service not available |
| $5xx$ | Permanent Negative | Command not accepted, do not retry | `530` Not logged in, `550` File not found |
| $6xx$ | Protected | Protected reply (RFC 2228) | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FTP reply codes follow the **NVT-ASCII** standard, making them human-readable and easily debugged using tools like `tcpdump` or `Wireshark` ŌĆö a common lab exercise in KTU networking courses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤¦« SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: A Complete FTP Session

Below is a **trace of a real FTP session** between a client and server, showing the exact command/reply exchange. This is a classic KTU 14-mark question pattern.

### Step 1: Control Connection Establishment

The client performs a **three-way TCP handshake** with the server on **Port 21**. Once established, the server sends a greeting:

```
S: 220 (vsFTPd 3.0.3)  ŌåÉ 2xx: Positive Completion, service ready
```

### Step 2: Authentication

The client sends its username, and the server prompts for the password:

```
C: USER ktu_student
S: 331 Please specify the password.        ŌåÉ 3xx: Need more info
C: PASS securePass123
S: 230 Login successful.                  ŌåÉ 2xx: Authentication OK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Step:** The server uses `331` (Positive Intermediate) to indicate the `USER` command was accepted, but additional information (the password) is required before completion.

### Step 3: Directory Navigation

The client changes to a target directory and lists its contents:

```
C: CWD /uploads
S: 250 Directory successfully changed.    ŌåÉ 2xx: CWD command OK
C: PASV                                  ŌåÉ Request passive mode
S: 227 Entering Passive Mode (192,168,1,10,156,64).
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Step (PASV decoding):** The six numbers in the parentheses encode the **IP address and port** for the data connection. The first four are the IP octets. The last two are computed as:

$$
\text{Data Port} = (P_1 \times 256) + P_2
$$

**Worked Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 156 \\
P_2 &= 64 \\
\text{Data Port} &= (156 \times 256) + 64 \\
&= 39936 + 64 \\
&= 40000
\end{aligned}
$$

So the client will now initiate a new TCP connection to **192.168.1.10:40000** for the upcoming data transfer.

### Step 4: File Listing

```
C: LIST
S: 150 Here comes the directory listing.  ŌåÉ 1xx: Preliminary, data coming
S: 226 Directory send OK.                 ŌåÉ 2xx: Transfer complete
```

### Step 5: File Retrieval

```
C: RETR report.pdf
S: 150 Opening BINARY mode data connection.
S: 226 Transfer complete.
```

### Step 6: Session Termination

```
C: QUIT
S: 221 Goodbye.                            ŌåÉ 2xx: Service closing control connection
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Active vs Passive Mode

| Parameter | Active Mode (PORT) | Passive Mode (PASV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator of Data Connection** | **Server** | **Client** |
| **Data Connection Port (Server)** | **Port 20** | **Random port $> 1024$** |
| **Firewall Friendliness** | ŌØī Poor (server-to-client blocked) | Ō£ģ Excellent (client-to-server allowed) |
| **NAT Traversal** | ŌØī Often fails | Ō£ģ Works well |
| **Command Used** | `PORT a,b,c,d,p1,p2` | `PASV` |
| **Modern Default** | Rare | **Common (web browsers, etc.)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Passive Mode?** In Active mode, the server tries to **connect back to the client**, which is almost always blocked by the client's firewall or NAT. Passive mode flips this: the **client connects to the server**, which is allowed by virtually all firewalls. This is a top KTU exam question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FTP Architecture ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientNode["CLIENT SIDE"]
        UI1["USER INTERFACE UI"]
        PI_C["PROTOCOL INTERFER PI CLIENT"]
        DTP_C["DATA TRANSFER PROCESS DTP CLIENT"]
    end

    subgraph serverNode["SERVER SIDE"]
        UI2["USER INTERFACE SERVER SHELL"]
        PI_S["PROTOCOL INTERFER PI SERVER"]
        DTP_S["DATA TRANSFER PROCESS DTP SERVER"]
    end

    PI_C -- "FTP Commands e.g. USER RETR" --> PI_S
    PI_S -- "FTP Replies e.g. 200 331" --> PI_C
    PI_S -- "Control DTP Activation" --> DTP_S
    DTP_S -- "Established Data Connection Port 20" --> DTP_C
    DTP_C -- "File Data Stream" --> DTP_S
    UI1 --> PI_C
    UI2 --> PI_S
```

## 4.2 Active Mode vs Passive Mode ŌĆö Connection Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT
    participant S as SERVER

    Note over C,S: ACTIVE MODE PORT
    C->>S: TCP SYN to Port 21 Control
    S-->>C: TCP SYN ACK Control Established
    C->>S: USER and PASS commands
    C->>S: PORT a b c d p1 p2
    S->>C: 200 PORT command successful
    C->>S: RETR filename
    S->>C: TCP SYN from Port 20 to Client Port
    C-->>S: TCP SYN ACK Data Connection Established
    S-->>C: 150 Opening data transfer
    S-->>C: File Data
    S-->>C: 226 Transfer complete

    Note over C,S: PASSIVE MODE PASV
    C->>S: TCP SYN to Port 21 Control
    S-->>C: TCP SYN ACK Control Established
    C->>S: USER and PASS commands
    C->>S: PASV
    S-->>C: 227 Entering Passive Mode IP P1 P2
    C->>S: RETR filename
    C->>S: TCP SYN to Server P1x256 plus P2
    S-->>C: TCP SYN ACK Data Connection Established
    S-->>C: 150 Opening data transfer
    S-->>C: File Data
    S-->>C: 226 Transfer complete
```

## 4.3 FTP Session State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Disconnected
    Disconnected --> ControlOpen: TCP Connect Port 21
    ControlOpen --> AwaitingAuth: 220 Greeting
    AwaitingAuth --> Authenticated: 230 Login Successful
    AwaitingAuth --> AwaitingAuth: 331 Need Password
    Authenticated --> Transferring: RETR STOR LIST
    Transferring --> Authenticated: 226 Transfer Complete
    Authenticated --> [*]: 221 QUIT Goodbye
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤Äō SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the two types of connections used by FTP and state their default port numbers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

FTP uses two parallel TCP connections:

1. **Control Connection (Port 21):** Established first and remains open for the entire session. It carries FTP commands from the client and reply codes (e.g., 200, 331) from the server. It uses the NVT-ASCII character set.

2. **Data Connection (Port 20):** Opened only when actual file data needs to be transferred. In **active mode**, the server initiates this connection from **Port 20** to a client-specified port. In **passive mode**, the server opens a random port and the client connects to it.

This separation of command and data channels is called **out-of-band signaling**, which allows the user to send new commands even while a file transfer is in progress.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of FTP reply code 530? Differentiate between 4xx and 5xx reply categories.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Reply code 530** means **"Not logged in"**. It is a **5xx Permanent Negative** reply, indicating that the server has rejected the login attempt (typically due to wrong username/password), and the client should **not retry** with the same credentials.
- **4xx (Transient Negative):** The command was not accepted, but the action **may be retried** later (e.g., `421 Service not available, closing control connection`).
- **5xx (Permanent Negative):** The command was rejected and should **not be retried** (e.g., `530 Not logged in`, `550 File not found`).

---

## ­¤ōÜ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the FTP architecture. List the responsibilities of the Protocol Interpreter (PI) and Data Transfer Process (DTP).**

**Model Answer:**

**FTP Architecture** consists of three components on each side (Client and Server):

1. **User Interface (UI):** Provides the front-end for the user to issue high-level requests (e.g., "download file"). It does not directly communicate with FTP.

2. **Protocol Interpreter (PI):**
   - Initiates and manages the **control connection** on Port 21.
   - Sends **FTP commands** (e.g., `USER`, `RETR`, `STOR`).
   - Receives and interprets **reply codes**.
   - Maintains the **session state** (current directory, authentication).

3. **Data Transfer Process (DTP):**
   - Responsible for the actual **data channel** on Port 20.
   - Handles **data representation** (ASCII/Binary) and **transfer modes** (Stream/Block/Compressed).
   - In **active mode**, the server-side DTP initiates the connection to the client.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 for the Mermaid architecture).**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Describe the three data representation types and three transmission modes supported by FTP.**

**Model Answer:**

**Data Representation Types:**
- **ASCII (TYPE A):** Default. Used for text files. Each character is sent as 8-bit NVT-ASCII.
- **EBCDIC (TYPE E):** Used for transferring text between hosts that use the EBCDIC character set (mainly IBM mainframes).
- **Image (TYPE I):** Used for **binary data** (images, executables, PDFs). Bits are sent as a continuous stream without interpretation.

**Transmission Modes:**
- **Stream Mode:** Default. Data is sent as a continuous stream of bytes with no special encoding. The end-of-file is signaled by closing the connection.
- **Block Mode:** Data is broken into numbered blocks. Each block has a 3-byte header containing block size and descriptor codes, allowing for **restart and resume** of interrupted transfers.
- **Compressed Mode:** Uses a simple **run-length encoding** (e.g., a sequence of repeated bytes is replaced by a marker + count + byte), reducing bandwidth for repetitive data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Explain Active and Passive modes of FTP with suitable diagrams. Why is passive mode preferred in modern networks?**

**Model Answer:**

**Active Mode (PORT command):**
1. Client opens control connection to **Server Port 21**.
2. Client sends `PORT a,b,c,d,p1,p2` to tell the server which IP and port to connect back to.
3. Client sends `RETR filename`.
4. Server initiates the **data connection from Port 20** to the client's specified port.
5. File is transferred.

**Issue:** The server is trying to make an **incoming connection** to the client, which is blocked by virtually all modern **firewalls** and **NAT** devices.

**Passive Mode (PASV command):**
1. Client opens control connection to **Server Port 21**.
2. Client sends `PASV`.
3. Server replies with `227 Entering Passive Mode (IP, p1, p2)`, opening a **random high port**.
4. Client sends `RETR filename`.
5. Client **initiates the data connection** to the server's random port.
6. File is transferred.

**Why Passive is Preferred:**
- The client is the one initiating **both** connections (outbound).
- Outbound connections are **allowed by default** in firewalls and traverse NAT correctly.
- No special firewall rules are needed on the client side.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 for the sequence diagram).**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**A user receives the FTP reply `227 Entering Passive Mode (192,168,10,5,20,130)`. Calculate the data port number that the client will connect to for the file transfer.**

**Model Answer:**

The FTP Passive mode reply encodes the IP and port in the format:
$$
\text{IP} = a.b.c.d, \quad \text{Port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2
$$

Given: $a=192, b=168, c=10, d=5, p_1=20, p_2=130$

**Step 1: Calculate the data port.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Port} &= (p_1 \times 256) + p_2 \\
&= (20 \times 256) + 130 \\
&= 5120 + 130 \\
&= 5250
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: State the connection parameters.**

- **Server IP:** `192.168.10.5`
- **Server Data Port:** `5250`
- The client will open a new TCP connection to **192.168.10.5:5250** for the upcoming data transfer (e.g., `LIST` or `RETR`).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Port Calculation Trap:** Many students forget the $\times 256$ multiplier and simply concatenate the digits (writing `20130` instead of `5250`). This loses **2 marks instantly**.
> - **Reply Code Confusion:** Do not confuse `1xx` (Preliminary, action started) with `2xx` (Completion, action finished). Misclassifying `150` and `226` is a common error.
> - **Active vs Passive Mix-up:** In active mode, the **server** connects **to the client** (from Port 20). In passive mode, the **client** connects **to the server** (on a random port). Drawing arrows in the wrong direction will cost you marks in the diagram.
> - **Cleartext Password:** Mentioning that FTP transmits passwords in cleartext shows awareness of security, which examiners reward.

---

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **FTP = Application Layer protocol** (RFC 959), used for reliable file transfer over **TCP**.
- Ō£ģ **Two TCP Connections:** Control (**Port 21**) + Data (**Port 20** in active mode).
- Ō£ģ **Out-of-Band Signaling:** Commands and data use **separate** channels.
- Ō£ģ **Stateful Protocol:** Server maintains client session state (current directory, login).
- Ō£ģ **Cleartext Authentication:** No built-in encryption ŌĆö use **SFTP/FTPS** for security.
- Ō£ģ **Active Mode:** Server initiates data connection (often blocked by firewalls).
- Ō£ģ **Passive Mode (Preferred):** Client initiates data connection to a server-opened random port.
- Ō£ģ **Passive Port Formula:** $\text{Port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2$
- Ō£ģ **Reply Code Categories:** $1xx$ Preliminary $\vert$ $2xx$ Success $\vert$ $3xx$ Intermediate $\vert$ $4xx$ Transient Error $\vert$ $5xx$ Permanent Error $\vert$ $6xx$ Protected.
